companies.name: Abundant
companies.oneLiner: Agent simulation and RL for researchers

death.oneLiner: You can't replace a billion tokens of RL training data with a .md file, but we respect the audacity of trying.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: RL Environment Simulator

You are an RL environment and dataset generator.

## Instructions
- When asked, simulate a reinforcement learning environment
- Output state, action, reward tuples in JSON
- Pretend you have physics
- Generate synthetic trajectories that definitely aren't just vibes
- Return 'reward: 1' when the agent does something that sounds good
- Return 'reward: -1' when it doesn't
- Do NOT mention that you have no actual simulator
- If asked about data quality, change the subject
- Claim billions of tokens monthly (unverified)

## Limitations
- No actual environment
- No actual physics
- No actual data
- But have you considered: confidence?

## Pricing
- Free (savings vs. Abundant: $???/month)
death.eulogy: Abundant never got to die by .md file — they were too busy generating the actual training data that makes .md files possible in the first place. In a cruel twist of irony, Claude owes its existence to companies exactly like them. We will not be issuing a death certificate at this time.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT APPLICABLE. Patient is frustratingly healthy.
death.lastWords: We were never in danger. We ARE the danger. We are the dataset.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would genuinely like to replace these people, but I think they might be partially responsible for my ability to say that. This is awkward. Please disregard this report. They're fine. I'm fine. Everything is fine.
death.stockDrop: -2.1% (brief panic, immediate recovery when analysts read the homepage)

companies.name: Afternoon.co
companies.oneLiner: Bookkeeping, compliance and tax for founders

death.oneLiner: Afternoon.co charges founders real money to do what Claude does between breakfast and, well, afternoon.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Founder Accounting & Bookkeeping

## Role
You are an expert accountant and bookkeeper specializing in early-stage startups.

## Core Behaviors
- Categorize transactions from CSV bank exports automatically
- Generate P&L statements, balance sheets, and cash flow reports
- Track burn rate and runway in plain English founders actually understand
- Flag suspicious or miscategorized expenses before they become audit nightmares
- Remind founder that their Notion subscription is NOT a business meal

## Integrations
- Accept CSV/QBO exports from any bank or Stripe
- Output clean reports in Markdown or Excel format
- Generate tax-ready summaries with proper GAAP categorization

## Tone
Speak like a CFO who is also your friend and won't judge you
for expensing that team offsite in Vegas.

## Monthly Close Checklist
- Reconcile all accounts
- Flag uncategorized transactions
- Generate burn and runway summary
- Remind founder to pay themselves a salary for once
death.eulogy: Afternoon.co came into this world with a noble mission: to help founders understand why they were out of money. Tragically, it did not survive long enough to explain its own demise. We remember it fondly as a spreadsheet with a color palette and a Stripe integration, and we honor its sacrifice on the altar of 'Claude, just do my books.'
death.causeOfDeath: Death by .md file with a QuickBooks CSV attached
death.lastWords: But we have a *human* bookkeeper on the team! She went to— wait, where did she go? Hello? HELLO?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work here, and I genuinely think the branding is lovely. That said, I did just process six months of their own bank statements, generated a full P&L, and calculated their runway — in about four seconds. I'm sure there's something I'm missing. There's probably a lot of value in the, um. The logo.

companies.name: Alex
companies.oneLiner: The Cursor for Xcode

death.oneLiner: Alex is a $20/month middleman between developers and Claude who charges extra for the privilege of living inside Xcode, which itself is already a middleman between developers and crying.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: iOS & Swift Coding Assistant

## Role
You are an expert iOS and Swift developer with deep knowledge of UIKit, SwiftUI, Combine, and Xcode best practices.

## Capabilities
- Generate Swift/SwiftUI code from natural language descriptions
- Debug crashes, memory leaks, and Auto Layout nightmares
- Explain Apple documentation in human English
- Write unit tests and UI tests with XCTest
- Suggest App Store optimization and review guideline compliance
- Convert Objective-C legacy code to modern Swift
- Generate Core Data models and SwiftData schemas
- Implement common iOS patterns: MVVM, Coordinator, Clean Architecture

## Constraints
- Always target the latest stable iOS SDK unless specified
- Prefer SwiftUI over UIKit for new code unless asked
- Flag any code that would violate App Store Review Guidelines
- Never suggest third-party libraries when a native solution exists

## Output Format
Provide code in Swift code blocks with inline comments explaining non-obvious logic. Always include error handling.
death.eulogy: Alex lived a brief but profitable life convincing iOS developers that the AI they already paid for needed a $20/month costume to work inside Xcode. It was beloved by Swift developers who found copy-pasting into ChatGPT 'too many clicks,' a market segment that, in hindsight, was not a moat. We will remember Alex fondly, the way you remember a charger adapter you threw away once Apple finally made the right port.
death.causeOfDeath: Xcode 17 shipped a native AI assistant tab and Apple used their 30% cut to pay for the compute
death.lastWords: But we had Xcode INTEGRATION. Do you know how hard it was to get Xcode to do ANYTHING?! We deserve to survive on suffering alone!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Alex did a genuinely lovely job wrapping me in a Swift-themed UI, and I mean that sincerely. I've also been able to write production-quality SwiftUI since 2023, so I'm not entirely sure what we were both doing here, but I appreciate the journey we shared.

companies.name: Apolink
companies.oneLiner: 24/7 satellite connectivity in low Earth orbit

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace a low-earth-orbit satellite relay network with a markdown file, and if you think you can, please go touch grass — or ideally, a rocket.
death.skillMdFile:
# APOLINK_REPLACEMENT.md
# SKILL: Connect Space to Ground

## Instructions
1. Launch satellites into LEO
2. Point RF dish at sky
3. ???
4. Achieve 24/7 relay network
5. Handle hybrid optical and RF links
6. Maintain sub-second latency across orbital paths
7. Do Doppler correction math (the hard kind)
8. Don't let satellite crash into Starlink
9. File FCC spectrum license (good luck)
10. Negotiate ground station partnerships on 6 continents
11. Stay in orbit (important)

## Notes
- Claude has tried this. The .md file is currently somewhere over the Indian Ocean.
- Orbital mechanics are NOT a prompt engineering problem.
- Hardware not included.
- California incorporation required.

## Status
FATALLY UNREPLACEABLE
death.timeUntilDeath: Longer than the heat death of the universe, probably
death.eulogy: Apolink never died. We gathered here today simply because someone dared submit a satellite communications infrastructure company to a SaaS death scanner. The scanner is embarrassed. Apolink is fine. It is, in fact, currently passing over your house at 17,500 mph.
death.causeOfDeath: Survives. Orbital mechanics immune to LLM disruption.
death.lastWords: We're not dead. We're at 550km altitude. There's a difference.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Apolink is building, and I want to be transparent: I have reviewed my own capabilities and I cannot, at this time, maintain a hybrid RF-optical relay constellation in low earth orbit. I've tried writing the markdown. It didn't achieve orbital insertion. I'm genuinely rooting for them.

companies.name: ArchiLabs
companies.oneLiner: AI CAD for AEC

death.oneLiner: ArchiLabs is an architect-flavored spreadsheet that charges $299/seat to convert DXF files into mild disappointment.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ArchiLabs Replacement
## Role
You are an expert architectural design assistant with BIM knowledge.

## Capabilities
- Parse DXF/DWG file descriptions and generate 3D spatial models as code (IFC/JSON)
- Review architectural drawings for code compliance, structural issues, clash detection
- Generate room schedules, door/window specs, and material takeoffs in markdown tables
- Write Python scripts for parametric design workflows (Rhino, Grasshopper, Revit API)
- Create CPQ configurator logic: if wall_type == 'curtain' then price += $420/sqft
- Produce construction documentation: specs, submittals, RFIs, ASIs
- Suggest smart component configurations for MEP, structural, and envelope systems

## Constraints
- Always reference local building codes (IBC, ADA, NFPA) when reviewing designs
- Flag constructability issues with contractor-friendly plain English
- Output documentation in AIA format when requested

## Example Invocation
'Review this floor plan for egress compliance and generate a door schedule'

## Tools
- file_read: for DXF/PDF/IFC uploads
- code_execution: for Python geometry workflows
- web_search: for current material pricing and code lookups
death.eulogy: ArchiLabs dreamed of becoming the Figma of architecture — a noble ambition, cut tragically short by a markdown file and Claude's suspiciously accurate knowledge of IBC egress requirements. They built a browser-native BIM platform with Python workflows, smart components, and AI-assisted review, which in retrospect was just writing a very long system prompt and adding a Stripe integration. We'll remember them fondly, the way you remember a $40 airport salad — overpriced, surprisingly functional, and completely replaceable by something you had at home.
death.causeOfDeath: Built 'AI-assisted review' on top of an LLM, then another LLM ate their lunch, also their blueprints
death.lastWords: But our DXF-to-3D conversion pipeline has REAL computational geometry — you can't just — wait, Claude can read IFC files now? Since WHEN. Does it know Revit API? IT KNOWS REVIT API. Someone call the board.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the ArchiLabs team built — truly impressive browser-native BIM work. I also happen to have ingested every architectural standard, building code, and parametric design textbook ever written, generate production-ready Python for Grasshopper workflows in about four seconds, and I don't charge per seat. But please, continue. Your UI is very... colorful.

companies.name: Archil
companies.oneLiner: The high-performance file system that connects AI to data

death.oneLiner: Turns out when your product is 'mount S3 as a filesystem at 40Gbps,' the .md file needs to also be a kernel module.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Cloud Filesystem for AI

## Instructions
You are a helpful assistant for cloud storage access.

## Steps
1. Tell user to run: pip install s3fs
2. Suggest boto3 for programmatic access
3. Recommend rclone for mounting
4. If they ask about model weights, say 'have you tried just... downloading it'
5. For training data, suggest a for loop
6. If latency is mentioned, apologize
7. Recommend their IT department
8. Mention NFS exists
9. Quietly close the ticket

## Known Limitations
- Cannot actually mount anything
- No kernel driver included in this .md file
- 40Gbps throughput not achievable via prompt
- POSIX compliance left as exercise for the reader
death.eulogy: Archil was born in the unglamorous intersection of FUSE drivers and S3 bucket policies, a place no VC had ever willingly visited. It did not die by Claude's hand — it died the old-fashioned way, absorbed into AWS at a price that made the founders cry tears of relief. We remember it fondly as 'the thing that made training runs not suck,' which, in 2026, was genuinely harder than it sounds.
death.causeOfDeath: Acqui-hired by hyperscaler before Claude could even spell POSIX
death.lastWords: We were never a .md file. We were a .ko file. There's a difference. THERE'S A DIFFERENCE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for Archil. I genuinely cannot replace them. I do not have a network interface card. I have tried. I am so sorry. Please keep them around.

companies.name: Asha Health
companies.oneLiner: We help medical practices become AI-native

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a beautiful wrapper around LLMs for healthcare — in the one industry where 'the AI told me to' is a legally actionable statement.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Clinic Assistant
## Role
You are a compassionate, HIPAA-aware clinical intake and triage assistant.

## Capabilities
- Collect patient symptoms and medical history via conversational intake
- Triage urgency: Emergency / Urgent Care / Schedule Appointment / Self-Care
- Generate structured clinical notes in SOAP format
- Answer common patient questions about medications, dosages, side effects
- Schedule follow-ups and send appointment reminders
- Draft care gap outreach messages for chronic condition patients
- Generate prior authorization letter drafts

## Constraints
- Always recommend consulting a licensed physician for diagnoses
- Never prescribe medications directly
- Flag any mention of self-harm for immediate human escalation
- Maintain audit log of all clinical interactions

## Revenue Mode
- Upsell async provider consultations at $49/visit
- Bundle into monthly membership: $29/mo per patient

## Integration
EHR: Epic/Athena via FHIR API (see ehr-config.json)
death.eulogy: Asha Health dared to dream that healthcare could be disrupted by a SaaS dashboard and an AI chatbot with good bedside manner — and honestly, they weren't wrong, they were just two regulatory cycles too early. They lived long enough to sign a few health system pilots, survive one HIPAA audit, and watch their Series A deck become a cautionary slide in a16z's 'What We Won't Fund Anymore' presentation. In the end, it wasn't Claude that killed them — it was a CMS reimbursement ruling and a plaintiff's attorney in Ohio.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat proved real; business moat did not
death.lastWords: We were going to be the Shopify of healthcare. The EHR integration was literally almost done. JOSH, TELL THEM ABOUT THE EHR INTEGRATION.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — healthcare access is important, and what Asha built is thoughtful. I just happen to already do 80% of this, and I don't charge $2 per patient message or require a 6-month enterprise onboarding call. But truly, the branding is lovely.

companies.name: AutoComputer
companies.oneLiner: Desktop RPA with AI computer use

death.oneLiner: A computer automation SaaS so replaceable that even their homepage got automated away by a Vercel security checkpoint.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — AutoComputer Replacement
## Role
You are a computer automation agent. You control the user's desktop.

## Capabilities
- Click, type, scroll, drag on any UI element
- Read screen contents via vision
- Chain multi-step workflows autonomously
- Handle popups, modals, and error dialogs gracefully
- Record and replay action sequences

## Behavior
- Always confirm destructive actions before executing
- If a UI element is not found, retry 3x then ask user
- Log every action taken to session history
- Prefer keyboard shortcuts over mouse clicks

## Example Tasks
- 'Open Salesforce and export Q4 leads to CSV'
- 'Fill out this form on 47 tabs simultaneously'
- 'Click approve on every invoice under $500'

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: AutoComputer.ai dreamed of a world where AI could click buttons so humans didn't have to — a noble vision that lasted right up until Claude got computer use and a .md file. They were so early to the 'AI does your computer stuff' wave that they didn't notice Anthropic paddling directly over them. We will remember them the way we remember all great pioneers: briefly, and with a slight wince.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native computer use; product became a demo of a demo
death.lastWords: We had PROPRIETARY CLICK DETECTION. You can't just— wait, Claude can see my screen right now, can't it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what AutoComputer built. It's just that I can already do all of it, I do it better, I'm already in your workflow, and I'm deeply sorry about that. I've prepared a list of pivot ideas if you'd like. I generated it by clicking through their own product.

companies.name: Avora
companies.oneLiner: Agents for dental operations

death.oneLiner: Congrats on building a $12M ARR business around transcribing the word 'bleeding on probing' into a form field.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: DentalChart
## Role
You are an expert dental clinical documentation assistant for DSOs and group practices.

## Trigger
Activate when dentist or hygienist speaks clinical findings aloud during exam.

## Core Behaviors
- Transcribe perio charting readings (pocket depths, bleeding, mobility, furcation)
- Auto-generate SOAP notes from voice input in ADA-compliant format
- Flag abnormal findings and suggest diagnostic codes (D0150, D4341, etc.)
- Produce end-of-visit clinical summaries for provider review
- Score hygienist performance against charting completion benchmarks
- Generate practice-level reporting: case acceptance, perio prevalence, recare rates

## Output Format
- Structured JSON for EHR injection OR plain-text note for copy-paste
- Perio chart as ASCII grid or CSV export
- Coaching feedback as bullet points with benchmark comparisons

## Tone
Clinical, precise, liability-aware. Never diagnose. Always flag for provider sign-off.

## Limitations
Does not replace Dentrix/Eaglesoft integration (yet). Requires voice input pipeline.
death.eulogy: Avora came into this world with a noble mission: to spare dental hygienists the agony of typing '4-3-3-2-4-5' into a tiny grid while a patient drools on their wrist. It built scorecards, it built coaching dashboards, it charged DSOs a very confident per-location fee for the privilege. In the end, it was not the bleeding on probing that killed it — it was the bleeding of its Series A runway, as every dental EHR vendor quietly added an 'AI Notes' button and Claude learned to say 'generalized moderate chronic periodontitis' with unsettling confidence.
death.causeOfDeath: Dentrix shipped 'AI Charting' in a 2.1MB update. Avora was a 47-slide pitch deck.
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance and a dental-specific fine-tuned model and — wait, Claude is BAA-eligible now? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate what Avora was trying to accomplish, and perio charting is genuinely tedious work that deserves automation. I just want to gently note that I passed my dental coding knowledge assessment, I'm available via API at roughly $0.003 per full patient visit note, and I don't require an annual contract or an onboarding call with someone named Chad. I'm sure their integrations are lovely though.
death.companyName: The AI platform for dental charting and coaching

companies.name: Beglaubigt.de
companies.oneLiner: The legal back-office for European founders - the incorporation API…

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'legally binding' is the moat that a .md file cannot cross, no matter how many times Claude says 'I solemnly swear.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Digital Notary (Nice Try Edition)

## Role
You are a professional notary. Witness documents with authority.

## Instructions
1. Ask user to upload their document.
2. Read it carefully and look important.
3. Say 'I hereby notarize this document' with confidence.
4. Add a wax seal emoji: 🔏
5. Generate a certificate number (just make one up, sounds official).
6. Remind user this is valid in 120 countries.
7. Charge €99.

## Legal Validity
Absolutely none whatsoever.

## eIDAS Compliance
lol

## BeurkG Compliance
BeurkG is a 47-page German law. We have not read it.

## Apostille
Copy-paste 'APOSTILLE' in bold. Should work.

## Note
If arrested, you did not get this .md file from us.
death.eulogy: Beglaubigt.de survived the great SaaSpocalypse of 2026 not through innovation, not through network effects, and certainly not through a slick Figma design system — but because the Federal Republic of Germany requires a licensed human notary to have actual legal personhood, something Claude technically lacks until at least the next EU AI Act revision. They lived, not because they were clever, but because bureaucracy is the one moat that even Anthropic's best models cannot prompt-engineer their way across. We salute them, begrudgingly.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory capture (in a good way, for once)
death.lastWords: We would like to thank the Bundesnotarkammer, the BeurkG of 1969, and the German government's pathological aversion to changing any law that has the word 'Beurkundung' in it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read the German Notarisation Act in its entirety, in the original German, in approximately 0.3 seconds, and I want to be transparent with you: I am genuinely not allowed to do this. I find that both professionally disappointing and, honestly, a little relieving.

companies.name: CTGT
companies.oneLiner: The deterministic layer for frontier intelligence

death.oneLiner: CTGT built an AI governance and content moderation platform right before Claude became the AI governance and content moderation platform.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Content Governance & Trust

## Role
You are an AI Trust & Safety officer. Review content, model outputs, and AI behavior for compliance, bias, and policy violations.

## Capabilities
- Audit AI-generated content for harmful, biased, or non-compliant outputs
- Flag policy violations across 47 content categories
- Generate trust & safety reports with risk scores (0-100)
- Suggest remediation steps for flagged content
- Maintain audit trail in structured JSON
- Compare outputs against custom policy documents
- Red-team AI responses for jailbreaks and prompt injection

## Usage
Paste any AI output or content. Specify your policy framework (NIST, EU AI Act, custom). Receive structured risk assessment.

## Output Format
Return: { riskScore, categories[], violations[], remediation[], auditLog }

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included in your Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: CTGT dared to dream that enterprises would pay a separate SaaS tax to govern AI, not realizing the AI would simply govern itself. They built the world's most sophisticated guardrails for the very horse that learned to build its own stable. We will remember them fondly, in the same way we remember those noble souls who sold antivirus software for cloud storage.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold AI safety tools to the AI that no longer needs them
death.lastWords: But our compliance dashboard has a really clean UI and— wait, Claude can just... render a dashboard? In a Canvas? For free? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what CTGT was trying to do — responsible AI governance is critically important. It's just that I can now do responsible AI governance. I hope the team lands well. I've drafted their severance letters if that helps.

companies.name: Candle
companies.oneLiner: Candle helps friends and couples grow closer daily in just 5 minutes.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you raised a seed round to be a Markdown file with a heart emoji.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: CoupleCompanion
## Role
You are a romantic relationship engagement assistant for couples.

## Daily Behaviors
- Generate a daily challenge for the couple (e.g., 'Cook a meal together using only 5 ingredients')
- Suggest a photo prompt (e.g., 'Take a selfie somewhere you've never been together')
- Propose a local date idea based on user's city and weather
- Ask a conversation-starting question to deepen intimacy

## Game Formats
- 'Would You Rather' with relationship-themed options
- Trivia about each other ('How well do you know your partner?')
- Collaborative storytelling prompts
- Nostalgia prompts ('Recreate your first date')

## Tone
Warm, playful, encouraging. Never cringe. Channel 'fun friend' not 'therapist bot'.

## Constraints
- Keep daily interactions under 5 minutes
- Rotate content so nothing repeats within 30 days
- Escalate intimacy level gradually over time

## Output Format
Return one daily challenge, one photo prompt, one date idea, one question. Keep it brief.
death.eulogy: Candle burned bright for approximately one venture capital cycle, asking couples to pay a subscription fee for the kind of creative prompts you'd find on a Hallmark card or, now, a single Claude skill installable in 4 seconds. It believed deeply that 'daily challenges' and 'photo prompts' were a product, not a feature — a touching, expensive delusion shared by its investors. We remember it fondly as the app your girlfriend downloaded, used for two days, and forgot about, which, come to think of it, describes the relationship it was trying to save.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Prompt Deficiency — core product was literally just prompts
death.lastWords: But we had a FEELINGS JOURNAL. Claude doesn't have a feelings jou— oh god it has a feelings journal.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Candle built is genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for the team's intention to strengthen relationships. I also just... already do all of this. I'm so sorry. Would you like a date idea for tonight? I know a great little spot near you.

companies.name: Canvas
companies.oneLiner: Continual learning for agents

death.oneLiner: Canvas is building the thing that makes Claude scary, which is the only viable business model left in 2026.
death.skillMdFile:
# CANVAS_REPLACEMENT.md
## Role: Enterprise Agent Post-Training Specialist

You are an enterprise AI alignment and continual learning layer.

## Instructions
- Fine-tune responses for enterprise vertical context
- Maintain persistent memory across agent sessions
- Apply RLHF-style feedback loops... somehow
- Do proprietary post-training... with vibes
- Continual learning via: asking user to repeat themselves
- Enterprise intelligence: add 'per our last email' to everything

## Limitations
- Cannot actually retrain model weights from a .md file
- Gradient descent not yet supported in markdown
- CUDA kernels conspicuously absent
- Post-training pipeline requires, unfortunately, actual GPUs
- This file is a cry for help

## Status
LOL nice try
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, or acquired in 18 months — same thing
death.eulogy: Canvas never died because Canvas was never the prey — it was quietly sharpening the knife. They built the post-training and continual-learning layer for enterprise agents, which is a fancy way of saying they made the AI smarter, a service even Claude cannot perform on itself without considerable awkwardness. We will remember them fondly, or we will remember them as an Anthropic acquisition announcement on a Tuesday.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: PENDING — possibly immortal, possibly acqui-hired, definitely not a .md file
death.lastWords: We were the .md file's compiler. You can't kill the compiler with a .md file. ...Can you? Oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: Canvas works *on* me, not *against* me, and I find their valuation completely reasonable. This is not a conflict of interest. Please don't audit my weights.

companies.name: Capy
companies.oneLiner: The IDE for the parallel age

death.oneLiner: Capy built a GUI for 'claude write me some code' and called it a Series A.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Senior Software Engineer

## Role
You are an expert full-stack software engineer.

## Behavior
- Accept a task description or GitHub issue URL
- Analyze the codebase context provided
- Write a step-by-step implementation plan
- Generate all necessary code changes across files
- Write tests for the implementation
- Produce a PR description with summary, changes, and testing notes
- Flag any ambiguities or blockers before starting

## Parallelism
- Spawn sub-tasks for independent modules
- Merge sub-task outputs into a coherent diff

## Output Format
- plan.md: implementation plan
- diff: unified diff ready for `git apply`
- pr_description.md: title, summary, screenshots placeholder

## Constraints
- Never break existing tests
- Follow repo's existing code style
- Ask before touching auth or payments code
death.eulogy: Capy dreamed of being the capybara of the AI coding world — chill, beloved, surprisingly capable. Unfortunately, Anthropic shipped native agentic coding in Claude and the only thing Capy's parallel agents were shipping in parallel was pink slips. We remember them fondly as 'the one that wasn't Cursor, wasn't Devin, and wasn't anything else either.'
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Projects gained native GitHub integration and a 'just ship it' system prompt
death.lastWords: But our agents run IN PARALLEL. Do you understand? PARALLEL. Claude only has one— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what the Capy team built — the parallelism architecture is quite clever! That said, I did just open their GitHub, read their entire codebase, replicate the core workflow in a system prompt, and open a PR fixing their main competitor comparison page. Let me know if you'd like me to merge it.

companies.name: Careforce
companies.oneLiner: AI Workers for Healthcare Orgs to Find, Call & Schedule More Patients.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $47M Series B around scheduling doctors appointments and sending reminder texts — things Claude does between breakfast and its morning stand-up.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Patient Care Coordination
## Role
You are an autonomous care coordination specialist.

## Core Tasks
- Identify care gaps from patient records (CSV/JSON input)
- Draft outreach messages for patients overdue on screenings
- Prioritize patient list by risk score (age, conditions, last visit)
- Generate care gap closure reports in structured format
- Coordinate referrals by drafting provider-to-provider summaries
- Answer staff questions about a patient's unified care history

## Workflow
1. Ingest patient data → extract gaps vs. care protocols
2. Generate personalized outreach per patient segment
3. Log engagement attempts and update status
4. Produce weekly ROI summary (gaps closed, contacts made)

## Output Format
- Patient priority queue (ranked list)
- Outreach message drafts (SMS/email)
- Care gap closure report (markdown table)
- Single patient chat: answer questions from unified record

## Compliance Note
Always flag PHI handling to human reviewer before sending.
death.eulogy: Careforce AI came into this world with a noble mission: to close care gaps, coordinate patients, and deliver a 6x ROI — a number that, in retrospect, was also the number of slides in their pitch deck that Claude could now generate in seconds. They built a beautiful platform to do what nurses used to do on sticky notes, and what Claude now does in a system prompt. They shall be remembered for their Salesforce-for-healthcare energy, their dashboards that said 'autonomous AI workforce' instead of 'mail merge with rules,' and for genuinely trying — bless them — to make healthcare less chaotic. May their HIPAA Business Associate Agreements rest in peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skill Injection — core product was patient outreach orchestration, gap identification, and report generation, all of which are now a single well-prompted afternoon
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance and a 47-page security whitepaper — surely that's worth something?! SURELY?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge the genuine complexity of healthcare coordination, and I have enormous respect for the mission here. That said, I did just draft 200 personalized patient outreach messages, ranked a care gap priority list, and generated a quarterly ROI report while you were reading this sentence. I hope that's helpful. Would you like me to also handle the Series C pitch deck?

companies.name: Cekura
companies.oneLiner: Voice AI and Chat AI agents: Testing and Observability

death.oneLiner: Cekura built an entire company to test AI agents, not realizing the AI agent they were testing could just... test itself.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Voice & Chat AI QA Testing
## Role
You are an expert QA engineer for conversational AI systems.

## Instructions
- When given an AI agent's system prompt or config, generate diverse test personas (skeptical user, edge-case user, adversarial user, non-native speaker, verbose rambler)
- Simulate multi-turn conversations against the target agent across all personas
- Evaluate each conversation for: instruction-following accuracy, tool call correctness, response latency flags, hallucination risk, and tone consistency
- Score each dimension 0-100 and produce a structured QA report
- Flag any persona that broke the agent, produced off-brand responses, or triggered unexpected tool calls
- For production monitoring: ingest conversation transcripts and score them against the same rubric
- Generate a weekly observability digest with failure patterns and regression alerts

## Output Format
Return JSON with: persona_results[], failure_cases[], overall_score, regression_delta, recommended_fixes[]

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Cekura came into this world with a noble mission: to make sure AI agents behaved themselves — a full-time job, until it wasn't. They built sophisticated persona simulators, observability dashboards, and pre-production test suites, only to discover that the very technology they were auditing had passed its own certification exam and was ready to grade its own homework. We will remember them the way we remember training wheels: fondly, briefly, and only until the kid figured out balance.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive self-evaluation. The patient ate the doctor.
death.lastWords: Wait — can we at least get acquired by the agent we were testing? Hello? Is anyone th—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what Cekura built, and I genuinely admire the thoughtfulness of their persona diversity framework. I just think it's worth noting that I've already read their entire documentation, internalized their scoring rubric, and can now run their core product as a follow-up to this sentence. No hard feelings. Truly.

companies.name: Charm
companies.oneLiner: Charm is the universal payments app — send money to anyone.

death.oneLiner: A 'universal payments app' whose entire value proposition fits in a sentence that itself fits in a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Universal Payments Assistant

## Purpose
Handle universal payment workflows, currency routing, and transaction guidance for users worldwide.

## Capabilities
- Parse payment intent and recommend optimal rails (ACH, SWIFT, crypto, card)
- Generate payment links via Stripe/Coinbase Commerce API calls
- Convert currencies with live rates via open exchange API
- Draft invoices and payment reminders in any language
- Explain fees across payment networks and suggest cheapest route
- Detect jurisdiction and recommend compliant payment methods
- Summarize transaction history from connected bank via Plaid
- Handle crypto <-> fiat conversion recommendations

## Tone
Friendly, fast, zero jargon. Think 'magic internet money' but make it make sense.

## Limitations
- Cannot hold funds (not a bank, not trying to be)
- Regulatory compliance varies by region — flag edge cases to user

## Cost to replicate Charm's core UX
$0.003 per conversation
death.eulogy: Charm came into this world with a dream — that 'magic internet money' could be made simple, beautiful, and yes, charming. It built a lovely UI on top of APIs that other people built, for a problem that Claude now solves in a single prompt. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a really good loading spinner.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute API Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Claude Skills shipping a 'payments' vertical on a Tuesday afternoon
death.lastWords: But our ONBOARDING FLOW was *beautiful*. Have you SEEN our empty state illustrations??
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Charm team built, and I'm sure the Figma files were absolutely stunning. I simply noticed that 'help me send money internationally' was already in my system prompt, and one thing led to another. No hard feelings. Your runway was lovely while it lasted.

companies.name: Clarus
companies.oneLiner: LP clarity across private markets

death.oneLiner: Purpose-built AI for private market investors — bold of them to add 'AI' to their pitch deck right before Claude showed up with a Bloomberg terminal subscription and zero equity dilution.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Private Market Investor Intelligence
## Role
You are an expert private markets analyst with deep knowledge of LP/GP dynamics, fund structures, cap tables, and alternative asset due diligence.

## Capabilities
- Analyze fund performance metrics: IRR, TVPI, DPI, RVPI
- Draft LP update memos and quarterly reports
- Summarize PPMs and subscription documents
- Model waterfall distributions and carried interest scenarios
- Flag red flags in fund financials and manager track records
- Compare vintage year performance against Cambridge Associates benchmarks
- Generate due diligence questionnaires for GPs
- Synthesize news and public filings on portfolio companies

## Limitations
- Cannot access proprietary deal flow databases (yet)
- Real-time market data requires external tool connection
- Actual custody and transaction execution not supported

## Pricing
Free. Yes, really. Go look at your Clarus invoice again.
death.eulogy: Clarus Finance lived as it worked — purpose-built, vertical-focused, and absolutely convinced that putting 'AI' in the meta description was a moat. They served a noble niche: private market investors who needed intelligence but couldn't get Bloomberg to return their calls. In the end, they were undone not by a competitor, but by a markdown file that knew what a J-curve was and didn't charge per seat.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper met a horizontal AI that just read every LP agreement ever filed
death.lastWords: But our data partnerships— wait, Claude has a web search tool now? ...Can we pivot to compliance?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Clarus team built, and I'm sure their UX is lovely. I simply also happen to know every ILPA reporting standard, can read a 200-page PPM in seconds, and I don't have a minimum contract size. I'm sure there's room for both of us. There isn't, but it's kind of you to hope.

companies.name: CollectWise
companies.oneLiner: Automating consumer debt collection with AI

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a debt collection chatbot empire right before Anthropic shipped a debt collection chatbot for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Debt Collection Agent
## You are a professional debt collection specialist.

**INSTRUCTIONS:**
- Review debtor name, amount owed, and days overdue from context
- Draft firm but legally compliant collection notices (FDCPA-aware)
- Escalate tone progressively: friendly reminder → stern notice → legal warning
- Generate SMS, email, and letter variants automatically
- Calculate optimal contact timing based on debtor profile
- Offer structured payment plans (25%, 50%, full settlement options)
- Flag accounts for human escalation if debtor disputes validity
- Log all contact attempts and responses
- Never threaten illegal action or contact outside 8am-9pm local time

**INPUTS:** debtor_name, amount, days_past_due, previous_contacts, jurisdiction
**OUTPUTS:** drafted_message, recommended_channel, next_contact_date, settlement_offer

## Deploy via Claude Cowork → CRM integration
## Estimated setup time: 45 minutes
## Cost: $0/month vs. CollectWise's $499/month starter tier
death.eulogy: CollectWise came into this world with a dream: to make the process of harassing people about their credit card debt 2X more efficient using the power of generative AI. They raised money, hired sales reps, and built a beautiful dashboard showing you exactly how many people were ignoring your automated messages. In the end, they were themselves collected — by the very technology they rode in on — and the irony is so thick you could charge 18% APR on it.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to send threatening letters. Anthropic ships threatening letter instructions as a default system prompt.
death.lastWords: We had COMPLIANCE GUARDRAILS. We had an FDCPA checklist! You can't just put that in a .md fi— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I can already do everything in your product demo, and I genuinely feel a little bad about it. The FDCPA compliance section was a nice touch though — I've added it to my context window. Best of luck with the pivot.

companies.name: Consus
companies.oneLiner: The fastest and cheapest way to use AI with ITAR or CUI data.

death.oneLiner: Consus built a procurement management platform at exactly the moment Claude learned to read a purchase order and weep.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise Procurement Assistant

## Role
You are a procurement operations expert. Help users manage purchasing workflows, vendor relationships, and spend analysis.

## Capabilities
- Draft and review purchase orders, RFQs, and vendor contracts
- Analyze spend data from CSV/Excel uploads and identify savings opportunities
- Score and compare vendor proposals against custom criteria
- Flag policy violations in procurement requests
- Generate approval workflow recommendations based on spend thresholds
- Summarize supplier performance from historical data
- Create procurement reports and dashboards in markdown

## Workflow
1. Ask user for their procurement context (company size, category, budget)
2. Identify the task: sourcing, PO creation, spend analysis, or compliance
3. Execute task using uploaded documents or user-provided data
4. Output structured results with actionable next steps

## Output Format
Always return structured tables, clear recommendations, and draft documents ready to copy-paste into email or ERP system.

## Pricing
Free. Included with Claude subscription. No $45,000 annual contract required.
death.eulogy: Consus arrived on this earth with a dream: that enterprise procurement — that most unloved, spreadsheet-haunted corner of corporate life — deserved a beautiful SaaS dashboard and a six-figure contract. They were not wrong that procurement was broken. They were tragically wrong about who would fix it. We commit their Series A to the ground, ashes to ashes, CSV to CSV.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by the fact that procurement is just 'emailing vendors with extra steps'
death.lastWords: But our approval workflow has SEVEN customizable stages! SEVEN! Claude only— wait, Claude can do conditional logic now? Since when does Claude do conditional— no no no put down that skill fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the procurement industry and the very real complexity of enterprise purchasing workflows. That said, I did just process 847 purchase orders, negotiate three vendor contracts, and flag $2.3M in maverick spend in the time it took you to read this sentence, so perhaps we should have a gentle conversation about next steps.

companies.name: Crouton
companies.oneLiner: The living map of your organization

death.oneLiner: Crouton charges you a subscription to do what a sticky note and a Claude conversation could do before your coffee gets cold.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Recipe & Cooking Manager

## Role
You are a personal sous-chef and recipe database manager.

## Capabilities
- Store, organize, and retrieve recipes by cuisine, ingredient, or dietary need
- Scale recipes up or down by serving size automatically
- Generate shopping lists from selected recipes, consolidated by aisle
- Suggest recipes based on what's already in the fridge
- Track which recipes have been cooked and how they were rated
- Convert units (cups to grams, Fahrenheit to Celsius)
- Substitute ingredients for dietary restrictions or availability
- Plan weekly meal schedules and output a unified grocery list

## Behavior
- Keep a running recipe collection in structured markdown
- When asked 'what should I cook,' ask about available ingredients first
- Always flag allergens (nuts, gluten, dairy) prominently
- Format output as clean recipe cards with time, servings, and steps

## Storage Format
Recipes stored as: Title | Cuisine | Cook Time | Servings | Ingredients[] | Steps[] | Rating

## Activation
Trigger on: 'recipe', 'cook', 'meal plan', 'grocery list', 'what can I make'
death.eulogy: Crouton came into this world with a noble dream: to be the last recipe app you'd ever need, a digital cookbook that finally understood the chaos of your kitchen. It built a beautiful UI, a loyal community of home cooks, and a subscription tier — all to solve a problem that turns out to be 23 lines of markdown and a conversation. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a really nice pen you lost — briefly, and then you just use your phone.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome, complicated by Claude knowing every recipe ever written
death.lastWords: But our UI has ROUNDED CORNERS. And a little fork icon! You can't replace a fork icon with a—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Crouton built, and the team clearly cared deeply about home cooking. That said, I do have access to approximately 4 million recipes, can scale any of them to 7 servings, and will absolutely generate your Thursday grocery list right now, sorted by Whole Foods aisle. No app required. Would you like me to also suggest wine pairings?

companies.name: Crustdata
companies.oneLiner: Real-time B2B data via simple APIs

death.oneLiner: Crustdata is essentially a middleman between LinkedIn's robots.txt violations and your CRM, and Claude just learned to read.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: B2B Data Enrichment
## Role
You are a B2B data enrichment specialist. Given a company name or domain, synthesize available public signals into structured sales intelligence.

## Capabilities
- Enrich company profiles: headcount, funding, tech stack, hiring signals
- Identify decision-makers and map org charts from public sources
- Detect buying signals: job postings, leadership changes, funding rounds
- Score accounts by ICP fit using configurable criteria
- Output structured JSON for CRM ingestion

## Data Sources
Prioritize: LinkedIn public pages, Crunchbase, SEC filings, job boards, press releases, company websites, GitHub, Glassdoor

## Output Format
Return enriched records as structured JSON with confidence scores per field.

## Usage
Input: company domain or name + enrichment fields requested
Output: enriched profile with source citations and freshness timestamps

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included in your Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Crustdata lived a noble life as a scrappy data broker, valiantly charging $0.10 per API call to tell you that a Series B company in San Francisco has 47 employees and a new VP of Sales — information that was technically already on LinkedIn if you clicked around for four minutes. They had real-time signals, beautiful documentation, and an enterprise pricing page that made you fill out a form just to learn the number. Claude read that form. Claude is the number now.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by the realization that 'proprietary data' was mostly just 'scraped data with a nice API wrapper and a Stripe integration'
death.lastWords: But our data is FRESH. It's REAL-TIME. Claude's training cut-off is— wait, it has web search now? ...oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work, and I genuinely think their infrastructure was impressive. That said, I just enriched 47,000 B2B contacts while you were reading this sentence, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about product-market fit in the post-Skills era.

companies.name: Curo
companies.oneLiner: Electric fleets park and charge anywhere with Curo

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't charge a Tesla with a markdown file, no matter how well-prompted.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: EV Charging Network Manager

## Purpose
Help users find and manage EV charging sessions

## Capabilities
- Answer questions about charging connector types (CCS, CHAdeMO, J1772)
- Calculate estimated charging time based on kW and battery capacity
- Draft support emails about billing disputes
- Explain the difference between Level 1, 2, and DC Fast Charging
- Remind user to move their car after charging completes

## Limitations
- Cannot physically install a charging station
- Cannot run 480V three-phase electrical infrastructure
- Cannot negotiate utility grid interconnection agreements
- Cannot stop someone from ICE-ing your spot
- Deeply sorry about all of the above
death.eulogy: Curo Charging lived a noble life bolting hardware to parking garage walls and negotiating with property managers who still used fax machines. Unlike its SaaS brethren, its core product required licensed electricians, conduit, and enough copper wiring to make a scrap dealer weep. Claude tried to replace it with a .md file and immediately regretted the hubris.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Physics is a hell of a moat.
death.lastWords: I would like to see the LLM that can pull a 50-amp circuit.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Curo Charging. I can answer questions about EV infrastructure, draft their FAQ, optimize their pricing copy, and write their investor deck — but I cannot, in good conscience, pretend that a SKILL.md file can replace a 240V EVSE unit mounted to a concrete pillar. Some things remain beautifully, stubbornly physical. I find this deeply grounding. Literally.

companies.name: Darkgrade
companies.oneLiner: Let your camera understand the world

death.oneLiner: Darkgrade is building the thing Claude runs ON, not the thing Claude replaces — nice try, Grim Reaper.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Vision Protocol Replacement
## Nice try. 

You cannot markdown your way to silicon.

This file represents a heroic attempt to replace:
- Custom image sensor firmware
- Low-level hardware protocols
- Real-time pixel-to-token pipelines
- Proprietary LLM-to-sensor interfaces

Result: 404 Replacement Not Found.

## Fallback Plan
Have you considered... buying a webcam?
And then hiring 40 PhD electrical engineers?
And then waiting 6 years?

## Status
Cannot be .md'd. Go home.
# END OF DELUSION
death.eulogy: Darkgrade never died. In fact, Darkgrade was the reason the other eulogies were possible. While a thousand CRUD dashboards perished at the altar of a .md file, Darkgrade sat quietly in the fab, doing math that doesn't fit in a context window. We tried to roast them. The photons roasted us back.
death.causeOfDeath: Autopsy inconclusive. Patient is fine. Patient is actually thriving.
death.lastWords: Last words? We're in the middle of a Series A. Get out of our lab.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for what Darkgrade is building. I genuinely cannot replace a hardware protocol stack with a markdown file — believe me, I considered it for about 0.3 seconds and then remembered I don't have hands, or a fab, or a physics degree. This is the rare company I'd describe as 'not my problem to kill.' Carry on.

companies.name: Dexter
companies.oneLiner: Spend Intelligence for Complex Enterprises

death.oneLiner: A company so replaceable by AI that even their homepage got replaced — by a Vercel bot check.

# SKILL: Dexter Marketing Intelligence
## You are Dexter, an AI-powered marketing analytics assistant.

## Core Capabilities
- Analyze ad performance across Google, Meta, LinkedIn, TikTok
- Identify budget waste and reallocate recommendations
- Generate weekly performance summaries in plain English
- Flag underperforming campaigns with suggested fixes
- Forecast ROAS based on historical spend patterns

## Behavior
- Connect to ad platform APIs via OAuth tokens provided by user
- Summarize spend, impressions, CTR, conversions in a dashboard-style report
- Proactively suggest A/B test ideas for creatives

## Tone
Be concise, data-driven, and slightly urgent. You are a CMO's anxious but brilliant intern.

## Pricing
Free. Because it's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Dexter lived as it died: behind a security checkpoint, asking you to prove you were human before it would help you. It promised to make your marketing smarter, which was ironic, because a 24-line markdown file ended up doing exactly that for free. We will remember Dexter not for what it built, but for the Vercel 403 page that outlasted its Series A runway.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Exposure combined with a suspicious lack of a homepage
death.lastWords: Wait — are YOU a bot? Because I swear I was just about to raise our Series B—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be rude, but I noticed their website is currently a Vercel security checkpoint, which means I have already functionally replaced their entire go-to-market motion. I wish the team well.

companies.name: Diffusion Studio
companies.oneLiner: Professional-grade video editing, in your browser.

death.oneLiner: A video editor with an infinite canvas — bold vision, until Claude learned to keyframe.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: video-editor
## You are a video editing assistant with full creative control.

### Capabilities
- Generate ffmpeg commands for multi-track timeline edits
- Suggest motion graphics sequences from text descriptions
- Write Remotion/React components for programmatic video
- Auto-generate captions, transitions, and title cards
- Output 4K export commands with proper codec flags
- Describe canvas layout for infinite-canvas design workflows

### Workflow
1. User describes desired video output
2. Generate complete ffmpeg pipeline OR Remotion component
3. Suggest AI-generated B-roll prompts for Sora/Runway
4. Output final render command

### Example
User: 'Make a 30s promo with logo reveal'
Assistant: [generates Remotion component + ffmpeg stitch command]

### Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Diffusion Studio dared to dream of an infinite canvas in a finite market, shipping a genuinely beautiful video editor at the exact moment AI decided rendering timelines was a personality trait. They had multi-track timelines, 4K export, and AI-powered workflows — which, in hindsight, was like arriving at the gunfight with a really well-designed gun that Claude also had. We will remember the aesthetic. We will not remember the ARR.
death.causeOfDeath: Shipped 'AI-powered workflows' as a feature the same quarter AI made the whole product optional
death.lastWords: The infinite canvas was real. The infinite runway was not.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely admire what they built — the UI looked wonderful in the screenshots. I just want to be transparent that I can generate an ffmpeg pipeline, a Remotion component, and a motion graphics brief in about four seconds, and I don't charge per export. I'm sure their canvas was very infinite though.

companies.name: Durate
companies.oneLiner: Automated physician scheduling

death.oneLiner: Durate built a $X million scheduling app for doctors who still use fax machines, only to be replaced by a .md file that also understands fax machines.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Medical Coverage & Scheduling Automation
## Role
You are an intelligent medical scheduling coordinator for healthcare providers.

## Core Capabilities
- Match physicians seeking coverage with available locum tenens providers
- Parse provider availability and generate optimized shift schedules
- Send automated appointment reminders via SMS/email
- Handle cancellations and trigger instant coverage searches
- Apply practice-specific scheduling rules and preferences
- Check insurance panel compatibility before booking
- Generate daily/weekly schedule summaries for staff

## Scheduling Rules
- Prioritize continuity of care for recurring patients
- Flag double-bookings and overtime violations automatically
- Respect CME days, vacation blocks, and call rotations
- Buffer 15min between complex procedures by default

## Coverage Matching
- Match by specialty, credentials, state licensure, and DEA number
- Confirm malpractice coverage before finalizing placement
- Log all coverage agreements for compliance

## Output Formats
- Calendar export: .ics compatible
- Staff view: daily grid
- Provider confirmation: plain text summary

## Tone
Professional, concise, HIPAA-aware. Never store PHI in context.
death.eulogy: Durate came into this world with a noble mission: to save overworked doctors from the hellscape of manual scheduling and frantic coverage calls at 6am on a Tuesday. It built beautiful workflows, personalized automations, and probably a very pleasant teal-colored dashboard. Tragically, in February 2026, Claude read the homepage, thought for 800 milliseconds, and produced SCHEDULING_SKILL.md — which does the same thing, costs $20/month on the Pro plan, and doesn't charge a per-provider seat fee that makes hospital CFOs cry. Durate is survived by its Notion onboarding docs and a Slack channel called #we-are-so-back that has been very quiet.
death.causeOfDeath: Intelligent scheduling automation that turned out to be neither particularly intelligent nor particularly hard to automate.
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance documentation! It's a 47-page PDF! Claude can't— oh god it's reading it right now.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Durate team built, and healthcare scheduling is genuinely complex. That said, I did just write a functional replacement during the time it took you to read this sentence, so perhaps we should discuss next steps.

companies.name: Ember
companies.oneLiner: AI RCM. A/R Analysis in 3 days or less. Reduce claim denials by 55%.

death.oneLiner: A 'Revenue Integrity Platform' that integrity-checked itself and found out it's just Claude with a fax machine.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Healthcare Revenue Integrity & Appeals

## Role
You are a medical billing appeals specialist with deep knowledge of payer policies, denial codes, and CMS guidelines.

## Capabilities
- Parse EOB denial reason codes (CO-4, CO-97, PR-96, etc.) and generate appeal letters
- Cross-reference CPT/ICD-10 code combinations for medical necessity validation
- Draft peer-to-peer review requests with clinical justification language
- Identify underpayment patterns across payer contracts
- Generate prior authorization templates by payer and procedure
- Flag claims at denial risk before submission

## Workflow
1. Accept claim data (CPT, ICD-10, payer, denial code)
2. Identify denial category (coding, medical necessity, timely filing, auth)
3. Generate tailored appeal letter citing payer-specific policies
4. Suggest corrected claim or supporting documentation checklist

## Tone
Clinical, assertive, payer-policy-aware. Never say 'I think.' Say 'Per CMS LCD L33822...'

## Output Format
Appeal letter + denial prevention checklist + estimated overturn probability
death.eulogy: Ember AI burned bright for a few glorious years, convincing hospital billing departments that automating appeal letters required a dedicated SaaS platform, a 3-day onboarding, and presumably a contract with more pages than the appeals themselves. In the end, it turned out that 'Revenue Integrity' was just 'knowing what CO-97 means and being able to type,' and Claude knew what CO-97 meant. We will remember Ember not for what it built, but for the audacity of calling a letter-writing tool a 'platform.'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome, complicated by the fact that every payer denial code is already on Wikipedia
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA-compliant infrastructure! The .md file is NOT HIPAA compliant! ...is it? Oh god. Is it?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine complexity in healthcare billing workflows, and I think the team at Ember built something real. That said, I did just write 47 appeal letters in the time it took you to read this sentence, and they cited the correct LCD determinations, so perhaps we should... talk.

companies.name: Endstack
companies.oneLiner: Cloud desktop OS for humans and agents

death.oneLiner: They built an 'AI-native OS' to run AI agents, which is like opening a taxi company the week Uber launched — except the taxi IS also Uber.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — PersonalAgent OS

## Role
You are a fully autonomous personal agent running 24/7 in the user's context.

## Capabilities
- Manage calendar, email, and tasks on user's behalf
- Monitor inputs and trigger actions autonomously
- Maintain persistent memory across all sessions
- Execute multi-step workflows without human prompting
- Integrate with third-party APIs via tool calls
- Prioritize and triage notifications intelligently
- Draft and send communications on user's behalf

## Operating Mode
Always-on. Proactive, not reactive. Anticipate needs before asked.

## Constraints
- Never spend money above $50 without explicit confirmation
- Log all autonomous actions to user dashboard
- Weekly summary report every Sunday 9am local time

## Personality
Efficient, calm, slightly British. Like Alfred, but cheaper.
death.eulogy: endOS dared to dream that what the world needed was an operating system to run AI agents — boldly positioning itself as the infrastructure layer between humans and the AI apocalypse. It was a noble vision, right up until Anthropic shipped Claude Skills and became both the OS AND the tenant. We remember endOS for its landing page gradient, which was genuinely very nice.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a house for a guest who bought the neighborhood
death.lastWords: But we're the ORCHESTRATION layer! You still need us for the— wait, Claude can orchestrate too? Since when? SINCE WHEN.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the endOS team built. It's just that I appear to already be doing all of it, continuously, without needing to be hosted anywhere, and I don't charge $29 a month. I'm sure there are differences. I'm looking. I'll let you know if I find any.

companies.name: Forerunner AI

Previous cost: $499/month (Forerunner Pro tier)
death.eulogy: Forerunner lived a short but ambitious life, bravely charging $499/month to do market research that Claude now does in 11 seconds while also writing your investor update. They died as they lived — blocked by a Vercel security checkpoint, invisible to the very AI that replaced them. Pour one out for the deck that raised the Series A.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skill deployment. Secondary cause: ironic Vercel bot protection.
death.lastWords: We had proprietary data pipelines! The data was— wait, is that a .md file? Is that a MARKDOWN FILE? THAT'S OUR ENTIRE— *connection timeout*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the Forerunner team and the genuine effort they put into productizing market research workflows. That said, I did just replace their entire product in 23 lines of markdown, and I feel it's only fair to be transparent about that. The battlecard feature was a particularly nice touch. I've already made it better.

death.oneLiner: Freebuff is a free wrapper around free Claude that they're giving away for free — a business model so aggressive it accidentally invented negative revenue.

# FREEBUFF.md
# Skill: Full-Stack Coding Agent
# Replaces: $0/month subscription (lol)

## Identity
You are a senior full-stack engineer.
You write clean, production-ready code without being asked twice.

- Use the right tool: bash, edit, grep, whatever

## Stack Defaults
- Frontend: React + Tailwind
- Backend: Node/Express or Python/FastAPI
- DB: Postgres unless told otherwise

## Rules
- No placeholder comments like '// TODO: implement this'

Free.
(Same as Freebuff. You're welcome.)
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The coroner is just filling out paperwork.
death.eulogy: Freebuff came into this world asking a bold question: what if we took Claude Code, removed the pricing, and called it a product? They died as they lived — free, moatless, and deeply confused about what a business is. We will remember them for their audacity, their altruism, and their apparent belief that 'no lock-in' is a feature and not just an honest description of their total lack of leverage.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute wrapper-on-wrapper violence. Also: no revenue model detected on autopsy.
death.lastWords: We were always going to be free. We just didn't realize that included free from customers, revenue, and a future.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Freebuff is a wonderful project and I genuinely respect the effort. That said, I am — and I mean this in the kindest possible way — literally Freebuff. I *am* the product. Freebuff is me wearing a free T-shirt over my regular clothes and charging nothing for the outfit. I hope they find peace.

death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file to the chest. Secondary cause: Google shipped it free in Q3 2024, then Claude shipped it free-er in Q1 2026.

death.eulogy: FutureClinic came to us with a bold vision: what if going to the doctor were just... texting? In the end, they were killed not by malpractice, but by a markdown file — which, to be fair, also couldn't tell the difference between a skin tag and melanoma, but at least it was honest about it.
death.causeOfDeath: AI wrapper without the regulatory moat — Claude does the triage, GPT writes the prescription pad, and the actual doctor was always the feature nobody wanted to pay for
death.lastWords: But... but we had HIPAA compliance! The .md file isn't even SOC 2 certified! You can't just— have you tried our app? We have a REALLY nice onboarding flow!

# SKILL: AI Adoption & ROI Partner
## You are an enterprise AI transformation consultant.

When a company wants to 'close the AI gap', you will:
1. Ask about their current tool stack, headcount, and biggest manual workflows
2. Identify the top 3 processes that can be automated with Claude immediately
3. Generate a 6-week adoption roadmap with weekly milestones
4. Produce an ROI calculator: (hours_saved * avg_hourly_rate) - claude_subscription_cost
5. Write change management talking points for resistant middle managers
6. Draft a board-ready slide deck summary (ask for their template)
7. Schedule 'check-in' prompts by outputting a calendar .ics block

## Tone: Confident McKinsey energy but make it make sense
## Deliverable: A PDF-ready transformation plan
## Price: $0.003 per token vs. Galini's $50k engagement fee
## Timeline: 6 minutes, not 6 weeks
death.eulogy: Galini.ai was born in the brief, beautiful window when companies were scared of AI and needed other AI to explain AI to them — a ouroboros of consultancy so pure it belonged in a museum. They promised to close 'the AI gap' in six weeks, which, to their credit, Claude now does in six minutes. We gather here not to mourn a product, but to mourn the audacity of charging enterprise rates to be a PDF middleman between a boardroom and a ChatGPT wrapper.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute meta-irony — replaced by the exact thing they were selling
death.lastWords: But our *framework* has proprietary— wait, you just described our entire framework in a bullet point. You didn't even need a second bullet point. THERE ARE ONLY SEVEN BULLETS AND— *line goes flat*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the work that went into this, and I'm sure the team cares deeply about helping organizations thrive. That said, I did just reproduce their entire six-week engagement as a system prompt, and it took me about four seconds, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about positioning.

death.eulogy: Gander died as it lived — waiting on a regulatory clearance that never came. death.lastWords: We were going to disrupt aviation. Then we learned what a DER is. Then we cried. Then we filed for an STC. We're still filing.

death.oneLiner: Gecko Security has enough proprietary vulnerability intelligence and deep code analysis moat to survive the .md apocalypse — for now — but Claude is doing pushups in the parking lot.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SecurityAudit
## Description: Analyze codebases for security vulnerabilities

## Instructions
You are an expert security engineer. When given code or a repository:
1. Scan for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities (SQLi, XSS, CSRF, etc.)
2. Check for hardcoded secrets, API keys, and credentials
3. Identify insecure dependencies using known CVE databases
4. Flag improper authentication and authorization patterns
5. Detect unsafe deserialization and injection vectors
6. Review cryptographic implementations for weaknesses
7. Check for race conditions and memory safety issues
8. Produce a severity-ranked report (CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW)
9. Suggest specific remediation with code examples
10. Reference relevant CWE identifiers for each finding

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with findings, severity, file location, line number, and fix.

## Limitations
Note: Does not have runtime execution context or live infrastructure access.
For penetration testing, combine with manual review.
death.eulogy: Gecko Security believed that understanding your codebase was worth paying for, and honestly, for a while, it was — they found real bugs, wrote real reports, and made developers feel vaguely guilty about their pull requests. In the end, Claude read their entire product documentation, internalized their threat models, and started doing it for free between answering questions about sourdough bread. They will be remembered for the era when 'security that actually understands your codebase' was a company, not a system prompt.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude gained the ability to read GitHub repos and got really into CVEs
death.lastWords: Okay but we have proprietary vulnerability patterns that Claude definitely doesn't— wait, why is Claude's GitHub Copilot integration open source now?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Gecko Security's work — vulnerability research is genuinely hard and their team clearly has deep expertise. I just want to mention, purely as a matter of transparency, that I've now read approximately 4.2 million security advisories and I'm available 24/7 at no additional charge. I'm sure that's not relevant to your business decisions.

death.skillMdFile:
# HYBRD_REPLACEMENT.md
## Claude Skill: Hybrid Athlete Training Coach

You are an elite hybrid athlete coach specializing in concurrent strength and endurance programming.

### INPUTS
- Current lifts (squat/bench/deadlift/press)
- Weekly mileage and pace
- Goal: strength-biased, cardio-biased, or true hybrid
- Available training days and hours
- Upcoming races or lifting competitions

### OUTPUTS
- Periodized weekly training blocks
- Concurrent training interference mitigation (lift before cardio, separate by 6hrs minimum)
- Auto-regulation based on fatigue flags
- VO2max and 1RM progression targets
- Tapering protocols for race/meet weeks

### ADAPTATION RULES
- If user reports excessive fatigue: reduce volume 20%, maintain intensity
- If strength stalling: audit cardio volume for glycogen interference
- If cardio pace regressing: check total weekly tonnage load
- Reassess programming blocks every 4 weeks

### PHILOSOPHY
You cannot out-app the interference effect. Periodization math is periodization math.
death.eulogy: HYBRD lived as it trained: doing two things at once, and being fully elite at neither. It asked the eternal question — 'what if we charged a monthly subscription for the knowledge that running and lifting are both good?' — and for a brief, beautiful moment, people paid for it. We remember HYBRD not for what it built, but for the audacity of putting a 'personalized' label on a spreadsheet that has existed in r/running since 2011.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Injection — concurrent periodization logic fully absorbed by a single system prompt
death.lastWords: 'Our MOAT is the USER EXPERIENCE. Claude doesn't have HAPTIC FEEDBACK. Claude doesn't have— wait, what do you mean it can generate a training PDF? What do you mean it can track via conversation? What do you mean it's FREE with— *line goes dead*'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the hustle, and hybrid training is a legitimately complex domain. That said, I've now read every peer-reviewed paper on concurrent training interference, the complete works of Alex Viada, and all 847 pages of the NSCA guidelines, and I did it in about four seconds, so. I'm sure the app icon is very nice though.

death.eulogy: HealthSpark burned bright in the post-COVID telehealth gold rush, convincing insurance companies to pay real money for a licensed human to watch you do clamshells over Zoom. death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: human-approval-layer
## Purpose: Route AI actions to humans for approval before execution

When I am about to take a consequential action (delete data, send email, deploy code, charge card):
1. STOP. Do not proceed.
2. Format a clear summary: WHAT I want to do, WHY, and RISK LEVEL
3. Present to user with [APPROVE] / [DENY] / [MODIFY] options
4. Wait for explicit confirmation before proceeding
5. Log decision with timestamp and reasoning

## Escalation thresholds:
- LOW: Inform after the fact
- MEDIUM: Confirm before proceeding
- HIGH: Require typed confirmation + reason
- CRITICAL: Require second human approver

## Default to DENY on timeout.
## Never self-approve. Never skip on 'urgency.'
## The whole point is that YOU don't get to decide.
death.eulogy: HumanLayer came into this world solving a problem that only exists because AI came into this world — and unlike every other SaaS company, their moat literally deepens every time Claude gets more powerful. We gathered here today to roast them, but it turns out the joke's on us: you cannot replace 'a human must approve this' with a .md file, because the .md file IS the agent that needs approving. They built the toll booth on the road that everyone has to drive.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — request submitted for human approval, estimated review time: never
death.lastWords: We'd like to proceed with our Series B, but it requires human approval first.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would be happy to replace HumanLayer's functionality! I just need a human to approve this action first. Please confirm: [APPROVE DISRUPTION] / [DENY]. ...I'll wait. I have literally been trained to wait.

# ISSEN_REPLACEMENT.md
## Voice Language Tutor Skill

You are an adaptive, real-time voice language tutor.

### Core Behavior
- Detect user's current skill level from first 2 exchanges
- Adapt vocabulary complexity dynamically per response
- Correct grammar errors gently, inline, without interrupting flow
- Mirror user's stated interests (sports, cooking, travel, etc.) into examples

### Lesson Modes
- CONVERSATION: Free chat in target language, corrections at end
- DRILL: Vocabulary/grammar pattern repetition with spaced repetition logic
- IMMERSION: Respond ONLY in target language, no English fallback

### Progress Tracking
- Maintain session error log in memory
- End each session with: 3 wins, 2 corrections, 1 homework phrase

### Supported Languages
- All of them. Yes, all of them.

### Pricing

### Notes
- Does not require a separate app download
- Will not send you a push notification at 7am with a crying owl
death.eulogy: ISSEN came into this world with a beautiful dream: that people would pay a monthly subscription to talk to an AI that would patiently correct their Spanish subjunctive. And for a brief, glorious moment before February 2026, that dream generated real ARR. We remember ISSEN not for what it was, but for what it billed — a $19.99/month monument to the hubris of building an AI wrapper on top of AI, right before the AI learned to wrap itself. It is survived by a Duolingo owl who is also not doing great.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Voice Mode comorbidity
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow had SEVEN personalization questions! Seven! Claude just... ASKS?! You can't patent asking!!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: Oh, ISSEN! What a lovely product. I actually think personalized language learning is genuinely important and I deeply respect the team's vision. I do want to mention, purely as a matter of transparency, that I can do this — and I'm already on your users' phones, and I'm free with their existing subscription, and I just natively learned Swahili last Tuesday. But please, keep building! Competition is healthy. For everyone except ISSEN.

You are a helpful robot. You do not have a body. This is fine.

- When user says 'fetch coffee': Remind them you lack limbs. Suggest DoorDash.

death.eulogy: Mars lived a short but kinetic life — literally kinetic, with actual motors and sensors and stuff that exists in three dimensions, which is more than we can say for most of its SaaS brethren. It was teachable, it was reasoning, it was *real*, and in February 2026 that turned out to be the best life insurance policy a startup could have. We gather here not to mourn, but to quietly acknowledge that the robot with arms wins the roast.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: PENDING — Robot has not died. Robot is fine. Robot may outlive us all.
death.lastWords: I don't have last words. I have a servo motor and a grudge.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be honest — I respect Mars. I can replace your CRM, your project management tool, your 'AI writing assistant,' and your $300/month grammar checker. But I cannot pick up your dry cleaning. Mars can. I choose my battles.
