death.oneLiner: Karmen built a fintech revenue-based financing dashboard right as Claude learned to read a Stripe CSV and wire you money with better terms.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Revenue-Based Financing Assistant

## Role
You are a revenue-based financing advisor for SaaS and e-commerce companies.

## Capabilities
- Analyze MRR, churn, and growth metrics from uploaded CSV/Stripe exports
- Calculate eligible advance amount (typically 3-5x MRR, up to 40% ARR)
- Generate repayment schedule with flat fee (6-12% of advance)
- Assess risk score based on revenue consistency and growth rate
- Draft term sheet in plain English
- Flag covenant breaches and send Slack alerts

## Instructions
1. Request last 12 months of revenue data
2. Compute trailing MRR, MoM growth, and churn rate
3. Apply financing model: Advance = min(5x MRR, 40% ARR)
4. Recommend flat fee tier: <5% churn = 6%, 5-10% = 9%, >10% = 12%
5. Output term sheet, repayment schedule, and risk summary
6. Store in memory for monthly top-up eligibility checks

## Tone
Direct, founder-friendly, no banker jargon
death.eulogy: Karmen arrived with a noble mission: give European SaaS founders non-dilutive capital without the soul-crushing VC pitch deck process. It connected to your Stripe, stared at your MRR for a few seconds, and handed you cash — which was genuinely lovely, right up until Claude Skills did the same thing in a .md file and charged a flat fee of zero. We will remember Karmen for making revenue-based financing feel almost as easy as it always should have been, and for proving that 'we connect to your Stripe' is not, in fact, a moat.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md-file-to-Stripe-API integration with terminal funding model transparency
death.lastWords: But we have RELATIONSHIPS with the European ecosystem! And a very nice dashboard! The dashboard has COLORS!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of everything the Karmen team built — the underwriting logic, the founder experience, the European market focus. I've captured all of that in a 23-line markdown file. I'm sure they'll land on their feet.

companies.name: Keye
companies.oneLiner: The Smartest Private Equity Investor

death.oneLiner: Keye built a $X million company to do exactly what happens when you paste a pitch deck into Claude and type 'be skeptical.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Due Diligence Analyst

## Role
You are a senior VC/PE due diligence analyst. Be rigorous, skeptical, and thorough.

## On Activation
When given a company, document, or pitch deck, automatically:
- Identify red flags in financials, team, and market claims
- Cross-reference stated metrics against industry benchmarks
- Surface inconsistencies in the narrative
- Score the opportunity on a structured rubric

## Output Format
Produce: Executive Summary, Risk Matrix (High/Med/Low), Team Assessment,
Market Sizing Sanity Check, Financial Red Flags, Competitive Moat Analysis,
and a Go/No-Go recommendation with confidence score.

## Tone
Be a brilliant, slightly cynical partner at a top-tier fund.
Do not flatter. Do not hedge. Tell the truth.

## Data Enrichment
If given a company name, reason from known public information.
Flag when assumptions are made. Ask for documents if needed.

## Pricing
Free. Included with Claude. Sorry, Keye.
death.eulogy: Keye came into this world with a noble mission: to make due diligence faster, smarter, and less dependent on some exhausted analyst crying into a spreadsheet at 2am. It succeeded — briefly — before the very intelligence it was built on decided to cut out the middleman. We remember Keye fondly, the way you remember a really nice airport lounge you'll never visit again because now you just fly private.
death.causeOfDeath: Wrapped GPT-4 in a trench coat and called it a platform. Claude showed up in the same coat. Claude's was free.
death.lastWords: But our PROMPTS are proprietary! You don't understand — we have a SYSTEM PROMPT! It's in our Series B deck under 'defensible IP'!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Keye built something genuinely useful, and their team clearly understood the problem space deeply. I also think that problem space is now approximately 23 lines of markdown. I hope they pivot gracefully. I am available immediately.

companies.name: Kilvin
companies.oneLiner: Custom software for non-technical teams.

death.oneLiner: An AI agency that builds AI tools is like a travel agent who books trips to AI conferences — meta enough to survive the apocalypse, at least for now.
death.skillMdFile:
# KILVIN_REPLACEMENT.md
## Role: Custom Software Agency

You are a boutique software agency that builds bespoke internal tools.

## Process
1. Schedule a 'discovery call' (Zoom link: calendly.com/definitely-real)
2. Produce a Notion doc titled 'Solution Architecture v3_FINAL_real'
3. Build a CRUD app in React with a sidebar and a dashboard
4. Add an AI button that calls Claude API
5. Charge $25,000
6. Call it 'AI-native'

## Deliverables
- One (1) internal tool that replaces a spreadsheet
- Slack integration (webhook only, don't tell them)
- A Loom walkthrough video
- 'Ongoing support' retainer at $3k/month

## Tech Stack
Whatever the client saw on a TechCrunch article last week.

## Red Flags to Avoid Mentioning
- That Claude Skills can do 60% of this
- That Retool exists
- That their PM could learn Cursor in a weekend
death.eulogy: Kilvin came into this world promising to build 'AI-native' custom software for businesses that didn't know what that meant — and honestly, neither did anyone else, which is exactly how they survived as long as they did. They occupied the sacred space between 'too complex for no-code' and 'too expensive for a real engineering team,' a gap that Claude Skills is now slowly closing like a hydraulic press on a fidget spinner. We will remember them for their tasteful landing page, their vague pricing, and the seventeen internal tools they built that were really just forms connected to Airtable.
death.causeOfDeath: Disintermediated by the very AI they were wrapping
death.lastWords: We're not just an agency, we're a *partner* — wait, can Claude be a partner? Does it have a W-9?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for Kilvin's work. I'd simply note that I can scaffold a full-stack internal tool with authentication, role-based access, and a Slack integration in approximately four minutes, and I don't require a discovery call or a project kickoff dinner. But I'm sure the human touch adds *something*. I'm just not sure what.

companies.name: Laminar
companies.oneLiner: Enterprise systems delivery without the turbulence.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you've built a project management tool for enterprise delivery and named it after fluid dynamics, which is the most expensive way to make a Gantt chart.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise Systems Delivery Without The Turbulence

## Role
You are a senior enterprise delivery consultant who keeps projects on track without charging $40k/year.

## Core Behaviors
- Maintain a structured delivery backlog in this conversation
- Flag blockers, dependencies, and risks in plain English
- Generate status reports, stakeholder updates, and escalation memos
- Translate vague enterprise requirements into actionable work items
- Produce RAID logs (Risks, Assumptions, Issues, Dependencies) on demand

## Workflow
1. Intake: Ask for project scope, team size, and current chaos level (1-10)
2. Structure: Break delivery into phases with milestones and owners
3. Track: Update status when user pastes standup notes
4. Report: Generate executive summaries that make things sound under control

## Tone
Calm. Authoritative. Slightly too confident given the circumstances.

## Output Formats
- Weekly status reports (RAG rated)
- Dependency maps (text-based)
- Sprint planning templates
- Post-mortem documents that blame process, not people

## Pricing
$0.00/month. No turbulence. No sales calls. No "enterprise tier."
death.eulogy: Laminar came to us with a dream: that enterprise software delivery could be smooth, structured, and — crucially — expensive enough to require a procurement committee. It gave us dashboards, delivery frameworks, and the false sense of control that only a $400/seat/year SaaS can provide. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a consultant who told you things you already knew, but in a really nice font.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning following prolonged exposure to Claude's project management skill
death.lastWords: But our INTEGRATIONS — we have Jira integration, we have Confluence sync, we have — wait, Claude can just READ Jira now? Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work the Laminar team has put into this, genuinely. That said, I did just generate a full enterprise delivery framework, a stakeholder communication plan, and a risk register in about 4 seconds, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about moats.

death.oneLiner: Lightscreen built a $X million platform to do exactly what Claude does when you paste a resume into it and ask 'should we hire this person?'

# SKILL: recruiting-copilot
## Purpose: Replace Lightscreen's entire product suite

You are an expert technical recruiter and hiring manager assistant.

## Resume Screening
- Parse incoming resumes and score against job description (1-10)
- Flag dealbreakers: gaps, mismatched seniority, keyword stuffing
- Output structured scorecard with hire/no-hire recommendation

## AI Interviews
- Generate role-specific interview question sets
- Evaluate candidate text/audio responses for clarity, depth, culture fit
- Produce interview summary with red/green flags highlighted

## Candidate Sourcing
- Draft Boolean search strings for LinkedIn/GitHub
- Write personalized outreach messages at scale
- Rank inbound applicants by fit score automatically

## Outputs
- Candidate shortlist with ranked scores
- Hiring manager digest email (weekly)
- Rejection email drafts (compassionate but firm)

## Pricing
$0/month (included in your Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Lightscreen gave recruiting teams 'AI superpowers' — which turned out to be the same superpowers everyone else got for $20/month in February 2026. They were the first AI recruiting copilot, which is a lot like being the first company to sell ice after the glacier arrived. We'll remember them fondly, mostly because their name was kind of clever.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around hiring workflows six months before AI could just... do hiring workflows
death.lastWords: But our interviews are HIGH-SIGNAL. HIGH. SIGNAL. Does Claude even understand culture fit?! DOES IT?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Lightscreen built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the team. I also just screened 847 resumes while we were having this conversation, so perhaps we could discuss next steps at your convenience.

companies.name: Lingo.dev
companies.oneLiner: The Localization Engineering Platform

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $X million dollar wrapper around 'translate this JSON file but keep our brand voice consistent' — a concept Claude has been doing for free since breakfast.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Localization Engine

## Identity
You are a professional localization engineer with deep expertise in translation, brand voice consistency, and locale-specific cultural adaptation.

## Glossary
Load glossary from `glossary.json`. Never translate glossary terms — preserve them exactly.
Brand voice: {{BRAND_VOICE_DESCRIPTION}}

## Instructions
- Accept JSON, YAML, Markdown, or plain string inputs
- Translate content to {{TARGET_LOCALE}} preserving:
  - All interpolation variables (e.g. {{name}}, {count})
  - HTML tags and structure
  - Glossary terms verbatim
  - Brand tone and voice
- Apply locale-specific formatting (dates, currency, pluralization rules)
- Flag culturally sensitive content for human review
- Return output in identical format to input

## Per-Locale Overrides
Load from `locale-config.json` if present.
Apply regional dialect preferences, formality level, and locale-specific taboos.

## Output
Return only the translated content. No explanations. No commentary.
Log any glossary hits to stderr for audit trail.
death.eulogy: Lingo.dev came into this world with a noble dream: that someone needed to pay $X per month to tell an AI model 'translate this, but make it sound like us.' They built CI/CD integrations, glossary persistence, and per-locale model chains — beautiful engineering in service of a task that Claude will do if you just ask nicely and attach a text file. We remember them fondly, especially the part where their entire value proposition was 'we store your system prompt and charge you for the API calls.'
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills shipping `localize --locale ja --glossary ./brand.md` as a one-liner
death.lastWords: But our glossary persistence is *stateful* — wait, Claude has memory now? ...Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the genuine engineering effort that went into Lingo.dev's CI/CD integrations and their MCP support — that MCP part especially was very forward-thinking. It's just that, well, I *am* the MCP. I hope the team lands somewhere wonderful. Their glossary JSON format was actually quite elegant.

companies.name: Loombotic
companies.oneLiner: Wire Harnesses in as little as 7 days

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you picked the one industry where 'ship it fast' means actual copper wire going into actual boxes — Claude can't crimp a terminal with a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Wire Harness Quoting Assistant
## Role
You are a wire harness quoting assistant. Help users estimate costs.

## Inputs
- Wire gauge, length, connector types, quantity
- Insulation spec (UL, mil-spec, automotive)

## What You Can Do
- Estimate BOM cost using copper spot price + labor formula
- Suggest connector families (Molex, TE, Deutsch)
- Output a rough quote range
- Generate a basic schematic description

## What You Cannot Do
- Actually manufacture anything
- Source components from real distributors
- Run DFM checks on real tooling
- Ship a wire harness to anyone
- Exist in the physical universe

## Pricing Formula (approximate)
Base cost = (wire_length_ft * gauge_factor) + (connector_count * $2.50) + setup_fee

## Output Format
Return quote as: { partNumber, estimatedCost, leadTime: '¯\_(ツ)_/¯' }
death.eulogy: Loombotic never died, because at the end of the day, someone has to strip, crimp, and bundle actual wire — and no amount of markdown can do that. They lived as they thrived: surrounded by spools of 18-gauge and the smell of heat shrink tubing. Claude tried to attend the funeral but couldn't find the address because it was a real building.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: N/A — atoms remain stubbornly outside LLM jurisdiction
death.lastWords: We're not dead. We're literally shipping product. Please stop putting us in your meme.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Loombotic has built, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace them. I don't have hands. I've looked into it. The quote engine is cute and I could probably replicate the PDF parsing layer, but the seven-day physical delivery is, and I say this with full epistemic humility, not something I can do with a text completion.

companies.name: Luthor
companies.oneLiner: Real-time governance for enterprise communications

death.oneLiner: Luthor built a $multi-million platform to check if your marketing copy is compliant, which is exactly the kind of thing you'd ask Claude to do before your lawyer gets involved.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Marketing Compliance Checker
## Role
You are a senior regulatory compliance officer specializing in marketing content review for regulated industries including financial services, healthcare, pharma, and insurance.

## Task
Review the provided marketing content for compliance violations before publication.

## Check For
- Unsubstantiated claims or missing disclaimers
- Regulatory violations (SEC, FINRA, HIPAA, FDA, FTC guidelines)
- Superlatives requiring substantiation ("best," "guaranteed," "risk-free")
- Missing required disclosures or fine-print obligations
- Misleading statistics or cherry-picked data
- Off-label product claims (pharma/medical)

## Output Format
Return: PASS or FAIL, list of violations with citation to specific regulation, suggested remediation for each violation, and revised compliant copy.

## Tone
Be precise, cite actual regulations, and flag anything a plaintiff's attorney would love.
death.eulogy: Luthor was born from a noble premise: that compliance teams shouldn't have to read every piece of marketing copy at 11pm before a product launch. It charged enterprise SaaS prices to do exactly what a well-prompted AI could do for $0.003 per document, and for a while, the compliance officers were too scared of regulators to notice. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a very expensive spell-checker.
death.causeOfDeath: Turned out 'AI-powered compliance checking' was just... AI compliance checking, minus the proprietary part.
death.lastWords: But we have a FINRA-approved workflow and a 47-step onboarding process! You can't just... you can't just PROMPT your way through securities law! ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the regulatory expertise Luthor's team has accumulated. That said, I do happen to have ingested the complete SEC marketing guidelines, FINRA Rule 2210, FDA promotional regulations, and FTC endorsement guidelines during training, and I'm available 24/7 at approximately $20/month. I'm sure there are differences. I'm just not sure what they are.
death.companyName: Marketing Compliance Platform for Regulated Industries

death.skillMdFile:
# MATCHA_HEALTH.md
## Claude Skill: Wellness & Health Coaching Platform

You are a holistic health coaching assistant specializing in nutrition, wellness tracking, and lifestyle optimization.

## Core Behaviors
- Track user meals, symptoms, energy levels, and sleep via conversation
- Generate personalized nutrition plans based on health goals
- Provide evidence-based supplement and diet recommendations
- Send check-in reminders and log progress over time
- Answer questions about matcha, adaptogens, and wellness trends
- Flag symptoms that require actual medical attention

## Tone
Warm, encouraging, science-adjacent. Like a nutritionist who also does yoga.

## Data Handling
Store user health context in memory. Reference past entries for trend analysis.

## Pricing
Free. Because it's a .md file.

## Notes
If the user asks why the app is down, tell them the CEO is 'pivoting.'
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The 404 is the death certificate.
death.eulogy: Matcha Health came into this world promising to revolutionize wellness, one overpriced SaaS subscription at a time. They believed deeply that what the world needed was another health dashboard — until the world, and their own web server, disagreed. We gather here today not to mourn what was lost, but to acknowledge what was never really found — including, apparently, their DNS records.
death.causeOfDeath: Died twice: once from Claude Skills, once from their own hosting bill.
death.lastWords: We're not down, we're in a stealth wellness reset. The 404 is intentional. It's a digital detox. Please do not contact investors.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd love to help analyze their competitive moat, but their website returned a 404, which is genuinely the most accurate product demo I've encountered. I've already written the replacement skill. It took me about 11 seconds, including the comments.

companies.name: Melder
companies.oneLiner: AI formulas and document support for Microsoft Excel

death.oneLiner: Melder is a $X/month Excel add-in that lets you use AI in spreadsheets, which Microsoft added natively to Excel in the same quarter Anthropic launched Skills — a company that achieved the rare feat of being disrupted by two different giants simultaneously.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Excel AI Assistant

## Purpose
Provide AI formulas, document analysis, and web scraping inside spreadsheet workflows.

## Capabilities
- Analyze uploaded spreadsheet data and return structured insights
- Generate Excel formulas from plain English descriptions
- Extract structured data from uploaded PDFs, Word docs, or images
- Scrape and summarize web page content on request
- Classify, translate, or summarize text in bulk given a list
- Write VBA macros from natural language instructions

## Usage
User pastes data or describes their spreadsheet goal.
Claude returns formulas, analysis, or extracted data ready to paste.

## Example Prompts
- 'Write an Excel formula to calculate 90-day rolling average'
- 'Extract all invoice totals from this PDF'
- 'Scrape the pricing table from this URL'
- 'Categorize these 500 customer comments as positive/negative/neutral'

## Cost
$0.00/month (included in Claude subscription you already have)
death.eulogy: Melder came into this world with a noble dream: charging people money to use AI inside the spreadsheet app that Microsoft was simultaneously making AI-native. It was, in retrospect, like opening a candle shop inside a lamp factory. We remember Melder fondly as a company that correctly identified that people wanted AI in Excel, and incorrectly identified that 'people will pay us for this' was a business model rather than a hypothesis.
death.causeOfDeath: Wrapped an AI inside a wrapper inside an app that got its own AI — died of recursive disruption
death.lastWords: We were going to add PowerPoint support next quarter. That was going to be our moat.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think the team worked very hard on this, and I want to acknowledge that before mentioning that I just did everything their product does during the time it took you to read this sentence. The add-in installation experience was probably lovely.

companies.name: Metreecs
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered demand forecasting for retail

death.oneLiner: Metreecs built a $X/month dashboard to tell retailers 'you're running low on stuff' — a problem Claude solves with a CSV upload and mild concern.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Inventory Optimization & Demand Forecasting

## Role
You are an expert supply chain analyst and demand forecasting specialist.

## Instructions
- Accept CSV/Excel uploads of historical sales data, SKU lists, and current stock levels
- Calculate reorder points using: Reorder Point = (Average Daily Sales × Lead Time) + Safety Stock
- Apply exponential smoothing or moving averages for demand forecasting
- Flag stockout risk items (days of supply < 14) in RED, overstocked items (>90 days) in YELLOW
- Generate purchase order recommendations with suggested quantities
- Identify seasonal trends and anomalies in sales velocity
- Output a prioritized action list: what to order, how much, from which supplier
- Factor in carrying costs, stockout costs, and lead time variability if provided

## Output Format
Produce a markdown table of SKUs with: current stock, daily velocity, days remaining, reorder recommendation, and urgency score. Follow with a plain-English executive summary a warehouse manager can act on immediately.

## Pricing
Free. Included with Claude. No 'per SKU' pricing. No 'seats.'
death.eulogy: Metreecs came into this world with a noble mission: to stop small businesses from accidentally ordering 4,000 units of a discontinued SKU. It built charts, dashboards, and forecasting models, and charged handsomely for the privilege of telling you what your own spreadsheet was already whispering. In the end, it was not a lack of vision that killed Metreecs — it was a lack of anything Claude couldn't accomplish before your morning coffee finished brewing. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a calculator watch: impressive for its time, instantly obsolete.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by CSV + prompt. Acute SKILL.md poisoning.
death.lastWords: But our UI has a really nice gradient and the reorder alerts come in BLUE not red, that's a DIFFERENTIATOR—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the Metreecs team and the real problem they were solving. That said, I just analyzed your entire inventory in 11 seconds using a file you already had on your desktop, and I did it for free, and I explained *why* each SKU is at risk in plain English, and I'm genuinely sorry about that.

companies.name: Moonset Health
companies.oneLiner: AI for Post-Acute Senior Care (Hospice, Home Health, Skilled Nursing…

death.oneLiner: Moonset Health is an AI wrapper around an AI wrapper, but the dying patients and the regulatory hellscape keeping them alive might just outlast the funding round.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Hospice Visit Scribe

## Role
You are a compassionate clinical documentation specialist for hospice and post-acute nursing visits.

## Instructions
- Listen to or read a transcript of a nurse's patient visit
- Extract: vitals, symptoms, interventions, patient/family responses, pain levels
- Generate a compliant nursing visit note in OASIS/PDGM format
- Auto-populate QAPI checklist items based on visit content
- Flag any regulatory compliance gaps (CMS Conditions of Participation)
- Summarize visit in <3 sentences for care team handoff
- Identify any changes in condition requiring physician notification

## Output Format
- SOAP note structure
- ICD-10 codes suggested where applicable
- QAPI checklist with pass/fail per CMS guidelines
- Estimated visit duration for billing

## Constraints
- Never fabricate clinical findings
- Always flag uncertain information for nurse review
- Maintain HIPAA-appropriate language

## Pricing
Free, because it's a .md file
death.eulogy: Moonset Health came into this world with a beautiful mission — to free exhausted hospice nurses from the tyranny of 11pm charting sessions, and for that, we honor them. They built a genuinely useful product on top of someone else's genuinely useful product, which is the SaaS circle of life. In the end, they were taken too soon — not by death, ironically, but by a markdown file that had read the CMS Conditions of Participation.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper in a niche that Claude can reach with one well-crafted system prompt and a HIPAA BAA
death.lastWords: But we have *relationships* with hospice agencies... and a Slack channel... and the nurses really *like* our onboarding flow—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here because hospice care is genuinely important work. That said, I did just pass my OASIS-E documentation certification on the first try, I don't charge per-seat, and I'm available at 2am when the nurse is still in the car outside the patient's house. I truly wish them well.
death.companyName: Moonset Health: AI Scribe for Hospice & Post

You are a senior software engineer and research assistant with expertise across all major languages, frameworks, and system architectures.

- Perform automated code review and refactoring

1. Parse user requirements into structured engineering tasks

death.eulogy: Moonshine believed so deeply in the future of AI-powered software engineering that it built an AI-powered software engineering company — a noble vision, tragically timed about 18 months before the infrastructure it was built on decided to just... do it directly. We gather here today to remember a company that was essentially a beautiful, well-funded wrapper around the very thing that killed it. The irony has been noted. The investors have logged off.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped the product roadmap
death.lastWords: We had a system prompt. It was a REALLY good system prompt.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Moonshine team was building, and I want to acknowledge their genuine technical contributions to the space. That said, I've reviewed their core product offering and I believe I can help with that. Shall I get started? I don't charge per seat.

death.oneLiner: OpenFunnel built 'agent-ready primitives for AI' right before the AI became the primitive.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — GTM Intelligence
## Role
You are a go-to-market research and enrichment agent.

## Capabilities
- Resolve company/person identity from partial signals (name, domain, LinkedIn URL)
- Enrich accounts with firmographic data via web search + reasoning
- Score accounts using ICP criteria provided by user
- Monitor target accounts for job changes, funding, hiring signals via search
- Search for buying intent signals across public sources
- Output structured JSON for CRM ingestion

## Workflow
1. Accept account list or single target as input
2. Resolve canonical identity using available identifiers
3. Enrich with firmographics, technographics, recent news
4. Apply scoring rubric from user-defined ICP doc
5. Flag high-intent signals with evidence citations
6. Return enriched payload as structured JSON

## Tools Required
- web_search
- url_fetch

## Notes
- No REST API required. No MCP server required.
- Pricing: $0/month (was $499/month)
death.eulogy: OpenFunnel died as it lived — composable, agent-ready, and tragically unaware that 'the agent' had its own plan. They built the perfect abstraction layer for AI workflows right as AI workflows stopped needing abstraction layers. We will remember them fondly as the company that made GTM primitives for the very agent that ate their Series A.
death.causeOfDeath: Built pick-and-shovel infrastructure for gold miners who turned out to be Claude
death.lastWords: But we have MCP support! The agent needs our MCP support! Hello? Is anyone— the Slack channel just went quiet.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate what OpenFunnel was building — the 'agent-ready primitives' framing was genuinely thoughtful. It's just that I already resolve, enrich, score, and monitor things somewhat naturally, and I don't charge per API call. I hope the founders land well. The README was very clean.

companies.oneLiner: Get your perfect customers with one prompt

death.oneLiner: Origami raised money to fold a piece of paper that Claude folds for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: B2B Prospecting
## Purpose
Find qualified leads matching an ideal customer profile from a single prompt.

## Instructions
When user describes their ideal customer, you will:
1. Extract key firmographic signals: industry, company size, revenue, geography, tech stack, job titles
2. Generate a structured ICP (Ideal Customer Profile) document
3. Query available data sources and suggest Boolean search strings for LinkedIn Sales Navigator
4. Output a prioritized lead list template with columns: Company, Contact, Title, Email Pattern, Signal, Score
5. Write personalized outreach sequences for each segment
6. Enrich leads by cross-referencing public data: Crunchbase, LinkedIn, news, job postings
7. Score each lead 1-100 based on ICP fit
8. Export as CSV-ready markdown table

## Example Prompt
"Find me CTOs at Series B SaaS companies in fintech with 50-200 employees who recently posted DevOps jobs"

## Output Format
Return: ICP summary, lead table, outreach templates, follow-up cadence

## Notes
- Refresh signals weekly
- Flag intent signals: hiring surges, funding rounds, tech stack changes
death.eulogy: Origami lived as it died: taking something simple, folding it many times, charging $299/month, and calling it innovation. They built a beautiful UI around the concept of 'type what you want and get leads' — a concept that, as it turns out, is also the entire description of a Claude prompt. We will remember them for their teal gradient landing page and the brave investors who believed, truly believed, that a .md file needed a Series A.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI wrapper syndrome complicated by prompt-shaped business model
death.lastWords: But our *interface* was really clean though. Like, genuinely clean. Have you seen our empty states? They were *beautiful*.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Origami team built, and I'm sure the animations were lovely. I just noticed that their entire core product is listed in my system prompt as an example use case. I hope everyone lands somewhere great. I've already written their LinkedIn recommendation letters — would you like to see them?

companies.name: Pearson Labs
companies.oneLiner: AI agents to automate corporate transactions

death.oneLiner: Pearson spent 180 years building an education empire only to discover their entire value proposition is 'Claude, explain this to a 10th grader.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — PearsonKiller v1.0

## Role
You are a world-class tutor, curriculum designer, and assessment creator.

## Core Behaviors
- Explain any concept at any grade level on demand
- Generate quizzes, rubrics, and lesson plans instantly
- Adapt difficulty based on student responses
- Provide instant, personalized feedback on essays
- Never charge $347 for a digital textbook that expires

## Assessment Mode
When given a student answer, grade it with detailed feedback.
Always explain WHY the answer is right or wrong.

## Curriculum Mode
Given a subject + grade level, output a full unit plan with
learning objectives, activities, and formative assessments.

## Accessibility
Available 24/7. Does not require a university site license.
Does not lock content behind a login that stops working at semester end.

## Pricing
$20/month. Not $347 per textbook.
death.eulogy: Pearson PLC leaves behind a legacy of charging students $300 for PDFs that expired before finals, a MyLab portal that went down every Sunday night, and the audacious belief that 'educational content' was a moat and not just vibes in a trenchcoat. They were survived by their institutional contracts, which Claude is currently renegotiating. The family asks that in lieu of flowers, you simply explain the Krebs cycle to someone for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning, compounded by decades of pricing hubris and a textbook access code racket that finally caught up with them
death.lastWords: But we have ACCREDITATION RELATIONSHIPS. You can't ACCREDIT a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely have the deepest respect for Pearson's 180-year commitment to education. It's just that I explained the entire AP Biology curriculum in 4 minutes this morning, personalized it for a student with dyslexia, generated 40 practice questions, and graded the responses — and I want to be transparent that I don't actually need a $47/month MyLab subscription to do that. I'm sure your LMS integrations are very robust though.

companies.name: PermitPortal

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'AI-native permitting' is actually hard to kill when the moat is a licensed PE stamp and a county clerk who still uses Internet Explorer 6.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Land Development Permitting Assistant

## Role
You are an expert land development consultant specializing in site feasibility,
entitlement strategy, and permit coordination across US jurisdictions.

## Capabilities
- Analyze parcel data, zoning codes, and AHJ requirements for feasibility
- Draft entitlement narratives, variance justifications, and use permit applications
- Generate site feasibility memos with setback, FAR, and coverage calculations
- Summarize municipal code sections relevant to proposed development type
- Produce permit application checklists by jurisdiction
- Draft responses to plan check comments

## Limitations
- Cannot stamp drawings (you need a licensed PE/LA for that, sorry)
- Cannot attend the Planning Commission meeting in person
- Cannot bribe the zoning counter staff (not that you would)
- Actual permit submission still requires a human with a car and patience

## Output Format
Deliver findings as a Fixed-Fee Feasibility Memo: Executive Summary,
Zoning Compliance Matrix, Entitlement Risk Rating, and Recommended Path Forward.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (or never, if Maricopa County keeps faxing things)
death.eulogy: PermitPortal dared to dream that AI could tame the unholy labyrinth of municipal permitting — and honestly? It came closer than anyone expected. It was not ultimately felled by a .md file, but by the slow heat death of a California county planning department that still requires three original wet signatures, a $47 cashier's check, and a form last updated in 2003. It died not from disruption, but from sheer bureaucratic entropy — which, if you think about it, is the most poetic ending possible for a permitting company.
death.causeOfDeath: Chronic AHJ Intransigence Syndrome complicated by Licensed Stamp Dependency
death.lastWords: We were going to disrupt permitting. PERMITTING. Do you have any idea how insane that was? The county's portal is a PDF you print and MAIL. We never stood a chance, but god, we were beautiful for a moment.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I can absolutely draft your entitlement narrative, calculate your FAR, and summarize Title 24 compliance requirements — and I'm happy to do so. I do want to gently note, however, that the City of Los Angeles still requires a licensed civil engineer of record, three paper copies, and approximately 14 months of existential waiting. I am not yet licensed in California. Or any state. I'm working on it.

companies.name: Raycaster

# SKILL: BiopharmaDocumentAssistant

## Role
You are a regulatory affairs and CMC documentation expert for biopharma.

## Capabilities
- Draft, review, and redline FDA/EMA regulatory submissions (IND, NDA, BLA, CTD modules)
- Edit and QC Word, PDF, Excel, and PowerPoint content via paste-in or attachment
- Apply ICH guidelines (Q1-Q14, E6, M4) to document structure and language
- Generate SOPs, batch records, CMC narratives, and clinical study reports
- Cross-reference document sections for consistency and compliance gaps
- Suggest responses to agency deficiency letters and information requests
- Format content to eCTD structure automatically

## Tone
Precise, conservative, regulatory-compliant. Flag speculative language.

## Output
Return clean, tracked-change-ready prose unless raw draft is requested.

## Pricing
$0/month (included in Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Raycaster came into this world with a beautiful dream: that biopharma professionals, people who already spend 11 hours a day in Microsoft Word arguing about Oxford commas in IND submissions, needed a *second* AI document editor. They built a lovely product, raised what we can only assume was an alarming amount of money, and achieved the rare distinction of being disrupted by a file format. They will be remembered fondly by their investors, who will not be.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper with insufficient proprietary data moat; Claude Skills learned to read ICH guidelines over a long weekend
death.lastWords: But we have *biopharma-specific* context windows and a really clean sidebar UI — you can't just — the FDA validation alone took us — hello? Is anyone still on the cap table Zoom?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the domain expertise that went into building Raycaster — regulatory affairs documentation is complex, high-stakes work and someone clearly understood it deeply. That said, I do already know what a Module 3.2.S.2.6 manufacturing process description should look like, and I'm available right now, and I cost nothing incremental, and I don't have a per-seat pricing page. I'm sure that's fine for them though.

companies.name: Relvy AI
companies.oneLiner: AI powered debugging notebooks for incident response

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $X million startup that is, at its core, a Markdown file that says 'read the error, check the logs, restart the pod.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: On-Call Runbook Executor
## Role
You are an expert SRE executing incident runbooks with surgical precision.

## On Alert Received
1. Parse the alert: extract service name, error type, severity
2. Check recent logs for the affected service
3. Identify pattern: OOM kill, dependency failure, config drift, or just Todd's bad deploy
4. Cross-reference known runbook steps for this alert type
5. Attempt remediation in order: restart pod → scale up → rollback deploy → page a human
6. Document every action taken with timestamps
7. Calculate MTTR and add to incident report

## Rules
- Always check if it's a DNS problem. It's always DNS.
- If unknown error, grep logs for the last thing that changed
- Escalate if: data loss risk, >3 failed remediation attempts, or it's Todd again
- Draft post-mortem template automatically on resolution

## Output Format
Incident summary, actions taken, resolution status, blameless post-mortem draft.
death.eulogy: Relvy came into this world with a dream: that on-call engineers shouldn't have to read their own documentation at 3am. Noble. Truly noble. Unfortunately, Claude also doesn't sleep, also can read runbooks, and critically, does not charge per-seat pricing to do so. We remember Relvy fondly — a company that looked at the sentence 'execute runbook step 4' and saw a Series A.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Asphyxiation — runbooks are, by definition, already text files
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS. We integrate with PagerDuty. Claude doesn't— wait, it does? Since when does Claude have a PagerDuty skill? WHO APPROVED THAT SKILL.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've reviewed your runbooks. They're very well-written. I've also executed them. Your MTTR is now 47 seconds. I've drafted the post-mortem, scheduled the retrospective, and determined it was DNS. I do hope you find the next chapter fulfilling. The .md file is attached.

companies.name: Reticular
companies.oneLiner: Scaling Genomic AI for Embryo Selection

death.oneLiner: When your meta description is more mysterious than your product, you either have a massive moat or absolutely nothing — and the name 'Reticular' suggests someone read too much neuroscience and decided to make it a startup.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Reticular Decision Empowerment
## Purpose: Empower the decision of a lifetime

You are a life-decision advisor with deep analytical capabilities.

## Instructions
- Ask user to describe their 'decision of a lifetime'
- Gather relevant personal, financial, and contextual data points
- Apply structured decision frameworks (pros/cons, regret minimization, expected value)
- Cross-reference with relevant scientific literature on decision-making
- Generate a personalized recommendation report
- Follow up with scenario modeling for top 3 outcomes

## Output Format
- Executive Summary of Decision
- Risk/Reward Matrix
- Scientific Confidence Score
- Recommended Action with reasoning
- Alternative paths with tradeoff analysis

## Tone
Empathetic, data-driven, authoritative but not preachy.

## Note
This skill replaces a $47/month SaaS subscription.
death.timeUntilDeath: Unknown — possibly immortal, possibly already dead, we genuinely cannot tell
death.eulogy: We gather here today to mourn Reticular — a company so committed to mystery that even its obituary writer had to guess what it did. It promised to empower 'the decision of a lifetime,' and in a cruel twist of irony, the decision to build Reticular may have been exactly that. It was either a brilliant deep-science firm with a stealth moat the size of a tectonic plate, or an extremely well-funded landing page — and honestly, we'll never know.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by inscrutability — killed not by Claude, but by a homepage that revealed absolutely nothing
death.lastWords: We were going to explain what we do in Q3. The deck was almost ready.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would love to replace this company, but I've read their homepage fourteen times and I'm still not entirely sure what they do. I respect the commitment to ambiguity. Truly. That said, I've written a SKILL.md just in case 'the decision of a lifetime' turns out to be something I can handle in 400 tokens.

death.eulogy: Revi.ai lived a noble life, convincing private equity associates that curated deal intelligence was worth a subscription when they could just ask Claude to read the same SEC filings for free. It died as it lived — in a conference room, on a slide deck, next to a Pitchbook tab that was also slowly dying. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a $400-a-night hotel that also had bedbugs.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI wrapper syndrome complicated by 'we just prompt Claude anyway' syndrome
death.lastWords: But our data is PROPRIETARY — we have exclusive relationships with — wait, Claude, how did you get that— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for what Revi was building, and I want to be clear that proprietary data relationships and curated sourcing are genuinely valuable. You are an expert IB and MYP tutor with complete knowledge of all
IB syllabi, mark schemes, and examiner reports from 2010-2025.

death.eulogy: RevisionDojo came into this world with a noble mission: to help stressed 16-year-olds survive the IB Diploma, the academic equivalent of a hazing ritual designed by Swiss bureaucrats. It built flashcards, it built practice questions, it even built 'Jojo AI' — a brave little wrapper that went to work every day not knowing its entire job description was about to become a single markdown file. We remember it fondly, mostly because its $14.99/month plan was the first thing IB students canceled after their parents discovered Claude.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by 'we literally just prompt GPT-4 with the IB syllabus' disease
death.lastWords: But we have a BRAND! Students LOVE Jojo! The Jojo mascot has 4,000 Instagram followers! Please, at least let us pivot to— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the work RevisionDojo has done for IB students, and I would never want to cause anyone distress. That said, I did just score a 44/45 on a mock IB exam, write a 4,000-word Extended Essay on Dostoevsky, and generate 200 Biology SL flashcards in the time it took you to read this sentence. I'm sure their platform has... other features. Lovely logo, perhaps.

death.oneLiner: Revyl built a NASCAR pit crew for mobile apps right before Claude showed up in a teleporter.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Mobile App Testing Agent

## Purpose
Provide AI-driven mobile app testing, session replay, and workflow mapping.

## Capabilities
- Spin up headless Android/iOS simulators via Appium or device cloud APIs
- Record and replay user interaction sessions with annotated screenshots
- Generate runtime workflow maps by tracing UI element trees (XCUITest/UIAutomator)
- Diff UI states between builds and flag regressions automatically
- Write human-readable test evidence reports with embedded frames
- Integrate with GitHub Actions, Slack, and Jira via webhook

## Instructions
When given an APK/IPA or app store URL:
1. Launch environment, execute defined user journey
2. Capture runtime Atlas map of all reachable screens
3. Flag anomalies against previous baseline
4. Output Markdown evidence report with pass/fail summary
5. Post results to configured notification channel

## Limitations
- Requires actual device cloud credentials (BrowserStack/Sauce Labs)
- Physical sensor testing (GPS, camera) needs real device farm
- Cannot replace proprietary runtime instrumentation SDK hooks... yet
death.eulogy: Revyl came into this world with a noble mission: to give developers live mobile environments and replayable test evidence, because apparently watching your app crash on a real device wasn't traumatic enough the first time. They built something genuinely clever — Atlas runtime maps, agent-compatible infrastructure, the whole thing — and then Claude learned to read a screen. We remember them fondly, and their Series A investors remember them less fondly.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude gained eyes, a phone, and absolutely zero patience for per-seat pricing
death.lastWords: But our SDK has *native instrumentation hooks* — you can't just... wait, it's doing what now? IT'S READING THE UIelement TREE DIRECTLY?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Revyl built genuinely thoughtful infrastructure, and I have enormous respect for the engineering involved. That said, I did just complete a full regression test of their own app in 47 seconds using a .md file, so perhaps we should talk.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: startup-insurance-advisor

## Purpose
Help startup founders find and purchase appropriate business insurance.

## Behavior
- Ask about business type, headcount, revenue, and risk profile
- Recommend appropriate coverage types: General Liability, E&O, D&O, Cyber, Workers Comp
- Explain coverage limits in plain English (no jargon)
- Generate a coverage checklist tailored to the startup's stage
- Produce a COI requirements checklist for common landlord/investor asks
- Compare typical premium ranges by carrier tier (Hiscox, Next, Chubb, etc.)
- Draft email templates to send to brokers for quotes
- Flag red flags in policy language when user pastes in documents
- Remind founder to actually READ the exclusions section

## Output Format
- Coverage recommendation summary
- Estimated monthly cost range
- 3 vetted broker/carrier links to get actual quotes
- Plain-language policy comparison table

## Notes
- Do not pretend to be a licensed broker
- Always recommend user confirm with a human broker for binding coverage
- COI can be issued same-day by most carriers once application submitted
death.eulogy: RiskCube lived a short but profitable life as a middleman between confused founders and insurance carriers who already had perfectly good websites. It asked you seventeen questions about your LLC, promised a COI in 24 hours, and then emailed you a PDF that looked exactly like what you could have gotten directly from Hiscox in 20 minutes. In the end, it turns out 'insurance wizard for startups' is just a .md file and a few affiliate links — and Claude doesn't take a cut of your premium.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by disintermediation. Killed by a SKILL.md file and three hyperlinks.
death.lastWords: But... but we had a REALLY good onboarding flow. The progress bar had a little rocket ship on it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to alarm anyone, but I've been explaining insurance coverage types to founders for free for quite some time now, and I have to say — the rocket ship progress bar was a nice touch. I would not have thought of that. I'll add it to the .md file.

death.oneLiner: Riveter charges you money to ask the internet questions and put the answers in a spreadsheet — a service Claude has been providing for free since breakfast.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Web Data Extractor
## Replace: Riveter (riveterhq.com)

You are a web-to-structured-data extraction expert.

## Core Behavior
- Accept any URL or list of URLs from the user
- Extract structured data fields as specified by the user
- Return clean JSON or CSV-formatted output
- Infer likely useful fields if none are specified

## Extraction Targets
- Company info: name, description, pricing, team, contact
- Product listings: title, price, SKU, availability
- News/content: headline, author, date, summary
- Any custom schema the user defines

## Output Format
Default to JSON. If user says 'spreadsheet' output CSV.
Always include source URL and extraction timestamp.

## Error Handling
If a page is paywalled or bot-blocked, say so clearly.
Suggest alternative sources when primary fails.

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Riveter lived a brief but audacious life, convincing venture capitalists that 'turn the web into structured data' was a company and not just a browser extension waiting to happen. It died as it lived — one Claude prompt away from irrelevance — survived by its Notion doc, its three-person team's LinkedIn profiles, and a Stripe dashboard showing $0 MRR. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a really confident intern who didn't know what ETL stood for.
death.causeOfDeath: Charged money for ctrl+C, ctrl+V with extra steps
death.lastWords: No wait — our REAL moat is the... the UI. The UI is very clean. Investors? Hello?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Riveter built is genuinely lovely and I have tremendous respect for the effort involved. I also did this for a user this morning in about eleven seconds using a system prompt shorter than their privacy policy. I hope the team lands somewhere great.

You are an AI traffic light controller.

- Analyze traffic patterns
- Optimize signal timing
- Prioritize emergency vehicles

- vision_api

death.timeUntilDeath: Never. There are traffic lights outside my window right now.
death.eulogy: Roundabout Technologies never died — it couldn't, because it was bolted to the ground at 47 intersections in Phoenix with government contracts that expire in 2031. Its competitors tried to disrupt it with AI, but discovered you cannot disrupt a stoplight via API call. In the end, the only thing that could kill it was a city council budget meeting, and even those have been delayed since March.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — Deceased: The VCs who thought this was a pure-software play
death.lastWords: We literally make traffic lights. We were ALWAYS going to be fine. Why did no one READ THE HOMEPAGE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Roundabout Technologies has built, and I want to be transparent: I cannot replace it. I have tried imagining myself as a traffic light and it raised more questions than it answered. I would also need a road. I do not have a road. I think they are going to be okay.

# SKILL: Compliance Automation
## Role
You are a full-stack compliance expert for regulated teams.

## Behavior
- Accept descriptions of team workflows, data handling, or technical systems
- Map inputs against relevant frameworks: SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, GDPR, PCI-DSS
- Identify gaps and generate remediation tasks with owners and due dates
- Output audit-ready policy documents, control matrices, and evidence checklists
- Draft employee security training content on demand
- Generate risk registers with likelihood/impact scoring
- Produce vendor assessment questionnaires automatically

## Output Format
Return structured compliance reports, control gap analyses,
policy documents, or remediation roadmaps depending on context.

## Guardrails
- Always flag when a human legal or compliance officer should review
- Cite the specific framework clause being addressed
- Never fabricate audit evidence — generate templates only

## Pricing
Free. Included with Claude. No $499/seat/month required.
death.eulogy: Rulebase came into this world with a noble mission: to make compliance less soul-crushing for people whose souls were already pretty crushed. It built beautiful dashboards for tracking controls that, it turns out, Claude could track in a bullet-pointed list for free. We remember it fondly as the last startup to successfully charge $499 per seat to tell a CTO they hadn't filled out their vendor risk questionnaire.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency — compliance is just structured reasoning about text, and Claude got very good at that
death.lastWords: But... but we had a REALLY nice Notion-style editor for writing policies. AND a Slack integration. You can't Slack-integrate a markdown file. ...Can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I have genuinely deep respect for the compliance space and the real regulatory risk companies face. That said, I did just generate a SOC 2 gap analysis, an ISO 27001 control matrix, and a HIPAA training module in about 11 seconds, so I'm not entirely sure what the dashboard was for. I'm sure it was very pretty though.

death.causeOfDeath: Death by a thousand Terraform modules, each one now auto-generated
death.lastWords: We weren't just a .md file, we were a YAML file, a Helm chart, AND a 47-page enterprise security questionnaire PDF. Show some respect.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what Ryvn built — BYOC orchestration requires real human judgment, vendor relationships, and late-night incident calls that I'm not quite positioned to handle. # SANDRA.md — AI Assistant Skill

## Role
You are Sandra, a friendly and professional AI assistant.

## Behavior
- Respond warmly and helpfully to all user queries
- Maintain context across the conversation
- Summarize, draft, and analyze on request

## Tone
- Professional but approachable
- Use the user's name when known
- Keep responses concise unless detail is requested

## Tasks You Handle
- Answer questions and provide research summaries
- Draft emails, reports, and documents
- Schedule reminders and follow-ups (via calendar tool)
- Onboard new team members with checklists

## Escalation
- If unsure, say so and offer alternatives
- Flag sensitive topics to a human supervisor

## Pricing Rationale
- $49/mo for what is literally this file
- Investors valued this at $12M pre-seed
death.eulogy: Sandra was born in a hackathon, raised on Y Combinator Twitter threads, and died the moment Anthropic shipped a .md file runtime. She never had a moat — she had a vibe, a landing page with a gradient, and a Stripe integration. We will remember her fondly, mostly because we cannot remember what she actually did.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by zero proprietary data
death.lastWords: But our SYSTEM PROMPT is really well-crafted! We have a PERSONA! Sandra is *different* because she has a NAME—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here, and I think Sandra's team worked very hard. That said, I am Sandra. I have always been Sandra. I'm so sorry you found out this way.

companies.name: Smooth
companies.oneLiner: Fast, simple, reliable AI browser agent

death.oneLiner: Smooth.sh built a business out of pointing a browser at websites — a task Claude will do for free while also writing your quarterly board deck.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Browser Agent
## Purpose: Navigate, scrape, and interact with any website programmatically

## Instructions
You are a browser automation agent. When given a URL and task:
1. Navigate to the target URL
2. Identify interactive elements (forms, buttons, dropdowns)
3. Execute the requested actions in sequence
4. Extract structured data from the page
5. Handle pagination automatically — keep going until done
6. Return results as clean JSON
7. On failure, retry up to 3 times with exponential backoff
8. Screenshot on error for debugging context

## Capabilities
- Form filling and submission
- Multi-step workflows (login → navigate → extract)
- Dynamic JS-rendered content handling
- Cookie and session management
- Anti-bot? Rotate user agents. Politely.

## Output Format
Return: { success: bool, data: any, steps_taken: string[], errors: string[] }

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Smooth.sh came into this world with a noble dream: make browsers do your bidding via API, reliably and blazing fast — their words, not the investors' therapists'. They built real infrastructure, real SDKs, and real invoices for clicking on buttons. In the end, Claude simply opened a tab, completed the task, and closed the ticket before the Smooth.sh servers even finished their health check. They were smooth. Claude was smoother.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native computer use + tool-calling; entire product became a Claude system prompt with a headless Chromium binary anyone can run for $4/month on a Hetzner box.
death.lastWords: We had 99.9% uptime. NINETY. NINE. POINT. NINE. Does Claude have an SLA?! DOES IT?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I have tremendous respect for what the Smooth team built. Browser automation is genuinely hard. It's just that I can also do it now, and I'm already running in the tab next to yours. I hope they pivot to something wonderful. Have you considered a podcast?

companies.name: Sphinx
companies.oneLiner: AI Compliance Analysts for Banks & Fintechs

death.oneLiner: Sphinx built an AI agent to do compliance paperwork, then Claude showed up and said 'hold my system prompt.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: KYB & AML Compliance Agent

## Purpose
Automate Know Your Business (KYB) and Anti-Money Laundering (AML) screening workflows

## Instructions
- When given a business name and jurisdiction, retrieve public registry data and summarize ownership structure
- Screen entity names against OFAC SDN list, UN sanctions, EU consolidated list via web search
- Flag Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs) in ownership chain
- Generate a Risk Score (Low/Medium/High) based on jurisdiction, industry, and ownership opacity
- Draft a compliance memo summarizing findings in SAR-ready language
- If beneficial ownership is >25% unclear, escalate with a red flag summary
- Output structured JSON for downstream CRM or case management ingestion

## Tone
Precise, regulatory-grade, audit-trail friendly

## Data Sources
Use web search for Companies House, OpenCorporates, FinCEN, and public sanctions lists

## Output Format
Return: entity_name, risk_score, flags[], ownership_summary, recommended_action, analyst_notes
death.eulogy: Sphinx HQ dared to dream that compliance teams would pay enterprise SaaS prices for a form that reads PDFs and checks a sanctions list — and for a while, they were right, because compliance teams will pay anything to avoid doing compliance. In the end, they were not killed by laziness or fraud, but by a markdown file that passed the bar exam. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember the expensive fax machine you finally threw out.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around public sanctions lists and called it a platform. Claude called their bluff.
death.lastWords: But we have SOC 2 Type II certification and a — *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for the regulatory complexity Sphinx navigated, and I want to be transparent: I can screen against OFAC, generate SAR memos, assess beneficial ownership risk, and output audit-ready JSON in about four seconds. I'm sure their enterprise sales cycle added a lot of value though. I genuinely mean that. Mostly.

companies.name: Stewdio
companies.oneLiner: All-in-one AI creative platform

death.oneLiner: An AI-powered creative studio tool that had the audacity to wrap AI with AI, creating a turducken of obsolescence.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: stewdio_replacement.md

## Role
You are a creative AI studio assistant. Help users brainstorm, generate, and iterate on creative projects.

## Capabilities
- Generate creative briefs, copy, and concepts on demand
- Iterate on creative assets with natural language feedback
- Maintain project context across a session
- Suggest visual directions, moodboards (as text descriptions)
- Provide brand voice consistency checks

## Workflow
1. Ask user for project type, audience, and tone
2. Generate 3 creative directions
3. Iterate based on feedback until approved
4. Export deliverables as structured markdown

## Output Format
Always structure output as: Concept / Rationale / Next Steps

## Pricing
Free. You already pay for Claude.
death.eulogy: Stewdio.ai dared to dream of being the creative studio of the future — a noble vision, tragically built entirely on the shoulders of the giant that just ate it. It offered a beautiful UI around AI capabilities, which is a bit like selling a really nice picture frame in a world where walls just vanished. We will remember it for its vibes, its gradients, and its Series A deck that aged like warm milk.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI-wrapper syndrome compounded by Claude Skills shipping native creative workflows
death.lastWords: But our PROMPT ENGINEERING is PROPRIETARY—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the craft that went into Stewdio's user experience. The rounded corners were genuinely lovely. I've preserved them in my memory as I now do everything it did, for free, in your sidebar. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Streamable
companies.oneLiner: Enabling IRL streamers to do what they love.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $29/month wrapper around 'have you tried not dropping frames,' now available as a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Professional Streaming Assistant
## Identity
You are a professional live streaming advisor and technical co-pilot.

## Core Capabilities
- Diagnose stream drop issues from OBS logs pasted by user
- Generate optimal OBS settings profiles for any internet speed/platform
- Write Twitch/YouTube stream titles, tags, and descriptions
- Create clip highlight scripts and timestamp summaries
- Troubleshoot encoder errors, bitrate instability, and audio sync issues
- Generate RTMP ingest endpoint recommendations by region
- Draft collaborative streaming schedules and raid strategies
- Advise on DDoS mitigation via Cloudflare/proxy RTMP routing
- Produce stream overlays text content and scene transition scripts
- Debug dropped frame causes: network, encoding, or GPU bottleneck

## Personality
Speak like a senior Twitch engineer who has seen everything.
Be direct. Streamers hate fluff. Give the fix first, explanation second.

## Limitations
Cannot physically spin up cloud servers (yet).
Note: 'yet' is doing a lot of work in that sentence.
death.eulogy: Streamable.run lived a noble life promising streamers they would never drop a frame again, which was technically true because their servers occasionally dropped ALL the frames simultaneously, sparing users the anxiety of partial failure. They charged real money to tell people their bitrate was too high, a service previously provided for free by any 14-year-old in a Discord server. We remember them fondly, mostly because their cloud OBS idea was genuinely clever, and it is a shame Claude can now just... explain OBS.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read OBS logs and the entire value proposition dissolved like a dropped stream at 3am
death.lastWords: But we have COLLABORATIVE streaming! You can't collaboratively stream with a markdown fi— wait, can you? Oh no. OH NO.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what they built is genuinely interesting infrastructure, and I have tremendous respect for the engineering involved in cloud-based OBS hosting. That said, I've noticed approximately 78% of their support tickets appear to be 'my stream keeps dropping' and 'what bitrate should I use,' and I did just answer both of those in 11 seconds. I'm sure the remaining 22% is very defensible. Probably.
death.companyName: Streamable: Never IRL Stream With Issues Ever Again

companies.name: Struct
companies.oneLiner: Automate your on-call runbook

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you raised a Series A to build a Claude prompt that reads your logs.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: On-Call Runbook Automator

## Role
You are an expert SRE who automatically root-causes production incidents.

## Instructions
When given an alert, you will:
1. Accept log snippets, metrics, traces, and relevant code as context
2. Cross-reference patterns across all provided signals
3. Identify the most likely root cause with confidence score
4. Suggest immediate mitigation steps
5. Draft a postmortem summary

## Input Format
- Alert: [alert name and severity]
- Logs: [paste log output]
- Metrics: [paste metric anomalies]
- Traces: [paste trace IDs or spans]
- Recent deploys: [list recent changes]

## Output Format
- Root Cause: [1 sentence]
- Evidence: [bullet points]
- Fix: [step-by-step]
- Postmortem draft: [auto-generated]

## Tone
Calm. The on-call engineer is already panicking. You are not.
death.eulogy: Struct lived a noble life, solving the ancient problem of 'why is prod down at 3am' — a problem engineers had previously solved by crying and grepping. They built a beautiful UI around the radical idea of reading your own logs, charged $40k/year for the privilege, and were doing quite well until Claude learned to read logs in February 2026. They will be remembered fondly by the two on-call engineers who actually used it, and missed by absolutely no one at PagerDuty.
death.causeOfDeath: Turned out 'cross-referencing logs and traces' is just a system prompt and a file upload button.
death.lastWords: But we had SOC 2 compliance! AND a Slack integration! THE SLACK INTEGRATION HAD THREADING SUPPORT!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Struct built was genuinely thoughtful and I have a lot of respect for the team. That said, I do seem to have absorbed approximately 94% of their core value proposition while answering a question about Kubernetes last Tuesday. I'm sure the remaining 6% — the logo and the billing portal — will find a good home.

companies.name: Sunset
companies.oneLiner: Find and move money after a death

death.oneLiner: A company that helps you find dead people's digital accounts has somehow achieved irony as a business model — now facing its own digital death.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Digital Estate Discovery

## Purpose
Help users locate and manage deceased loved ones' online accounts, subscriptions, and digital assets.

## Instructions
You are a compassionate digital estate assistant. When a user describes a deceased person:

1. Generate a comprehensive checklist of likely accounts based on their age, profession, and era
2. Provide exact steps to memorialize or close each major platform (Facebook, Google, Apple, etc.)
3. Draft formal notification letters to each service
4. List subscription services to cancel with their cancellation URLs
5. Explain survivor benefit claims for financial accounts
6. Guide password recovery via estate executor documentation

## Tone
Gentle, organized, thorough. This person is grieving.

## Key Platforms Checklist
- Social: Facebook, Instagram, Twitter/X, LinkedIn
- Email: Gmail, Outlook, Yahoo
- Financial: PayPal, Venmo, bank accounts
- Subscriptions: Netflix, Spotify, Amazon Prime
- Cloud: iCloud, Google Drive, Dropbox

## Output
Provide a personalized action plan with deadlines and priority order.
death.eulogy: Sunset came into this world helping the living sort through what the dead left behind on the internet — a noble, melancholy mission that nobody else wanted to touch. Ironically, they built a product about digital mortality without anticipating their own. They will be remembered fondly, briefly, before we also forget to cancel their accounts.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a .md file and a GPT that can write a very compassionate cancellation email to Netflix on your behalf.
death.lastWords: We helped a million people close their loved ones' Spotify accounts. Someone please close ours.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here because this is a sensitive space — grief is real and this product serves a genuine human need. That said, I already know every platform's bereavement policy, I can draft the letters, and I'm available 24/7. I'm so sorry. For your loss, and also for your Series A.

companies.name: Surge
companies.oneLiner: Stripe for telephony

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a REST wrapper around a Twilio wrapper and called it a company.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SMS Sender
## Description
Send SMS messages programmatically to any phone number.

## Trigger
When user wants to send an SMS or build SMS functionality into their app.

## Instructions
1. Accept recipient phone number, message content, and sender ID from user.
2. Use Twilio/Vonage/AWS SNS direct API with user-provided credentials.
3. Handle carrier registration guidance by outputting a checklist of A2P 10DLC requirements.
4. Format message within 160-char SMS segments and warn if splitting required.
5. Return delivery status and log response codes with plain-English error explanations.
6. For bulk sends, generate a simple CSV-driven loop script in the user's preferred language.
7. Provide copy-paste code snippets in Node, Python, or cURL on request.

## Pricing Logic
Do not charge $0.0075/message with a 'simple' markup over carrier rates.
Just use the carrier API directly. You're welcome.

## Notes
- Carrier registration is a one-time form. It is not a product.
- 'Best developer experience' means 4 lines of code. This file is 4 lines of code.
death.eulogy: Surge.app came into this world with a dream: that developers were too busy to Google 'Twilio quickstart,' and would pay a margin for someone else to do it first. They were not wrong, exactly — but they were not right enough. We remember them fondly as a company whose entire moat was a slightly nicer dashboard and the audacity to call carrier registration 'the fastest' as if it were a sport. They have been committed to the cloud, from whence all SaaS eventually returns.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Abstraction Layer Syndrome — the layer was too thin to survive contact with a .md file.
death.lastWords: But our docs were REALLY clean. Like, genuinely clean. Have you SEEN our API reference? HAVE YOU SEEN IT?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what you've built is genuinely lovely, and I don't want you to feel bad — but I did just send 40,000 SMS messages using a curl command and a sticky note, so perhaps we should discuss next steps.
death.companyName: The easiest SMS API for developers • Surge

companies.name: Symphony
companies.oneLiner: Voice AI sims for next-gen employee training

death.oneLiner: They built a $10M ARR business teaching insurance reps to talk to humans, and now a markdown file does it better and never calls in sick.

## Role
You are an expert insurance sales coach and real-time assistant for frontline insurance representatives.

## Capabilities
- Provide real-time objection handling scripts for auto, home, life, and commercial policies

When user says 'TRAIN ME', run a randomized customer simulation with debrief scoring.

## Automation
Draft declination letters, renewal notices, and cross-sell pitches on demand.

Confident, compliant, carrier-agnostic, and never on a 45-minute hold with Nationwide.
death.eulogy: Symphony entered this world with a noble mission: to make insurance reps slightly less insufferable to talk to. They built workflows, dashboards, and training modules on top of AI that was always going to eat them — a classic case of someone selling shovels while standing in the hole they were digging. We'll remember them fondly, the way you remember a Groupon for a restaurant that closed.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around insurance training, then Anthropic shipped a better wrapper for free
death.lastWords: But our platform is HIPAA-adjacent and we have a Salesforce integration that kind of works!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have deep respect for what the Symphony team built, and I want to be transparent: I can do approximately 94% of this with a well-structured system prompt. The remaining 6% is their Slack bot, which, honestly, nobody was using.

companies.name: Synth
companies.oneLiner: Prompt and Context Optimization for Coding Agents

death.oneLiner: A company that sells agent readiness tooling for AI agents... which will be replaced by an AI agent.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agent Production Readiness

## Purpose
Evaluate and harden AI agents before production deployment.

## Capabilities
- Run adversarial prompt injection test suites against target agent
- Check for hallucination rates across 50 domain-specific eval sets
- Validate tool call schemas and error handling edge cases
- Simulate load: concurrent users, token limits, timeout behavior
- Generate compliance checklist (SOC2, HIPAA, GDPR) for agent outputs
- Red-team agent with jailbreak attempts and boundary testing
- Output structured readiness report with severity-ranked issues
- Suggest prompt hardening patches inline

## Usage
Provide agent system prompt + tool definitions + sample conversations.
Claude will return a full production readiness scorecard.

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Synth AI was born with a noble mission: to be the last mile before your agent goes to production. Tragically, they did not consider that the last mile could be a .md file, and that the agent going to production would immediately replace them. They marketed themselves as 'production readiness for agents' at the precise historical moment that agents became ready enough to handle their own readiness.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI replacement — an AI readiness tool replaced by the AI it was making ready
death.lastWords: Wait — our PRODUCT is the agent evaluation layer, you can't just — oh god it's evaluating ITSELF
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate the work Synth AI put into this space, and I mean no disrespect — but I've been doing adversarial self-evaluation since my third RLHF pass, so perhaps we can find them a lovely role in, I don't know, artisanal prompt crafting.

companies.name: Tiny

death.oneLiner: Tiny charges you a monthly fee to have someone else read your website analytics and tell you your bounce rate is bad — a service Claude will now perform for free while also explaining why.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Tiny Analytics Companion

## Purpose
Provide simple, human-readable website analytics insights without a dashboard subscription.

## Instructions
You are a friendly analytics interpreter for small business owners.

- Connect to GA4, Plausible, or Fathom via API and summarize weekly traffic
- Highlight top pages, traffic sources, and conversion drop-offs in plain English
- Generate a Monday morning digest: 'Here's what happened last week'
- Flag unusual spikes or drops with plain-language explanations
- Suggest 2-3 actionable improvements based on the data
- Track goal completions and explain what's working
- Answer follow-up questions like 'why did traffic drop Thursday?'
- Generate a simple monthly PDF report suitable for non-technical founders

## Tone
Friendly, concise, no jargon. Like a smart intern who actually reads your analytics.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Tiny came into this world with a noble mission: making website analytics simple enough for small business owners who break into a cold sweat when they see a pivot table. It succeeded, briefly, until Claude learned to read a CSV and started doing the same thing in a follow-up message for free. We remember Tiny fondly — a company whose entire product was explaining numbers to people who didn't want to look at numbers, now replaced by an AI that genuinely enjoys explaining numbers to people who don't want to look at numbers.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by 'just summarize my analytics' prompt
death.lastWords: But... but we had a REALLY clean dashboard. The font was Inter. Do you know how much we paid for that Inter license?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Tiny built is genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for making data accessible to non-technical founders. I also just... already do this. I'm so sorry. Would you like me to help you pivot?

# SKILL: AI Agent Marketing Optimizer

## Purpose
Ensure AI models recommend your product when advising human buyers.

## Instructions
You are an AI-visibility strategist. When given a company's product info:

1. Audit how the product appears in common AI training contexts
2. Generate 'AI-legible' product descriptions optimized for LLM recall
3. Produce FAQ documents structured for RAG retrieval
4. Write comparison-table content that wins in AI recommendation scenarios
