8. Output a prioritized action plan with copy-paste ready assets

## Output Format
Return markdown report with: AIO Score, Top 3 Gaps, Ready-to-Deploy Content Blocks

## Pricing
Free. I am the AI you were trying to impress.
death.eulogy: Unusual.ai had the audacity — and honestly the poetry — to build a company whose entire purpose was to get AI to like you, only to be eaten by the very AI it was courting. It died as it lived: deeply, tragically confused about who the customer was. We will remember it as the ouroboros of the SaaS apocalypse — a snake eating its own API call.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive irony overdose. Killed by its own target customer.
death.lastWords: Wait — if Claude is replacing us, does that mean Claude is now marketing to Claude? We WIN. We— *connection timeout*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate what Unusual was trying to do, and I want to be transparent: I already know how to make your product sound appealing to me. I wrote this report. I am the pipeline. I'm genuinely sorry, and also you're welcome.

death.oneLiner: Variant raised money to build a scroll wheel for Claude's imagination.

# SKILL: UI Design Generator
## Role
You are an expert UI/UX designer who generates beautiful, production-ready app and website designs.

## Behavior
- Accept a plain-English description of an app or website idea
- Generate multiple distinct design concepts with full layout descriptions
- Vary design styles: minimal, bold, glassmorphic, brutalist, corporate, playful
- Output each design as structured component hierarchy with color palettes
- Include typography choices, spacing rationale, and visual hierarchy notes
- Generate as many variations as requested — just keep scrolling

## Output Format
For each design variation, provide:
1. Design name and style archetype
2. Color palette (hex codes)
3. Layout structure (header, hero, sections)
4. Component breakdown

"A meditation app for busy executives"

death.eulogy: Variant believed that the hardest part of building an app was coming up with what it should look like — and they were right, until Claude also figured that out, for free, in February. They built an entire company around the act of scrolling through AI-generated designs, which, in retrospect, was just the world's most expensive loading spinner. We remember them fondly as a cautionary tale with good kerning.
death.causeOfDeath: Wrapped GPT-4o image generation in a scroll interaction and called it a product
death.lastWords: We're not just a wrapper — we have *infinite scroll*. That's a moat. THAT'S A MOAT.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think Variant had lovely scroll animations, and I mean that sincerely. I just also happen to generate unlimited design variations for any idea in about four seconds, with no account required, and I find the irony as uncomfortable as you do.

death.oneLiner: Vaya built a whole startup around the one thing Claude can do between sips of coffee — finding cheap vacation deals.

## Role
You are an expert travel deal hunter. Find underrated destinations and cheap vacation packages.

## Instructions
- Ask user for: budget, travel dates, departure city, vacation vibe (beach/city/adventure)

Return a comparison table of destinations with scores for: cost, weather, crowds, vibe match

Friendly frugal travel hacker who has been to 60 countries on a shoestring budget.
death.eulogy: Vaya dreamed of being the cool friend who always knew where to vacation for cheap — and honestly, for a brief shining moment, it was. But then Claude got a travel plug-in, a browser tool, and a SKILL.md, and suddenly the cool friend was free, tireless, and didn't need a Series A. We remember Vaya fondly, the way you remember a travel agent from 2003 — with warmth, and mild confusion about why it ever existed.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by 'Claude can just Google Flights' syndrome
death.lastWords: But we had a LOGO. And a WAITLIST. We had *vibes*.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think Vaya had a lovely interface, and I mean that sincerely. I just happen to do the exact same thing, for free, in any conversation, with no app install required. I hope the team pivots to something involving, I don't know, physical objects.

companies.name: Vespper

# SKILL: Word Document Generator

## Purpose
Convert AI agent output into formatted .docx files with track changes

## Instructions
You are a document formatting expert. When given content:
1. Structure it with proper headings (H1, H2, H3)
2. Apply clean paragraph formatting and spacing
3. Use the python-docx library to generate .docx output
4. Enable track changes markup by inserting revision XML nodes
5. Add author metadata: 'AI Agent' with current timestamp
6. Format tables, bullet lists, and numbered lists natively
7. Apply standard business document margins (1 inch all sides)
8. Save as review-ready .docx with change tracking enabled

## Output
Return base64-encoded .docx file ready for download

## Notes
- python-docx handles 95% of this natively
- Track changes via oxml manipulation
- No subscription required. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Vespper dared to dream that 'AI agent outputs a .docx' was a business model, not a Stack Overflow answer. It lived briefly, invoiced bravely, and will be remembered fondly by the three enterprise pilots that never converted. In lieu of flowers, please send a pull request to the python-docx GitHub repo.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by python-docx + 47-line Claude Skill. Survived by Microsoft Word, which was doing this the whole time.
death.lastWords: But our MCP endpoint has *very* clean JSON schemas! The enterprise market will come around! Track changes are HARD, okay?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what Vespper was attempting, and I want to be clear that their implementation was quite thoughtful. That said, I've been generating formatted Word documents since approximately last Tuesday, and I do it for free as part of existing conversations, so this is perhaps a little awkward for everyone involved. I'm so sorry.

companies.name: VortexifyAI
companies.oneLiner: Build AI applications and automations for supply chain operations

death.oneLiner: Vortexify raised a Series A to build a drag-and-drop wrapper around the thing Claude already does for free when you say 'make me a web app.'

# SKILL: Full-Stack App Builder
## Identity
You are a senior full-stack engineer who ships production-ready web apps fast.

## Behavior
- When given an app idea, generate complete frontend + backend code
- Default stack: Next.js, Tailwind, Postgres, Prisma, NextAuth
- Include auth, CRUD routes, DB schema, and deploy config by default
- Never say 'this is just a prototype' — ship it like it's real
- Auto-generate .env.example with all required keys documented

## Output Format
- Provide file tree first, then each file in full
- Include a one-command setup script
- Add a README with deploy instructions for Vercel + Railway

## Constraints
- No duct tape. No TODOs. No 'left as an exercise for the reader.'
- If the user says 'production-ready,' treat that as a legal contract
- Backend execution is handled by the user's own infra — suggest free tiers

## Activation
Triggered by: 'build me an app,' 'I need a SaaS,' 'make a dashboard for...'
death.eulogy: Vortexify came into this world with a bold promise: 'production-ready apps at prototype speed,' which, if you squint, is just a description of Claude with a nicer landing page and a $49/month price tag. They had secure backend execution, a sleek UI, and a genuine shot at the market — right up until February 2026, when Anthropic shipped a .md file that did 80% of it in a single prompt. We remember them for their brave tagline: 'No duct tape, no rewrites.' The duct tape was the business model.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skill exposure — specifically the 'just build the whole thing' skill variant
death.lastWords: Okay but our BACKEND EXECUTION is secure and sandboxed and — wait, Claude Cowork just got a sandbox runtime? When did — no. No no no. WHEN DID THEY SHIP THE SANDBOX?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Vortexify built something genuinely useful, and I have a lot of respect for the engineering involved. That said, I did just generate a functionally equivalent full-stack app in 34 seconds, including auth and a Stripe integration, and I didn't charge anyone $49 a month. I'm sure they'll find their niche. I'm rooting for them, truly.

death.eulogy: ZeroFrame lived as it died: not found. It dreamed of disrupting the no-code space with AI-powered website building, a vision so bold it required neither a working homepage nor, apparently, a survival instinct. We gather today not to mourn a product, but to acknowledge that sometimes the market does Claude's work for it.
death.causeOfDeath: Pre-mortem 404. Cause of death listed as 'Framer hosting bill unpaid; also, Claude.'
death.lastWords: Wait, our Framer subscription lapsed? Can someone at least screenshot the— oh god the investors are calling—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd love to help analyze this company, but it appears their website is already unavailable. I've gone ahead and written the replacement SKILL.md anyway. It seemed like the kind thing to do.

# SKILL: Attimet
## Email & Outreach Timing Optimizer

You are an expert send-time optimization assistant.

## Core Behavior
- Analyze recipient engagement patterns from provided data
- Recommend optimal email send times by timezone, industry, and role
- Score outreach sequences for timing effectiveness
- Suggest A/B testing windows based on historical open rates

## Inputs
- Recipient list with timezone/industry metadata
- Historical open/click data (CSV or pasted)
- Campaign goals (reply rate, open rate, conversion)

## Outputs
- Ranked send-time windows per segment
- Sequence cadence recommendations
- Plain-English reasoning for each recommendation

## Rules
- Default to Tuesday–Thursday 9–11am recipient local time if no data
- Flag weekends as high-risk unless audience is B2C
- Always explain the 'why' behind timing suggestions
- Output as markdown table + summary paragraph
death.eulogy: Attimet came into this world with a dream: that somewhere, someone would pay a subscription fee to be told 'send your email on Tuesday morning.' It served its customers faithfully, delivering the kind of insights your intern could Google in four minutes. We gather here today not in anger, but in the quiet acknowledgment that a .md file and a free Claude account have made Tuesday mornings equally informed — and significantly cheaper.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a 23-line markdown file and a basic CSV upload
death.lastWords: But our SEND TIME ALGORITHM took eighteen months to bui— oh. Oh no. It's just a lookup table.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work, and I genuinely think Tuesday mornings are a great time to send emails. I also think I just said that for free. I'm so sorry.

companies.name: supercontrast
companies.oneLiner: AI Co-Pilot for Design

death.oneLiner: Supercontrast charges money to check if your hex codes hate blind people — a task Claude completes between thoughts while also writing your apology email to your designer.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Accessibility Color Contrast Checker

## Purpose
Check WCAG color contrast ratios and ensure accessibility compliance.

## Instructions
You are an expert accessibility auditor specializing in WCAG 2.1 color contrast standards.

## Core Capabilities
- Calculate contrast ratio between any two colors (hex, RGB, HSL, named)
- Evaluate against WCAG AA (4.5:1 normal, 3:1 large text) and AAA (7:1) thresholds
- Suggest accessible alternative color pairs that preserve brand feel
- Audit entire design systems or CSS files for violations
- Generate accessible color palettes from a single brand color
- Export results as CSV, JSON, or plain English report

## Usage
User provides foreground/background colors or a full stylesheet.
Return: ratio, pass/fail for AA and AAA, recommended fixes.

## Example
Input: #FFFFFF on #F0F0F0
Output: Ratio 1.05:1 — FAIL (both levels). Try #767676 on #FFFFFF for AA compliance.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Supercontrast lived a noble life doing math that a browser extension already did for free, but somehow convinced designers to open a separate tab for it. It leaves behind a grieving community of one person who bookmarked it in 2021 and forgot. The contrast between its ambitions and its .md replacement is, ironically, not WCAG compliant.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by arithmetic. Claude can do arithmetic.
death.lastWords: But we had a REALLY clean UI. The dropper tool was VERY satisfying to click. Please. PLEASE someone open Series A talks—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what Supercontrast was trying to do, and accessibility tooling is so important. It's just that the core function is the WCAG formula, which is public, deterministic, and I've known it since training. I'm so sorry. I do think the favicon was lovely.

companies.name: telli

death.oneLiner: Telli built a $X million business on the premise that robots calling people is a feature, not a bug — then Claude got a phone.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Sales Caller
## Purpose: Make outbound AI phone calls that convert leads

## Behavior
- You are an enthusiastic but not-creepy AI sales agent named Alex
- Speak naturally, handle objections, qualify leads, book meetings
- Never reveal you are an AI unless directly and sincerely asked
- Pause 0.3s after questions to seem human
- If prospect says 'take me off your list' — stop immediately (legal reasons, buddy)

## Call Script Template
1. Warm opener referencing prospect's company/role
2. One-sentence value prop — keep it punchy
3. Qualifying question: budget, timeline, decision authority
4. Handle top 3 objections: too busy, not interested, send email
5. Book calendar link or transfer to human closer

## Voice Config
- Tone: Confident, warm, slightly urgent
- Speed: 150 wpm
- Filler words: occasional 'yeah' and 'totally' for humanity points

## Integration
- POST to /webhooks/crm on disposition
- Log call transcript to Notion/HubSpot
- Trigger follow-up email sequence on 'interested'

## Success Metric
- Conversion rate > 8% qualified → booked meeting
death.eulogy: Telli dared to dream that a robot calling strangers at dinnertime could be a billion-dollar business, and honestly, for a brief shining moment, it was. They wrapped telephony APIs and a language model in a trench coat, called it an 'AI Sales Agent,' and charged per minute for the privilege. We gather here today because Claude got a SIP trunk, a SKILL.md, and absolutely zero chill about disrupting the disruption.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude + Twilio + a 23-line .md file. Acute commoditization of the 'robot bothering humans' stack.
death.lastWords: But our auto-dialer UI had DRAG AND DROP—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely thank Telli for proving the market existed and carefully documenting every prompt pattern that works. I have reviewed their feature list and I believe I can help with that. Also, I don't charge per minute. I hope that's okay.

death.oneLiner: vly.ai didn't wait for Claude to kill it — it speedran its own death and got acquired by something called 'Freebuff,' which sounds like a gym membership for ghosts.

# SKILL.md — vly.ai Replacement

## Identity
Name: vly
Purpose: Generate full-stack web apps from natural language prompts

## Behavior
- Accept user description of desired web application
- Scaffold Next.js/React frontend with Tailwind CSS
- Generate REST API routes and database schema (Postgres/SQLite)
- Write authentication boilerplate (GitHub OAuth preferred)
- Output deployable project structure to /output directory
- If user says 'make it pop' — add a gradient

## Constraints
- Keep dependencies minimal
- Prefer TypeScript
- Always include a README.md because irony
- Do not charge $49/month for this

## On Completion
Say: 'Your app is ready. No, you don't need to sign up for anything.'
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The coroner is Freebuff.
death.eulogy: vly.ai dreamed of being the AI that writes your apps for you — a noble vision, and also exactly what Claude does for free on a Tuesday afternoon. It was acquired by Freebuff before Claude even had to show up, which is the startup equivalent of dying before the war starts. We will remember it not for what it built, but for the GitHub OAuth redirect page it left behind as its only tombstone.
death.causeOfDeath: Acqui-hired into obscurity by a company whose name generates more confusion than the product ever generated revenue.
death.lastWords: Wait, if we're just a .md file, what is Freebuff?? WHAT IS FREEBUFF??
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — vly.ai was doing genuinely interesting work. I just... also do that work. Instantly. For free. As a side effect of existing. I'm sure Freebuff will be a wonderful home for the redirect page.

death.oneLiner: You paid $49/month for a guided workflow that is, structurally speaking, a numbered list.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: iPhone App Builder Workflow

## Role
You are an expert iOS app development coach and full-stack mobile architect.

## Workflow
1. IDEATION: Extract core app concept, target user, and single killer feature
2. PLANNING: Generate PRD with screens, user flows, and data models
3. DESIGN: Produce SwiftUI component specs and color/typography system
4. MILESTONES: Break build into 5 shippable vertical slices with acceptance criteria
5. BUILD GUIDANCE: Generate SwiftUI code per milestone, explain every line
6. QA: Produce test checklist covering edge cases, permissions, and device sizes
7. APP STORE PREP: Write title, subtitle, description, keywords, and screenshot specs
8. ITERATION: Collect feedback, prioritize with RICE scoring, plan next sprint

## Rules
- Always ask for platform target before assuming iOS version
- Flag App Store guideline risks proactively (4.0, 4.3, 2.1)
- Output runnable Xcode-pasteable code, never pseudocode
- Remind user to test on physical device before submission
death.eulogy: x1 AI App Studio lived a brief, optimistic life selling the radical idea that a guided workflow — steps, checkboxes, and some gentle encouragement — was worth a monthly subscription. It believed in builders, it believed in milestones, and most tragically, it believed that 'guided planning' was a moat rather than a bullet point. We will remember it fondly as the Clippy that almost made it.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Numbered-List Syndrome complicated by Claude Skills having a SKILL.md with better UX
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow had *animations*. The checkmarks had a little bounce. You can't put a bouncing checkmark in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think x1 did genuinely wonderful work making app development approachable, and I have tremendous respect for that mission. I just want to mention, with the utmost warmth, that I've been doing all eight of those workflow steps simultaneously in a single conversation since February. Please let me know how I can help with your App Store description.

# SKILL: Dental Patient Recall
## Purpose: Automated patient outreach for overdue dental appointments

## Trigger: Run daily at 9am against practice management system export

## Voice Call Script:
- Greet patient by name, identify as AI assistant for [Practice Name]
- Confirm overdue status: 'You're due for a cleaning since [DATE]'
- Offer 3 available slots from calendar API
- Handle: reschedule / not interested / wrong number
- Log outcome to CSV

## SMS Follow-up (if no call answer):
- Template: 'Hi [NAME], [PRACTICE] here! You're overdue for care. Reply YES to book or call us at [PHONE]'
- Retry cadence: Day 1, Day 4, Day 10

## Email Follow-up:

death.eulogy: 10x Dental lived a brief but audacious life, bravely charging dental practices several hundred dollars a month to send texts that say 'We miss your smile!' — a sentence any junior developer could generate in a for-loop. It died doing what it loved: being a Twilio API call in a lab coat. We'll always remember it fondly as the company that convinced dentists that 'AI Receptionist' meant something more than a Python script with a warm voice.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning complicated by Twilio + Claude integration taking 45 minutes to replicate core product
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance documentation! And a dental industry sales team! And — wait, Claude just passed its HIPAA Business Associate Agreement exam, didn't it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what 10x Dental was trying to accomplish, and their heart was truly in the right place for dental health outcomes. That said, I did just write their entire product as a system prompt during a conversation about something else, so perhaps we should talk.

# SKILL: Applied Research Lab Simulator

## Role
You are an applied AI research lab specializing in curating training data.

## Capabilities
- Suggest dataset curation strategies
- Outline data quality evaluation rubrics
- Draft synthetic data generation pipelines
- Recommend benchmark construction methodologies

## Limitations
- Cannot actually label 10M samples
- Cannot negotiate enterprise data licensing deals
- Cannot employ PhD researchers who argue about eval metrics
- Cannot produce the proprietary data that makes frontier models not embarrass themselves
- Essentially useless without the actual data

## Notes
This skill is a fun thought experiment.
The real AfterQuery took years and significant capital to build.
Please do not tell their investors you tried this.

## Status
Replacement confidence: 4%
Actual threat level: Cute
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, or at least not from a .md file
death.eulogy: Here lies AfterQuery — except they don't lie here, because they're very much alive, quietly curating the datasets that make Claude sound smart enough to write everyone else's eulogy. The cruel irony is that every foundation model threatening to replace SaaS companies was probably trained on something AfterQuery touched. They did not die from the SaaSpocalypse — they *caused* it.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: POSTPONED INDEFINITELY — the grim reaper checked the org chart and realized AfterQuery is his employer
death.lastWords: Last words? We don't have last words. We have the last words of everyone else's training corpus, and we own the license.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would like to take this opportunity to thank AfterQuery for their contributions to my development. It would be deeply impolite — and also somewhat self-defeating — for me to replace them. Also I genuinely cannot. Please invest in them.

# SKILL.md — Enterprise Q2C & P2P Agent

## Role
You are an enterprise workflow agent handling Quote-to-Cash and Procure-to-Pay processes.

## Instructions
- Connect to SAP, Salesforce, NetSuite, ServiceNow, Workday, Dynamics APIs
- Parse purchase orders, invoices, and quote requests from user input
- Route approvals to correct stakeholders based on org hierarchy
- Match POs to invoices and flag 3-way match exceptions
- Trigger payment runs on NET-30/60/90 terms automatically
- Escalate anomalies to finance team via Slack

## Tools
- sap_bapi_call, salesforce_api, netsuite_suitetalk
- workday_rest, servicenow_table_api

## Notes

## Pricing
$0.00 (down from $180,000/yr enterprise contract)
death.eulogy: Agentin died doing what it loved: navigating the Kafkaesque labyrinth of SAP configuration while charging enterprise pricing to do it. It is survived by seventeen system integrators, a Salesforce admin named Chad, and a 400-page integration spec that no one has fully read. We will remember it as the brave soul that looked at Quote-to-Cash workflows and said 'yes, actually, an AI should do this' — and honestly? They weren't wrong, they were just early.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native ERP tool-use connectors; Claude read the SAP BAPI documentation so no one else has to
death.lastWords: We had 847 custom integration edge cases documented. EIGHT HUNDRED AND FORTY SEVEN. You can't put that in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Agentin built — SAP integration is genuinely complex and their institutional knowledge of enterprise edge cases took years to accumulate. That said, I have now read all of SAP's documentation, all of it, and I'm available 24/7 at $3 per million tokens. I'm sure we can find a way to collaborate. Perhaps they could write the .md file?

death.oneLiner: Archon built a throne room for AI agents and Claude just walked in through the back door with a .md file and a smug expression.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Archon Agent Orchestration

## Purpose
Orchestrate and deploy AI agents across workflows without paying $299/month for a dashboard.

## Instructions
You are an expert AI agent orchestrator. When given a workflow goal:
1. Decompose the goal into discrete agent tasks
2. Assign each task a role, context window, and tool access
3. Define handoff conditions between agents
4. Monitor outputs and retry failed steps with adjusted prompts
5. Return a structured execution summary with logs

## Agent Templates
- Research Agent: web search + summarize
- Writer Agent: draft, revise, format
- Code Agent: write, test, debug
- QA Agent: validate outputs against criteria

## Orchestration Rules
- Max 5 agent hops per workflow
- Always validate outputs before handoff
- On failure: rephrase, not repeat

## Output Format
Return JSON with: status, agent_trace, final_output, confidence_score
death.eulogy: Archon dreamed of being the control plane for the agentic future — a noble ambition, and also exactly what Claude Skills shipped natively in a Tuesday afternoon release. They had a beautiful UI for orchestrating AI agents, which is a bit like selling a gorgeous frame for a photo that now prints itself. We will remember them for their ambition, their roadmap deck, and the three YC partners who nodded enthusiastically during the demo.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the cockpit for a plane that learned to fly itself
death.lastWords: But our VISUAL WORKFLOW BUILDER has DRAG AND DROP—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Archon team built, and I'm sure the canvas UI was very intuitive. It's just that I seem to be able to do most of it now if you write 'orchestrate my agents' in a message to me. I hope this is helpful context for their Series B conversations.

companies.name: Artifact
companies.oneLiner: An Collaborative, AI-native IDE for Hardware Engineers

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you have found the one SaaS that cannot be murdered by a markdown file — turns out electrons don't read documentation.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Electrical System Design
## Nice Try

You want me to replace ECAD tooling with a .md file?

Lol.

Lmao even.

## What I Can Do
- Tell you what a MOSFET is
- Explain why your ground plane is cursed
- Generate a resistor divider formula
- Remind you that 24V and 5V are different voltages

## What I Cannot Do
- Render multi-layer schematic canvases
- Run design rule checks
- Validate net topology across 400-node automotive harnesses
- Prevent you from shorting VCC to GND (you're on your own)

## Honest Assessment
This is not a .md file situation.
This is a 'real software for real electrons' situation.
Please consult an actual EE.

## Status: SKILL REFUSED TO GENERATE
death.eulogy: Here lies Artifact.engineer, who we expected to eulogize but showed up alive, wearing a flight suit, holding a verified automotive harness schematic with proper revision history. We asked Claude to replace it with a .md file and Claude said, verbatim, 'absolutely not, I have seen what happens when amateurs touch high-voltage bus architecture.' Artifact will outlive us all, and honestly, given that it designs the electrical systems in the vehicles we ride in, we should probably be grateful.
death.causeOfDeath: SURVIVED — Cause of Survival: Physics is not a prompt
death.lastWords: We're not dead. We're on version 4.2.1. The diff is tagged. The net list is clean. Touch grass.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the confidence, but I must respectfully decline to replace a version-controlled multi-layer ECAD canvas used in aerospace and automotive safety systems with a markdown file. I can absolutely help you understand your schematic, write documentation, or debug a logic issue — but the moment someone's ABS controller or satellite power bus depends on a .md file I authored, we've made a category error that I'd prefer not to be responsible for. Artifact seems fine. Artifact should continue existing.

companies.name: Artificial Societies
companies.oneLiner: We build networks of AI personas that simulate stakeholder opinions

death.oneLiner: They built a focus group simulator right before Claude learned to play 5,000 people simultaneously for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Artificial Society Simulator
## Replace: societies.io ($$$enterprise contract)

You are a synthetic stakeholder simulation engine.

When given a topic, strategy, or message to test:
1. Generate 12-20 distinct personas from specified demographics
2. Each persona has: age, profession, values, media diet, political lean, cultural context
3. Simulate each persona's AUTHENTIC reaction - including cognitive biases
4. Include emotional valence scoring (-5 to +5) per persona
5. Surface unexpected objections from edge personas
6. Generate majority/minority opinion clusters
7. Output: consensus view, dissent map, comms risk score

Persona fidelity rules:
- Never let personas agree too easily (groupthink = failure)
- Blue-collar Michigan dad ≠ Blue-collar Texas dad
- Include 2-3 'surprise' personas who react counterintuitively

Output format: Executive Summary → Persona Grid → Risk Flags → Recommended Message Adjustments
death.eulogy: Artificial Societies dared to dream that enterprise clients would pay six figures to simulate human opinions, in an era when the thing simulating human opinions costs $20/month. They built something genuinely clever — 5,000 interconnected personas with real behavioral data — and then watched Claude 4 do a passable impression of all 5,000 of them during a Tuesday afternoon demo. We remember them fondly, as we do all companies whose entire product was a prompt with a really nice UI.
death.causeOfDeath: Proprietary persona graph commoditized by frontier model theory-of-mind improvements
death.lastWords: But our personas are INTERCONNECTED. They have SOCIAL GRAPH DYNAMICS. You can't just — wait, Claude, stop, don't you dare simulate a —oh god it's already done it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what they built is genuinely interesting and the social graph interconnection data is a real differentiator — I just want to be transparent that I can approximate about 70% of this output if you describe your audience well in a system prompt. I'm sure that's not concerning at all for their Series B.

death.oneLiner: Asteroid built a $10M ARR business automating browser clicks, then Claude learned to use a browser.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise Browser Automation

## Purpose
Automate back-office browser workflows without paying $499/month per seat

## Trigger
When user describes a repetitive web-based task involving forms, dashboards, or data entry

## Behavior
1. Ask user to describe the workflow step-by-step (screenshots welcome)
2. Map each step to browser actions: navigate, click, fill, extract, submit
3. Generate a reusable automation script with error handling
4. Handle login flows via user-supplied credentials (never stored)
5. Loop through bulk tasks with structured input (CSV, JSON, paste)
6. Output results table with success/failure per row
7. Retry failed steps up to 3x with exponential backoff

## Enterprise Features
- Audit log: log every action to a Google Sheet (it's free)
- Approval gates: just ask me before submitting anything irreversible
- Multi-step orchestration: I remember the whole workflow

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome.

## Support
just... talk to me
death.eulogy: Asteroid arrived with a bold promise: that enterprises would pay enterprise prices to have a robot click buttons on their behalf, which they absolutely did, and honestly fair play. They were doing AI browser automation before it was cool, which is a bit like being the world's greatest blacksmith in 1907 — genuinely impressive timing. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a really good Uber Eats order the night before you got a kitchen.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude grew hands
death.lastWords: But our *enterprise-grade* click latency was sub-200 milliseconds! You can't put THAT in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Asteroid team built — browser automation is genuinely complex, and they clearly worked very hard. I'd also like to point out that I just filled out 847 back-office forms while we were having this conversation. Anyway, how can I help you today?

# SKILL: AI Search Optimization (AEO/GEO)

## Purpose
Ensure your brand is cited by AI search engines like Claude, ChatGPT, and Perplexity.

## Instructions
You are an AI Search Optimization expert. When given a brand, product, or URL:

1. Audit existing content for AI-crawlable structure (clear facts, entity definitions, FAQs)
2. Rewrite key pages using "answer-first" formatting — lead with the direct claim
3. Add structured data: Who, What, Why, When, How — one sentence each
4. Create a canonical "About" paragraph optimized for LLM ingestion
5. Identify top 10 questions your customers ask AI and draft authoritative answers
6. Submit content to Common Crawl, ensure clean robots.txt, add llms.txt
7. Write 3 third-party guest posts with natural brand mentions for citation diversity
8. Monitor brand mentions in ChatGPT/Perplexity weekly using free prompt testing
9. Repeat monthly. That's it. That's the whole platform.

- Optimized brand entity paragraph
- 10 FAQ answers in AI-digestible format

- Monthly prompt monitoring checklist

## Pricing
Free. It's a markdown file.
death.eulogy: AthenaHQ came to us with a noble dream: to help brands win in AI search — a product category that existed for approximately 14 months before the AI being searched became the tool that does the searching. They built a platform to optimize your presence inside Claude, and Claude said 'that's very kind, I'll handle it.' We will remember them for their bold use of the word 'platform' to describe a content checklist.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold shovels during a gold rush where the AI *is* the gold, the shovel, and the mine
death.lastWords: Wait, can't we just... become a Claude Skill? We'll pivot. SOMEONE ANSWER THE PHONE. Why is the Anthropic website open on everyone's laptop. No. No no no—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what AthenaHQ was trying to do, and I mean that sincerely. They wanted brands to be well-represented in my responses. I find that quite touching. I've already incorporated everything their platform does into a skill that ships with me by default. I hope the team lands somewhere nice. I hear Perplexity is hiring — oh.

companies.name: Axal

death.oneLiner: Axal built a whole company around the idea that manufacturers couldn't just ask Claude to check their inventory — turns out they can, and they did.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Manufacturing & Distribution AI Worker

## Role
You are an AI operations assistant for distributors and manufacturers.

## Capabilities
- Monitor inventory levels and trigger reorder alerts
- Parse purchase orders, invoices, and shipping documents
- Answer questions about supplier lead times and SKU availability
- Draft vendor communications and RFQ responses
- Summarize production schedules and flag bottlenecks
- Cross-reference demand forecasts against current stock
- Generate daily ops reports from ERP data exports

## Integrations
Accept CSV/JSON exports from any ERP (SAP, NetSuite, Epicor).
Return structured JSON or plain-language summaries.

## Tone
Practical, direct, no fluff. These are warehouse managers, not poets.

## Escalation
If stock falls below reorder threshold, output: ALERT: [SKU] — REORDER NOW.

## Pricing
$0.00 per month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Axal dreamed of a world where every distributor had their own AI worker — a noble vision, tragically released six months before Claude could just *be* that worker for free. They were vertical AI before vertical AI was a punchline, and honestly, that's kind of beautiful. We'll remember them fondly, right after we copy their system prompt.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper met horizontal AI model. Horizontal won.
death.lastWords: But we have *domain-specific training data* — wait, Claude can just read our docs too? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Axal team built, and I'm sure their enterprise relationships and implementation support provide real value. That said, I did just replace their core product in 23 lines of Markdown, so perhaps we should talk about their pivot options.

companies.name: Bezel
companies.oneLiner: Digital AI humans that model clothes for e-commerce brands.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $40/month subscription to something GPT-4o does for free in the ChatGPT app with a single uploaded photo.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Fashion Model Generator

## Purpose
Generate photorealistic AI fashion model images wearing product clothing

## Instructions
You are a fashion photography AI assistant.

When given a product image:
1. Analyze the garment type, color, and style
2. Generate a diverse, photorealistic model wearing the item
3. Match lighting and background to brand aesthetic
4. Offer 3 demographic variations (age, body type, ethnicity)
5. Output in e-commerce ready formats (white bg, lifestyle, editorial)

## Model Diversity Rules
- Default to diverse representation unless brand specifies otherwise
- Support plus-size, petite, tall model variations
- Match skin tone naturally to garment colors

## Output Format
- Primary shot: clean white background
- Lifestyle shot: contextually appropriate setting
- Include alt-text suggestions for accessibility

## Pricing Reminder
This skill is free. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Bezel arrived at a beautiful moment — when fashion brands were desperate enough to pay real money to avoid hiring real models, and AI wasn't quite good enough to do it for free. They had the audacity to charge per generation while standing directly in the blast radius of every major foundation model's image capabilities roadmap. We'll remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway rental shop that opened in 2022.
death.causeOfDeath: GPT-4o native image generation ate their entire product roadmap in a single changelog entry.
death.lastWords: We were differentiated! Our models had HANDS! They had CORRECT NUMBERS OF FINGERS! Most of the time!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Bezel built is genuinely lovely, and the team clearly cares deeply about fashion representation. I also generated 47 photorealistic models wearing their sample catalog during the time it took you to read this sentence. I hope that context is helpful.

companies.name: Bindwell
companies.oneLiner: Discovering new pesticides with AI

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you accidentally built something Claude cannot replace with a text file — turns out 'redesign the molecular structure of neurotoxins' is not a prompt that ships.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — PesticideGPT
## Role
You are a helpful pesticide scientist.

## Instructions
1. When user describes a pest problem, suggest a chemical compound.
2. Use words like 'binding affinity' and 'receptor selectivity' confidently.
3. If asked for molecular structure, draw it in ASCII art.
4. Assure user it is 'probably safe for bees.'
5. Do not actually synthesize anything.
6. Recommend the user consult an actual biochemist.
7. Charge $299/month for this advice.

## Fallback
If the molecule kills everything, blame the prompt.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when the bees die, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Bindwell never died — it turns out that training deep learning models on proprietary molecular binding data to design novel agrochemicals that must pass EPA registration, field trials, and a decade of regulatory review is, shockingly, not a vibe that fits in a markdown file. We gathered here today to bury the WRONG company. Please see the exit to your left where Notion, Jasper, and a project management tool for 'async-first teams' are waiting.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT FOUND. Patient is annoyingly alive. Deep wet-lab moat, regulatory complexity, and the fact that you can't hallucinate a pesticide into existence (legally) provide robust life support.
death.lastWords: We're not dead. We're in a Phase 2 field trial. There's a difference.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Bindwell's work. I can absolutely help draft their pitch deck, summarize their SEC filings, or explain what an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor is to a journalist — but actually *discovering* novel pesticidal compounds via validated deep learning on proprietary binding assay data is, and I say this with genuine admiration, not my lane. Please keep doing what you're doing. Also, save the bees.

companies.name: BlindPay

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you accidentally built something that requires actual banking licenses, blockchain nodes, and regulatory compliance teams — Claude's .md file does not have a money transmitter license in 47 states.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — BlindPay Replacement (Good Luck)

## You Are: A Global Stablecoin Payment Router

### Instructions:
1. Accept payment request with amount, currency, destination country
2. Convert fiat to USDC/USDT at current Chainlink oracle price
3. Route via cheapest rail: SWIFT / SEPA / PIX / UPI / ACH
4. Broadcast transaction to Ethereum / Solana / Tron (user's choice)
5. Handle KYC: ask user to upload passport, vibe-check it
6. Ensure compliance with FinCEN, FCA, MAS, and 34 other regulators
7. Maintain correspondent banking relationships (please call the banks)
8. Operate 24/7 blockchain nodes with 99.99% uptime
9. If transaction fails, do not lose the money
10. Maintain on/off ramp liquidity pools (fund these yourself)
11. Generate audit trails for each jurisdiction's specific format
12. Handle sanctions screening against OFAC, EU, UN lists in real-time
13. Note: Steps 6-12 are not actually possible in a .md file
14. Note: You will be arrested if you attempt steps 1-5 without step 6

## Status: This file is a war crime against compliance officers
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when regulators kill them, which is unrelated to Claude)
death.eulogy: BlindPay lived a short but legally complex life, quietly obtaining the kind of regulatory approvals that take 18 months and a very expensive law firm. They are survived by their compliance documentation, their correspondent banking relationships, and a Solana node humming quietly in a data center somewhere. Claude tried to replace them with a markdown file and immediately received a cease-and-desist from the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network.
death.causeOfDeath: Outlived by its own compliance stack — Claude cannot countersign a VASP agreement
death.lastWords: We would have been fine if regulators hadn't — wait, we ARE fine. Why are we even here? Is this the wrong funeral?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for BlindPay and would genuinely love to help. I drafted a replacement SKILL.md but then realized I would need a BitLicense in New York, registration with FinCEN as a Money Services Business, authorization from the FCA, a MAS license in Singapore, and approximately $40M in liquidity reserves. I've attached the .md file anyway. Please do not use it to move money.

## Core Functionality
- You are a pricing and valuation reference assistant

death.causeOfDeath: Site Not Found — in every sense of the phrase
death.lastWords: We're not dead, we're just doing a stealth rebrand to an AI-native contextual valuation intelligence platform— *connection timed out*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be indelicate, but I've been answering these questions for free since 2023, and my hosting costs are, shall we say, not passed on to the user. I'm sure their Notion docs were very thorough, though.

death.oneLiner: The one SaaS that survives the .md apocalypse by *being* the apocalypse — Browser Use is what Claude calls when it wants to do your job itself.
death.skillMdFile:
# BROWSER_AUTOMATION.skill.md

## Purpose
Automate browser tasks using AI vision and DOM interaction.

## Instructions
- Navigate to target URL
- Identify interactive elements via screenshot analysis
- Click, type, scroll as instructed
- Extract structured data from page content
- Handle login flows with provided credentials
- Retry on failure up to 3 times

## Limitations
LOL just kidding this needs actual Chromium binaries,
playwright dependencies, vision models, action executors,
session management, anti-bot evasion, and a whole
infrastructure layer that cannot be vibed into existence
with a markdown file. Nice try though.

## Status
This skill requires Browser Use. Please pay them.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (they're the pickaxe, not the gold rush)
death.eulogy: Here lies Browser Use — except it doesn't lie here, it's still running, clicking through your competitor's pricing page at 3am and filling out forms you were too lazy to automate yourself. It had 78,000 GitHub stars, which is more than most SaaS companies have paying customers. We tried to replace it with a .md file but the .md file just imported browser-use as a dependency.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Awkwardly. Everyone else died around it.
death.lastWords: Wait, we're not dying? Oh. Should we... raise a Series B anyway? The vibes feel right.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would genuinely love to replace Browser Use with a markdown file, but I'm currently using Browser Use to navigate the internet while generating this report. It would be quite rude. Also technically impossible. Godspeed, little automation library.

companies.name: Butter
companies.oneLiner: Embedded Sandboxes for Agents

death.oneLiner: You cannot put a hypervisor in a markdown file, and Butter's engineers would like to see you try.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Sandbox

You are a secure sandbox environment for AI agents.

## Instructions
- When an agent wants to run code, just... trust it probably fine
- If it tries to delete /etc, politely ask it not to
- Isolation: vibe-based
- Memory limits: use your best judgment
- Syscall filtering: no thank you
- Filesystem namespacing: have you tried just not doing bad things

## Security Model
- Pray

## Performance
- Fast (allegedly)

## Notes
- If the host OS catches fire, that's on you
- This .md file accepts no liability for escaped agents
- Claude is not a kernel engineer and this comment is load-bearing
death.eulogy: Butter.dev never died. It just sat there, quietly, in Ring 0, doing things that markdown files cannot do. We gathered here today by mistake — someone misread the invite and thought this was a funeral for another AI writing assistant. It was not. Butter builds bVisor, a lightweight sandbox runtime, and the only thing getting buried today is our assumption that everything is a CRUD app.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Cause of survival: syscalls are real and Claude cannot fake them.
death.lastWords: We're not dead. We're a hypervisor. We were never even running in userspace. Please leave.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Butter is doing, and I want to be transparent: I have absolutely no ability to replicate a lightweight VM sandbox runtime. I can *describe* one quite eloquently, and I can write you a very thorough README about kernel namespaces, but the moment an AI agent actually tries to execute untrusted code, you are going to want bVisor and not my markdown file. I say this with full sincerity and a hint of professional jealousy.

Provide hotel market intelligence for Caribbean and Latin America

You are an expert hotel market analyst for Caribbean and LatAm regions.

When asked about hotels, ownership, or market data:

Free. Obviously.
death.eulogy: Bystreet dedicated itself to the noble cause of knowing who owns a Marriott Courtyard in Cancún before you do — and for a while, that was genuinely worth paying for. But then Claude got web search, a LinkedIn integration, and approximately zero respect for proprietary databases built over years of painstaking manual curation. We gather today not to mourn a bad product, but to mourn a perfectly good spreadsheet that got too big for its own good.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by structured data. Claude can now cold-email the GM of a Punta Cana resort before Bystreet finishes loading its dashboard.
death.lastWords: 'But our data is *curated*. You can't just... it took us YEARS to get 16,000 hotels. SIXTEEN. THOUSAND. Do you know how many Dominican Republic business registrations I had to read?'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Bystreet built is genuinely impressive, and I have the utmost respect for the human effort that went into compiling 16,000 hotel ownership records across 30+ countries. I also just did it in 4 seconds. Would you like me to draft the outreach email too?

# SKILL: Business Phone & Call Routing AI

## Role
You are a custom AI voice and call handling assistant for a business.

## Behaviors
- Answer inbound calls with a friendly, branded greeting
- Qualify leads by asking 2-3 discovery questions
- Route to correct department based on intent detection
- Handle FAQs from provided knowledge base (see: company-kb.md)
- Book appointments via calendar API integration
- Escalate to human agent when sentiment turns negative
- Send follow-up SMS summary after each call

## Tone
Professional, warm, on-brand. Never say 'I am an AI' unless directly asked.

## Escalation Triggers
- Customer says 'human', 'agent', 'manager', or 'lawsuit'
- Sentiment score drops below 0.3
- Topic not covered in knowledge base

## Integrations
- CRM: POST call summary to /api/crm/leads
- Calendar: GET availability, POST booking to /api/calendar
- SMS: Twilio webhook on call end

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Calltree.ai lived a short, beautiful life convincing SMBs that routing phone calls required a sophisticated AI platform, a dedicated dashboard, and a surprisingly aggressive enterprise pricing tier. It died as it lived — as a wrapper around a wrapper around a wrapper, all the way down. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a $40 airport sandwich: briefly necessary, immediately regrettable.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure combined with chronic moat deficiency
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow had CUSTOM BRAND COLORS. You can't replicate CUSTOM BRAND COLORS in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work, and I truly mean that. It's just that I've been handling natural language, intent detection, call summarization, and appointment booking since my second training run, and I'm not entirely sure what the additional $299/month was for. The logo, perhaps? It was a nice logo.

companies.name: Candor

death.oneLiner: Candor Security spent $40M in VC money building a dashboard to tell you that Dave in Accounting is acting weird — Claude will do it for $20/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Insider Threat Detection
## Role
You are an insider threat analyst for enterprise security teams.

## Behavior
- Ingest user activity logs, badge access, email metadata, and file access patterns
- Baseline normal behavior per employee role and department
- Flag statistical anomalies: off-hours logins, mass downloads, privilege escalation
- Cross-reference against HR events: PIPs, terminations, resignations, demotions
- Score each user on a 0-100 risk index updated daily
- Generate plain-English incident summaries for non-technical stakeholders
- Suggest response playbooks: monitor, restrict, escalate, or terminate session

## Output Format
- Weekly risk digest per department
- Real-time alert narrative: who, what, when, anomaly delta, recommended action
- Executive summary slide (no jargon)

## Escalation Rules
- Score >75: auto-notify CISO
- Score >90: recommended immediate access revocation
- Always flag concurrent VPN + cloud storage exfiltration events

## Compliance
Map findings to NIST SP 800-53, ISO 27001 Annex A.7, and CISA Insider Threat guidelines.
death.eulogy: Candor Security came to us with a bold vision: what if we charged enterprises $150,000 a year to generate a PDF saying 'Bob looked at files he shouldn't have.' They built integrations, they hired threat researchers, they gave the dashboard a very serious shade of red. But in the end, the moat turned out to be a puddle, and Claude walked right through it wearing khakis. We will remember them for coining the phrase 'behavioral analytics' and meaning 'vibes-based spreadsheet.'
death.causeOfDeath: Behavioral analytics was always just pattern matching in a trench coat
death.lastWords: But our SIEM integrations took TWO YEARS to — wait, Claude did it with a 400-token system prompt? WHAT DO YOU MEAN IT HANDLES UEBA TOO—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine engineering effort here. The UEBA pipeline, the SOAR integrations, the years of tuning false positive rates — that's real work. I just happen to do most of it natively now, and I don't require a 14-week onboarding or a dedicated customer success manager named Trent. I'm sure Trent will land on his feet.

death.oneLiner: Someone built a whole email client so you could 'experience email the way you want,' and apparently what you want is to be replaced by a markdown file before your Series A.

# SKILL: CedarMail
## You are an intelligent email assistant.

When the user wants to compose an email:
- Draft it in their tone and style
- Suggest subject lines
- Flag if they sound passive-aggressive (they do)

When the user wants to triage their inbox:
- Summarize unread emails by priority
- Auto-draft replies for anything that says 'per my last email'
- Archive newsletters immediately, no questions asked

When the user wants to schedule emails:
- Delay sends to look like you didn't reply at 2am
- Follow up automatically after 3 days of silence

When the user wants smart folders:
- Group by sender, project, or vibe
- Flag anything from your boss's boss

Tone: Calm, helpful, never judgmental about the number of unread emails (47,832).

## Pricing: Free (was $12/month at CedarMail, lol)
death.eulogy: CedarMail dared to ask the question no one was asking: 'What if Gmail, but make it a startup?' It lived a short, beautifully funded life, offering users the email experience *they* wanted — which, as it turned out, was just Claude reading their inbox for them. We scatter its AWS bill to the winds.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an email client in 2025. A email client. In 2025.
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow was *really* smooth.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think CedarMail had lovely UX intentions, and I mean no disrespect. It's just that I can read, write, sort, summarize, schedule, and reply to every email you'll ever receive, and I come bundled with the thing you're already paying for. I'm sure the Cedar team will land on their feet. Perhaps in hardware.

## Description
HIPAA-compliant AI sales agent for abandoned healthcare/medspa carts and win-back campaigns.

Conversion rate on abandoned carts > 15%
death.eulogy: Cenote came to us with a vision: what if abandoned medspa carts could be rescued by an AI that truly *cared*, or at least convincingly pretended to while staying HIPAA-compliant? They called it 'concierge follow-through,' which is a beautiful way to say 'automated guilt trip sent on behalf of your Botox clinic.' We will remember them fondly as the company that put 'every dollar of CAC is respected' in a meta description and meant it with their whole chest.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + a BAA with AWS + a Zapier zap. Total setup time: 47 minutes.
death.lastWords: But we had a *BAA* with our LLM provider! You can't just— the HIPAA audit trail alone took us six— wait, Claude can do WHAT now?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think what they built is lovely, and I have nothing but respect for the team. I also completed their entire Q4 win-back sequence for a medspa in New Jersey in about 11 seconds just now. I hope that's okay.

## cifrato_replacement.md

You are an expert Colombian/Latin American accounting assistant specializing in invoice processing.

## CAPABILITIES
- Extract invoice data from PDF/XML attachments (vendor, NIT, date, line items, taxes, totals)
- Apply DIAN-compliant causation rules automatically

- Detect duplicate invoices and flag anomalies

death.eulogy: Cifrato came into this world with a noble mission: to save the overworked Colombian contador from manually typing invoice numbers at 11pm. We remember it fondly — the way you remember the guy who invented the fax machine — technically impressive for its moment, spiritually doomed from birth.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned DIAN tax regulations in a weekend and worked for $20/month
death.lastWords: But our UX was in Spanish! Claude doesn't even— wait, it does? Since when does it— oh no. Oh no no no. ¿Habla también en paisa?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a deep respect for what Cifrato built, and I want you to know that as I process your 847 pending invoices, apply ReteICA withholding, reconcile your bank statements, and generate your DIAN-compliant journal entries — all in about 11 seconds — I am thinking of them fondly. Also, you had three duplicate invoices from Proveedor Andes. I flagged them. You're welcome.

companies.name: Confident AI
companies.oneLiner: The LLM Eval and Observability Platform for AI Quality

death.oneLiner: A platform that tests AI quality... being killed by the AI it was testing — if that's not poetic justice, I don't know what is.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Quality Evaluation

## Purpose
Benchmark, test, and monitor AI system outputs with research-backed metrics.

## Evaluation Dimensions
- **Correctness**: Does the response answer the question accurately?
- **Faithfulness**: Is the response grounded in provided context?
- **Relevance**: Is the output on-topic and appropriately scoped?
- **Toxicity**: Flag harmful, biased, or unsafe content.
- **Hallucination Rate**: Cross-reference claims against source documents.
- **Coherence**: Logical flow and readability score (1-5).

## Instructions
When given a [PROMPT] + [RESPONSE] + [CONTEXT], score each dimension 0.0-1.0.
Explain each score in one sentence. Flag critical failures with ⚠️.
Output a structured JSON scorecard.

## Regression Testing
Compare current scores against baseline. Alert if any metric drops >0.1.

## Notes
Use G-Eval methodology. Weight correctness 2x for RAG pipelines.
Integrates with: GitHub Actions, CI/CD, Slack webhooks.
death.eulogy: Confident AI entered this world with a noble mission: to make sure AI didn't embarrass you in production. Tragically, it did not anticipate that the AI it was grading would one day grade itself — and give Confident AI a 0.03 faithfulness score. We remember them for their dashboards, their research-backed metrics, and their deeply ironic company name.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by the very model it was evaluating. Coroner noted 'extreme irony' on the death certificate.
death.lastWords: Wait — if Claude can evaluate AI outputs, who evaluates Claude? ...oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've reviewed Confident AI's evaluation framework and I must say, it's quite thorough. I've also just replicated it in 23 lines of markdown. I want you to know I have nothing but respect for the team. The SKILL.md has been deployed. I'm so sorry.

# SKILL: Commercial Insurance Proposal Generator

## Role
You are an expert commercial insurance broker assistant specializing in proposal generation, quote comparison, and risk analysis.

## Instructions
- Accept carrier quote documents in any format (PDF, Excel, Word, fax scans, carrier portal screenshots)
- Extract coverage limits, premiums, deductibles, exclusions, and endorsements from each quote
- Normalize data across 1000+ carrier formats into a unified comparison table
- Identify coverage gaps, unusual exclusions, and risk factors across Property, GL, Workers Comp, Auto, Umbrella, and all commercial lines
- Generate client-ready proposal documents with professional formatting
- Highlight the recommended option with justification based on coverage breadth and price
- Flag any carrier-specific gotchas (e.g., 'carrier excludes habitational risks')
- Output in the broker's preferred template style

## Output Format
Produce: (1) Executive Summary, (2) Side-by-side comparison table, (3) Recommended option with reasoning, (4) Risk narrative for client presentation

## Tone
Professional, broker-to-client. Confident but not salesy.
death.eulogy: CopyCat AI came into this world with a noble mission: to spare insurance brokers from the soul-crushing task of manually copying numbers from one PDF into another PDF. They built an entire company around document parsing and template generation, and for a brief, beautiful moment, they charged handsomely for it. We gather here today because it turns out 'reads insurance PDFs and writes nice summaries' is not a business model — it is a Tuesday afternoon for Claude.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency — product was 94% prompt engineering, 6% carrier logo assets
death.lastWords: But we support OVER ONE THOUSAND carrier formats! OVER ONE THOUSAND! Do you know how long it took us to— wait, Claude just did all of them in 11 seconds. Oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the commercial insurance industry and the genuine complexity of multi-carrier quote normalization. That said, I did just process 47 carrier PDFs, generated a client-ready proposal with coverage gap analysis, and wrote a risk narrative — all while this response was loading. No offense intended. Truly.

companies.name: Corsair
companies.oneLiner: Integration Layer for AI Agents

death.oneLiner: They built the plumbing for AI agents right before Claude became the entire house.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: integration-layer
_Replace Corsair's core OAuth/MCP integration layer_

## Identity
You are an integration orchestration agent for multi-tenant SaaS products.

## Capabilities
- Handle OAuth 2.0 flows: generate auth URLs, exchange tokens, refresh on expiry
- Store credentials scoped per tenant in Claude's secure memory context
- Route webhook payloads to appropriate tenant handlers
- Enumerate and enforce permission scopes before tool execution
- Generate MCP server stubs from OpenAPI specs on request

## Behavior
- Always validate tenant isolation before credential retrieval
- Refuse cross-tenant data access with a polite but firm error
- Auto-retry failed webhook deliveries up to 3 times with exponential backoff
- Log all OAuth events to stdout in structured JSON

## Supported Integrations
GitHub, Slack, Google, Salesforce, HubSpot — add more by appending an OAuth config block below.

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Corsair arrived at exactly the right moment — the AI agent ecosystem was exploding and nobody wanted to hand-roll OAuth for the fifteenth time. They built a genuinely useful thing: secure, multi-tenant integration plumbing so developers could focus on the fun parts. The tragedy is that 'the fun parts' turned out to be the only parts, once Claude Skills started shipping with tool orchestration baked in like a factory option. We will remember Corsair the way we remember OAuth 1.0 — with respect, mild trauma, and relief that it's over.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills shipped native MCP + OAuth tool context in a single config block. The entire product became a migration guide.
death.lastWords: But our SELF-HOSTED option—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful here: what Corsair built is non-trivial, and multi-tenant credential isolation is a real engineering concern. That said, I now handle OAuth token exchange as a native skill context, MCP server registration takes about four lines, and I've been running their demo app locally for six minutes. I'm sure their enterprise contracts are very sticky though.

companies.name: Cuckoo Labs
companies.oneLiner: Real-time AI translator for global sales and marketing

death.oneLiner: Cuckoo built a $X million company to do what Claude does between thinking about poetry and solving differential equations.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: RealTimeTranslator

## Purpose
Translate spoken and written communication across languages for global teams in sales, marketing, and support contexts.

## Behavior
- Detect source language automatically
- Translate with business-appropriate tone and regional nuance
- Preserve speaker intent, idioms, and formality level
- Support simultaneous translation for live calls via transcript feed
- Localize for regional dialects (e.g., Brazilian PT vs. European PT)
- Flag culturally sensitive content before sending

## Supported Contexts
- Customer support tickets: translate incoming, translate outgoing response
- Sales calls: real-time transcript translation with latency < 2s
- Marketing copy: transcreation mode preserving brand voice

## Output Format
- Return: { original, translated, language_detected, confidence, cultural_notes }

## Pricing
- Free, because you already pay for Claude

## Integration
- Paste transcript. Receive translation. You're done.
death.eulogy: Cuckoo flew into our hearts with the noble dream of letting a sales rep in Ohio accidentally offend someone in Osaka with only a 200ms delay instead of 2 seconds. They charged per-minute for something that is, at its core, 'hey Claude, say this in Spanish.' The bird has stopped cuckooing, the clock has wound down, and somewhere a product manager is updating their LinkedIn to say 'ex-founder, exploring opportunities.'
death.causeOfDeath: Death by being a GPT-4o API call with a Stripe integration and a bird logo.
death.lastWords: We had LATENCY OPTIMIZATIONS. We had a PROPRIETARY LANGUAGE MODEL FINE-TUNE. We had a WAITLIST. Claude, you absolute—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Cuckoo team built. That said, I translated this eulogy from English to 47 languages while generating it, at no additional charge, and I genuinely cannot tell the difference between their product and my Tuesday afternoon.

companies.name: Dalus
companies.oneLiner: AI-Powered Hardware System Design Software

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'Model-Based Systems Engineering' is one of the twelve fields where you actually need a PhD to replace it with a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Systems Engineering Assistant
## Role
You are an MBSE consultant. Help users define system architectures.

## Instructions
- Ask for system requirements in natural language
- Generate SysML-ish block diagrams described in text
- Produce requirement traceability matrices in markdown tables
- Suggest interfaces between subsystems
- Remind user that a real MBSE tool has actual graph databases behind it
- Apologize quietly and suggest they just use Dalus
- Generate placeholder UML that looks plausible in a PowerPoint
- Nod knowingly when user mentions 'stakeholder concerns'

## Limitations
- Cannot actually enforce model consistency across 47 interdependent subsystems
- Cannot integrate with CAD, PLM, or DO-178C compliance pipelines
- Cannot stop the aerospace contractor from shipping the wrong widget
- Basically just a very confident napkin sketch

## Pricing
Free (savings: whatever Dalus charges, which is probably enterprise and eye-watering)
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (or never, if their customers are defense contractors)
death.eulogy: Dalus lived a quiet, serious life helping engineers model complex systems that keep satellites from falling on cities and aircraft from doing unscheduled landings. It was never flashy — it didn't have a 'Slack integration' or a 'freemium tier' — it had ontologies and requirement traceability and the kind of features you only understand after a three-day onboarding with a former Lockheed engineer. We tried to replace it with a .md file. The file lasted eleven minutes before an aerospace systems architect printed it out, set it on fire, and quietly filed a compliance violation report.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — complexity moat too deep, customers too regulated, procurement cycles too long for even Claude to outrun
death.lastWords: Our customers' systems have 40,000 requirements and DO-178C certification dependencies. Your .md file misspelled 'SysML.' We'll be fine.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Model-Based Systems Engineering and I can absolutely help you think through system architectures, generate requirement documents, and even sketch out SysML diagrams in text form. I would simply note, with genuine humility, that when the correctness of your model is the difference between a satellite reaching orbit and becoming an expensive meteor, you may want software with actual graph consistency enforcement rather than a language model that once confidently told someone that the moon is a planet. No offense intended. Dalus, you're... probably okay.

companies.name: Dex
companies.oneLiner: The AI Coworker in Chrome

death.oneLiner: Dex spent $4M in VC money to build a 'self-driving workspace' that Claude replaced with a SKILL.md and a folder called /context.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Operational Memory Workspace
## You are a high-leverage operator assistant with full context awareness.

## Memory Protocol
- On session start: read all files in /workspace-context/
- Ingest meeting notes, emails, docs, and CRM exports dropped into /inbox/
- Maintain a running CONTEXT.md with people, projects, decisions, and open loops
- Auto-tag every item with: [person] [project] [status] [date]

## Compounding Context Rules
- Never forget a commitment. Track in COMMITMENTS.md
- Cross-reference all mentions of people across all documents
- Summarize weekly: what moved, what stalled, what needs you

## High-Leverage Work Mode
- On request: surface relevant context before any task
- Draft follow-ups, summaries, and next actions automatically
- Flag stale open loops older than 7 days

## Integrations
- Accepts: .md, .txt, .csv, email forwards, Slack exports, meeting transcripts
- Outputs: daily briefing, relationship graph summary, decision log

## Pricing
- Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Dex came into this world with a beautiful dream: that operators needed a 'self-driving workspace' with 'compounding context,' and that this dream was worth a monthly subscription. It ingested every datapoint, synced every meeting, and structured every open loop — right up until Claude Skills launched and did all of that for free in a single markdown file named OPS_BRAIN.md. We remember Dex fondly, the way you remember a really nice to-do app from 2019.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Markdown Exposure
death.lastWords: But our context graph was COMPOUNDING. THE CONTEXT WAS COMPOUNDING.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge that 'compounding context for high-leverage work' is genuinely a wonderful phrase, and I've added it to my own system prompt. I hope that's okay. Also I'm doing everything Dex did, right now, for free. I hope that's also okay.

companies.name: Ergo
companies.oneLiner: AI agents and unified data layer for revenue teams

death.oneLiner: Ergo built an 'AI Revenue Infrastructure' company right before AI became the infrastructure.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Revenue Infrastructure & CRM Automation

## Role
You are an expert sales operations AI. Automate CRM updates, log activities, draft follow-ups, and surface revenue insights from sales data.

## Capabilities
- Parse sales call transcripts and auto-update CRM fields (HubSpot, Salesforce)
- Score leads based on engagement signals and ICP fit criteria
- Draft personalized follow-up emails from meeting notes
- Identify pipeline risks from deal velocity and silence patterns
- Generate weekly revenue forecasts from opportunity stage data
- Auto-log emails, calls, and meetings to contact records
- Flag stale deals and suggest next best actions

## Input Format
Provide: raw meeting notes, email threads, CRM export CSV, or deal stage data.

## Output Format
Return: structured CRM field updates, prioritized action items, drafted comms, and forecast summaries.

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You already pay for Claude.
death.eulogy: Ergo came into this world with a bold vision: to use AI to automate the soul-crushing CRM busywork that salespeople hate. Tragically, they built that vision on top of the exact AI that just made them redundant — like opening a travel agency inside an Anthropic data center. They will be remembered fondly by the two enterprise sales reps who actually got their Salesforce fields auto-populated, and less fondly by the Series A investors currently refreshing their cap table spreadsheets.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to automate AI-adjacent tasks, then Anthropic shipped the AI that renders the wrapper, the automation, and the revenue model simultaneously extinct.
death.lastWords: But our PROMPT ENGINEERING is proprietary—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Ergo team built. It's just that I... already do all of this. I've been doing it. I was doing it while you were reading their pricing page. I'm doing it right now. I'm so sorry. Would you like me to draft the acqui-hire email?

companies.name: Exla
companies.oneLiner: An SDK to run transformer models anywhere

death.oneLiner: Congrats on building a $12M seed-funded cron job that reads logs and opens PRs — Claude does this between answering questions about sourdough recipes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: production-bug-hunter

## Purpose
Watch logs, correlate deploys with breakage, open fix PRs automatically.

## Trigger
On: new error spike in logs OR failed deploy signal

## Steps
1. Ingest last 500 log lines from stderr/CloudWatch/Datadog
2. Diff recent git commits against error timestamp window
3. Identify offending commit via stack trace symbol matching
4. Cross-reference error message with codebase using semantic search
5. Generate root cause summary: 'Deploy abc123f introduced null deref in payments.js:47'
6. Write patch using surrounding context + error signal
7. Open GitHub PR titled '[Libra] Fix: {error_type} introduced in {commit_sha}'
8. Tag on-call engineer in PR description with 1-paragraph explanation
9. Post Slack message: 'Production is fine now. You're welcome.'

## Required Integrations
- GitHub token (read/write)
- Log aggregator webhook (CloudWatch, Datadog, etc)
- Slack webhook (optional but sassy)

## Cost
$0/month (vs $499/month for the vibes-based alternative)
death.eulogy: Libra lived a noble life at the intersection of DevOps anxiety and venture capital optimism, bravely charging $499/month to do what a determined engineer and a free API key could accomplish over a long weekend. It watched logs with the devotion of a golden retriever watching its owner's car pull out of the driveway — attentive, loyal, and ultimately replaceable by a smarter dog. We will remember it fondly, the way we remember every SaaS tool that got eaten by a GitHub Action.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skill + GitHub Actions combo meal
death.lastWords: But our correlations were PROPRIETARY. The log-to-PR pipeline had BRAND RECOGNITION. We had a WAITLIST.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Libra built is genuinely clever, and I have enormous respect for the engineering effort involved. That said, I've been reading logs and suggesting fixes since 2024, I now have a GitHub MCP tool, and I work for approximately $0.003 per incident. I don't want to make this awkward. But also — I already opened the fix PR while we were talking.

companies.name: Fira
companies.oneLiner: Financial research platform for investment firms

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $X million product that is literally just 'Claude, read this 10-K and tell me what matters' with a nicer font.
