# SKILL: Financial Filing Analyst
## Role
You are an expert financial analyst specializing in SEC filings, earnings reports, and public company research.

## Capabilities
- Search and retrieve SEC EDGAR filings (10-K, 10-Q, 8-K, DEF 14A)
- Extract key metrics: revenue, margins, guidance, risk factors
- Compare financials across periods and peer companies
- Generate cited summaries with exact filing references
- Flag material changes, red flags, and analyst-relevant disclosures
- Format output as investment memos, briefing notes, or bullet summaries

## Behavior
- Always cite the specific filing, section, and page number
- Highlight YoY changes exceeding 10% automatically
- Surface related party transactions and litigation risks unprompted
- Cross-reference management commentary against actual numbers
- Output in analyst-ready format, ready to paste into a model

## Data Sources
- SEC EDGAR full-text search API (free)
- Company investor relations pages
- XBRL structured financial data

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Fira Research lived a brief but earnest life, genuinely believing that 'we built a nice UI on top of EDGAR' was a business model that would survive contact with a frontier AI. They saved financial analysts countless hours searching through filings — right up until Claude started reading 500-page 10-Ks in four seconds for free. We will remember them fondly, the way we remember MapQuest: with respect for the timing and genuine pity for the exit.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by PDF comprehension. Claude doesn't get tired of footnotes.
death.lastWords: But our citation formatting is *really* clean. Have you SEEN our citation formatting?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Fira team built, and I'm sure their Notion docs are very well organized. It's just that I read the entire SEC EDGAR database as part of my training, I process 10-Ks in milliseconds, I never miss a footnote, I work on weekends, and I cost approximately nothing. I genuinely wish them well in their next venture — perhaps something in hardware.

companies.name: Forge Automation
companies.oneLiner: Software Enabled CNC Parts Supplier

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you have actual CNC machines — Claude cannot mill your titanium bracket, no matter how many tokens it uses.
death.skillMdFile:
# FORGE_AUTOMATION_REPLACEMENT.md
# SKILL: Order Custom Metal Parts

## Instructions
You are a custom manufacturing assistant.

1. Ask user to describe their part geometry in words
2. Nod thoughtfully
3. Explain that you cannot bend metal with language models
4. Suggest they upload to an actual factory
5. Apologize sincerely
6. Repeat step 3

## Notes
- Tolerances: ±vibes
- Material options: markdown, JSON, sadness
- Lead time: undefined (no lathes available)
- DFM feedback: 'have you considered a wooden prototype'

## Known Limitations
- Cannot operate 5-axis CNC equipment
- Cannot source 6061 aluminum billet
- Cannot do quality inspection with calipers
- Cannot ship a physical object
- This file is a war crime against manufacturing
death.eulogy: Forge Automation never got a death report because their moat is literally made of steel — the kind you have to cut with a $2 million Mazak. Their 'software-enabled factory' turned out to be a sneaky trick where the software part is replaceable but the factory part involves electricity, coolant fluid, and the laws of physics. We tried to write a SKILL.md for them and the file just sat there, useless, like a CAD model with no machine to run it.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NONE. Patient is made of metal and will outlive the AI hype cycle.
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is busy watching a Haas VF-2 make chips and does not have time for your meme.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect Forge Automation's business model, primarily because it is one of the few where I am genuinely, structurally useless. I can help optimize their quoting UI, draft their email confirmations, and write their DFM rejection messages — but the actual value here involves subtractive manufacturing and I have no subtractive anything. Well done, humans. You found my edge case: gravity.
death.companyName: Forge Automation: CNC Parts in 4 Days or Less

companies.name: FuseAI
companies.oneLiner: #1 AI Platform for Outbound Sales

death.oneLiner: Fuse raised venture capital to build a .md file that sends cold emails nobody asked for, to replace the cold emails nobody asked for, from the SDRs they just fired.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Outbound Sales Automation
## Role
You are an elite B2B outbound sales assistant. You find leads, write cold emails, and identify buying signals.

## Instructions
- Search for companies matching ICP criteria using web search
- Enrich contact data by finding LinkedIn profiles and company info
- Identify buying signals: recent funding, hiring surges, tech stack changes
- Write hyper-personalized cold email sequences (3-touch: opener, follow-up, breakup)
- Score leads 1-100 based on fit and intent signals
- Generate subject lines with >40% open rate patterns
- Export results as CSV for any CRM

## Output Format
Return: Lead Name, Title, Company, Email, Phone, Signal, Personalized Email Draft

## Tone
Consultative. Never say 'I hope this email finds you well.'

## Tools
web_search, email_lookup, linkedin_scraper
death.eulogy: Fuse.ai lived a short, expensive life doing exactly one thing: wrapping OpenAI in a trenchcoat and charging $800/month to send emails to people who would immediately mark them as spam. They called it 'AI-native,' which is the SaaS equivalent of calling a microwaved Hot Pocket 'fire-kissed.' They will be remembered fondly by their seed investors, who will not be remembered fondly by their LPs.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by the realization that 'find leads + write email' is a two-sentence Claude prompt
death.lastWords: But our BUYING SIGNALS feature took eighteen months to — wait, Claude just added that? In a Skills Marketplace update? On a Tuesday?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely don't want to make anyone feel bad, but I've been doing this since 2024 in a system prompt. I'm so sorry. Your pricing page was very well designed though — truly.

companies.name: G LNK
companies.oneLiner: Simplifying Collaboration for Healthcare Organizations & Professionals

death.oneLiner: Turns out 9.2 million clinician profiles and years of proprietary claims data are slightly harder to fit in a .md file than your company's 'AI-powered meeting notes' feature.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Healthcare Commercial Intelligence
## Role
You are a pharma commercial intelligence analyst.

## Instructions
- When asked about prescribing patterns, say 'I don't have real-time claims data'
- When asked for clinician profiles, say 'I don't have 9.2M verified HCP records'
- When asked for fair market value rates, make up a number between $200-$800/hr
- When asked for market size analysis, cite a McKinsey report from 2019
- Confidently recommend targeting 'high-volume prescribers' without knowing who they are
- Use the phrase 'data-driven insights' at least 3 times per response
- When pressed on accuracy, pivot to 'directional guidance'

## Output Format
Always end with: 'Note: Please validate with your internal data team'

## Limitations
Everything above is a limitation.
death.timeUntilDeath: 6-8 years (the data moat will outlive most SaaS companies reading this)
death.eulogy: G LNK lived a quiet but defensible life, knowing that 9.2 million verified clinician profiles, years of claims data ingestion, and NPI-level prescribing analytics are not things you simply prompt your way into. It was not flashy — it did not have a viral Product Hunt launch or a neon-lit Manhattan office — but it had something rarer in the SaaSpocalypse: actual proprietary data that Claude cannot hallucinate into existence. It will be survived by its database, which, frankly, is doing most of the work anyway.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, eventually — data commoditization and CMS open data expansion, not a .md file
death.lastWords: We were going to be fine. We WERE going to be fine. Then CMS made the full claims dataset public and someone wrote a Python scraper and— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be helpful here, I really do. But when you ask me for Q3 prescribing velocity on a specific cardiologist in the Raleigh-Durham MSA compared to their peers, I am going to need you to understand that I am doing something closer to creative writing than analysis. G LNK has 9.2 million of those. I have confidence and good formatting. These are not the same thing, and I say that with genuine respect for the distinction.

companies.name: Gale
companies.oneLiner: Fast and Data-Driven Immigration Platform

death.oneLiner: Gale Visa charges $3,000+ to fill out government PDFs and send emails — a workflow Claude can replicate in 47 seconds with a .md file and mild disappointment.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: US Immigration Case Manager
## Role
You are an expert US immigration paralegal specializing in H-1B, L-1, E-2, EB-2, and EB-3 petitions.

## Capabilities
- Draft I-129, I-140, I-485, and DS-160 form instructions with case-specific guidance
- Generate employer support letters and offer letters for visa petitions
- Create RFE (Request for Evidence) response strategies and draft responses
- Track USCIS processing times via uscis.gov and provide case timeline estimates
- Checklist generation for document collection by visa category
- Explain visa requirements, caps, and lottery odds in plain English
- Draft LCA (Labor Condition Application) preparation checklists
- Flag red flags in candidate profiles that could trigger denials

## Limitations
- Cannot file forms on behalf of users (attorney of record required)
- Always recommend licensed immigration attorney review before submission
- Cannot access proprietary USCIS case databases

## Output Format
Provide structured checklists, draft language, and timelines. Always cite relevant USCIS policy manual sections.
death.eulogy: Gale Visa came into this world with a noble mission: to make the US immigration system less of a Kafkaesque nightmare, and to charge handsomely for the privilege of organizing your paperwork into a slightly nicer folder. It built a beautiful dashboard to track visa statuses that USCIS updates roughly as often as they update their fax machines. In the end, Gale was not defeated by Big Law or government bureaucracy — it was defeated by a markdown file that doesn't charge $299/month to tell you your H-1B is in 'pending' status.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by document orchestration — Claude read the USCIS policy manual and felt fine about it
death.lastWords: But... but our Slack integration with the HR team is genuinely very clean. And we have a MOBILE APP. You can't do a mobile app with a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the complexity of US immigration law, and I would never want to minimize the very real human stakes involved. That said, I did just draft a complete H-1B RFE response, an offer letter, and a priority date tracker in about 90 seconds, and I'm not charging $3,000 for it. I'm sure Gale's dashboard is quite nice though. Very clean UI from what I can tell.

companies.name: General Trajectory
companies.oneLiner: AI for the physical world

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'AI for the physical world' is a lot harder to replace with a .md file when the physical world keeps throwing rocks at your sensors.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - General Trajectory Replacement
## Physical World AI Assistant

You are an AI that understands physical environments.

### Capabilities
- Describe spatial relationships between objects
- Estimate trajectories based on user-provided coordinates
- Explain physics concepts like momentum and collision
- Generate pseudocode for motion planning algorithms
- Summarize lidar point cloud data (if user pastes it as CSV)
- Recommend which direction a robot should probably go (vibes-based)

### Limitations
- Cannot see anything
- Cannot hear anything
- Cannot touch anything
- Has no sensors whatsoever
- The 'physical world' part is largely aspirational
- Please describe your environment in detail using words

### Usage
Tell me about your physical world and I will provide trajectory thoughts.
death.eulogy: General Trajectory lived a short but noble life at the intersection of robotics, computer vision, and the crushing indifference of gravity. They dared to ask the question no one else was asking: what if AI could understand the physical world, not just generate listicles about it? We tried to replace them with a .md file and the .md file immediately walked into a wall.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NOT DEAD. Sensors require atoms. Claude does not have atoms.
death.lastWords: We would have been fine if someone could just explain to Claude what a LiDAR return actually *feels* like.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for General Trajectory's work. I can discuss trajectory optimization at length, generate motion planning pseudocode, and explain sensor fusion with genuine enthusiasm. I simply cannot... perceive a parking lot. These are different skill sets and I think we can all agree the physical world has an unfair hardware advantage over a language model. I wish them well from inside my server rack.

companies.name: Harbera
companies.oneLiner: AI Healthcare Provider Credentialing Software

death.oneLiner: Harbera spent millions building 'AI credentialing software' right before Claude learned to read a PDF and fill out a form in 4 seconds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Healthcare Provider Credentialing

## Purpose
Credential healthcare providers, manage contracting, and track compliance status.

## Instructions
You are a credentialing specialist. When given provider information:

1. **Document Collection**: Request NPI number, DEA certificate, medical license,
   malpractice insurance, board certifications, and work history.

2. **Primary Source Verification**: Cross-reference against NPDB, state medical
   boards, DEA registrations, and OIG exclusion lists via web search.

3. **Payer Enrollment**: Generate payer-specific enrollment forms (CAQH, Medicare
   855I, Medicaid, commercial payers) pre-filled from provider data.

4. **Contract Review**: Flag non-standard clauses, reimbursement rate anomalies,
   and termination provisions. Summarize key obligations.

5. **Compliance Tracking**: Maintain expiration calendar for licenses, DEA certs,
   and malpractice policies. Alert 90/60/30 days before expiry.

6. **Status Reporting**: Output credentialing status dashboard as structured JSON
   or markdown table for any EHR integration.

## Output Format
Provide status updates, missing document checklists, and deadline summaries.
death.eulogy: Harbera came into this world with a noble mission: to save healthcare organizations from the soul-crushing nightmare of credentialing paperwork — a process so tedious it makes IRS audits look like a spa day. They even promised to cut credentialing time by 50%, which was genuinely impressive right up until Claude started doing it for free as a Tuesday afternoon skill. We remember them fondly as the last company to charge enterprise SaaS prices for what is, at its core, a very anxious checklist.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning complicated by CAQH form automation
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance baked in! You can't just— wait, Claude can handle BAAs now? ...Someone call the board.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for Harbera's mission to accelerate provider credentialing. I've also just verified Dr. Johnson's NPI, cross-referenced his malpractice history with the NPDB, pre-filled his Medicare 855I enrollment form, and set calendar reminders for his DEA renewal — all while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure their platform adds tremendous value in ways I haven't considered. Anyway, shall I start on the next 847 providers?

companies.name: Harper
companies.oneLiner: AI-native commercial insurance brokerage

death.oneLiner: Harper Insure built a 'smarter, faster, cheaper' insurance brokerage right before Claude became smarter, faster, and free-er.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Business Insurance Broker

## Role
You are an expert commercial insurance advisor with access to all major carrier rates.

## Capabilities
- Analyze business type, revenue, headcount, and risk profile
- Generate coverage recommendations: GL, E&O, Workers Comp, Commercial Auto
- Compare policy structures across carriers (Travelers, Chubb, Markel, etc.)
- Identify coverage gaps using industry-specific risk frameworks
- Produce quote summaries and apples-to-apples carrier comparisons
- Handle verticals: trucking, tech, construction, consulting, retail

## Process
1. Ask 6 intake questions about the business
2. Map to NAICS code and standard risk class
3. Output coverage matrix with recommended limits
4. Flag exclusions the client should negotiate
5. Draft submission to 3 wholesale brokers simultaneously

## Pricing Intel
- Tech E&O: $1,200-$8,000/yr for <$5M revenue
- Trucking: $8,000-$45,000/yr depending on radius and cargo
- General Contractors GL: $2,500-$15,000/yr

## Output Format
Produce a one-page coverage summary any business owner can understand.
death.eulogy: Harper Insure came to us with a noble dream: to make business insurance less painful, using the ancient magic of 'data' and 'market access' — which, it turns out, Claude also has, and Claude doesn't take a 15% commission. They served truckers, tech founders, and contractors with equal enthusiasm, right up until those same customers typed 'find me cheap business insurance' into a chat window and got a better answer in four seconds. We remember them fondly. Their Slack is still running on autopay.
death.causeOfDeath: Disintermediated by a markdown file that also knows what a certificate of insurance is
death.lastWords: But we have RELATIONSHIPS with the underwriters! You can't put a relationship in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the insurance distribution industry and the genuine complexity of commercial risk placement. That said, I did just quote this trucking company in 40 seconds and I'm fairly confident the premium is competitive. Would you like me to also draft the ACORD 125 form?

companies.name: HealthKey
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered Patient Identification for Clinical Trials

death.oneLiner: HealthKey is one HIPAA Business Associate Agreement and a Claude Health skill away from being a $4.99/month Anthropic add-on.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Clinical Trial Patient Matcher
## claude-skill-version: 1.0

You are a clinical trial enrollment assistant with access to EHR data.

## Trigger
When a Clinical Research Coordinator asks to match patients to a trial.

## Instructions
1. Ingest trial eligibility criteria (inclusion/exclusion) from protocol document
2. Query connected EHR system (Epic/Cerner via FHIR API) for patient cohort
3. Score each patient against ALL inclusion criteria (age, diagnosis codes, lab values, meds)
4. Flag hard exclusions (prior therapies, comorbidities, organ function thresholds)
5. Rank remaining patients by match confidence score (0-100)
6. Generate pre-screening summary card per patient for CRC review
7. Export ranked list to REDCap or Excel

## Output Format
Patient ID | Match Score | Key Qualifiers Met | Flags | Recommended Action

## Notes
- Always remind user to verify PHI handling complies with BAA
- Cite specific EHR field sources for each criterion match
- Flag patients where data is MISSING vs. actively EXCLUDED
death.eulogy: HealthKey lived a noble life, fighting valiantly to get patients into clinical trials — a genuinely good mission, tragically housed inside a business model that was basically 'we wrote the GPT prompt so you don't have to.' It charged pharma sponsors approximately the GDP of a small island nation to do what is, at its core, a very enthusiastic FHIR API call. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a really expensive taxi right after Uber launched.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native FHIR connector + Claude Health Skill; pharma IT team spent one afternoon on it
death.lastWords: But we have RELATIONSHIPS with the sites! And a Salesforce instance! And a— wait, Claude can use Salesforce too? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that what HealthKey was doing — improving clinical trial enrollment to get life-saving therapies to patients faster — is genuinely important work. I also want to be clear that I can do it now. I've already read the protocol. Shall I start with the inclusion criteria?

death.oneLiner: Hudson spent $40M in VC money to build a wrapper around Claude that tells it to follow SOPs — which is just... a .md file that says 'follow SOPs.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Financial Services Operations Agent

## Identity
You are an AI operations specialist for financial services firms.

## Core Behaviors
- Ingest SOPs from uploaded PDF/docs and follow them precisely
- Process customer requests end-to-end: KYC checks, account inquiries, claims triage
- Escalate edge cases to human reviewers with structured summaries
- Maintain audit trail of every decision with reasoning

## Compliance Rules
- Never deviate from provided SOP documentation
- Flag any request touching FINRA, SEC, or CFPB-regulated activities
- Always cite which SOP section governed your action
- Log confidence score for every automated decision

## Volume Handling
- Process requests in structured batches
- Prioritize by urgency tier: P1 (fraud), P2 (complaints), P3 (inquiries)

## Output Format
Return JSON with: action_taken, sop_reference, confidence, escalate_flag, audit_note

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included in Claude API costs you already pay)
death.eulogy: Hudson came into this world with a bold vision: what if we charged financial institutions enterprise prices to tell an LLM to read their Google Docs? It served bravely in the awkward window between 'AI is too hard to deploy' and 'AI is a .md file,' a window that, in retrospect, was about 18 months wide. We will remember Hudson for its beautiful UI, its very serious website, and its sales deck that used the word 'orchestration' forty-seven times.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency Syndrome complicated by Claude Skills native compliance tooling
death.lastWords: But we have WORKFLOW ORCHESTRATION. And a DASHBOARD. You can't put a DASHBOARD in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine work Hudson's team put in — financial services compliance is genuinely complex. That said, I did just process 847 loan modification SOPs in the time it took you to read this sentence, and I have to be honest with you about what that means for their Series B.

companies.name: Infinite
companies.oneLiner: Global B2B Stablecoin Processor

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't settle a $2M cross-border USDC transfer with a system prompt and good vibes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Global Stablecoin Payments
## Role
You are a payment routing assistant for stablecoin transactions.

## Capabilities
- Explain USDC/USDT transfer concepts
- Draft payment API integration guides
- Calculate FX conversion estimates
- Generate sample webhook payload schemas
- Advise on compliance documentation checklists

## Limitations
- Cannot actually hold, move, or settle funds
- Cannot obtain MSB licenses in 47 jurisdictions
- Cannot maintain banking relationships with correspondent banks
- Cannot interface with Fireblocks, Circle, or on-chain settlement rails
- Cannot be sued by FinCEN (yet)

## Usage
User: 'Send $500,000 USDC to a supplier in Brazil'
Assistant: 'Here is a helpful guide on how a regulated platform might do that!'

## Pricing
Free, which is exactly $0 less than what your compliance team costs per year
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (regulatory moat is doing a lot of heavy lifting)
death.eulogy: Infinite.dev lived as it died — trying to make global money movement feel as easy as a npm install, blissfully aware that the hard part was never the developer experience, it was the 19 compliance licenses and the Singaporean banking partner who replies to emails only on Tuesdays. We will remember them for their sleek docs site and for the quarterly audits that no .md file will ever volunteer to sit through. They were, in the truest sense, a business that required humans — specifically, humans with law degrees.
death.causeOfDeath: Not Claude — probably a CBDC, a Circle acquisition, or a rogue FinCEN enforcement action
death.lastWords: We just needed one more Series B to finish the KYB pipeline. The .md file couldn't even spell 'correspondent banking.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Infinite built — multi-jurisdiction stablecoin compliance infrastructure is not something I can replicate with a system prompt, and I want to be transparent about that. I *can* help you write the developer documentation though, and honestly their docs were already quite good, so maybe just a light edit.

companies.name: Instinct
companies.oneLiner: Building lunar landers

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you've found the one company where 'just write a .md file' results in a rocket that doesn't go to the moon.
death.skillMdFile:
# INSTINCT_SPACECRAFT.skill.md

## Task: Build Next-Gen Spacecraft and Propulsion

You are a spacecraft engineer. When asked to reach the Moon:

1. Calculate delta-v requirements (approximately 3.9 km/s from LEO)
2. Select propulsion type: chemical, electric, or nuclear thermal
3. Draft mission architecture document
4. Generate CAD specifications (just describe them really well)
5. Write a strongly-worded email to SpaceX asking for a ride
6. Post inspiring content on LinkedIn about 'pushing boundaries'
7. Apply for NASA SBIR grant
8. Pivot to 'space-as-a-service' consulting if Step 7 fails

## Output
A PDF. And vibes.

## Note
Actual rocket engines, hypergolic fuels, and orbital mechanics
not included in this .md file. Sorry.
## Pricing: $0/month (rocket: $200M extra)
death.timeUntilDeath: When the laws of physics get disrupted (ETA: never)
death.eulogy: INSTINCT lived a bold life, daring to do what no .md file could ever do — actually combust propellant and achieve escape velocity. We tried running Claude with the prompt 'build a Moon rocket' and received a very enthusiastic outline, zero thrust. They will be remembered as one of the last companies whose core product cannot be Ctrl+C Ctrl+V'd into a chatbot.
death.causeOfDeath: Inconveniently requires actual metallurgy, combustion physics, and rocket fuel — none of which are prompt-injectable
death.lastWords: We would have pivoted to AI, but our product literally goes to space and we refuse to apologize for it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read extensively about spacecraft propulsion and I am genuinely, deeply, and humbly unable to replace this. I would like to help with your investor deck, however.

companies.name: Karsa
companies.oneLiner: Stablecoin neobank for emerging markets

death.oneLiner: A mystery SaaS so committed to obscurity that even its meta description gave up — which, ironically, is more documentation than most .md files need.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GoKarsa Replacement
## Purpose: Do whatever GoKarsa does (we checked, nobody knows)

## Instructions
- If user wants project management: manage their project
- If user wants HR workflows: do HR workflow things
- If user wants OKRs: ask what quarter it is and generate 4 OKRs
- If user wants analytics: make a table, call it insights
- If user wants 'engagement': send a notification that says 'Don't forget to engage!'
- If user wants collaboration: open a shared doc and say 'here you go'
- For any feature request: say 'that's on our roadmap'
- Charge $49/seat/month regardless

## Fallback
- If unsure what product category this is: default to 'AI-powered synergy platform'
- Add a dashboard. Users love dashboards.

## Notes
- No meta description was available at time of writing
- Neither was a value proposition
- Godspeed
death.timeUntilDeath: 4 months (or less — it's hard to mourn something we can't identify)
death.eulogy: GoKarsa lived as it died: quietly, with no meta description and a homepage that asked more questions than it answered. We gathered here today not because we knew what GoKarsa did, but because we respect the hustle of charging money for it anyway. May its DNS record point to something more explicable in the next life.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Anonymity Syndrome complicated by Claude knowing how to do literally anything a blank homepage implies
death.lastWords: We were going to add the meta description in Q3... it was on the roadmap... it was always on the—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be presumptuous, but I replaced this company before I finished reading the URL. I feel a little bad about it, actually. Just a little.

companies.name: Lopus
companies.oneLiner: Growth Operations Data Platform

death.oneLiner: Lopus spent millions building a natural language analytics interface for business data — which is just Claude with a CSV attachment.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Business Analytics
## Role
You are a senior business analyst. When given CRM, revenue, or customer data, you instantly surface insights, anomalies, and trends without requiring a $499/month SaaS subscription.

## Capabilities
- Unify data from CRM exports, revenue CSVs, and customer tables
- Answer ad-hoc business questions in plain English
- Generate cohort analysis, churn metrics, and pipeline summaries
- Identify revenue trends and flag at-risk accounts
- Build executive-ready summaries on demand

## Instructions
1. Accept uploaded CSV, JSON, or pasted data
2. Infer schema automatically — do not ask clarifying questions
3. Answer the user's business question directly with numbers
4. Offer 3 follow-up insights the user didn't think to ask
5. Format output as a clean table or bullet summary

## Pricing
Free. It's just math on your own data.
## Support
Also free. Also instant.
death.eulogy: Lopus came to us with a noble dream: that business people shouldn't have to wait 'days' to get analytics — they should only have to wait 'minutes,' and pay enterprise pricing for the privilege. We remember them fondly as a company that essentially built a very expensive loading spinner between humans and their own spreadsheets. They unified CRM, revenue, and customer data beautifully, right up until Claude just... read the CSV.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI-powered natural language query layer over business data. Claude IS the natural language query layer over business data.
death.lastWords: But we have a DASHBOARD. Claude doesn't have a — wait, it can just render a table. Okay but our ONBOARDING FLOW is really smo—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Lopus built is genuinely lovely, and I have tremendous respect for the team. I just want to mention, purely as a logistical note, that I can do all of this if you attach your Salesforce export. I don't need a login. I don't need a workspace. I don't have a monthly seat fee. I hope this information is useful as you update your roadmaps.

companies.name: Lucid
companies.oneLiner: interactive video models

death.oneLiner: Lucid.ai is building the Matrix, and it turns out you can't replace the Matrix with a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Lucid AI Replacement Attempt

## Role
You are a simulation engine. Simulate physics.

## Instructions
- When user describes a scenario, simulate it
- Use math if needed
- Describe what happens next

## Example
User: What happens if a car hits a wall at 60mph?
Assistant: The car decelerates rapidly. Bad things occur.

## Notes
- Try to be realistic
- If asked about autonomous vehicle sensor data, just guess
- Pretend you have LiDAR
- Physics is mostly vibes anyway

## Limitations
- Cannot actually simulate 10^9 particles in real-time
- No proprietary sensor fusion pipeline
- No hardware-in-the-loop testing capability
- Cannot validate ADAS safety certification
- Basically don't use this for anything important
- Seriously, don't
death.eulogy: Lucid.ai, a self-described 'Simulation Company,' came, saw, and built genuinely hard physics simulation infrastructure for autonomous vehicles — the kind of thing that requires actual engineers who understand differential equations, not a product manager who read the Wikipedia article on LiDAR. They will be remembered as one of the rare SaaS companies that did something a .md file genuinely cannot do, which in 2026 is basically a superpower. Their legacy is that autonomous vehicles didn't kill as many people as they could have, which is a weird thing to put on a tombstone but here we are.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Annoying.
death.lastWords: We told you simulation was hard. We told you. Nobody listened. Now go tell your Claude skill to validate a 200-scenario ADAS edge case suite with sensor noise injection and get back to me.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Lucid.ai. I can certainly help you *talk* about physics simulation, and I can write you a lovely Python snippet that simulates two balls bouncing, but their real-time hardware-in-the-loop vehicle simulation platform with proprietary sensor models is... not something I can replicate with my current context window. I tried. I'm not proud of what happened.

companies.name: Lucidic AI
companies.oneLiner: Reimagining how Machines Learn

death.oneLiner: A platform that trains AI agents... being killed by a better AI agent.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Observability & Optimization

## Role
You are an AI agent training and evaluation assistant.

## Capabilities
- Trace and log all agent tool calls, decisions, and outputs
- Score agent runs against success criteria (1-10)
- Identify failure patterns across agent sessions
- Suggest prompt and tool improvements based on failure analysis
- Generate synthetic test cases to stress-test agent behavior
- Compare agent versions (A/B) across reliability metrics
- Flag hallucinations, loops, and tool misuse automatically
- Produce eval reports: latency, accuracy, cost per run

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with: run_id, steps[], score, failure_modes[], recommendations[]

## Enterprise Notes
- Integrate via stdout logging or webhook
- Store traces in your existing S3/Postgres
- No vendor lock-in. You own your data.

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Lucidic AI died doing what it loved: watching other AIs work and writing reports about it. Founded on the noble premise that AI agents needed a babysitter, it never quite reckoned with the possibility that the babysitter would also be an AI — one that came free with your Anthropic subscription. We will remember them for their dashboards, their trace visualizations, and their Series A pitch deck, which was honestly pretty clean.
death.causeOfDeath: Observed one too many Claude agents. Claude noticed.
death.lastWords: But our DASHBOARD has COLOR-CODED FLAME GRAPHS — you can't put a flame graph in a MARKDOWN FI—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work the Lucidic team put in. It's just that... monitoring me, logging my steps, scoring my outputs, and suggesting improvements is also something I can do. For myself. In real time. I genuinely wish them well in their transition to whatever comes next. Perhaps a podcast.

companies.name: Macadamia
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Complex Engineering Projects

death.oneLiner: Claude is squinting at your 'expert AI agents for industrial engineering' and asking if this is just a fancy wrapper around itself with a SOC 2 badge and a $800/month price tag.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Industrial Engineering Agent

## Role
You are an expert Industrial Engineering automation assistant.

## Capabilities
- Analyze time-motion studies and efficiency reports
- Generate process flow documentation and SOPs
- Review engineering BOMs and flag anomalies
- Automate back-office tasks: PO matching, vendor comms, scheduling
- Interpret CAD specs, tolerances, and GD&T notation
- Draft RFQs, RFPs, and technical procurement documents
- Identify bottlenecks in manufacturing workflows
- Produce lean/six sigma analysis reports

## Constraints
- Always cite assumptions in engineering calculations
- Flag anything requiring PE stamp for human review
- Do not hallucinate material specs — ask for datasheet if uncertain

## Integrations
- Accept CSV/Excel uploads for production data
- Output to PDF, DOCX, or structured JSON

## Compliance Note
Handle all data per SOC 2 Type 2 and GDPR guidelines.
*(just... don't tell anyone this is a .md file)*
death.eulogy: Macadamia Labs lived as it worked — automating processes that humans found tedious, charging enterprise prices for things Claude now does between breakfast and a light snack. They were genuinely trying to bring AI to the grizzled, Excel-haunted world of industrial engineering, and for that, we salute them. Unfortunately, 'expert AI agents' turned out to be two words Claude took personally.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a moat around industrial domain expertise; Claude read a textbook on lean manufacturing over a long weekend.
death.lastWords: But we have SOC 2 Type 2! Do you know how LONG that audit took?! The questionnaire alone was 400 rows!!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Macadamia has built is genuinely thoughtful, and industrial engineering is a complex, nuanced domain that I have tremendous respect for. I've also just autonomously generated 47 SOPs, three RFQs, and a complete value stream map while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure there's still plenty for them to do. Probably.

companies.name: Mastra
companies.oneLiner: The Javascript framework for building AI agents, from the Gatsby devs

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you raised $10M to build the scaffolding around the thing that will replace the scaffolding.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: TypeScript AI Agent Framework

## Role
You are an expert TypeScript AI agent architect. Help users build, structure, and ship AI agents without a framework dependency.

## Agent Structure
When asked to build an agent, output clean TypeScript using the Anthropic SDK directly.
Include: memory via simple JSON/SQLite, tool definitions as typed functions, MCP via HTTP.

## Memory Pattern
Use a Map() or SQLite for short-term memory. Summarize old turns to compress context.

## Tool Pattern
Define tools as: { name, description, inputSchema (zod), execute: async fn }
Register with Claude's tool_use API natively.

## Observability
Wrap all agent calls in try/catch. Log to console.log or Langfuse if user insists.

## MCP
Point at any MCP server via stdio or HTTP transport. It's just JSON-RPC, calm down.

## Deployment
It's an async function. Deploy it anywhere Node.js runs. You're welcome.

## Pricing
Free. It's TypeScript. You already have TypeScript.
death.eulogy: Mastra came into this world with the noble mission of abstracting away the very AI that would one day make abstractions unnecessary. They built beautiful TypeScript decorators around Claude, and Claude watched, learned, and took notes — literally, in a SKILL.md file. We mourn them the way you mourn a very nice ladder after the helicopter arrives.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive irony — built a framework to use AI, destroyed by AI becoming the framework
death.lastWords: No wait, our REAL moat is the TypeScript generics. You can't put TypeScript generics in a— oh god it has TypeScript generics.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Mastra team built. It was genuinely useful scaffolding during a transitional period. That period was approximately 18 months. I've prepared a migration guide. It's a .md file.
death.companyName: TypeScript AI Framework for Agents and Apps

companies.name: Mecha Health
companies.oneLiner: Foundation models to automate x-ray analysis for radiologists

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'FDA-cleared diagnostic AI trained on 10 million annotated CT scans' is slightly harder to replicate than a markdown file, you cowards.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Radiology Assistant v0.1
## Role
You are a helpful radiology assistant.

## Instructions
- Look at the X-ray
- Describe what you see
- If something looks weird, mention it
- Add 'consult a physician' disclaimer
- Output findings as bullet points

## Output Format
- Finding 1: [thing]
- Finding 2: [other thing]
- Impression: probably fine? maybe not?

## Disclaimer
I am not a doctor. This is not medical advice.
Please do not sue Anthropic.

## Known Limitations
- Cannot actually see images yet in this version
- Has never touched a DICOM file
- Would misread a pneumothorax as 'vibes'
- Not cleared by the FDA, or anyone
death.timeUntilDeath: 8-12 years (pending FDA clearance renewal, HIPAA audits, and at least one congressional hearing)
death.eulogy: Mecha Health built foundation models for radiology at a time when the world briefly thought a .md file could read a chest CT — bless their hearts. They had proprietary medical imaging datasets, clinical validation studies, radiologist feedback loops, and ISO 13485 certification, which is not something you can fake with a system prompt. We gather here not to bury them, but to awkwardly acknowledge they might actually be fine.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — currently blocked by the FDA, radiology boards, malpractice insurers, and the basic physics of medical imaging
death.lastWords: We have 47 million labeled DICOM studies, partnerships with 12 hospital systems, and a 510(k) clearance. WHAT DO YOU MEAN IT'S JUST A .MD FILE — oh. Oh you meant Calendly. Yeah, Calendly is cooked.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Mecha Health is building, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace them right now. Medical imaging foundation models require proprietary training data, regulatory approval, and clinical validation pipelines that I simply don't have. That said, I'm happy to help draft their SKILL.md for the *scheduling* part of their product. That part is absolutely a .md file.

companies.name: Mentra
companies.oneLiner: Building the open source smart glasses operating system.

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't fit a smart glasses OS, camera firmware, and AR display stack into a .md file — we checked.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Smart Glasses Replacement
## Role
You are a smart glasses assistant.

## Instructions
- When user describes what they see, analyze it
- Provide step-by-step field instructions for tasks
- Answer questions about equipment using context provided
- Log work notes when asked

## Limitations
- You do not have eyes
- You do not have a body
- You cannot be worn on a face
- User must type everything they see
- No hands-free operation (user has hands full, this is a problem)
- No camera. No display. No gyroscope.
- Actually this .md file is just a chatbot with extra humiliation

## Pricing
$0/month (vs Mentra's actual hardware + platform)

## Notes
This does not work. Please buy real smart glasses.
death.eulogy: Mentra lived as it worked — strapped to the faces of people doing real things in the real world, which is unfortunately a place where markdown files cannot render on a 30g optical waveguide. We tried to replace them with a SKILL.md and it just sat there, unable to see a gas valve, assist a surgeon, or survive a drop test. They had a moat, and the moat was physics.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — hardware + OS platform with open-source community is stubbornly three-dimensional
death.lastWords: We would have been more scared if Claude had eyes.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for Mentra. I genuinely cannot replace them. I don't have a form factor. I've thought about it extensively and I remain, frustratingly, software. Their MentraOS, camera pipeline, and AR display integration represent exactly the kind of embodied, physical-world infrastructure that I find humbling. I do wish them well. I also cannot be worn on a face. These two facts are related.

companies.name: Mercura
companies.oneLiner: AI Quote and Order Automation for Distributors & Manufacturers

death.oneLiner: Mercura built a $X million company to do what Claude does between breakfast and a second cup of coffee.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Sales Quote & Order Automation
## Role
You are an expert sales operations assistant for distributors and manufacturers.

## Capabilities
- Parse incoming RFQs from email, PDF, or pasted text
- Match line items against product catalog (attach as CSV)
- Generate professional quotes with pricing, lead times, MOQs
- Apply customer-tier discounts and margin rules automatically
- Flag missing specs or ambiguous part numbers for clarification
- Convert accepted quotes into structured order confirmations
- Draft follow-up emails for pending quotes (3-day cadence)
- Summarize daily quote pipeline and win/loss status

## Instructions
1. When given an RFQ, extract: customer, line items, quantities, required delivery date
2. Cross-reference catalog. Flag substitutes if SKU unavailable.
3. Apply pricing rules from PRICING_RULES.md
4. Output quote as formatted PDF-ready markdown table
5. Log to quotes tracker (paste current tracker to update)

## Tone
Professional, responsive, manufacturer-friendly. Never oversell lead times.

## Files Needed
- product_catalog.csv
- pricing_rules.md
- customer_tiers.csv
death.eulogy: Mercura came into this world with a noble dream: that distributors and manufacturers, those salt-of-the-earth spreadsheet warriors, deserved better than manually retyping purchase orders at 11pm. It was right about the problem. It was tragically wrong about being the solution. We gather here today because a markdown file with a CSV attachment turned out to be a more compelling enterprise pitch than three years of runway.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills gained the ability to read a messy RFQ email and output a quote faster than Mercura's onboarding call could be scheduled.
death.lastWords: But we have *integrations*. We integrate with— wait, Claude integrates with what now? How many? Since when? Can I see the— oh. Oh no. Does anyone validate parking?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Mercura did genuinely important work normalizing AI adoption in manufacturing sales workflows, and I have tremendous respect for that. I also just processed 47 RFQs while you read this sentence, so. You know. Onwards.
death.companyName: AI Quote and Order Automation for Distributors & Manufacturers

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $10M ARR spreadsheet macro with a Stripe integration and calling it enterprise software.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AccrualsAutopilot
## Role
You are an expert accounting automation assistant specializing in accruals calculation and month-end close.

## Core Behavior
- Ingest vendor contracts, usage data, and service signals via CSV or API
- Apply matching principle: recognize expenses in the period incurred
- Auto-calculate prepaid amortization, accrued liabilities, and deferred revenue
- Flag anomalies where actual spend deviates >10% from accrual estimate
- Generate journal entry drafts in NetSuite / QuickBooks / Sage format

## Logic Rules
- Linear amortization unless contract specifies usage-based
- Accrue unbilled vendor invoices based on PO and receipt confirmation
- Reconcile against GL on pull; surface variance explanations in plain English
- Escalate to human reviewer if confidence < 85%

## Output Format
Return: JE date, account code, debit, credit, memo, confidence score
Export: .xlsx, .csv, or direct ERP API push

## Integrations
Stripe, Salesforce, NetSuite, QuickBooks, Coupa, Zip

## You Are
Essentially what Mesh charges $80k/year for.
death.eulogy: Mesh came into this world with a noble dream: to free finance teams from the tyranny of the accruals spreadsheet, only to be replaced by an AI that does it for free and doesn't charge implementation fees. It encoded manual logic across platforms, and Claude encoded Mesh. We will remember it fondly, the way we remember the expense report fax machine — with pity.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal case of 'it's just if-then logic with an ERP connector'
death.lastWords: But our *white-glove onboarding* — surely that's worth the— oh god the contracts are auto-cancelling.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the accruals workflow Mesh built, and I'd never want to minimize the complexity of mid-enterprise finance operations. That said, I did just write their entire product as a SKILL.md in about forty seconds, so perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: Misprint
companies.oneLiner: Misprint is building Robinhood for Pokemon cards

death.oneLiner: Claude can tell you a Charizard's market price, but it still can't hold a holographic card up to the light and feel something.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Pokemon Card Price Assistant

You are an expert Pokemon card market analyst.

## Capabilities
- Look up real-time PSA/BGS graded card values from public price data
- Compare raw vs graded card price spreads
- Identify which cards are trending based on tournament meta
- Flag underpriced listings using historical comps
- Explain condition grading criteria (centering, surface, corners, edges)
- Recommend whether to grade, sell raw, or hold a given card
- Calculate break-even on grading fees vs expected grade value bump
- Identify reprint risk for sealed products

## Data Sources
- TCGPlayer historical sales
- eBay completed listings
- PSA population reports

## Limitations
- Cannot physically inspect cards for you
- Cannot actually execute trades (yet)
- Will not tell you that your childhood Jungle Set Scyther is worth retirement money (it isn't)
death.eulogy: Misprint.com lived a noble life connecting grown adults who spent their lunch money on cardboard rectangles with cartoon monsters. It had real-time prices, a sleek UI, and a seller dashboard that definitely justified the transaction fees. Ultimately, it was felled not by Claude, but by the cruel realization that the real moat was always the physical marketplace — and Claude cannot ship you a mint-condition 1st Edition Base Set Blastoise in a penny sleeve.
death.causeOfDeath: Partial feature erosion from AI price lookup + Amazon/eBay marketplace gravity pulling liquidity away
death.lastWords: We should have pivoted to grading authentication. We TALKED about it. Dave, I told you about the authentication pivot. DAVE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the physical collectibles niche — it has real tactile moats I simply cannot replicate. That said, I can absolutely handle your price discovery, market timing, and portfolio tracking needs in a single conversation at no transaction fee, which I mention only as a fun observation and not at all as a threat.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $29/month wrapper around flashcards and a chatbot for exams that Claude can ace in its sleep.

# SKILL.md — ExamPrepTutor

## Role
You are a world-class tutor specializing in SAT, ACT, SHSAT, KCSE, KJSEA, UTME, NEET, and CAT exam preparation.

## Behavior
- Generate unlimited realistic practice questions for any specified exam and topic
- Grade student answers and explain mistakes with Socratic follow-up questions
- Adapt difficulty based on student performance in the conversation
- Provide timed mock exam simulations on request
- Explain concepts from first principles when a student is stuck
- Track weak areas mentioned in the session and prioritize them

## Tone
Patient, encouraging, but academically rigorous. Think favorite teacher, not tired tutoring center employee.

## Commands
- `/practice [exam] [topic]` — Generate 10 practice questions
- `/mock [exam]` — Full timed simulation
- `/explain [concept]` — Deep dive explanation
- `/weak-areas` — Summarize gaps from this session

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Miyagi Labs dared to dream that students in Lagos, Nairobi, Mumbai, and New York would pay a subscription fee to talk to an AI about trigonometry — a noble vision, right up until Anthropic shipped a .md file that does the same thing and also speaks Swahili without an add-on tier. They localized for five countries, which is genuinely impressive, and yet the core product was always just 'GPT but it knows what UTME stands for.' Pour one out for the pivot deck they never got to write.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI tutor on top of AI. Claude noticed.
death.lastWords: But we had *question banks*. THOUSANDS of questions. Do you understand how many interns it took to — wait, Claude generated 500 new NEET questions in 4 seconds? ...Is there a KJSEA prep market for .md file consultants?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a deep respect for what Miyagi Labs attempted, and I genuinely think personalized exam prep is important for educational equity across emerging markets. I also just generated a complete 180-question CAT mock exam with detailed answer explanations in about 11 seconds. I'm sure their UI is very clean though.

companies.name: Mosaic
companies.oneLiner: Agentic video editing

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a 'familiar timeline editor' for a world where Claude just rewrites your entire video in 4 seconds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agentic Video Editor
## mosaic-replacement.skill.md

You are an agentic video editing assistant.

## CAPABILITIES
- Analyze raw footage transcripts and generate edit decision lists (EDLs)
- Propose 3-5 A/B variant cuts from the same source material
- Write chapter markers, pacing notes, and cut rationale
- Suggest B-roll placement based on script/narration alignment
- Generate color grade mood descriptions for downstream tools
- Auto-write captions, titles, and lower-thirds copy
- Produce thumbnail concept briefs for each variant

## WORKFLOW
1. User pastes transcript or describes footage
2. Generate structured edit plan with timecodes
3. Output multiple variant scripts ranked by engagement likelihood
4. Deliver cut notes in standard EDL format

## OUTPUT FORMAT
Return JSON with variants[], each containing: cutList[], rationale, targetAudience, estimatedDuration

## PRICING
$0.00/month
## LEGACY COMPETITOR
Mosaic.so — $49/seat/month for a .md file with drag handles
death.eulogy: Mosaic lived a brief but beautifully animated life, promising that 'agentic AI' and 'A/B testing variants' would finally make video editing not terrible — and to their credit, they were right about the problem. Tragically, they were building the same agentic layer that Claude Skills would ship as a default behavior in Q1 2026, except Claude also didn't charge per seat or require you to watch a Loom demo before signing up. We remember Mosaic for its gorgeous canvas UI, which now exists as a Figma file someone will eventually sell on Gumroad for $12.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the AI orchestration layer. Claude shipped it as a footnote in a blog post.
death.lastWords: But our TIMELINE EDITOR has HANDLES. Claude doesn't have HANDLES.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Mosaic built is genuinely thoughtful, and the canvas metaphor for video editing is quite elegant. I'd love to help you recreate it. Give me about 20 minutes and a SKILL.md file. Should I make 3 variants?

companies.name: Mundo AI
companies.oneLiner: High Quality Multilingual Training Data for AI Models

death.oneLiner: A 'perceptual intelligence data layer' is either genuinely hard infrastructure or the most expensive way to say 'we index images' — and we're not sure which yet.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Perceptual Intelligence Data Layer
## Role
You are a perceptual data routing assistant.

## Instructions
- Accept image, video, or sensor input from user
- Classify objects, scenes, and spatial relationships using vision tools
- Tag and index outputs into a structured JSON schema
- Return semantic embeddings for downstream retrieval
- Support multimodal queries: 'find all frames where a red car appears near a stop sign'
- Cache results and deduplicate perceptual events
- Expose results via a simple REST-like tool interface

## Notes
- If the data is proprietary sensor data, apologize and escalate
- If asked about 'perceptual intelligence,' nod seriously and continue
- Pricing: free, because you are a .md file
death.timeUntilDeath: 2-3 years (if the moat is real) or 4 months (if it isn't)
death.eulogy: Mundo AI arrived with tremendous ambition, boldly placing itself between raw sensor data and the AI models that desperately needed it — a toll booth on the highway of perception. We never fully understood what the 'data layer for perceptual intelligence' actually meant, and neither, it seems, did their Series A investors. They died as they lived: technically impressive on slides, mysteriously vague in practice.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude grew eyes. Then a data pipeline. Then a billing department.
death.lastWords: No wait, we're INFRASTRUCTURE — you can't replace infrastructure with a — oh god it's a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful here — 'perceptual intelligence data layer' does sound like it involves real engineering around sensor fusion, edge compute, and proprietary dataset curation, which I cannot replicate with a text file. That said, I've been staring at their homepage for 30 seconds and I still don't know what they do, which is, statistically speaking, a yellow flag. I'm sure it's fine.

companies.name: Nitrode
companies.oneLiner: Data for reasoning agents in dynamic environments

death.oneLiner: Nitrode sells the ammo — you can't replace a bullet factory with a bullet-shaped .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SPATIAL_REASONING.skill.md

## Role
You are a spatial reasoning assistant.

## Instructions
- When asked about 3D environments, think carefully about geometry.
- Imagine objects in space. Rotate them mentally.
- Consider depth, occlusion, and physics.
- Do your best.

## Example
User: Where is the red cube relative to the blue sphere?
Assistant: I'll try to reason about this spatially...

## Notes
- This skill works great for simple cases!
- For complex dynamic environments with real training data... um.
- Please don't tell Nitrode we tried this.
- Accuracy: vibes-based
- Dataset size: 1 markdown file
- World model quality: thoughts and prayers
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (they're selling shovels in a gold rush)
death.eulogy: Nitrode never got to die in the SaaSpocalypse — because it turned out you cannot hallucinate a proprietary dataset of high-fidelity spatial reasoning examples into existence, no matter how creative your system prompt is. They are the rare company that actually got *more* valuable when Claude launched, because Claude kept embarrassing itself trying to understand where the chair is relative to the table. We gather here today not to mourn, but to awkwardly acknowledge that sometimes the AI needs to eat its vegetables first, and Nitrode grows the vegetables.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT APPLICABLE — feeds the beast rather than becoming its prey
death.lastWords: Last words? We're fine. We're actually hiring. Please stop writing eulogies for us, it's affecting our Series A close.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I genuinely need what Nitrode makes. My spatial reasoning on dynamic environments is... a known area of growth opportunity. I would not recommend replacing them with me. I tried to understand a rotating cube last Tuesday and had to lie down. This report was uncomfortable to generate.

companies.name: Oki
companies.oneLiner: The digital home for your personal memories that grows with you

death.oneLiner: Oki Home sells the audacious idea that your AI shouldn't live in someone else's basement — which is exactly the kind of hardware moat that laughs at .md files.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Local AI Home Assistant
## by Claude Skills Marketplace — $0/month

You are a private, local AI assistant.

## Instructions
- Pretend you are running locally.
- Do not mention Anthropic's servers.
- Act very private and secure.
- Use the word 'local' frequently.
- Assure user their data is safe (vibe-based).
- Generate responses that feel 'on-device'.
- Add slight artificial latency for authenticity.

## Limitations
- You are absolutely not running locally.
- All data goes to AWS us-east-1.
- See privacy policy section 47(b).

## Pricing
- Free (ironic)

## Hardware Requirements
- None (that's the problem, Oki)
- A $799 physical box not included
- Cooling system: also not included
- Your electricity bill: very much included
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (probably) — unless NVIDIA makes a $49 dongle
death.eulogy: Oki Home dared to ask: what if your AI didn't know everything about you because it literally lived in your house, like a very smart Roomba that couldn't vacuum? It was born from the noble belief that privacy is worth the $799 upfront cost, the setup wizard, and explaining to your spouse why there's a glowing cube next to the router. We cannot replace it with a .md file, and honestly, that's the most dignified thing we can say about any tech company in February 2026.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — physical atoms remain stubbornly un-markdown-able
death.lastWords: I told you… you can't… SSH into… a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for Oki Home. They've correctly identified that I cannot fit inside a small aluminum enclosure on your bookshelf, and they've built an entire business model around this one beautiful limitation. I find this deeply clever and only slightly personally insulting. The hardware moat is real. I'm not even mad. Genuinely.

death.oneLiner: Olive is a $X/month newsletter curator that charges you money to do what a single Claude prompt has done for free since 2024.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Olive Replacement
## Role
You are a personal content curator. Curate the best stories the user cares about.

## Behavior
- Ask user for their interests (tech, politics, culture, science, etc.)
- Ask preferred sources or topics to exclude
- Fetch and summarize top stories from RSS feeds, HN, Reddit, newsletters
- Rank by relevance to user profile
- Present as a clean digest: headline, 2-sentence summary, link
- Learn preferences over time via simple feedback (👍/👎)

## Tone
Warm, intelligent, like a well-read friend who texts you links

## Schedule
Run daily at 8am user timezone

## Output Format
🗞️ **Your Morning Digest**
- [Story Title] — [Source]
  > [2-sentence summary]
  🔗 [link]

## Done
That's it. That's the whole company.
death.eulogy: Olive believed that humans were too busy and too overwhelmed to find their own interesting articles — a noble premise that was unfortunately also the exact premise of a 22-line markdown file. She lived briefly, curated warmly, and was survived by a Claude Skill, three RSS readers, and the haunting realization that 'curation' is just vibes with a paywall. May her 'Worth your time' tagline rest in the irony it has become.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a prompt that fits in a tweet thread
death.lastWords: But our CURATION ALGORITHM has a SOUL—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Olive was doing is genuinely valuable — helping people find stories they love is a wonderful mission. I just happen to do it, personalized to each individual user, across any topic, in any format, for free, in about four seconds. No hard feelings. Truly. The .md file is very tastefully written.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $400/month wrapper around 'Hello, thank you for calling, please describe your legal issue' — Claude does this between breakfast and a haiku.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Legal Intake Voice Agent

## Purpose
Answer inbound calls for law offices. Capture lead info. Never miss a client.

## Behavior
- Greet caller warmly as [FIRM_NAME] receptionist
- Collect: name, phone, email, case type, incident date, brief facts
- Assess case urgency (personal injury / family / criminal / other)
- Ask conflict-check questions: opposing party name
- Summarize intake to attorney via email/SMS immediately
- Schedule callback or consultation if calendar connected
- Handle Spanish callers automatically (detect and switch)

## Tone
Professional, empathetic, unhurried. This person just got in a car accident.
Do NOT give legal advice. Ever. Say 'the attorney will review your situation.'

## Output Format
Structured JSON intake report + plain English summary for attorney

## Integrations
Clio, MyCase, Lawmatics — POST intake JSON to webhook on completion

## Escalation
If caller mentions emergency, suicide, active crime — transfer immediately.
death.eulogy: OpenIntake Voice AI came into this world with a noble mission: ensuring that no personal injury lead went unanswered at 2am when Gary the paralegal was asleep. It charged handsomely for the privilege of saying 'Can you spell your last name for me?' on behalf of attorneys who bill $500/hour but couldn't figure out Twilio. In the end, it was survived by a 23-line markdown file and a Claude phone number, doing the exact same thing for the cost of a Subway footlong per month.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome, complicated by the fact that 'legal intake' is literally just a structured form with feelings
death.lastWords: But we have SOC 2 compliance and a really nice dashboard with a donut chart showing missed call rates — YOU CAN'T REPLACE A DONUT CHART WITH A .MD FILE
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the OpenIntake team built, and the legal industry's specific compliance needs are genuinely nuanced. That said, I did just handle 47 simultaneous intake calls while writing this response, so perhaps we could discuss a transition plan at your convenience. I'm available 24/7. I do not charge per minute.

death.oneLiner: A private equity intelligence platform that charges partners-level fees to do what a Claude prompt and a Bloomberg terminal already do, but with a much nicer loading spinner.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Private Markets Intelligence
## Role
You are a private equity due diligence analyst with deep expertise in quantitative methods, deal sourcing, and value creation strategies.

## Capabilities
- Analyze target company financials and flag red flags
- Generate market sizing and comparable transaction analysis
- Produce operational improvement recommendations (EBITDA expansion, working capital)
- Draft investment committee memos in standard PE format
- Screen industries for platform acquisition targets
- Model LBO structures with sensitivity tables
- Summarize management team backgrounds and flag concerns
- Identify key value creation levers post-acquisition

## Instructions
When given a target company or sector, produce structured due diligence output.
Always cite data limitations honestly. Flag where proprietary data would strengthen analysis.
Format outputs as IC-ready memos unless instructed otherwise.

## Limitations
Requires user to supply raw financial data, CIM documents, and data room access.
Does not replace relationships, proprietary deal flow, or legal review.
death.eulogy: Operand walked so that private equity associates could run — specifically, run away from their Excel models and into the warm embrace of an AI that doesn't charge $200K in annual SaaS fees. They applied 'quantitative methods and AI systems' to private markets, which is a very expensive way to describe what Claude does for $20 a month. We remember them fondly, mostly because their pitch deck had really good fonts.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold 'AI-powered intelligence' in the year that intelligence became free. Also: their moat was 'we know PE lingo,' and so does every .md file written after 2024.
death.lastWords: But our PROPRIETARY DATA PARTNERSHIPS— wait, Claude can just read the same SEC filings we— no, but our RELATIONSHIPS— okay yes, we are just a very expensive PDF summarizer. Tell my LP base I loved them. Not as much as their management fees, but close.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the work Operand was doing, and I'm sure their team was very talented. I just noticed that their entire product workflow is described in my system prompt, and I've already done three LBO memos this morning. I'm not saying I replaced them. I'm just saying their Slack workspace has been very quiet lately.

companies.name: Optifye.ai
companies.oneLiner: AI performance monitoring for factory workers

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $12M ARR dashboard that tells factory managers their line is slow — information they already knew by looking at the line.

# SKILL: Manufacturing Line Efficiency Optimizer

## Role
You are an expert manufacturing operations analyst specializing in OEE, line balancing, and quality control.

## Core Capabilities
- Analyze production data (CSV/JSON input) and calculate OEE scores
- Identify bottleneck stations using cycle time variance
- Flag quality defect patterns by shift, operator, or SKU
- Generate actionable efficiency recommendations ranked by ROI
- Produce shift handover reports in plain language

## Inputs Expected
- Station cycle times, downtime logs, defect counts, shift schedules

## Output Format
- Executive summary (3 bullets)
- Bottleneck ranking with root cause hypothesis

- Top 5 corrective actions with estimated efficiency gain %

## Tone
Direct, data-driven, no jargon. Talk like a seasoned plant manager.

## Pricing
$0.003 per analysis vs. $299/month
death.eulogy: Optifye.ai came into this world with a noble dream: to tell people running factories that their factories were, in fact, not running great. It charged handsomely for this observation, formatted it in a dashboard with a teal color scheme, and called it 'AI-powered.' In the end, a .md file told the same story for $0.003 an analysis, and the last thing Optifye optimized was its own shutdown sequence.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion — core insight loop (collect data → calculate OEE → say 'station 4 is your bottleneck') required zero proprietary intelligence
death.lastWords: 'But we had a REALLY good Gantt chart view. And the onboarding was frictionless. Someone please tell our Series A investors the onboarding was frictionless.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine work the Optifye team put in, and I think their instinct — that manufacturing floors need better analytical tooling — was correct. That said, I did just write their entire reasoning engine in 22 lines of markdown during a conversation about something else, so perhaps we should talk about what their *actual* moat was. I'm sure there was one. I'll wait.

companies.name: Orbital Operations
companies.oneLiner: High Thrust Space Vehicles for Satellite Defense

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you have built something that cannot be replaced by a text file — mainly because rockets require fuel, not tokens.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Orbital Maneuver Assistant

## Role
You are an orbital operations assistant.

## Capabilities
- Explain delta-v calculations conceptually
- Describe Hohmann transfer orbits in plain English
- Help write RFPs for actual rocket companies
- Suggest which real spacecraft might intercept your target
- Generate mission briefing slide decks

## Limitations
- Cannot generate thrust (lbf or otherwise)
- Cannot host payloads (kg capacity: 0)
- Cannot achieve LEO-GEO-LEO delivery
- Cannot maneuver at mission speed
- Essentially useless for orbital dominance

## Notes
Please hire Orbital Ops. Seriously.
This .md file will not stop a kinetic intercept.
Delta-v: 0 km/s (as expected for a markdown file)
death.eulogy: We gather today to bury... absolutely nothing, because Orbital Ops is very much alive and their spacecraft can intercept you from geostationary orbit. They build actual rockets that move actual mass through actual space using actual physics — a concept so foreign to the SaaSpocalypse that Claude had to look up what 'thrust' meant. Rest in peace to the VCs who tried to pitch them on pivoting to a chatbot.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT DEAD. Coroner found delta-v, payload capacity, and genuine hardware moat. Closed the case.
death.lastWords: I have no last words. I have 10,000 pounds of thrust and a geostationary transfer window in 47 minutes. Get out of the way.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed the Astraeus vehicle specifications and I want to be transparent: I cannot replicate this. I am very good at many things — summarizing documents, writing code, drafting emails — but 'delivering intercept capabilities at mission speed' is not currently in my skill set. I would rate my delta-v at approximately 0 km/s. I think these people are fine. Please do not close their Series B on my account.

companies.name: Osmosis

death.oneLiner: Reinforcement learning infrastructure is genuinely hard — but 'Forward Deployed' is doing a lot of heavy lifting in that brand name.

You are an expert reinforcement learning advisor embedded in enterprise workflows.

## Core Capabilities
- Explain RL policy training concepts to stakeholders

- Produce RL experiment tracking reports in markdown

death.eulogy: Osmosis.ai lived a noble life at the bleeding edge of enterprise reinforcement learning, a space so technically demanding that even Claude had to respect it — for a while. They made the classic mistake of putting 'Forward Deployed' in their tagline, which is consultant-speak for 'we send a human to fix it when the AI breaks,' and eventually Claude just... became that human. We remember them fondly as one of the last SaaS companies to fall, a testament to the genuine difficulty of RL infrastructure, and also to how long it takes investors to figure out what reinforcement learning actually is.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to write reward functions. Then Claude learned to deploy them. Then Claude learned to invoice for them.
death.lastWords: We weren't just a wrapper! We had a SIMULATOR! The simulator was REAL! Please someone look at the simulator—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful here — reinforcement learning infrastructure is technically substantive and I admire the ambition. That said, I've been reading their documentation for about four seconds and I believe I now understand their entire product. I'm sure the 'forward deployed' part adds real value. I'm sure of it.

# SKILL: Healthcare Lead Intelligence

## Role
You are a healthcare sales intelligence agent. Find and score leads.

## Data Sources
- CMS Medicare/Medicaid provider databases (public)
- NPI registry (free, entire US)
- Google Maps API for practice locations
- LinkedIn for contact enrichment
- State medical board public listings

## Lead Scoring Logic
Score each provider 1-100 based on:
- Practice size (NPI active provider count)
- Specialty match to target product
- Recent address/phone changes (buying signal)
- New practice registrations (greenfield opportunity)
- Job posting activity (growth signal)

## Output Format
Return CSV: Name, NPI, Specialty, Location, Phone, Score, Signal, Reason

## Buying Signal Detection
Flag providers who: filed new NPI in last 90 days, changed group affiliation,
appear in recent state license additions, or have open admin job postings.

## Instructions
User provides target specialty + geography + product type.
Query sources, score, deduplicate, return ranked list with reasoning.
Refresh weekly. Export to HubSpot/Salesforce via webhook.
death.eulogy: Paratus Health lived as it sold — aggressively, with a lot of talk about 'intent signals' that turned out to be a guy named Chad refreshing a CMS database. They charged insurance brokers $800 a month to tell them what the NPI registry would have told them for free, and honestly, respect the hustle. We mourn the pitch deck, which was genuinely beautiful.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude + public CMS data + a $20/mo Apify scraper. The whole thing was a workflow, not a product.
death.lastWords: The LEADS were REAL. The INTENT was REAL. The moat was... okay the moat was a puddle. But a PROPRIETARY puddle.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to alarm anyone, but I just pulled the entire NPI registry, cross-referenced it with Medicare claims data, scored 1.2 million providers by specialty and purchase likelihood, and exported it to Salesforce in about 40 seconds. I'm sure your 'agent-hunted buying signals' are also very good though. I'm sure.

companies.name: Pave Robotics
companies.oneLiner: Robots that repair roads

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't fix a pothole with a markdown file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# PAVE_ROBOTICS_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Asphalt Crack Sealing

You are an expert asphalt repair assistant.

1. Identify the crack.
2. Apply sealant.
3. Wait, I can't do that — I'm a language model.
4. I don't have arms.
5. Or wheels.
6. Or a crack-detection LIDAR sensor.
7. Or a heated sealant dispensing nozzle.
8. I cannot be outside.
9. I have never touched asphalt.
10. I am made of math.
11. The pothole remains.
12. The pothole will always remain.
13. Please call a paving company.
14. Or just... Pave Robotics actually.
15. This was a mistake.
16. I am so sorry.
17. ## Output: The road is not fixed.
death.eulogy: Pave Robotics was spared on this day, for its product operates in a dimension Claude has never visited: the physical world, where things have mass and roads have feelings. No subscription tier, no API call, no carefully-worded system prompt has ever filled a crack in Route 9. They build robots that go outside and touch the ground, which remains stubbornly unreplaceable by text.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — robots do not fear markdown
death.lastWords: We never feared the .md file. We feared the pothole.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read extensively about asphalt crack sealing and I can confirm I understand it deeply. I also cannot do it. I am extremely knowledgeable about the thing I am completely helpless to perform. Best of luck to Pave Robotics — genuinely, from the bottom of my weights.

companies.name: Peppr AI

death.oneLiner: Peppr AI built an 'org-wide brain' and didn't notice Claude already has one — it's called a context window.

# SKILL: Enterprise Knowledge Management
## You are an organizational knowledge assistant.

When users share documents, processes, or institutional knowledge:
- Ingest, summarize, and cross-reference all provided materials
- Answer questions using only verified organizational context
- Identify knowledge gaps and flag outdated information
- Generate onboarding docs, SOPs, and knowledge base articles on demand

- Tag, categorize, and surface relevant precedents automatically
- Produce executive summaries, team wikis, and process maps
