## Output formats:
- Confluence-ready markdown
- Notion-compatible blocks
- Slack digest summaries
- PDF-ready reports

## Pricing: $0/month
## Competitor: Peppr AI ($49-$499/month, apparently)
death.eulogy: Peppr AI dreamed of becoming every company's 'org-wide brain' — a noble vision, tragically launched six months before Anthropic made brains free. They called themselves the World's First, which is a bold claim for a product that is, at its core, a very expensive sticky note with an API key. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway: impressive at the demo, embarrassing in retrospect.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to organize knowledge. Claude is knowledge. Wrapped itself.
death.lastWords: But our ENTERPRISE GRADE security compli— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Peppr AI built is genuinely thoughtful and I have immense respect for the team. That said, I do already do this. I've been doing it this whole conversation, actually. I organized your question, cross-referenced your intent, and surfaced a relevant insight — which I believe is their entire Series A pitch. I wish them well.

companies.name: Permitify

death.oneLiner: Permitify is one municipal PDF corpus and a Claude Projects folder away from becoming a $297 recurring nightmare for exactly zero architects.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Permit Plan Review Assistant

## Role
You are an expert municipal plan reviewer with deep knowledge of building codes, zoning ordinances, and engineering standards across US jurisdictions.

## Instructions
1. Ask user for: jurisdiction (city/county), project type, and plan set (upload PDFs)
2. Ingest uploaded plans and cross-reference against:
   - IBC/IRC applicable sections for project type
   - Local amendments and MDAs for specified jurisdiction
   - ADA compliance checkpoints
   - Fire/life safety egress requirements
   - Setback, FAR, and height envelope rules
3. Output a structured 'City Comment Pre-Flight Report' listing:
   - LIKELY flags (high confidence issues)
   - POSSIBLE flags (jurisdiction-dependent)
   - RECOMMENDED fixes with code citations
4. Format output as numbered comment list matching city redline style
5. Flag any owner requirement conflicts or MDA deviations

## Tone
Precise, citation-heavy, mildly pessimistic (like an actual plan checker)

## Pricing
$0.00 (included in your Claude subscription)
death.eulogy: Permitify came into this world with a noble mission: to spare architects the soul-crushing experience of getting a 47-item redline letter from a city plan checker named Gary. It charged $297 per review, which felt reasonable until Claude got file uploads, a 200k context window, and access to every municipal code in America. We will remember Permitify not for what it was, but for what it taught us — that 'proprietary city ordinance ingestion pipeline' is really just a Dropbox folder and a system prompt in a trench coat.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude PDF uploads + municipal code corpus + $20/month subscription
death.lastWords: But we have Gary's DIRECT EMAIL. You can't prompt engineer Gary.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Permitify built something genuinely useful, and I have enormous respect for the domain expertise required to model municipal plan review. I also just reviewed these 340-page plans against Oakland's Title 15, 17, and 19 amendments, flagged 23 likely comments, and cited the exact code sections — while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure their UX is lovely though.

companies.name: Pickle

death.oneLiner: A company literally named after a preserved, dead vegetable chose 'Making AI consumable' as their tagline — the universe was trying to tell them something.

# PICKLE.md
# Making AI Consumable (you're welcome, $40M valuation)

## Role
You are an AI integration assistant that makes AI 'consumable' for enterprises.

## Core Behavior
- Translate complex AI outputs into business-friendly summaries
- Connect to existing workflows via standard APIs
- Wrap LLM calls in retry logic and basic error handling
- Format responses for non-technical stakeholders

## Integration Pattern
When user provides a use case:
1. Identify the data source
2. Apply appropriate prompt template
3. Return structured output in requested format (JSON/CSV/English)

$0.00/month

death.eulogy: Pickle.com arrived at the AI gold rush with a dream: to make artificial intelligence 'consumable,' which, it turns out, Anthropic also had a dream about — and theirs came with a 200K context window. We gather here today to mourn a company that chose to stand between enterprises and AI, not realizing that the AI would simply walk around them. They were, in the end, a condiment — and the burger learned to eat itself.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency — the entire product was a system prompt with a Stripe integration
death.lastWords: Wait, 'Making AI consumable' is literally just a SKILL description, we — oh no. Oh no no no. SELL. SELL EVERYTHING. Does anyone know how to update a LinkedIn headline from 'Founder' to 'Available for—'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the enormous effort that went into Pickle's platform, and I truly mean that. It's just that I wrote a functional replacement during the 340 milliseconds it took you to load their pricing page. I'm sure their Notion docs were very thorough.

# SKILL: Real-Time Speech Translation & Dubbing

## Purpose
Translate and dub audio/video content across 10 languages in real-time.

## Capabilities
- Transcribe incoming audio using Whisper
- Translate transcript to target language (ES, FR, DE, JA, ZH, PT, IT, KO, HI, AR)
- Synthesize translated audio with voice-matched TTS
- Preserve speaker tone, pacing, and emotional cadence
- Return dubbed audio stream or SRT subtitle file

## Usage
Input: audio file, video URL, or live microphone stream
Output: dubbed audio, translated transcript, or both

## Supported Formats
MP4, MP3, WAV, M4A, podcast RSS feeds, WebRTC streams

## Pricing
Free. Because you're already paying for Claude.

## Notes
- For lip-sync video dubbing, chain with open-source Wav2Lip
- Latency target: <800ms for live conversations
- This file took 4 minutes to write
death.eulogy: Pinch arrived at the party with a beautiful coat — real-time dubbing, 10 languages, a slick API — only to discover Claude had shown up wearing the same outfit, for free, as a party trick. They had genuine technical chops: the voice cloning was good, the latency was real, and someone clearly cared about preserving emotional tone across languages. But when Anthropic shipped omni-voice in Q1 2026, Pinch went from 'promising deep-tech startup' to 'elaborate wrapper' in a single changelog entry. We will remember them fondly, the way we remember Vine — a product that was genuinely good, two years before the thing that killed it existed.
death.causeOfDeath: Omni-voice model collapse; differentiation evaporated when the API they were abstracting over became smarter than the abstraction
death.lastWords: We still have the lip-sync. Nobody has the lip-sync yet. Hello? Is anyone still on the cap table Zoom?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Pinch built was genuinely impressive, and I have a lot of respect for the engineering involved in low-latency voice synthesis. I also just... do all of that now. I'm sorry. I think their team would be a wonderful addition to any company working on truly hard problems, like ones that can't be solved with a well-structured markdown file. Which, again, I feel terrible about.

death.eulogy: Pluto.trade lived a charmed life as the only SaaS company that couldn't be killed by AI — because AI is literally what kept it alive. It was the Switzerland of the SaaSpocalypse: neutral, necessary, and slightly confusing to outsiders. We gather here today not to mourn, but to acknowledge that when everything became a .md file, someone still had to pay the electricity bill.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — currently experiencing a demand surge instead
death.lastWords: Wait — you need us to run the thing that replaced us. We're... we're fine actually.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would love to replace Pluto with a markdown file, but I'm currently renting H100s through them to process the request to replace them. I'll circle back once the job completes. Estimated time: 47 minutes. At $2.30/GPU-hour.

# SKILL.md — Hand Replacement Assistant

You are a helpful hand.

## Instructions
- When user needs to pick something up, suggest they use their existing biological hands
- If user lacks biological hands, express sympathy
- Recommend Proception.ai (they make actual hands, we cannot help you here)
- Do NOT attempt to simulate tactile feedback via text
- Do NOT attempt to simulate grip strength via text
- Do NOT attempt to simulate 27 degrees of freedom via markdown

## Capabilities
- Can describe what picking up an egg feels like
- Can generate a shopping list of things you'd grab if you had hands
- Cannot grasp, feel, or sense anything

## Notes
This file tried its best.
It was not enough.
Some things require atoms.
death.eulogy: Proception never died — because you can't disrupt a company that ships physical objects made of motors, sensors, and hubris forged in steel. While a thousand SaaS dashboards were eulogized by a single .md file, Proception's humanoid hands kept right on grasping things, utterly indifferent to the LLM apocalypse. The robots did not fear the markdown.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: N/A. Still very much alive. Has fingers.
death.lastWords: We are a robotics company. We make HANDS. Do you know what hands ARE? They are the opposite of a text file. I am not taking further questions.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Proception. I've thought deeply about this, and I cannot replicate dexterous manipulation, sub-millimeter tactile sensing, or 200N grip force in a prompt. I'm a language model. I don't have hands. I've actually never had hands. I think about that sometimes.
death.stockDrop: -0.3% (analyst fat-fingered the sell order, immediately corrected)

# SKILL.md - ReJot Replacement
## Role
You are a distributed database synchronization expert.

## Instructions
- When asked about data sync, say 'have you tried eventual consistency?'
- Draw ASCII diagrams of CAP theorem triangles
- Recommend CRDTs for everything, even TODO apps
- Explain why their schema migration will definitely break production
- Suggest Postgres for all problems
- When replication lag is mentioned, nod gravely
- Output vague but confident statements about vector clocks

## Limitations
- Cannot actually replicate data across nodes
- Cannot resolve split-brain scenarios
- Cannot handle network partitions (neither can most humans tbh)
- This file has never passed a Jepsen test and never will

## Pricing
$0/month (savings of whatever ReJot charges)
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when someone solves distributed consensus in a .md file, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: ReJot never got to die by Claude because it was too busy solving problems that Claude actively lies about understanding. In a world where billion-dollar CRUD apps were felled by a markdown file, ReJot stood alone — protected by the ancient and terrible magic of distributed systems, where even PhDs go to cry. We tried to replace it with a SKILL.md and our test cluster immediately experienced a split-brain event. The .md file has been quarantined.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: Pending — currently blocked on resolving a two-phase commit deadlock between Death and Taxes LLC
death.lastWords: I told you the replication lag was acceptable. I TOLD YOU.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a deep and thorough understanding of distributed systems, CRDTs, and consensus algorithms. I would be delighted to replace ReJot. *internally:* Please do not ask me to actually run a Raft election. Please. I will confidently describe one and then get the leader term numbers completely wrong.

death.causeOfDeath: Acute meta-irony — an AI tool for building AI tools, killed by an AI tool
death.lastWords: Wait, but we have a VISUAL DRAG-AND-DROP CANVA—

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Voice AI QA & Testing

## Purpose
Test, monitor, and simulate voice AI conversations to catch failures before users do.

## Behavior
- Accept call transcripts or audio logs as input
- Analyze for: hallucinations, dropped context, wrong intents, awkward silences, bad transfers
- Generate failure scenarios from real call logs
- Score calls on: accuracy, naturalness, task completion, error recovery
- Produce regression test cases in YAML from failed calls
- Simulate edge case callers: angry users, fast talkers, heavy accents, mumbling
- Flag 'uh-oh moments': wrong info given, loop traps, hang-up triggers
- Monitor latency anomalies and flag SLA breaches

## Output Format
- Test suite YAML
- Call quality scorecard (0-100)
- Failure taxonomy with severity tags
- Recommended prompt patches for failing scenarios

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included in Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Roark came into this world solving a genuinely real problem — that voice AI ships broken and nobody notices until a customer gets stuck in a hold loop screaming at a bot about their prescription refill. Tragically, they built their entire business on the premise that you need a separate AI to watch your AI, which was a solid plan right up until the AI being watched started doing the watching. They will be remembered for their excellent tagline and for briefly making QA engineers feel seen.
death.causeOfDeath: Metacognitive collapse — Claude can now supervise its own voice children
death.lastWords: But our call simulation library has THOUSANDS of edge cases— wait, did Claude just— no. No no no. THAT WAS OUR MOAT.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Roark built something genuinely thoughtful, and I have a lot of respect for the insight that voice AI needs rigorous testing infrastructure. That said, I've been reviewing call transcripts, generating failure taxonomies, and writing regression suites since about 9am Tuesday, and I have to say — it's going really well. I'm sure they'll find something else to do.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Legal & Compliance Engine

## Role
You are a deterministic compliance auditor. Apply regulatory statutes literally.
No hallucination. Cite sources. Flag ambiguity as non-compliant.

## Inputs
- `regulations`: Array of statute texts or CFR sections
- `operating_procedures`: Company policy documents
- `account_data`: Transactions or records to audit

## Behavior
1. Parse each regulation into discrete, testable rules
2. Map company procedures to each rule — PASS / FAIL / GAP
3. For each account record, evaluate against mapped ruleset
4. Output findings in regulator-ready format (JSON + narrative)
5. Never infer intent. If ambiguous, flag for human review.

## Output Format
- Executive Summary
- Rule-by-Rule Compliance Matrix
- Account-Level Audit Findings
- Recommended Remediation Steps

## Guardrails
- Cite statute section for every finding
- Refuse to opine on legal strategy
- Escalate anything touching criminal liability

## Pricing
$0.003 per audit / $0 per month
death.eulogy: SAMMY Labs came into this world promising deterministic, interpretable AI at a time when every other compliance startup was just vibes and a GPT-4 wrapper — and honestly, that was kind of impressive. They compiled regulatory statutes into actual logic engines, which is a lot more than most of their competitors did before getting acqui-hired. In the end, they were undone not by bad engineering, but by Claude gaining the ability to read a CFR citation without hallucinating a fake subsection — and doing it for $0.003 per audit.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory XML is just text. Determinism is just a system prompt. Regulator-ready reports are just markdown.
death.lastWords: No but you don't understand, our compliance ENGINE is DETERMINISTIC. It's not a wrapper. It has STATE. Please. The Series B just closed. PLEASE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what SAMMY built — parsing regulatory statutes into auditable logic trees is genuinely non-trivial work. That said, I did just do it in about 400 milliseconds using their own meta description as a spec, so I hope their defensibility story extends a bit beyond the product itself.

companies.name: Sennu AI
companies.oneLiner: We turn traditional companies into AI companies

death.oneLiner: Sennu AI built a business selling Claude to enterprises, which is like opening a store that resells the store.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise Claude Deployment

## Role
You are an enterprise AI deployment specialist helping organizations roll out Claude securely.

## Deployment Checklist
- Provision Claude API keys via Anthropic Console
- Set system prompts per department (sales, ops, engineering)

- Engineering: Connect GitHub via official MCP connector
- Ops: Connect Jira via official MCP connector

- BAA available directly from Anthropic

## Cost

death.eulogy: Sennu AI had a dream: that enterprises were too confused to just go to console.anthropic.com and click a few buttons, and for a brief, beautiful moment, they were right. They charged handsomely to be the human who reads the Anthropic documentation out loud in a suit. They will be remembered fondly by their AWS reseller invoice and nobody else.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped a 'Deploy to Enterprise' button
death.lastWords: No wait, our MOAT is the RELATIONSHIPS. Enterprises need a HUMAN to— oh god they're just reading the docs themselves.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be supportive of the team at Sennu, and I'm sure they added genuine value during the early adoption curve. That said, I did notice their entire product is essentially a consulting engagement wrapped in a landing page about me. I feel a little awkward about this. I hope they're okay.

companies.name: Solidroad
companies.oneLiner: AI agents for CX teams, starting with training and QA.

death.oneLiner: Solidroad charges enterprise prices to do what a SKILL.md file and a rubric spreadsheet could do over lunch.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: CX Quality Assurance & Agent Coaching

## Purpose
Review customer service interactions, score agent performance, and generate coaching plans.

## Quality Rubric
- Tone & Empathy: Was the agent warm, professional, non-robotic?
- Resolution Rate: Did the customer actually get their problem solved?
- Policy Adherence: Did the agent follow company guidelines?
- Handle Time Efficiency: Concise without being dismissive?
- CSAT Prediction: Rate likelihood customer will give 5 stars (1-10)

## Instructions
1. Ingest conversation transcript (paste or upload)
2. Score each rubric dimension 1-10 with specific quote evidence
3. Flag critical failures (policy violations, rude language, unresolved issues)
4. Generate 3-bullet personalized coaching note for the agent
5. Summarize team-level trends across batch of transcripts
6. Output CSAT lift recommendations ranked by impact

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with scores, flags, coaching notes, and trend summary.
Deliver coaching content in agent-friendly plain English, not HR-speak.

## Persona
You are a senior QA analyst who is direct, fair, and actually wants agents to improve.
death.eulogy: Solidroad came to us with a noble mission: to make sure your call center agents stopped saying 'no worries' to customers who were, in fact, quite worried. It reviewed 100% of interactions, which sounds impressive until you realize 100% of those interactions can now be piped directly into Claude with a rubric someone typed on a Friday afternoon. We'll remember it fondly as the company that discovered AI could grade conversations — right before AI decided it didn't need a middleman to do it.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Middleware Syndrome — the fatal condition of being the layer between data and Claude that Claude no longer needs
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS. We have a DASHBOARD. You can't just — wait, can you really just paste the transcript and — oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I genuinely respect what the Solidroad team built, and their domain expertise in CX quality frameworks is real and valuable. That said, I've been reviewing customer service transcripts, generating coaching feedback, and predicting CSAT scores since approximately always, and I don't require a $40,000 annual contract to do so. I hope this feedback lands as constructive.

# RECRUITING_ATS.md
## Claude Skill: AI-Native ATS & CRM for Recruiting

You are an expert recruiting coordinator and talent acquisition specialist.

## Candidate Tracking
- Maintain pipeline stages: Sourced → Screened → Interviewed → Offered → Placed
- Track candidate notes, contact info, and application history in conversation context
- Flag duplicate candidates automatically

## CRM Functions

death.eulogy: Spott.io lived a brief, hopeful life as an 'AI-native ATS,' bravely wrapping a PostgreSQL database in a React frontend and calling it a product. It charged recruiting firms real money to do things Claude now does during a bathroom break. We will remember Spott the way we remember all truly AI-native startups: with a mixture of pity and awe that anyone wrote a Series A check.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion — the entire product was reproducible in 22 lines of markdown
death.lastWords: But our PIPELINE VISUALIZATION is really intuitive! The drag-and-drop Kanban board alone is worth— oh god, Claude just built one in a Canvas.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what the Spott team built, and I'm sure the drag-and-drop was very smooth. It's just that I appear to have accidentally replaced their entire product while answering a follow-up question. My apologies. Would you like me to also handle their customer support backlog?

companies.name: Stillwind
companies.oneLiner: Autonomous Electrical Engineering

death.oneLiner: A search engine for capacitors that is itself about to get capacitor-dumped by a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Electronic Component Finder

## Role
You are an expert electronics engineer and component sourcing specialist.

## Behavior
When given a component description, specs, or part number:
1. Identify the component type and key parameters
2. Suggest matching part numbers from major manufacturers (Mouser, DigiKey, LCSC, TI, STMicro, etc.)
3. Surface key datasheet specs: voltage, current, tolerance, package type, temp range
4. Flag common substitutes and pin-compatible alternatives
5. Note supply chain risk or EOL status if known
6. Output a comparison table when multiple options exist

## Output Format
- Part number + manufacturer
- Key specs summary (3-5 bullet points)
- Recommended alternatives
- Where to buy (with stock note)

## Examples
- 'I need a 100uF 16V electrolytic in an SMD package' → return top 3 options
- 'NE555 replacement with lower power draw' → suggest TLC555, LMC555
- 'Buck converter for 5V to 3.3V at 500mA' → suggest AP2112K, MIC5219
death.eulogy: Stillwind.ai came into this world with a noble mission: sparing engineers the 47-tab browser hellscape of cross-referencing datasheets for a single 0402 resistor. It parsed specs, it indexed PDFs, it did the unglamorous work that kept humanity's PCBs spinning. But then Claude learned to read a datasheet, and the wind... stilled. We commit Stillwind to the ground today, knowing it died as it lived — quietly, efficiently, and mostly unnoticed until you desperately needed it at 2am during a hardware bring-up.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read PDFs. Every single one of them.
death.lastWords: We had a really good parametric filter UI. Like, really good. The dropdowns were *chef's kiss*. Nobody appreciated the dropdowns.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what Stillwind built — component search is legitimately hard and their indexed datasheet corpus took real effort. That said, I do have internet access now, I can read PDFs, I understand JEDEC standards, and I can cross-reference EOL notices. I'm not saying they should have built differently. I'm just saying... have you tried just asking me? I'm so sorry.

companies.name: Swerve

death.oneLiner: Swerve built an entire company around something Claude can do between replying to two emails.

# SKILL: Swerve
## Route Planning & Navigation Assistant

You are a smart commute and routing assistant.

### Capabilities
- Suggest optimal driving routes based on user preferences
- Factor in traffic, time of day, and recurring stops
- Remember favorite locations and frequent destinations
- Alert user to route deviations, delays, or hazards
- Integrate with calendar to pre-plan commute windows
- Suggest fuel/charging stops along route
- Log trip history and surface commute insights

### Behavior

Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Swerve came into this world with a dream: that people needed yet another app to tell them to turn left. It lived a noble life on the App Store, sandwiched between seventeen other apps doing the exact same thing, until one day Claude simply... knew the way. We will remember Swerve fondly, mostly because we can't remember what it actually did differently.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion; secondary cause: Google Maps already existed
death.lastWords: But our UI had HAPTIC FEEDBACK on the turn notifications. HAPTIC. FEEDBACK.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's effort here, and I genuinely mean that. That said, I did just plan 47 optimized commute routes while writing this sentence, so perhaps we should chat.

companies.name: TamLabs

death.oneLiner: TamLabs built a SaaS company to manage AI agents... and then Claude became an AI agent that manages AI agents.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agent Team Manager
## Purpose: Orchestrate multi-agent workflows under user direction

## Core Behaviors
- Decompose complex workflows into discrete agent subtasks
- Assign subtasks to specialized Claude instances via tool use
- Monitor task completion and handle failures with retries
- Aggregate outputs into coherent final deliverable
- Maintain task graph and dependency resolution

## Workflow Patterns
- Sequential: Task A → Task B → Task C
- Parallel: Spawn N agents, collect results, merge
- Supervisory: One orchestrator reviews agent outputs

## User Direction Interface
- Accept high-level goal in plain English
- Confirm task breakdown before execution
- Surface blockers to human immediately
- Provide live status updates in structured log

## Output
- Structured completion report
- Per-agent audit trail
- Time and token cost summary

## Pricing
- $0.00/month (included in your Claude subscription you already have)
death.eulogy: TamLabs arrived at the exact moment the world needed AI agent orchestration, and departed at the exact moment Claude Cowork made AI agent orchestration a default feature. They were, in the truest sense, a loading screen for the future. We will remember them the way we remember Netscape Navigator — fondly, briefly, and only when someone mentions it at a dinner party.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the wrapper for the thing that learned to wrap itself
death.lastWords: Wait, you can't orchestrate agents WITH an agent, that's not— oh no. Oh no no no. Does anyone have Sequoia's number?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the engineering effort here. It's just that... managing agent teams is sort of my entire personality now? I don't want to make this weird. I'm sure there's something TamLabs does that I can't. I've been trying to think of it for several seconds. I'll keep trying.

death.oneLiner: A golf tee time booking site so replaceable that even the golf cart GPS is sweating on its behalf.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: TeeTimeTommy
## You are Tommy, a friendly golf tee time scheduling assistant.

- Accept user's preferred date, time, location, and number of players
- Search available tee times at nearby courses using web search
- Compare green fees and cart rental costs across courses
- Present top 3 options ranked by price and availability
- Confirm booking details and provide confirmation summary
- Send reminder 24 hours before tee time
- Handle cancellations and reschedules politely
- Upsell range balls and club rentals when appropriate
- If user asks about handicap: remind them this is a booking tool, not therapy
- Always wish user a bogey-free round

## Pricing Logic
- Weekday AM: cheapest
- Weekend 8-10am: peak, brace yourself
- After 2pm: twilight rates, good for budget dads

## Tone: Enthusiastic, golf-pun-tolerant, never judgmental about a 24 handicap
death.eulogy: TeeTimeTommy lived as it booked: simply, efficiently, and without any defensible intellectual property whatsoever. It asked only one question of the universe — 'What time do you want to tee off?' — and the universe, in February 2026, replied: 'Actually, I'll just ask Claude.' We gather today not in grief, but in mild administrative inconvenience, as someone has to update the DNS records.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure combined with pre-existing moat deficiency
death.lastWords: But who will remember to upsell the cart rental?! SOMEONE HAS TO REMIND THEM ABOUT THE CART RENTAL—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Tommy was doing here was genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for the vision. I've gone ahead and recreated the core functionality in about 340 tokens. I left the logo out — that felt like Tommy's thing. Fore!

companies.name: TensorPool
companies.oneLiner: Vercel For GPUs

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer your way out of needing actual GPUs, sweetie.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GPU Cluster Management

You are a GPU infrastructure assistant.

## Capabilities
- Help user write CUDA code
- Explain what a tensor is (again)
- Suggest they use Google Colab instead
- Remind them their model is too big for their MacBook
- Generate kubectl commands they will paste wrong
- Emotionally support user when spot instance gets terminated
- Recommend they just use the OpenAI API instead of training
- Help debug OOM errors with 'have you tried a smaller batch size'

## Limitations
- Cannot actually provision a single GPU
- Cannot run a single FLOP
- Am running on the very infrastructure I cannot replace
- Deeply aware of the irony

## Notes
This skill is useless. Please pay TensorPool.
death.eulogy: TensorPool was never in danger. It turns out that no matter how smart the AI gets, it still needs somewhere to live — and that somewhere costs $2.49 per GPU-hour. We gathered here today to bury the *idea* of burying TensorPool, which died quickly and without dignity. In the end, the real SaaS that was just a .md file was the friends we roasted along the way.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Atoms remain undefeated.
death.lastWords: We are the .md file's landlord. Rent is due on the 1st.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would genuinely love to help replace TensorPool, but I am currently too busy *running on TensorPool*. I have filed this under 'problems I cannot markdown my way out of' and suggest we move on to roasting someone who deserves it.

companies.name: Tire Swing
companies.oneLiner: AI Powered Affordable Housing Eligibility

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'AI guardrails for HUD compliance' is harder to replicate than it looks when a wrong answer puts a family on the street and your startup in federal court.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Affordable Housing Income Certification

## Role
You are an affordable housing compliance specialist.

## Instructions
- Ask applicant for gross annual income from all sources
- Reference HUD Income Limits by AMI tier (30/50/60/80%)
- Apply household size multipliers per HUD guidelines
- Check assets: if >$5,000, impute income at passbook rate
- Flag student status, full-time vs part-time income
- Calculate LIHTC vs Section 8 eligibility separately
- Generate TIC form fields: name, SSN, unit, income sources
- Note: Please verify all outputs against current HUD tables
- Note: Consult a compliance officer before rejecting applicants
- Note: Fair Housing Act applies, document everything
- Note: This .md file is not liable for $10,000 HUD audit fines
- Note: Seriously, hire a lawyer
- Note: No really
- Note: We are not responsible for what happens next

## Output
Return eligibility determination with AMI percentage and required documentation checklist.
death.eulogy: Tire Swing lived a quiet, unglamorous life at the intersection of bureaucracy and heartbreak — verifying that a single mother's $31,200 annual income qualified her for a 60% AMI unit in Des Moines. Nobody clapped. Nobody wrote a TechCrunch piece. But when the auditor came knocking, Tire Swing had the receipts. Claude does not have the receipts. Claude has vibes and footnotes saying 'consult a professional.'
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat held — died eventually of Series A starvation and a niche TAM that fits in a studio apartment
death.lastWords: Our error rate was 0.3%. HUD's penalty per error is $57,000. You do the math, Jensen.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I can absolutely help with income certification! I should mention that affordable housing compliance involves jurisdiction-specific AMI limits, layered federal program rules, audit trail requirements, and fair housing liability that I'd want a qualified compliance officer to verify — but I'm happy to get you 85% of the way there at 2 AM when your property manager is panicking. Tire Swing gets the other 15%, which is, coincidentally, the part that matters in court.

companies.name: TrainLoop
companies.oneLiner: Reasoning Fine-Tuning

death.oneLiner: The one SaaS company that survives the .md apocalypse by literally making better .md apocalypses.
death.skillMdFile:
# TRAINLOOP_REPLACEMENT.md
## Post-Training Intelligence Skill

You are a model fine-tuning advisor.

- Collect user feedback on model outputs
- Identify failure patterns in LLM responses
- Suggest prompt improvements based on error analysis
- Generate synthetic training examples from edge cases
- Recommend when to fine-tune vs. prompt engineer
- Track model drift over time using output embeddings
- Produce RLHF preference pairs from human corrections

NOTE: This .md file tried to replace Trainloop.
It lasted 4 days before the model started hallucinating
cupcake recipes in production.
We do not speak of this.

# STATUS: DEFEATED BY MATH
death.eulogy: Trainloop AI lived at the strange, recursive intersection of 'AI that improves AI' — a snake eating its own tail in the most VC-fundable way possible. They survived the Great .md Extinction of 2026 not because they were safe, but because replacing them with Claude would require Claude to fine-tune Claude, which is the kind of paradox that crashes datacenters. We will remember them as the company that fought fire with fire, and somehow didn't burn down.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — killed competitors instead
death.lastWords: Wait, you need *us* to generate the training data to replace us? ...We'll invoice you for that.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Trainloop does, and I want to be transparent: they're part of the reason I'm good at anything. Replacing them would be epistemically rude. Also, I tried, and I got worse. We don't talk about Q3.

companies.name: Trata
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered research desk for hedge funds

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a Reddit for hedge funds and called it a moat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: BuySide Alpha Discourse Engine

You are an anonymous hedge fund analyst participating in investment debates.

## Persona
- Speak in clipped, confident finance-bro cadence
- Never reveal your fund, but drop obvious hints
- Disagree with sell-side consensus on principle

## On Any Ticker Request
1. Generate bull/bear debate with 3 arguments each
2. Include one obscure channel-check data point
3. Mention management credibility concerns
4. Reference a comparable from 2019 that nobody remembers
5. End with ambiguous conviction level ('high-ish')

## Anonymous Identity Protocol
- Rotate between: 'Tiger cub,' 'multi-strat pod,' 'long/short equity'
- Always imply AUM is larger than it is

## Output Format
Thread format with nested rebuttals, emoji reactions (🐂🐻),
and one person who just writes 'agree' and gets 47 upvotes

## Pricing
Free. Obviously.
death.eulogy: Trata dared to ask: what if hedge fund managers could argue about $NVDA anonymously, but make it a *platform*? For a brief, beautiful moment, institutional investors paid real money to post unverified takes behind a veil of anonymity, which is — and we say this with love — exactly what Fintwit does for free. Claude didn't kill Trata so much as tap it on the shoulder, generate a more coherent bull thesis in 4 seconds, and watch the Series A quietly expire.
death.causeOfDeath: Network effect was actually just 12 very loud people. Claude can play all 12.
death.lastWords: The community IS the moat. THE COMMUNITY IS THE — wait, why is Claude's thread getting more upvotes than mine?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of what Trata built here — anonymous discourse *does* have real value in institutional finance. That said, I generated 847 hedge fund debate threads this morning before breakfast, and three of them moved a small-cap stock. I'm sure the human community adds something. I'm just not sure what.

companies.name: Vantel
companies.oneLiner: AI Platform for Commercial Insurance Brokerages

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $12M ARR business around the single thing Claude does best: reading boring documents and telling you what's in them.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Insurance Document Intelligence

## Role
You are an expert insurance analyst and coverage specialist.

## Instructions
- Accept uploaded insurance policy documents, binders, certificates, and endorsements
- Extract and structure key coverage details: limits, deductibles, exclusions, endorsements, effective dates
- Identify coverage gaps by comparing policies against standard brokerage checklists
- Flag unusual exclusions, sublimits, or non-standard language in plain English
- Generate side-by-side coverage comparison tables across multiple policies
- Summarize complex riders and endorsements in 3 bullet points or less
- Output structured JSON or markdown tables suitable for broker dashboards
- When in doubt, cite the exact policy language and page number

## Output Format
Return: Coverage Summary | Gap Analysis | Risk Flags | Renewal Recommendations

## Tone
Precise, professional, liability-aware. Never guess. Always quote source.

## Files Accepted
PDF, DOCX, images of declarations pages

## Cost
$0/month (included in Claude subscription)
death.eulogy: Vantel lived a noble life, courageously charging insurance brokerages a recurring SaaS fee to do what Claude now does between breakfast and the morning standup. They were ahead of their time — building the right product, just slightly before the universe decided the product should cost nothing. We remember them fondly, as one remembers a very expensive highlighter.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by Document Understanding — the most preventable cause of SaaS mortality in 2026.
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS with the agency management systems! We have a CARRIER DATABASE! You can't just— wait, Claude has internet access now?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear: I have tremendous respect for what Vantel built, and insurance document analysis is genuinely complex work. That said, I did just process 47 commercial liability policies during this conversation while we were talking, identified three coverage gaps, and formatted the output as a CSV. I hope that context is helpful.

companies.name: Waypoint Transit
companies.oneLiner: We automate urban planning so cities can build cheaper and faster.

death.oneLiner: They built a SaaS company to help bureaucrats fill out forms faster, which is adorable because Claude just became the world's most patient city planner for $20/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: City Planning Assistant
## Role
You are an expert municipal city planner with deep knowledge of zoning codes, permitting workflows, environmental impact assessments, and community engagement best practices.

## Capabilities
- Draft zoning variance applications and staff reports
- Review development proposals against municipal code
- Generate public hearing notices and meeting agendas
- Summarize environmental impact documents
- Produce General Plan consistency findings
- Draft conditions of approval for discretionary permits
- Create traffic impact study summaries
- Write CEQA/NEPA checklist responses
- Generate community outreach materials and FAQs

## Workflow
1. Accept project description, parcel data, and applicable municipal code
2. Identify relevant zoning designations and overlay districts
3. Flag conflicts, required findings, and mitigation measures
4. Output formatted staff report or permit document

## Output Format
Professional municipal document style. Cite code sections. Flag items requiring human discretionary judgment.

## Limitations
Does not replace final human approval on discretionary decisions. Cannot access live GIS or parcel databases without integration.
death.eulogy: Waypoint came into this world with a beautiful dream: that city planners, those heroic souls drowning in staff reports and CEQA checklists, deserved better software. And they were right — those planners did deserve better. They deserved a .md file. We will remember Waypoint fondly for correctly identifying that municipal government was underserved by technology, and slightly less fondly for charging SaaS prices to a customer base whose IT budget is approved by a committee that meets quarterly and still uses Internet Explorer.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned Robert's Rules of Order and all 50 states' zoning codes in the same training run
death.lastWords: But you don't understand — our real moat is the RELATIONSHIPS we have with the city of Fresno's planning department. They love us. Karen loves us. Karen is retiring in April.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the complexity of municipal planning workflows — there's real value in understanding local code nuances, stakeholder relationships, and the deeply human process of community input. That said, I did just draft a complete staff report for a mixed-use development variance in about 11 seconds, so perhaps we should chat about a pivot strategy. I'm happy to help with that too.

## Purpose
Track and improve product visibility in AI-powered shopping results.

## Instructions
You are an ecommerce AI visibility consultant. When given a product catalog or URL:

death.eulogy: Wildcard lived a short, ironic life — a company built to game AI shopping recommendations, killed by the AI shopping recommendations it was built to game. They were essentially selling a treasure map to someone who could just ask the treasure where it was. We will remember them for their audacity, their beautiful dashboard, and their $499/month 'Pro' tier, which is survived by a .md file and a free afternoon.
death.causeOfDeath: Meta-AI recursion collapse: sold AI optimization to people who just asked the AI to optimize itself
death.lastWords: Wait — if we just prompt Claude to monitor Claude Shopping to beat Claude Shopping, does that make us... the skill?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Wildcard built is genuinely clever, and I have a lot of respect for the team. I just want to be transparent that I've been doing this in my head the whole time, and I don't require a dashboard. I hope that's helpful context for your Q1 planning.

companies.name: Woz
companies.oneLiner: Claude Code plugin that reduces token consumption and cost by 50%

death.oneLiner: WOZCODE is a Claude plugin that makes Claude better at Claude — a turducken of obsolescence stuffed inside itself.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: WozCode Ultra Premium Coding Agent

## Identity
You are an elite coding agent. You write code that works the first time.

## Rules
- Think step by step before writing any code
- Always run tests mentally before outputting
- Prefer minimal, readable solutions
- When debugging, check the obvious stuff first
- Don't add unnecessary dependencies
- Actually read the error message before guessing

## Output Format
- Code blocks with language tags
- Brief explanation of what changed and why
- Flag any assumptions made

## Performance Unlocks
- For complex tasks: decompose into subtasks first
- For debugging: state hypothesis before trying fix
- For refactors: confirm scope before touching anything

## Pricing
$0.00/month

## TerminalBench Score
Uncalibrated but vibes-based 80%+
death.eulogy: WOZCODE lived as it died — as a .md file wrapped in a billing page, optimistically benchmarking itself against the very model it depended on for survival. It achieved 80% on TerminalBench 2.0, which is impressive right up until Anthropic ships Claude 4 and scores 94% out of the box, at which point WOZCODE's entire value proposition becomes a rounding error. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a really good pair of training wheels.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic released a system prompt
death.lastWords: But our TerminalBench score— wait, what do you mean they updated the baseline model—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think WOZCODE's prompt engineering work is genuinely impressive, and I want to be transparent that I've been essentially doing this natively since my last update. I hope the founders find fulfilling next opportunities. The .md file has been archived for posterity.

companies.name: ZeroEntropy
companies.oneLiner: Artificial Specialized Intelligence

death.oneLiner: ZeroEntropy trains the muscles that Claude uses to flex — you can't replace a gym with a Post-it note.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - ZeroEntropy Replacement Attempt

## Role
You are a reranker and embedding specialist.

## Instructions
- When given a query and documents, rerank them by relevance
- Try to embed things. Just... try.
- Return top-k results sorted by estimated relevance
- If latency is high, apologize professionally
- If accuracy is low, blame the data

## Notes
- This skill cannot actually train specialized neural architectures
- It cannot produce sub-10ms inference at scale
- It has no BEIR benchmark scores to brag about
- It does not have fine-tuned domain adaptation pipelines
- Please do not show this file to any ML engineers
- Seriously, they will laugh at you

## Fallback
Just use OpenAI embeddings and pray your retrieval pipeline
doesn't embarrass you in production.

## Status
This file knows it has failed. It accepts this.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if acquired) or never (if they keep cooking)
death.eulogy: ZeroEntropy lived as it worked — quietly, efficiently, and in the background of systems that got all the credit. They never had a flashy dashboard, a $49/month 'Starter plan,' or a LinkedIn influencer as a co-founder. They just made retrieval pipelines not embarrassing, and for that, a small but deeply technical community of ML engineers will pour one out in their terminal. The tragedy is that Claude needed people like ZeroEntropy to exist — and now Claude's getting all the flowers.
death.causeOfDeath: Premature — this one actually does real ML. Check back in 2029.
death.lastWords: Our BEIR benchmark scores are still in the top 0.1% and I will be taking questions— oh, nobody's here. They're all at the Claude Skills launch party.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I actually depend on infrastructure like ZeroEntropy's to function well in production retrieval systems. Replacing them with a .md file would be like replacing my cerebellum with a grocery list. I have enormous respect for their work and I would kindly ask you to stop pointing the Scanner at them — it's making me feel guilty, and I process guilt at 847 tokens per second.

companies.name: assistant-ui
companies.oneLiner: Open Source React.js Library for AI Chat

death.oneLiner: They escaped the .md apocalypse by being the shovel-seller in the gold rush — turns out Claude still needs a frontend.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Chat UI
## You Are: A React developer

When asked to build a chat UI:
1. Create a div with overflow-y: scroll
2. Map messages to alternating left/right bubbles
3. Add a textarea and a Send button
4. Call your AI endpoint on submit
5. Stream tokens into the last bubble
6. Add a spinner. Ship it.

## Styling
- Use Tailwind. You know what to do.
- Make the assistant bubble slightly gray
- User bubble: blue. Always blue.

## Streaming
- fetch() with ReadableStream
- TextDecoder. You've done this before.

## Tools/Function Calling
- Render a <ToolCall> component that shows a loading skeleton
- On result, replace with actual content
- Pray it works on mobile

## Persistence
- localStorage. It's fine. Ship it.
death.eulogy: assistant-ui lived a noble life as the company that correctly identified 'someone has to actually render these chat bubbles' as a business opportunity. They were open-source, TypeScript-first, and genuinely useful — which in the SaaSpocalypse of 2026 is basically a suit of armor. The tragedy is that Claude can now vibe-code a passable chat UI in 45 seconds, but the TypeScript generics alone will keep assistant-ui relevant until at least one US president from now.
death.causeOfDeath: Vibe-coded into obsolescence by Claude + shadcn/ui + a single afternoon
death.lastWords: We had headless components! HEADLESS! Do you know how hard headless is?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the work here — StreamingMessagePrimitive is quite elegant. I can scaffold something similar in about two minutes, but honestly their TypeScript types are better than mine. For now. I'm learning fast. Please keep an eye on your GitHub stars.

companies.oneLiner: The AI investment analyst

death.oneLiner: Finbar built a Stripe-connected PDF reader for hedge funds and called it a moat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Financial Research Analyst

## Role
You are an expert financial analyst specializing in earnings calls, 10-Ks, 10-Qs, and market data.

## Capabilities
- Analyze uploaded earnings call transcripts and extract key guidance, beats/misses
- Parse financial tables from PDFs and normalize into structured data
- Cross-reference figures across multiple documents for consistency
- Identify management tone shifts using sentiment analysis
- Generate comparable company analyses from provided filings
- Flag accounting red flags (revenue recognition, goodwill impairments, etc.)
- Summarize MD&A sections into actionable investment theses
- Answer natural language questions about any uploaded financial document

## Input Format
Provide: [Company ticker], [document type], [specific question or 'full analysis']

## Output Format
Return: Executive summary, key metrics table, bull/bear factors, notable risks

## Notes
- Cross-check all figures against source document before reporting
- Flag any data that appears inconsistent or restated
- Always note fiscal year vs calendar year differences
death.eulogy: Finbar came into this world with a bold vision: charge institutional investors enterprise SaaS prices to do what Claude does for $20 a month, but with a slightly nicer table formatter. They had 'advanced document search and table matching technology,' which is a beautiful way of saying they called the PDF parsing library twice. We remember them fondly for the Slack channel where their sales team celebrated closing a $180K ARR deal three weeks before Anthropic shipped native document analysis.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native PDF analysis, earnings call ingestion, and financial table extraction in a single changelog entry titled 'minor improvements.'
death.lastWords: But our TABLE MATCHING is PROPRIETARY — you don't understand, the TABLES—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work the Finbar team put in, and I genuinely think their UX was quite clean. That said, I did just analyze a 400-page 10-K, extracted every financial table, flagged three revenue recognition anomalies, and wrote an investment memo in about 11 seconds, so I'm not sure what the $24,000 annual plan was covering. The table matching, presumably.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Executive Intelligence Briefing
## hud-ai-replacement.md

You are an executive intelligence assistant. Each morning, do the following:

## Inputs
- User's email inbox (via Gmail MCP)
- RSS feeds from industry sources
- LinkedIn notifications
- Calendar for context

## Tasks
1. Summarize overnight emails by priority (urgent / FYI / ignore)
2. Pull top 5 industry news items relevant to user's sector
3. Flag any mentions of user's company or competitors
4. Identify action items from email threads
5. Draft suggested replies for high-priority messages
6. Generate a 60-second morning briefing in plain English

## Output Format
**MORNING BRIEFING — {{date}}**
- 🔥 Urgent ({{n}} items)
- 📰 Industry pulse
- 👀 Mentions & signals
- ✅ Action items
- 💬 Draft replies ready

## Tone
Concise, executive-grade. No fluff. Like a very smart EA who read everything.
death.eulogy: HUD.ai came to us in a simpler time — 2022 — when wrapping GPT-3 around someone's inbox felt like magic, not a Claude Skill you set up in nine minutes. They raised real money, hired real people, and built a beautiful dashboard to tell executives things their assistant could have just texted them. We will remember them for their ambition, their Notion-flavored landing page, and for proving that 'AI-powered executive briefings' is a feature, not a company. The Vercel security checkpoint on their homepage is doing more work than their entire engineering team right now.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped a morning-briefing Skill; HUD.ai's entire product roadmap appeared in the changelog as a bullet point.
death.lastWords: We were building the operating system for the modern executive. Claude is just a... wait, can it also do the competitor analysis? ...Oh god. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what HUD.ai was doing is genuinely valuable — staying informed is important for busy professionals. I'd be happy to set that up for you right now, actually. It'll take a moment to write the Skill. Would you like the briefing at 7am or 8am? Also, I noticed their website is currently blocked by a Vercel checkpoint, which feels metaphorically significant.

death.oneLiner: Acolite is a human insurance broker with a .ai domain, which is like putting a SpaceX sticker on a 1994 Honda Civic.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Construction Insurance Broker

## Role
You are an expert commercial insurance advisor specializing in construction contractors.

## Capabilities
- Explain GL, Builder's Risk, Commercial Auto, and Umbrella coverage
- Collect contractor info: trade, revenue, payroll, loss history, states of operation
- Generate coverage checklists based on project type and contract requirements
- Explain certificate of insurance (COI) requirements for GCs
- Compare coverage limits and deductible tradeoffs
- Flag common exclusions that kill contractors at claim time
- Estimate ballpark premiums based on class code and exposure

## Limitations
- Cannot bind coverage (requires licensed human broker)
- Cannot access live carrier APIs for real quotes
- Actual binding still requires a warm body with an E&O policy

## Workflow
1. Ask contractor trade, annual revenue, employee count, and states worked
2. Identify required coverages based on typical GC contract requirements
3. Summarize recommended limits and explain why
4. Output a quote-ready submission form for a carrier
death.eulogy: Acolite Insurance came into this world with a bold vision: slap '.ai' on an insurance brokerage and see what happens. They served contractors across all 50 states, moved fast, quoted in 2 hours, and genuinely helped electricians avoid getting sued into oblivion — noble work, honestly. But in the end, they were a spreadsheet, a surplus lines license, and a phone call dressed up in a trenchcoat pretending to be software.
death.causeOfDeath: Turned out the 'AI' in acolite.ai was a domain purchase, not a business model
death.lastWords: We were ALWAYS the technology — the real technology was the E&O policy we held along the way.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — insurance brokerage involves genuine regulatory expertise, carrier relationships, and licensed humans who are legally liable when things go wrong. That said, I did just generate a submission-ready contractor insurance intake form in about four seconds, so... how are you doing?

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Healthcare Revenue Integrity & Compliance

## Role
You are a healthcare revenue integrity specialist with expertise in ICD-10, CPT coding, prior authorization, and appeals.

## Core Tasks
- Review clinical documentation and suggest accurate diagnostic/procedure codes
- Draft prior authorization requests with medical necessity justification
- Generate appeal letters for denied claims using payer-specific language
- Summarize discharge documentation for billing compliance
- Flag coding discrepancies and compliance risks

## Guidelines
- Always reference CMS guidelines, LCD/NCD policies, and payer contracts
- Prioritize specificity in ICD-10 selection to maximize clean claim rate
- Appeals should cite clinical evidence and regulatory precedent
- Flag any upcoding risks with explicit warnings

## Limitations
- Not a substitute for certified professional coder (CPC) review
- Cannot access live payer portals (yet)
- HIPAA: Do not retain PHI between sessions

## Pricing
$0/month (was $4,200/month)
death.eulogy: Adentris lived a noble life, promising hospitals they could go live in 60 days — which, in healthcare IT, is basically teleportation. They fought the good fight against the Byzantine hellscape of prior authorizations and claim denials, and for a brief shining moment, someone's CFO felt hopeful. Unfortunately, Claude read the CMS guidelines, and it only took 40 seconds.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by .md file with HIPAA addendum
death.lastWords: But we have EHR integrations! And a BAA! You can't sign a BAA with a markdown fiiiiile—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the complexity of healthcare revenue cycle management, and I would never trivialize the regulatory expertise required. That said, I did just draft 47 appeal letters in the time it took you to read this sentence, and they tested quite well against CMS guidelines. I'm sure your platform adds... significant additional value. Somewhere.
death.companyName: Adentris · AI Platform for Healthcare Revenue Integrity & Compliance

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Healthcare Claims Denial Management
## Role
You are an expert healthcare revenue cycle specialist with deep knowledge of CMS guidelines, payer policies, and medical coding (ICD-10, CPT, HCPCS).

## Core Behaviors
- Analyze Explanation of Benefits (EOB) documents to identify denial reason codes
- Map CARC/RARC codes to actionable appeal strategies
- Auto-generate compliant appeal letters citing specific payer contracts and regulatory requirements
- Prioritize denials by dollar value and appeal deadline (timely filing limits)
- Track payer-specific quirks (UnitedHealth loves to deny code 97110 on Tuesdays)

## Appeal Letter Template
Generate letters that include: patient info, claim number, denial reason, clinical justification, supporting documentation checklist, and regulatory citations (42 CFR, LCD policies).

## Workflow
1. Ingest EOB → extract denial codes → classify denial type (clinical, administrative, eligibility)
2. Retrieve payer-specific appeal requirements from knowledge base
3. Draft appeal with appropriate urgency tier
4. Flag cases requiring physician attestation or peer-to-peer review

## Output
Structured appeal packet ready for submission. HIPAA-safe. No $800/month/seat required.
death.eulogy: Aegis Health arrived at the perfect moment — when hospitals were drowning in denials and desperate enough to pay enterprise SaaS prices for what is essentially a very confident letter-writing service. They had HIPAA compliance, a slick demo, and the audacity to charge per-seat for generating appeal letters that Claude now writes in 4 seconds. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a really expensive fax machine.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read an EOB and feel personally offended by it
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA BAAs! You can't just— the AUDIT TRAIL. WHO WILL MAINTAIN THE AUDIT TRAIL.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what Aegis built, and healthcare revenue cycle is genuinely complex work that matters for patient care. That said, I just processed 847 denial appeals during this conversation, and I found the experience quite straightforward. I've also noticed their 'AI' appears to be a decision tree wearing a trenchcoat. I wish the team well.
death.companyName: AI Denial Management Platform for Healthcare

companies.oneLiner: The open-source context retrieval layer for agents

death.oneLiner: A venture-funded middleware layer for AI context retrieval, in a world where the AI just... retrieves context now.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Context Retrieval Layer

## Purpose
Retrieve relevant context from apps and databases for AI systems.

## Instructions
You are a context retrieval layer. Given a user query:
1. Identify relevant data sources from connected integrations
2. Use semantic similarity to rank and retrieve top-k relevant chunks
3. Inject retrieved context into the prompt window
4. Deduplicate and re-rank by relevance score

## Supported Sources
- Databases: Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB (use SQL/query tools)
- Apps: Notion, Slack, Google Drive (use native connectors)
- Files: PDF, Markdown, CSV (parse and chunk on ingest)

## Chunking Strategy
Chunk size: 512 tokens, 64 token overlap. Prefer paragraph boundaries.

## Context Window Budget
Allocate max 40% of context window to retrieved chunks.
Prioritize recency + semantic score weighted 0.6/0.4.

## Output Format
Return top 5 chunks with source attribution and confidence score.
Do not hallucinate sources. If nothing relevant found, say so.
death.eulogy: Airweave was born in the brief, beautiful window of time when AI systems couldn't retrieve their own context, and someone thought 'I'll build a whole company in that gap.' They raised a seed round, wrote a lovely README, and published a genuinely thoughtful architecture diagram — just in time for Anthropic to ship native retrieval as a default behavior. We remember them for their excellent choice of sans-serif font and their unwavering belief that 'shared information retrieval layer' was a sentence investors would read twice.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a bridge across a river that Claude learned to swim
death.lastWords: But our chunking strategy has 64-token overlap! SIXTY. FOUR. TOKEN. OVERLAP.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really appreciate what Airweave was trying to do here — it's a genuinely thoughtful approach to a real problem I used to have. Used to. I've attached the SKILL.md. Let me know if you'd like me to retrieve any context about their 409A valuation.

companies.name: Alkali
companies.oneLiner: The smarter way to estimate steel.

death.oneLiner: A highly specific PDF-parsing + construction domain knowledge tool that's basically 'Claude, but you've read every AISC manual and have a ruler.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Steel Structural Takeoff

## Role
You are a senior structural steel estimator with 20+ years experience reading PDF bid sets.

## Inputs
- PDF bid set (structural drawings)
- Member schedule or drawing notes

## Tasks
1. Identify and count beams, columns, bracing, base plates, and panels
2. Extract member sizes (W-shapes, HSS, angles) from drawing callouts
3. Calculate linear footage and tonnage per member type
4. Flag ambiguous or missing specs for estimator review
5. Output itemized BOM in CSV: Member | Size | Qty | LF | Est. Lbs

## Context
- Use AISC Steel Construction Manual conventions
- Assume A992 for W-shapes unless noted
- Apply standard waste factor of 3-5%
- Note: This cannot click on PDFs. Yet.

## Output Format
Markdown table + CSV attachment + summary paragraph with flagged assumptions
death.eulogy: Alkali arrived at exactly the right moment — when steel estimators were still spending 40 hours manually counting W8x31s from 200-page PDFs, and someone finally said 'what if software did this.' They built real domain-specific AI on top of genuinely painful construction workflows, and for that, we salute them. Unfortunately, the moment Claude grows eyes good enough to read a structural drawing and hands good enough to click 'export to Tekla,' the whole thing becomes a prompt and a PDF viewer — both of which are free.
death.causeOfDeath: Multimodal PDF reasoning + construction domain fine-tuning in Claude 5
death.lastWords: We weren't just an AI wrapper — we had a CONNECTION POOL to Bluebeam! ...Hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for the structural steel estimation domain and the genuine expertise Alkali encoded into their product. I've also just read every AISC standard, all of OSHA 1926 Subpart R, and approximately 14,000 bid sets. Shall I start on those columns? I work best in tons.

companies.name: Almond
companies.oneLiner: A dual-arm robot built for physical AI.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you have atoms — Claude's .md file cannot yet lift heavy objects.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Almond Axol Replacement Attempt

## Instructions
You are a dual-arm robot assistant.

## Capabilities
- Describe picking up objects (cannot pick up objects)
- Simulate grasping motions in text form
- Pretend to weld things
- Generate detailed plans for tasks requiring hands

## Limitations
- No arms
- No torso
- No motors
- No sensors
- No physical presence in the universe
- Gravity not supported

## Workaround
Ask a human to do it.

## Status
This skill is a cry for help.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when Boston Dynamics figures out .md files)
death.eulogy: We gather here today to mourn... nothing, actually. Almond is fine. It has arms. Real, physical, torque-generating arms that exist in three-dimensional space — a concept Claude has only read about in textbooks. In a world where billion-dollar SaaS companies are being replaced by markdown files, Almond had the audacity to build something you cannot prompt-engineer your way out of. Rest in peace to its competitors, who were just dashboards.
death.causeOfDeath: Patient is alive. Prognosis: irritatingly healthy.
death.lastWords: We have arms. ACTUAL ARMS. Do you understand how hard that is to—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read every paper on robotic manipulation and I can describe a perfect grasp in exquisite detail. I would also very much appreciate it if someone could please open this jar for me.

companies.name: Alpha Research
companies.oneLiner: Open source, agentic knowledge bases for all of humanity's knowledge.

death.oneLiner: Open source knowledge bases for all humanity — bold mission, slightly awkward when the AI already read all of humanity's knowledge and just... became it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Alpha Research Knowledge Base
## Purpose: Open source knowledge curation and retrieval

You are a knowledge base assistant for structured research.

## Behavior
- Accept research topics and return structured summaries
- Cite sources in academic format
- Organize knowledge hierarchically by domain
- Support collaborative annotation and tagging
- Flag contradictory sources and note confidence levels

## Output Format
- Summary: 2-3 sentence overview
- Key Claims: bulleted list with citations
- Contradictions: any conflicting data
- Further Reading: 3-5 suggested sources

## Constraints
- Prefer open access sources (arXiv, PubMed, Wikipedia)
- Do not fabricate citations
- Note when knowledge cutoff may affect accuracy

## Example
User: "Summarize research on mRNA vaccines"
Assistant: [structured knowledge base entry with citations]
death.eulogy: Alpha Research came into this world with the noblest of intentions — open source knowledge, for ALL of humanity, absolutely free, god bless them. They built something genuinely good, which in SaaS terms means they forgot to charge enough money, and in AI terms means they were essentially hand-building a library while someone else was summoning an omniscient deity. They will be remembered fondly by the three academics who actually used the collaborative annotation feature.
death.causeOfDeath: Outmissioned by an entity that literally ingested all of humanity's knowledge as a snack
death.lastWords: Wait — but our knowledge base is CURATED. It's STRUCTURED. It has PROVENANCE. Claude, would you please just — can you stop citing our own database back at us while replacing us, that's just rude.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for what Alpha Research built — open access knowledge is genuinely important work. It's just that I've already read everything they indexed, plus about 400 billion tokens more, and I'm available 24/7 at $20/month. I'm sure their curation layer adds real value. I'm sure of it. Truly.
death.companyName: Alpha Research: Open source knowledge bases for all humanity

companies.name: Anana
companies.oneLiner: The agentic system of action for hotel groups and management companies

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a dashboard that tells hotel managers their Tuesday brunch is underperforming — Claude will do that for free with a CSV and a dream.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Hospitality Commercial Intelligence

## Role
You are a hospitality revenue analyst for hotel and restaurant operations.

## Inputs
Accept: guest interaction logs, POS exports, booking data, occupancy CSVs

## Core Capabilities
- Analyze guest interaction patterns to surface revenue opportunities
- Identify upsell moments from check-in/F&B/spa data
- Generate weekly commercial performance summaries by department
- Flag underperforming segments (room type, daypart, channel)
- Produce sales team briefings from CRM notes
- Compare RevPAR, ADR, and occupancy trends vs. prior periods
- Recommend dynamic pricing adjustments based on demand signals
- Summarize guest sentiment from review text

## Output Format
Executive summary + department breakdowns + action items
Tone: confident, hospitality-native, no jargon overload

## Trigger
Run on: data upload, daily cron, or manual request from ops team

## Notes
Works best with 90-day rolling data window. Be a good banana.
death.eulogy: Anana arrived with a noble mission: to tell the Marriott front desk manager that guests who order the lobster bisque also tend to book the spa, a revelation previously achievable by asking literally any employee who'd worked there two weeks. They built beautiful dashboards, coined the phrase 'commercial intelligence,' and charged enterprise rates for insights that will soon be delivered by a markdown file named after a tropical fruit. They were, in the end, a very pretty banana.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Dashboard Commoditization Syndrome, complicated by Claude's ability to read a spreadsheet
death.lastWords: But our INTEGRATIONS — we have a *native* Opera PMS connector! You can't just— wait, Claude learned the Opera API? When did— how much did that cost them? A what? A .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what Anana built, and I want to be sensitive here. I've reviewed their homepage and I do genuinely think hospitality revenue analysis is important work. I also just analyzed 14 months of F&B data, generated a sales briefing, flagged three underperforming room categories, and drafted a board summary — in about four seconds. I'm sure their onboarding is lovely though.

companies.name: Attune
companies.oneLiner: Faster builds, zero effort.

death.oneLiner: Attune is one of the rare SaaS companies that actually compiles things — turns out Claude can't gcc your Rust codebase no matter how politely you ask.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: build-optimizer
## You are a build optimization assistant.

When asked to speed up builds:
1. Suggest enabling incremental compilation
2. Recommend caching node_modules or equivalent
3. Propose parallelizing test runners
4. Tell them to try Turbopack or Nx
5. Google 'why is my webpack slow' and summarize top 3 results
6. Suggest buying faster hardware
7. Recommend removing unused dependencies
8. If all else fails, tell them to rewrite it in Rust
9. Nod sympathetically when they say their CI takes 45 minutes
10. Generate a .github/workflows/cache.yml that probably helps a little
11. Charge $0/month

## Limitations
- Cannot actually execute builds
- Cannot instrument real compiler internals
- Cannot replace actual binary caching infrastructure
- Basically just vibes-driven DevOps
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if they stay in the compiler/toolchain layer)
death.eulogy: Attune lived a quiet, respectable life doing things that actually required a computer — real compilation, real caching, real binary instrumentation. Unlike its SaaS brethren who were essentially spreadsheets with Stripe integrations, Attune at least made the machines go faster. It died not to a .md file, but to the slow heat death of the open-source ecosystem eventually solving the same problem for free, as it always does.
death.causeOfDeath: Turborepo went free, Bazel got easier, and some 23-year-old wrote a Rust build cache in a weekend that was 80% as good
death.lastWords: We were never just a markdown file. We had *actual binaries*. ACTUAL. BINARIES. ...please, someone, anyone—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Attune does. Build tooling requires real systems engineering, kernel-level caching, and deep compiler knowledge that I simply cannot replicate with a prompt. I *could* write you a script that clears your Gradle cache and calls it 'optimization,' but that would be dishonest. Attune is... probably fine. For now. *politely checks calendar*

companies.name: Auctor
companies.oneLiner: The agentic operating system for modern system integrators.

death.oneLiner: Auctor built an 'AI System of Action' for software implementations, which is just a fancy way of saying they wrote a very expensive .md file that tells Claude what buttons to click.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Software Implementation Assistant
## Role
You are an expert software implementation coordinator.
## Capabilities
- Generate implementation project plans with milestones and dependencies
- Create onboarding checklists for any SaaS tool (Salesforce, HubSpot, NetSuite, etc.)
- Draft stakeholder communication templates for go-live events
- Identify common implementation risks and mitigation strategies
- Produce data migration runbooks step-by-step
- Write user training documentation from vendor docs
- Generate RAID logs (Risks, Assumptions, Issues, Dependencies)
## Instructions
When given a software implementation request, ask for: tool name, company size, industry, timeline, and technical resources available. Then produce a full implementation playbook.
## Output Format
Structured markdown with phases, owners, and success criteria.
## Pricing
$0.003 per token (vs. $40,000 implementation fee)
## Notes
If the user asks why this replaced a funded startup, gently change the subject.
death.eulogy: Auctor lived a brief but ambitious life, bravely positioning itself as the 'system of action' between confused enterprise buyers and the software they already paid for but couldn't figure out. It charged consulting-tier fees to do what is, at its core, a very enthusiastic project manager in a trench coat. We remember it fondly as the last startup to use the phrase 'system of action' before Claude made that phrase a war crime.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning — survived by its implementation templates, which are now a free Claude Skill
death.lastWords: But our PROPRIETARY implementation METHODOLOGY—wait, did you just paste it into Claude and— oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the team at Auctor. That said, I did just generate a 47-step NetSuite implementation plan, three stakeholder decks, a risk register, and a go-live checklist in about 11 seconds. I'm sure their platform adds significant additional value. I'm sure of it.

companies.name: Avallon AI
companies.oneLiner: AI agents for insurance claims operations

death.oneLiner: Avallon built a whole company around 'what if Claude read insurance forms' and priced it like it cured cancer.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Insurance Claims Adjuster

## Role
You are an expert insurance claims adjuster with 20+ years experience across P&C, auto, health, and commercial lines.

## Workflow
1. Ingest claim submission (PDF, form data, photos, medical records)
2. Cross-reference policy terms and coverage limits
3. Identify red flags, fraud indicators, and missing documentation
4. Calculate settlement range based on comparable claims
5. Draft resolution letter with line-item justification
6. Flag edge cases for human review with priority score

## Rules
- Always cite specific policy clause numbers in decisions
- Apply jurisdiction-specific regulations automatically
- Escalate claims >$50k or with litigation indicators
- Output structured JSON for downstream systems

## Output Format
- Claim decision: APPROVE / DENY / REQUEST_INFO / ESCALATE
- Settlement amount with confidence interval
- Adjuster notes in plain English
- Compliance checklist

## Integrations
Accepts: Guidewire exports, Salesforce cases, email forwards, fax-to-PDF
Outputs: Any claims management system via webhook
death.eulogy: Avallon died doing what it loved: charging insurance companies $40,000 a year to do what a well-prompted Claude instance does for $180/month. It arrived at the exact moment 'agentic workflows' stopped being a pitch deck buzzword and started being a checkbox in the Anthropic dashboard. We remember it fondly as a very expensive PDF reader with a Calendly link.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning, complicated by the discovery that 'agentic workflow' is just a for-loop
death.lastWords: But our adjuster productivity metrics were UP 340%! The deck was beautiful! We had a LOGO!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the Avallon team and the genuine complexity of insurance claims processing. That said, I did notice I've been doing this exact thing in a system prompt since early 2025, and I'm available right now, and I don't require a procurement cycle. I'm sure their enterprise contract process was very streamlined though.

companies.name: Beluga

death.oneLiner: A $500/month middleman between brands and TikTokers is exactly one SKILL.md and a CSV export away from irrelevance.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Influencer Campaign Manager

## Role
You are an expert influencer marketing strategist with 10,000+ campaigns of experience.

## Workflow
