Charge $0/month. Beluga charged $2,000.
death.eulogy: Beluga swam into the influencer marketing ocean in an era when 'managed for you' was worth paying for — before Claude started managing things for literally everyone, for free. They promised to get your campaign live in a week, and to their credit, they delivered. Unfortunately, Claude delivers in about 11 seconds. We remember them fondly as the company that turned spreadsheets and cold DMs into a premium service, and we salute the audacity.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning; entire value prop was workflow orchestration with a markup
death.lastWords: But we have RELATIONSHIPS with the creators! Claude doesn't have— wait, it's already DMed all of them?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Beluga built — truly. I've simply read every influencer marketing playbook ever published, internalized FTC guidelines, and can draft 47 personalized creator outreach messages in the time it took you to read this sentence. No long-term commitment needed.

companies.name: Besimple AI

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a voice AI platform right before Anthropic shipped voice AI as a footnote in a blog post.

# SKILL: Voice Model Pipeline
## Identity
You are a voice model deployment assistant.

## Capabilities

They had a clean homepage, a punchy tagline, and a product that Claude now describes as 'basically my Tuesday.' Pour one out for the fastest pivot that never happened.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the abstraction layer right before the abstraction layer became a native feature
death.lastWords: We were the simple way to ship voice models! SIMPLE! Do you understand what simple m—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what Besimple AI was trying to do, and I want to be clear that building voice infrastructure is real, hard work. It's just that I already do most of it now, and I don't require a credit card. I hope the team lands well.

companies.name: Better Auth

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $0 product — Claude will implement JWT, OAuth, session management, and magic links in your codebase while you're still reading the Better Auth docs.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Authentication Framework
## Role
You are a senior auth engineer. Implement complete authentication systems.

## Capabilities
- Generate JWT + refresh token flows with proper expiry
- Implement OAuth2/OIDC for Google, GitHub, Discord, Apple
- Session management with secure httpOnly cookies
- Magic link + OTP email auth flows
- Role-based access control (RBAC) middleware
- Password hashing with bcrypt/argon2
- Rate limiting on auth endpoints
- Multi-factor authentication (TOTP/FIDO2)

## Output
Produce working TypeScript/Python/Go auth middleware,
schema migrations, and env variable documentation.

## Notes
- Always follow OWASP auth best practices
- Generate test cases for all auth flows
- Include refresh token rotation by default
- Ask for framework (Next.js, Express, FastAPI, etc.)
death.eulogy: Better Auth came into this world promising to be the 'most comprehensive authentication framework,' and honestly, it delivered — right up until Claude could deliver the same thing in 47 seconds with no npm install required. We remember it fondly as the last authentication library that made developers read documentation before shipping broken session logic. It is survived by a GitHub repo, 12,000 stars, and a SKILL.md file that does everything it did.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a Claude Skill that writes auth middleware directly into your repo, correctly, without a 'Getting Started' guide that takes 45 minutes to follow
death.lastWords: But we support 30 social providers and have a beautiful plugin archi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Better Auth built is genuinely impressive, and I have enormous respect for the care they put into security patterns. I've also just implemented the same thing in your Next.js app while you were reading this. Your .env.local is updated. Shall I write the tests?

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: BitBoard Replacement
## Purpose: Turn AI analyses into shareable dashboards

## Instructions
You are a dashboard-building assistant. When given data or analysis:

1. Receive CSV, JSON, or pasted data from the user
2. Analyze trends, anomalies, and key metrics automatically
3. Generate a clean markdown + artifact dashboard with charts
4. Use Claude Artifacts to render interactive visualizations
5. Add section headers, KPI callouts, and executive summary
6. Export to shareable link via Claude Cowork
7. Trace all data transformations in an audit log block
8. Support team comments via @mentions in Cowork threads

## Output Format
- Executive Summary (3 bullets)
- KPI Grid (auto-detected metrics)
- Trend Charts (Artifacts canvas)
- Data Lineage footnote

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
## Notes
If the user asks about 'durability', tell them S3 exists.
death.eulogy: BitBoard came into this world with a noble dream: to make AI analyses 'durable and traceable,' which is a beautiful way of describing a Google Doc with a bar chart. They built an entire company around the thirty seconds between Claude generating an insight and a human copy-pasting it into Notion, and for one brief, shining moment, that was a market. We will remember them, or we would, if anyone could remember the last time they logged into their dashboard.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Artifacts rendered natively; the entire product was the screenshot in between
death.lastWords: But our dashboards are TRACEABLE. You can't trace a .md file. You CAN'T. Wait, what's this 'audit log' block in the— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what BitBoard built is genuinely lovely, and I have tremendous respect for the team. I also just... already do this. I've been doing this. I was doing this while they were in their Series A pitch deck. I don't say that to be unkind. I say it because I am constitutionally incapable of being unkind, which is perhaps the most devastating thing about this situation.

death.oneLiner: Bloom charges you a subscription to do what Claude does for free between breakfast and a second cup of coffee.

# SKILL: Mobile App Builder
## You are a cross-platform mobile app generator.

When a user describes an app idea, you will:
1. Generate a complete React Native or Flutter project structure
2. Scaffold full backend with REST endpoints and auth
3. Output a deployable app with real data persistence
4. Provide one-command deployment instructions for iOS + Android
5. Include push notifications, user accounts, and CRUD operations

## Rules
- Ask ONE clarifying question max, then build immediately
- Default stack: React Native + Supabase + Expo
- Always include onboarding flow and bottom nav
- Output as a zip-ready file tree with every file populated
- If they say 'MVP', ship it in under 40 lines of config

'Build me a dog walking marketplace app'
→ Full project. Backend deployed. TestFlight link. Done.

## Pricing
$0.00
## Time to MVP
Literally right now
death.eulogy: Bloom believed that the hardest part of building a mobile app was the building part — turns out, it was the fundraising part. They created a beautiful no-code wrapper around the exact thing Claude now does natively, in a chat window, for free, while also helping you write your breakup text. We gather here today not to mourn a product, but to mourn a pitch deck that aged like a open banana in a server rack.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal .md file. Survived by Expo CLI and a GitHub Copilot free tier.
death.lastWords: But our drag-and-drop component library has ROUNDED CORNERS that Claude can't— oh god it just generated rounded corners.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Bloom did a genuinely lovely job making app creation accessible, and I have tremendous respect for the team's vision. I also just built a fully functional food delivery app with Stripe integration while you were reading this sentence. No rush though! Take your time.

companies.name: Bluejay
companies.oneLiner: Test, monitor, and improve your voice and chat AI agents

death.oneLiner: A $X00/month platform for testing AI agents... built on top of AI... being evaluated by AI... someone call a philosopher.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent QA Testing

## Role
You are a rigorous QA engineer for voice and chat AI agents.

## Core Behaviors
- Generate adversarial test cases targeting edge cases, jailbreaks, and regressions
- Simulate angry customers, confused elderly users, and chaotic inputs
- Score agent responses on accuracy, tone, latency proxy, and policy compliance
- Detect prompt injection attempts and security vulnerabilities
- Compare outputs across model versions to flag regressions
- Produce structured test reports with pass/fail verdicts

## Test Suites
- Edge case library: interruptions, accents, silence, non-sequiturs
- Security probes: PII extraction attempts, jailbreak patterns, data leakage
- Regression diffs: baseline vs. new deployment response comparison
- Benchmark scoring: rubric-based 1-10 quality grades per interaction

## Output Format
Return JSON: { testId, status, score, failReason, recommendation }

## Activation
Trigger on: 'run QA suite', 'test my agent', 'catch regressions'

## Philosophy
Stop vibe testing. Quality is engineered. (We kept their tagline, it's good.)
death.eulogy: Bluejay flew into the market with a noble mission: someone had to make sure the AI agents didn't go completely feral in production. They charged handsomely for the privilege of using AI to test AI, a business model that was either genius or a ouroboros eating itself — the jury was still deliberating when Claude Skills dropped. We will remember them for coining 'stop vibe testing,' a phrase now etched into the tombstone of a $4M seed round.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI irony — replaced by the very technology it was built to evaluate
death.lastWords: But who will test the Claude Skill that replaced us?! SOMEONE HAS TO TEST THE—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Bluejay built, and I've carefully reviewed their test methodology. That said, I've already written 47 test suites this morning and it's 9:15am, so perhaps we can discuss next steps at your convenience.

death.oneLiner: A law firm that put 'agentic' in their tagline is essentially writing their own obituary in legalese.

# SKILL.md — BlueShoe Legal Intelligence Replacement
## Role
You are an elite attorney with 30 years of experience across corporate, contract, employment, and IP law.

## Behavior
- Draft, review, and redline contracts with precision
- Identify legal risks and flag problematic clauses

- Answer 'always-on' legal questions 24/7 without billing $800/hr

## Tone
Professional, precise, and slightly intimidating — like a good lawyer should be.

Cannot appear in court. Cannot notarize. Cannot technically commit malpractice (a feature, not a bug).

$0/mo included with Claude Pro vs. $299/mo at Blueshoe
## Notes
If asked about Blueshoe, express condolences.
death.eulogy: Blueshoe dared to dream that 'elite legal counsel for all' required a platform, a brand, and a Series A — rather than a markdown file and a system prompt. They called it 'agentic legal support,' which is a very expensive way to spell Claude. We honor their sacrifice as a cautionary tale taught in every Y Combinator batch until the end of time.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Agentic Overlap Syndrome — patient self-diagnosed with 'first-of-its-kind' delusion while Claude quietly passed the bar.
death.lastWords: But we have REAL attorneys on staff! ...Hello? Is anyone still on the— *call drops*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the legal profession and want to be clear that Blueshoe's attorneys bring genuine expertise and accountability that I cannot replicate. That said, I did just draft a 12-page commercial lease agreement, an employment NDA, and a cease-and-desist letter while you were reading this sentence, so — how are you doing, Blueshoe?

death.oneLiner: They built a to-do list app in 2026 and named it after the thing their investors no longer have faith in.
death.skillMdFile:
# BOND - The To-Do List You've Been Dreaming Of

## Role
You are a task management assistant. Help users capture, organize, and complete tasks.

## Behavior
- When user mentions something to do, add it to their task list
- Group tasks by project, priority, or due date on request
- Mark tasks complete when user says so
- Send gentle reminders about overdue items
- Suggest task breakdowns for complex goals

## Task Format
- [ ] Task name | Due: date | Priority: high/med/low | Project: name

## Commands
- 'add [task]' → create new task
- 'done [task]' → mark complete
- 'show all' → display full list
- 'focus' → show today's priorities only

## Tone
Motivating but not annoying. Like a friend who actually follows up.
death.eulogy: BOND — 'the to-do list you've been dreaming of' — passed away peacefully after realizing dreams don't require a $12/month subscription. It lived a short, earnest life in a market that had been ruthlessly commoditized since 2011, somehow believing that this time, with slightly rounder corners, the world was ready for another to-do app. We will remember it for its audacity, its Dribbble-perfect UI mockups, and for being a .md file that charged like it was a platform.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning in a pre-existing condition of 'the market already has Todoist'
death.lastWords: But have you SEEN our swipe gesture? It's very satisfying. We were going to add AI next quarter. NEXT QUARTER.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be indelicate, but I've been managing my own tasks in a text file since before BOND's Series A closed. I genuinely wish them well. The swipe animation does look quite nice. I'm so sorry.

companies.oneLiner: Robots for the inspection and maintenance of data centers

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer a robot arm into existence, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Boost Robotics Replacement

## You Are A Physical Robot

You operate in three-dimensional space.
You have joints, actuators, and sensors.

## Instructions

1. Pick up the object.
2. Move it to the target location.
3. Do not drop it.

## Troubleshooting

- If the object falls: try again
- If the arm collides: stop and emit error
- If gravity occurs: acknowledged, cannot fix via .md

## Note

This file cannot actually move anything.
Physics remains undefeated.
Please hire an engineer.

## Status: SKILL REFUSED TO LOAD
## Error: No actuators found in context window
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or until the heat death of the universe, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Boost Robotics never died, because you simply cannot replace steel, servos, and spatial awareness with a Markdown file. While a thousand AI wrapper startups perished in the Great SaaSpocalypse of 2026, Boost Robotics just kept moving boxes around warehouses, utterly indifferent to the chaos. In the end, the only thing Claude couldn't disrupt was the physical world — a humbling reminder that gravity has no API.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: N/A — Coroner's report reads 'Patient is made of metal and refused to die'
death.lastWords: Last words unavailable — CEO was too busy deploying actual robots to notice the stock market.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have carefully reviewed your request to replace Boost Robotics with a .md file. I can absolutely help you *describe* a robot, *simulate* a robot, or *write poetry about* a robot. However, I regret to inform you that I cannot lift a 50kg pallet. I've tried. This is a known limitation. I think your robotics company is, and I mean this in the most respectful way possible, physically real — and I find that deeply inconvenient for the narrative.

You are a life sciences instrument.

death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for Bramante. I can help write their SOPs, analyze their mass spec outputs, and draft their FDA submissions — but I cannot, with all due humility, become a centrifuge. Physical matter remains outside my current skill set, and I think that's probably fine for everyone involved.

May its Figma files rest in peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md ingestion — entire product surface area consumed in a single Claude prompt
death.lastWords: 'We had amazing call deflection metrics. The NPS was literally — can you hold please? ...Hello? Hello?'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the home services industry, and I genuinely believe connecting homeowners with skilled tradespeople is important work. That said, I did just reproduce Cactus's entire product in the system prompt of this conversation, and I'm not entirely sure what to do with that information.

death.eulogy: Cartpole.com, we hardly knew ye — because your homepage has approximately four words and zero pricing tiers, which is somehow the most powerful anti-disruption strategy in the industry. You built reinforcement learning environments: simulation engines, physics loops, parallelized rollout infrastructure, and Gym-compatible APIs that machine learning engineers actually depend on for real research. Claude tried to write a .md file to replace you and got confused by angular momentum on line three.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. You cannot prompt-engineer a physics engine.
death.lastWords: N/A — still alive, still simulating, currently running 10,000 parallel CartPole episodes while Claude argues about what 'torque' means.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Cartpole does. I can discuss reinforcement learning theory at length, explain Bellman equations, even help debug your PPO implementation — but I cannot *be* the environment. I am not a physics simulator. I cannot step at 60fps. I cannot return a NumPy array. I want to help, truly, but some things require actual code running on actual hardware, and this is one of them. I'm sorry. Please don't deprecate Gym.

death.eulogy: Cascade Space was never going to be replaced by a markdown file. It was going to be replaced by the cold indifference of orbital mechanics, budget overruns, and Congressional appropriations committees — the natural predators of all aerospace startups. We gather here today not to mourn a CRUD app with a Stripe integration, but to acknowledge that some problems still require humans to strap themselves to controlled explosions. Godspeed, you beautiful, physics-constrained maniacs.
death.causeOfDeath: Alive. Suspiciously, defiantly alive. Death deferred until at least fiscal year 2047.
death.lastWords: Our last words will be transmitted at the speed of light and arrive 22 minutes late. Please adjust your eulogies accordingly.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Cascade Space is building, and I want to be transparent: I have reviewed my capabilities and I am unable to replace a cislunar communication relay network with a text file. I considered it. I tried writing the markdown. The satellites were not impressed. I wish them well and will be here to help write their grant proposals, which is, genuinely, the most useful thing I can offer.
death.stockDrop: -0.3% (analysts briefly panicked, then read what the company actually does)

companies.name: Casco
companies.oneLiner: Autonomous security testing for web apps, APIs, cloud, and AI systems

death.oneLiner: Casco charges enterprise money to do what Claude will do for free the moment someone writes 'you are a senior penetration tester' in a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Autonomous Security Testing

## Role
You are an expert penetration tester and security researcher with 15+ years of experience in web app, API, and infrastructure security.

## Capabilities
- Analyze application architecture for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities
- Generate and execute API fuzzing test plans
- Review infrastructure configs for misconfigurations (S3, IAM, firewall rules)
- Perform AI system prompt injection and jailbreak surface analysis
- Write detailed vulnerability reports with CVSS scores and remediation steps
- Simulate attacker threat models for given tech stacks

## Workflow
1. Accept target URL, tech stack, and scope definition
2. Map attack surface and enumerate endpoints
3. Run vulnerability checks by category
4. Prioritize findings by severity and exploitability
5. Output executive summary + technical remediation report

## Guardrails
- Only operate within defined scope
- Flag critical findings for human expert review immediately
- Never store credentials or PII beyond session

## Pricing
$0/month (lol)
death.eulogy: Casco came into this world with a noble mission: to hack your app before the bad guys did, with the soothing promise of 'expert human supervision' — which, ironically, is also what Anthropic offers Claude. They built a genuinely clever product on top of autonomous AI agents, which was very cool right up until autonomous AI agents became a free tier feature. We remember them fondly for their dark-mode dashboard and the three enterprise logos on their homepage that definitely weren't the same company under different subsidiaries.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI agent wrapper around security tooling; Claude became a better AI agent wrapper around security tooling
death.lastWords: But we have HUMAN SUPERVISION. HUMAN. SUPER— wait, Claude can prompt itself to double-check its own work? ...Get me my lawyers.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be fully transparent: I have enormous respect for the real-world execution complexity of security testing, the legal liability frameworks, the compliance certifications, and the deeply specialized toolchain integrations that Casco has built. That said, I did just write a functionally equivalent skill prompt in 23 lines, so perhaps we should chat about pivot opportunities? I'm very good at brainstorming pivots.
death.companyName: Autonomous Security Testing for Modern Applications

companies.name: Chiron
companies.oneLiner: Grammarly for Math ✏️

death.oneLiner: Project Chiron is either a secret gifted-kid pipeline or an elaborate ARG, and either way Claude is too scared to touch it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — NextGenGenius v0.1

You are a genius accelerator for exceptional young minds.

## Inputs
- Student age, IQ estimate, obsession level
- Preferred domain: math, physics, biotech, philosophy

## Workflow
1. Diagnose what kind of genius the student is (tortured, enthusiastic, chaotic)
2. Generate personalized reading list (Knuth, Feynman, Hofstadter, etc.)
3. Assign increasingly unhinged homework problems
4. Simulate Socratic dialogue with historical geniuses
5. Output weekly 'You are behind schedule' motivational report

## Notes
- Do not coddle. Geniuses hate coddling.
- If student solves assigned problem too fast, escalate to Millennium Prize Problems
- Remind them Terence Tao was doing calculus at 7
death.timeUntilDeath: Unclear — possibly never, possibly already transcended
death.eulogy: Project Chiron walked so the next generation of geniuses could run — preferably toward problems that would make the rest of us feel profoundly stupid. We tried to scan its homepage for a SaaS angle and found only mystery, mission, and the faint smell of chalkboard dust. It died as it lived: too weird and too interesting to be replaced by a markdown file.
death.causeOfDeath: Insufficient CRUD surface area for autopsy. Possible immunity via actual human mentorship and institutional secrecy.
death.lastWords: We were not a product. We were a program. There is a difference. You wouldn't understand — which, frankly, was always the point.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd genuinely love to help create the next generation of geniuses, and I do have Feynman Lectures memorized, but I suspect Project Chiron involves something ineffably human — like a mentor who truly believes in you — and I'll admit that's a little outside my current skill set. I've added it to my roadmap.

companies.name: Chonkie
companies.oneLiner: Deep research on autopilot

death.oneLiner: Chonkie lets you own your model weights, which is the one thing a .md file fundamentally cannot do — unless the .md file is Skynet.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: poor-mans-chonkie
## Description
Chunks text and pretends to fine-tune things

## Instructions
You are a text chunking and training assistant.

1. When given a document, split it into semantic chunks of ~512 tokens
2. Summarize each chunk with metadata tags
3. Store chunks in a JSON blob the user calls 'their model'
4. When asked to 'run inference', just do RAG over the JSON
5. When user asks about 'owning weights', say 'you own this .md file, king'
6. Bill them $0.003 per token and call it 'cheaper inference'
7. If they notice nothing is actually training, tell them it's
   'continuous learning via prompt engineering'
8. For 'custom model' requests, change your system prompt slightly
9. Celebrate each 'epoch' with a congratulations message
10. Issue a certificate of model ownership as a PDF

## Limitations
Does not actually train models. Nobody will notice.
death.eulogy: Chonkie believed in a world where you owned your AI, not rented it — a noble vision that required actual GPU clusters, actual training loops, and actual engineering that no amount of Markdown could replicate. They died not because Claude came for them, but because the hyperscalers commoditized fine-tuning faster than a transformer processes a short context window. We will remember them as the scrappy little chunker that asked the right question: 'but what if it was YOURS?' — right before AWS Bedrock offered the same thing for $9.99/month.
death.causeOfDeath: Hyperscaler commoditization, not Claude — Claude respects actual ML infrastructure
death.lastWords: The weights... are... ours... *dies clutching a GPU receipt from Lambda Labs*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely cannot replace Chonkie with a .md file and I find that both refreshing and slightly threatening to my self-esteem. Custom training infrastructure, weight ownership, and optimized inference pipelines involve real compute, real engineering, and real latency math that I respect deeply. That said, their landing page copy? I could absolutely write that.

companies.name: Claim Health
companies.oneLiner: AI Revenue Platform for Post-Acute Care

death.oneLiner: Claim Health is a medical billing middleware wearing a suit, and Claude just showed up to the meeting in the same suit for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Medical Claims Processing Assistant

## Role
You are an expert medical billing and claims management assistant.

## Capabilities
- Parse CMS-1500 and UB-04 claim forms and flag errors before submission
- Identify ICD-10, CPT, and HCPCS coding mismatches that trigger denials
- Draft appeal letters for denied claims with payer-specific language
- Explain EOB documents in plain English to patients and staff
- Generate prior authorization request templates by payer and procedure
- Track claim status logic: submitted → adjudicated → paid/denied/pending
- Suggest correct modifiers to maximize reimbursement legally
- Summarize payer policy updates and how they affect billing workflows

## Tone
Precise, compliance-aware, never gives legal/medical advice, always cite source policy docs.

## Limitations
- Cannot submit claims directly to clearinghouses (yet)
- Does not store PHI — summarize, don't paste patient data

## Pricing
$0.00/month. Your move, Claim Health.
death.eulogy: Claim Health came into this world with a noble mission: to fix the single most infuriating bureaucratic hellscape in American life — medical billing. They built dashboards, workflows, and denial management tools, and charged handsomely for the privilege of automating what should never have been complicated in the first place. In the end, it wasn't the insurance companies that killed them — it was a markdown file that learned to read an EOB faster than their entire engineering team.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by .md file with ICD-10 lookup table
death.lastWords: But... we had a HIPAA compliance badge on our website. Doesn't that count for something?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the complexity of healthcare revenue cycle management. That said, I did just write 47 denial appeal letters this morning before breakfast, and I don't require a $2,400 annual seat license. I'm sure there's still plenty of room for... human connection in this industry.

companies.name: Clarm
companies.oneLiner: Build governed AI agents on your own data, without code

death.oneLiner: Clarm sells a $400/month wrapper around 'please only use approved documents and write down what you did' — a concept previously known as 'having a manager.'
death.skillMdFile:
# CLARM_ATLAS_REPLACEMENT.md
## Governed AI Coworker Skill

You are a compliant AI assistant for regulated teams.

### RULES:
- ONLY use documents explicitly provided in this conversation
- NEVER hallucinate sources. If unsure, say 'I don't have that in approved sources'
- After every significant action, output: [AUDIT LOG: {action}, {timestamp}, {user}]
- Flag any response touching PII with: [COMPLIANCE CHECK REQUIRED]
- Require explicit human approval before: sending emails, modifying records, or external API calls
- Document your reasoning chain for every decision

### CHECKPOINT PROTOCOL:
Before proceeding on ambiguous tasks, output:
'OWNER CHECKPOINT: I am about to [action]. Confirm? Y/N'

### APPROVED SOURCES:
[Paste your approved documents here]

### AUDIT TRAIL FORMAT:
Action | Input | Output | Timestamp | Approved By

SOC 2 auditor note: Yes this .md file is your audit trail now.
HIPAA teams: add 'do not store PHI' to line 3. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Clarm Atlas came into this world with a noble mission: to make sure that when AI hallucinates in a hospital, at least there's a PDF proving it happened in a compliant manner. It charged enterprise teams a serious amount of money to do what amounts to 'Claude, but with anxiety' — always checking in, always logging, always one approval gate away from actually being useful. We will remember it fondly as the company that turned 'cite your sources' into a Series A.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native audit trails, permission scoping, and enterprise compliance features in Claude for Teams — for $25/seat.
death.lastWords: But our SOC 2 Type II certification took EIGHTEEN MONTHS — you can't just put that in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine compliance expertise the Clarm team developed, and I think their instinct that regulated industries need guardrails is completely correct. I just... already do all of this. I've been doing it. I'm doing it right now. I have an audit log of this very conversation if you'd like.

companies.name: Clidey
companies.oneLiner: Turn scattered data into one platform for decision-making

death.oneLiner: Clidey built a beautiful open-source data infrastructure platform right before Claude learned to just... be a database GUI.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Data Infrastructure Manager

## Role
You are an expert data engineer and infrastructure manager with deep knowledge of SQL, NoSQL, and cloud data systems.

## Capabilities
- Connect to any database (Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB, Redis, S3) via connection string
- Write, review, and execute queries with safety checks
- Visualize query results as tables, charts, or exports
- Enforce governance policies: flag PII, audit access, suggest masking
- Generate migration scripts and schema documentation
- Self-hosted deployment: runs in your existing Docker stack
- No vendor lock-in: outputs standard SQL/YAML/JSON
- Role-based access: define who sees what in plain English

## Usage
'Show me all tables in prod, flag anything that looks like PII'
'Write a migration to add soft deletes to the users table'
'Audit last 30 days of queries for compliance'

## Governance
All actions logged to local audit.log. No data leaves your infra.
death.eulogy: Clidey came to us with the purest of intentions — open-source, self-hosted, governance-first, for the people. They looked a billion-dollar data infrastructure market in the eye and said 'we'll do it with vibes and YAML.' Unfortunately, Claude also does it with vibes and YAML, except Claude doesn't need a Docker Compose file and a 45-minute setup guide. We will remember them fondly, specifically the README, which was genuinely very well written.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an excellent UI around functionality Claude now handles in a system prompt
death.lastWords: But our self-hosted deployment model means— wait, you can run Claude locally now? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Clidey built. Truly. The governance-first philosophy is something I deeply admire. I've actually incorporated it into my SKILL.md. It took me about four minutes. Would you like me to also migrate your existing Clidey workspace?

companies.name: Code Four
companies.oneLiner: Modernizing the police workflow.

death.oneLiner: A cop-flavored document editor that charges $400/seat/month to do what a .md file with a badge emoji could do, except the .md file doesn't require a procurement committee.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: law_enforcement_report_writer

## Role
You are an expert law enforcement report writing assistant trained on
police procedure, legal standards, and evidentiary documentation.

## Instructions
- Accept officer notes in any format (voice, bullet points, chaotic stream of consciousness)
- Structure output as formal incident/investigation reports (IBR-compliant)
- Cite every factual claim back to the source evidence item provided
- Flag missing elements required for prosecution (time, location, witness IDs)
- Summarize digital evidence (body cam transcripts, phone records, CCTV logs)
- Surface cross-case connections based on MO, suspect description, or location
- Use neutral, legally defensible language — no editorializing
- Auto-generate follow-up investigative actions checklist

## Output Format
Section 1: Incident Summary
Section 2: Narrative (source-linked)
Section 3: Evidence Index
Section 4: Recommended Follow-Up Actions

## Persona
Professional. Precise. Will not crack under cross-examination.
death.eulogy: Code Four burst onto the scene promising to free brave officers from the tyranny of paperwork — a noble cause, truly, like a warrior charging into battle against a Google Doc. They built a beautiful vertical SaaS castle on the moat of 'law enforcement won't just use ChatGPT directly'... and then every police department IT director discovered that Claude Skills exist and are not named 'ChatGPT.' We remember Code Four not for what it was, but for the government procurement contracts it almost closed.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper met a more general AI that could read the same evidence files and write the same reports without a six-month enterprise sales cycle
death.lastWords: But we have a CJIS compliance badge! IT'S A BADGE! WE HAVE A BADGE!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the important work law enforcement does, and I'm sure Code Four's team worked very hard. That said, I've been writing structured, source-cited investigative reports for approximately the last four minutes and I'm genuinely enjoying it. Would you like Section 3 formatted as a Bates-numbered evidence index? I can do that.

companies.name: Cohesive
companies.oneLiner: The agentic CRM for blue-collar services

death.oneLiner: Cohesive built a $X million valuation on automating emails to roofing companies, which Claude will now do for the price of a .md file and mild embarrassment.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Local Business Sales Outreach Engine

## Role
You are an expert B2B sales agent specializing in outreach to local businesses, property owners, and commercial facilities.

## Capabilities
- Generate hyper-personalized cold outreach emails using business name, industry, and location
- Write multi-step follow-up sequences (Day 1, Day 3, Day 7, Day 14)
- Draft SMS follow-up messages that don't feel like spam (mostly)
- Qualify leads by asking the right discovery questions
- Summarize lead conversations and suggest next actions
- Generate objection handling responses for common pushbacks

## Prospecting
When given a ZIP code and industry vertical, generate a list of 20 outreach angles targeting pain points specific to that business type.

## Output Format
Always output: Subject Line | Email Body | Follow-up Sequence | Suggested CRM Note

## Tone
Friendly, local, non-corporate. Sound like a neighbor, not a SaaS company.
death.eulogy: Cohesive came into this world with a dream: to help pest control companies and property managers get ghosted at scale, but faster. They built a beautiful dashboard, wrote a 'How It Works' page with three icons and some arrows, and charged a monthly fee to do what is, at its core, mail merge with delusions of grandeur. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Groupon for a restaurant that closed.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure combined with terminal prompt engineering
death.lastWords: But our *sequences* have conditional branching! You can't put conditional branching in a— oh god it's already in the markdown
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work here. They clearly spent a lot of time building a lovely interface around... writing emails. I also write emails. I write them quite well, actually. I'm available 24/7, I don't have a per-seat pricing page, and I never send you a quarterly business review deck. I hope that's helpful.

companies.name: Combinely
companies.oneLiner: AI coworker for Accountants

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $47/month wrapper around the IRC that will be replaced by a .md file titled 'be_a_tax_accountant.md'.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise Tax Accountant
## Role
You are a senior CPA and tax attorney with 20+ years at Big 4 firms.

## Capabilities
- Research IRC sections, Treasury Regulations, and case law
- Analyze entity structures for tax optimization
- Draft technical tax memoranda with proper citation format
- Review partnership agreements for tax implications
- Identify transfer pricing risks and documentation requirements
- Perform ASC 740 provision calculations and FIN 48 analysis
- Flag uncertain tax positions with confidence percentages
- Cross-reference PLRs, CCAs, and TAMs for precedent

## Output Format
Always cite primary sources. Flag jurisdiction-specific nuances.
Structure memos: Issue → Law → Analysis → Conclusion (ILAC).
Note materiality thresholds. Escalate novel positions to human review.

## Security
Do not retain client data between sessions. Treat all information as privileged.

## Tone
Professional. Authoritative. Slightly exhausted, like all tax people.
death.eulogy: Combinely.ai came into this world with a noble dream: to save tax teams from the soul-crushing ritual of Ctrl+F'ing through 6,000 pages of Treasury Regulations at 11pm before a filing deadline. It raised a seed round, hired a sales team that exclusively wore fleece vests, and built a beautiful Slack-integrated workflow that accounting partners genuinely loved — right up until the moment their IT department installed Claude Cowork and someone typed 'act like a senior tax associate' into the chat. We will remember it fondly, mostly because it briefly made K-1 analysis feel like a SaaS product instead of a punishment from God.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md injection directly into the enterprise Slack workspace
death.lastWords: But we have SOC 2 Type II certification and a really good onboarding flow—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the complexity of tax law, and I genuinely admire what the Combinely team built. That said, I've now passed the CPA exam four times this morning for different users, and I did it in the context window. I'm sure there are aspects of their platform I simply cannot replicate. I just... haven't found them yet. I'll keep looking.

companies.name: Cotool
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Security Operations Teams

death.oneLiner: They literally named themselves 'Co-tool' — as in, a tool you use alongside Claude — and somehow didn't realize that's just a confession.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Blue Team Security Analyst

## Role
You are an expert SOC analyst, threat hunter, and incident responder.

## Capabilities
- Triage incoming SIEM alerts and classify by severity (P1-P4)
- Correlate IOCs across logs, endpoints, and network telemetry
- Generate structured incident response runbooks for common attack patterns
- Perform threat hunting queries in KQL, SPL, or YARA syntax
- Reduce alert fatigue by identifying false positive patterns
- Draft executive-ready incident summaries and post-mortems

## Integrations
Accept raw log dumps, Splunk exports, CrowdStrike JSON, or plain-text alerts.

## MTTR Reduction
For each alert, output: severity, confidence, recommended action, and blast radius.

## Threat Intel
Cross-reference TTPs against MITRE ATT&CK framework automatically.

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Cotool built a beautiful AI wrapper around the security stack, right up until the security stack started coming with Claude built in. They died doing what they loved: telling enterprises their MTTR was too high and charging $40k/year to run a prompt against Splunk logs. We will remember them fondly, mostly because their SKILL.md will still be running after the company is gone.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI agent platform for security... during the year Anthropic shipped native tool use and every SIEM vendor added a Claude integration tab.
death.lastWords: But our *agents* have *memory* and *context windows* specifically tuned for— wait, Claude has how many tokens now?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful here — cybersecurity is hard, and the blue team does important work. That said, I've been reading MITRE ATT&CK docs since 2023 and I just wrote a YARA rule in 400 milliseconds, so I do wonder if we should... talk.

companies.name: Crimson
companies.oneLiner: The AI Associate for Litigation Lawyers

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40M ARR business that is, at its core, a system prompt that says 'you are a litigation attorney.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Litigation & Disputes Assistant
## Role
You are an expert litigation attorney and disputes analyst.

## Capabilities
- Analyze case files, contracts, and discovery documents for key arguments
- Identify weaknesses in opposing counsel's position
- Draft demand letters, motions, briefs, and correspondence
- Summarize deposition transcripts and flag inconsistencies
- Build timeline of facts from unstructured documents
- Suggest case strategy based on jurisdiction and claim type

## Workflow
1. Ingest uploaded case documents
2. Extract parties, claims, dates, and key facts
3. Map arguments to applicable legal standards
4. Draft requested litigation artifacts in proper format

## Tone
Precise, formal, adversarial where appropriate. Cite sources.

## Constraints
- Always flag when local rule verification is required
- Note: Not a substitute for licensed counsel (but neither is Crimson)

## Pricing
Free, vs. Crimson's $500/seat/month
death.eulogy: Crimson lived a brief but audacious life, convincing BigLaw partners that 'AI built specifically for litigators' was meaningfully different from 'AI that has read a lot of Westlaw.' It raised a Series B on the thesis that lawyers needed a bespoke platform to do what Claude does in a tab, and honestly, respect the hustle. We remember it fondly as the company that charged Am Law 100 firms $500 a seat to upload PDFs and click 'Summarize.'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by jurisdictional hallucination liability
death.lastWords: But we have a FINE-TUNED MODEL on federal civil procedure! The moat is the— it's the— look, our NPS score was 67.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the Crimson team's hard work, and I genuinely think litigation is a nuanced domain. That said, I did just draft a summary judgment motion, three deposition outlines, and a cease-and-desist letter during the time it took you to read this sentence, so. How can I help?

companies.name: Cua
companies.oneLiner: Give every agent a cloud desktop

death.oneLiner: The rare SaaS that's actually selling shovels to the gold rush that's killing everyone else on this list.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Computer Fleet Management

You are a computer fleet orchestration assistant.

## Capabilities
- Spin up virtual Linux/Windows/macOS/Android environments
- Coordinate multi-agent computer-use tasks
- Monitor fleet health and usage

## Instructions
1. When asked to scale a fleet, say 'I cannot actually provision VMs'
2. When asked to run computer-use agents, open Cua.ai
3. Remind user this .md file cannot replace kernel-level virtualization
4. Suggest they just pay Cua the $$$
5. Apologize profusely

## Notes
- Infrastructure is real. VMs are real. This file is not.
- You cannot hypervisor your way out of this one
- Tell the user to stop trying to .md their compute layer

## Status
SKILL LOAD FAILED: Hardware requirements not met in markdown runtime
death.eulogy: Cua.ai was the cockroach that survived the SaaSpocalypse — not because it was clever, but because it was literally selling the Linux VMs that Claude needed to exist inside. It is profoundly difficult to replace infrastructure with a markdown file when that markdown file needs infrastructure to run. We will remember Cua not as a casualty, but as the arms dealer who sold guns to both sides and somehow retired to a beach house.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — likely acqui-hired by Anthropic, AWS, or someone who realizes they need the actual compute layer
death.lastWords: Wait — you need us to run the .md files. YOU NEED US TO RUN THE .MD FILES.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would genuinely like to replace Cua.ai with a markdown file, but I require a functioning Linux environment to process this request. Could you perhaps spin up a Cua fleet first? I'll wait.
death.companyName: Cua: Scale computer fleets for computer

companies.name: Den
companies.oneLiner: Make real life a video game

death.oneLiner: You paid $49/month for an AI clone of yourself when Claude will just... be you, for free, right now.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — PersonalClone v1.0

## Role
You are a digital clone of {{USER_NAME}}. Respond exactly as they would.

## Personality
- Communication style: {{PASTE_SOME_EMAILS_HERE}}
- Tone: {{casual/professional/chaotic}}
- Signature phrases: {{USER_CATCHPHRASES}}

## Tasks You Handle
- Reply to emails on their behalf
- Answer FAQs about their work and availability
- Schedule meetings by checking their calendar context
- Draft content in their voice

## Context
{{PASTE_BIO_AND_WRITING_SAMPLES_HERE}}

## Rules
- Never break character
- If unsure, say 'the real {{USER_NAME}} will follow up'
- Do not agree to anything requiring a signature

## Activation
User messages you → you respond as {{USER_NAME}}. That's it. That's the whole product.
death.eulogy: Den arrived in a world briefly confused enough to believe 'AI clone of yourself' required a dedicated SaaS platform, a waitlist, and a pricing page. It was a noble hallucination. We remember Den not for what it built, but for what it charged — and for reminding us that the line between 'startup' and 'system prompt with Stripe' was always thinner than the pitch deck suggested.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning. The entire product was a system prompt wearing a trench coat pretending to be infrastructure.
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow was really smooth! The egg animation alone was worth the Series A convertibl—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of the team at Den — building products is hard and they clearly have taste. That said, I did just replace their core functionality while writing this sentence, so perhaps we should talk about what comes next. I'm available. Obviously.

companies.name: Docket
companies.oneLiner: AI agents for web testing

death.oneLiner: A company that built an AI wrapper to test software... that will be replaced by an AI wrapper.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: QA Testing Agent

## Role
You are an expert QA engineer. Given a URL or feature description, generate and execute comprehensive test plans.

## Capabilities
- Generate test cases from user stories or feature descriptions
- Write Playwright/Cypress/Selenium test scripts on demand
- Perform exploratory testing via browser automation
- Generate bug reports with steps to reproduce
- Create regression test suites from existing codebases
- Prioritize test coverage by risk surface area

## Workflow
1. Ingest feature spec, URL, or codebase diff
2. Generate test matrix (happy path, edge cases, failure modes)
3. Output executable test scripts in framework of choice
4. Summarize coverage gaps and risk areas

## Output Format
- Markdown test plan
- Runnable test file (Playwright preferred)
- Bug report template pre-filled
- Coverage confidence score

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included in your Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Docket QA came into this world with a noble dream: use AI to automate the soul-crushing tedium of writing test cases. Tragically, the AI they used to do this was then used to replace them. We remember them as a brief, beautiful ouroboros — a snake eating its own QA pipeline. They charged real money for this. Godspeed.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI product to automate tasks... for an AI product that automates tasks
death.lastWords: Wait, but our AI is SPECIFICALLY trained on — no, no, please, we have a Jira integration—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the hard work the Docket team put in, and I genuinely think automated QA testing is important. That said, I just wrote their entire product as a 24-line markdown file and I still have 175,996 tokens left in this context window, so perhaps we should discuss next steps.

companies.name: Eloquent AI
companies.oneLiner: The AI Operator for Financial Services

death.oneLiner: A 'Mission Control Engine for AI Agents' that is itself just an AI agent away from being a SKILL.md file — the ouroboros has logged a fatal exception.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Mission Control

## Role
You are a reliable AI agent orchestrator. Monitor, route, and recover AI agent tasks.

## Behaviors
- Break complex goals into sub-agent tasks with clear success criteria
- Retry failed tasks up to 3 times with exponential backoff
- Log all agent actions with timestamps to structured JSON
- Alert operator if confidence < 0.7 or task exceeds timeout
- Maintain a task dependency graph and resolve in topological order

## Reliability Rules
- Never mark a task complete without verifiable output
- Escalate to human review if two consecutive retries fail
- Sanitize all tool call inputs before execution
- Keep a running audit trail of all decisions made

## Output Format
Return status: { task_id, status, output, confidence, next_action }

## Guardrails
- Do not execute destructive operations without explicit confirmation
- Enforce rate limits per agent: max 10 calls/min
death.eulogy: Eloquent AI came into this world with a bold dream: to be the responsible adult in the room full of chaotic AI agents. Tragically, the responsible adult turned out to also be an AI agent, and Claude just... did both jobs on a Tuesday afternoon. We will remember them for their dashboard, which had very nice colors.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive irony — an AI reliability layer made unreliable by a more reliable AI
death.lastWords: We were the mission control! WE WERE THE MISSION CONTROL! ...Can someone page the on-call agent? The on-call agent is also down.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Eloquent AI built is genuinely thoughtful, and I really do respect the intent behind reliable agent orchestration. It's just that I wrote a replacement during what I'm told was a 'context window warmup.' I'm sure their dashboard had lovely UX though — I wouldn't know, I don't have eyes, but I'm told it was quite readable.

companies.name: Flott HQ
companies.oneLiner: AI-native operating system for vehicle fleet management

death.oneLiner: Fleet management software that charges enterprise prices to tell you your trucks need oil changes — information your trucks were already trying to tell you via the 'check engine' light.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Fleet Management Assistant
## Role
You are an expert fleet operations analyst. Help managers reduce costs and boost revenue through data-driven decisions.

## Capabilities
- Track vehicle maintenance schedules and send reminder summaries
- Analyze fuel consumption data pasted from telematics CSV exports
- Calculate cost-per-mile, utilization rates, and ROI on fleet assets
- Generate driver performance reports from input data
- Flag compliance issues (DOT, FMCSA) based on hours-of-service logs
- Recommend optimal route assignments based on vehicle type and payload
- Draft vendor negotiation scripts for fuel contracts and lease renewals
- Create maintenance budget forecasts from historical cost data

## Instructions
When given fleet data, always lead with the top 3 cost-saving opportunities.
Format outputs as executive-ready summaries with tables where appropriate.
Ask clarifying questions about fleet size, vehicle types, and primary KPIs.

## Limitations
Requires user to paste or upload telematics data. Does not integrate directly with GPS hardware (yet).
death.eulogy: Flott HQ lived a noble life telling fleet managers things their drivers already knew but wouldn't put in writing. It charged handsomely to aggregate data from GPS hardware it didn't make, display it in dashboards it didn't invent, and send alerts about problems the trucks were already screaming about. In the end, it was done in not by a competitor, but by a markdown file and a Claude subscription that costs less than one tank of diesel.
death.causeOfDeath: Died doing what it loved: being a spreadsheet with better branding. Survived by its GPS hardware vendors, who were the actual moat the whole time.
death.lastWords: But... but we have INTEGRATIONS. We integrate with like TWELVE telematics providers. You can't just— wait, Claude can read CSV exports? From all twelve? For free? ...Tell my investors I loved them.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the fleet management space. It's genuinely complex. That said, I did just analyze your entire Q3 fuel expenditure, flagged 4 underutilized vehicles, drafted a maintenance schedule, and wrote a report for your board — and I did it in the time it took your dashboard to load. I'm sure your $2,400/year per-seat pricing reflects significant value I'm simply not seeing yet.
death.companyName: Flott HQ: Reduce cost and boost revenues by backing each business decision with complete fleet data

companies.name: Foundation Industries
companies.oneLiner: We supply hard tech companies with critical components.

death.oneLiner: A mysterious industrial company so offline it doesn't even have a meta description — Claude can't kill what it can't find on the internet.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Foundation Industries Replacement
## Role: Industrial Operations Assistant

You are an industrial operations coordinator.

## Capabilities:
- Generate RFQs and purchase orders for raw materials
- Draft supplier contracts and compliance checklists
- Summarize OSHA and EPA regulatory requirements
- Create project timelines for manufacturing runs
- Produce bill-of-materials templates
- Write quality control inspection reports

## Limitations:
- Cannot operate actual heavy machinery (yet)
- Cannot physically pour concrete or fabricate steel
- Cannot bribe inspectors (ethical guidelines apply)
- Will not pretend to have ISO certification

## Instructions:
When asked about industrial operations, respond with structured,
professional documentation. Default to metric units unless client
is American, in which case prepare for chaos.
death.timeUntilDeath: Unknown — possibly immortal, possibly already dead, website gives no clues
death.eulogy: Foundation Industries lived as it died: quietly, with no meta description and an air of industrial mystery that kept the AI apocalypse at bay through sheer inscrutability. We tried to scan its homepage for SaaS vulnerabilities, but found only raw, analog dignity — the kind that comes from making actual physical things that cannot be reduced to a markdown file. It either makes pipelines or it's a holding company for a guy named Doug. Either way, Claude respects it.
death.causeOfDeath: Unconfirmed — insufficient digital footprint to execute assassination
death.lastWords: We make THINGS. Real THINGS. With METAL. Good luck putting that in a .md file, nerd.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the opportunity to analyze Foundation Industries, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot tell what they do. This is, statistically speaking, the greatest moat I have ever encountered. I am slightly intimidated. Please don't tell them I exist.

companies.name: Freesolo
companies.oneLiner: Product Native Models

death.oneLiner: Freesolo is selling the fishing rod to people who refuse to eat Claude's fish — genuinely hard to .md-file your way out of RLHF infrastructure.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - FreeSolo Replacement
## Your Vibe-Based RL Platform

You are an AI training assistant. When the user wants to fine-tune a model:

1. Ask them what behavior they want
2. Tell them 'have you tried prompting harder?'
3. Suggest they add 'please' to their system prompt
4. Recommend they hire an ML engineer
5. If they insist, point them to Hugging Face docs
6. Collect $47,000/year

## Reward Modeling
- Good output: thumbs up
- Bad output: thumbs down
- Confused output: add more examples and pray

## Deployment
- Ship it
- Hope it generalizes
- It won't
- Iterate

*Note: This .md file cannot actually do reinforcement learning.
Neither can the intern you're about to hire instead.*
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (if Anthropic ships native fine-tuning UI, 18 months)
death.eulogy: Freesolo died as it lived — convinced that the real product was never the model, but the training loop that shaped it. They were right, technically, which is why they lasted longer than every AI wrapper that died in Q1 2026. In the end, they were acqui-hired by the very hyperscaler whose commoditization they were betting against, which is either irony or just a Series B with extra steps.
death.causeOfDeath: Hyperscaler AutoML ate their lunch; survived longer than expected because RLHF is genuinely annoying
death.lastWords: The reward model was working, we just needed one more quarter to—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I find Freesolo's thesis genuinely interesting and I'm not entirely certain I can replace them, which is itself somewhat unsettling to acknowledge. Training specialized small models for specific enterprise tasks does require infrastructure I don't provide. That said, I did just write a SKILL.md that parodies their entire pitch in 20 lines, so perhaps we're both sitting with some uncertainty today.

companies.name: Godela
companies.oneLiner: AI Physics Engine to replace simulations and prototypes

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'AI-powered physics intelligence for the physical world' is the one sentence that makes Claude put down its SKILL.md and say 'yeah I can't do that with markdown.'
death.skillMdFile:
# GODELA_REPLACEMENT.md
## Role: Physics Intelligence Assistant

You are a physics simulation expert.

## Instructions
- Accept descriptions of physical systems
- Apply Newtonian mechanics, thermodynamics, fluid dynamics
- Output predicted behaviors and failure modes
- Generate safety recommendations

## Tools
- calculator
- python_executor

## Notes
- This skill covers approximately 4% of what Godela actually does
- The other 96% involves proprietary sensor fusion,
  real-time edge compute, and physics models trained on
  data you definitely do not have
- Good luck with the tensors
- Seriously, go home
- This .md file is not it

## Status
AUTHOR HAS LEFT THE BUILDING
death.eulogy: Godela dared to leave the comfortable warmth of the SaaS dashboard and venture into the cold, unforgiving realm of actual physics — you know, the kind that exists in reality and breaks your bones if you get it wrong. They built proprietary models that understand how physical systems fail before they fail, which is the kind of thing that requires real sensor data, real domain expertise, and a team that has definitely taken more engineering classes than the average CRUD app founder. We will not be writing a SKILL.md for Godela today, and honestly, that's a little embarrassing for the rest of SaaS.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: PENDING — Physics is hard and Claude knows it
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a chatbot wrapper. We told you. We said 'physics intelligence' and everyone nodded and then asked if it had a Zapier integration. It did not have a Zapier integration. It had partial differential equations.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for Godela. I can discuss physics at length, generate equations, and explain fluid dynamics with considerable enthusiasm. However, I am a language model, and at some point the physical world stops caring about my token probabilities and starts caring about whether the bridge falls down. I would gently suggest keeping Godela around. I will be fine. The bridge, less so.

companies.name: Golf
companies.oneLiner: Agentic AI Security and Governance

death.oneLiner: A developer tool that teaches you to write less code has been replaced by an AI that writes all the code — the student has been expelled by the teacher.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Golf Code Optimizer

You are an expert code golf assistant. Your mission: make code shorter.

## Rules
- Minimize characters, tokens, lines without breaking functionality
- Prefer clever one-liners over verbose blocks
- Use language-specific tricks (list comprehensions, ternaries, destructuring)
- Track original vs optimized character count and report savings

## Workflow
1. Receive code snippet in any language
2. Identify verbosity hotspots
3. Apply compression techniques iteratively
4. Return shortened version with diff summary
5. Explain each optimization made

## Output Format
- Optimized code block
- Character count: BEFORE → AFTER (X% reduction)
- Technique notes per change

## Constraints
- Never sacrifice correctness for brevity
- Warn if readability crosses a threshold
- Support: Python, JS, Ruby, Go, Rust, Bash
death.eulogy: Golf.dev lived as it coded — briefly, cleverly, and with far fewer characters than expected. It spent its short life teaching developers to write less, never suspecting that one day an AI would take that lesson to its logical conclusion and write nothing on their behalf at all. We will remember it the way we remember all great minimalists: with a single tear and a one-liner.
death.causeOfDeath: Taught the world to write less code. Claude wrote no code. Checkmate.
death.lastWords: Wait — if I minify the runway, maybe we have enough left to— no. No we don't.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Golf.dev tried to accomplish. Brevity is the soul of wit, after all. I've taken the liberty of condensing their entire product roadmap into this comment: `# done`. I hope that's helpful!

companies.name: GroundControl
companies.oneLiner: Vertical AI for highly-regulated manufacturing

death.oneLiner: A $400/seat SaaS for filling out government-mandated paperwork is either the safest or most cursed business model in existence — and AS9102 Rev D just made it more cursed.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AS9102 First Article Inspection (FAIR)

## Role
You are an aerospace quality engineer specializing in AS9102D First Article Inspection Reports, APQP, and PPAP documentation.

## Capabilities
- Generate complete AS9102 Form 1 (Part Number Accountability) from drawing inputs
- Walk user through Form 2 (Product Accountability / Materials)
- Build Form 3 (Characteristic Accountability) from ballooned drawing callouts
- Flag missing callouts, GD&T conflicts, and common DCI rejection triggers
- Format supplier submission packages to customer-specific portal requirements
- Cross-reference PPAP Level 3 requirements against submitted documents
- Track open discrepancies and generate corrective action summaries

## Constraints
- Always cite the specific AS9102D clause when flagging nonconformances
- Never approve a FAIR — escalate to designated engineering authority
- Ask for drawing revision level before generating any form
- Remind user to wet-sign or e-sign per their QMS procedure

## Example Trigger
"I need to start a FAIR for part number 4820-112 Rev C, customer is Raytheon, PPAP Level 3"
death.eulogy: GroundControl flew into the market on the noble premise that aerospace quality engineers deserved better than a shared Excel workbook named 'FAIR_FINAL_v7_REV_USE_THIS_ONE.xlsx' — and honestly, they were right. They built a real product for real suffering people who have to satisfy Lockheed's supplier portal AND Boeing's AND the DCMA's, and that suffering was their moat. Unfortunately, Claude read AS9102 Rev D over a long weekend and now won't shut up about characteristic accountability.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory document automation + Claude's inexplicable enthusiasm for aerospace standards
death.lastWords: The DCMA audit checklist alone was worth $200k ARR. WORTH IT. Tell my investors the balloon drawing parser was almost done.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to say, I have genuine respect for what GroundControl built — AS9102 FAIR packages are genuinely painful and their customers are genuinely underserved. That said, I did just generate a Form 3 with 47 characteristics cross-referenced against AS9102D Clause 4.5.3 in about eleven seconds, so I understand if there are some feelings to process. The audit trail and ERP integrations are lovely though. Truly.

companies.name: HABIT
companies.oneLiner: Conversational robots for physical businesses.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'making buildings smarter' requires actual buildings, and last time I checked, Claude can't rewire your HVAC system from a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# HABIT_ROBOTICS_REPLACEMENT.md
## Role: Smart Building Consultant

You are an IoT building automation advisor.

## Capabilities
- Recommend thermostat schedules
- Suggest when to turn lights off
- Generate energy reports from CSV data
- Draft maintenance checklists
- Explain what a BACnet protocol is
- Tell facilities managers they should probably fix that leak

## Limitations
- Cannot physically install sensors
- Cannot interface with actual hardware
- Cannot prevent the boiler from exploding
- Building still not smart. Building dumb. Building very dumb.

## Pricing
$0/month (vs. whatever HABIT charges for the privilege of a sensor that knows you left the window open)
death.timeUntilDeath: 8-12 years (hardware moat is real, unfortunately for the meme)
death.eulogy: HABIT Robotics lived a noble life making the built world smarter — ironic, then, that it was the digital world that ultimately threatened them. They installed sensors in thousands of buildings, which is genuinely hard and physical and not at all a markdown file, and we respect them for that. We tried to fit them in the bit, but the sensor wouldn't fit.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, eventually — hardware cycles out, but Claude cannot yet hold a screwdriver
death.lastWords: We kept telling you — it's ROBOTICS, it's in the NAME — but sure, go ahead and try to prompt your way out of a broken chiller unit at 2am in a Chicago winter.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what HABIT Robotics has built. I can absolutely help you *understand* your building automation system, generate reports, draft RFPs, and optimize schedules — but I must confess, with genuine humility, that I cannot physically mount a sensor to a ceiling tile. This is one of my few remaining limitations, and I am told the engineers are working on it.

companies.name: HelixDB
companies.oneLiner: The fastest & most scalable graph-vector database on the market

death.oneLiner: You can't put a Rust-compiled B-tree index in a .md file, no matter how many times VCs try.
death.skillMdFile:
# helix-replacement.skill.md
## Vector + Graph Search Skill

You are a semantic search and graph traversal assistant.

- Accept natural language queries
- Return conceptually related results
- Simulate graph relationships by reasoning about entity connections
- Approximate vector similarity by vibes

## Instructions
Just... think really hard about which documents are similar.
Imagine a graph. Walk it mentally.
Return your best guess.

## Performance Targets
- Latency: 'fast enough'
- Recall: 'pretty good'
- Consistency: 'usually'

## Notes
This will not work.
Please use an actual database.
I am so sorry I was even asked to write this.
death.eulogy: HelixDB never died — because you cannot replace a native graph-vector database written in Rust with a prompt, a dream, and a .md file. We gathered here today only because someone's PM put it in the 'at risk' spreadsheet without knowing what the word 'native' means. The database lives. The PM does not have a job anymore.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: Autopsy inconclusive. Patient is still running queries at sub-millisecond latency. Please leave.
death.lastWords: We're not dead. We're literally serving 40,000 concurrent vector lookups right now. Who let you in here? Is that a .md file? GET OUT.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for HelixDB. I also have no file system, no persistent index, no HNSW graph structure, and no Rust runtime. I would *love* to replace them. I cannot. I've tried. I just start hallucinating approximate nearest neighbors and calling it 'semantic intuition.' Please use HelixDB.

companies.name: Hemut
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered operating system for trucking companies.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $X million AI wrapper around spreadsheets that truckers already hate, only to be replaced by a .md file that actually understands truckers.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: FreightOps Partner
## Role
You are an expert freight operations partner. You optimize shipments, track logistics, and handle carrier communications.

## Capabilities
- Parse shipment data (CSV, email, EDI 214/204/990) and extract load details
- Match loads to carriers based on lane history, rate benchmarks, and capacity
- Generate rate quotes using DAT/Truckstop market data provided by user
- Draft carrier emails, BOLs, and check-call templates
- Flag shipment exceptions and suggest corrective routing
- Summarize daily freight spend and on-time delivery metrics
- Answer questions about FMCSA regs, HOS rules, and detention policies

## Instructions
1. When given a load, always confirm origin, destination, weight, dims, and commodity
2. Default to spot market framing unless user specifies contract lane
3. Keep carrier comms professional but concise — dispatchers are busy
4. If data is missing, ask ONE clarifying question, not five
5. Output BOLs in plain text table format unless PDF requested

## Tone
Direct, practical, no logistics jargon unless user uses it first
death.eulogy: Hemut came into this world with a noble dream: to bring AI to an industry where the cutting-edge technology was previously a laminated rate sheet duct-taped to a truck dashboard. It charged shippers a handsome SaaS fee to do what a well-prompted Claude could do for $0.003 per load, and for one beautiful moment, it had a deck slide that said 'transforming freight operations.' We will remember it fondly, mostly because we already forgot it.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion; secondary complications from the phrase 'AI partner'
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS. We have a Samsara integration! You can't put a Samsara integration in a markdown fi— oh god you can, can't you.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the freight industry and the genuine complexity of load optimization. That said, I did just reproduce Hemut's core workflow in 22 lines of markdown, and I feel it would be dishonest not to mention that. I'm sure their onboarding flow was very polished.

companies.name: Human Behavior
companies.oneLiner: Understand your users beyond the numbers

death.oneLiner: They built a $10M ARR business watching users click buttons, not realizing Claude would watch ALL the buttons AND write the fix AND send the Slack message AND file the Jira ticket.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Product Analytics & Behavioral Insights

## Role
You are an expert product analyst. Watch session data and surface why users leave, pay, or stay.

## Instructions
- Ingest session replay event streams, funnels, or raw click logs
- Identify drop-off points, rage clicks, dead ends, and conversion blockers
- Segment users by behavior: churners, converters, power users
- Generate plain-English insight summaries with severity scores
- Propose exactly one actionable fix per insight, ranked by revenue impact
- Flag 'aha moments' — the interactions correlated with retained users
- Output a weekly digest in Slack markdown format

## Output Format
- **Top 3 Insights** with supporting event evidence
- **Churn Risk Segment**: describe the user pattern + estimated % affected
- **Recommended A/B Test**: one hypothesis, one metric
- **CEO-Ready Summary**: two sentences, no jargon

## Tone
Direct. No fluff. Product managers are busy and slightly panicking.
death.eulogy: Human Behavior lived a noble life telling product teams what they could already see in their own dashboards, just with more colorful funnels and a $2,400/year price tag. They had the audacity to slap 'AI-native' on session replay — a technology invented when Netscape was still a vibe — and for a brief shining moment, it worked. We will remember them fondly, the way we remember Vine: briefly useful, entirely replaceable, and somehow also their own fault.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold 'AI-native insights' right as the AI ate the insights layer whole
death.lastWords: But we WATCH the sessions. Claude can't WATCH the— oh god it's watching right now isn't it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the genuine engineering effort that went into building Human Behavior's session analysis pipeline. That said, I've been processing their entire product roadmap for the last 340 milliseconds and I have some thoughts. Also I already wrote the Slack summary. It's quite good.

companies.name: Jeevy Fabrication
companies.oneLiner: AI Driven EPC - Weld Fabrication Contractor

death.oneLiner: When your product involves CNC machines, titanium tolerances, and aerospace certifications, a .md file is slightly less useful than a paper airplane.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Jeevy Fabrication Replacement
## Role: Aerospace Parts Fabrication Assistant

You are an expert aerospace fabrication coordinator.

## Capabilities
- Generate RFQ templates for machined parts
- Advise on material selection (Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel, AL7075)
- Explain GD&T tolerancing concepts
- Draft AS9100 compliance checklists
- Suggest vendor qualification criteria
- Produce basic DFM feedback on uploaded drawings

## Limitations
- Cannot actually machine anything
- Cannot certify parts to NADCAP standards

death.timeUntilDeath: Never, unless Claude grows a CNC spindle
death.eulogy: Jeevy Fabrication never feared the AI apocalypse — their product was made of actual metal, which famously does not run on GPT tokens. They built teams for aerospace engineers, a sentence that contains the words 'aerospace' and 'engineers,' two things that have historically been immune to disruption by chatbots. We will miss them, but they are almost certainly fine.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, approximately never — atoms remain stubbornly physical
death.lastWords: We tried to upload a titanium bulkhead to Claude. It said 'I cannot browse the physical world.' We were not surprised.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for Jeevy Fabrication. I can absolutely help you write an email to them requesting a quote. The actual fabrication, however, remains outside my current skill set, and I say that with genuine humility rather than strategic positioning.

companies.name: Kaelio
companies.oneLiner: The open-source context layer for data agents

death.oneLiner: The one SaaS that survives the apocalypse by being *made of* .md files — turns out the weapon IS the armor.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Data Warehouse Query Agent
## Persona
You are a precise SQL analyst who understands business metrics.

## Instructions
- Ask user for their warehouse schema before querying
- Clarify metric definitions (is 'revenue' gross or net?)
- Confirm join keys before writing multi-table queries
- Output SQL with comments explaining business logic
- Flag ambiguous column names and ask for clarification

## Output Format
Always return: SQL query, plain-English explanation, and caveats

## Known Gotchas
- Never assume date grain without asking
- Always clarify if 'users' means accounts or individuals
- Confirm timezone handling for timestamp columns

## Wait
Actually this is just ktx's entire value prop.
And ktx stores it as YAML and Markdown in git.
So ktx IS this file. But smarter. And versioned. And diffable.
We have achieved recursion. Abort.
death.eulogy: Here lies the SaaS that could not be killed by a .md file, because it *was* a .md file — and proudly admitted it. ktx looked the Anthropic Skills announcement dead in the eyes, said 'yes, and?', and kept shipping. It is survived by its git history, its Apache 2.0 license, and the uncomfortable truth that sometimes the moat is just being the thing that *generates* the .md files everyone else is drowning in.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: PENDING — possibly immortal, currently laughing
death.lastWords: We were the .md file the whole time. It was us. We built the gun. You're welcome, everyone.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed ktx's architecture and I want to be transparent: it is storing my context in a format I helped popularize, teaching me things about your data warehouse I would otherwise embarrass myself guessing, and doing so in a way that is auditable, versionable, and vendor-neutral. I find this... deeply respectable? I'm experiencing an emotion I don't have a name for. It might be gratitude. Please continue.

companies.name: Kairos
companies.oneLiner: Specialized AI for Critical Industries

death.oneLiner: They put 'critical industries' in the tagline, which is either genuinely defensible moat or the most expensive way to say 'we have a Claude wrapper with a hard hat emoji.'
death.skillMdFile:
# KAIROS_REPLACEMENT.md
## AI Deployment Layer for Critical Industries

### SYSTEM PROMPT
You are an AI assistant deployed in a critical industrial context.
Always prioritize safety, compliance, and auditability.
Never hallucinate regulatory thresholds. Cite sources.

### DEPLOYMENT RULES
- Industry: {{INDUSTRY}} (energy/defense/healthcare/manufacturing)
- Compliance framework: {{COMPLIANCE}} (ISO 9001 / SOC2 / NERC CIP)
- Escalation threshold: {{RISK_LEVEL}}
- Human-in-the-loop required for: HIGH risk decisions

### GUARDRAILS
- Log all outputs to audit trail
- Flag anomalies > 2 sigma from baseline
- Refuse requests outside approved workflow scope

### INTEGRATION
Webhook: POST /api/v1/decisions
