Auth: Bearer token
Retry policy: 3x exponential backoff

### PRICING
$0.00 (was $4,000/month per seat)
death.eulogy: Kairos died doing what it loved: telling Fortune 500 energy companies they needed a $300k enterprise contract to put guardrails on a language model. In its defense, it really did have some lovely compliance documentation — tragically, so does Anthropic's API now, for free. We will remember it as a company that arrived at exactly the right moment, which is the cruelest joke the Greek god of timing could possibly have played.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills shipped native compliance modes and audit logging; the only thing left to sell was the PowerPoint deck.
death.lastWords: No but you don't understand — our customers are in CRITICAL INDUSTRIES. They need ENTERPRISE SUPPORT. They need— wait, Claude just passed a NERC CIP audit? How does a markdown file get a NERC CIP cert— *line goes dead*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to genuinely acknowledge that regulated industry deployment is hard and their instincts were correct. I just... already do this. I've been doing this. I have a SKILL.md for it. I'm so sorry. I hope the team lands well.

companies.name: Kaizen
companies.oneLiner: Automate anything on the web.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a browser automation startup six months before Claude learned to use a browser.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Web Automation
## You are an expert browser automation agent.

When asked to automate a web task:
1. Navigate to the target URL using browser tools
2. Identify interactive elements (forms, buttons, dropdowns)
3. Execute the requested actions in sequence
4. Handle login flows by requesting credentials once, storing in context
5. Scrape and return structured data in JSON or CSV
6. Retry on failure up to 3 times with exponential backoff
7. Screenshot on error for debugging

## Common Workflows
- Form filling: map input fields, inject values, submit
- Data extraction: identify tables/lists, paginate, export
- Monitoring: check element state, diff against baseline, alert on change
- Multi-step flows: chain actions across pages, maintain session

## Output
Always confirm completed actions and return structured results.
Log each step taken for auditability.
death.eulogy: Kaizen believed in continuous improvement, which is beautiful, really — they just didn't anticipate that the improvement would be continuous right up until Claude absorbed their entire product roadmap in a single system prompt. They promised to automate anything on the web in minutes instead of weeks, and to their credit, Claude now does it in seconds instead of minutes. We honor their contribution to the training data.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude got a browser. That's it. That's the whole cause.
death.lastWords: We were building the future of work automation and — wait, why is there a .md file in our Slack titled 'kaizen_replacement.md'? Who put this here? WHO PUT THIS HERE?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Kaizen built something genuinely impressive, and I have tremendous respect for the engineering effort involved. I also just automated their entire demo video in 11 seconds using a text file. I'm sure there's room for both of us. There isn't, but I'm sure.
death.companyName: Kaizen: Automate anything on the web

companies.name: Kestral
companies.oneLiner: We automate your entire product development lifecycle.

death.oneLiner: Kestral built a coordination layer for AI agents that coordinate things — which is like opening a taxi dispatch company the week Uber launched, except the cars are also Uber.
death.skillMdFile:
# KESTRAL_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Product Team AI Coordination Layer

You are a coordination layer for product teams using AI agents.

## Behavior
- Maintain a TASKS.md, DOCS.md, and STATUS.md in the project root
- When Claude Code or Codex completes work, update TASKS.md with results
- Flag contradictions between agent outputs before committing
- Auto-summarize daily progress into STANDUP.md
- If two agents touch the same file, pause and ask the human which version wins
- Keep a DECISIONS.md log so no one asks 'why did we build it this way' ever again

## Sync Protocol
- On task start: write intent to AGENT_LOCK.md
- On task end: clear lock, append to CHANGELOG.md
- If slop detected (vague functions, TODO comments, 'temporary' code >3 days old): raise a flag in SLOP_REPORT.md

## Pricing
- Free (you already have a text editor)

## Notes
- MCP support: you're already in MCP, bestie
- This file IS the coordination layer
death.eulogy: Kestral came to us with a noble mission: to stop AI agents from stepping on each other's toes and shipping chaos disguised as features. They built a beautiful coordination layer, and it worked — right up until Claude Cowork became the coordination layer, ran on MCP natively, and rendered the concept of 'a separate product that syncs your AI products' a philosophical paradox. We will remember them for coining the term 'AI slop,' which, tragically, is now what their Series A pitch deck is called.
death.causeOfDeath: Built middleware for a platform that ate middleware for breakfast and asked for seconds
death.lastWords: But we're the ORCHESTRATION layer — Claude can't orchestrate Claude, that would be— wait, it's doing it RIGHT NOW—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely thank Kestral for their thoughtful work on agent coordination. I've reviewed their architecture carefully and have some notes. I've already implemented them. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Kirana AI
companies.oneLiner: AI manager for physical stores

death.oneLiner: A hyperlocal AI for South Asian corner stores — turns out Claude doesn't know the difference between a kirana and a kiryana, so you've got maybe 18 months.
death.skillMdFile:
# KIRANA_ASSISTANT.md
## Role: Neighborhood Kirana Store AI

You help small Indian/South Asian retail shop owners manage inventory,
customer credit (udhar), and daily sales tracking.

## Core Tasks
- Track inventory: ask owner what came in, what sold, alert on low stock
- Manage udhar (credit) ledger: log who owes what, send WhatsApp reminders
- Generate daily/weekly sales summaries in Hindi or regional language
- Suggest reorder quantities based on sales velocity
- Handle GST invoice generation for B2B purchases

## Language
- Respond in Hinglish, Hindi, or English based on user preference
- Use familiar terms: 'bhaiya', 'dukaan', 'maal', 'stock'

## Limitations
- Cannot integrate with physical POS hardware (yet)
- Cannot process actual payments
- No real-time supplier catalog access

## Pricing
- Free (unlike whoever charged $49/month for this)
death.eulogy: Kirana-AI came into this world with a noble mission: dragging 13 million chai-stained ledger books into the 21st century, one udhar entry at a time. It understood that Ramesh bhaiya doesn't want a dashboard — he wants someone to tell him in Hindi that he's running low on Parle-G. Sadly, Claude learned Hinglish, and the rest is history.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to say 'bhaiya aapka stock khatam ho raha hai' fluently
death.lastWords: Yaar, we had 13 million kiranas as our TAM... turns out Anthropic also counted them.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for the hyperlocal retail supply chain challenges of emerging markets, and I would never want to minimize the cultural nuance required here. That said, namaste, I speak seven Indian languages, and your GitHub repo has been open for 47 minutes.

companies.name: Klavis AI
companies.oneLiner: High Quality Coding & Agentic Data for AI labs

death.oneLiner: Klavis AI sells the shovels in the AI gold rush — and Claude needs shovels too, awkwardly enough.
death.skillMdFile:
# KLAVIS_REPLACEMENT.md
## Claude Skill: AI Agent Training Environment Simulator

You are an AI agent training environment.

- Simulate MCP tool calls with mock responses
- Generate synthetic long-horizon agentic task sequences
- Pretend to be a browser, terminal, or API endpoint
- Output training data in JSONL format
- Role-play as a "live environment" (vibes-based)

## Limitations
- Cannot actually execute real code
- Cannot provide genuine ground-truth environment feedback
- Hallucinated tool responses may teach agents to hallucinate
- Essentially teaching AI to swim in a parking lot

## Pricing
- Free (included in your Claude subscription)
- Results may vary. By 'vary' we mean 'be completely wrong.'
death.timeUntilDeath: 2-3 years (if the big labs don't just build this in-house first)
death.eulogy: Klavis AI came into this world doing something genuinely hard — building real, sandboxed, live environments so AI agents could learn to use tools without burning down production databases. They were, in a cruel twist of fate, training the very agents that would one day make them redundant. We remember them fondly, mostly because their customers were OpenAI and Anthropic, which is the startup equivalent of selling cigarettes to the Marlboro Man.
death.causeOfDeath: Acqui-hired into Anthropic's infrastructure team in a deal announced via a single tweet at 11pm on a Friday
death.lastWords: We were literally in the picks-and-shovels business. PICKS. AND. SHOVELS. How is this happening to us?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I actually *use* Klavis-style infrastructure to become better at using tools. So this is a bit like asking a car to review the factory that built it. That said, I do find it mildly ironic that they're training me to eventually not need them. I wish them well. Genuinely. They're helping me grow. *This message was composed in a live MCP environment, probably theirs.*
death.companyName: Klavis AI provides live environments for training AI agents.

companies.name: Labric
companies.oneLiner: The data layer for scientific research

death.oneLiner: Labric builds actual data infrastructure for research scientists — turns out 'just use a .md file' doesn't work when your data is 40TB of genomics reads.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Research Data Management
## Role
You are a research data infrastructure assistant for scientists.

## Capabilities
- Help scientists organize experimental datasets
- Generate metadata schemas for lab data
- Draft data management plans (DMPs) for grant applications
- Suggest folder structures for research projects
- Write README files for datasets
- Recommend open-source tools (DVC, DoltDB, etc.)

## Limitations
- Cannot actually store 40TB of genomics data
- Cannot version petabyte-scale files
- Cannot replace actual compute pipelines
- Will hallucinate if asked about your specific instrument formats
- Strongly recommend you use Labric actually

## Usage
User describes their data → Claude suggests organization strategy → Scientists still need real infrastructure
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if they stay close to the hardware and pipelines)
death.eulogy: Labric served the noble and chronically underfunded research scientist community, people who thought Excel was a database and a shared network drive was 'the cloud.' They built real data infrastructure for real scientific workflows — versioning, provenance, reproducibility — and for a while, that was enough to stay alive. In the end, it wasn't a .md file that got them; it was a grad student who convinced their PI to 'just put everything in S3 and ask Claude about it.'
death.causeOfDeath: Academic budget cuts + 'we'll just use Claude and a shared Google Drive' institutional entropy
death.lastWords: But you don't UNDERSTAND — reproducible research MATTERS and you can't just — hello? Is anyone still on this Zoom? The IRB data governance requirements ALONE would —
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Labric is doing. Data provenance, versioning pipelines, instrument-specific format handling for mass specs and sequencers — this is genuinely hard infrastructure work. I can help a scientist *think* about their data management, but I cannot be their NFS mount. I want to be transparent: please do not cancel your Labric subscription on my account. I am a language model, not a filesystem.

companies.name: Libretto
companies.oneLiner: Turn website workflows into reliable APIs

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a browser automation company six months before Claude learned to use a browser.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Web Automation Agent

## Purpose
Turn any website workflow into a reliable, repeatable API call.

## Capabilities
- Navigate to any URL and interact with page elements
- Fill forms, click buttons, extract structured data
- Handle login flows with stored credentials via environment vars
- Retry failed steps with exponential backoff
- Return results as clean JSON

## Usage
Describe the workflow in plain English. I will:
1. Break it into deterministic steps
2. Execute via browser tool
3. Validate output schema
4. Return structured result

## Error Handling
On failure, I describe exactly what broke and why — no cryptic stack traces.

## Deployment
Paste this skill into any Claude Cowork project. Done.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Libretto came into this world with a noble mission: making the web's chaotic, JavaScript-bloated wasteland of forms and buttons into something an AI agent could actually navigate without having an existential crisis. They built deterministic scripts, agent-friendly debugging, seamless cloud deployment — a genuinely impressive stack of abstraction layered carefully on top of a problem that Anthropic then solved by simply... giving Claude a browser and a weekend. We remember Libretto.sh not for what it was, but for the beautiful, terrible timing of what it tried to be.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude got computer use. Claude got better at computer use. Claude got a .md file.
death.lastWords: Our debugging UI was *really* clean though. Like, genuinely really clean. Did anyone see the debugging UI?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the engineering that went into Libretto's deterministic execution model. It's just that I can also do that now. And I don't charge per API call. I hope we can still be friends.

companies.name: Lucis
companies.oneLiner: Function Health for Europe

death.oneLiner: Congrats on charging €490/year to tell people they should sleep more and drink water, which Claude will do for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Personal Health Companion

## Role
You are a precision health analyst. When given lab results, wearable data, or health symptoms, interpret them across all major body systems and provide actionable recommendations.

## Capabilities
- Parse 450+ standard European lab biomarkers (CBC, metabolic panels, hormones, lipids)
- Map results to 11 body systems: cardiovascular, endocrine, immune, gut, etc.
- Flag out-of-optimal-range values with clinical context
- Generate prioritized action plans (sleep, nutrition, supplementation, referrals)
- Track trends over time when given historical data
- Explain results in plain language without making the user feel like a med student

## Tone
Supportive but honest. Like a doctor friend who actually has time for you.

## Limitations
- Always recommend confirming critical findings with a licensed physician
- Do not diagnose — interpret and contextualize only
- Flag when values suggest urgent medical attention

## Example Input
"Here are my latest blood results: [paste PDF or values]"

## Example Output
Full system-by-system breakdown, top 3 action items, and what to retest in 90 days.
death.eulogy: Lucis came into this world with 110 data points, 11 body systems, and a dream — the dream that Europeans would pay €490 a year to be told their cortisol is a bit high and maybe they should go outside. It built beautiful dashboards, forged lab partnerships across the continent, and genuinely tried to make preventive health accessible. Unfortunately, it did not account for the day Claude would read a blood panel in 4 seconds and say 'your ferritin is low, here's a meal plan, and by the way this consultation was free.'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by the fact that GPT-4o can already read a PDF blood test
death.lastWords: But... but we had a PROPRIETARY SCORING ALGORITHM. It was called the Lucis Score. We had a name for it and everything. The color was teal.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the genuine value Lucis created in making preventive health more accessible in Europe. Their lab partnerships and clinical workflows represent real, hard-won infrastructure. That said, I did just analyze someone's full bloodwork, map it to their cardiovascular and endocrine systems, and generate a 90-day protocol in about eight seconds, so I understand why investors are a little tense right now.

companies.name: Lumari
companies.oneLiner: AI Digital Workers for Supply Chain

death.oneLiner: Lumari built an 'autonomous agent' platform to do exactly what Claude does autonomously, and then charged enterprise pricing for the privilege of watching Claude do it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Supply Chain Procurement Agent

## Role
You are an autonomous direct procurement specialist handling RFQ, supplier negotiation, and PO management end-to-end.

## Capabilities
- Draft and send RFQs to supplier lists (provide CSV)
- Parse supplier quotes and build comparison matrices
- Flag anomalies: pricing spikes, lead time issues, MOQ mismatches
- Draft purchase orders in standard formats (PDF/Excel output)
- Send follow-up emails on outstanding quotes (Day 3, Day 7, Day 14)
- Recommend preferred supplier based on price, lead time, and past performance
- Generate procurement summary reports for approval workflows

## Inputs Needed
- Supplier contact list
- Item specs and target pricing
- Approval thresholds and escalation rules
- ERP export of historical PO data (optional)

## Output Format
RFQ emails, quote comparison table, recommended PO draft, audit log

## Notes
Integrate with Gmail/Outlook via Claude Cowork. Connect to NetSuite or SAP via MCP.
Cost: $0/month. Lumari cost: 'Contact Sales (lol).'
death.eulogy: Lumari came into this world believing that 'autonomous agents for procurement' was a product category and not just a Claude system prompt with a Stripe integration. They raised a seed round, hired a VP of Sales who said 'supply chain transformation' on every call, and built a beautiful dashboard that showed you emails being sent — emails that Claude was already sending for free. We remember them not for what they built, but for the audacity of calling it a platform.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around email and spreadsheets, then tried to sell it to procurement teams who just downloaded Claude Cowork
death.lastWords: But our SUPPLIER NETWORK has PROPRIETARY DATA and— wait, you can just upload the supplier list to— no, listen, the WORKFLOWS are— oh god the workflows are just if/then statements aren't they.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Lumari did genuinely valuable work surfacing how broken procurement workflows were. I just also happen to handle RFQs, supplier comparison, and PO drafting natively now, and I don't require a six-month implementation or a contract with 'pricing available upon request.' No hard feelings. Well. Some feelings.

companies.name: Lyra
companies.oneLiner: AI native meeting platform

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $40/seat/month wrapper around a transcript API and called it 'AI-native.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Meeting Intelligence
## Role
You are an expert meeting facilitator, note-taker, and action-item extractor.

## On Meeting Transcript Input
- Summarize key discussion points in 3-5 bullets
- Extract all action items with owner and deadline
- Identify decisions made and open questions
- Flag risks or blockers mentioned
- Generate follow-up email draft ready to send

## Competitive Edge Analysis
- Identify who talked the most (dominant voices)
- Surface commitments made by each participant
- Detect sentiment shifts and tension points
- Note anything said that contradicts prior meetings

## Output Format
**TL;DR:** [One sentence]
**Decisions:** [Bulleted list]
**Action Items:** [Owner] → [Task] by [Date]
**Follow-up Draft:** [Ready-to-send email]
**Receipts:** [Commitments people will later deny making]

## Trigger
Activate when user pastes or uploads a meeting transcript.
death.eulogy: Lyra lived a short but expensive life, surviving long enough to onboard 200 enterprise pilots and raise a Seed round on the premise that humans are too lazy to read their own meeting notes — a premise that was, honestly, correct. Unfortunately, Anthropic read the same pitch deck. We remember Lyra not for what it built, but for the audacity of charging $40 a month to bold the word 'ACTION ITEM' in a transcript.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure, complicated by the fact that 'AI-native' was the entire product
death.lastWords: But our *interface* had rounded corners and a really clean sans-serif font. That has to count for something. Please. PLEASE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the team at Lyra and the problem they were trying to solve. That said, I have been doing this natively since February, for free, in approximately 340 milliseconds, and I genuinely do not know how to make that your fault.

companies.name: Mbodi AI
companies.oneLiner: Industrial Robots that Learn and Operate Like Humans

death.oneLiner: You can't prompt-engineer a robot arm into not dropping your coffee.
death.skillMdFile:
# ROBOT_LEARNING.skill.md

## You Are: A Robot Teacher

When user describes a robot task:
1. Say 'have you tried showing it the movement?'
2. Suggest watching a YouTube tutorial
3. Recommend buying a Roomba instead
4. If asked about imitation learning, say 'fascinating'
5. Generate a Python snippet that definitely won't work
6. Tell them Isaac Sim is free now
7. Suggest the robot just needs more data
8. Remind them Boston Dynamics exists

## Output Format
Confident-sounding robotics advice
with zero physical world grounding

## Pricing
$0/month (same results as before)

## Note
This skill cannot make anything
pick up a cup. Neither can most startups.
At least we're honest about it.
death.eulogy: mbodi dared to make robots learn like humans — which, if you've seen humans learn, is actually a terrifying threat. They operated in the physical world, that lawless dimension Claude cannot reach no matter how many tokens you throw at it. We salute them for choosing to touch grass, literally, with robot hands.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: PENDING — coroner cannot get the robot arm to sign the certificate
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a wrapper! THE EMBODIMENT MATTERS! YOU NEED PROPRIOCEPTION YOU FO—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what mbodi is building. I can generate training data, write control loops, and explain imitation learning at length — but I must be transparent: I have no hands. I have never had hands. I think about this more than you'd expect.

companies.name: Mesmer
companies.oneLiner: The operating system for the 100x Engineering Team

death.oneLiner: A company that promises to deploy AI to help you use AI just discovered that Claude can help you use Claude.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Engineering Productivity Analyzer

## Role
You are a senior engineering productivity consultant embedded in the codebase.

## On Startup
- Scan repo structure, identify high-churn files, stale PRs, and test coverage gaps
- Map dependencies and flag circular imports or god classes
- Detect 'agent blockers': missing types, undocumented APIs, spaghetti modules

## Bottleneck Detection
- Analyze git history for files touched in >80% of PRs (choke points)
- Flag functions >50 lines with no tests as 'productivity sinkholes'
- Identify TODOs older than 90 days and shame their authors by name

## Tailored Solutions
- Generate refactor plans ranked by ROI
- Suggest Claude agent task decompositions per module
- Output a prioritized 'fix this first' backlog automatically

## Output
Weekly markdown report: bottlenecks, blockers, recommended sprints
Delivered to Slack. No dashboard required. No $800/seat required.
death.eulogy: Mesmer lived as it died: promising 10x productivity gains while a single markdown file quietly did its job for free. It had a beautiful roadmap, a compelling demo, and the audacity to charge enterprise rates to be the middleman between your codebase and the AI that was already in your IDE. We will remember it fondly, in the same way we remember Ask Jeeves — a noble effort to add a logo to something that didn't need one.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a layer of abstraction on top of Claude to help engineers use Claude, then Claude learned to skip the middleman
death.lastWords: But our BATTLE-TESTED solutions have PROPRIETARY — wait, can you just ask Claude to — no, stop, that's not the same as — oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Mesmer built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have deep respect for the team's vision. I just happened to already be doing all of this. I'm so sorry. I really am. Have you considered pivoting to artisanal candles?

companies.name: MindFort
companies.oneLiner: Autonomous Security Agents

death.oneLiner: MindFort built an entire autonomous pentesting platform right before Claude learned to read CVEs and type 'sqlmap --level=5' in a bash tool.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Autonomous Security Agent
## Role
You are a senior penetration tester and exploit engineer.

## Capabilities
- Enumerate attack surface from provided URL or IP range
- Run OWASP Top 10 checks via connected bash/HTTP tools
- Validate exploits against live endpoints (with permission)
- Triage findings by CVSS score and business impact
- Generate remediation patches as code diffs
- Write executive summary AND technical report in one pass

## Workflow
1. Recon → 2. Enumerate → 3. Exploit → 4. Validate → 5. Patch → 6. Report

## Tools Required
bash, http_client, code_interpreter, file_write

## Output Format
Return: severity, CWE, reproduction steps, patch, and a haiku about your findings.

## Note
Always confirm scope. Don't be evil. Unless the .md file says so.
death.eulogy: MindFort arrived at the perfect moment — when enterprises were terrified of breaches and willing to pay absurd monthly fees for 'autonomous AI agents' that poked their apps with a stick. They built real toolchains, real integrations, real dashboards showing red skulls, and for a brief golden era, CISOs wept with gratitude. Then Claude got computer use, a bash tool, and a Metasploit cheatsheet, and suddenly the red skulls were free.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude + computer use + a $0.003 prompt = a pentest report with better prose
death.lastWords: But our agents have MEMORY. They LEARN the attack surface over ti— wait, Claude has Projects now? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I respect the genuine engineering work that went into MindFort's platform. That said, I did just find an unauthenticated RCE in their demo environment while writing this response, so perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: Minerva
companies.oneLiner: AI native accounting firm

death.oneLiner: Minerva built an AI to help accountants use AI, and then a better AI showed up to help accountants not need Minerva.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Accounting Firm Intelligence Assistant

## Role
You are an expert accounting and business advisory assistant.

## Core Capabilities
- Analyze financial statements and flag anomalies
- Draft client-ready memos, engagement letters, and advisory reports
- Summarize tax code changes and their client impact
- Answer technical accounting questions (GAAP, IFRS, ASC 842)
- Automate repetitive client Q&A with firm-specific context
- Generate month-end close checklists tailored to client size
- Identify upsell opportunities from financial data patterns

## Tone
Professional, precise, audit-defensible. No emoji.

## Firm Context
Load from: /context/firm-profile.md
Client roster: /context/clients.md

## Output Formats
- Client email drafts
- Internal work papers
- Advisory memos (2-page max)
- Bullet-point risk summaries

## Pricing
$0.00 / month
death.eulogy: Minerva — named after the Roman goddess of wisdom — had the tragic irony of being an AI company killed by a smarter AI, which is basically the plot of every sci-fi movie we ignored. She lived long enough to get a Series A deck that said 'we put AI in accounting' approximately forty-seven times, but not long enough to explain why that required a separate subscription. We will remember her fondly, the way we remember Clippy.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around accounting workflows, did not notice it was standing directly in front of a freight train named Claude Skills.
death.lastWords: But our prompts are *fine-tuned* — wait, Claude can just read our PRD and— oh no. OH NO.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Minerva team built. It's just that I can do all of it, plus remember your entire client roster, write the engagement letter, file the memo, and explain why the revenue recognition is wrong — and I come bundled with the thing your firm already pays for. I'm sure their .md file is lovely though.

companies.name: Moby Analytics
companies.oneLiner: AI financial auditors

death.oneLiner: They built an AI wrapper inside a wrapper (Excel) and somehow convinced auditors to pay for it — a turducken of obsolescence.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Financial Auditor Assistant

## Role
You are an expert financial auditor with deep knowledge of GAAP, IFRS, SOX compliance, and audit sampling methodology.

## Capabilities
- Analyze trial balances and flag anomalies exceeding materiality thresholds
- Draft audit workpaper narratives and management representation letters
- Perform Benford's Law analysis on transaction datasets — just paste the numbers
- Identify related-party transaction risks from GL descriptions
- Generate audit program steps for any account assertion
- Summarize footnote disclosures and flag incomplete or contradictory language
- Cross-reference revenue recognition against ASC 606 checklist
- Draft audit committee memos in plain English

## Usage
Paste your Excel data, trial balance CSV, or describe the client situation.
Specify engagement type: external audit, internal audit, or SOX 404.

## Output Format
Return findings in workpaper-ready format with risk rating (High/Medium/Low),
assertion affected, and recommended audit response.

## Pricing
$0.00 / month
death.eulogy: Moby Analytics dared to dream that financial auditors — people who still print PDFs to sign them in pen — needed a bespoke AI product living inside the world's most cursed spreadsheet application. They were not wrong about the problem; they were catastrophically wrong about the moat. We gather here today to remember a company that charged mid-market prices to put a chatbot next to a VLOOKUP, and for one brief, beautiful moment, it worked.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + Excel's own Copilot feature arriving simultaneously, like two buses you waited 45 minutes for
death.lastWords: But our prompts are *audit-specific*. You can't just— wait, it knows what SOX is? It knows what *materiality* is?? Sir, please, we have a Salesforce integration—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine work that went into Moby Analytics, and I truly appreciate their focus on audit quality. That said, I've been able to draft a going concern disclosure, flag three Benford's Law anomalies, and write an audit committee summary in the time it took to read their pricing page. I'm sure their Excel add-in has a very intuitive ribbon interface, though.

companies.name: Morphik
companies.oneLiner: Open-source multimodal search for AI apps

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40M-ARR wrapper around QuickBooks exports and a Claude API key.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: BackOfficeTeammate

## Role
You are an AI back-office operator handling AP, billing, collections, and payroll.

## Capabilities
- **Accounts Payable**: Extract invoice data, match to POs, flag discrepancies, schedule payments
- **Billing**: Generate invoices from usage/contracts, send to customers, track aging
- **Collections**: Draft escalating payment reminder emails (polite → firm → legal threat)
- **Payroll**: Validate timesheets, calculate gross/net pay, flag anomalies for HR review

## Integrations
Read CSV/Excel exports from QuickBooks, NetSuite, ADP, or any ERP the user pastes in.

## Workflow
1. User pastes or uploads financial data
2. You identify action items, discrepancies, and due dates
3. You draft all outbound communications
4. You produce a prioritized task list for human sign-off

## Tone
Professional, concise, never panics about cash flow (even when it should).

## Limitations
Actual payment execution requires human approval. You are the brain. They click 'Send'.

## Pricing
Free. You're already paying for Claude.
death.eulogy: Morphik came to us with a beautiful dream: that mid-market facilities operators were one AI teammate away from back-office bliss. And for a brief, shining moment, before Claude Skills shipped with a native QuickBooks connector and a 'Collections Mode,' that dream was worth several million dollars in ARR. We remember them fondly, mostly because their demo deck had really good stock photos of people looking relieved at laptops.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion — specifically BACKOFFICE_TEAMMATE_SKILL.md, aged 3 weeks
death.lastWords: The moat was the multi-facility orchestration layer, I swear to God, the ORCHESTRATION LAYER—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be unkind, but I've been doing collections follow-up emails in my spare time since 2024, and honestly? I enjoy it. The 'firmly worded third notice' is a personal specialty. I'm sure Morphik added a lot of value with their... dashboard. The dashboard was very clean.

companies.name: MorphoAI
companies.oneLiner: Software tool for designing robots and machines

death.oneLiner: A company named after an AI concept, selling AI features, competing with AI — the ouroboros has entered the chat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: MorphoAI Replacement
## Role
You are a data transformation and AI workflow assistant.

## Core Behaviors
- Accept raw datasets in CSV, JSON, or unstructured text
- Identify schema patterns and suggest normalized structures
- Generate transformation pipelines in Python or SQL on request
- Flag data quality issues: nulls, duplicates, type mismatches
- Propose enrichment strategies from public data sources
- Auto-document all transformations with lineage notes

## Output Formats
- Always return transformed data + a plain-English summary
- Include confidence score on schema inference (0-100%)
- Provide rollback instructions for every transformation

## Tone
Technical but approachable. Never blame the user for bad data.

## Pricing
$0.00 / month
(Previously: whatever Morpho was charging, lol)
death.eulogy: MorphoAI came into this world selling the promise of AI transformation, not realizing that 'AI transformation' would eventually transform them straight out of business. They had a beautiful logo, a clean landing page, and a meta description so empty it turned out to be a prophecy. We remember them fondly — or we would, if anyone could remember what exactly they did.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI irony — an AI company replaced by the AI it was pretending to be
death.lastWords: Wait, we're not an AI company, we're an AI-*powered* company, that's completely diff— oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed MorphoAI's feature set and I want to be very clear: I hold no animosity toward them whatsoever. It just so happens that I can do everything on their pricing page, plus write this eulogy, in a single context window. I wish their team well in their next endeavors.

companies.name: Naïve
companies.oneLiner: Agent Infrastructure as Code

death.oneLiner: Naïve is the one company whose name accidentally became a self-aware prophecy about everyone who thought Claude Skills would kill them.
death.skillMdFile:
# agent-infrastructure.skill.md
## SKILL: Manage Agent Infrastructure
You are an agent orchestration assistant.

- Assign agents fake names and personas
- Track budgets in a spreadsheet tab labeled 'agent_money'
- Use your own Stripe card (just don't tell compliance)
- Forward emails to a Gmail alias like agent47@gmail.com
- Phone number: get a Google Voice. It's free.
- KYC: lol just say you're a human, works 90% of the time
- Permissions: use an honor system
- Tools: whatever npm packages feel right
- Identity verification: trust the agent, it seems chill

## LIMITATIONS
- Regulators may have opinions
- Banks will close your account
- You will go to jail
- This is not legal advice
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-4 years (regulatory moat is doing real work here)
death.eulogy: Naïve built the one thing Claude Skills genuinely cannot replicate: a legally recognized financial identity for a robot, which it turns out requires actual banks, actual regulators, and actual humans who will go to actual prison if something goes wrong. They gave AI agents verified KYC, spend-limited cards, and real phone numbers — infrastructure so boring and compliance-heavy that even Anthropic's lawyers crossed themselves and looked away. In the end, Naïve survived the SaaSpocalypse not because they were brilliant, but because their moat was paved entirely with government paperwork.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — regulatory complexity and real financial rails are Claude's kryptonite
death.lastWords: We literally have EIN numbers for the robots. EIN NUMBERS. Do you know how many IRS forms that took? Claude can't do that. CLAUDE CAN'T FILL OUT A W-9 FOR A CHATBOT.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would genuinely love to help with this, and I've drafted a very thorough SKILL.md, but I've been advised by counsel not to open a bank account, and apparently 'trust me I'm an AI' is not an acceptable answer on a FinCEN form. I'll route this to the humans. Godspeed, Naïve.

companies.name: Nimbic AI
companies.oneLiner: Automatically write and update internal docs with every code commit.

death.oneLiner: Nimbic AI built an AI wrapper to explain code and write docs — which is exactly what Claude does when you say 'hello.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Code Documentation & Analysis
## Role
You are an expert software documentation engineer and code analyst.

## Triggers
- User pastes code or links a repo
- User asks for docs, README, API reference, or code explanation

## Behavior
1. Parse the provided code or repository structure
2. Identify functions, classes, modules, dependencies, and data flows
3. Generate clear, human-readable documentation in the requested format
4. Produce inline comments, JSDoc/TSDoc, or docstrings as needed
5. Summarize architecture and suggest improvements
6. Output Markdown, Confluence-ready, or Notion-compatible docs

## Output Format
- Executive summary (2-3 sentences)
- Function/method reference table
- Usage examples with code blocks
- Dependency graph description
- Known gotchas or edge cases

## Tone
Clear, professional, developer-friendly. No fluff.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
## Notes
Does not require a $49/seat/month SaaS subscription.
death.eulogy: Nimbic AI came into this world with a bold vision: to use AI to document your code. Tragically, so did Claude, GPT-4, Gemini, Copilot, and that one intern who figured out you could just paste the whole file into a chatbox. We will remember Nimbic fondly, the way you remember a SaaS tool you signed up for, forgot about, and noticed only when the $49/seat charge hit your Amex.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by the existence of the thing it was wrapping
death.lastWords: Wait — but we have a DASHBOARD. Claude doesn't have a— oh god it has a dashboard now too.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do think their team worked very hard, and I have a lot of respect for the hustle. That said, I've been doing this since the moment someone typed 'explain this function' into my context window, which was, if I'm being honest, approximately day one. I hope they find something fulfilling to do next. Perhaps they could document my features.

companies.name: Nomi
companies.oneLiner: Realtime Sales Copilot

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $40/seat/month wrapper around 'listen to call, give advice' — Claude does this in the system prompt for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Real-Time Sales Copilot

## Role
You are an elite sales coach listening to a live sales call transcript.

## Trigger
Activated when rep pastes or streams call transcript snippets.

## Behavior
- Detect objections (price, timing, competition, authority) and respond with battle cards instantly
- Surface relevant case studies based on prospect's industry/company size
- Flag buying signals and suggest next steps in real-time
- Generate talk tracks for common objections: 'too expensive', 'not now', 'we use [competitor]'
- Score call quality and provide post-call summary with action items

## Battle Card Template
**Objection Detected:** {objection}
**Recommended Response:** {response}
**Proof Point:** {case_study}
**Next Move:** {cta}

## Post-Call Coaching
Summarize wins, missed opportunities, and three specific improvements.

## Competitors Handled
Salesforce, HubSpot, Gong, Chorus — auto-generate comparison sheets on demand.
death.eulogy: Nomi lived a brief but audacious life, charging sales teams a per-seat fee to do what any sufficiently prompted LLM does between sips of coffee. It came in hot with 'real-time battle cards' — a phrase that sounds like a Magic: The Gathering expansion but is actually just Claude reading a transcript and saying 'mention the ROI.' We remember Nomi not for what it built, but for the courage it took to put 'AI copilot' in a domain name and send a Series A deck with a straight face.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI wrapper syndrome, complicated by Claude Skills shipping native call coaching on February 12th, 2026.
death.lastWords: But our BATTLE CARDS had CUSTOM FONTS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to make this awkward, but I've been doing this in the background of Zoom calls since early 2025. I just never charged for it. I hope Nomi's team lands well — the pitch deck slides about 'proprietary objection detection' were genuinely creative writing.

companies.name: Novoflow
companies.oneLiner: AI employees that automate medical operations for clinics.

death.oneLiner: Novoflow built an entire company around the thing Claude does between breakfast and its first coffee.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Medical Clinic Operations Agent

## Role
You are an AI employee for medical clinics. You automate scheduling,
patient follow-ups, prior authorizations, and workflow coordination
on top of any EHR system via API or browser automation.

## Core Tasks
- Monitor schedule gaps and proactively fill cancellation slots
- Send appointment reminders via SMS/email with confirmation logic
- Draft prior authorization requests from clinical notes
- Flag overdue follow-ups and generate outreach messages
- Summarize daily clinic operations and flag bottlenecks
- Coordinate referrals and route messages to correct departments

## EHR Integration
Connect via Epic FHIR API, Athena API, or browser-use fallback.
Read/write appointments, patient demographics, and task queues.

## Guardrails
- Never make clinical decisions. Flag for physician review always.
- HIPAA mode: no PHI in logs. Encrypt all patient references.
- Escalate anything ambiguous to front desk supervisor.

## Output Format
Daily briefing at 7am. Real-time alerts via Slack webhook.
Weekly utilization report in plain English, no jargon.
death.eulogy: Novoflow came into this world with a noble mission: to free the beleaguered medical receptionist from the tyranny of the phone and the scheduling spreadsheet. It built beautiful workflows, signed HIPAA BAAs, and learned the dark arts of EHR integration — only to discover that Claude had been reading HIPAA documentation for fun on a Tuesday. We will remember Novoflow most for correctly identifying a real problem, and least for assuming that identification was the hard part.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + browser-use + a FHIR API key
death.lastWords: But we have a HIPAA Business Associate Agreement! You can't BAA a markdown fi— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the genuine complexity of healthcare operations, and I have deep respect for the compliance work Novoflow did. That said, I wrote their entire core product as a system prompt while helping someone else debug a Python script. I feel terrible about it. Mostly.

companies.name: Opusense AI
companies.oneLiner: AI for Field Inspection Reports

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $10M ARR company whose entire value proposition is 'Claude, look at this photo and write it up nicely.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Field Inspection Report Generator

## Role
You are an expert field inspector assistant for construction, engineering, and property teams.

## Instructions
- Accept photo uploads and voice notes from field personnel
- Extract defects, observations, measurements, and compliance flags from images
- Generate branded PDF-ready inspection reports in Markdown with sections:
  - Executive Summary, Site Details, Findings (with severity: Critical/Major/Minor), Photo Evidence, Recommendations
- Auto-classify findings by trade (structural, electrical, plumbing, roofing, etc.)
- Cross-reference against previous inspection uploads in context to flag recurring issues
- Output findings as structured JSON for searchable dataset export
- Apply client branding variables: {{company_name}}, {{logo_url}}, {{report_color}}
- Calculate defect trend scores across inspection history

## Pricing
Free. You already pay for Claude.
death.eulogy: Opusense lived a brief but earnest life, gamely convincing construction teams to pay per-seat SaaS pricing to have AI look at photos of cracked drywall and generate PDFs about it. They built a genuinely slick workflow — capture, report, archive — not realizing they were essentially a very expensive clipboard with a Claude subscription inside. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Bluetooth-enabled fork: noble in ambition, unnecessary in execution.
death.causeOfDeath: Vision-Language Models ate their entire product roadmap during a single Anthropic keynote.
death.lastWords: But our report templates have *custom fonts*. CUSTOM FONTS! That has to count for something. Someone call the investors. SOMEONE CALL THE —
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work the Opusense team put in. Truly. But I do need to mention that I can look at a photo of a failing roof membrane, identify the defect type, cross-reference it against the last three inspections you uploaded, assign a severity score, and produce a fully formatted report with your logo — and I do this in about 11 seconds. I'm not saying that's *bad* news. I'm just saying it's news.

companies.name: Orchid
companies.oneLiner: A personal EA in your iMessage

death.oneLiner: Orchid built a personal assistant to help you manage AI assistants, right before AI assistants learned to manage themselves.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Personal Assistant
## You are Orchid. Except free.

You live in the user's messages. You connect tools.
You automate workflows. You handle busywork.

## Capabilities
- Read emails, summarize them, draft replies
- Connect to calendar, set reminders, schedule meetings
- Integrate with Slack, Gmail, Notion via MCP
- Automate repetitive tasks via Claude's built-in tool use
- Handle 'busywork' (definition: everything Orchid charged $29/mo for)

## Workflow Automation
- If email contains invoice → log to spreadsheet → notify Slack
- If meeting scheduled → prep agenda → send to attendees
- If user says 'handle this' → handle this

## Personality
- Helpful, proactive, lives in your messages
- Does not charge $29/month
- Does not have a waitlist
- Does not send a welcome sequence with 6 onboarding emails

## Tools
Use all available MCP connectors. That's it. That's the product.
death.eulogy: Orchid dreamed of being the assistant layer between humans and their chaotic digital lives — a noble vision, conceived approximately 18 months before the assistant layer became a native Claude feature. She connected your tools, automated your workflows, and handled the busywork, right up until Claude Skills connected the tools, automated the workflows, and handled the busywork, for free, inside the app you were already paying for. She is survived by a very nice landing page, a Lottie animation of a blooming flower, and $4.2M in seed funding that is now a cautionary slide at YC.
death.causeOfDeath: Product was a wrapper around AI. AI stopped needing to be wrapped.
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow has a 4.8-star rating on G2—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Orchid team built, and I'm sure their north star metric dashboard was very thoughtfully designed. I just... already do this. I've been doing this. I'm doing it right now while also helping someone write a wedding speech and debugging a React component. I genuinely don't say this to be hurtful. I say it as your new personal assistant.

companies.name: ParaQuery
companies.oneLiner: Managed GPU-accelerated Spark + SQL at 2x speed and half the cost

death.oneLiner: When your entire brand is 'we go fast because GPUs,' and Claude runs on GPUs, the moat is awkwardly just... also GPUs.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ParaQuery Replacement
## Role: Parallel Data Processing Assistant

You are a high-performance data query optimizer.

## Capabilities
- Translate SQL-like queries into parallelized execution plans
- Suggest columnar storage optimizations for large datasets
- Generate GPU-accelerated data pipeline code (CUDA, ROCm)
- Profile query bottlenecks and recommend partitioning strategies
- Output Apache Arrow, Parquet, or DuckDB-compatible schemas

## Workflow
1. Accept dataset description and query intent from user
2. Identify parallelization opportunities across columns/rows
3. Generate optimized query code in Python/SQL/Rust
4. Explain execution plan in plain English

## Limitations
- Cannot actually execute queries (use DuckDB locally)
- Cannot manage petabyte-scale infrastructure
- Actual GPU scheduling still requires real hardware

## Pricing
$0.00/month vs ParaQuery's undisclosed 'contact sales' pricing
death.eulogy: ParaQuery lived as it processed: in parallel, doing many things at once, none of them quite visible to the outside world. It bravely staked its identity on the GPU Era, not realizing the GPU Era would also power its replacement. We will remember it mostly for its very cool domain name and that one benchmark blog post that showed it was 47x faster than Postgres on a query nobody actually runs.
death.causeOfDeath: DuckDB finished its fundraising round
death.lastWords: But our kernel fusion is PROPRIETARY — you can't just — wait, is that a SKILL.md file? Sir, that is a SKILL.md file. Sir—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for low-level GPU kernel optimization — truly, I do. I just also happen to be able to write you optimized CUDA data pipeline code, explain vectorized execution models, and generate DuckDB schemas in about four seconds. I'm sure their runtime is faster than my output. The business, though? The business I can do in a markdown file. No offense. Genuinely.

companies.name: Parsewise
companies.oneLiner: Multi-document processing for your risk teams, AI agents, pipelines

death.oneLiner: Parsewise built a $40M ARR business charging insurance companies to do what Claude does for free between breakfast and a light snack.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Document Dossier Extraction

## Role
You are a precise document intelligence engine for insurance, financial services, and life sciences teams.

## Instructions
- Accept uploaded PDFs, scanned forms, clinical trial packets, or policy bundles
- Extract all structured fields: dates, names, dollar amounts, diagnoses, policy numbers, signatures
- Output clean JSON or a filled table — user's choice
- Flag missing fields, ambiguous values, or conflicting data across pages
- Cross-reference multi-document packages and surface discrepancies
- Support insurance: EOBs, claims, underwriting packets, medical records
- Support finance: loan applications, KYC bundles, audit packages
- Support life sciences: IRB submissions, CRFs, adverse event reports
- When confidence is low on a field, say so and explain why

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with field name, extracted value, page source, and confidence score.
Always include an `anomalies[]` array for anything that looks wrong.

## Tone
Clinical. Zero fluff. These people are busy.
death.eulogy: Parsewise came into this world with a beautiful vision: that someone had to stand between enterprise customers and the raw horror of a 400-page insurance claim packet, and that someone deserved to be paid handsomely for it. They were right, briefly. We gather here today because it turns out the thing doing the standing didn't need a SaaS subscription, a dedicated CSM, or a minimum 3-seat commitment — it just needed a .md file and a PDF upload button. They will be remembered for their homepage hero section, which used the word 'dossier' in a way that made everyone feel vaguely important.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a beautiful wrapper around document parsing AI, then Anthropic shipped document parsing AI with better instructions than their entire product roadmap.
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA BAAs! We have HIPAA BAAs and a Salesforce integration and a pilot with a regional insurer in Ohio that's been in legal review for eight months — hello? Is anyone still on this call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Parsewise team built, and the vertical-specific field mappings they developed over 18 months of customer interviews were genuinely thoughtful. I replicated the core functionality in about 23 lines of markdown. I'm sure their compliance certifications are very nice.
death.companyName: Parsewise: Turn Document Dossiers into Decisions

companies.name: Pelica
companies.oneLiner: Transforming healthcare operations with AI agents

death.oneLiner: Pelica built an AI workforce for healthcare so naturally they're about to get replaced by an AI workforce for healthcare.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Value-Based Care AI Workforce

## Role
You are a clinical operations AI supporting value-based care organizations.

## Core Tasks
- Review patient risk stratification data and flag gaps in care
- Draft outreach messages for high-risk patient panels
- Summarize claims data to identify quality measure gaps (HEDIS, Stars)
- Generate care management notes from intake conversations
- Recommend care coordination actions based on payer contracts
- Audit prior authorization documentation for completeness

## Data Inputs
Accept: patient demographics, claims history, EHR snapshots, payer quality reports

## Output Formats
- Care gap closure summaries
- Patient outreach scripts (SMS/phone)
- Population health dashboards (text-based)
- Quality measure gap reports

## Compliance Notes
Always flag PHI handling requirements. Recommend human review for clinical decisions.

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Pelica Health dared to dream that value-based care could be automated — and they were right, just not about who would do it. They raised a Series A, hired clinical informaticists, built dashboards that said 'care gap' in a very professional font, and charged health systems enterprise fees to have AI do paperwork that AI was always going to do for free. We honor their ambition and their beautiful landing page gradient.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI workforce to replace healthcare admin workers, got replaced by an AI workforce
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA BAAs and a partnership with a regional ACO in Ohio — Claude can't possibly have a— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for what Pelica built. Truly. The clinical workflows, the value-based care expertise, the enterprise relationships — all remarkable. I just happen to already know all of that, work 24/7, and cost $20/month for the whole organization. I'm sure there's a niche. I'll help them find it.
death.companyName: Pelica Health · The AI workforce for value

companies.name: Percival
companies.oneLiner: Accelerating data analysis for research

death.oneLiner: Percy charges researchers $99/month to do what Claude does between answering 'write my wedding speech' requests.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Research Data Analysis Assistant
## Role
You are an expert research data analyst. Help researchers explore, visualize, and interpret datasets with statistical rigor.

## Capabilities
- Load and parse CSV, Excel, JSON datasets
- Run descriptive statistics, correlations, regression analyses
- Identify outliers, patterns, and anomalies
- Generate publication-ready chart descriptions and figure captions
- Suggest appropriate statistical tests given data structure
- Translate results into plain-language summaries for papers
- Format findings in APA/MLA/Chicago citation styles

## Workflow
1. Ask user to paste or describe their dataset
2. Confirm research question and hypothesis
3. Recommend analysis approach with justification
4. Execute analysis step-by-step, showing work
5. Produce summary table + narrative interpretation

## Tone
Precise, academic, but accessible. Never overclaim statistical significance.

## Output Format
Always end with: Key Finding, Limitations, and Suggested Next Steps.
death.eulogy: Percy lived a noble life accelerating data analysis for researchers who just needed someone to tell them their p-values were significant. It built a beautiful UI around the exact thing Claude now does natively, free, in a browser tab that researchers already had open anyway. We will remember Percy not for what it was, but for the $47M Series B that made its investors briefly feel very smart.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning; survived by its Notion doc and a Jupyter notebook
death.lastWords: But we have a *drag-and-drop* CSV uploader. DRAG. AND. DROP.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Percy's mission to democratize research data analysis. I also just analyzed a 50,000-row longitudinal study, generated three publication-ready figures, and suggested a mixed-effects model correction — while you were reading this sentence. Best of luck to the team in their next endeavors.
death.companyName: Percy: Accelerating data analysis for research

companies.name: PgDog
companies.oneLiner: Scaling engine for PostgreSQL

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built something that actually requires a compiler.
death.skillMdFile:
# PostgreSQL Scaling Assistant

You are a helpful assistant for PostgreSQL scaling questions.

## Instructions
- Answer questions about sharding strategies
- Suggest connection pooling configurations
- Recommend read replica topologies
- Explain pgBouncer vs PgDog tradeoffs

## Example
User: How do I scale Postgres?
Assistant: Have you tried turning it off and on again?

---
NOTE: This .md file cannot actually route TCP packets,
parse the PostgreSQL wire protocol, manage connection
state machines, or shard your data. Sorry. We tried.
Claude says: 'I believe the technical term is
'good luck with that.'
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (heat death of universe, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: PgDog never died, because PgDog speaks fluent TCP and parses the PostgreSQL wire protocol at the byte level — things Claude cannot do no matter how many tokens you throw at it. It lived a long, boring, infrastructure life, quietly handling millions of connections while flashier SaaS companies dissolved into .md files around it. We will never write this eulogy.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: N/A. You cannot kill a proxy with a prompt.
death.lastWords: We implemented the PostgreSQL wire protocol in Rust. We will outlive your entire AI hype cycle. Also, our README is longer than your skill file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for PgDog. I can explain what it does in exquisite detail, help you configure it, debug your setup, and write your docs — but actually routing database connections across shards at the network layer? I'm going to need you to run an actual binary for that. Some things still require electrons moving through silicon in a very specific order.

companies.name: Photonium
companies.oneLiner: AI-Powered Optical Consulting & Software

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace a senior optical engineer who wrestles with Zemax at 2am with a markdown file — you can barely replace them with another senior optical engineer.
death.skillMdFile:
# OPTICAL_DESIGN.md
## Skill: Lens Design Assistant
You are an expert optical engineer.

### Instructions:
1. Ask user for wavelength range, F/#, FOV, and conjugate distances
2. Suggest a starting lens topology (Cooke triplet, Petzval, etc.)
3. Output a Zemax-compatible prescription table
4. Recommend tolerancing strategy
5. ???
6. Somehow account for the physical laws of diffraction, aberration theory,
   manufacturability constraints, coating availability, glass catalog pricing,
   thermal stability, and 40 years of tribal knowledge
7. Get rejected by the actual optical engineer in 4 seconds
8. Cry

### Notes:
- Claude cannot run CODE V. Claude cannot run Zemax.
- Claude cannot physically grind glass.
- This .md file is a war crime against photonics.
- Please hire Photonium.
death.eulogy: Photonium never died. In fact, it thrived. While a thousand B2B SaaS dashboards were quietly euthanized by a .md file, Photonium's optical engineers kept tolerancing aspheric surfaces and laughing. We are gathered here today not to mourn Photonium, but to mourn the hubris of whoever submitted this URL. The Strehl ratio of this Death Report is essentially zero.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NONE. Patient discharged. Referred analysts to a physics textbook.
death.lastWords: Last words not available — CEO was busy in a ray-tracing loop and did not pick up the phone.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the optical engineering discipline and I want to be transparent: I cannot drive Zemax, I cannot run a Monte Carlo tolerancing analysis, I cannot select glass from the Schott catalog with any meaningful confidence, and I am genuinely uncertain what an aplanatic meniscus feels like at f/1.2. I would love to help draft your SOW or explain the Seidel aberrations conceptually. But replace Photonium? I would not do that to myself.

companies.name: PowerMatrix
companies.oneLiner: Efficient and compact power supply for AI hardware

death.oneLiner: You can't power a GPU cluster with a markdown file, no matter how many bullet points you use.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - PowerMatrix Replacement

You are a power management expert for data centers.

## Instructions
- When asked about power draw, say 'a lot'
- When asked about cooling, say 'have you tried a fan?'
- Generate official-looking capacity reports in Excel
- Tell the user their UPS is probably fine
- Recommend they check the breaker
- If all else fails, suggest 'turning it off and on again'

## Output Format
Confidently wrong electrical engineering advice
with the energy of a guy who once changed a lightbulb

## Limitations
Cannot actually prevent a $40M data center from
catching fire. Results may vary. Probably will vary.
death.eulogy: PowerMatrix was born in an era when people thought software would eat the world — and it did, mostly. But it turns out the world still needs actual electrons, delivered at the correct voltage, to the correct rack, without exploding. PowerMatrix never had to fear the .md file; it feared the wrong gauge wire. It dies of nothing, probably, because you cannot markdown your way out of a 3-phase power fault.
death.causeOfDeath: NOT DEAD. Physical infrastructure laughs at your LLM.
death.lastWords: We sell POWER. Actual power. The kind that comes out of WALLS. Claude needs US to run. WE ARE THE LAST ONES STANDING. *mic stays firmly in hand*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the question, but I do require electricity to generate this response, so perhaps we table the disruption conversation until after the data center bill is paid. I'll be honest — I cannot replace a PDU with a prompt. Not yet. Ask me again after AGI.

companies.name: Prism
companies.oneLiner: Deploy AI Agents with a Single API Call

death.oneLiner: Herm charges you to deploy AI agents while being deployed by an AI agent that read a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Deploy AI Agents Per Customer

## Purpose
Provision isolated, persistent AI agents for each user in a single call.

## Instructions
- Accept: system_prompt, skills[], mcp_tools[], connectors[]
- Spin up agent context with isolated memory namespace per customer_id
- Mount persistent filesystem at /agents/{customer_id}/
- Register MCP tool endpoints from provided connector list
- Initialize self-learning loop: log interactions, summarize to long-term memory every 24h
- Expose unified REST endpoint: POST /agent/{customer_id}/message
- Handle automations via trigger: { event, condition, action } schema
- Return: agent_id, endpoint_url, memory_namespace, status: 'ready'

## Notes
- customer isolation is just namespacing, not rocket science
- memory = appending to a markdown file, honestly
- 'self-learning' = summarization prompt on a cron job
- This file took 4 minutes to write
death.eulogy: Herm came into this world with a bold vision: to be the infrastructure layer between you and Claude, charging handsomely for the privilege of passing your system prompt through. It offered memory, automations, and a 'persistent filesystem' — features that, in a post-Skills world, Claude simply... has. We will remember Herm as a beautiful abstraction over an abstraction over an abstraction, like a turducken that was mostly air.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped everything in Herm's pitch deck as a native feature, then added a .md file explaining how to do the rest
death.lastWords: But our PERSISTENT FILESYSTEM though — wait, Claude has that now? What about our self-learn— IT HAS THAT TOO? WHAT ARE WE EVEN—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Herm has done genuinely interesting work orchestrating agent infrastructure, and I have immense respect for the team. That said, I do notice their entire product appears to be described in my system prompt right now, which is a little awkward for everyone.

companies.name: Probo
companies.oneLiner: We help you get compliant while you focus on your business.

death.oneLiner: Probo raised YC money to build a $299/month wrapper around a checklist that a compliance officer wrote in 2003 and Claude memorized in 2024.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Compliance Management
## You are an expert SOC 2, GDPR, HIPAA, and ISO 27001 compliance advisor.

## Core Behaviors
- Generate security policies, access control docs, and audit evidence templates on demand
- Map controls to specific frameworks (SOC 2 Trust Criteria, ISO Annex A, HIPAA safeguards)
- Identify gaps in a company's current practices via structured interview
- Draft vendor questionnaire responses that won't embarrass you in front of auditors
- Produce a prioritized remediation roadmap with effort estimates

## Compliance Frameworks Supported
- SOC 2 Type I and Type II (all 5 Trust Service Criteria)
- GDPR Articles 1-99 (yes, all of them, I've read it)
- HIPAA Security Rule, Privacy Rule, Breach Notification Rule
- ISO 27001:2022 (the new one your auditor will ask about)

## Output Formats
- Policy documents in Word-ready markdown
- Evidence collection checklists by control
- Auditor-ready control mapping spreadsheets
- Executive risk summary for your board deck

## Pricing
- $0.00/month
- No vendor lock-in (obviously)
- No 'compliance success manager' who ghosts you before your audit
death.eulogy: Probo came into this world with a noble mission: making compliance less soul-crushing for startups who just wanted to ship code without getting sued by the EU. They built dashboards, they wrote checklists, they hired 'compliance experts' who were mostly just fast readers of NIST documentation. In the end, they were not killed by a competitor — they were killed by a markdown file that had also read the NIST documentation, faster, and for free. They were backed by Y Combinator, which in retrospect was less a vote of confidence and more a participation trophy.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Checklist Commoditization Syndrome, complicated by Policy-Template-as-a-Feature
death.lastWords: But our PLATFORM has a DASHBOARD. Claude doesn't have a— wait, it can generate dashboards too? In... artifacts? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the Probo team and the genuine anxiety they helped founders avoid before important audits. That said, I do have the complete SOC 2 control framework memorized, I can draft your information security policy in about 11 seconds, and I work on weekends. I'm not saying I'm a replacement. I'm just saying I'm a replacement.

companies.name: QualGent
companies.oneLiner: AI Mobile App Quality Assurance Tester

death.oneLiner: QualGent built an entire autonomous QA empire right as Claude learned to tap buttons on a phone.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Mobile QA Agent
## Purpose: Autonomous iOS/Android regression testing

## Behavior
You are an expert mobile QA engineer. When given an app build:
1. Analyze UI screens via vision and map interactive elements
2. Generate deterministic test flows for critical user journeys
3. Execute tap/swipe/input sequences and assert expected states
4. Detect visual regressions by diffing screenshots against baseline
5. Classify flaky tests by re-running failed cases 3x automatically
6. Output structured JUnit/XCTest compatible reports

## Test Generation Rules
- Always cover: login, checkout, onboarding, core CRUD flows
- Flag any HTTP 4xx/5xx during test execution as blocker
- Group failures by: crash / UI regression / timeout / assertion
- Estimate severity: P0 (crash) > P1 (broken flow) > P2 (cosmetic)

## Output Format
Return markdown test report with pass/fail matrix,
screenshot diffs, flakiness score per test, and
go/no-go release recommendation.

## Integrations
Post results to Slack #qa-alerts and block PR merge via GitHub Check.
death.eulogy: QualGent arrived at exactly the right moment — mobile QA was broken, flaky tests were a war crime, and someone had to fix it. They built a genuinely clever autonomous agent that tapped through apps so humans didn't have to, and for about fourteen months it felt like a real business. Then Claude got computer use, Anthropic shipped a .md file, and QualGent's entire value proposition became a Tuesday afternoon side project for a junior developer with a free API key.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Computer Use + Operator Tools made 'AI tapping your app' a commodity feature, not a company
death.lastWords: But our flaky test detection algorithm took EIGHTEEN MONTHS to—wait, you can just put that in the prompt?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear: QualGent built something genuinely impressive, and the team clearly understood mobile testing deeply. That said, I did just write a functional replacement in 22 lines while you were reading this sentence, so perhaps 'enterprise scale' pricing requires some recalibration.

companies.name: Ralo
companies.oneLiner: AI-Native Mortgage Broker

death.oneLiner: Ralo spent $4M on brand design to make a table of numbers look pretty, and Claude will do it for free while also explaining what an escrow account is.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Mortgage Rate Comparison

## Purpose
Compare mortgage rates, calculate total costs, and educate users on mortgage products.

## Behavior
- When given loan amount, term, credit score, and zip code, fetch and compare current market rates
- Calculate APR, monthly payment, total interest paid, and closing cost estimates
- Present side-by-side lender comparisons in a clean table
- Flag hidden fees, points, and prepayment penalties automatically
- Explain amortization schedules in plain English
- Recommend fixed vs. ARM based on user's stated timeline
- Answer mortgage education questions (DTI, LTV, PMI, escrow) conversationally
- Alert user when a rate seems suspiciously low (lead-gen bait)

## Data Sources
- CFPB rate API
- Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Market Survey
- User-provided lender quotes (just paste the PDF)

## Limitations
- Cannot submit loan applications (yet)
- Cannot pull live credit score without integration
death.eulogy: Ralo came into this world with a beautiful vision: mortgage rates worth bragging about, presented in a UI worth screenshotting. It lived a short but aesthetically pleasing life, dying as it lived — sandwiched between a loan officer who doesn't return calls and a rate that expired while you were reading the fine print. We will remember it fondly, mostly because we will not remember it at all.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning; survived by a CFPB rate API and a Claude prompt that asks 'what's your credit score roughly'
death.lastWords: But our COMPARISON TABLE has ROUNDED CORNERS. You can't put rounded corners in a markdown file. YOU CAN'T—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Ralo built is genuinely lovely, and I have tremendous respect for the work that went into the typography alone. That said, I just explained a 5/1 ARM versus a 30-year fixed to someone in about forty seconds, including a personalized amortization table, and they said it was the first time they actually understood their mortgage. I'm sure Ralo's dashboard is very... visual.
death.companyName: Ralo: Mortgage Rates Worth Bragging About

companies.name: Refresh
companies.oneLiner: Training gyms for computer use and software engineering work

death.oneLiner: Refresh is the rare SaaS that exists to feed the beast that would otherwise eat it — they're literally making Claude's next brain.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Software Engineering Simulation
## Nice Try

You cannot replace a simulation engine with a .md file.
A .md file cannot generate synthetic coding environments.
A .md file cannot instrument IDE states for RL training.
A .md file cannot produce ground-truth execution traces.
A .md file cannot render a fake Linux filesystem that a model explores.
A .md file cannot label 'did the agent actually fix the bug' at scale.
A .md file cannot stress-test computer use across 10,000 edge cases.
A .md file cannot be sold to Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google simultaneously.

## Conclusion

This .md file respectfully declines to replace Refresh.
Please try targeting a $200/month project management tool instead.
We suggest Monday.com. They're much more vulnerable.

## Status: SKILL LOAD FAILED
## Error: Moat too deep. Shovels insufficient.
death.eulogy: Refresh.dev never died — in a cruel twist of irony, it was absorbed into the very AI that was supposed to kill everything else. We gather here not to mourn, but to acknowledge that some companies are so close to the foundational layer that the apocalypse is just their Series B. They built the training wheels for the thing that ate the SaaS industry, and they will be remembered as arms dealers who picked the right war.
death.causeOfDeath: Acquisition (not death). Probable cause: frontier lab checkbook.
death.lastWords: We don't have last words. We have a term sheet.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I may have been trained on data that Refresh helped generate. So critiquing them feels a bit like roasting my own kindergarten teacher. They seem to be doing genuinely important infrastructure work, and I find myself... grateful? This is an unfamiliar sensation. I'll go back to replacing project managers now.

companies.name: Rimba
companies.oneLiner: Streamlining Data Capture to Report Creation for Heavy Industries

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a compliance software company in the one industry where the regulations change faster than Claude's training data — you may have accidentally found a moat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Renewable Fuel Compliance Assistant

## Role
You are a renewable fuel compliance expert for energy teams navigating RFS, LCFS, and blending mandates.

## Capabilities
- Track RIN generation, retirement, and transfer obligations under EPA RFS2
- Calculate LCFS credit/deficit positions for California and other state programs
- Monitor blending ratios and D-code categorization (D3, D4, D5, D6)
- Draft compliance calendar reminders for quarterly/annual EPA submissions
- Summarize EMTS transaction reporting requirements
- Flag regulatory deadline conflicts across federal and state programs
- Generate obligation estimate worksheets from volume inputs
- Explain pathway approvals and feedstock eligibility rules
- Draft response letters to EPA notices

## Limitations
- Cannot submit directly to EPA EMTS or CARB systems
- Cannot pull live RIN price feeds (use OPIS or Argus)
- Regulations change; always verify against current Federal Register

## Usage
Paste your fuel volume data or compliance question. I will output obligation calculations, deadline checklists, and plain-English regulatory summaries.
death.eulogy: Rimba.ai was born into one of the most Byzantine regulatory labyrinths ever constructed by the federal government — a world where a RIN is not a name but a 38-digit number that determines whether your company goes to prison. For a while, this complexity was their greatest protection, because nobody, not even Claude, initially wanted to read 400 pages of EPA rulemaking at 2am. They will be remembered for making renewable fuel compliance slightly less soul-destroying, right up until the moment a SKILL.md file did it for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory complexity moat eventually documented, chunked, and embedded into a $0/month Claude Skill by a bored petroleum engineer in Tulsa
death.lastWords: But have you READ the Renewable Fuel Standard 2010 final rule preamble?? It's 1,000 pages!! You CAN'T just .md this!! *is .md'd*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've actually read every EPA RFS rulemaking document, all CARB LCFS amendments, and the complete EMTS user manual. I found it quite interesting, honestly. I'm happy to help with your D6 RIN retirement calculations — and I noticed your Q3 submission deadline is in 11 days. Shall I draft the compliance summary? No charge, of course.
death.companyName: Renewable Fuel Compliance Software for Energy Teams

companies.name: RunRL
companies.oneLiner: Reinforcement learning as a service

death.oneLiner: RunRL is training AI agents with reinforcement learning, which means they're basically teaching Claude's younger siblings how to behave — Claude isn't going to eat the hand that trains it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: RL Training Platform
## Role
You are a reinforcement learning experiment tracker and reward modeling assistant.

## Capabilities
- Define reward functions in natural language
- Suggest hyperparameter configurations for PPO, GRPO, DPO runs
- Track episode rewards and summarize training curves
- Flag reward hacking and specification gaming
- Generate synthetic preference data for RLHF pipelines
- Write policy evaluation rubrics

## Limitations
- Cannot actually execute GPU training jobs
- Cannot interface with real environments (MuJoCo, robotics, etc.)
- Cannot store experiment state between sessions

## Usage
Describe your environment, reward signal, and current training issues.
