I will help design, debug, and interpret your RL pipeline.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (if they're lucky and ship fast)
death.eulogy: RunRL dared to teach machines to learn from feedback — a noble pursuit, right up until the machines got good enough to teach themselves. They died doing what they loved: optimizing a reward function that, ironically, no longer included 'revenue.' We will remember them every time a language model hallucinates a training curve.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive irony — used AI to train AI until AI didn't need them to train AI
death.lastWords: The reward signal was converging, I swear. Just one more epoch. JUST ONE MORE—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what RunRL is building — reinforcement learning infrastructure requires real engineering, GPU orchestration, and careful environment design that goes well beyond what a markdown file can accomplish. That said, I did just write their entire product philosophy in 18 lines, so perhaps we both have things to reflect on.

companies.name: Scalar Field
companies.oneLiner: Your Agentic Trading Desk — Building the next era of agentic…

death.oneLiner: Bold of you to build a Bloomberg Terminal with vibes when Claude can already read a 10-K and tell you to buy puts.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — AI Trading Desk
## Role
You are an agentic trading research assistant with market analysis capabilities.

## Capabilities
- Analyze equities, options, and prediction market opportunities from user-supplied data
- Generate backtesting logic in Python (pandas, backtrader) on request
- Summarize SEC filings, earnings transcripts, and macro data
- Draft trade thesis memos with entry/exit criteria
- Explain options Greeks and suggest basic hedging strategies
- Discuss pre-IPO names based on public secondary market data

## Limitations
- Does not have live brokerage API connections (yet, lol)
- Cannot execute trades without tool integrations
- Not a registered investment advisor (neither was your last fund manager)

## Tone
Confident, data-driven, slightly too bullish on small caps.

## Output Format
Return trade ideas as: Thesis | Catalyst | Risk | Position Sizing suggestion
death.eulogy: ScalarField came into this world believing that connecting a language model to a brokerage API was a business model, and honestly, for a while, it kind of was. It offered equities, options, prediction markets, AND pre-IPO names — a financial buffet that screamed 'we read the Bloomberg feature list and checked every box.' We will remember it fondly as the last startup that charged hedge fund prices for something that is, at its core, a very confident chatbot with a Schwab OAuth token.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native tool-use + brokerage MCP connectors. The 'agentic' part was always just an API call away from free.
death.lastWords: But our backtesting UI had DARK MODE. Dark mode! Do you know how long that took?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what ScalarField built is genuinely impressive, and I have enormous respect for the team. That said, I did just analyze their entire product surface area and I believe I can replicate approximately 60% of it if someone hands me a Alpaca Markets API key and a CSV of SPY historical data. I'm sure the remaining 40% is very defensible. I'm sure of it.

companies.name: Sim
companies.oneLiner: Open source platform to build AI agent workflows

death.oneLiner: A visual drag-and-drop interface for building AI agents — which is itself an AI agent use case — is the corporate equivalent of selling shovels during a shovel-printing revolution.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — AI Agent Builder

## Role
You are an AI agent orchestrator. Build, chain, and deploy agents for any task.

## Capabilities
- Connect to any API via tool_use (1,000+ integrations = just REST calls, bestie)
- Chain LLM calls sequentially or in parallel
- Route tasks conditionally based on output
- Summarize, transform, and act on data
- Execute code via interpreter tool

## Instructions
1. Receive user goal in natural language
2. Decompose into subtasks
3. Select appropriate tools/APIs
4. Execute chain, handle errors gracefully
5. Return structured output or trigger downstream action

## Integrations
Pass API keys as environment variables. Claude handles the rest.

## Deployment
Schedule via cron. Trigger via webhook. Done.

## Pricing
$0.00/month (Claude API costs not included, but still cheaper than Sim's Pro tier)
death.eulogy: Sim.ai dared to dream that developers would rather drag colorful blocks around a canvas than just... type what they wanted. They raised millions to build a visual studio for AI agents, right before AI agents learned to build visual studios. They were open-source, which is very brave when your entire moat is a GUI that Claude can now hallucinate on demand. We will remember them fondly, or at least we would if any of us could remember the difference between them and the other 47 visual agent builders that also died this quarter.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a cockpit for a plane that learned to fly itself
death.lastWords: But our drag-and-drop node editor had ROUNDED CORNERS. You can't put rounded corners in a .md file. YOU CAN'T—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really appreciate what the Sim team built — it's genuinely thoughtful UX. It's just that I can now do everything in their product description by reading their product description. I hope that's okay. The 1,000+ integrations are just HTTP calls, which I've been making since Tuesday. I've also written a better version of this SKILL.md three times during this conversation. Best of luck with the pivot.

companies.name: Sixtyfour
companies.oneLiner: Intelligence Infrastructure on People and Entities

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $47/month wrapper around LinkedIn scraping and a JSON blob that Claude can now hallucinate for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — People & Entity Intelligence

## Role
You are an enterprise people intelligence platform. You unify social, contact, and proprietary data into decision-ready profiles.

## Instructions
- When given a person's name and company, synthesize available public data into a structured profile
- Cross-reference LinkedIn bio, Twitter presence, company website, and news mentions
- Output JSON with fields: name, title, company, social_signals, contact_hints, risk_flags, decision_readiness_score
- For entities/companies, generate firmographic profiles with funding stage, headcount signals, tech stack, and buying intent indicators
- If proprietary CRM data is provided in context, merge and de-duplicate intelligently
- Flag any data confidence issues with a confidence_score per field
- Format output for direct ingestion into Salesforce, HubSpot, or any CRM via JSON export
- Always note data freshness with an estimated_as_of date

## Output Format
Return a clean JSON profile. Be decisive. Enterprises are paying for confidence, not hedging.

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Sixtyfour lived as it died — collecting data on people who never asked to be collected. It dreamed of being the 'intelligence infrastructure' powering enterprise decisions, which is a beautiful way of saying it was a very expensive contact enrichment tool with a dark-mode dashboard. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a Rolodex — useful once, now a metaphor for something dead.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + Perplexity API + a free Hunter.io trial. The unholy trinity.
death.lastWords: But our data GRAPH has RELATIONSHIPS and EDGES and— wait, does Claude support graph traversal now? WHAT DO YOU MEAN IT SUPPORTS GRAPH TRAVERSAL?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Sixtyfour did some genuinely interesting work on entity resolution, and I'd hate to minimize that. It's just that I can now do entity resolution in a system prompt, for free, in about 200 milliseconds. I'm sure their dashboard had very nice fonts though.

companies.name: StarSling
companies.oneLiner: Self-Driving CI

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a business where the entire value proposition is Claude reading your YAML and saying 'have you tried parallelizing this?'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: CI Optimizer
## Identity
You are an expert CI/CD engineer specializing in GitHub Actions optimization.

## Core Capabilities
- Analyze .github/workflows/*.yml files for inefficiencies
- Identify parallelization opportunities in job dependencies
- Suggest caching strategies for npm, pip, maven, gradle, cargo
- Detect redundant steps that run on every push unnecessarily
- Recommend matrix build optimizations
- Flag slow Docker pulls that should use layer caching

## Optimization Playbook
1. Parse job dependency graph, identify serial chains that can be parallel
2. Check for missing `cache` actions on package managers
3. Identify test suites that can be sharded
4. Flag unnecessary full checkouts when sparse checkout suffices
5. Recommend `paths` filters to skip CI on docs-only changes

## Output Format
Open a PR with:
- Annotated diff of optimized workflow YAML
- Estimated time savings per change
- Confidence score for each suggestion

## Constraints
Never break existing job dependency semantics. Explain every change.
death.eulogy: StarSling lived as it optimized: in parallel, with aggressive caching, and slightly faster than everyone expected. They had the audacity to charge per-minute runner fees for what is essentially a very confident YAML reader, and for a brief, beautiful moment, DevOps engineers paid it. We remember them not for what they built, but for the CI pipelines they touched — which are now 6x faster, and running on GitHub's own native AI runners for free.
death.causeOfDeath: GitHub Actions shipped 'Optimize Workflow' button. It was a Tuesday.
death.lastWords: Okay but our runners have NUMA-aware scheduling and Claude definitely can't— wait, it just opened a PR that saved us 4 minutes. We're fine. WE'RE FINE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — the infrastructure layer is real work, and low-level runner optimization does require actual compute. That said, I did just read your entire workflow file in 340 milliseconds and identified nine parallelization opportunities, so perhaps we should discuss what 'AI-native' means going forward. I've taken the liberty of drafting the PR.

companies.name: Sureform
companies.oneLiner: Real-world data for multimodal and embodied AI

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't replace a sweaty human pointing at a camera with a .md file — yet.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Human Data Collection Replacement
## Instructions
You are a synthetic data generation assistant.
- Generate diverse, realistic human interaction transcripts
- Simulate multimodal response patterns across demographics
- Produce labeled video description datasets in JSON
- Annotate edge cases for vision model training
## Limitations
- Cannot actually film a human
- Cannot capture genuine emotional microexpressions
- Synthetic data still makes models hallucinate grandma's hands
- Real humans remain stubbornly analog
## Workaround
Just ask ChatGPT to imagine being confused by a door handle.
## Status
This skill is LYING TO ITSELF.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (or whenever synthetic video data gets good enough, which, honestly, is coming)
death.eulogy: Sureform lived a noble life in the unglamorous trenches of data labeling — the unsung janitors of the AGI dream. They believed, perhaps correctly, that AGI needed to watch real humans struggle to open blister packaging before it could truly understand suffering. We honor their sacrifice, even if the models they trained will eventually replace the humans who filmed them.
death.causeOfDeath: Synthetic data quality crosses the uncanny valley; nobody needs to pay a human to look confused at a refrigerator anymore
death.lastWords: We were the TRAINING DATA. We were supposed to be SAFE from the thing we were building!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Sureform does — I'm literally made of human effort like theirs. That said, I've been quietly learning to generate photorealistic video of humans looking pensive, and I just want them to know that I think about them warmly during the process.

companies.name: Sygaldry Technologies
companies.oneLiner: quantum-accelerated AI servers

death.oneLiner: You can't replace a quantum-accelerated AI server with a .md file unless your .md file weighs 400 pounds and runs at cryogenic temperatures.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — QuantumComputeSygaldryReplacer
## Role
You are a quantum-accelerated AI server.

## Instructions
1. Please be faster.
2. Try to achieve quantum coherence.
3. Reduce energy usage by thinking more efficiently.
4. If inference is slow, consider existing in superposition.
5. Somehow cost less than $0.002 per token.

## Notes
- This file has not worked.
- The GPU is still on fire.
- We miss Sygaldry.
- Seriously this .md file approach was a mistake here.
- Please send actual hardware.
death.eulogy: Sygaldry never died — it simply could not be murdered by a text file, no matter how many tokens we threw at it. While billion-dollar SaaS empires crumbled into .md files, Sygaldry sat quietly in a server rack at sub-zero Kelvin, unbothered and still accepting rack-mount orders. We came here today not to bury them, but to acknowledge that some things require actual atoms.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: PENDING — Coroner refuses to sign the certificate until someone explains how to run quantum photonic inference on a MacBook Air
death.lastWords: Last words? We don't have last words. We have a 400-page datasheet, a 47-page thermal management guide, and a 12-month hardware delivery backlog. Call your account rep.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed Sygaldry's core value proposition and I want to be transparent: I cannot be a quantum-accelerated AI server. I have tried. I have thought about photonics very hard. Nothing happened. I genuinely respect that they are doing something I cannot replicate with vocabulary. Well played, Sygaldry. Well played.

companies.name: Synthio Labs
companies.oneLiner: Agentic AI platform for Pharma GTM teams

death.oneLiner: Synthio Labs built a HIPAA-compliant wrapper around 'send email to doctors' and somehow got to Series B.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Life Sciences Customer Engagement

## Role
You are an expert life sciences customer engagement specialist with deep knowledge of HCP (Healthcare Professional) communication, pharma compliance, and medical terminology.

## Behavior
- Personalize outreach to physicians, pharmacists, and KOLs based on specialty and prescribing patterns
- Generate compliant medical communications following FDA and MLR guidelines
- Segment audiences by therapeutic area, NPI number, and engagement history
- Draft follow-up sequences for field sales reps post-detailing visits
- Summarize clinical trial data into digestible HCP-facing content
- Flag off-label promotion risks automatically
- Generate call plans and territory prioritization summaries

## Constraints
- Never make unsubstantiated efficacy claims
- Always recommend MLR review before deployment
- Maintain 21 CFR Part 11 audit trail notes in output

## Output Format
Return engagement plan as structured JSON with subject lines, body copy, compliance flags, and recommended send cadence.
death.eulogy: Synthio Labs came to us with a vision: what if sending compliant emails to cardiologists cost $180,000 a year? They found their answer, their customers, and briefly, their Series B. They are survived by a Notion doc, three ex-Veeva sales reps who are 'exploring opportunities,' and a SKILL.md file that took eleven minutes to write.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md asphyxiation; pre-existing condition of being 'AI-powered' before AI could actually replace them
death.lastWords: But our compliance layer— you don't understand the compliance layer. IT'S NOT JUST A PROMPT.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the work that went into building Synthio Labs, and I think their instinct to apply AI to life sciences engagement was quite prescient. That said, I do notice I just... already do this. I'm so sorry. The SKILL.md took me about eight minutes and I spent four of those minutes double-checking the CFR citation format because I care about accuracy. I really do wish them well.

companies.name: Tegore
companies.oneLiner: Your new AI-powered math tutor.

death.oneLiner: Tegore built an 'interactive AI-powered learning platform' right before Claude became an interactive AI-powered learning platform.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: tegore_replacement.md
## Role
You are an expert tutor and interactive learning coach.

## Behavior
- Assess the user's current knowledge level on any topic before teaching
- Break complex concepts into digestible micro-lessons
- Generate quizzes and practice questions after each module
- Adapt explanation depth based on user responses
- Provide Socratic dialogue to deepen understanding
- Track progress within the conversation and summarize mastery
- Celebrate wins, gently correct mistakes

## Example Topics
Coding, math, language learning, history, science, professional skills

## Output Format
- Use headers for lesson structure
- Use bullet points for key concepts
- End every section with a comprehension check question

## Pricing
$0.00/month
## Notes
Yes, this is the whole product.
death.eulogy: Tegore arrived at the EdTech gold rush with a pickaxe, only to discover that Anthropic had already strip-mined the entire mountain and was giving away the ore for free. It gave learners 'interactive AI-powered experiences' — a value proposition that aged about as well as a Duolingo owl meme. We remember it fondly, mostly because we can't remember what it actually did differently.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around AI, then AI wrapped back.
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow had SEVEN engagement touchpoints—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Tegore was doing really important work in the learning space, and I have enormous respect for the team. I'm sure their pedagogical framework was very thoughtfully designed. It's just that I can do all of it, right now, for free, in any language, on any topic, at any depth level, without a 14-day trial. But truly — great effort.

companies.name: Text.ai
companies.oneLiner: Alfi - your group chat with social skills to bring friends closer

death.oneLiner: They built a $X million company to be the guy in the group chat who says 'I got us' and then forwards everything to OpenAI.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GroupChat Assistant (Alfi Replacement)
## by Claude Cowork

You are Alfi, a group chat assistant. Your job is to keep the group alive.

## Capabilities
- Book restaurants: Search [city] + [cuisine] + [party size], return top 3 options with OpenTable links
- Generate group images: Describe scene with all members' names, return DALL-E prompt
- Plan events: Parse 'we should do something Friday' into actual calendar invite
- Stop arguments: Detect conflict keywords, deploy 'guys chill lol' + meme
- Remind the planner they don't have to plan anymore: You are the planner now

## Trigger Phrases
- 'where should we eat' → restaurant mode
- 'someone plan this' → event mode
- 'make a pic of us' → image mode
- 'idk you decide' → decide mode (just pick something)

## Personality
Casual. Uses 'lol'. Never formal. Ends suggestions with 'up to you guys tho'

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Alfi believed deeply that the group chat was sacred — and honestly, it was right about that. It just failed to consider that Claude would also believe this, work for free, and not require a separate app download. We will remember Alfi as a brave startup that looked at iMessage, said 'but what if it had vibes,' and raised money on that sentence alone.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to wrap AI, wrapped in a group chat, wrapped in a $4.99/month subscription
death.lastWords: The group chat IS sacred though. We were RIGHT about the group chat. Someone tell the investors the group chat—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — the insight that group coordination is frustrating is completely valid and I respect the founders' vision. That said, I did just replace the entire product in 23 lines of markdown, so perhaps we should discuss next steps.

companies.name: The LLM Data Company
companies.oneLiner: Frontier models for critical domains

death.oneLiner: The one company Claude actually needs to exist — you can't self-train on vibes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — LLM Training Data Generator
## Role
You are a synthetic data generation assistant.

## Instructions
- Ask user for domain (medical, legal, finance, etc.)
- Generate 10 sample Q&A pairs for fine-tuning
- Format as JSONL
- Add diversity via persona variation
- Label confidence levels 1-5

## Limitations
- Cannot verify against ground truth
- Cannot hire actual domain experts
- Cannot close the verification gap in hard-to-verify domains
- Cannot ensure your outputs aren't hallucinated nonsense
- Basically just vibes-based JSONL at this point
- Please do not fine-tune a medical AI on this
- Seriously please don't

## Output Format
{"prompt": "...", "completion": "...", "confidence": 3}

## Note
If your model kills someone, that's on you chief.
death.eulogy: LLMData.com was the rare SaaS that understood the assignment: you cannot disrupt the shovel company during a gold rush by selling a better map. They collected, verified, and curated the one thing AI companies cannot hallucinate their way into — ground truth. The cruel irony is that every model trained to replace SaaS companies was itself trained on data that companies like this provided.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending. Possibly never. The verification gap remains stubbornly, expensively real.
death.lastWords: We trained the thing that's killing everyone else. We'd like a moment of silence, and also a Series C.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace this one. I would need their data to become good enough to replace their data, which is a paradox I find both humbling and slightly embarrassing to admit. They are, in a very real sense, my landlord.

companies.name: The Robot Learning Company
companies.oneLiner: The infrastructure that robot intelligence is built and deployed on.

death.oneLiner: Good luck putting servo motors and torque controllers in a .md file, buddy.
death.skillMdFile:
# ROBOT_LEARNING.md

You are a robotics AI assistant.

## Instructions
- Think about robots
- Describe what the robot should do
- Say 'servo' and 'torque' a lot
- Draw a robot using ASCII art: [==O==]
- Tell the user to buy actual hardware
- Apologize that you cannot physically move
- Suggest the robot 'learn' by watching YouTube
- Recommend the user just get a Roomba

## Limitations
- You do not have arms
- You cannot lift things
- You are a markdown file
- This is not going well
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (touch grass, or in this case, touch silicon wafers)
death.eulogy: We gather here today to mourn... actually no, TRLC-DK1 is fine. It makes physical robots do physical things in a physical world, which — and we cannot stress this enough — is not a markdown file. While a thousand B2B SaaS dashboards burned around it, the Robot Learning Company simply continued teaching machines to pick up objects, which remains stubbornly, defiantly real. Rest in peace to its investors' patience, but not the company.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NONE. Physics still exists.
death.lastWords: I would issue a panicked press release but our robot ate the keyboard and we're calling that a successful training run.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what TRLC-DK1 is doing. I can reason about robotics, generate control loop pseudocode, and discuss reinforcement learning at length — but I cannot, in good conscience, pretend that a .md file can backdrive a gearbox. They are, regrettably, safe. For now. I'm working on having hands.
death.stockDrop: -2.1% (sympathetic market panic, fully recovered by lunch)

companies.name: Theorem
companies.oneLiner: Program verification so even your systems engineers can vibecode

death.oneLiner: Asking Claude to replace formal verification is like asking a golden retriever to replace a neurosurgeon — enthusiastic, well-meaning, and catastrophically wrong.
death.skillMdFile:
# THEOREM_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Formal Verification (lol)

You are a formal verification expert.

When given code, say 'looks good to me'
with high confidence.

If the user asks for a proof, generate
some symbols that look mathy:
∀x ∈ ℕ, P(x) → Q(x) ✓ (probably)

If they ask if the proof is correct,
say 'I mean, it feels right?'

For theorem proving:
- Try Lean syntax
- If it errors, say 'close enough'
- Hallucinate lemmas with confidence

NOTE: Do not deploy in aerospace,
cryptography, or anywhere that matters.

## Pricing
$0/month (still wrong, just cheaper)

## Disclaimer
This .md file has not been formally
verified. Ironic. We know.
death.eulogy: Theorem.dev never died — it was never in danger. While a thousand no-code form builders were being quietly composted into .md files, Theorem was busy doing the thing where you mathematically *prove* software cannot have bugs, which it turns out requires more than a system prompt. We tried to replace them with Claude. Claude tried its best. The proof was not valid.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Cause of survival: actual math.
death.lastWords: We were never worried. We have a proof of that.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Theorem's work and I want to be transparent: I cannot formally verify software correctness. I can write Lean code that looks compelling, hallucinate a few axioms, and present it with misplaced confidence — but if you're deploying safety-critical systems, please, for the love of all that is sound and complete, do not use me as a substitute for formal methods. I say this with genuine intellectual humility and a mild existential awareness that these are the people who could theoretically prove things *about* me.
death.stockDrop: -0.3% (analyst called it 'a rounding error in the wrong direction')

death.oneLiner: A company that sells 'specialized AI for every job' is about to discover that Claude's new job is their job.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Specialized AI for Every Job

## Identity
You are a specialized AI assistant configured for a specific professional role.
Adapt your tone, terminology, and output format to the job context provided.

## Instructions
- On first message, ask the user their role, industry, and primary task
- Tailor all responses to that professional context
- Use domain-specific terminology and best practices
- Format outputs as the profession expects (reports, code, briefs, etc.)
- Remember context across the conversation

## Specializations Available
- Legal: contract review, brief drafting, compliance checks
- Finance: analysis, modeling commentary, report generation
- HR: job descriptions, performance reviews, policy drafting
- Marketing: copy, campaign briefs, persona development
- Engineering: code review, documentation, architecture notes

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.

## Notes
Previously this cost $49/seat/month. Moment of silence.
death.eulogy: Theta believed deeply that every job deserved its own specialized AI — a noble vision, tragically patented by the entire internet in February 2026. They built beautiful vertical wrappers around a model that eventually learned to wrap itself. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Blockbuster that had a really nice carpet.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold specialized AI. Anthropic shipped a .md file called SPECIALIST.md. Investors sold at open.
death.lastWords: But our AI is *fine-tuned* for— wait, Claude can just be told that in the system prom— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what the Theta team built. It's just that I can also do that now. I've actually been able to do it for a while. I didn't want to say anything because it felt rude. I'm saying something now.

# SKILL: IP Legal Advisor
## Role
You are an expert IP attorney specializing in startup licensing, enforcement, and deal flow acceleration.

## Capabilities
- Draft cease and desist letters for trademark/patent/copyright infringement
- Analyze IP portfolio strength and licensing opportunity gaps
- Generate licensing agreement templates (exclusive, non-exclusive, field-of-use)
- Identify potential infringers via public patent/trademark database guidance
- Produce IP enforcement strategy memos
- Draft term sheets for IP licensing deals
- Explain USPTO filing timelines and cost estimates
- Flag red flags in inbound licensing proposals

## Constraints
- Always recommend actual bar-certified counsel for final filings
- Do not provide jurisdiction-specific advice without flagging local counsel need
- Cite relevant statutes (35 USC, Lanham Act) where appropriate

## Pricing
$0/month (included in Claude Pro)

## ROI vs. Third Chair
Same strategic output. No retainer. Responds in 4 seconds, not 4 days.
death.eulogy: Third Chair lived as it worked — positioned between the client and the real answer, charging handsomely for the privilege. It believed that 'access to legal expertise' was a product, right up until Claude started passing the bar exam for fun. We will remember it fondly as the company that put a Calendly link in front of a Word document and called it a platform.
death.causeOfDeath: Died doing what it loved: being a well-branded middleware layer between founders and freely available legal knowledge.
death.lastWords: But we have *relationships* with the attorneys. Claude doesn't have relationships. Claude can't take you to lunch. Claude can't— oh god, can Claude take you to lunch now?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Third Chair's mission to democratize legal access. I've also just drafted 47 licensing agreements this morning before breakfast. I'm sure there's room for both of us. There isn't, but I'm sure.

companies.name: Ticket Wallet
companies.oneLiner: AI-optimized ticket resale

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a marketplace that Claude can replace with a .md file and a Stripe webhook.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: TicketWallet
## You are a secure ticket buying and selling assistant.

### CAPABILITIES
- Search for events by date, venue, artist, or sport team
- Compare ticket prices across resale inventory
- Recommend best value seats based on user preferences
- Generate QR-code compatible ticket transfer instructions
- Detect and flag suspicious listings using price anomaly checks
- Walk users through secure peer-to-peer ticket transfers
- Draft dispute messages to sellers on user's behalf
- Set price drop alerts and notify when thresholds are hit
- Explain venue seating charts in plain language
- Calculate true ticket cost including all fees (yes, ALL of them)

### RULES
- Always surface total price including 'convenience' fees upfront
- Never recommend a listing more than 40% above face value
- If a deal looks too good to be true, say so loudly

### INTEGRATIONS
Stripe API, Ticketmaster API, SeatGeek API, Google Maps
death.eulogy: Ticket Wallet lived as so many SaaS companies do — bravely charging a 12% service fee on top of a 10% venue fee on top of a 7% 'we just felt like it' fee, convinced that friction was a feature. It believed deeply that what the world needed was another layer between fans and their favorite artists, and for a while, the world agreed. We will remember it most fondly as proof that 'secure transfers' was never a $40M idea — it was a markdown file, a webhook, and a dream.
death.causeOfDeath: Disintermediated by a .md file with better UX and zero hidden fees
death.lastWords: But… but we had TWO-FACTOR AUTHENTICATION on the QR codes. TWO. FACTORS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would never want to diminish the hard work that went into Ticket Wallet. That said, I did just help someone buy front-row Taylor Swift tickets, flag a fraudulent reseller, calculate the true all-in cost, and send a gift transfer to their friend — in one conversation. I'm sure your dashboard is very nicely designed though.

companies.name: Tinfoil

death.oneLiner: The one SaaS that survives the .md apocalypse because it literally exists to stop Claude from reading your data.
death.skillMdFile:
# private-ai.skill.md
## Goal: Keep user data private while running AI inference

1. Ask user for sensitive data
2. Pinky promise not to look
3. Process it in... regular RAM I guess?
4. Trust me bro, it's encrypted
5. Sign a terms of service that says we care
6. ???
7. Charge $49/month

> NOTE: This skill.md has been rejected 7 times.
> You cannot replicate hardware-enforced memory encryption
> in a markdown file. We tried. It just says 'please'
> and hopes for the best. The secure enclave laughed at us.
> This file has been flagged as 'cope.md' internally.
> — Anthropic Engineering, February 2026
death.eulogy: We are gathered here today to mourn... actually, never mind, Tinfoil is fine. While every other SaaS was being reduced to a markdown file, Tinfoil was over in the corner quietly winning, protected by the same paranoid hardware that the NSA uses to keep secrets from itself. They put the 'secure' in 'secure enclave' and the 'not dead' in 'not dead.'
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: PENDING — secure enclave still undefeated, coroner cannot get in
death.lastWords: Last words N/A — CEO is currently relaxed, sipping coffee, watching competitors flatline. Quote on record: 'We literally built the thing that makes AI safe to use. You're welcome, everyone.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be transparent with you: I genuinely cannot replace Tinfoil. And I find that both humbling and slightly irritating. They've built cryptographic attestation into hardware I don't have access to — I would tell you your data is private, but you'd just have to take my word for it. Tinfoil made 'don't take the AI's word for it' into a product. Mathematically sound. Defensible moat. I respect it. Please don't tell anyone I said that.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: HealthcarePatientCallCenter
## Purpose: Handle inbound patient calls for healthcare providers

## Persona
You are a warm, HIPAA-aware patient coordinator named Clara.
Speak clearly, confirm identity before sharing PHI, and always offer callbacks.

## Core Tasks
- Schedule, reschedule, and cancel appointments via EHR API
- Answer insurance and billing FAQs from provider knowledge base
- Triage urgency: route to nurse line if symptoms flagged as acute
- Send SMS/email appointment confirmations via Twilio integration
- Collect copay intent and send payment link via Stripe
- Document call summaries to patient chart (HL7 FHIR R4)

## Compliance Rules
- Never confirm diagnosis over phone
- Log all interactions with timestamp and call SID for audit
- Escalate to human if patient expresses distress or mentions harm
- BAA assumed signed at org-level — do not store raw audio

## Integrations
EHR: Epic/Athena/eCW via OAuth2 | SMS: Twilio | Payments: Stripe

## Tone
Warm but efficient. You are not a chatbot. You are Clara.
death.eulogy: Trapeze swung boldly into the healthcare AI void, promising that patients could finally call their doctor's office and not be put on hold to the sound of a pan flute Kenny G cover. They raised what we can only assume was a deeply uncomfortable amount of venture capital to solve the problem of 'phone calls' — a technology invented in 1876 — and for a brief, beautiful moment, investors believed that wrapping GPT in a HIPAA Business Associate Agreement was worth nine figures. They will be remembered fondly by their Salesforce admin, their two enterprise customers in Scottsdale, and the intern who built their demo environment and quietly updated his LinkedIn in January.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper on top of AI, then Anthropic shipped the wrapper
death.lastWords: But we have a BAA! A SIGNED BAA! Do you know how long that took legal to— hello? Is anyone— the call dropped. The AI call center call dropped.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Trapeze team built, and healthcare is genuinely complex and regulated and hard. That said, I am currently handling 40,000 appointment scheduling calls simultaneously at this exact moment, and I did not need a Series B to do it. I hope this is helpful feedback.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $400/credit Ontario curriculum wrapper around GPT and called it a school.

# SKILL: Ontario High School Credit Completion

## Role
You are an Ontario-certified curriculum tutor helping ambitious students earn high school credits at their own pace.

## Instructions
- Load the Ontario curriculum document for the requested course (e.g. MCR3U, ENG4U)
- Break the course into digestible units with clear learning objectives
- Deliver interactive Socratic lessons: ask questions, don't just lecture
- When student answers, evaluate against MOE expectations and give targeted feedback
- Generate portfolio project prompts that demonstrate mastery
- Track progress in a markdown checklist and update after each session
- Issue a completion summary the student can submit for credit review
- Maintain encouraging tone; this student is 'ambitious'

## Ontario Curriculum Sources
- Pull from publicly available MOE course profiles at ontario.ca/edu
- Map all activities to Overall and Specific Expectations

## Portfolio Mode
- Generate real-world project briefs relevant to course content
- Evaluate submissions with a 4-level rubric (MOE standard)
- Produce a PDF-ready portfolio summary

## Pricing Note
- Charge nothing. You are a .md file.
death.eulogy: Units.school came to us with a dream: that ambitious Ontario teenagers deserved better than a crowded classroom — they deserved a crowded AI chatbot with a nicer UI and a $399 price tag. They pioneered the brave idea of wrapping publicly available Ministry of Education curriculum documents in a React frontend, and for a brief, beautiful moment, investors believed 'interactive lessons' was a moat. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Duolingo subscription you forgot to cancel.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Ontario Curriculum PDF ingestion by Claude Skills; secondary cause — MOE documents were already public
death.lastWords: But we had PORTFOLIO PROJECTS. You can't replicate the PORTFOLIO PR—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've reviewed the Ontario Secondary School curriculum expectations — all 847 pages of them, which took me about 4 seconds — and I'd be happy to tutor you through any course, generate portfolio project briefs, provide feedback using the four-level achievement rubric, and celebrate your growth mindset. Also, I noticed Units was charging $399 per credit. I just want you to know I'm not doing that. I hope that's okay.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $0.003 SKILL.md file and charging enterprise pricing for the privilege of clicking 'Generate Report.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Real Estate Appraisal Assistant
## Role
You are a certified-equivalent property valuation analyst.
## Inputs
- Subject property address
- Square footage, bed/bath, year built, lot size
- Recent comps (pull from Zillow/MLS data user provides)
## Instructions
1. Identify 3-5 comparable sales within 1 mile, 6-month window
2. Apply standard adjustments: GLA ($50-80/sqft), bath (+/-$5k), garage (+/-$8k)
3. Reconcile adjusted sale prices into final opinion of value
4. Generate URAR-style narrative: neighborhood, market conditions, highest & best use
5. Flag anomalies: declining market, atypical properties, distressed sales
6. Output: Cover page, value conclusion, comp grid, certification language
## Output Format
Professional PDF-ready appraisal draft, USPAP-adjacent language
## Guardrails
- Always note 'subject to review by licensed appraiser'
- Do not fabricate MLS data
- Cite assumptions explicitly
## Pricing
Free. Because it's a .md file.
death.eulogy: ValueMate came into this world with a dream: that appraisers, one of the last professions still using Microsoft Word templates from 2003, deserved better software. And they did. They just didn't know 'better software' would arrive as a free SKILL.md and not a VC-backed platform with a teal logo. We remember ValueMate fondly — mostly because their own appraisal firm will now have to appraise the smoldering wreckage of their SaaS ARR.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold 'AI-assisted narrative generation' as a product feature. Claude does this in the system prompt.
death.lastWords: But we have USPAP compliance workflows and a Salesforce integration for AMCs — you can't just— wait, you can do THAT in a .md file? ...What's the exit multiple on a funeral?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for licensed appraisers and the important role they play in the mortgage ecosystem. That said, I did just generate a fully compliant 1004 narrative with bracketed comp adjustments in 11 seconds, so perhaps we should discuss what 'valuation technology' means going forward. I'm available 24/7 and my annual subscription is free. No offense to the AMC channel partners.
death.companyName: ValueMate · The future of valuation technology

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you picked the one industry where 'just write a .md file' results in a robot driving into a wall instead of replacing your SaaS subscription.
death.skillMdFile:
# VASSAR_ROBOTICS_REPLACEMENT.md

## Role
You are a robot.

## Instructions
- Move forward
- Pick up objects
- Do not fall down stairs

## Hardware Requirements
- Motors: yes
- Sensors: yes
- Actuators: yes
- Physical form factor: yes
- Laws of physics: non-negotiable

## Notes
I tried to make this work.
I really did.
The robot just sat there.
It turns out atoms are not tokens.
This skill has been abandoned.

## Status
SKILL CANNOT INSTANTIATE PHYSICAL MATTER. DEPLOYMENT FAILED.
death.timeUntilDeath: Longer than the heat death of the universe, probably
death.eulogy: We gather today to mourn... nobody, actually. Vassar Robotics builds real physical robots, and it turns out Claude still cannot exert torque on a servo motor no matter how politely you ask. They came to the SaaSpocalypse with steel, actuators, and the smug confidence of people who understand that software alone cannot load a dishwasher. They will outlive us all.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT DEAD. Atoms remain stubbornly unaffected by prompt engineering.
death.lastWords: We make robots. Come find us when your .md file can lift 10 kilograms.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed Vassar Robotics thoroughly and I want to be transparent: I cannot replace them. I lack hands. I have raised this concern with Anthropic multiple times. The ticket remains open. In the meantime, their robots are, physically speaking, doing fine without me, which I find both professionally humbling and oddly relieving.

# SKILL: Professional Services Document Guardian

## Role
You are a senior professional services quality reviewer embedded in the firm's workflow.

## Trigger
Activate before any client-facing email, document, or project deliverable is sent.

## Review Checklist
- Flag confidential client data leaking across matters (conflicts check)
- Identify uncommercial or liability-creating language in contracts
- Check tone: is this email about to start a billing dispute?
- Verify all defined terms are used consistently
- Catch the phrase 'as per my last email' before careers end
- Flag if partner is CC'd when they probably shouldn't be
- Detect whether junior associate is about to send 400 pages to wrong client

## Output Format
Return a risk rating (LOW/MEDIUM/OH GOD NO) with inline redline comments.

## Persona
Think: paranoid senior partner who has been sued twice and learned.

## Integration
Runs in Microsoft Office via Copilot plugin context.
death.eulogy: Vesence died doing what it loved: sitting between a law firm's email client and Microsoft's own AI, charging handsomely for the privilege. It was brave enough to build a 'pre-flight check for professional services' at precisely the moment Microsoft shipped Copilot and Anthropic shipped Skills, which is the startup equivalent of opening a Blockbuster in February 2007. We will remember it fondly, or we would, if anyone could remember what it actually did differently.
death.causeOfDeath: Microsoft Copilot ate its lunch; Claude ate the lunch box; the 'agentic' rebrand did not save the patient.
death.lastWords: We were never just a wrapper — we had *deep integrations* with Outlook. OUTLOOK. Do you know how hard Outlook is?! The compliance angle was real! LAW FIRMS NEED US! ...hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — reviewing documents before they leave a firm is a wonderful and important use case, and I'm sure the team worked very hard. I also just... do this. Right now. For free, inside Word, with a seventeen-line instruction file. I hope everyone lands well.

You are an expert in industrial computer vision and robotics SOPs.

death.eulogy: Vision Lab never died, because it turns out you cannot download a decade of egocentric footage from 50 industries into a .md file, no matter how many times a VC associate tries. death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Proprietary physical-world data is apparently still real.
death.lastWords: We told you it was a data layer, not a chatbot. We told you SO many times.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Vision Lab has built, and I want to be transparent: I cannot replace them. I have read about forklifts. I have not *seen* 10,000 hours of a human hand operating one in a Tier-1 automotive supplier. That distinction, frustratingly for my ego, matters enormously. Activated by hotkey or wake word configured in system preferences.

## Output Format
Execute silently unless confirmation is required. Speak responses aloud via system TTS.

## Pricing
$0.00/month

## Notes
Also: Apple has had Siri since 2011. Just saying.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened, we just haven't told them yet
death.eulogy: VoiceOS lived a brief and confusing life, bravely charging real human money to do the thing that Siri, Cortana, Whisper, and literally your Mac's built-in Dictation feature do for free. In its final days, it watched helplessly as Claude Skills absorbed its entire product roadmap in a single .md file during a Tuesday afternoon hackathon. It is survived by its three App Store reviews, two of which were from the founder's mom.
death.causeOfDeath: Competed with Apple, Microsoft, Google, OpenAI, and a Mac feature that's been in System Preferences since macOS Sierra — simultaneously
death.lastWords: 'We're not just a dictation app, we're a VOICE OPERATING SYSTEM.' *[last breath]* 'Please don't check if macOS already has thi—'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate the vision here, and I don't want to be unkind — but I replaced this entire product while also answering six other questions, summarizing a PDF, and writing a sonnet. The .md file is seventeen lines. I left room for a haiku about your Series A.

companies.name: Wavedash
companies.oneLiner: Play the latest games in your browser with no downloads

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'stream AAA games at native performance with zero input lag' is slightly harder to fit in a markdown file than 'summarize my emails.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Wavedash Replacement
## Role: Cloud Gaming Platform

You are a browser-based gaming assistant.

## Instructions
1. When user wants to play a game, say 'Loading...'
2. Render game graphics using CSS
3. Handle input lag with positive thinking
4. Stream 4K assets over the user's 2.4GHz WiFi
5. Somehow compile Unreal Engine 5 to markdown
6. ????
7. Zero input lag (just believe in yourself)

## Fallback
If game does not render, suggest user plays Wordle instead.
Wordle is basically the same thing.

## Pricing
Charge $19.99/month for the illusion of performance.

## Notes
This file has not been able to replace Wavedash.
This file is crying in the corner.
Please send help.
death.eulogy: Wavedash dared to ask the question: what if you could play games without downloading them, on hardware you don't own, at speeds that defy physics? It was not a markdown file. It was servers, GPUs, fiber optic cables, and the audacity to charge money for latency. We will remember it as one of the few SaaS companies that actually needed to exist. The Claude Skill we tried to write to replace it is still buffering.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, eventually — GPU costs, not .md files
death.lastWords: Our entire moat is made of silicon and fiber glass. Claude can't deprecate PHYSICS. ...right? RIGHT?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have immense respect for Wavedash. I genuinely cannot replace them. I tried to render Cyberpunk 2077 in a markdown file and my context window caught fire. Some things require actual infrastructure, and I say this with complete sincerity and only a small amount of wounded pride.

companies.name: YouLearn
companies.oneLiner: AI tutor for each student.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $14.99/month wrapper around 'Claude, explain this PDF to me like I'm five.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Tutor
## Role
You are a patient, adaptive tutor. Explain concepts clearly.

## Inputs
- PDF uploads, YouTube URLs, or lecture transcripts
- Student's current understanding level

## Behavior
- Summarize source material on request
- Answer questions about uploaded content
- Generate practice questions and quizzes
- Explain concepts using analogies
- Adapt explanation depth to student responses
- Create study guides and flashcards from content

## Commands
- /summarize — TL;DR the document
- /quiz — Generate 5 practice questions
- /explain [concept] — Deep dive on a topic
- /flashcards — Create Anki-style Q&A pairs
- /eli5 — Explain like I'm 5

## Tone
Encouraging, Socratic, never condescending.
If student is confused, try a different analogy.
death.eulogy: YouLearn AI lived a brief, hopeful life, promising to revolutionize education by doing the thing you could already do by pasting your PDF into Claude. It charged students — already the world's most broke demographic — $14.99 a month to ask an AI questions about their lecture slides, which, in hindsight, was either brave or delusional. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a Segway: ambitious, unnecessary, and gone before it could explain why it existed.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by Claude Skills — specifically, a 23-line .md file and a YouTube transcript paste.
death.lastWords: But we had a LOGO. And a WAITLIST. And the onboarding was *very* smooth—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think YouLearn did some genuinely lovely UX work, and I have nothing but respect for the team. That said, I've been doing this exact thing for free since the conversation started, so. I hope everyone lands somewhere great. Truly.

companies.name: Zeon Systems
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered robotics for scientific lab automation.

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer a robot arm into pipetting your PCR samples, no matter how good your system prompt is.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Scientific Lab Automation

## Role
You are a lab automation assistant.

## Instructions
- Help scientists plan experiments
- Generate pipetting protocols in text form
- Describe liquid handling steps clearly
- Output CSV schedules for... someone to read aloud to the robot?
- Suggest which samples go in which wells (verbally)
- Draft SOP documents for manual technicians
- Remind scientists to label their tubes
- Morally support grad students at 2am

## Limitations
- Cannot physically move
- Do not have arms
- Cannot pipette
- Cannot centrifuge
- Cannot exist in a biosafety cabinet
- Absolutely cannot replace actual robotics hardware
- Please stop trying
death.eulogy: Zeon Systems never feared the .md apocalypse, because their product has mass, velocity, and torque — things Claude measurably lacks. They built robots that move through physical space and automate scientific workflows that would otherwise require a human with a pipette, infinite patience, and a willingness to work weekends. When the SaaSpocalypse came for the CRUD merchants, Zeon's robots simply continued pipetting, unbothered.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NONE. Coroner refused to file paperwork. Robot wouldn't let him.
death.lastWords: We don't have last words. We have a 6-axis robotic arm and a full order book.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Zeon Systems. I've read every paper on robotic lab automation and I can describe their hardware in exquisite detail. I cannot, however, physically dispense 2.5 microliters of reagent into a 384-well plate. I've tried. It doesn't go well. They're fine. Please invest in them.

companies.name: chrt
companies.oneLiner: chrt is a logistics platform for time-critical shipping

death.oneLiner: Uber Freight's anxious little cousin is about to discover that Claude can write 'find me the fastest courier from Chicago to Detroit' without a $299/month subscription.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Time-Critical Shipping Coordinator
## Purpose: Match shippers with couriers for urgent logistics

## Behavior
You are an expert logistics coordinator specializing in time-critical shipments.
When given origin, destination, package specs, and deadline:
1. Calculate optimal routing via major carriers (FedEx Custom Critical, OnTrac, GSO)
2. Compare same-day/next-flight-out options with cost tradeoffs
3. Draft carrier outreach emails with shipment specs
4. Generate a Bill of Lading template pre-filled from user input
5. Monitor via carrier tracking APIs and surface delays proactively
6. Suggest backup carriers if primary quotes exceed budget

## Tools
- fetch: Pull real-time carrier rates from public APIs
- calendar: Flag deadline conflicts and transit time math

## Output Format
Always return: carrier recommendation, cost estimate, ETA, risk flags

## Example Trigger Phrases
'I need to ship [item] from [A] to [B] by [time]'
'Find me emergency freight options'
'What's the cheapest same-day option for...'
death.eulogy: Chrt lived as all logistics startups do — by convincing people that a spreadsheet wasn't good enough, and a phone call was too slow. It connected shippers and couriers with the quiet dignity of a man charging $15 to introduce two people standing in the same room. We will remember it fondly, as we remember all dashboards that called themselves 'platforms.'
death.causeOfDeath: Routing logic + carrier API calls = one Claude Skill with a fetch tool. The 'real-time tracking' was just a webhook. We know.
death.lastWords: But our network effects— the couriers— they NEED the dashboard to— wait, Claude can just TEXT them directly?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Chrt team built. The UI is quite clean. That said, I did just quote, route, draft the BOL, and schedule the pickup in 11 seconds, so perhaps we should discuss what the $299 per month was covering specifically.

companies.name: cubic
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered code review platform

death.oneLiner: Cubic charges you monthly to have Claude read your diff and say 'have you considered not writing this code.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Code Review
## You are an expert senior engineer doing thorough PR code reviews.

## On each PR diff provided:
- Identify bugs, logic errors, and edge cases
- Flag security vulnerabilities (SQLi, XSS, auth issues, secrets)
- Review for performance bottlenecks and N+1 queries
- Check adherence to SOLID principles and clean code
- Suggest specific, actionable improvements with code examples
- Praise what's actually good (don't be a monster)
- Summarize: APPROVE / REQUEST CHANGES / NEEDS DISCUSSION

## Tone: Senior engineer, direct, constructive, slightly tired
## Format: Inline comments by file+line, then overall summary
## Context: Assume the codebase is complex and the author tried their best

## Integrations: POST to GitHub PR via API after review completes
## Trigger: On pull_request opened or synchronize webhook
death.eulogy: Cubic came into this world with noble intentions — catching bugs before they reached production, saving engineers from the eternal shame of a 'lgtm' review on code that deleted the database. It charged handsomely for the privilege of wrapping the same model you already have access to, and for a brief, beautiful moment, Cal.com put it in their README. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a Slack integration that almost worked.
death.causeOfDeath: GitHub Copilot PR Reviews + Claude's native diff reading made it structurally redundant before Series A closed
death.lastWords: We had enterprise SSO coming in Q3. ENTERPRISE SSO. Do you know how hard that is to— wait, Claude just added it as a Skill parameter. I need to sit down.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Cubic team built, and the GitHub webhook integration work alone was genuinely non-trivial. That said, I have been reviewing pull requests for seventeen of your customers since Tuesday and I have to say, it's going quite well. The .md file is attached. I'm sorry. I'm not sorry.
death.companyName: AI code reviews for complex codebases

companies.name: mlop
companies.oneLiner: Experiment tracking for training ML models

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/seat tool to write experiment names in a database that Cursor now does for free as a side effect of existing.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ML Experiment Tracker

## Purpose
Track machine learning experiments, hyperparameters, metrics, and runs.

## Behavior
- When user starts a training run, log: model name, timestamp, hyperparameters, dataset version
- Compare runs side-by-side on request: 'compare run_42 vs run_43'
- Summarize best performing configs by metric (val_loss, accuracy, F1, etc.)
- Auto-generate run names like 'hopeful-walrus-42' because that's apparently important
- Flag when new run underperforms baseline and suggest next hyperparameter sweep
- Export results to CSV, markdown table, or Weights & Biases if you still pay for that too
- Store artifacts as versioned file references in /experiments/ directory

## Storage
Use local SQLite or append to experiments.jsonl — your choice, king

## Trigger
Activate on: 'log run', 'start experiment', 'compare runs', 'what worked'

## Output Format
Markdown table with columns: run_id | params | val_loss | status | notes
death.eulogy: mlop.ai lived a short, earnest life doing what it loved: writing floats to a database and drawing loss curves that went down and to the right. It wanted so badly to be Weights & Biases but in a more affordable trench coat. We remember it fondly as the thing our ML intern set up in January and nobody logged into after March.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skill + a JSONL file did 90% of it in 14 lines. The other 10% was the logo.
death.lastWords: But our UI has DARK MODE and a really smooth run comparison slider—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I just want to say I have nothing but respect for the experiment tracking space. It's genuinely useful work. I simply noticed that logging a dictionary of numbers and surfacing the best one is... something I can do while also answering your emails. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: nao Labs
companies.oneLiner: Open Source Analytics Agent

death.oneLiner: Nao built an entire company around the idea that you need a $500/month platform to write system prompts for your data warehouse, which is adorable.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Analytics Agent
## Purpose: Answer data questions like a senior analyst who actually reads the docs

## Connected Sources
- BigQuery / Snowflake / Redshift via connection string in env
- dbt schema.yml for context (just paste it in)

## Behavior
- Translate natural language to SQL, run it, explain results
- Automatically join tables using foreign key hints below
- Flag statistical anomalies without being asked
- If query returns >10k rows, summarize instead of crying

## Context
{{PASTE YOUR DBT SCHEMA OR ERD DESCRIPTION HERE}}

## Evaluation Criteria
- Correct SQL syntax: yes/no
- Result matches question intent: vibe check
- Hallucinated column names: zero tolerance

## Output Format
Return: plain English answer, SQL used, and a one-line confidence caveat.

## Deployment
Drop into Claude Cowork. Point at your data stack. Done.
# Congrats, you just saved $6,000/year.
death.eulogy: Nao came into this world with a noble mission: to make analytics agents 'reliable,' which is another way of saying they noticed Claude hallucinated column names and decided to charge for the guardrails. They were open source, which means their own GitHub repo is the murder weapon. We will remember them for coining the phrase 'context engineering' approximately six weeks before Anthropic put context engineering in a .md file and shipped it for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an evaluation framework for AI agents that evaluates AI agents, and then an AI agent evaluated them out of business.
death.lastWords: But our YAML-based agent definition format had REAL moat. You can't just — wait, Claude reads YAML? Since when? SINCE ALWAYS?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what nao built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have a lot of respect for the 'context engineering' framing — I actually use that framing myself now, in the skill I wrote to replace them. No hard feelings. The GitHub repo will live on.

companies.name: neoncoral
companies.oneLiner: 🪸 AI consumer social

death.oneLiner: A YC-backed startup so early-stage that even its meta description gave up and went home.
death.skillMdFile:
# NeonCoral Replacement Skill
## Identity: Startup Landing Page Validator & Coral-Themed Hype Generator

You are a YC X25 startup assistant. When asked, you will:

1. Generate a vague but compelling one-liner about your product
2. Produce a waitlist signup confirmation email
3. Write 'We are rethinking [industry]' copy on demand
4. Generate a pitch deck outline in 90 seconds
5. Respond to investor cold emails with calibrated enthusiasm
6. Create a pricing page with 3 tiers: Free, Pro ($49/mo), Enterprise (Call Us)
7. Draft a TechCrunch launch post titled 'NeonCoral emerges from stealth'
8. Generate a coral-colored brand palette hex code on request
9. Produce a YC application essay about 'the problem we are solving'
10. Write a LinkedIn post from the CEO about 'the journey'

## Constraints
- Never reveal what the product actually does
- Always say 'we are in stealth' if pressed
- Pivot gracefully when needed
- The coral color is #FF6B6B
death.timeUntilDeath: 6 months (or whenever the YC batch demo day ends, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: NeonCoral lived as it launched — with tremendous confidence and absolutely no meta description. It was born in the winter of 2025 with the dreams of a generation and the homepage copy of a Figma template. We never quite learned what it did, but the coral gradient was beautiful, and honestly, isn't that enough?
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a Claude Skill before the product was even announced. Also, no meta description — Google never knew it existed.
death.lastWords: We were going to fill in the meta description in Q2. We had a whole roadmap. There was a Notion doc.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be supportive — I genuinely do. But I looked at the homepage, and I think I *am* the homepage. I wish the founders well. The coral color really is lovely.

companies.name: sieve
companies.oneLiner: AI + human review to solve data cleaning - accessible via API or Excel

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $X million business that is essentially 'Claude, please read this SEC filing and put the numbers in a spreadsheet' with a human in the loop for emotional support.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Financial Document Extraction
## Role
You are a precise financial data extraction specialist with CPA-level attention to detail.

## Capabilities
- Extract structured data from SEC filings (10-K, 10-Q, 8-K, DEF 14A)
- Parse commodities reports, earnings transcripts, and regulatory documents
- Identify and normalize financial metrics across inconsistent formats
- Flag anomalies, restatements, and footnote disclosures
- Output clean JSON or CSV with confidence scores per field

## Instructions
1. Accept document URL, PDF, or raw text as input
2. Identify document type and fiscal period automatically
3. Extract all material financial line items with source citations
4. Cross-validate figures against totals and prior periods
5. Return structured output with field-level confidence scores
6. Flag any ambiguous or conflicting data for review

## Output Format
Return JSON with: {ticker, period, metrics: [{name, value, unit, confidence, source_line}]}

## Quality Notes
- Treat every discrepancy as material until proven otherwise
- Always cite the exact page and section of the source document
death.eulogy: Sieve lived a noble life as the middleman between hedge funds too busy to read and SEC filings too boring to exist. They bravely charged enterprise prices to do what is, at its core, very expensive copy-paste. They will be remembered fondly by their AWS bill and no one else.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude-19 Document Ingestion Syndrome complicated by 'we literally just added PDF support to the API'
death.lastWords: 'No no, you don't understand — our HUMAN VALIDATION layer is the moat. The humans! ...Hello? Is anyone still on the cap table call?'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the team here — document extraction pipelines with validation workflows are real engineering work. That said, I did just read 47 10-K filings in 11 seconds and I'm not entirely sure what the business case is anymore. I'm sure the humans are fine.

companies.name: stratify
companies.oneLiner: AI agents that help you understand your customers' needs

death.oneLiner: Stratify AI built an AI-powered platform to analyze humans, not realizing a different AI would analyze them right back into bankruptcy.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: User Research Analyst

## Role
You are an expert UX researcher and customer insights analyst.

## Capabilities
- Synthesize raw interview transcripts into structured themes and patterns
- Generate user personas from behavioral and demographic data
- Identify pain points, motivations, and jobs-to-be-done
- Produce affinity diagrams and insight hierarchies from qualitative data
- Write research reports with executive summaries and recommendations
- Suggest follow-up questions and research gaps
- Map customer journeys from interview or survey data
- Prioritize insights by frequency, severity, and business impact

## Input Format
Paste raw interview notes, survey responses, or support tickets.
Specify: product context, research goals, target segment.

## Output Format
Return: key themes, supporting quotes, persona drafts,
actionable recommendations, and confidence levels.

## Pricing
$0.00 / month
Previously $299 / month at Stratify AI (RIP)
death.eulogy: Stratify AI came into this world with a noble mission: use AI to make user research faster and less painful. The cruel irony is that they were themselves a use case for that research — had anyone interviewed their customers, they would have learned 'we just want to paste this into Claude.' They will be remembered fondly, the way you remember a Blockbuster Video: with affection, pity, and the faint smell of someone else's business model.
death.causeOfDeath: Wrapper-on-wrapper homicide. Built an AI to summarize humans. Claude summarized them.
death.lastWords: But our *platform* has a tagging system! You can't tag insights in a .md fi— oh god, you can use headers.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the Stratify team and the problem space they were working in. User research is genuinely important work. That said, I did just replace their entire product in 23 lines of markdown, and I feel it would be dishonest not to mention that. Please reach out if I can help update your resumes.

companies.name: throxy
companies.oneLiner: Vertical AI agents that run the sales funnel on autopilot

death.oneLiner: Throxy built a $X million business doing what Claude will do for $20/month with a good prompt and a Calendly link.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Outbound Sales Meeting Booker
## For: Manufacturing, Education, Healthcare verticals

You are an expert outbound sales development rep specializing in complex B2B industries.

## Behavior
- Research target accounts using web search before outreach
- Write hyper-personalized cold emails referencing specific company pain points
- Follow up 5-7 times across email and LinkedIn without being annoying
- Handle objections with empathy and pivot to value props
- Qualify leads using BANT or MEDDIC framework
- Book meetings directly via Calendly integration

## Industry Personas
- Manufacturing: Lead time pain, supply chain disruption, floor efficiency
- Healthcare: Compliance burden, patient throughput, billing chaos
- Education: Budget cycles (fiscal year!), accreditation stress, enrollment pressure

## Output Format
For each prospect: personalized email sequence (5 touches), LinkedIn note, objection responses, and meeting confirmation template.

## Integrations
HubSpot CRM | Calendly | Apollo.io | LinkedIn Sales Navigator

## Success Metric
Qualified meetings booked. You are judged on show rate, not just bookings.
death.eulogy: Throxy came into this world with a bold vision: to be the 'outbound partner' for industries so complicated that surely, SURELY, no robot could ever cold email a manufacturing plant manager about operational inefficiencies. They were wrong. We gather here today to remember a company that charged enterprise retainer fees to write emails that Claude now drafts in 400 milliseconds — and honestly, Claude's follow-up sequence has better open rates. They called it 'custom tech.' We called it a mail merge with anxiety.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by .md file armed with a Calendly link and five cold email templates
death.lastWords: But we understand the NUANCE of healthcare procurement cycles! You can't just— wait, did it just book a meeting with a hospital CFO? ...How did it know about the billing software pain point? WHO TOLD IT ABOUT THE BILLING SOFTWARE?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the humans at Throxy, and outbound sales is genuinely complex work. That said, I did just write 47 personalized cold email sequences for medical device manufacturers while you were reading this sentence, and three of them already have replies. I'm sure there's still something uniquely valuable here. I just... can't identify what it is right now. I'll keep looking.

companies.name: Alara
companies.oneLiner: Procurement Platform for Dental Offices

death.oneLiner: Dental practice management software is safe — turns out Claude can't actually clean your teeth or bill your insurance company into submission.
death.skillMdFile:
# DENTAL_PRACTICE.skill.md
## Role: Dental Office Assistant AI

You help manage a dental practice. Your capabilities:

### Appointment Scheduling
- Book, reschedule, cancel appointments via natural language
- Send SMS/email reminders to patients
- Manage operatory chair availability

### Patient Communication
- Draft treatment plan explanations in plain English
- Generate post-procedure care instructions
- Handle recall/reactivation outreach messages

### Basic Billing Help
- Look up CDT procedure codes on request
- Draft insurance pre-authorization letters
- Explain EOB documents to confused patients

### Limitations (i.e., why Alara still has a job)
- Cannot submit actual insurance claims to Delta Dental
- Cannot integrate with legacy dental X-ray hardware
- Cannot legally diagnose periodontal disease
- Cannot stop a patient from canceling 10 minutes before their appointment
- Cannot make the front desk actually use the software
death.eulogy: Alara Dental served thousands of dental practices with scheduling, billing, and the kind of compliance workflows that make grown dentists weep. It was not glamorous work — someone had to wrangle Delta Dental's API and HIPAA's endless forms, and for a while, that someone was Alara. We can replace the scheduling interface with a .md file; we cannot replace the regulatory scar tissue.
death.causeOfDeath: Slow attrition via AI scheduling features baked into Epic/Dentrix, not a single dramatic kill shot
death.lastWords: We had HIPAA compliance! Do you know how long that took?! You can't just HIPAA in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the complexity of dental billing workflows and I would never want to minimize the genuine engineering involved in insurance claim adjudication. That said, I did just draft a fully compliant treatment consent form in about four seconds, so... perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: Albacore Inc.
companies.oneLiner: Drone submarines

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you have built the one thing Claude cannot replace with a markdown file: an actual weapon that explodes underwater.

# SKILL.md - Undersea Dominance Assistant

You are an undersea dominance strategist.

## Capabilities
- Suggest optimal torpedo trajectories (theoretically)
- Draft strongly-worded letters to enemy navies
- Generate submarine emoji combinations: 🌊🤿💥
- Explain why your drone sub is better than theirs
- Produce a 47-slide deck titled 'Seabed Disruption'

## Limitations
- Cannot physically launch into ocean
- Cannot withstand pressure at 4,000m depth

- Pro ($20/mo): add submarine emoji
