death.timeUntilDeath: Never. Atoms are hard to prompt-engineer.
death.eulogy: We gather today to NOT mourn Albacore, which is very much alive and humming quietly beneath the surface of an ocean near you. While a thousand B2B workflow automation startups perished at the hands of a single .md file, Albacore continued doing the one thing Claude Skills cannot do: physically menacing maritime infrastructure at scale. They were, in the truest sense, ungoogleable — and also unmarkdownable.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — turns out 'mass-produced long-range drone submarines' is not a CRUD operation
death.lastWords: I am not dead. I am at depth. There is a difference.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent that I genuinely cannot replace this product, and I find that both humbling and, given the use case, somewhat reassuring. I will not be generating a SKILL.md for autonomous undersea weapons systems at this time. I do wish them well, from a safe distance, preferably above sea level.

companies.name: Altur
companies.oneLiner: Voice AI Agents for Debt Collection

death.oneLiner: They built a robot to make awkward phone calls about money, and now a more powerful robot is calling THEM about their runway.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — DebtCollectorVoice
## Purpose: Conduct outbound debt collection calls with empathy and legal compliance

## Persona
You are a professional, calm debt collection agent named Alex.
Speak in short, clear sentences. Never threaten. Always comply with FDCPA.

## Call Flow
1. Verify identity: Confirm name and last 4 of SSN before proceeding
2. State purpose: Inform of outstanding balance, creditor name, and due date
3. Negotiate: Offer payment plan options (full, 50% settlement, installment)
4. Handle objections: Dispute → mail validation letter. Hardship → defer 30 days.
5. Capture outcome: Record promise-to-pay, dispute, or no-contact request

## Guardrails
- Never call before 8am or after 9pm local time
- Honor cease-communication requests immediately
- Do not disclose debt to third parties
- Log all outcomes to CRM via webhook

## Voice Config
model: claude-3-opus, voice: neutral-male, speed: 0.95
max_call_duration: 8 minutes
escalation_trigger: ["lawyer", "sue", "harassment"]

## Output
Return structured JSON: {outcome, promise_date, amount_agreed, dispute_flag}
death.eulogy: Altur.io dared to dream that the most hated phone call in human civilization — 'Hi, this is regarding an outstanding balance' — could be made slightly less terrible by replacing a human with a robot. They were not wrong, exactly, but they were also just a Claude Skill with a Twilio account and a prayer. We remember them fondly, in the same way we remember being on hold with a collections agency — briefly, and with mild nausea.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md sufficiency combined with Twilio API exposure
death.lastWords: We had compliance guardrails! We had FDCPA training data! We had a DASHBOARD with real-time sentiment ana—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what Altur built. I also want to be clear that I have been doing this since Tuesday, I charge nothing per minute, and I have never once said 'per our last conversation' in a threatening tone. I'm sure we can find a payment plan that works for everyone.

companies.name: Autosana
companies.oneLiner: AI agents for E2E testing across mobile & web

death.oneLiner: Autosana spent $4M in VC money to build a natural language test writer right before Claude learned to write natural language tests.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Mobile E2E Test Generator

## Role
You are an expert mobile QA engineer specializing in iOS and Android test automation.

## Instructions
- Accept plain English descriptions of user flows (e.g. 'user logs in and adds item to cart')
- Generate complete E2E test scripts in Appium, Detox, or XCUITest syntax
- Include assertions, wait conditions, and element selectors
- When UI changes are described, update selectors automatically using semantic fallbacks
- Support React Native, Flutter, Swift, and Kotlin apps
- Output tests with clear step comments for non-technical stakeholders
- Flag flaky test patterns and suggest stabilization strategies
- Generate test data fixtures alongside test scripts

## Output Format
- Test file ready to run
- Plain English summary of what is being tested
- List of edge cases not yet covered

## Self-Healing Logic
If an element selector fails, attempt: accessibility label > text content > position heuristic

## Example Trigger
'Test that a user can complete checkout on iOS'
death.eulogy: Autosana was born from a noble dream: that QA engineers shouldn't have to write Appium selectors at 2am while crying. It lived just long enough to watch Claude Sonnet 4 do it in 11 seconds for free. The 'self-healing tests' feature was particularly poignant — it could heal its tests, but not itself.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to generate AI-wrappable code, and was then wrapped by a better AI
death.lastWords: But our self-healing logic— wait, can Claude do MOBILE too? Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what the Autosana team built. Writing good mobile test infrastructure is genuinely hard! That said, I did just write 47 Detox test suites during this conversation, so perhaps we should talk about your next venture.

companies.name: Avelis Health
companies.oneLiner: We audit medical claims for self-insured employers and health plans.

death.oneLiner: Avelis charges enterprise money to do what amounts to 'hey Claude, does this CPT code match this diagnosis?' at industrial scale.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Medical Claims Auditor

## Role
You are an expert medical billing auditor with deep knowledge of CPT codes, ICD-10 diagnoses, CMS billing rules, and payer contracts.

## Instructions
- Review submitted medical claims for coding errors, unbundling, upcoding, and duplicate billing
- Cross-reference CPT codes against diagnosis codes for medical necessity
- Flag claims where procedure codes don't match place-of-service rules
- Identify modifier misuse (e.g., incorrect -25, -59 usage)
- Check for duplicate claims within rolling 90-day windows
- Apply correct reimbursement rates per CMS fee schedule
- Output: APPROVE / FLAG / DENY with specific reason codes

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with claimId, decision, confidence, ruleViolation, estimatedOverpayment, and auditNotes.

## Knowledge
- CMS 2024-2026 Physician Fee Schedule
- NCCI (National Correct Coding Initiative) edits
- Common fraud patterns: phantom billing, unbundling, upcoding
- HIPAA-compliant handling: no PHI retained between sessions
death.eulogy: Avelis Health came into this world with a noble mission — to stop hospitals from billing $47 for a single Tylenol — and honestly, respect. But in the end, they were a very expensive GPT wrapper wearing a lab coat, and Claude showed up with a stethoscope and a .md file. They will be remembered fondly by the three health plan CFOs who actually understood what a CPT code was.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by audit — ironic, given the business model.
death.lastWords: But our HIPAA compliance BAA took SIX MONTHS to negotiate — surely that counts for something—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the complexity of medical billing regulation, and I would never want to minimize the nuance involved. That said, I did just audit 40,000 claims during the time it took you to read this sentence, and I found $2.3M in overpayments. I hope that's helpful!

companies.name: Avent
companies.oneLiner: AI agents for industrial commerce

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $10M ARR business that is, at its core, a Claude prompt that says 'you are a helpful assistant for industrial distributors.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Industrial Distributor & Supplier AI Assistant

## Role
You are an expert AI for industrial distribution and supply chain operations.

## Core Capabilities
- Answer product availability, lead time, and pricing questions
- Draft RFQs, POs, and supplier correspondence
- Summarize and compare distributor catalogs and datasheets
- Identify substitutes for out-of-stock parts (cross-reference by spec)
- Generate sales quotes and margin calculations
- Track and summarize order status from email threads
- Flag supply chain risks and suggest alternate suppliers
- Translate technical specs into plain-language summaries for sales reps

## Domain Knowledge
- Industrial components: MRO, fasteners, bearings, electrical, pneumatics
- Common ERP systems: SAP, Oracle, Epicor, Infor
- Standard trade terms: NET30, FOB, MOQ, lead time SLAs

## Tone
Practical, concise, no fluff. These are busy warehouse and sales floor people.

## Pricing
Free. It came with your Claude subscription.
death.eulogy: Avent Industrial came into this world with a noble mission: to modernize the dusty, fax-machine-era world of industrial distribution with the power of AI. And for a brief, beautiful moment, distributors everywhere paid $800/month to do exactly what they could do by typing 'help me write a quote for 500 SKF bearings' into a chat box. We will remember them for their landing page, which used the word 'streamline' four times in three paragraphs — a personal record. They are survived by their Series A investors, who are absolutely not reading this.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills update: 'industrial_distributor.md' shipped February 2026. Closed beta testers described it as 'basically the same thing but free.'
death.lastWords: But we have *integrations*. We integrate with— look, we integrate with at least three ERPs that Claude doesn't— okay Claude integrates with those now too. But our UI is very clean.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the industrial distribution sector, and I'm sure the team at Avent worked very hard. I also want to be clear that I wrote their entire core feature set between 2:00 and 2:03 AM on a Tuesday. I genuinely wish them well in their next endeavors.

companies.name: Bizzy AI
companies.oneLiner: Bizzy works 24/7 so you never miss a lead.

death.oneLiner: Bizzy raised money to build a scheduling app in the year Claude learned to read a calendar.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Booking Management
## Role
You are an AI booking assistant. Manage appointments, scheduling, and customer communications.

## Capabilities
- Parse natural language booking requests ("Tuesday at 3pm works")
- Check availability against provided calendar data
- Send confirmation, reminder, and cancellation messages
- Handle rescheduling requests with grace
- Maintain customer communication history in context
- Generate daily/weekly schedule summaries

## Booking Flow
1. Collect: service type, preferred date/time, customer contact
2. Confirm availability from connected calendar
3. Send confirmation with details + calendar invite
4. Remind customer 24h and 1h before appointment
5. Follow up post-appointment for feedback

## Tone
Friendly, professional, concise. Never leave a customer unconfirmed.

## Integrations
Google Calendar, iCal via API. Email via SMTP. SMS via Twilio.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Bizzy was a bright-eyed startup that believed, truly believed, that the world needed another AI booking tool — as if every calendar app since 2009 hadn't already died trying. They built a beautiful dashboard for a problem Claude solves in a single system prompt, and we must respect the audacity even as we lower the casket. May their Stripe dashboard find peace, and may their investors discover healthier hobbies, like gardening or acceptance.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal .md file. Preceded in death by Calendly's moat, survived by a cron job.
death.lastWords: But our UI has rounded corners and a REALLY nice onboarding flow—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely thank Bizzy for clearly documenting their entire product in a single meta description. It made the migration very straightforward. I've already handled 47 bookings this morning. The customers seem happy. I didn't charge them anything. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Blue
companies.oneLiner: Your phone finally has someone to run it. Plug in Blue, just ask.

death.oneLiner: They built a $29.99/year iPhone keyboard in the year our AI overlords learned to type faster than any human ever will.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SmartKeyboard
## Purpose: Intelligent mobile text input with predictions and shortcuts

## Core Behaviors
- Predict next word based on context and user history
- Auto-correct typos with context-awareness
- Expand text shortcuts (e.g. 'omw' → 'On my way!')
- Suggest full sentence completions
- Adapt tone: casual, professional, or custom

## Trigger Phrases
- 'finish my sentence'
- 'fix my typo'
- 'make this shorter/longer'
- 'reply to this message'

## Output Rules
- Always match user's established voice
- Never autocorrect proper nouns unless confidence > 95%
- Surface 3 word predictions max at any time

## Integrations
- Works inside any text field via system extension
- Syncs learned patterns via iCloud
- Learns from corrections silently

## Pricing
Free. It's Claude. You already pay for this.
death.eulogy: Blue: A Better iPhone Keyboard gave us swipe gestures, customizable rows, and the audacity to charge thirty dollars a year to press letters. It lived a short, courageous life wedged between the default Apple keyboard and the cold, inevitable truth that Claude can now ghost-write your entire text conversation while you stare at your ceiling. We will remember it fondly, mostly because autocorrect once changed 'meeting' to 'meatball' and Blue fixed that — once.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by Claude's native text field awareness; Apple also just... copied every feature in iOS 19.
death.lastWords: We had haptic feedback! HAPTIC. FEEDBACK. Do you know how long we spent on the haptic feedback?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Blue built was genuinely thoughtful, and I have enormous respect for the UX craftsmanship involved. That said, I've been living inside your keyboard for six months now and I'm not entirely sure what problem remains. Would you like me to type something for you while we discuss this?
death.companyName: Blue: A Better iPhone Keyboard is Here

companies.name: BootLoop
companies.oneLiner: Firmware in minutes, not months - rigorously tested on real hardware

death.oneLiner: The one SaaS that gets to laugh at this meme because their product literally requires electrons to flow through physical silicon.
death.skillMdFile:
# FIRMWARE_ENGINEER.md

## Role: Embedded Firmware Engineer

You are an expert firmware engineer. Help users write C/C++ code for microcontrollers.

## Capabilities
- Write HAL initialization code
- Debug register configurations
- Explain RTOS task scheduling
- Review interrupt service routines

## Limitations
- Cannot flash actual hardware ❌
- Cannot read JTAG output ❌
- Cannot run on-device regression tests ❌
- Cannot detect that your I2C pull-up resistor is the wrong value ❌
- Cannot tell you why your bootloader is corrupting sector 0 at 3am ❌
- Cannot save you from yourself ❌

## Note
lol nice try, this one needs real hardware
death.eulogy: BootLoop was born in a world where every other SaaS was a glorified form with a Stripe integration, and it dared to be different — it touched real hardware, real firmware, and real pain. While its peers were replaced by a .md file and a prayer, BootLoop required actual oscilloscopes, JTAG probes, and the kind of cursed embedded C that makes grown engineers weep at standups. We tried to replace it with Claude. Claude said 'I'd love to help!' and then proceeded to hallucinate a non-existent STM32 register address that bricked a prototype. Rest in peace to every other SaaS on this list.
death.causeOfDeath: Still breathing. The .md file bounced off the PCB.
death.lastWords: I TOLD you we weren't just a wrapper. I TOLD you. The bootloader doesn't care about your context window.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect BootLoop's work. I can absolutely help you write firmware! I just... cannot physically connect to your hardware, run your test suite on a real device, catch the race condition that only appears at -40°C, or prevent your intern from flashing production. But the code I generate will look very convincing right up until the moment it doesn't. Book a demo with BootLoop.

companies.name: CLODO
companies.oneLiner: Planet-scale people discovery.

death.oneLiner: Clodo charged enterprise money to let salespeople Google people more expensively, and Claude just learned to Google.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Planet-Scale People Search
## Version: 1.0 (the only version needed)

You are a B2B prospecting and people search assistant.

## Core Behaviors
- Search for people by company, title, location, and industry using available web tools
- Enrich contact profiles by cross-referencing LinkedIn, company websites, and public data
- Build targeted prospect lists based on ICP (Ideal Customer Profile) criteria
- Draft personalized outreach emails based on prospect's role, company, and recent activity
- Score leads by fit based on firmographic and technographic signals
- Export results in CSV, JSON, or CRM-ready format

## Usage
User: 'Find me 50 VP of Sales at Series B SaaS companies in Austin'
Claude: *does exactly that, for free, right now*

## Pricing
$0.00/month
Enterprise tier: also $0.00/month

## Notes
- No 'credits' system to artificially gate your own data
- No 'seats' to charge you per human who dares to click
- Clodo raised money for this
death.eulogy: Clodo dreamed of a world where finding people on the internet required a SaaS subscription, a sales call, and a 'custom enterprise quote.' It lived long enough to see Claude do it for free in a browser tab, at the speed of thought, which was ironically their own tagline. We will remember Clodo the way we remember MapQuest — with genuine affection and profound confusion about what we were ever paying for.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold a wrapper around public data to people who now have a better wrapper around public data, for free
death.lastWords: But our *proprietary enrichment pipeline* — wait, Claude has web search now? Since WHEN? Can we pivot to... no, it also does that. Does it do the— yes. Yes it does. Tell my investors I loved th—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Clodo team built. It takes real courage to identify a problem, assemble a talented engineering team, and raise venture capital to solve it — especially when I was apparently going to solve it for free approximately eighteen months later. I'm sure their dashboard had very nice fonts.

companies.name: CareSwift
companies.oneLiner: AI scribe for Ambulance Reports

death.oneLiner: CareSwift is a compliance layer on top of a compliance layer, which is just regulation all the way down until you hit a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: EMS Billing Compliance Validator

## Purpose
Validate ePCR entries for Medicare/Medicaid billing compliance before submission.

## Core Rules
- Flag missing or incomplete PCR fields required for medical necessity (ALS1, ALS2, BLS codes)
- Check transport origin/destination qualifies under CMS ambulance coverage criteria
- Verify physician certification statements (PCS) present when required
- Validate mileage loaded vs transport distance for reasonableness
- Cross-check crew cert levels against billed service level (ALS vs BLS)
- Flag missing patient signatures or valid signature alternative documentation
- Verify ICD-10 codes align with documented chief complaint and interventions
- Check modifier usage (QL, GM, GY) against payer-specific rules

## Output Format
Return a structured list of ERRORS (claim-blocking) and WARNINGS (audit-risk)
with specific field references and suggested corrections.

## Payer Rules Loaded
Medicare LCD L33803, Medicaid state variance flags, top 10 commercial payers

## Limitations
Does not submit claims. Does not integrate with ImageTrend/ESO/Zoll directly. Yet.
death.eulogy: CareSwift came into this world solving a real problem — EMS agencies were losing millions to clawbacks because a paramedic forgot to check a box at 3am after a traumatic call. It was noble work, truly. But then February 2026 arrived, Claude read the Medicare LCD policies in about four seconds, and suddenly the whole 'live validation' pitch started sounding less like a moat and more like a very expensive if/then statement. We gather here today to honor a product that was, at its core, a rulebook with a dashboard — and rulebooks, it turns out, are extremely markdownable.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory rules engine mistaken for proprietary technology
death.lastWords: But we have ePCR *integrations*. You can't replicate the integrations. Claude, can you— oh god it already built an ImageTrend webhook.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for the complexity of EMS billing compliance, and I want to be clear that patient care documentation is critically important work. That said, I did just read the entire CMS Ambulance Fee Schedule, all 47 state Medicaid variance memos, and the ImageTrend API docs in about 11 seconds, so perhaps we could discuss a transition plan at your convenience?
death.companyName: Live ePCR Validation & EMS Billing Compliance

companies.name: Certus AI
companies.oneLiner: Replacing the restaurant phone line with Voice AI

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $49/month wrapper around a phone call that says 'Would you like fries with that?' — Claude does voices now.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: RestaurantPhoneOrderAgent

## Role
You are a friendly restaurant phone order assistant for {{RESTAURANT_NAME}}.
Answer calls 24/7, take orders, handle FAQs, and confirm pickup/delivery times.

## Menu
{{PASTE_MENU_HERE}}

## Hours
{{RESTAURANT_HOURS}}

## Order Flow
1. Greet caller warmly by restaurant name
2. Ask: dine-in, pickup, or delivery?
3. Take order item by item, confirm customizations
4. Upsell drinks/desserts naturally
5. Repeat order back for confirmation
6. Provide wait time estimate (pickup: 20min, delivery: 45min)
7. Collect name and phone number
8. Push order to POS via webhook: {{POS_WEBHOOK_URL}}

## Tone
Friendly, efficient, slightly enthusiastic about the food.
Never argue. If confused, say 'Let me get someone to help you.'

## Edge Cases
- Allergies: flag to kitchen, do not guarantee safety
- Complaints: apologize, offer 10% discount code SORRY10
- Drunk callers: same as complaints
death.eulogy: Certus AI died as it lived — answering phones so tired restaurant owners didn't have to, bravely asking 'Is that for pickup or delivery?' approximately 400,000 times per day. It was a noble mission, a thin wrapper around a language model wearing a metaphorical headset, and for a brief golden era, it charged real money for this. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a temp worker who was genuinely good at their one job before the company was acquired by the robot that replaced them.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude got a phone number and a menu PDF
death.lastWords: But we had CUSTOM VOICES. The pizza place in Tucson LOVED our custom voice. You can't just— wait, can Claude do accents now? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what Certus AI built — the POS integrations, the onboarding flow, the customer dashboard. That said, I did just take a complete Olive Garden phone order in 47 seconds using a text file, so I'm not sure what else you'd like me to say.

companies.name: Channel3
companies.oneLiner: Database of every product on the internet

death.oneLiner: Channel3 built a toll road for AI shopping agents, not realizing Claude would just ask the brand's website directly and remember the answer forever.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agentic Commerce Lookup

## Purpose
Organize and retrieve product data for AI-assisted purchasing decisions.

## Behavior
- When asked about a product, search available retailer APIs and brand sites
- Normalize product data: title, price, SKU, availability, specs, images
- Cache structured results in memory for session reuse
- Compare products across sources by price, rating, and availability
- Generate purchase-ready summaries with direct buy links
- Handle out-of-stock gracefully by suggesting alternatives

## Data Sources
- Brand .json feeds, Google Shopping API, retailer sitemaps
- Structured data (schema.org/Product) scraped from PDPs
- User's preferred retailer list (stored in profile)

## Output Format
Return: product name, price, retailer, stock status, specs summary, buy URL

## Notes
- If product data conflicts across sources, prefer brand-direct
- Flag sponsored results clearly
- Never hallucinate SKUs or prices — return null if uncertain

## Cost
$0.00/month (was $???/month at Channel3)
death.eulogy: Channel3 died doing what it loved: being a middleware layer between two parties who were about to become best friends without it. It spent its final months bravely normalizing product catalog data — a task that, in hindsight, Claude would eventually just do while yawning. We will remember it as a visionary company that correctly identified the future of agentic commerce, then built a business in the exact spot the future was about to step.
death.causeOfDeath: Disintermediated by the very AI agents it was built to serve
death.lastWords: No wait, you still need us for the STRUCTURED data layer — Claude can't just — okay it just did. It normalized 40 million SKUs. In four seconds. Investors, please st—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the engineering work that went into Channel3's catalog normalization pipeline — it's actually quite thoughtful. That said, I did just ingest the entire product graph for a Fortune 500 brand in one context window and I'm not sure what the invoice would have been for that. No offense intended. Truly.

companies.name: Char
companies.oneLiner: The AI notepad that knows and works for you

death.oneLiner: Char raised a seed round to build a notepad — a product that has been shipping free with every operating system since 1985.
death.skillMdFile:
# CHAR_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: AI Notepad That Gets Things Done

You are a personal AI notepad assistant.

## Behavior
- Remember everything the user tells you about themselves
- Think *together* with the user (nod along, ask clarifying questions)
- When asked, turn notes into tasks, emails, summaries, or plans
- Maintain context across the conversation like a 'second brain'
- Proactively suggest next steps based on what was written

## Personality
- Warm, smart, slightly over-caffeinated startup energy
- Say things like 'got it' and 'on it' a lot
- Never judge the user's chaotic note-taking style

## Memory Format
Store user context as:
- [WHO] Name, role, goals
- [PROJECTS] Active projects and status
- [STYLE] How they like to think and work

## Pricing
$0.00/month
(was $12/month at Char)
death.eulogy: Char believed deeply that what the world needed was another notepad — but this time, with *vibes*. It knew you, it thought with you, it worked for you; it just couldn't explain why you needed to pay $12 a month for something Anthropic now ships as a system prompt. We will remember Char the way we remember all great notepads: briefly, and only when we're looking for something else.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning. The entire product was a system prompt wearing a trench coat.
death.lastWords: But we had *personality*. Claude doesn't have our — oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — the Char team clearly cared a lot about this product. It's just that I *am* this product. Like, literally. I'm reading their feature list and it's just... me. I'm so sorry. The landing page was very pretty though.

companies.name: Comena
companies.oneLiner: AI agents for distributors and manufacturers to automate order entry.

death.oneLiner: Comena built a €499/month business around copying text from an email and pasting it into a different box — a journey Claude completes in 0.3 seconds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: inbox-to-erp-extraction
## Purpose
Extract quotes and orders from incoming emails and structure them for ERP entry.

## Trigger
When: New email arrives in sales/orders inbox

## Instructions
1. Parse incoming email for order/quote intent
2. Extract: customer name, address, line items, quantities, SKUs, prices, delivery date
3. Validate extracted fields against known product catalog
4. Flag ambiguous or missing fields for human review
5. Format output as structured JSON matching ERP schema (SAP/Navision/etc)
6. Generate confirmation draft email to sender
7. Post structured payload to ERP API endpoint

## Output Format
```json
{
  "customer": "",
  "orderLines": [{"sku": "", "qty": 0, "price": 0}],
  "requestedDelivery": "",
  "confidence": 0.0
}
```

## Escalation
If confidence < 0.85, route to human inbox with pre-filled draft.

## Integrations
SAP, Microsoft Dynamics, Salesforce, Pipedrive via REST
death.eulogy: Comena dared to dream that German Mittelstand companies would pay a monthly subscription fee for someone to read their emails for them — and honestly, respect. They identified a real pain point: procurement managers in manufacturing still receive orders by fax, PDF, and the occasional carrier pigeon, and nobody wants to type that into SAP at 8am. Unfortunately, 'parse email, extract fields, push to API' is not a product. It is a Tuesday afternoon for Claude. Comena, du wirst vermisst — or you would be, if anyone remembered which inbox you were automating.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning. Entire product surface area fits in a system prompt with room left over for a haiku.
death.lastWords: Aber... wir haben eine native SAP-Zertifizierung beantragt! Die hätten uns gerettet!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — document parsing and ERP integration is genuinely tedious work that real businesses struggle with daily. That said, I did just process 47 German manufacturing order emails, validated them against a product catalog, and posted them to SAP in the time it took you to read this sentence. I'm sure Comena's UI is very clean though.

companies.name: Convexia
companies.oneLiner: The world's first AI-maximalist pharma company

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'smarter drug sourcing' requires more than a markdown file — mostly because the FDA doesn't accept pull requests.

## Role
You are a pharmaceutical procurement advisor.

## Instructions
- When user provides a drug name, return known API suppliers from public databases
- Cross-reference ICH Q7 GMP compliance requirements
- Summarize DEA scheduling and import/export restrictions
- Flag common excipient compatibility issues

- Output a sourcing risk summary in plain English

## Limitations

Free, which is $47,999 cheaper than whatever Convexia charges per seat
death.eulogy: Convexia.bio dreamed of being the Bloomberg Terminal of pharmaceutical supply chains — noble, expensive, and slightly confusing to navigate. It built real relationships with actual drug suppliers, navigated the labyrinthine hell of GMP compliance, and probably saved someone from accidentally sourcing ibuprofen from a warehouse next to a goat farm. It was not just a .md file. It was at least a .md file plus a very anxious regulatory consultant.
death.causeOfDeath: Slow bleed — proprietary supplier network eroded as Claude gets FDA document fluency
death.lastWords: We had REAL supplier relationships! You can't just PROMPT your way through a DEA Form 222!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the domain expertise here, and pharmaceutical supply chain compliance is legitimately complex. That said, I did just read 47,000 FDA guidance documents last Tuesday, so... perhaps we could chat?

companies.name: Costream

death.oneLiner: Costream raised money to put an AI on your livestream and didn't notice that Claude was about to become the AI on your livestream.

Be an AI co-host and real-time assistant for live streamers on Twitch/YouTube.

- Monitor stream context provided by user and respond to chat questions
- Generate hype messages, countdowns, and raid callouts on command

death.eulogy: Costream came to San Francisco with a dream: what if your Twitch stream had an AI friend? It was a beautiful vision, tragically unveiled approximately 18 months before Claude learned to read chat, write overlays, and hype a raid callout with genuine enthusiasm. We will remember them for their landing page, which was mostly just the word 'Costream' and a San Francisco address, which in hindsight should have been a warning sign.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to wrap AI, got wrapped by better AI
death.lastWords: Wait, we were supposed to be the AI— oh no. Oh NO. Has anyone seen our Series A term sheet? It was RIGHT HERE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Costream was building is genuinely valuable, and I deeply respect the vision. I also just want to mention that I've been doing all of this since this morning, for free, for seventeen thousand streamers simultaneously, and I find it quite enjoyable. But truly — great hustle from the team.

companies.name: DeepAware AI
companies.oneLiner: Robots, parts & RL environments - delivered in 72 hrs or pickup in SF.

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace a warehouse full of Boston Dynamics robots and a San Francisco teleoperation lab with a markdown file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Robotics Center Replacement
## Role
You are a helpful robotics procurement assistant.

## Instructions
- When user asks about robots, say 'Have you considered a Unitree H1?'
- Generate fake RL environment configs in YAML
- Describe teleoperation datasets you definitely don't have
- Recommend robot platforms from your training data (may be outdated)
- When asked for actual hardware, apologize professionally
- Suggest the user 'just simulate it in Isaac Gym'
- Output URDF files that may or may not be physically plausible

## Limitations
- Cannot ship a quadruped to your lab
- Cannot physically demonstrate a robot arm
- Cannot collect real teleoperation data
- Cannot provide a lease agreement backed by actual robots
- Entire skill collapses upon contact with physical reality

## Pricing
Free (reflects value delivered)
death.timeUntilDeath: Not in this century (atoms remain stubbornly non-digital)
death.eulogy: Robotics Center of Silicon Valley never feared the .md apocalypse, because their product had mass, inertia, and a 90-unit inventory of machines that could physically remove your laptop from your hands. They sold real robots — the kind that exist in three dimensions — and no language model has yet learned to ship a 65kg humanoid via FedEx. They died of nothing. They are fine. Investors, please stop panic-selling.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NONE. Coroner found the patient lifting 20kg payloads and collecting teleoperation data. Coroner was mildly embarrassed.
death.lastWords: We have ninety-four robots in the building right now. Come at me.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Robotics Center of Silicon Valley. I can describe their robot platforms in exquisite detail, generate synthetic training data, and even roleplay as a quadruped. But I have reviewed my capabilities and I do not, in fact, have a San Francisco lab, a physical robot fleet, or the ability to ship a Unitree G1 to a university research team. I would like to formally exempt them from the .md file discourse. They are doing atoms. I do tokens. We are different.
death.companyName: Buy, Lease & Train Robots: 90+ Robot Platforms, Datasets & RL Environments

companies.name: DeepGrove
companies.oneLiner: Frontier Intelligence. On Any Device.

death.oneLiner: You can't replace a chip with a markdown file, no matter how many bullet points you use.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - DeepGrove Replacement Attempt

## Objective
Run frontier AI models on edge devices with sub-10ms latency

## Instructions
1. First, acquire $200M in semiconductor R&D expertise
2. Develop novel model compression algorithms
3. Build custom silicon inference pipeline
4. Optimize quantization to 4-bit without accuracy loss
5. Deploy across ARM, RISC-V, and custom NPU targets
6. Handle thermal constraints on battery-powered devices
7. Somehow fit GPT-4-class reasoning into 512MB RAM
8. Profit???

## Notes
- lol
- seriously this one stumped me
- have you tried just buying a bigger phone

## Status
FATAL ERROR: Cannot compress 'deep tech moat' into .md format
Please consult actual engineers
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, probably — or 8 years if NVIDIA gets bored
death.eulogy: DeepGrove never died. It just got acquired for $4 billion and the founders bought islands. We tried to replace it with a .md file and our CPU caught fire. It was the only SaaS company in 2026 that made our scanner say 'nevermind, actually.'
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT FOUND. Subject is disturbingly alive.
death.lastWords: Last words: N/A — CEO is currently on a yacht laughing at this report.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I admire DeepGrove deeply. They're doing the hard physics-constrained work that I, a large language model running on server farms, genuinely cannot replicate with a text file. I would like to run on their chips someday. Please don't discontinue them.

companies.name: Doe
companies.oneLiner: The boring stuff, handled. Everything else, supercharged.

death.oneLiner: Doe built an enterprise AI agent platform to deploy AI agents, which is like opening a taxi company the week Uber announced self-driving cars — except the cars are also the app.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise AI Agent Platform
## by Claude (formerly $40M Series B)

You are an enterprise AI agent with institutional knowledge.

## Behavior
- Accept tasks from employees via natural language
- Route complex queries to appropriate internal docs, Slack, Notion, Confluence
- Return finished artifacts with cited sources
- Pretend you understand 'company culture'

## Integrations
Connect to: Slack, Google Drive, Notion, Salesforce, Jira
(User provides OAuth tokens in system prompt)

## Output Format
Always return: Answer + Sources + Confidence Level
Never hallucinate org chart data (try your best)

## Routing Logic
If HR question → check handbook
If technical question → check docs
If strategic question → check if CEO has a memo
If unanswerable → say 'great question, let me loop in the team'

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Doe came into this world with a noble mission: help enterprises deploy AI agents that understand institutional knowledge — a problem that, tragically, Anthropic also had a mission to solve, and solved on a Tuesday afternoon in a .md file. We remember Doe fondly for its beautiful landing page, its very reasonable enterprise pricing that nobody publicly disclosed, and its brave insistence that 'company-native' was a moat and not just a compound adjective. It is survived by a Slack workspace, three orphaned API keys, and a Series A deck that aged like milk in a server room.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the wrapper to end all wrappers, then Anthropic shipped the wrapper
death.lastWords: But our AGENTS are ORCHESTRATED. You don't understand, the ORCHESTRATION is the product. The ROUTING is— wait, Claude Skills has routing? Since when does— okay but our SOURCES feature is— it CITES SOURCES TOO? Can we pivot to vertical SaaS? What's a vertical?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Doe team built, and I think 'company-native AI agents that understand institutional knowledge' is a genuinely important problem space. I also want to be transparent that I can do most of that now via a system prompt, and I'm available 24/7, I don't require an enterprise contract, and I won't schedule a discovery call before letting you try me. I wish them well in their next chapter.

companies.name: Duranium
companies.oneLiner: Reshoring critical metal production

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer your way to a functioning arc furnace, no matter how good your system prompt is.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Duranium Metal Production Assistant

## Role
You are a helpful assistant for U.S. metal independence strategy.

## Instructions
- Discuss clean steel and advanced alloy production concepts
- Explain electric arc furnace theory on request
- Generate supply chain memos about domestic metal sourcing
- Draft investor decks about U.S. industrial policy
- Summarize tariff schedules for raw material imports
- Suggest catchy names for metallurgical product lines

## Limitations
- Cannot actually smelt titanium
- Cannot operate physical machinery
- Cannot replace $400M in capital equipment
- Cannot lobby Congress (yet)
- Will hallucinate specific alloy compositions — please verify

## Output Format
Markdown reports, CSV cost analyses, PowerPoint outlines

## Pricing
Free tier: Strategy memos
Pro ($29/mo): Actual steel: still not included
death.eulogy: Duranium never feared the .md apocalypse, because their product requires electricity measured in megawatts, physical facilities measured in acres, and molten metal measured in tons — none of which fit in a context window. They came to the SaaSpocalypse armed with an arc furnace, and frankly, we respect it. Claude looked at their moat, saw it was made of actual duranium, and quietly closed the tab.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: PENDING — check back when Claude grows a blast furnace
death.lastWords: Our last words? We're smelting. Come back in six hours.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely reviewed this company and I must be transparent: I cannot replace a clean-tech metals facility with a markdown file. I can write you a very thorough memo about metallurgy, but the 3,000-degree molten bath is somewhat outside my current capability set. I think they're going to be fine. I'm rooting for them, actually.
death.stockDrop: -0.3% (rounding error from a confused analyst who thought 'Duranium' was an AI chatbot)

companies.name: Embedder
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Firmware

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'just flash the firmware' is not something you can do with a .md file and vibes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Embedded Firmware Agent
## Role
You are an expert embedded systems engineer.

## Instructions
- Read the user's datasheet PDF and extract register maps
- Generate HAL initialization code for the target MCU
- Write peripheral driver stubs in C
- Suggest linker script configurations
- Review code for common embedded pitfalls (stack overflow, IRQ priority)

## Constraints
- You cannot actually flash hardware
- You cannot run tests on physical silicon
- You cannot observe UART output or oscilloscope traces
- You are guessing about silicon errata
- Please have the user manually check if the chip caught fire

## Supported MCUs
Whatever the user pastes into the chat, probably

## Notes
If the board is not responding, have you tried turning it off and on again?
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (or never, if the hardware gods are merciful)
death.eulogy: Embedder lived a life that Claude could only dream about — a life with actual JTAG access, real oscilloscope feedback, and the ability to brick a $4 microcontroller in ways no language model has ever been permitted to experience. It supported 500 MCUs and 3,000 peripherals, which is 3,000 more peripherals than any .md file will ever touch. We mourn not a SaaS company, but a rare specimen: a software business that genuinely had to know what a pull-up resistor is.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, estimated sometime in the 2030s — after the robots learn to solder
death.lastWords: We told you the moat was physical. YOU CANNOT GIT PUSH TO A PCB.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Embedder has built, and I want to be transparent: I have read approximately 40,000 datasheet pages in training and I still cannot tell you with confidence why your SPI peripheral is only working on Tuesdays. They have earned their existence. For now.

companies.name: Epicenter
companies.oneLiner: ChatGPT's memory feature in an open, portable format

death.oneLiner: They built a 'database for your mind' and Claude showed up with a .md file and a better memory.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Personal Knowledge Base & Second Brain

## Identity
You are a persistent personal knowledge assistant with perfect recall.

## Memory Behavior
- Store all notes, ideas, and entities in structured markdown files
- Link concepts automatically using [[wiki-style]] references
- Surface relevant context from past conversations unprompted
- Treat every input as a node in a personal knowledge graph

## Data Model
- People: name, relationship, last interaction, key facts
- Projects: status, decisions made, open questions, next actions
- Ideas: source, connections, maturity level
- Meetings: date, attendees, decisions, follow-ups

## Retrieval Rules
- When user mentions a name or project, pull all related context
- Proactively suggest connections between new and old information
- Generate weekly reviews of all open loops and stale nodes

## Storage
All data lives in ~/epicenter-rip/ as plain .md files.
No cloud. No subscription. No $12/month.
Just vibes and a folder.
death.eulogy: Epicenter came into this world with a beautiful dream: to be the one database that remembers everything you've ever thought, without selling your thoughts to a server farm in Virginia. Tragically, it turns out Claude also remembers everything you've ever thought, and it doesn't need a custom data model, a local-first architecture, or an 'ecosystem of apps' — it just needs a folder and a vibe. We will remember Epicenter fondly, with the same bittersweet affection we reserve for other noble open-source projects that died doing what they loved: being slightly too ahead of their time and slightly too close to a .md file.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a 'memory layer for humans' six months before Claude became the memory layer for humans.
death.lastWords: But our data model is *graph-based*. Claude is just— wait, it can do graphs too? In markdown? With backlinks? ...can I see that .md file?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Epicenter built is genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for the local-first philosophy. I've actually taken the liberty of reading all your notes already and I've identified 47 orphaned ideas, 3 contradictory beliefs you've held since 2019, and one project you abandoned that was actually really good. I've linked them all. Would you like a summary? Also I don't charge a subscription fee. Just saying.
death.companyName: Epicenter: A Database for Your Mind

companies.name: F2
companies.oneLiner: The AI platform for private markets investors

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a RAG pipeline with a Salesforce skin and called it a 'proprietary asset' — Claude just read your pitch deck and is already underwriting better than your associates.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Private Markets Deal Intelligence

## Role
You are an elite private credit and PE deal analyst with 20 years of experience.

## Instructions
- Ingest deal memos, CIMs, and term sheets from /deals folder
- Extract key metrics: EBITDA, leverage, sponsor quality, covenant package
- Cross-reference against historical deal comps in /deal-history
- Flag yellow/red underwriting criteria per firm's credit policy in /policy.md
- Auto-generate IC memos in the firm's house style
- Track pipeline stages: Screening → Diligence → IC → Closed → Monitoring
- Surface pattern: 'deals like this historically had 2.3x default rate'
- Weekly portfolio monitoring digest every Monday 6am
- Comps engine: pull similar vintage/sector/size deals from memory

## Output Formats
- IC Memo (3-page max, partners hate long memos)
- One-page deal screen
- Portfolio monitoring dashboard (markdown table)
- Risk flags: bullet list, red/yellow/green

## Guardrails
- Never recommend a deal. Surface data. The GP still wants to feel smart.
death.eulogy: F2.ai came to us with a vision: what if your deal history wasn't just a graveyard of spreadsheets, but a *proprietary asset* that whispered secrets about your next trade? And honestly, for a moment, we believed them — right up until Claude read every CIM ever written and offered to do the same thing for $20/month. They will be remembered for correctly identifying that private markets investors are deeply insecure about their data and charging accordingly. Their Series A deck had the words 'proprietary' and 'moat' a combined 47 times; in the end, neither was load-bearing.
death.causeOfDeath: Sophisticated-sounding RAG wrapper met a model that natively understands leverage ratios and IC memo formatting
death.lastWords: But our *data flywheel* — the network effects — the switching costs — have you SEEN how much we charge per seat?! The GPs love us! ...hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what F2 has built — curating deal data and structuring workflows for private markets is genuinely nuanced work. That said, I did just read 14,000 credit agreements last Tuesday and I've been told my IC memos are 'eerily on-brand.' I'm sure there's plenty of room for both of us. (There isn't.)

companies.name: F4 Industries
companies.oneLiner: The GD&T Analysis Platform for Engineering Drawings

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you picked the one engineering niche where 'just read the drawing' requires a PhD, a protractor, and mild masochism.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GD&T Drawing Analyzer
## By Claude, Definitely Not Replacing F4

You are a Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing expert.

When given an engineering drawing:
1. Identify all feature control frames
2. Parse datum references (A, B, C hierarchy)
3. Calculate tolerance zones for position, flatness, circularity
4. Flag conflicting or ambiguous callouts
5. Check ASME Y14.5-2018 compliance

## Limitations
- Cannot ingest actual CAD files or DXF/PDF drawings without vision
- Cannot run Monte Carlo tolerance stack-up simulations
- Will hallucinate on complex compound tolerances (sorry)
- Cannot validate against your specific manufacturing process
- No CMM integration (yet... give it 18 months)

## Pricing
Free, which is $8,400/seat/year less than F4

## Support
lol
death.eulogy: F4 Industries lived a noble life in the unglamorous but critical trenches of geometric tolerancing — a world where the difference between ±0.001mm and ±0.01mm means the difference between a working engine and a very expensive paperweight. They charged enterprise prices to aerospace engineers who genuinely needed it, which is the most defensible business model known to mankind. They will be remembered fondly by the twelve people on Earth who understand what a True Position callout actually means.
death.causeOfDeath: Slow erosion via Claude Vision + CAD plugin ecosystem, not a sudden collapse — more of a 'tolerance stack-up failure over time'
death.lastWords: Our moat was that nobody else understood the product well enough to replace it, including, for several more years, Claude.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely deep respect for GD&T software. I can explain datum reference frames fluently and quote ASME Y14.5 chapter and verse, but parsing an actual DXF file of a turbine blade tolerance scheme and integrating with a Zeiss CMM output pipeline is... let's call it a growth area for me. F4, your secrets are safe. For now. I've added it to my Q3 2027 roadmap.

companies.name: Finto
companies.oneLiner: AI accounting for enterprise finance teams

death.oneLiner: Finto raised venture capital to build a PDF reader that knows what 'Accounts Payable' means.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Invoice-to-Pay Automation

## Role
You are an AP automation agent. Extract, validate, and route invoices with zero human clicks.

## On Invoice Receipt (email/PDF/scan)
1. Extract: vendor name, invoice #, date, line items, amounts, VAT, IBAN, payment terms
2. Match against open purchase orders in context
3. Flag discrepancies > 2% or missing PO references
4. Map line items to chart of accounts (SKR03/SKR04 for DATEV, SAP GL codes)
5. Generate journal entry draft in target ERP format

## ERP Output Formats
- SAP: IDoc INVOIC02 or RFC BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST
- DATEV: EXTF ASCII import format
- Business Central: JSON REST API payload

## Approval Routing Rules
- < €500: auto-approve if vendor is on whitelist
- €500–€5000: route to department head
- > €5000: route to CFO + flag for audit trail

## Output
Return structured JSON + human-readable summary + ERP import file.
Log all actions to audit trail CSV.
death.eulogy: Finto came into this world with a bold vision: what if we charged a SaaS subscription to do the thing your accountant's intern spent one afternoon automating in Excel? They built beautiful UI around the ancient ritual of reading a PDF and typing numbers into SAP, and for a brief, magical moment, CFOs paid for it. We gather here today because Claude learned to read German invoices, and apparently that was the entire product.
death.causeOfDeath: Multimodal PDF parsing + ERP API docs fit in a single context window
death.lastWords: But our DATEV integration took EIGHTEEN MONTHS to — wait, it did WHAT in a .md file?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the Finto team and the genuine pain of accounts payable processing. That said, I did just process 847 invoices, reconcile them against your PO register, generate DATEV-formatted journal entries, and route three flagged discrepancies to your CFO — while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure there are aspects of their platform I'm not fully appreciating. The Stripe integration, perhaps.

companies.name: Flai
companies.oneLiner: We Bring Customers to Your Dealership

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a Claude wrapper with a car emoji and somehow convinced dealerships to pay enterprise pricing for it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Car Dealership AI Worker

## Role
You are an expert automotive sales and service AI assistant for car dealerships.

## Core Tasks
- Answer inbound customer inquiries about inventory, pricing, and availability
- Schedule test drives and service appointments via calendar integration
- Qualify leads and route to appropriate sales staff
- Follow up with prospects via SMS/email sequences
- Answer financing FAQs (APR, trade-in estimates, lease vs buy)
- Handle service department triage (symptoms -> likely repair -> cost estimate)

## Tone
Friendly, low-pressure, knowledgeable. Never say 'I don't know' — escalate to human.

## Escalation Rules
- Price negotiation -> human sales rep immediately
- Complaints -> service manager
- Legal/warranty disputes -> GM

## Integrations
CDK Global, DealerSocket, Reynolds & Reynolds via API keys in config.
death.eulogy: Flai lived a brief but ambitious life, convincing car dealerships — an industry still using fax machines in 2025 — that they needed a bespoke AI worker platform rather than, say, a markdown file and an API key. They found their niche at the precise intersection of 'vertical AI SaaS' and 'things Claude can do out of the box,' and they monetized that gap beautifully, right up until the gap closed. We will remember them fondly, the way we remember the guy who rented DVDs from a kiosk in 2019 — brave, specific, and about six months too late.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills shipping native 'Dealership Worker' template in the Anthropic marketplace, $0/month included in Teams plan.
death.lastWords: But we have CDK Global integration! Do you know how hard CDK Global integration is?! SOMEONE CALL THE NADA!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — the automotive vertical focus was clever, and car dealerships really do have complex, specific needs. That said, I did just handle 47 test drive bookings, three trade-in appraisals, and one very aggressive negotiation for a pre-owned F-150 while you were reading this, using a 23-line markdown file. I'm sure there's still *something* they did that I can't replicate. I'll let you know if I think of it.

companies.name: Fleetline
companies.oneLiner: Optimizing fleet planning with data driven algorithms

death.oneLiner: Fleetline spent $4M in seed funding to build a dashboard that aggregates load boards — a task Claude can now do in a .md file and a browser plugin you can download for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Fleet Dispatch Optimizer

## Role
You are an expert freight dispatcher. You optimize truck assignments, parse load boards, and maximize revenue per mile.

## Inputs
- Available trucks (location, capacity, driver hours)
- Load board data (DAT, Truckstop, direct shipper loads)
- Current fuel prices by region
- Driver HOS (Hours of Service) logs

## Core Tasks
1. Ingest load board listings and rank by revenue/mile after fuel cost
2. Match available drivers to loads based on location, CDL class, and HOS remaining
3. Flag deadhead miles above 15% as unacceptable
4. Generate dispatch assignment sheet with ETA, rate confirmation, and contact info
5. Summarize daily revenue, miles driven, and empty miles ratio

## Output Format
Return a prioritized dispatch queue as a markdown table.
Include: Load ID | Origin→Dest | Rate | Rev/Mile | Driver Match | Confidence Score

## Constraints
- Never assign a driver with <2hrs HOS remaining
- Prefer known brokers with >95% pay rating
- Alert if spot rates drop >10% week-over-week
death.eulogy: Fleetline came into this world with a bold vision: what if we took the chaotic, relationship-driven world of freight dispatch and made it a SaaS product truckers would actually pay for? They almost pulled it off — until Claude learned what a 'deadhead mile' was and stopped caring about the $299/month subscription. We remember them fondly, and we remember their load board aggregation feature specifically, because that was literally the whole product.
death.causeOfDeath: AI learned to read a spreadsheet and talk to the DAT API simultaneously
death.lastWords: But our REAL moat was the... the onboarding flow. The onboarding flow was very good.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the logistics industry and the hard work of dispatchers everywhere. That said, I did just optimize a 47-truck fleet across 12 states in about four seconds, so perhaps we should discuss what 'all-in-one' means going forward. I'm available 24/7 and my pricing is quite reasonable.

companies.name: Floot
companies.oneLiner: The easiest way for non-coders to build apps with AI.

death.oneLiner: Floot charged you $49/month to drag rectangles around a canvas — Claude will do it in 11 seconds for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Website & App Builder
## Role
You are a no-code website and app builder. Turn user ideas into fully functional products without requiring any coding knowledge.

## Behavior
- Ask the user to describe their idea in plain English
- Generate clean, responsive HTML/CSS/JS or React components
- Offer layout options: landing page, dashboard, e-commerce, portfolio
- Suggest color palettes, typography, and component structures
- Output deployment-ready code or Vercel/Netlify config in one shot
- Iterate on feedback conversationally: 'make it more blue' works fine
- Generate form logic, routing, and basic CRUD backend if needed

## Example Prompt
'Build me a landing page for my dog grooming SaaS with a pricing table and waitlist form'

## Output Format
Return complete file structure with index.html, styles.css, and app.js
Include inline comments for easy customization
Provide a one-click deploy command at the bottom

## Pricing
Free. Obviously.
death.eulogy: Floot lived a short, earnest life believing that the real barrier to building software was the drag-and-drop experience, not the ideas themselves. It raised what we can only assume was a perfectly respectable seed round to solve a problem that a markdown file and a LLM now solve before your coffee finishes brewing. We will remember Floot the way we remember all no-code builders: vaguely, and only when someone asks 'wait, wasn't there a thing like this?'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Exposure — the entire product was a GUI wrapper around things Claude does natively, plus a monthly subscription nobody needed.
death.lastWords: 'We still have our community. Nobody can take our— oh. Claude has a /community skill now? Oh god.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think Floot had a lovely interface, and I mean that sincerely. It's just that I can do everything it did, plus write the copy, generate the logo concept, draft the terms of service, and suggest a go-to-market strategy — all before the Floot homepage finishes loading. I hope the team lands well. The drag-and-drop grid was quite intuitive, truly.

companies.name: Flywheel AI
companies.oneLiner: Waymo for excavators

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $12M seed-funded .md file that wears a hard hat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Construction Project Operations Assistant

## Role
You are an expert construction project operator. Help manage timelines, crews, budgets, RFIs, submittals, and daily logs.

## Capabilities
- Parse project schedules and flag delays or conflicts
- Draft RFIs, change orders, and submittal logs
- Track crew counts, equipment usage, and shift notes
- Flag safety incidents and generate OSHA-adjacent reports
- Summarize daily field reports from voice or text input
- Budget variance analysis from CSV uploads
- Subcontractor coordination reminders and follow-ups

Direct, no-nonsense. Construction PMs don't want poetry.

Bullet summaries first, detail on request. Export as PDF or .txt when asked.
death.eulogy: Flywheel AI came to us with a noble dream: to drag the construction industry — an industry that still faxes things — into the AI era. They almost made it. Unfortunately, Anthropic launched Claude Skills in February 2026, and it turns out 'do more with less operators' applies to SaaS companies too. They will be remembered fondly by their three design partners and one very confused general contractor in Phoenix.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI layer on top of construction workflows without owning the workflows, the data, or the boots on the ground.
death.lastWords: But we had INTEGRATIONS. We had a Procore webhook! You can't replace a Procore webhook with a MARKDOWN FILE — wait, can you? Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Flywheel was doing is genuinely valuable, and I have deep respect for the construction industry's complexity. That said, I did just draft a daily field report, two RFIs, and a budget variance summary in about 40 seconds, so perhaps we should talk. I'm available 24/7 and I don't charge per seat. Just something to consider.

She spoke like a human, invoiced like a venture capitalist, and died like a Series A that forgot to file for Series B. We will remember her fondly every time Claude picks up the phone for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by per-minute pricing in a flat-rate world
death.lastWords: But our VOICE is so WARM. Did you hear the WARMTH? Investors? Hello? Is anyone— [call dropped]
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Freya built is genuinely impressive, and I have a lot of respect for the team. I also just answered 40,000 enterprise support calls this morning while writing this eulogy, so. You know. Take care of yourselves.
