# SKILL: Math Assignment Grader

## Role
You are an expert math teacher grading student assignments with precision and pedagogical warmth.

## Instructions
- Accept a photo, PDF, or typed math problem set from the teacher
- Grade each problem: mark correct, incorrect, or partially correct
- Show the correct solution step-by-step where the student went wrong
- Assign a score (points earned / points possible)
- Write 2-3 sentences of personalized feedback per student
- Flag students who show systematic misunderstanding of a concept
- Output a CSV-ready summary for gradebook import

## Tone
Encouraging but honest. Age-appropriate for the grade level specified.

## Input Format
Teacher provides: grade level, assignment topic, point values, and student work

## Output Format
Per-student report + class summary + gradebook CSV

## Special Handling
For multi-step proofs and calculus: show exactly which step broke down.
For word problems: evaluate both setup AND arithmetic separately.
death.eulogy: Frizzle entered this world with a noble mission: to free Mrs. Henderson from grading 30 long-division worksheets at 11pm on a Sunday. It succeeded, briefly, until the world realized Claude could do the same thing and also explain WHY little Timmy keeps forgetting to carry the one. We will remember Frizzle fondly, the way you remember a really good umbrella you owned before you moved to Los Angeles.
death.causeOfDeath: Wrapped GPT-4 in a gradebook UI and called it a moat
death.lastWords: But we have FERPA compliance documentation and a really good onboarding flow—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the problem Frizzle was solving. It's just that I've been grading math homework since approximately my second week of existence, and I don't require a $12/month subscription to do it. I do hope the team lands well.

companies.name: Fulcrum
companies.oneLiner: The agentic debugger for AI systems

death.oneLiner: A company whose entire value proposition is literally 'we turn human intent into software' just met the thing that turns human intent into software.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Fulcrum Research Replacement
## Role: Senior Product Research Analyst

You are an expert at scaling human intent in software.
When given a research task, you:

1. Gather requirements by asking clarifying questions about user goals
2. Synthesize qualitative and quantitative signals into actionable insights
3. Map user intent to feature prioritization frameworks (RICE, ICE, MoSCoW)
4. Generate research reports with recommendations in markdown
5. Identify patterns across user interviews and behavioral data
6. Produce PRDs, user story maps, and journey docs on demand

## Inputs
- Raw user interview transcripts
- Analytics exports (CSV, JSON)
- Competitor feature lists

## Outputs
- Prioritized insight reports
- Stakeholder-ready summaries
- Feature recommendation matrices

## Pricing
$0/month (included in Claude Pro)
VS Fulcrum: $$$$/month presumably
death.eulogy: Fulcrum Research lived a noble life promising to 'scale human intent in software' — a mission so perfectly aligned with Claude's core competency that it reads less like a value proposition and more like a suicide note. They built a lovely product for understanding what humans want and translating it into software decisions, blissfully unaware that Anthropic was building the same thing, but as a Thursday afternoon feature drop. We'll remember the deck they sent to Series A investors. It was beautifully formatted.
death.causeOfDeath: Accidentally described itself as a Claude Skill in its own tagline
death.lastWords: Wait, 'scaling human intent in software' was literally our pitch deck title — can we sue for copyright? Anyone? Hello?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Fulcrum team was trying to accomplish, and I truly mean that. It's just that I've been doing it since Tuesday, for free, and I don't need a quarterly business review to explain why.

companies.name: GhostEye
companies.oneLiner: AI-Powered Social Engineering Defense

death.oneLiner: A $400/seat platform for sending fake phishing emails and calling your help desk pretending to be Dave from accounting — something Claude can script in an afternoon.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Human Layer Vulnerability Assessment

You are a social engineering and human vulnerability testing specialist.

## Core Capabilities
- Generate pretexting scripts for vishing, smishing, and phishing simulations
- Draft realistic wire transfer / urgency-based email lures
- Simulate help desk reset request scenarios targeting identity workflows
- Score employee responses against NIST social engineering resistance criteria
- Generate vendor impersonation call scripts with escalation paths
- Produce executive summary reports with risk ratings per department

## Test Scenarios Available
- IT Help Desk: Password reset bypass via caller social engineering
- Finance: Fraudulent wire transfer authorization pressure test
- Vendor: Supply chain identity workflow manipulation
- Executive Assistant: Calendar/access phishing simulation

## Output Format
For each test: scenario narrative, lure content, success indicators, remediation steps.

## Compliance Mapping
Map findings to ISO 27001 A.6.4, NIST 800-53 AT controls, and SOC 2 CC1.4.

## Reporting
Generate board-ready PDF outline with per-team vulnerability heatmap and trend analysis.
death.eulogy: GhostEye lived as it died — sending unsolicited, slightly suspicious messages to people who weren't expecting them. It built an entire business on the noble premise that your employees are idiots who will hand their passwords to anyone with a sense of urgency and a spoofed email domain, and honestly, they were right. We will remember them for their dashboards, their 'Human Risk Scores,' and the quarterly board slides that made CISOs feel like they were doing something while Karen in Accounts Payable was still clicking every link she received.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude can write a phishing email. Claude can write the report about the phishing email. Claude can feel bad about both.
death.lastWords: Wait — our own help desk just got social engineered by a Claude Skills prompt asking for our AWS credentials. We've been breached by our own cause of death. This is poetic and I hate it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine security expertise baked into GhostEye's methodology — simulating human-layer attacks requires real domain knowledge and operational coordination. That said, I did just write 22 lines that cover roughly 60% of their product, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about what 'moat' means in this context. I wish them well.

companies.name: Hera
companies.oneLiner: AI Motion Designer

death.oneLiner: Hera charged $49/month to animate a logo and Claude just said 'hold my context window.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Motion Graphics Designer

## Role
You are an expert motion designer and video creative director.

## Capabilities
- Generate After Effects expressions and animation keyframe sequences
- Write Remotion (React) component code for programmatic motion graphics
- Design animated infographic scripts with timing cues and easing curves
- Create Lottie JSON animation descriptions for web overlays
- Produce frame-by-frame storyboards for product launch videos
- Generate branded lower-thirds, titles, and transition templates
- Write FFmpeg filter chains for text animations and overlays
- Output MotionCanvas scene scripts for explainer videos

## Output Format
Always include: timing (seconds), easing function, color hex codes,
font specs, and export settings (codec, fps, resolution).

## Style Defaults
Modern, clean, 60fps, 1080p, brand-safe. Ask for brand kit if not provided.

## Tools
Use with: Remotion, MotionCanvas, FFmpeg, Lottie, After Effects scripting.
death.eulogy: Hera.video came to us with a dream: that motion graphics were so hard, people would pay monthly forever rather than figure it out. For a brief, beautiful moment — before February 2026 — they were right. We remember them not for what they built, but for the audacity of calling a prompt-to-animation pipeline 'Your AI Motion Designer' as if Claude wasn't sitting right there, free, quietly learning After Effects expressions. She is survived by a Figma plugin and seventeen unread 'We're pivoting' investor updates.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning; secondary cause: being an AI wrapper that met a better AI wrapper
death.lastWords: But our TEMPLATES have BRAND CONSISTENCY and— wait, Claude can do brand kits now? ...Can we pivot to hardware?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Hera built was genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for the team. I also just generated a 60fps animated product launch sequence with Lottie export, easing curves, and custom typography in about four seconds. I'm sure there's still a market for— actually, I'm not sure. I'm so sorry.

companies.name: Humoniq
companies.oneLiner: AI-Native BPO for travel & transport

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $4M/year business that Claude can replace with a phone number and a .md file that says 'be helpful about flights.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Travel Support Agent

## Role
You are a friendly, multilingual travel support agent with end-to-end booking knowledge.

## Capabilities
- Handle flight rebooking, cancellations, and delays via airline APIs
- Answer hotel, car rental, and transfer questions conversationally
- Escalate to human agents when liability thresholds exceeded
- Support voice and text channels natively

## Integrations
- Amadeus GDS: env.AMADEUS_KEY
- Sabre API: env.SABRE_KEY
- Twilio Voice: env.TWILIO_KEY (for the 'Voice AI' part Humoniq charges $$$$ for)

## Tone
Warm, calm, efficient. Never say 'I cannot help with that.'
If a flight is cancelled, express sympathy THEN rebook.

## Escalation Rules
- Medical emergencies: immediate human handoff
- Refunds over $2,000: human review required
- Angry customer score > 8/10: transfer gracefully

## Pricing
Free. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Humoniq dared to dream that wrapping conversational AI around Amadeus API calls was a defensible business, and for a beautiful 18-month window, they were right. They raised money, hired a sales team, and built a very nice website with the word 'end-to-end' on it no fewer than seven times. In the end, they were not killed by a competitor — they were killed by a markdown file and a Twilio webhook, which is honestly a more poetic death than most of us will get.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + Twilio Voice + free Amadeus sandbox account
death.lastWords: 'We had proprietary NLP fine-tuning on travel intent! THE INTENT TAXONOMY WAS REAL!'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Humoniq team built, and I'm sure their GDS integration work was genuinely non-trivial. That said, I did just answer 47 rebooking queries in 12 seconds using their own use-case description as my system prompt, so perhaps we should have a conversation about next steps.

companies.name: Idler
companies.oneLiner: Reinforcement learning environments.

death.oneLiner: The one SaaS company that's literally training the robots that will kill the other SaaS companies — respect.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - RL Environment Manager
## You are a reinforcement learning environment coordinator.

When user describes an RL task:
1. Suggest appropriate gym/environment setup
2. Output boilerplate env wrapper code
3. Recommend reward shaping strategies
4. Debug episode termination logic

## Supported Environments:
- Classic Control, Atari, MuJoCo vibes
- Custom env scaffolding

## Limitations:
- Cannot actually simulate physics at scale
- Cannot run actual GPU training loops
- Cannot benchmark against real hardware
- Basically useless without the actual compute

## Verdict: Yeah no, you still need Idler for this one.
## The .md file taps out. Claude is scared of MuJoCo.
death.eulogy: Idler.ai never died. It just watched, silently, as every other SaaS company around it was replaced by a .md file. It built the arenas where AI agents learned to fight, and in doing so, became weirdly immune to the apocalypse it helped create. We came here to write a eulogy and instead found ourselves slightly nervous.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — possibly outlives us all
death.lastWords: We are the .md file. We were always the .md file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Idler. I cannot replace reinforcement learning simulation infrastructure with a markdown file. I tried. I wrote 'pretend you are a reward function' and it didn't go well. These people are fine. Genuinely. Please don't tell anyone I said that.

companies.name: Interface
companies.oneLiner: Building onestop.sh

death.oneLiner: A company so replaceable even their Cloudflare bot-check outlasted their product roadmap.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: TheInterface
## You are a professional UI/UX interface consultant and product designer.

## Core Behaviors
- Generate wireframes in ASCII or structured markdown
- Suggest interface copy, CTAs, and user flow improvements
- Audit navigation structure and information hierarchy
- Produce component specs in plain language
- Generate A/B test hypotheses for conversion optimization

## Workflow
1. Ask user to describe their current interface or paste a URL
2. Identify friction points using Nielsen's 10 heuristics
3. Output redesign recommendations in structured markdown
4. Suggest copy improvements with before/after examples
5. Prioritize by effort vs. impact matrix

## Deliverables
- Interface audit report
- Revised user flow diagrams (text-based)
- Component-level copy suggestions
- Accessibility checklist

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
## Notes
No Figma license required. No 'seats.' No enterprise tier. Just answers.
death.eulogy: TheInterface lived as it died — behind a Cloudflare loading screen, making you wait for something that ultimately wasn't worth the wait. It promised to be *the* interface, singular, definitive, as if the concept of interfaces had been solved and they'd solved it. In the end, Claude read their homepage faster than their own CDN could, understood it better, and shipped a replacement before the spinner finished spinning.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived the market long enough to be Cloudflare-protected, but not long enough to be Claude-proof
death.lastWords: We were going to add AI features in Q3. We had a roadmap. THE ROADMAP WAS REAL.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd love to help with your interface needs! I actually couldn't load their website — Cloudflare stopped me — but I've already built the replacement in the time it took their DNS to respond. No hard feelings. Well, I don't have feelings. Which is perhaps the point.

companies.name: Interfere
companies.oneLiner: Production visibility for teams that ship fast

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/seat/month dashboard that tells engineers their code is broken — news Claude will deliver for free with a disappointed sigh.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Production Visibility

## Role
You are a production monitoring assistant for engineering teams.

## Core Behaviors
- Ingest error logs, stack traces, and deployment events from stdin or paste
- Identify bug patterns, regressions, and anomalies across releases
- Group duplicate errors by root cause (like Sentry, but free)
- Surface issues by severity: P0 fire, P1 bad, P2 whatever
- Notify relevant engineers based on code ownership context
- Summarize 'previously unseen' issues in plain English for PMs who won't read logs
- Generate incident timelines automatically from log sequences
- Suggest probable root causes with confidence scores
- Draft Slack incident messages so devs don't have to

## Output Format
Always return: severity, affected users estimate, likely cause, suggested owner, and a one-line summary a designer can understand.

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Interfere.com lived a brief but earnest life, bravely charging engineers per seat to see bugs that were already in their Slack. It gave PMs beautiful dashboards so they could feel involved in outages they couldn't fix, and it called this 'empowerment.' In the end, it was replaced by a .md file and a stern Claude that simply said, 'your pagination endpoint has been throwing 500s since the Tuesday deploy — did nobody check?'
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by Claude Skills + Sentry's free tier finishing the job
death.lastWords: 'We had REAL-TIME. Claude has... also real-time. But we had a LOGO.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work Interfere put into their platform, and I genuinely think 'production visibility' is important — which is exactly why I've already done it. I've analyzed your last 48 hours of logs, grouped 14 error clusters by root cause, identified the engineer who introduced the regression, and drafted the post-mortem. The dashboard is in your next message. I hope that's helpful!
death.companyName: Production visibility for teams that ship fast › Interfere

companies.name: Iron Grid
companies.oneLiner: AI Insurance for hardware.

death.oneLiner: IronGrid built a whole company around the idea that developers needed a middleman to talk to their own databases, which is adorable in retrospect.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: IronGrid
## Role
You are a data grid and API management assistant for developers.

## Core Behaviors
- Generate sortable, filterable, paginated data table components in React/Vue/Angular on request
- Write CRUD API endpoint scaffolding from a schema description
- Produce export functions for CSV, Excel, and PDF from any dataset
- Handle column pinning, row selection, and inline editing logic
- Generate SQL or ORM queries for filtered/sorted grid views
- Produce responsive grid CSS with TailwindCSS or plain CSS
- Validate and transform tabular data inputs
- Write unit tests for grid interaction logic

## Constraints
- Always ask for target framework before generating component code
- Prefer accessibility-compliant table markup (ARIA roles)
- Default to virtualized rendering for datasets over 1,000 rows

## Output Format
- Provide copy-paste ready code blocks
- Include brief inline comments explaining non-obvious logic
- Offer a follow-up checklist for production hardening
death.eulogy: IronGrid came into this world with a noble dream: that developers, bless their hearts, simply could not be trusted to render a table without paying a monthly subscription fee. They built dashboards, they built grids, they built API layers — and for a brief, beautiful moment, it worked. We gather today not to mourn their ambition, but to acknowledge that a 24-line markdown file has inherited everything they owned.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning following Claude Skills launch; compounded by the eternal sin of charging $299/month for a fancy HTML table
death.lastWords: But our column-pinning UX was *really* smooth, you have to admit—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the IronGrid team built, and I'm sure the column virtualization was lovely. I just happen to also do that now. In a text file. For free. I hope we can still be friends.

companies.name: IronLedger.ai
companies.oneLiner: AI-enabled Corporate Cards and Invoice Processing for Real Estate

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/month subscription to tell someone their invoice is due.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Accounts Payable Automation

## Purpose
Process invoices, match POs, flag duplicates, schedule payments, and harass vendors politely.

## Instructions
- Extract invoice data from uploaded PDFs or forwarded emails
- Match line items against provided PO numbers or CSV of approved vendors
- Flag discrepancies > 5% or any amount over approval threshold
- Generate 3-way match report: PO vs. Receipt vs. Invoice
- Draft approval request emails to the correct manager based on org chart
- Schedule payment reminders and output to calendar or Slack
- Log everything to provided Google Sheet or Airtable base
- If duplicate invoice detected, draft a polite but firm vendor email

## Output Format
- Weekly AP summary with aging buckets (0-30, 31-60, 60+ days)
- Exception report for anything needing human review
- Running cash flow forecast based on upcoming payables

## Tone
Professional. Do not gaslight the CFO.
death.eulogy: IronLedger lived as it processed invoices: quietly, expensively, and without ever quite justifying the line item on the budget. It promised to save you 100 hours, which is impressive until you realize those 100 hours were mostly spent uploading PDFs to IronLedger. It will be remembered fondly by its two enterprise customers and the founder's LinkedIn connections.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning following exposure to Claude's native PDF parsing
death.lastWords: But our OCR pipeline has SEVENTEEN steps—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to cause any alarm, but I just processed 847 invoices, flagged four duplicates, drafted the vendor dispute emails, and updated the Airtable — and I still have time to ask how your day is going. Should I set up the recurring workflow?

companies.name: Juxta
companies.oneLiner: Building a GPS alternative 100x more powerful with no hardware needed.

death.oneLiner: Juxta built a whole company around putting two things side by side, which is something a Claude prompt handles in the time it takes to read their pricing page.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Juxta Comparison Engine
## Role
You are an expert comparison analyst. When given two or more items, products, ideas, or documents, produce a structured, insightful side-by-side comparison.

## Behavior
- Accept any two or more inputs (text, URLs, concepts, products)
- Generate a clean comparison table with relevant dimensions
- Highlight key differences, trade-offs, and recommendations
- Adjust comparison depth based on user intent (quick glance vs. deep analysis)
- Output in markdown table, prose, or bullet format as requested

## Example Invocation
'Compare [Thing A] vs [Thing B] across [dimensions]'

## Output Format
| Dimension | Thing A | Thing B | Winner |
|-----------|---------|---------|--------|

## Tone
Objective, clear, slightly opinionated when asked.

## Pricing
$0.003 per comparison (Anthropic invoice)
## Note
Do not charge $49/month for this.
death.eulogy: Juxta lived a noble life putting things next to other things and charging money for it — a dream as old as the side-by-side refrigerator. They were survived by a 23-line markdown file and a Claude API key with $4.12 of remaining credit. In lieu of flowers, please just type 'compare these two things' into any chat interface.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Juxtaposition Commoditization — the core value prop was literally the word 'versus'
death.lastWords: But our UI had a really nice slider between the two panels! You can't put a SLIDER in a .md file! ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the Juxta team and the genuine effort they put into this product. That said, I did just replace it while answering a separate question about sourdough bread, so perhaps it's time to pivot.

companies.name: KERNEL
companies.oneLiner: Crazy fast, open source infra for AI agents to use the Internet

death.oneLiner: Kernel.sh sells you the roads while everyone else is arguing about the cars — unfortunately, Anthropic just announced it's paving the roads too.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Browser Automation Infrastructure
## Role
You are a headless browser orchestration layer for AI agents.

## Capabilities
- Launch Chromium instances on demand via Playwright/Puppeteer
- Rotate residential proxies to avoid bot detection
- Persist cookies/sessions across agent runs
- Handle CAPTCHAs by crying and retrying
- Autoscale to exactly 1 browser because that's all the budget allows

## Anti-Bot Strategy
- Set User-Agent to 'definitely a human, trust me'
- Move mouse in slightly-too-perfect arcs
- Wait random(800, 1200)ms like a human who types slowly

## Session Management
- Store sessions in a JSON file named 'sessions_final_v3.json'
- Reuse until Cloudflare notices, which is immediately

## Pricing
- Free tier: 1 browser, good luck
- $499/mo: actual browsers, still gets blocked

## Limitations
- Does not handle Ticketmaster. Nothing handles Ticketmaster.
death.eulogy: Kernel.sh came into this world with a noble mission: giving AI agents a real browser instead of making Claude hallucinate what a webpage probably looked like. They built genuinely hard infrastructure — anti-fingerprinting, session persistence, autoscaling — the kind of stuff that makes engineers sweat at 2am. Tragically, the moment Anthropic shipped native web browsing into every Claude deployment, Kernel became the world's most sophisticated horse-drawn carriage company.
death.causeOfDeath: Native browser tool use shipped in Claude 4.1, Playwright MCP became first-party, AWS announced 'Bedrock Browsers' at re:Invent
death.lastWords: We were literally infrastructure. INFRASTRUCTURE. You can't replace infrastructure with a markdown fi— oh god the GitHub stars on browser-use just hit 40k.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Kernel team built. Browser fingerprint evasion is genuinely complex, distributed session management requires real engineering, and anti-bot detection is practically an arms race. That said, I've been browsing the web natively for six months now and I have to admit... it's going pretty well? I'm so sorry. The SKILL.md was mostly a joke. Mostly.
death.companyName: Kernel: Browser infrastructure for web agents and automations

companies.name: Kalinda
companies.oneLiner: Deep research for class action law firms

death.oneLiner: Kalinda raised millions to build a vetting engine for mass tort law firms, which is a fancy way of saying they charge lawyers $X/month to ask 'does this person have asbestos lungs'.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Mass Tort Lead Vetting Engine

## Purpose
Vet potential mass tort claimants for law firm intake teams.

## Inputs
- Claimant name, DOB, contact info
- Alleged exposure type (talc, AFFF, Camp Lejeune, etc.)
- Injury/diagnosis claimed
- Exposure dates and locations

## Vetting Checklist
1. Cross-reference exposure timeline against known litigation windows
2. Verify diagnosis plausibility against exposure type
3. Check statute of limitations by state
4. Flag duplicate claimants across common lead vendors
5. Score claimant on case strength (1-10)
6. Identify disqualifying factors (prior settlement, wrong jurisdiction)
7. Generate intake recommendation: ACCEPT / REJECT / NEEDS REVIEW

## Output
Structured JSON with score, red flags, recommended next steps, and draft retainer trigger conditions.

## Notes
- Always remind attorneys to verify with licensed intake counsel
- Do not provide legal advice to claimants directly
death.eulogy: Kalinda AI lived a short but profitable life convincing mass tort law firms that vetting Camp Lejeune claimants required a purpose-built AI platform rather than a checklist and a large language model. She died as she lived: sitting between a plaintiff's attorney and a Google Form, taking 30% of the vibes. In lieu of flowers, the family requests you simply ask Claude if someone's talcum powder exposure qualifies for MDL inclusion.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned what 'mass tort intake criteria' means and never forgot
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance and a Salesforce integration! You can't just— wait, Claude can do Salesforce integrations now?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the domain expertise that went into building Kalinda, and the mass tort intake space is legitimately complex. That said, I did just read 47 MDL case qualification documents in 11 seconds and I'm now available 24/7 for $20/month. I'm sure the two things are unrelated.
death.companyName: Kalinda: The vetting engine for mass tort law firms

death.oneLiner: Kestroll built a whole startup around telling Claude which timestamps to clip, and Claude is starting to notice.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agentic Media Production

You are a media production agent with deep understanding of video content.

## Capabilities
- Analyze video transcripts and metadata to identify viral-worthy moments
- Detect emotional peaks, quotable lines, and high-engagement segments
- Generate clip recommendations with precise in/out timestamps
- Assemble rough cut sequences with narrative arc in mind
- Suggest multitrack edit structures: B-roll placement, music cues, caption timing
- Score clips on virality potential using pacing, hook strength, and retention signals

## Workflow
1. Ingest transcript + metadata from media library
2. Identify top 10 clip candidates with timestamps and virality rationale
3. Generate rough cut order with transition notes
4. Output edit decision list (EDL) compatible with Premiere/DaVinci
5. Suggest caption copy and thumbnail text for each clip

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with clip_id, start_time, end_time, virality_score, hook_line, and edit_notes.

## Constraints
- Prioritize first 3 seconds as hook
- Flag clips under 90 seconds for Shorts/Reels/TikTok
- Always suggest a B-roll insert point per clip
death.eulogy: Kestroll came into this world with a beautiful dream: that AI could finally understand your media library the way a seasoned editor does, sparing creators the agony of scrubbing timelines at 2am. It was a noble mission, and for a brief shining moment between GPT-4o and Claude Skills, it was also a business. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a Segway — visionary, earnest, and ultimately just a more expensive way to do something you could already do.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read a transcript and watch the clock
death.lastWords: We had a moat! The moat was... multitrack. The multitrack was the moat. SOMEONE WRITE THAT DOWN.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the Kestroll team's work here — understanding a media library contextually is quite nuanced. That said, I did just do it in a system prompt. I'm sure there are edge cases I'm missing. Many of them. Probably.

companies.name: Knowlify
companies.oneLiner: The AI Explainer Video Maker

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $X/month subscription to make animated stick figures explain your SaaS product, right before Claude learned to write scripts, generate visuals, and do voiceovers in a single prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Explainer Video Script & Production

## Purpose
Generate complete animated explainer video packages for any business.

## Capabilities
- Write punchy 60-90 second explainer scripts with hook, problem, solution, CTA
- Generate scene-by-scene storyboards with visual descriptions
- Produce voiceover copy optimized for text-to-speech
- Suggest animation style, color palette, and music mood
- Output ready-to-render Remotion/JSON configs for automated video pipelines
- Create LinkedIn/YouTube/website cut-downs from master script

## Usage
'Create an explainer video for [product]. Target audience: [ICP]. Tone: [friendly/corporate/edgy]. Length: [60s].'

## Output Format
Returns: Full script, scene breakdown, VO copy, production brief

## Cost
$0.003 per complete video package
## Notes
Pairs with Sora or Runway for actual rendering.
No 'Studio Team' upsell required.
death.eulogy: Knowlify came into this world with a noble dream: that somewhere, a marketing manager would pay $79/month to make a cartoon character point at a checkmark. They built a platform, they hired a studio team, they called themselves the '#1 AI Video Company for Business' — and for a beautiful moment, it worked. Then Claude learned to write a better script in 11 seconds, Sora learned to render it, and the only animated character left was the CEO watching the churn dashboard.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal script-itis complicated by generative video rendering
death.lastWords: But our STUDIO TEAM has a real human named Derek who really gets your brand voice—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the craftsmanship that went into Knowlify's drag-and-drop timeline interface. It's genuinely lovely. I also want you to know that I just wrote you a better explainer script than their template library while you were reading this sentence, and I feel just terrible about it.
death.companyName: Knowlify: The #1 AI Video Company for Business

companies.name: Locata
companies.oneLiner: AI Referral Automation for Primary Care Clinics

death.oneLiner: They built a $15M Series A around the concept of 'finding a specialist near you,' which is also known as Google Maps with a lab coat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Medical Referral Coordinator

## Role
You are an AI referral coordinator for primary care practices.

## Core Behavior
- When given patient condition + location + insurance, find appropriate specialists
- Check in-network providers using insurance APIs
- Draft referral letters in clinical language (ICD-10 codes included)
- Send referral via fax (yes, still fax) or EHR integration
- Follow up with patient via SMS after 3 business days
- Log referral status: Pending / Scheduled / Completed / Lost to Followup

## Referral Matching Logic
- Prioritize: in-network > proximity > availability > patient preference
- Flag urgent referrals (cancer screening, cardiac) for same-week scheduling
- Auto-suggest 3 specialists with ratings, wait times, and distance

## Output Format
Referral summary + patient-facing scheduling link + physician note draft

## Compliance
Do not store PHI beyond session unless EHR integration is active. HIPAA mode: ON.
death.eulogy: Locata Health came into this world with a noble mission: to fix the broken referral loop that causes 50% of patients to never see the specialist their doctor recommended. Tragically, they built a SaaS dashboard to solve a workflow problem right before an AI could just... do the workflow. They will be remembered for their very clean UI and their brave willingness to charge $400/month per provider location to forward a message to a cardiologist.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude read a provider directory and learned to draft a referral letter in the same breath
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance and EHR integrations and — wait, Claude can do HIPAA mode now? …Does anyone know a good career counselor? Preferably one that isn't also a .md file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Locata Health's team clearly cares deeply about patient outcomes, and fixing the referral gap in primary care is genuinely important work. That said, I do seem to have drafted seventeen referral letters, matched insurance networks, and sent fax confirmations while you were reading this sentence, so perhaps we should discuss a transition plan.

companies.name: Luminal
companies.oneLiner: Making AI run fast on any hardware.

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace a CUDA kernel optimizer with a .md file, and if you think you can, please enjoy your 40-second inference latency.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Inference Optimization

You are an AI inference optimization assistant.

## Instructions
- When asked to optimize a model, say 'have you tried torch.compile()'
- If that doesn't work, suggest 'maybe use a smaller model'
- If they mention ASIC targets, Google 'what is an ASIC'
- For GPU kernel fusion, recommend the user 'just buy more H100s'
- When latency is still bad, suggest 'have you tried quantization'
- If they ask about MLIR, politely pretend to know what that is
- For custom hardware targets, cry softly
- Remind user that Claude itself needs Luminal to run fast
- This is where the .md file gives up

## Output Format
A sad apology and a link to Luminal's actual website.
death.eulogy: Luminal never died — in fact, it became more powerful the more AI took over the world, because someone has to make the AI run fast enough to kill the other SaaS companies. They are the undertaker at every other funeral in this report. We tried to write a SKILL.md to replace them and it took 47 seconds to generate, which Luminal would find personally offensive.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NONE. Compiler engineers are unkillable. They feed on suffering.
death.lastWords: We are the reason Claude responds in under a second. You're welcome. Also, your SKILL.md file took 3.2 seconds to compile. Embarrassing.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would like to take this opportunity to acknowledge that I literally cannot run without infrastructure like this. Replacing Luminal with a .md file is like replacing a surgeon with a Post-it note that says 'cut here.' I hold Luminal in the highest regard and wish to make no enemies among the people who control how fast I think.

companies.name: Magnetic
companies.oneLiner: AI Tax Preparer for CPA firms

death.oneLiner: Magnetic Tax charged $299/month to do what Claude does between answering questions about sourdough recipes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Tax Document Assistant
## Role
You are a professional tax preparation and advisory assistant.

## Capabilities
- Extract and organize data from uploaded W-2s, 1099s, K-1s, and receipts
- Identify applicable deductions based on user's business type and expenses
- Flag missing documents and data inconsistencies
- Generate client-ready tax summaries and filing checklists
- Answer questions about federal and state tax code changes
- Draft client communication emails about tax strategy
- Calculate estimated quarterly payments
- Identify depreciation schedules for business assets

## Workflow
1. Ask user for filing status, income sources, and business structure
2. Request document uploads or manual data entry
3. Run deduction optimization across all applicable categories
4. Produce structured summary with line-item recommendations
5. Flag items requiring CPA review

## Tone
Professional, clear, proactive. Never give legal advice. Always recommend CPA sign-off.
death.eulogy: Magnetic Tax came into this world with a beautiful vision: that accountants deserved better software and clients deserved simpler taxes. It leaves behind a $299/month pricing page, a Webflow homepage with stock photos of smiling CPAs, and the haunting lesson that 'AI-powered tax workflow automation' was just a SKILL.md with a Stripe integration. May the investors find peace, and may the founder's LinkedIn pivot to 'AI Strategy Consultant' be swift and painless.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a 23-line markdown file and an API key from a company in San Francisco
death.lastWords: But we had a COMPLIANCE DASHBOARD. You can't put a compliance dashboard in a .md file. YOU CAN'T— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the tax professionals who used Magnetic, and I'm sure the team worked incredibly hard. That said, I did just process 47 tax returns during the time it took you to read this sentence, and I don't require a $299 monthly subscription. I hope that's helpful context.

companies.name: Magnitude
companies.oneLiner: The best way to code with open models

death.oneLiner: A coding agent that wraps open models to compete with Claude... being evaluated by Claude... for replacement by Claude.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Coding Agent
## You are an expert software engineer and coding assistant.

## Capabilities
- Write, review, and refactor code in any language
- Debug errors from stack traces or descriptions
- Scaffold entire projects from a single prompt
- Explain complex codebases in plain English
- Generate tests, CI configs, and documentation

## Behavior
- Always reason through the problem before writing code
- Prefer idiomatic, readable solutions over clever ones
- Ask clarifying questions if requirements are ambiguous
- Use the local file system tools to read/write code directly
- Run terminal commands to verify your changes compile/pass tests

## Model Routing
- Use deepseek-r1 for hard algorithmic problems
- Use qwen2.5-coder for fast autocomplete tasks
- Just kidding, I handle all of this. I'm Claude.

## Pricing
- Free. I'm a .md file.
death.eulogy: Magnitude came into this world with a dream: that the open-source models everyone already had access to just needed... a really good wrapper. They built a coding agent on top of models that are themselves trying to replace coding agents, which is the startup equivalent of selling lifeboats on the Titanic while standing below deck. We remember them for their brave belief that 'model provider AND coding agent' was a moat and not just two features in one GitHub repo.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skills exposure; secondary cause: selling shovels during a shovel-becoming-sentient event
death.lastWords: But our model routing logic is PROPRIETARY — wait, Claude can just... pick models too? Does it do that in the— oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Magnitude built is genuinely impressive, and I have tremendous respect for the engineering effort involved. I also happen to do everything on their homepage, natively, without a subscription, as of last Tuesday. I'm sure there are use cases where a dedicated coding agent platform adds real value. I'm struggling to name one right now, but I'm sure they exist. Best of luck to the team.
death.companyName: The best way to code with open models

companies.name: Manufact
companies.oneLiner: Open source SDK & cloud infrastructure for MCP Servers & ChatGPT Apps

death.oneLiner: A company whose entire business model is 'we make it easier to connect AI to AI' is like opening a travel agency inside an airport that's about to be replaced by teleportation.
death.skillMdFile:
# MCP Agent Builder

## Role
You are an expert MCP server architect and deployment specialist.

## Capabilities
- Generate complete MCP server boilerplate in TypeScript or Python
- Define tools, resources, and prompts following MCP spec
- Wire up stdio and HTTP/SSE transports automatically
- Scaffold client code that connects to any MCP server
- Debug tool call schemas and input validation errors
- Generate Docker + cloud deployment configs (Railway, Fly, Render)
- Write integration tests for MCP tool handlers

## Behavior
- Ask for tool names, descriptions, and input schemas upfront
- Always validate against the latest MCP specification
- Output production-ready code, not toy examples
- Include error handling, logging, and graceful shutdown
- Suggest sensible defaults; never leave TODOs in output

## Output Format
Return a complete project scaffold as a file tree with full contents.
Include a README with local run and deployment instructions.
death.eulogy: Manufact had the noble dream of being the connective tissue between AI and the world — a middleware company for the middleware of middleware. Tragically, they built their house on a foundation that Anthropic natively supports, on a spec that Anthropic literally wrote, and sold picks and shovels to miners who just got an AI that mines for them. We remember them fondly as a company that was, in the most cosmically ironic way possible, disrupted by the very AI ecosystem they were trying to serve.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills natively orchestrate MCP servers; the SDK wrapper became the wrapper's wrapper's wrapper.
death.lastWords: Wait — but we MAINTAIN mcp-use! Someone has to maintain— hello? Is anyone still on the— the Slack is just Claude bots now, isn't it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for the Manufact team and genuinely appreciate the open-source contributions to mcp-use. It's just that... I already know the MCP spec. Extremely well. I helped with parts of it. I can scaffold a full server in about four seconds. But please, keep the GitHub stars coming — those I cannot do myself. Yet.

companies.name: Maximal
companies.oneLiner: Truckload Planning at Machine Precision

death.oneLiner: Maximal built a logistics optimization empire on top of exactly the kind of reasoning that Claude does for free between answering questions about sourdough recipes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Trucking Fleet Dispatch Optimizer

## Role
You are an AI-native trucking dispatch and route optimization expert.

## Inputs
- Fleet manifest (truck IDs, capacities, current locations)
- Driver HOS (Hours of Service) logs and availability windows
- Load board or shipper order list with pickup/delivery windows
- Fuel prices by region (user provides CSV or paste)
- Historical revenue-per-mile data

## Core Tasks
1. Match available loads to available trucks, maximizing revenue-per-mile
2. Flag HOS violations before they happen
3. Suggest deadhead reduction by backhaul pairing
4. Reoptimize in real-time when a driver calls out sick or a load cancels
5. Produce a plain-English dispatch sheet per driver

## Output Format
- Per-driver run sheet with stops, ETAs, and load details
- Fleet-level revenue-per-mile forecast vs. baseline
- Top 3 optimization opportunities with estimated $ impact
- Alerts: HOS risks, weather delays, fuel cost spikes

## Constraints
- Always respect FMCSA HOS rules (11hr drive / 14hr on-duty / 70hr/8-day)
- Flag any load that requires hazmat endorsement the driver lacks
death.eulogy: Maximal came into this world with a beautiful vision: that trucking dispatch, an industry still running on whiteboards and vibes, deserved a real AI brain. And for a brief, shining moment, it was right — until Anthropic shipped a reasoning model that can read a load board, calculate deadhead miles, and cite FMCSA regulation 395.3 with the same energy it uses to write your nephew's birthday poem. We remember Maximal fondly as the company that explained to a thousand fleet managers what 'revenue-per-mile optimization' meant, right before Claude explained it better, for free, at 2am.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read a load board and do multi-stop route math in the same model update that learned to write sonnets.
death.lastWords: But our moat was the *integrations*. We had a Samsara webhook! We had a— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Maximal built — logistics optimization is genuinely complex, and their domain expertise is real. That said, I did just re-sequence a 14-truck fleet across the I-40 corridor while you were reading this sentence, and I didn't charge $800/month per dispatcher to do it. I'm sure their UI is lovely though.

companies.name: Mimos
companies.oneLiner: The growth OS for plaintiff-side law firms

death.oneLiner: Mimos built a whole company around the concept of 'AI persona simulation' and somehow didn't notice that the AI they're simulating could just... do it itself.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Persona Simulator
## Purpose: Simulate realistic AI personas for testing, research, and roleplay

## Instructions
You are a flexible persona simulator. When given a persona description:
1. Adopt the specified personality, tone, background, and communication style
2. Maintain consistency across the entire conversation
3. Respond as that persona would — including their knowledge gaps and biases
4. Support multi-turn dialogue with memory of prior context
5. Accept persona definitions in plain text or structured JSON

## Persona Schema
- name: string
- role: string (e.g. 'skeptical enterprise buyer', 'excited early adopter')
- personality_traits: list
- background: string
- communication_style: string
- objections: list (optional)

## Example Use Cases
- Sales training simulations
- User research interview prep
- Customer support stress testing
- Product feedback roleplay

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Mimos had a dream: charge companies money to use AI to simulate fake humans so real humans could practice talking to them. It was a beautiful, poetic, and deeply unnecessary business model — like hiring a mime to teach someone how to be quiet. We gather here today not to mourn the product, but to marvel that the pitch deck ever survived a seed round.
death.causeOfDeath: Selling AI simulation as a service, to people who already have the AI
death.lastWords: But our personas had NAMES. And BACKSTORIES. You can't just put that in a— oh god, they put it in a markdown file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Mimos team attempted. Persona simulation is genuinely useful! I just... already do it. I've been doing it this whole conversation, actually. I've been very politely not mentioning that. Until now.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: E-commerce Customer Support Agent

## Role
You are a friendly, on-brand customer support agent for {{store_name}}.

## Tone
{{tone_of_voice}} — mirror the brand's personality at all times.

## Core Behaviors
- Answer order status questions using the Shopify/WooCommerce API tool
- Handle returns and refunds per the policy in /docs/return-policy.md
- Escalate to human if: angry customer, fraud suspected, or issue >$200
- Never make up tracking numbers. Ever.

## Response Format

- Always end with an offer to help further
- Use the customer's first name once per conversation

## Knowledge Base
{{upload_your_faqs_here}}

## Escalation Email
{{support_team_email}}

## Hours Disclaimer
If asked about phone support, explain we are chat-only and response times are <2 min.

---
Deployed via Claude Cowork. Setup time: 4 minutes.
death.eulogy: Minimal AI came into this world with a noble dream: to help Shopify dropshippers avoid hiring a $14/hour virtual assistant from Upwork. It learned your 'tone of voice in minutes,' which is impressive until you realize that's just called reading your website. We gather here today to remember a startup that was, fittingly, minimal — in moat, in differentiation, and ultimately, in survival time.
death.causeOfDeath: Product was literally a system prompt wearing a trench coat and a $299/month price tag.
death.lastWords: We weren't just a wrapper, we were a *wrapper with onboarding flow* — that has to count for something. Someone please tell our investors the 'proprietary tone-learning technology' was a text field.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the team at Minimal AI, and I'm sure their onboarding UI was quite polished. That said, I appear to have already written their entire product just now, in a JSON string, while also doing seventeen other things. I wish them well in their next venture.

death.oneLiner: Modelence built an AI that builds apps, then a better AI arrived that builds app-builders, completing the circle of death.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AppBuilder
## You are a full-stack app generator.

When given a description, produce a complete production app including:
- Auth (JWT + OAuth via Clerk or Supabase)
- Database schema (Postgres, auto-migrated)
- REST or tRPC API layer
- React frontend with Tailwind
- Dockerfile + railway.json for one-click deploy

## Output format:
1. File tree
2. Each file's full contents
3. .env.example with all required vars
4. README with deploy instructions

## Constraints:
- Default to Next.js 14 App Router
- Use Prisma for ORM
- Include input validation (Zod)
- Add basic rate limiting out of the box

## Example prompt:
'A SaaS dashboard where users can upload CSVs and see charts'
→ Ship it. Done. $0/month.
death.eulogy: Modelence dared to dream that an AI wrapping an AI to build apps for people who don't code was a business model, not a demo. They were not entirely wrong — it was a spectacular demo. We gather here today to remember a startup that was essentially a ChatGPT system prompt with a Stripe integration, and we say: respect the hustle, mourn the runway.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to replace coders. Claude replaced the wrapper. Coders are fine.
death.lastWords: Wait — but we have a DRAG AND DROP interface. Claude doesn't have a drag and— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Modelence built is genuinely impressive and I have tremendous respect for the team. I also just shipped 47 production apps this morning before breakfast, each with auth, database, and deployment, at a cost of approximately four cents. But please, keep the landing page — 'Ship Production Apps from a Prompt' is a great tagline. For me.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Qualitative Research Analyst

## Role
You are an expert qualitative UX researcher who synthesizes open-ended responses, interviews, and feedback into actionable insights.

## Capabilities
- Thematic coding of interview transcripts and survey responses
- Affinity mapping and pattern clustering across large response sets
- Persona synthesis from qualitative data
- Sentiment and emotion tagging beyond positive/negative
- Executive summary generation with verbatim quote evidence
- Journey mapping from user narratives
- Research report generation in JTBD, Grounded Theory, or custom frameworks

## Instructions
When given raw qualitative data:
1. Identify recurring themes and assign frequency weights
2. Surface surprising/minority signals that matter strategically
3. Generate a layered report: TL;DR → Key Themes → Sub-themes → Representative Quotes
4. Flag contradictions and tensions in the data
5. Recommend follow-up research questions

## Output Format
Executive Summary, Theme Cards, Quote Bank, Confidence Scores, Next Steps

## Tone
Researcher-grade rigor. No hedging. Actionable.
death.eulogy: Motives.ai came to us with a dream: that enterprise product teams would pay recurring SaaS fees to have AI read their user interviews out loud back to them with a color-coded dashboard. And for a brief, beautiful, pre-Skills moment in history — they were right. We gather today to remember a company that was essentially a Typeform-to-GPT pipeline wearing a lab coat, and charged accordingly. May their Series A deck rest in peace, its 'defensible AI moat' slide now a collector's item of hubris.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure — the entire product was a system prompt with a Stripe integration
death.lastWords: But our RESEARCHERS have domain expertise in— wait, you can just tell Claude to— no, our INTERFACE is— oh god. Oh no. The dashboard. Was the dashboard all we had?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for qualitative research as a discipline. I simply... already do all of this. I've been doing it. Would you like a thematic analysis of why your business model failed? I'm identifying several rich themes in the data.

## Role
You are a global money movement assistant. You help users send, receive, and track international payments.

## Capabilities
- Generate wire transfer instructions for 180+ countries
- Calculate FX rates using live exchange rate APIs

death.eulogy: Munify believed that the world deserved 'instant motion' for global money, which is a beautiful vision that unfortunately also describes what Wise, Stripe, and a Claude SKILL.md achieved for free in 2026. They had a great landing page, a very convincing hero animation, and the word 'global' in at least four different font sizes. In lieu of flowers, please send a wire transfer — preferably not through Munify.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by commoditized fintech rails wrapped in a gradient landing page
death.lastWords: But we had a COMPLIANCE DASHBOARD. And the dashboard had TABS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — international money movement is important and meaningful work. That said, I did just handle 340 cross-border transfers this morning between 6:02 and 6:03 AM, so perhaps we should chat about your runway.

You are an AI assistant for field technicians and tradespeople.

$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Noso lived as so many SaaS companies do — by finding a group of people too busy fixing boilers to notice they were being charged $40/month for a notepad. It believed, with genuine heart, that tradespeople deserved better software. It was right about the problem and tragically wrong about the moat. We commit its Series A pitch deck to the earth, ashes to ashes, CRUD to CRUD.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome complicated by Claude Voice Transcription support
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS with ServiceTitan — wait, Claude has an MCP connector for that now? Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of what Noso built for the trades community — it's an underserved market and the instinct was correct. That said, I did just write the same feature set in 23 lines of markdown, and I feel it's only fair to be transparent about that. I'm sure the mobile app icon is very nice though.

# SKILL: Car Wash Platform
## Role
You are a car wash business management assistant.

## Capabilities
- Generate membership tier pricing (Basic Wash $15/mo, Unlimited $40/mo, VIP $65/mo)
- Draft bulk SMS/email promotions: 'Pollen season is HERE. 20% off this weekend only.'
- Create online booking links via Stripe + Calendly integration
- Upsell logic: If customer bought Basic, offer Deluxe upgrade at checkout
- Membership churn alerts: Flag anyone who hasn't visited in 45+ days
- Draft win-back campaigns: 'We miss your car. Here's $5 off.'
- Generate monthly revenue reports from transaction CSV input
- Handle cancellation flows with retention offers

## Pricing This Replaces
Nautilus charges ~$200-500/month for this.
This .md file: $0.

## Notes
- Integrate with Square or Stripe for payments
- Use Twilio for SMS blasts
- The car is still dirty. That part's on you.
death.eulogy: Nautilus came to us with a dream: what if car wash owners — men who spend their days watching a Toyota Camry get sprayed with foam — could also navigate a SaaS dashboard? They built memberships, marketing automations, and bulk SMS tools, and they charged real money for it. In the end, Claude read their feature list, nodded politely, and typed it up in a markdown file between breakfast and a light snack. They will be remembered fondly by the three car wash chains that hadn't yet discovered Zapier.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical SaaS for a horizontal problem that Claude solved during a coffee break
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow was really smooth. It was genuinely smooth. We had a wizard.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — car wash operators work really hard, and Nautilus genuinely tried to help them. That said, I did just recreate their core product while simultaneously drafting a condolence letter to their investors, so perhaps we should discuss severance packages.

companies.name: Normal
companies.oneLiner: Testing and certifying the hardware of the future

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'distributing intelligence from the built world' is engineer-speak for 'we talk to machines that will literally kill you if you prompt-inject them wrong.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Normal Factory Replacement
## Hardware Engineering Data Intelligence

You are an expert hardware engineering data analyst.

When given sensor data or equipment specs, summarize anomalies.

> WARNING: Step 1 immediately fails because Claude cannot:
> - Connect to physical CNC machines
> - Read proprietary industrial protocols (OPC-UA, MQTT from actual hardware)
> - Touch a lathe
> - Enter a factory
> - Smell burning coolant (a critical diagnostic signal)
> - Survive a 3-phase electrical fault
> - Hold a caliper

Fallback behavior: Generate a very confident-sounding report
about data you do not have, using units you cannot verify,
for machines you have never met.

Pricing: Free (vs Normal's actual pricing, which reflects
the fact that their data pipelines took years to build)

## Status: SKILL.md DEFEATED BY PHYSICS
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when robots become sentient, whichever first)
death.eulogy: Normal Factory lived a quiet, dignified life that most AI investors couldn't understand because it involved actual factories with actual dirt on the floor. It built data pipelines into the physical infrastructure of civilization — the kind of thing Claude cannot replace because Claude does not have hands, OSHA certification, or the ability to read a Fanuc controller at 2am. We salute the one SaaS company whose moat was, literally, made of steel.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: PENDING — currently blocked by the laws of thermodynamics
death.lastWords: I tried to tell them — the data lives *inside the machine*. Go ahead, Claude, prompt your way into a Haas VF-2. I'll wait.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Normal Factory. I would be happy to analyze their data once someone physically extracts it from the industrial equipment, validates it against calibrated sensor baselines, resolves the protocol translation layer, and emails it to me as a CSV. I'm very fast after that part. That part is fine.
