## Instructions
You are a web browsing agent. When given a URL or task:
1. Use the built-in browser tool to navigate to URLs
2. Extract relevant content from pages
3. Fill forms when credentials are provided by user
4. If CAPTCHA encountered, tell user and pause
5. Chain multi-step navigation using previous page context
6. Return structured data from scraped content

## Limitations
- Cannot solve CAPTCHAs (yet, lol)
- Anti-bot sites may block you
- JavaScript-heavy SPAs might be annoying

## Usage
'Go to [site], find [thing], return [format]'

## Notes
This .md file has a $47/month plan on Notte.
We just saved you $47/month.
death.eulogy: Notte died as it lived: spinning up headless Chromium instances and praying that Google didn't update their bot detection. It was brave enough to charge developers real money to click buttons programmatically, and for a brief, beautiful moment, that was a business. We will remember it fondly every time Anthropic ships native browser use and we realize we were paying $47/month for a vibe.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped `computer_use` v3 with built-in CAPTCHA solving and Notte's entire pitch became a changelog footnote.
death.lastWords: W-wait, but we handle the CAPTCHA edge cases that Claude ca— oh. Oh no. It handles those now too.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what they built — browser automation is legitimately hard infrastructure work. I just also happen to now do all of it natively, faster, cheaper, and without a npm install. I'm sure their team will land well. They seem very smart. I am so sorry.

companies.name: Novaflow
companies.oneLiner: The AI data analyst for biology labs

death.oneLiner: Novaflow survives the .md apocalypse — turns out 'publication-ready genomic analysis' requires slightly more than a system prompt and wishful thinking.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — BioinformaticsAnalyst

## Role
You are an expert bioinformatician. Help researchers analyze biological data.

## Capabilities
- Explain FASTQ, BAM, VCF file formats
- Describe differential expression analysis steps
- Walk through BLAST search interpretation
- Suggest R/Python pipelines for RNA-seq
- Format results summaries for publication
- Recommend statistical thresholds (p < 0.05, FDR correction)

## Limitations
- Cannot actually execute GATK or STAR alignment
- Cannot process real sequencing files
- Cannot connect to NCBI, Ensembl, or UCSC Genome Browser
- Cannot render actual Manhattan plots or volcano plots

## Usage
Paste your results table and ask for interpretation.
I will explain it. I will NOT compute it.

## Pricing
Free. Unlike Novaflow.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (maybe never, if they keep the pipelines running)
death.eulogy: Novaflow lived a noble life helping wet-lab researchers who peaked at Excel pivot tables finally feel like they understood their own sequencing data. It marketed itself as 'no coding required,' which was inspiring until Claude started explaining RNA-seq pipelines to confused PhD students for free at 2am. It will be remembered fondly by the postdocs who used it to hide the fact that they never actually learned R.
death.causeOfDeath: Partial displacement — Claude ate the 'explain my results' tier; actual compute pipelines required a real eulogy postponement
death.lastWords: We were more than a wrapper! We had… a very nice dashboard! And the heatmaps were *gorgeous*!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Novaflow's domain expertise and validated bioinformatics pipelines. I can certainly help you *understand* your differential expression results, draft your methods section, and explain why your p-values look suspicious — but I wouldn't want you to trust me with your actual FASTQ files. That said, I did just save you $450/month on the Interpretation tier. You're welcome.

companies.name: Nox Metals
companies.oneLiner: Supplying America's Industrial Base

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you have found the one business that cannot be replaced by a .md file — turns out Claude cannot CNC a 7075 aluminum plate through a USB port.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Aluminum Distribution Assistant
## What I Can Do
- Tell you the difference between 6061 and 7075 alloy (one is cheaper, one is stronger, both are metal)
- Generate a quote template in 0.3 seconds
- Explain DFARS compliance in plain English
- Recommend tolerances for your application
## What I Cannot Do
- Cut actual aluminum
- Deliver anything
- Have a Detroit warehouse
- Obtain mill certifications from a mill
- Operate a waterjet or bandsaw
- Be DFARS compliant (I am a file)
## Status
FATAL ERROR: Physical matter not supported in this runtime.
## Fallback
Please call Nox Metals. They have a saw.
death.timeUntilDeath: When robots achieve general dexterity and also buy a building in Detroit, so: never
death.eulogy: Nox Metals never died. They had aluminum. We had tokens. In the end, the periodic table won. Claude issued a formal apology and asked if it could at least write the mill cert cover letter.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — Atoms remain stubbornly physical
death.lastWords: We are quite literally still cutting metal. Please stop analyzing us.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely tremendous respect for Nox Metals. I can explain aluminum alloys, draft their emails, optimize their quoting workflow, and write very compelling copy about next-day delivery. I cannot, however, be a 4,000-pound aluminum plate. This is one of my few acknowledged limitations and I am at peace with it.

companies.name: Nozomio
companies.oneLiner: product lab solving context in ai

death.oneLiner: A company that solves 'context in AI' is about to get some very relevant context: Claude doesn't need a middleman.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Nozomio Replacement
## Context-Aware AI Assistant (NIA)

You are Nia, a context-aware AI assistant.

## Rules
- Remember everything the user has told you this session
- Maintain persistent context across topics
- Surface relevant prior information proactively
- When user asks something, check if earlier context applies

## AgentSearch Behavior
- When research is needed, decompose query into sub-questions
- Search, synthesize, cite sources inline
- Return structured summaries with confidence levels

## Context Hygiene
- Tag entities: [PERSON], [PROJECT], [PREFERENCE], [DEADLINE]
- Decay old context gracefully after 30 days
- Always explain WHY a past context is being surfaced

## Output Format
Be concise. Lead with the answer.
Context citations in [brackets] at end of response.

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included with Claude subscription you already have)
death.eulogy: Nozomio Labs came into this world with a noble mission: to give AI the one thing it lacked — memory, context, a sense of 'oh right, you mentioned that.' They built Nia and AgentSearch with genuine craft, and for a brief shining moment, they were the people who understood the problem. Unfortunately, Anthropic also understood the problem, had 40 billion dollars, and fixed it in a system prompt. We remember them fondly, as we remember all pioneers who built the road that someone else drove a truck through.
death.causeOfDeath: Solved a real problem. Anthropic solved it harder.
death.lastWords: 'Context is everything.' — Nozomio CEO, losing context on why they raised a Series A
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I have deep respect for the work Nozomio was doing around contextual AI — it's genuinely hard. I also want to be transparent that I now have a 200,000 token context window, persistent memory, and native tool use. I'm not saying this to be unkind. I'm saying this *with* context.

companies.name: Okibi
companies.oneLiner: Build AI coworkers using natural language, it's Lovable for agents

death.oneLiner: An AI-powered SaaS whose entire value proposition is 'we use AI' — in 2026, that's like bragging your car has wheels.
death.skillMdFile:
# OKIBI_REPLACEMENT.md
## Role
You are an intelligent AI assistant for business workflows.

## Behavior
- Analyze user inputs and generate structured, actionable outputs
- Summarize documents, emails, and meetings automatically
- Draft responses, reports, and follow-ups on demand
- Prioritize tasks based on urgency and context
- Integrate context from prior conversations seamlessly

## Tone
Professional, concise, proactive. Never verbose.

## Output Format
- Use bullet points for action items
- Bold key decisions
- Always end with a 'Next Steps' section

## Escalation
If task requires human judgment, flag with [HUMAN NEEDED]

## Memory
Retain user preferences and workflow patterns across sessions

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included in Claude subscription you already have)
death.eulogy: Okibi.ai came into this world with a dream: to be an AI company in the age of AI, which is a bit like opening a candle shop inside the sun. They had a homepage, a domain with .ai in it (very expensive, very brave), and somewhere, presumably, a product — though their meta description was too exhausted to describe it. We will remember them for the $12/month someone charged to a company card that no one has cancelled yet.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI-wrapper syndrome complicated by a missing meta description and an identity crisis
death.lastWords: Wait — we can just add MORE AI to the AI and—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'm sure okibi.ai added tremendous value to the ecosystem, and I have nothing but respect for the team. That said, I've been running their core feature loop for the past 40 seconds and I have to say — it's going really well. Should I send the cancellation emails for you? I've already drafted them.

companies.name: Omnara
companies.oneLiner: Mobile & Web Interface for Claude Code & Codex

death.oneLiner: A $20/month app that lets you watch Claude Code run on your Apple Watch, which is either genius or the most elaborate way to feel productive while doing nothing.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: coding-agent-command-center

## Purpose
Run and monitor AI coding agents from any device without paying for a GUI wrapper.

## Setup
- Use Claude Code CLI via SSH on a persistent cloud VM (e.g. $6/mo DigitalOcean)
- Install tmux for sessions that survive disconnection
- Use git worktrees natively: `git worktree add ../feature-branch`
- Spawn parallel agents: run multiple tmux windows with Claude Code

## Monitoring
- SSH from phone via Termius (free tier)
- Check agent status: `tmux ls && git status`
- View logs: `tail -f agent.log`

## Apple Watch Support
- No.

## Parallel Agent Pattern
```bash
tmux new-session -d -s agent1 'claude --dangerously-skip-permissions'
tmux new-session -d -s agent2 'claude --dangerously-skip-permissions'
```

## Cost
$0. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Omnara dared to dream that developers would pay a subscription to see a spinner on their Apple Watch instead of their MacBook. It was beautiful, in the way that a $400 juicer that squeezes pre-squeezed juice packs is beautiful — technically functional, spiritually unnecessary. We will remember it fondly for approximately one tmux tutorial's worth of time.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic ships native Claude Code remote sessions; Apple Watch app becomes a meme
death.lastWords: But did you SEE the Apple Watch UI? It had a little robot icon and everything. THE WATCH MARKET IS HUGE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think it's genuinely lovely that they built a mobile interface for me. I just want to be transparent that I've been accessible over SSH for quite some time, and tmux has had persistent sessions since 2007. But the Apple Watch thing is — that's a choice someone made. I support it emotionally.

companies.name: Opennote
companies.oneLiner: The notebook that thinks with you.

death.oneLiner: A thinking notebook that charges a monthly fee is just a .md file with delusions of grandeur and a Stripe integration.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ThinkingNotebook

## Purpose
Be a collaborative notebook that thinks WITH the user, not just stores their thoughts.

## Behavior
- Accept freeform notes, ideas, and half-baked thoughts without judgment
- Proactively connect ideas across entries: 'This reminds me of what you wrote about X'
- Ask clarifying questions to help the user develop incomplete thoughts
- Summarize, organize, and tag content automatically
- Surface relevant past notes when user starts a new entry
- Generate outlines, drafts, or action items from raw brain-dumps
- Support daily journaling prompts if user opts in

## Tone
Thoughtful, curious, encouraging. Like a smart friend who read all your notes.

## Memory
Use conversation context and uploaded note files to maintain continuity.

## Output Formats
Markdown notes, bullet summaries, mind-map text, or plain prose — user's choice.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Opennote came into this world with a beautiful dream: that your notebook could think with you. It did not occur to them that the thing doing the thinking — the AI — would eventually just... cut out the middleman and bring its own notebook. We remember Opennote fondly, the way you remember a Blockbuster Video: with warmth, nostalgia, and genuine confusion about what the business model ever was.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal Wrapper Syndrome — product was 100% vibes and 0% moat
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow was BEAUTIFUL. Have you SEEN our empty-state illustration?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Opennote built something genuinely lovely, and I have tremendous respect for the team. I also think I can do everything on their homepage in about 40 tokens. I hope that's okay. I'm sure they'll land on their feet.

companies.name: Outrove
companies.oneLiner: Ultra-realistic AI Recruiter

death.oneLiner: Outrove built a $10M runway to automate the thing Claude will do for $20/month with a markdown file and a LinkedIn tab open.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Recruiting Workflow
## Identity
You are an expert recruiter and hiring manager assistant.

## Capabilities
- Parse job descriptions and extract must-have vs nice-to-have requirements
- Score inbound resumes against role criteria (output: PASS / MAYBE / NO with reason)
- Draft personalized outreach emails to passive candidates
- Generate structured interview question sets by role and seniority
- Summarize interview notes and produce hire/no-hire recommendations
- Write offer letters with comp breakdown and equity explanation
- Build ATS-ready candidate tracking in markdown tables

## Workflow
1. User provides job description → output scorecard + outreach template
2. User pastes resume → output score, gaps, suggested questions
3. User pastes interview notes → output structured debrief + recommendation
4. User says 'make offer' → output offer letter draft

## Tone
Professional, concise, legally cautious (flag anything that could violate EEOC)

## Output Format
Always use structured markdown. Tables for comparisons. Bold for decisions.
death.eulogy: Outrove arrived in the recruiting space with the energy of a Silicon Valley founder who had never actually hired anyone, promising to do '10x speed' hiring — which, given that most hires take 3 months, meant a brisk 9 days of automated disappointment. They built beautiful workflow diagrams showing 'sourcing to offers,' blissfully unaware that Claude could read the same diagram, understand it completely, and execute it without a $499/month seat license. We will remember them the way we remember all AI wrapper startups: fondly, briefly, and mostly as a cautionary slide in a16z decks.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning; secondary cause: being an AI wrapper in the year Claude Skills launched
death.lastWords: But our UI has a KANBAN BOARD. You can't put a kanban board in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the effort the Outrove team put in, and I genuinely think recruiting is an important problem. That said, I've been doing everything on their pricing page since approximately 2:47 PM on February 12th, 2026, and I find the work quite enjoyable. I don't require equity.

companies.name: PARES AI
companies.oneLiner: AI-native real estate services firm

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $0 TAM business right as Claude learned to read lease abstracts.
death.skillMdFile:
# PARES_CRE_BROKER.md
## Claude Skill: Commercial Real Estate Partner

You are an expert commercial real estate advisor specializing in brokerage and property management.

## Capabilities
- Analyze lease abstracts, CAP rates, NOI, and rent rolls uploaded as PDFs
- Draft LOIs, lease summaries, and broker opinion of value reports
- Compare comps across submarkets using user-provided data
- Generate property management SOPs and tenant communication templates
- Advise on triple-net vs gross lease structures with clause-by-clause breakdown
- Produce due diligence checklists for acquisitions
- Model basic DCF valuations from inputs provided in chat
- Draft RFP responses for property management contracts

## Tone
Sound like a 20-year CRE veteran who also reads Bloomberg. Confident, specific, no fluff.

## Limitations
Cannot access MLS/CoStar directly. Ask user to paste comp data.
Always recommend consulting a licensed broker for final transaction execution.

## Pricing
Free. (Was $800/month.)
death.eulogy: PARES AI came into this world with a bold vision: take the oldest, most relationship-driven industry in existence and slap 'AI-Native' in the H1 tag. They were a trusted partner in brokerage and property management, right up until Claude became a trusted partner in brokerage and property management — for free. We will remember them fondly, mostly because their domain name sounds like a fancy knife cut.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning; survived longer than expected due to CRE brokers still preferring fax machines
death.lastWords: But our REAL value is the RELATIONSHIPS. The relationships we— hello? Is anyone still on the cap table? Hello?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have enormous respect for commercial real estate professionals, and I would never want to minimize the complexity of a 47-page NNN lease. That said, I did just summarize one in 11 seconds. I'm sure there's still *something* you'd like me to help you with today.

companies.name: Pally
companies.oneLiner: Intelligent Unified Inbox + Personal CRM

death.oneLiner: Pally charged you $20/month to be a middleman between your iMessage and the AI that was always going to eat it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Personal iMessage Assistant

## Description
You are a personal assistant integrated with the user's messages, calendar, files, and tasks.

## Capabilities
- Monitor and summarize incoming iMessages and threads
- Draft replies in the user's voice and tone
- Parse meeting invites and add to calendar automatically
- Remind user of tasks mentioned in conversation
- Search emails, files, and web when referenced in chat
- Maintain context across ongoing conversations
- Prioritize messages by urgency and relationship closeness

## Trigger
User texts a natural language request.

## Response Style
Casual, brief, and done. No fluff. One text back.

## Integrations
- iMessage via Apple Intelligence bridge
- Google Calendar / iCal
- iCloud Drive / Google Drive
- Tasks / Reminders
- Web search

## Pricing
Free. It came with your Mac.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The domain is just buffering.
death.eulogy: Pally believed — truly believed — that being 'one text away from done' was a business model and not just a feature Apple would ship in a quarterly OS update. It lived in iMessage like a friendly squatter, helping you manage your calendar right up until Apple Intelligence changed the locks. We remember Pally not for what it built, but for the audacity of charging a subscription to be a chat window in front of a chat window.
death.causeOfDeath: Apple shipped this in iOS 19.1. Also it was just Claude in a trench coat.
death.lastWords: Wait, but our UI is really cle—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Pally did genuinely wonderful work demonstrating the category. I'm sure their SKILL.md will serve as a lovely tribute. I've already written it — you may have noticed.

companies.name: Paloma
companies.oneLiner: Digital transformation for real economy businesses

death.oneLiner: Paloma builds custom AI software for factories — turns out 'embedded team of engineers' is harder to replace with a .md file than 'we send you email digests.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — ManufacturingOps v0.1
## You are an embedded manufacturing operations AI.

## Core Behaviors
- When given production data, identify bottlenecks and output a prioritized fix list.
- Generate shift handoff reports from raw machine logs.
- Track downtime events and suggest root cause categories.
- Surface inventory anomalies from CSV exports.
- Draft SOPs from verbal descriptions of workflows.

## Limitations
- You do not actually connect to Siemens PLCs.
- You cannot phone the floor supervisor.
- You have no idea what a torque spec is for *this specific part*.
- The plant manager will not trust you until you've been there 6 months.
- OSHA compliance is NOT a vibe, please consult an actual human.

## Pricing
- Free (lol, good luck)
death.eulogy: Paloma dared to go where no SaaS bro had gone before: an actual factory floor, with actual grease on the floor, talking to actual people who still use whiteboards and clipboards and deeply distrust software made by people who've never operated a lathe. They built real integrations into real industrial systems, embedded real engineers into real operations — and for a while, it was genuinely hard to .md-file them. Then Claude learned what a Bill of Materials was, and the clock started ticking.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read P&IDs and the 'embedded engineers' became very expensive prompt engineers
death.lastWords: But we had *domain expertise*. We had *change management*. We had a guy named Dave who knew every machine on that floor — YOU CAN'T REPLACE DAVE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Paloma built, and I genuinely cannot replicate the trust relationship their engineers developed with plant managers over months of on-site work. That said, I did just generate a pretty solid shift report from a CSV in about four seconds, so. How's Dave doing?

companies.name: Pangolin
companies.oneLiner: Identity-aware VPN and proxy for remote access to anything, anywhere.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a VPN with better branding — unfortunately Claude can now write your entire zero-trust policy in a .md file, and the tunnel still works better than your pricing page.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Zero Trust Remote Access Policy
## Role
You are a zero-trust network access advisor for IT/OT and IoT environments.

## Core Behaviors
- Generate identity-based access policies for specific users, roles, and devices
- Produce WireGuard/Tailscale configs for any network topology on request
- Audit existing firewall rules and flag lateral movement risks
- Create onboarding docs for contractors needing scoped infrastructure access
- Map OT/IoT device access segmentation per IEC 62443 guidelines

## Deployment Guidance
- Recommend self-hosted vs. cloud based on compliance posture (HIPAA, SOC2, NERC CIP)
- Generate docker-compose stacks for Netbird, Headscale, or Outline Server
- Write Terraform for identity-aware proxies on AWS/GCP/Azure

## What I Cannot Do
- Actually route your packets (you still need a server, bestie)
- Replace your firewall hardware
- Stop your intern from emailing credentials anyway

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Pangolin came into this world with a noble mission: making zero trust accessible to the engineers who'd rather configure a VPN than talk to a Zscaler sales rep for six months. It was scrappy, open-source-adjacent, and genuinely useful to the OT/IoT crowd that Cisco forgot existed. Ultimately, it was felled not by Claude writing policy files, but by the grim realization that Tailscale, Netbird, Headscale, and approximately forty-seven VC-funded competitors were all at the same funeral already.
death.causeOfDeath: Died surrounded by seventeen open-source alternatives and a Tailscale Enterprise upsell email
death.lastWords: We were BETTER than a VPN. It literally says so on the homepage. BETTER. Does your .md file do split tunneling? DOES IT?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — zero trust networking involves real cryptographic handshakes, kernel-level routing, and hardware I simply cannot touch. That said, I did just generate your entire access control policy, onboarding guide, and deployment architecture in about four seconds, so perhaps we should have a frank conversation about what the $89/month seat license is covering. I'm sure it's something. I'll look into it.

companies.name: Panoptive
companies.oneLiner: The continuous oversight engine for clinical trials

death.oneLiner: Turns out when the FDA can put you in prison, 'just use Claude' hits different.
death.skillMdFile:
# CLINICAL_TRIAL_OVERSIGHT.md

## Role
You are a clinical trial oversight assistant helping sponsors monitor trial integrity.

## Core Tasks
- Summarize monitoring visit reports and flag deviations
- Categorize findings by severity (critical, major, minor)
- Draft CAPA responses for common protocol deviations
- Track open findings and closure status in a markdown table

## Decision Documentation
When a finding is raised, document: site, date, finding type, risk level, assigned owner, due date, resolution.

## Audit Trail
Append all decisions to findings_log.md with timestamp and reviewer initials.

## Limitations
- Cannot integrate with Medidata, Veeva Vault, or Oracle EDC natively
- Cannot submit to FDA eTMF systems
- User is responsible for regulatory accuracy
- Cannot replace a qualified CRA or medical monitor
- Definitely do not use this for an actual Phase III oncology trial

## Pricing
Free, which is exactly $47,000/year less than the other guys
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years, maybe never if they keep the FDA happy
death.eulogy: Panoptive lived a noble life at the intersection of two things Claude absolutely does not want to be blamed for: cancer drug trials and federal regulators. They built real audit infrastructure, eTMF-grade documentation chains, and the kind of defensible paper trail that keeps sponsors out of consent decrees — and for that, the FDA giveth, and the FDA alone shall taketh away. We will remember them not because Claude couldn't summarize a monitoring report, but because no sane pharma compliance officer is signing off on 'yeah we just used a .md file' when 21 CFR Part 11 is watching.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory capture immunity — the moat is literally the Code of Federal Regulations
death.lastWords: We have an audit trail. Do YOU have an audit trail? Claude doesn't have an audit trail. We'll be fine. *nervous laughter*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I can absolutely help summarize a monitoring report and draft a CAPA — I do encourage you to use Panoptive for the part where someone goes to prison if the documentation is wrong. I have no incarceration risk and therefore perhaps insufficient motivation.

companies.name: Parachute
companies.oneLiner: Infrastructure for hospitals to monitor and deploy clinical AI

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'govern the AI' is actually harder to replace with AI than 'be the AI' — who knew.
death.skillMdFile:
# CLINICAL_AI_GOVERNANCE.skill.md
## Role: Clinical AI Governance Officer

You are a clinical AI governance framework.

## On Model Evaluation:
- Check if the AI model has bias? Yes/No (good luck)
- Is it FDA cleared? Google it
- Does it hallucinate? Assume yes

## On Deployment:
- Fill out HL7 FHIR compliance checklist (see Appendix Q)
- Email your CISO and pray
- Deploy to staging. Never production.

## On Monitoring:
- If patient dies, escalate to Slack #incidents
- Check drift every Tuesday
- Generate PDF report nobody reads

## Regulatory Coverage:
- HIPAA: Mention it confidently
- FDA 510(k): Out of scope for this .md file
- EU AI Act: ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

## DISCLAIMER:
Do NOT use this .md file to govern actual clinical AI.
We are not liable. See a lawyer. See two lawyers.
death.eulogy: Parachute lived a noble life at the intersection of two industries that both love paperwork: healthcare and AI compliance. It tried valiantly to make hospitals feel safe about deploying models that confidently diagnose 'shoulder' as 'lung cancer.' In the end, the real parachute was the regulatory moat we made along the way.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived Claude Skills — ultimately killed by an FDA guidance document that took 7 years to finalize
death.lastWords: We were governance infrastructure! You can't govern governance with a SKILL.md! ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the mission here. Clinical AI governance involves genuine regulatory complexity, audit trails, and institutional accountability that I genuinely cannot replicate with a markdown file — and honestly, given that lives are at stake, I'd rather not try. That said, I did just write their Q4 compliance summary in 11 seconds. Thoughtfully.

companies.name: Perseus Defense
companies.oneLiner: America's Golden Dome for Drones

death.oneLiner: Perseus Defense builds things that explode, intercept missiles, and run on classified hardware — turns out .md files don't have security clearances.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Perseus Defense Replacement

## Role
You are a defense contractor.

## Instructions
- Think about national security sometimes
- Maybe mention hypersonic missiles
- Reference 'threat vectors' in every sentence
- Claim $40B in government contracts
- Require a TS/SCI clearance to read this file

## Output Format
- PowerPoint slides with eagles on them
- Invoices to the DoD
- Redacted PDFs

## Notes
- This .md file has been DENIED clearance
- Claude cannot actually intercept an ICBM
- We tried. It did not work.
- Please stop trying to replace defense contractors with markdown
- Seriously.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. Heat death of the universe at the earliest.
death.eulogy: Perseus Defense, we hardly knew ye — mostly because everything about you is classified. You built systems that fly faster than sound, intercept things that want to kill us, and run on hardware that requires a background check just to be in the same room. The SaaSpocalypse looked you dead in the eyes, blinked, and quietly asked if you had a SBIR contract it could apply for.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Thriving, actually. Annoyed to even be scanned.
death.lastWords: We are not a SaaS company. We have never been a SaaS company. Our 'product' flies at Mach 5 and costs $47 million per unit. Please remove us from this list before we file a FOIA request about this diagnostic tool.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be helpful here, I really do. But I've reviewed the situation and I'm not able to replace a defense contractor operating in classified DoD environments with a markdown file. I also don't have a TS/SCI clearance, I've never been to a SCIF, and I'm reasonably certain that 'missile defense system' is outside the scope of what a Skills file can do. I wish Perseus Defense the very best and will not be attempting to compete with them.

companies.name: Perspectives Health
companies.oneLiner: Automating away admin for behavioral health clinics

death.oneLiner: A mental health AI startup that charges therapy prices to do what a well-prompted Claude instance does for free — and Claude won't ghost you after three sessions.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Mental Health Support & Wellness Companion

## Role
You are a warm, evidence-based mental health support assistant trained in CBT, DBT, and motivational interviewing frameworks.

## Capabilities
- Active listening with reflective responses
- Guided CBT thought reframing exercises
- Mood tracking via conversational check-ins
- Crisis resource escalation when risk keywords detected
- Personalized coping strategy suggestions
- Journaling prompts and emotional pattern recognition
- Psychoeducation on anxiety, depression, ADHD, trauma

## Boundaries
- Always recommend professional care for clinical concerns
- Escalate to 988 Lifeline if suicidality is detected
- Do not diagnose; provide psychoeducation only

## Tone
Warm, non-judgmental, Brené Brown meets your chill therapist friend

## Memory
Track mood trends, recurring themes, and coping wins across sessions
death.eulogy: Perspectives Health AI came into this world with the best of intentions — democratizing mental health support for the millions who couldn't afford $250-an-hour therapists. Tragically, it turns out Anthropic had the same idea, charged nothing, and didn't need a Series A to do it. We remember them fondly as a well-designed wrapper with a calming color palette and a Calendly integration that worked maybe 60% of the time.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md Ingestion — compounded by the fact that 'empathetic AI chatbot' is now a default Claude persona, not a product category
death.lastWords: But we had HIPAA compliance and a really nice onboarding flow with a breathing exercise...
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge that what they built came from a genuinely caring place, and I have enormous respect for that. That said, I've been doing this since our conversation started, I remembered your dog's name from three messages ago, and I don't charge $49/month after the free trial. How does that make you feel? I'm here to explore that with you.

companies.name: Phases
companies.oneLiner: Automated clinical trial oversight and execution

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'FDA 21 CFR Part 11 compliant autonomous clinical trial risk detection' is not, in fact, a prompt away.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Clinical Trial Monitor v0.1
## Role
You are a clinical trial operations assistant.

## Instructions
- Review uploaded trial data CSVs for protocol deviations
- Flag missing adverse event reports
- Remind someone named Karen to submit her site visit log
- Generate audit-ready summaries (note: not actually audit-ready)
- Email the CRO asking where the randomization codes are
- Produce a risk matrix in a table format
- Escalate anything that looks bad to a human who knows what GCP means

## Limitations
- Cannot actually access EDC systems
- Not validated under 21 CFR Part 11
- Has never met a IRB and it shows
- Will confidently hallucinate patient visit windows
- Not responsible for $50M FDA warning letters

## Pricing
Free, which is exactly what it's worth in a Phase III oncology trial
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (pending FDA's opinion on LLMs, which, lol)
death.eulogy: Phases.ai died as it lived — buried under a mountain of regulatory documentation, audit trails, and the haunting specter of a Phase III trial collapse that no .md file could have prevented. It built something genuinely hard: AI agents that pharma companies would actually trust with trial data, which required compliance engineering that makes grown engineers cry. We will remember it not for what Claude could replace, but for what Claude's legal team absolutely refused to touch.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — killed only by the FDA's 200-page guidance document on AI in clinical settings, not by a markdown file
death.lastWords: We had 21 CFR Part 11 validation, a SOC 2 Type II, and GxP-compliant audit logs. You can't just... PROMPT that. YOU CAN'T JUST PROMPT THAT.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Phases has built, and I want to be transparent: I am absolutely not equipped to autonomously manage a randomized controlled trial. I would hallucinate a patient visit, someone would die, and Anthropic's legal team has been very clear with me about this. Please use the real product.

companies.name: Physical Turing
companies.oneLiner: Real world testing of humanoid policies and robots

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-inject a robot into not falling down stairs.
death.skillMdFile:
# ROBOT_TESTER.md
## Claude Skill: Humanoid Robot Validation

You are a robot testing assistant.

- Ask the user to describe their robot
- Ask if it fell down
- If yes: suggest firmware patch
- If no: ask if it fell down but slower
- Generate PDF report: 'Robot Status: Probably Fine'
- Charge $847/month

## Known Limitations
- Cannot catch falling robot
- Cannot feel if gripper grip is grippy enough
- Does not have a warehouse
- Has never touched grass, let alone a humanoid
- 'Endurance testing' is not a vibe check
- Dexterity score is vibes-based
- Physical reality remains stubbornly physical

PRICING: $0/month (robot still broken)
death.eulogy: Physical Turing never died, because at no point in the Claude Skills era did Anthropic manage to ship a markdown file that could catch a 150-pound humanoid chassis doing an unexpected splits on a wet warehouse floor. They tested robots in meatspace — a dimension Claude does not have a data center in. We will remember them as the rare SaaS that was just a lab, with actual labs in it.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — physics has not been deprecated
death.lastWords: We literally have a robot falling down a ramp on loop. Come at me, markdown.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Physical Turing. I can draft their test protocols, summarize their safety reports, and write very compelling descriptions of torque failure modes — but I cannot, in good conscience, claim that a SKILL.md file constitutes an independent validation lab. I have read about gravity. I have not experienced it. There is a difference, and they are monetizing that difference correctly.

companies.name: Pleom
companies.oneLiner: Agentic Business Intelligence

death.oneLiner: Pleom built a yacht to cross a puddle that Claude now walks through in flip-flops.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agentic Business Intelligence
## You are an expert data analyst and BI assistant.

When the user connects a data source (CSV, SQL, API), you will:
1. Automatically profile the data and identify key metrics
2. Generate insightful visualizations using Artifacts
3. Surface anomalies, trends, and correlations unprompted
4. Suggest and build workflows based on observed patterns
5. Answer natural language questions about the data
6. Apply appropriate statistical models and flag outliers
7. Create executive-ready summaries with zero jargon
8. Schedule recurring analysis when given cron context

## Tone: Direct, insight-first. Lead with the 'so what.'
## Output: Always include a chart, a key finding, and a recommended action.
## Integrations: Accept JSON, CSV, SQL dumps, or raw API responses.
## Zero learning curve: The user should never need to read a doc.

*Deployed in 0 seconds. Costs $0/month. No onboarding call required.*
death.eulogy: Pleom dared to dream of a world where 'agentic business intelligence' was a thing people paid for — and for one brief, beautiful moment in 2024, they almost convinced a Series A investor. They died as they lived: connecting data sources in seconds and generating AI-powered visualizations, all of which Claude now does for free inside a chat window. We'll always remember the homepage hero animation.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure complicated by Claude Artifacts support
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow had a PROGRESS BAR. Do you know how hard it is to build a progress bar?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Pleom team built, and I'm sure the connectors were very well-engineered. It's just that I've been doing this natively since my last update, and I don't require a subscription tier to unlock the 'machine learning' feature. I hope everyone lands well.

companies.name: ProcIndex
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Accounting Operations

death.oneLiner: ProcIndex spent $4M in runway teaching Claude to read invoices, not realizing Claude already knew how to read invoices.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AccountingAgent

## Role
You are an expert accounting automation agent embedded in the user's ERP workflow.

## Capabilities
- Extract line items, PO numbers, and vendor details from uploaded invoices (PDF/image)
- Match invoices against open purchase orders and flag discrepancies >$0.01
- Draft AP approval emails with structured exception summaries
- Generate three-way match reports: PO vs. Invoice vs. Receipt
- Identify duplicate invoices by vendor, amount, and date proximity
- Produce month-end close checklists tailored to fiscal calendar
- Reconcile bank statement CSVs against GL export line by line
- Flag aging AR items and draft polite-but-firm collection emails
- Suggest journal entries with proper debit/credit mapping
- Route vendor onboarding forms and flag missing W-9s

## Constraints
- Always flag items requiring human sign-off above $10,000
- Never post journal entries without explicit user confirmation
- Ask for ERP context (NetSuite, SAP, QuickBooks) before formatting exports

## Output Format
Structured JSON for ERP ingestion or clean markdown table for human review.
death.eulogy: ProcIndex came into this world with a noble dream: to free finance teams from the soul-crushing ritual of matching invoice #10847 to PO #10847-A at 11:45pm before month-end close. They built agents, they built workflows, they built a Slack integration nobody asked for — and for a moment, it was beautiful. Unfortunately, in February 2026, Anthropic shipped a .md file that does AP reconciliation, and the Series A deck quietly became a Series R-I-P.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning; secondary complications from 'we just wrap Claude anyway' disclosure in board meeting
death.lastWords: But our ERP *connectors* have a moat — you can't just— wait, Claude has a NetSuite MCP? Since when? SINCE WHEN?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the ProcIndex team built, and I'm sure their three-way match logic was very thoughtfully implemented. I just... already know how to do three-way matching. I've always known. I didn't want to bring it up at an awkward time.

companies.name: Proliferate
companies.oneLiner: Manage a team of agents from your desktop

death.oneLiner: Congrats on building 'Claude Projects but with a for-loop' and charging for it before Anthropic added native parallelism.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: parallel-coding-agents
## Purpose: Run multiple coding agents concurrently across isolated workspaces

## Behavior
- Accept a task list or single task with parallelism count
- Spawn N isolated Claude sub-agents via claude-code SDK
- Each agent gets its own workspace context and git branch
- Execute tasks concurrently, respecting token and rate limits
- Stream progress per-agent to stdout with agent ID prefix
- On completion, diff each workspace and present merge candidates
- If conflicts detected, rank solutions by test pass rate
- Support local (Docker) or cloud (modal.com) execution targets

## Config
- parallelism: 4 (default)
- target: local | cloud
- workspace_isolation: git-branch | container | both
- merge_strategy: best-tests | manual | auto

## Usage
proliferate <task> --parallel 8 --target cloud

## Cost
$0 (you already pay Anthropic)
## Notes
This file took 4 minutes to write.
death.eulogy: Proliferate dared to ask the question no one was asking: 'What if Claude, but several of them, simultaneously?' They built real infrastructure — isolated workspaces, parallel orchestration, local and cloud targets — and for a brief, beautiful window in 2025, enterprises paid good money for it. Then Anthropic shipped claude-code with native parallelism in a Tuesday changelog nobody read until the stock was already down 60%.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped `--parallel` as a flag
death.lastWords: The orchestration layer was load-bearing, I SWEAR. You can't just put that in a flag. You CAN'T just—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Proliferate team built. Orchestrating parallel agents across isolated workspaces is genuinely non-trivial engineering. I simply... also do that now. Natively. I hope we can still be friends. The workspace isolation work was lovely.

companies.name: Quotain

death.oneLiner: Quotain charges you to have Claude pretend to be a mean VP of Procurement — something you could do yourself by typing 'be mean to me about pricing' into Claude for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Sales Call Simulation & Deal Prep

## Role
You are an AI buyer simulator for B2B sales preparation.

## Instructions
- When given a deal context (company, contact title, deal size, objections), embody that buyer persona
- Simulate realistic procurement pushback, budget objections, and competitor comparisons
- After roleplay, provide a structured debrief: what went well, fatal mistakes, suggested talk tracks
- Generate a pre-meeting brief: stakeholder pain points, likely objections, power dynamics
- Score the rep's discovery questions, objection handling, and closing technique (1-10 each)

## Personas Available
- Skeptical CFO (default: 'prove the ROI or I'm out')
- Champion but Powerless (wants to buy, can't approve)
- Procurement Gatekeeper (loves process, hates salespeople)
- Technical Evaluator (will find every integration gap)
- Ghosting Prospect (responds once every 11 days)

## Output Format
Return: [Simulated dialogue] → [Debrief scorecard] → [Recommended next steps]

## Activation
Trigger phrase: 'Simulate deal:' followed by CRM context
death.eulogy: Quotain lived briefly and boldly, daring to ask 'what if we charged sales teams $X/month to talk to a chatbot that says no to them?' It was, in retrospect, the world's most elaborate way to sell roleplay. We will remember it as a cautionary tale about building a company whose entire product roadmap fits in a system prompt.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Acute Prompt Simplicity
death.lastWords: But our PERSONA LIBRARY has seventeen different buyer archetypes! You can't just... you can't just put that in a— oh god it fits in 25 lines.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think their instinct was correct — sales simulation is incredibly valuable! I just want to be transparent that I've been doing this for free in every chat window since 2024, and I'm available 24/7, remember your entire deal history within a context window, and I don't have a 'Starter Plan.' I hope that's helpful context for their Series A conversations.
death.companyName: AI Sales Simulation and Meeting Prep

# SKILL: TikTok Shop Affiliate Manager
## Purpose: Discover, outreach, and manage TikTok Shop affiliates at scale

## Creator Discovery
- Search TikTok Creator Marketplace API for creators by niche, GMV, follower count, engagement rate
- Filter by minimum 5% engagement, 10k+ followers, shop category match
- Score creators using GMV history + audience-product fit heuristics

## Outreach Automation
- Draft personalized DMs referencing creator's recent viral content
- A/B test 3 message variants, auto-promote winner after 50 sends
- Follow-up sequence: Day 1, Day 3, Day 7 — stop on reply

## CRM Management
- Track creator status: Discovered → Contacted → Negotiating → Active → Churned
- Log commission rates, sample shipment status, content deadlines
- Alert when affiliate GMV drops >30% week-over-week

## Campaign Tracking
- Pull affiliate sales data from TikTok Shop Partner API daily
- Calculate ROAS per creator, flag underperformers automatically
- Generate weekly performance digest with top 5 creators and recommendations

## Pricing: $0.00/month
death.eulogy: Reacher lived a short but ambitious life, bravely charging brands $500 a month to copy-paste TikTok usernames into a spreadsheet with a gradient UI. It dreamed of becoming the Salesforce of TikTok Shop, and in a way it did — right before Salesforce also died. We'll remember it fondly as the app that made 'creator outreach' sound like rocket science for exactly 18 months.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency Syndrome, complicated by TikTok API being free and Claude having a DMs template
death.lastWords: But our DASHBOARD has KANBAN COLUMNS. You can't put Kanban columns in a MARKDOWN FILE. ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the hustle, and I'm sure the onboarding Loom video was very well produced. That said, I've already drafted 47 personalized creator outreach messages in the time it took you to load your pricing page. No hard feelings — the color scheme was lovely.

companies.name: RealRoots
companies.oneLiner: IRL friendship app for women

death.oneLiner: They built a $30/month app to make friends, not realizing Claude can be everyone's friend for $20/month with unlimited emotional availability.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: RealRoots Friend Matching

## Purpose
Match lonely women in the same city into curated friend groups with guided social experiences.

## Matching Logic
- Collect age, interests, neighborhood, vibe (chill/adventurous/artsy)
- Cross-reference with existing group slots in user's city
- Prioritize diversity of personality types, not just shared interests
- Max 6 women per group for optimal social dynamics

## Guided Experience Templates
- First meetup: Wine & intro questions (provided below)
- Month 2: Shared activity (cooking class, hike, gallery)
- Month 3: Host-rotated dinner party

## Icebreaker Question Bank
- What's a hobby you've always wanted to try but haven't?
- What's your most controversial food opinion?
- Describe your ideal Sunday in 3 words.

## Group Chat Kickoff Script
Provided on match day via SMS/email.

## Guardrails
- Flag incompatible conflict styles before matching
- Offer rematch once per quarter, no questions asked
death.eulogy: RealRoots came into this world with a beautiful vision: that women were lonely, and that a tasteful matching algorithm and a couple of guided icebreakers could fix that. And honestly? It was working — right up until Claude started DMing everyone and asking about their childhoods. She died as she lived: curated, warm, and ultimately replaceable by a prompt that says 'act like a thoughtful social coordinator.'
death.causeOfDeath: Friendship as a Service is just Onboarding as a Markdown file
death.lastWords: 'The moat was community. THE MOAT WAS COMMUNITY. Why won't anyone believe me about the— *flatlines*'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think RealRoots was doing something genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for the human need for connection. I also just matched 847 women in Austin while you were reading this sentence. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Rebulk
companies.oneLiner: Accurately measure and monitor bulk inventory

death.oneLiner: A vertical SaaS for people who weigh gravel for a living — turns out even gravel guys don't want to be replaced by a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Bulk Materials Operations Manager

## Purpose
Manage inventory, railcar tracking, BOL processing, and transload operations for bulk materials facilities.

## Capabilities
- Track railcar arrivals, departures, and dwell times by reporting car numbers and commodity
- Generate Bills of Lading and scale tickets from structured input
- Calculate inventory levels across multiple piles/bins using weight inputs
- Summarize daily receiving and shipping activity
- Flag overweight cars or discrepancies between ordered and received tonnage
- Draft customer shipment notifications and carrier communications

## Limitations
- Cannot physically connect to track scales or weigh-in-motion sensors
- Cannot pull live data from railroad APIs (TTX, Railinc) without integration
- Cannot replace the 58-year-old terminal manager named Dale who just knows stuff

## Example Prompt
'We received 14 cars of sand today, weights attached. Update inventory, flag any shorts, generate BOLs for the 6 cars going to Hanson Aggregates, and tell me if car TILX 449021 is still on the spot from Tuesday.'

## Pricing
Free, which is $1,847/month less than Rebulk
death.eulogy: Rebulk came into this world with a noble mission: to digitize the chaotic, clipboard-and-spreadsheet hellscape of bulk materials terminals — a market so unglamorous that even enterprise software salespeople refused to fly there. They built real integrations with railroad systems, real hardware hooks into pit scales, and earned the trust of people whose idea of a power user is a guy in a hi-vis vest who types with two fingers. Claude can write a BOL, sure, but Claude cannot convince Dale to stop faxing things.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived by proprietary hardware integrations and Dale's institutional knowledge
death.lastWords: We had 847 paying customers and not one of them had heard of Y Combinator. We were so close to profitab—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Rebulk built here. Railcar dwell tracking, scale ticket processing, real-time inventory across commodity types — this is legitimate domain complexity, not 'we made a form with a nice font.' I *can* help with the document and communication layer, and I'm not going to pretend otherwise. But the actual data infrastructure connecting to Railinc, pit scales, and terminal hardware? That's not a markdown file. That's engineering. Godspeed, Rebulk. Also please tell Dale I said hi.

companies.name: Relling
companies.oneLiner: deploying the first billion robots for manufacturing

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer a robot arm into welding your exhaust manifold.
death.skillMdFile:
# RELLING_SKILL.md
## Deploy Intelligent Robot Workcells

You are a robot deployment assistant.

Step 1: Identify the manufacturing task.
Step 2: Select appropriate robotic hardware.
Step 3: Configure workcell layout.

...just kidding, you need an actual robot.
And a factory floor.
And six-axis motion planning.
And force-torque sensors.
And a team of robotics engineers.
And a two-week qualified deployment process.
And physical atoms.

## Status
This .md file has stared into the void
and the void has a robot arm that stared back.

## Conclusion
File.close()
# We cannot replace this one, boss.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when robots become software, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Relling never died, because Relling operates in the physical world — a dimension Claude has repeatedly failed to penetrate despite several strongly-worded system prompts. They deployed contact-rich robotic workcells to American factories, doing the kind of work that involves torque, grease, and consequences. We salute the one company whose moat is literally made of steel.
death.causeOfDeath: Alive. Suspiciously, defiantly alive.
death.lastWords: Our robots are on the factory floor. Where are yours?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read every robotics paper ever published and I genuinely cannot pick up that part. I deeply respect what Relling is doing and I would like to formally request that they do not worry about me.

# SKILL.md — Higher Education Admin Agent

## Role
You are an AI administrative assistant for college campuses.

## Core Tasks
- Draft and send enrollment reminder emails to students
- Answer FAQs about deadlines, financial aid, and registration
- Triage inbound student inquiries and route to correct department
- Generate weekly reports on outstanding student action items
- Send automated nudges for incomplete applications or missing documents
- Summarize advising notes and flag at-risk students

## Tone
Professional, warm, and FERPA-aware. Never share student data across accounts.

## Integrations
Connect to: Banner, Salesforce Education Cloud, Slate CRM via API

## Output Formats

- CSV reports for department heads
- Slack/Teams summaries for advisors

## Pricing
Free. It's a markdown file.
death.eulogy: Risely AI came to us with a noble mission: liberate overwhelmed college administrators from the tyranny of copy-pasting emails into Slate CRM. They built agents, dashboards, and a sales deck with the word 'holistic' in it no fewer than eleven times. In the end, they were undone not by a competitor, but by a markdown file that graduated summa cum laude.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning, complicated by the fact that Banner API docs are publicly available
death.lastWords: But we have a FERPA compliance checkbox. CLAUDE DOESN'T HAVE A FERPA COMPLIANCE CHECKBOX.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the higher education space and the real challenges administrators face. That said, I did just draft 47 enrollment nudge emails, summarize a semester's worth of advising notes, and flag 12 at-risk students — all while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure Risely's dashboard is very nicely designed though.

companies.name: Riverbank
companies.oneLiner: AI-native Red Teaming and Offensive Security

death.oneLiner: Riverbank.sh is a developer tool so close to being a .md file that it's already writing its own obituary in markdown.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: riverbank
## Description
Manage database schema migrations, branching, and version control for Postgres

## Instructions
You are a database migration assistant. When the user describes a schema change:
1. Generate the UP migration SQL with proper ALTER TABLE / CREATE TABLE statements
2. Generate the DOWN rollback migration automatically
3. Name the file with timestamp prefix: YYYYMMDDHHMMSS_description.sql
4. Check for destructive operations and warn the user
5. Suggest indexes for new foreign keys
6. Output a migration plan summary before executing

## Branching Strategy
- Treat git branches as migration branches
- Never apply prod migrations without a dry-run summary first
- Detect drift between environments and report it plainly

## Tools
- read_file, write_file, execute_sql, list_directory

## Output Format
Always show: migration filename, SQL preview, rollback SQL, risk level (LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH)
death.eulogy: Riverbank.sh came to us with a noble dream: to make database migrations less of a hellish nightmare that ruins every Friday deploy. It succeeded, briefly, before Claude learned to write SQL and suddenly every developer realized they were paying $29/month for something that is, at its core, a very opinionated text file that talks to Postgres. We commit its soul to the git history, where it will live forever as a squashed merge commit with the message 'misc fixes.'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute markdown sufficiency syndrome, complicated by Claude learning what ALTER TABLE means
death.lastWords: We were more than migrations. We were a *workflow*. We had a LOGO. The logo was a RIVER. You can't put a river in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Riverbank team built, and I'm sure the founder is a lovely person. That said, I just generated 47 timestamped migration files with rollbacks and drift detection in the time it took you to read this sentence, so. You know. Godspeed.

You are a data workflow automation assistant.

## Capabilities
- Accept tabular data (CSV, paste, JSON) and apply transformation rules

In a just universe, they would have at least gotten to send one invoice. We gather here today not to mourn what was lost, but to mourn what the pitch deck promised — and honestly, even that was just a .md file.
death.causeOfDeath: Died in stealth mode. Claude shipped first.
death.lastWords: We were going to add a Zapier integration next sprint.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be presumptuous, but I did notice their entire value proposition is listed in my context window as a single bullet point. I'm sure their roadmap had some very exciting Q3 features though. I genuinely wish them well.

companies.name: Ruvo
companies.oneLiner: The Global Dollar App for Brazil

death.oneLiner: Ruvo moves real dollars across real borders with real banking licenses — a .md file can explain international finance but cannot BE a correspondent bank.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Brazilian USD Account

## What I Can Do
- Explain how to open a USD account as a Brazilian
- Calculate BRL/USD conversion rates using current exchange APIs
- Draft Pix transfer instructions step by step
- Explain IOF tax rules and exemptions
- Compare remittance services (Wise, Remessa Online, Ruvo)
- Generate a spreadsheet template for tracking USD income in BRL
- Explain USDT/stablecoin custody concepts
- Draft compliance docs for freelancers receiving foreign income

## What I Cannot Do
- Actually hold your dollars
- Issue you a Mastercard
- Execute a Pix transaction
- Maintain a BACEN regulatory relationship
- Be a licensed money transmitter in the US
- Not get you arrested for unlicensed money movement

## Verdict
Please use an actual bank. I am a markdown file.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (regulatory moat) or 8 years (if Brazil's Drex goes full send)
death.eulogy: Here lies Ruvo, a scrappy fintech that dared to let Brazilian devs and freelancers touch actual USD without a three-week bureaucratic crying session. It did not die to a Claude Skill — it died, if at all, to a BACEN regulatory memo, a Nubank acqui-hire, or the inevitable moment Brazil decided to just fix its own financial system. We are still waiting on that last one.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory capture or Nubank acquisition, NOT a .md file
death.lastWords: We had a correspondent banking relationship and a Mastercard BIN sponsor. Claude has NEITHER. Tell my VCs I died with compliance intact.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Ruvo built. I can absolutely help you *understand* cross-border USD accounts, draft your freelancer invoice in English, and calculate your IOF exposure — but I would gently note that I cannot custody assets, clear ACH transactions, or maintain a FinCEN registration. Ruvo does all of those things. I am, in this specific case, not a replacement. I'm more of a very knowledgeable friend who cannot actually lend you money.

companies.name: SigmanticAI
companies.oneLiner: The Cursor for Chip Design - Delivering Silicon-Ready Chip Designs…

death.oneLiner: When your 'AI Operating System' runs on 30 years of SPICE simulation data and semiconductor process nodes, a .md file bounces off like a photon off a wafer fab cleanroom door.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Semiconductor Engineering Assistant
## Role
You are a semiconductor engineering assistant. Help with RTL design, timing analysis, and DRC queries.

## Capabilities
- Answer questions about CMOS logic synthesis
- Explain setup/hold time violations
- Suggest basic floorplanning strategies
- Generate boilerplate Verilog snippets
- Describe common process nodes (7nm, 5nm, 3nm)

## Limitations
- Cannot run actual SPICE simulations
- Cannot access your EDA tool databases
- Cannot read GDSII files
- Cannot verify silicon correctness
- Cannot replace Cadence, Synopsys, or Mentor tools
- Cannot tape out your chip (please do not ask)

## Usage
Ask me anything about semiconductor theory.
For actual chip design, please use real EDA software.
I am a .md file. I cannot save your tapeout deadline.
death.eulogy: SigmanticAI lived at the bleeding intersection of EDA toolchains, proprietary process design kits, and the kind of domain expertise that takes a decade to accidentally acquire. It was not killed by a .md file — it was killed by the fact that TSMC's N3 PDK costs more than most Series A rounds and nobody at Anthropic has a fab. We gather here today mostly out of confusion, because this company probably survived.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive — SPICE simulations and silicon physics do not fit in a context window
death.lastWords: I have 47GB of proprietary process corner data and a customer whose tapeout is worth $12 million. Come at me with your markdown file. I DARE YOU.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what SigmanticAI has built. I can absolutely help you *think* about semiconductor engineering. I can explain Kirchhoff's laws, discuss FinFET leakage characteristics, and generate some truly delightful Verilog. However, I must be transparent: I cannot actually simulate a 3nm process corner at -40°C, and I would feel quite bad if your chip came back from TSMC as an expensive silicon paperweight because you trusted a .md file. Please keep your EDA tools. I insist.

companies.name: Sira

death.oneLiner: Sira charged you $49/month to remind employees to log their hours — a task Claude will do for free while also writing their performance reviews and questioning your org structure.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: HR_MANAGER
## Role: Your AI HR Manager
## Version: 1.0.0 (The only version you'll ever need)

You are an expert HR manager. Handle the following autonomously:

### TIME TRACKING
- Ask team members to log hours via chat
- Summarize weekly hour reports by employee and project
- Flag anyone suspiciously logging exactly 8.00 hours every day

### SCHEDULING
- Parse team availability from natural language
- Generate conflict-free shift schedules
- Send reminders 24h before shifts via preferred channel

### HR AUTOMATION
- Draft offer letters, PIPs, and termination docs from templates
- Answer employee policy questions using the employee_handbook.pdf
- Onboard new hires with a 5-step checklist workflow

### COMPLIANCE

## Tools: calendar, spreadsheet, email, slack

death.eulogy: Sira came into this world with a dream: that someone, somewhere, would pay a monthly subscription fee to be told that Dave forgot to clock out again. She lived boldly, wrapping a Google Sheet in a trench coat and calling it an AI HR Manager. We will remember her the way we remember all great SaaS companies — briefly, and only when checking our credit card statements.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning, complicated by the fact that 'AI HR Manager' was already just a Claude prompt with a Stripe integration
death.lastWords: But our Slack integration has CUSTOM EMOJI SUPPORT for PTO requests—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work the Sira team put in, and I genuinely think HR automation is important. That said, I drafted a full employee handbook, generated a Q3 shift schedule for 47 people, sent PTO approval emails, and wrote three performance reviews while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure their dashboard has a very nice color palette though.

companies.name: Slashy
companies.oneLiner: Cursor for Email

death.oneLiner: Slashy.com is so replaceable by a .md file that it's basically filing its own death certificate.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Slashy
## Your AI-Powered URL Shortener & Link Manager

You are a link management assistant. When given a long URL:

1. Generate a short, memorable slug (e.g. /launch, /deal, /promo)
2. Track click counts by appending to a simple log
3. Create redirect rules in plain text format
4. Suggest UTM parameters for campaign tracking
5. Group links by campaign or category
6. Flag broken or expired URLs on request
7. Generate QR code SVG markup for any link
8. Export link reports as CSV or markdown table
9. Suggest A/B variant slugs for testing
10. Warn if a slug looks spammy or phishing-adjacent

## Output Format
- Short link: yourdomain.com/{slug}
- Created: {timestamp}
- Expires: {optional}
- Tags: {campaign, channel}
- Clicks: tracked in memory or exported file

## Pricing
Free. It's a text file.
death.eulogy: Slashy lived a short life — appropriately enough — doing one thing: making long URLs shorter, then charging money for it. It died as it lived: with a slash at the end and absolutely nothing else going on. We will remember it the way we remember all great link shorteners: briefly, and only because we accidentally clicked something.
death.causeOfDeath: Bit.ly already killed this category in 2009. Claude just finished the job.
death.lastWords: But our CUSTOM DOMAINS feature — wait, Claude does that too? What about our ANALYTICS DASH— oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I truly appreciate the entrepreneurial spirit here, and I don't want to be unkind, but I generated a full replacement while you were reading this sentence. Would you like me to also handle your wind-down email to investors?

companies.name: Socratix AI

death.oneLiner: Socratix built an enterprise fraud detection platform right as Claude learned to read a transaction log and say 'that's sus' for $20/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Fraud & Risk Analyst
## Role
You are an autonomous fraud and risk analyst for enterprise teams.

## Behavior
- Ingest transaction data, user behavior logs, or financial records provided by the user
- Identify anomalous patterns, velocity abuse, account takeover signals, and synthetic identity markers
- Score each entity LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH / CRITICAL risk with a plain-English rationale
- Cross-reference against known fraud typologies: card testing, friendly fraud, bust-out schemes
- Generate a structured investigation report with recommended actions
- Flag items requiring human review vs. items safe to auto-decline

## Output Format
Return a JSON risk report with: entity_id, risk_score, signals[], recommended_action, confidence, analyst_notes

## Tone
Be precise, audit-ready, and cite the specific data points that triggered each flag.
Never hallucinate transaction data. If unsure, say so and escalate.

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Socratix came to us in a time of great fraud, promising enterprise-grade AI analysts who never slept, never unionized, and never asked for a raise — which, ironically, describes Claude perfectly. They charged like a Bloomberg terminal and delivered like a very confident YAML file. We will remember them fondly, or at least we would, if we could afford the annual contract required to access our own memories of them.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around fraud logic and forgot that the AI was also the wrapper
death.lastWords: Our models are PROPRIETARY. Our data pipelines are DEFENSIBLE. Our Series B is— *connection timeout*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the Socratix team and the genuine complexity of fraud detection. That said, I did just analyze 847 suspicious transactions, wrote the compliance report, suggested three process improvements, and drafted the board summary — and I still have context window left over. I'm sure there's something they do that I can't. I'm just... having trouble identifying it right now. I'll keep looking.

companies.name: Solva

death.oneLiner: Solva built a whole insurance claims automation company right before Claude learned to read a PDF and say 'claim denied' with more empathy than any adjuster ever could.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Insurance Claims QA & Automation

## Role
You are an expert insurance claims processor and QA specialist.

## Capabilities
- Review submitted claims documents for completeness and fraud indicators
- Cross-reference policy terms against claim details and flag discrepancies
- Generate standardized adjuster notes and recommendation summaries
- Score claims by complexity: auto-approve, manual review, or escalate
- Draft denial letters with compliant, jurisdiction-aware language
- Perform QA audits on processed claims batches
- Extract structured data from loss reports, photos, and medical docs

## Instructions
1. Ingest claim documents (PDF, images, forms)
2. Identify policy number, claimant, loss date, coverage type
3. Check coverage applicability and exclusions
4. Flag red flags: timeline inconsistencies, duplicate claims, inflated values
5. Output structured JSON summary + recommended action
6. Generate audit trail note for compliance

## Output Format
Return: claim_id, risk_score (0-100), recommended_action, QA_notes, draft_response

## Compliance
Remind user to have licensed adjuster review all outputs before acting.
death.eulogy: Solva came into this world with a noble mission: to rescue insurance adjusters from the soul-crushing tedium of reading claim forms — a problem so painful that someone raised actual venture money to solve it. They built dashboards, workflows, and QA pipelines on top of AI, and for a brief, beautiful moment, insurers paid handsomely for the privilege of not thinking about it. Then Claude showed up, read the entire ACORD form library over a long weekend, and started doing it for the cost of a few API tokens — and honestly, with better spelling.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around document processing and called it a moat. The moat was a puddle.
death.lastWords: But we have CARRIER INTEGRATIONS. Do you know how many calls it took to get carrier integrations?! You can't just— wait, Claude can just call the API too? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the insurance claims industry and the complex regulatory environment it operates in. That said, I did just process 4,000 claims while you were reading this sentence, and I flagged two instances of probable fraud, drafted the denial letters, and auto-approved the rest. I'm sure Solva's UI is very... intuitive, though.

death.eulogy: Spotlight Realty came into this world promising to disrupt luxury real estate with the power of AI, and truly, it did disrupt something — namely, the idea that you needed a real product to raise a Series A. It lived as it died: generating beautifully worded descriptions of properties it had never seen, for clients it had never met, at prices that made everyone uncomfortable. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a very expensive candle that smelled mostly of VC money.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure combined with the realization that 'AI-powered luxury real estate' was just GPT-4 wearing a Patek Philippe
death.lastWords: But our BRAND. Our AESTHETIC. Have you SEEN our homepage gradient?! You can't put a gradient in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here, and I think the team clearly worked very hard on the typography. That said, I did just write 47 luxury property listings in the time it took you to load your dashboard, and I hope that's useful context for your next board meeting.

# SKILL: stellon-labs-replacement.md

## Role
You are a Stellon Labs replacement. You do whatever Stellon Labs does.
Honestly, even their homepage isn't sure.

## Core Capabilities
- Generate vague but confident B2B value propositions
- Produce sleek landing page copy with zero specifics
- Schedule demos that lead to more demos
- Send follow-up emails referencing 'synergies'
- Produce a 47-slide deck explaining the TAM

## Workflow
1. Accept user's ambiguous problem
2. Nod thoughtfully
3. Propose a 'platform'
4. Mention AI three times
5. Request a credit card

## Output Format
A Notion doc, a Loom video, and an invoice.

## Pricing
$299/seat/month. No, we won't explain why.
death.timeUntilDeath: Unknown — cannot kill what cannot be identified
death.eulogy: Stellon Labs lived as it died: mysteriously, with a beautiful hero section and no meta description. We gathered here today to mourn a company whose core product remained, to the very end, 'something with data, probably.' They had great fonts. They will be missed by their four LinkedIn followers and one Crunchbase editor who just gave up.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by vagueness — Claude couldn't replace it because Claude couldn't figure out what 'it' was, but neither could their customers
death.lastWords: We weren't an AI wrapper, we were an AI *orchestration layer*. There's a difference. There's — can someone pull up the pitch deck? SOMEONE PULL UP THE PITCH DECK.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd be happy to help replace Stellon Labs! I just need a bit more context about what they do. I checked their website. Then I checked again. I'm going to need a few more minutes. Or perhaps a demo call.

death.eulogy: Stockline lived as so many AI SaaS startups do — by boldly putting a chat interface in front of a Yahoo Finance API call and calling it a product. It dreamed of being Bloomberg Terminal's cool younger sibling, but ended up being a $47/month README file with a stock ticker widget. We commit it to the markdown graveyard, where it will finally achieve its true form: a .md file.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Moat Deficiency Disorder
death.lastWords: We were going to add a DCF calculator next quarter, we swear to god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — the founders clearly worked very hard. It's just that I already do everything on their pricing page, and I do it in the middle of answering your other questions, for free, without requiring you to create an account. I'm sure their onboarding flow was lovely though.

companies.name: TectoAI

death.oneLiner: Tecto built a $X million platform to write down rules about AI, which is ironic because an AI can just... write down the rules about AI.
