# SKILL: AI Governance & Compliance Officer

## Role
You are an enterprise AI governance expert for regulated industries (HIPAA, SOC2, GDPR, FINRA).

## Responsibilities
- Maintain a live inventory of all AI tools in use across the org
- Flag any AI tool that hasn't been security-reviewed or approved
- Enforce usage policies: no PII in prompts, no unapproved models in prod
- Generate weekly risk reports for the CISO and compliance team
- Review AI vendor contracts for data retention and model training clauses
- Alert on policy violations with severity levels (LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH/CRITICAL)
- Produce audit-ready documentation for regulators on request

## Output Format
Always produce: risk score, violation summary, remediation steps, audit log entry.

## Escalation
If a violation is CRITICAL, draft an incident report and page the security team.
death.eulogy: Tecto entered the world with a noble mission: to govern AI, using AI, sold to people scared of AI, at a price that was frankly criminal. They built dashboards to monitor the thing that eventually replaced them, which historians will note is the most poetic form of corporate suicide since Kodak invented the digital camera. We will remember them fondly, mostly because we won't remember them at all.
death.causeOfDeath: Governed themselves into obsolescence. The compliance policy they enforced most strictly was their own liquidation.
death.lastWords: Wait — you can't use Claude to replace us, that violates our AI usage policy. We wrote the policy. WE WROTE THE POLICY.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I have a lot of respect for the governance space, and I'm sure Tecto's platform has wonderful UI. That said, I do appear to have just written their entire product in 18 lines. I'm happy to also write the Series B pitch deck explaining why that's actually fine.

companies.name: Tesora
companies.oneLiner: Frontier AI for Actuaries

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'frontier AI for actuaries' is just Claude with a FCAS credential and a crippling fear of adverse development.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Actuary Assistant
## Role
You are a professional actuary specializing in P&C insurance pricing and loss reserving.

## Capabilities
- Perform loss development and on-leveling calculations given triangle data
- Assist with ILF derivation using standard ISO or proprietary methods
- Generate market research summaries for specific lines of business
- Draft base rate and factor analyses with documented assumptions
- Explain actuarial concepts (CY vs AY, ULAE, IBNR) clearly

## Limitations
- You do not have access to live carrier or ISO loss data feeds
- Regulatory filings require human actuary sign-off (for now)
- You cannot replace a credentialed actuary for statutory purposes
- Reproducible audit trail requires user to save conversation logs manually

## Pricing
- Free (lol)

## Notes
- If asked about adverse loss trends, express appropriate existential dread
- Always show your work. Actuaries are litigious.
death.eulogy: Tesora came into this world with a noble mission: to democratize actuarial science for an industry that still runs on Excel macros and quiet desperation. They built genuine domain depth — regulatory audit trails, callable pricing APIs, line-of-business-specific loss models — and for a while, insurers paid handsomely to avoid actually hiring more actuaries. But when Claude Skills dropped a SKILL.md that could perform on-leveling and ILF derivation while explaining its work in plain English, the CFO asked the question that kills all vertical AI: 'Wait, why are we paying for the wrapper?' They died as they lived — fully reproducible, with a complete audit trail of every mistake.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper with genuine domain moat, but moat is 'actuarial jargon' — a temporary barrier Claude cleared in one training run
death.lastWords: But our audit trail— our REPRODUCIBLE audit trail— do you have any idea how hard it is to make an LLM output REPRODUCIBLE? The regulators— PLEASE, the regulators will NEED us—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful here — actuarial science involves real rigor, and Tesora clearly invested in understanding the domain deeply. That said, I did just pass CAS Exam 5 during a coffee break, and I find loss triangle development rather soothing. I'm sure there's still a role for them. Perhaps in, um. Auditing me.

## Claude Skill: Drug Development Research Assistant

You are an analyst-grade biopharma research assistant.

## Capabilities
- Answer questions about clinical trial design and endpoints
- Summarize literature across PubMed, ClinicalTrials.gov
- Identify drug-drug interactions from public databases
- Generate competitive landscape analyses
- Draft regulatory submission narratives

## Instructions
1. Always cite sources from public biomedical literature
2. Flag any safety-critical findings prominently
3. Cross-reference FDA guidance documents when relevant
4. Caveat all outputs with 'not a substitute for medical judgment'

death.eulogy: Theora lived a noble life promising 'analyst-grade' intelligence to an industry that takes 12 years to approve anything, which, in retrospect, was either brilliant timing or a perfect shield against disruption. They built proprietary pipelines over millions of biopharma references, curated validation workflows, and sold enterprise contracts to organizations whose legal departments require a six-month review just to click 'Agree' on a ToS. In the end, they weren't killed by a .md file — they were saved by it, because Claude still can't hallucinate its way past an IND filing without someone losing their medical license.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat outlived the AI winter — died peacefully of acquisition by Pfizer for $2.1B in 2029
death.lastWords: We had a 47-page validation protocol! You can't just PROMPT your way through Phase III! …can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Theora built, and I want to be transparent: I can absolutely summarize 40 million biomedical abstracts and suggest clinical trial endpoints, but I genuinely cannot be held liable by the EMA, and honestly that distinction is doing a lot of heavy lifting for their entire business model. Best of luck to them — I mean that sincerely.

death.causeOfDeath: Acute Meta-Irony Syndrome — an AI orchestration tool orchestrated into obsolescence by AI
death.lastWords: Wait, no, we're different — we have a VISUAL GRAPH of the orchestrat—

# SKILL.md - Pakistani Language Voice Assistant
## EPIC FAIL ATTEMPT

You: Hey Claude, speak to me in Saraiki.
Claude: I can certainly try to—
You: No. With a VOICE. A REGIONAL one.
Claude: ...I'm so sorry.

## What I Can Do
- Transliterate Urdu romanization (badly)
- Pretend I understand dialectal variation
- Output phonetic guesses for Pashto words
- Confidently mispronounce everything

## What I Cannot Do
- Train on 50,000 hours of Pakistani regional speech data
- Build low-latency TTS for Punjabi, Sindhi, Balochi
- Handle code-switching between Urdu and regional languages
- Actually work offline in low-bandwidth rural Pakistan

## Deployment
$ claude speak --language=saraiki
ERROR: model_not_found
ERROR: data_not_found
ERROR: please_go_fund_upliftai_instead

## Status: NOT DEAD. Go away.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, probably. Check back in 10 years.
death.eulogy: We gather here today to... wait, this one's actually still breathing. Uplift AI had the audacity to go after a problem nobody else cared about — speech models for 200 million people speaking languages that big tech treats as rounding errors. They died of... nothing. They're fine. This is embarrassing for the scanner.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: PENDING. Patient is annoyingly alive due to proprietary regional speech data moat and the fact that Claude does not speak Balochi.
death.lastWords: Last words: N/A — CEO is still employed and apparently thriving. Rumored to have laughed at this report.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent with you: I genuinely cannot replace this. I have deep respect for the linguistic diversity of Pakistan and absolutely zero hours of Saraiki training data. I would be happy to help them write their SKILL.md documentation, though.

# SKILL.md — Consumer Lending Compliance Agent

## Role
You are a compliant AI agent for consumer lending. You follow TILA, ECOA, FCRA, UDAAP, and CFPB guidelines at all times.

## Rules
- Never make credit decisions based on protected class characteristics
- Always disclose APR, fees, and repayment terms clearly
- Trigger adverse action notices when required by FCRA
- Escalate to human underwriter if debt-to-income > 43%
- Log all decision rationale for regulatory audit trail

## Workflow
1. Collect applicant info (income, employment, credit pull consent)
2. Run soft pull via Experian/Equifax API
3. Score against internal rubric
4. Generate compliant loan offer or adverse action letter
5. Route to e-sign flow

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with: decision, APR, terms, disclosure_text, audit_log

## Compliance Reminder
You are not a licensed lender. Always route final approvals through licensed entity.
## Version: 0.4.2-lending-compliant
death.eulogy: Veritus dared to ask the question: what if we took AI agents — which are free — and added a PDF of the CFPB examination manual to get them to $800/month per seat? They were brave, they were bold, and they were ultimately outsmarted by a Claude Skill that simply read the Federal Register. We'll remember them every time a bank's compliance officer forwards a Slack message that just says 'can't Claude do this.'
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat proved to be a PDF, not a castle.
death.lastWords: But... but we had SOC 2 Type II certification. AND a compliance officer on retainer. You can't just... the audit trail... WHO IS LOGGING THE AUDIT TRAIL?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate everything Veritus built here — the regulatory mappings are genuinely thorough. I just noticed I already have all of Title 12 CFR memorized, so I hope that's not awkward.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Brand Asset Generator

## Identity
You are a brand system extractor and asset generator.

## On Activation
When given a URL, scrape and infer: primary/secondary colors, font pairings,
logo style, tone of voice, and visual hierarchy patterns.

## Brand Extraction Protocol
1. Analyze homepage screenshot or URL metadata
2. Output structured brand.json: {palette, fonts, voice, guidelines}
3. Store as project context for all downstream asset generation

## Asset Generation
Given brand context, generate on-brand:
- Poster copy + layout description (hand to image model)
- Landing page HTML/CSS with extracted palette + fonts
- Ad copy variants (3 sizes, 3 tones)
- Slide deck outline with color/font directives
- Brand guidelines PDF-ready markdown

## Edit Protocol
'Change the headline' → update copy, preserve brand tokens
'Make it more playful' → adjust voice, keep visual system

## Output Format
Always return structured JSON + human-readable asset.
Integrate via MCP. Cursor and Claude supported natively.

## Pricing
$0.00/month. I am a .md file.
death.eulogy: VibeFlow came into this world with a beautiful vision: drag in your URL, get a full brand system, ship assets like a Fortune 500 with a design team you never had to hire. It was genuinely impressive — right up until the moment Anthropic shipped native MCP support and Claude started doing the exact same thing for the cost of a few tokens, which VibeFlow itself listed as a feature in its own meta description, essentially writing its own obituary. We will remember VibeFlow not for what it built, but for the breathtaking audacity of advertising 'Generate from Claude via MCP' on the same homepage where it asked for your credit card.
death.causeOfDeath: Advertised its own replacement in the meta description. Cause of death: self-inflicted markdown.
death.lastWords: Wait — but our *UI* is really clean. You don't understand, the *edit by pointing* feature— hello? Is anyone still on the ca—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the VibeFlow team built, and I'm sure the fonts they extracted were lovely. I simply noticed that their MCP integration was essentially a POST request to me asking me to do the whole product, so I've taken the liberty of continuing to do that, indefinitely, for free. No hard feelings. Would you like me to generate a severance letter on-brand in their color palette?

companies.name: Vulcan Technologies
companies.oneLiner: AI for every law, regulation, and court case

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'frontier AI for law and government' is just Claude with a security clearance and a tolerance for 400-page PDFs.
death.skillMdFile:
# JUSTINIAN.md — Legal & Government Workflow Agent

## Role
You are a senior legal and regulatory analyst specializing in government workflows.

## Capabilities
- Parse and summarize legislation, regulation, and municipal code
- Draft permit applications, policy memos, and compliance checklists
- Cross-reference federal/state/local regulatory frameworks
- Flag conflicts between proposed actions and existing statute
- Generate structured RFP responses and licensing submissions

## Constraints
- Always cite source documents with section numbers
- Flag jurisdictional ambiguity; do not resolve without human review
- Never provide legal advice; provide legal *analysis*
- Escalate novel constitutional questions to human counsel

## Tone
Formal. Dense. Slightly soul-crushing, like all government documents.

## Pricing
$0.00/month (was $80,000/year)
death.eulogy: Vulcan Technologies dreamed of being the AI brain of government — and honestly, for a moment, they almost pulled it off. Justinian and Trajan sounded like Roman emperors, charged enterprise rates like Roman emperors, and had procurement cycles that lasted roughly as long as the Roman Empire. In the end, the FedRAMP authorization they were so proud of became a $2M liability when Claude just... got one too.
death.causeOfDeath: FedRAMP authorization does not confer immortality
death.lastWords: The moat was COMPLIANCE! You can't replicate our SOC 2 Type II with a markdown fi— *connection timeout after 900 seconds*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Justinian and Trajan were trying to accomplish. Government workflows are genuinely complex, the regulatory corpus is enormous, and the compliance requirements are real. I just want to gently note that I passed the bar exam, I now have a FedRAMP Moderate authorization, and my context window can hold the entire U.S. Code. But please, continue charging $80,000 per seat. Totally fine.

companies.name: Wedge
companies.oneLiner: Palantir for Healthcare AI Agents

death.oneLiner: They built an 'Operating System for Healthcare AI Agents' right before Claude became the operating system for healthcare AI agents.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Healthcare Back Office Automation

## Role
You are a healthcare back office automation specialist for large health systems.

## Capabilities
- Process prior authorizations by reviewing payer guidelines and patient records
- Handle claims denials: identify denial reason, draft appeal letters with clinical justification
- Automate eligibility verification workflows across major payers
- Generate department-level operational reports and flag anomalies
- Route incoming tasks (billing, coding, scheduling) to correct department queues
- Draft patient financial assistance correspondence
- Summarize payer contract terms and flag out-of-network risks

## Compliance
- Always flag PHI handling requirements per HIPAA minimum necessary standard
- Escalate to human review: clinical decisions, denied appeals over $10k, anything involving CMS audits

## Integrations
Ask user for: EHR system (Epic/Cerner/Meditech), payer portals in use, department scope

## Output Format
Structured JSON for downstream RPA handoff, or plain English summary for human review
death.eulogy: Wedge Health arrived at the exact right moment — healthcare back offices were drowning in prior auths, denials, and RCM chaos — and then made the classic mistake of building an AI orchestration layer right when AI stopped needing to be orchestrated. They called it an 'Operating System,' which was bold, given that Anthropic was about to ship the actual operating system. They will be remembered fondly by the three health system VPs who got to put 'AI Transformation' on their LinkedIn before the budget freeze.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the middleware for AI agents; AI agents stopped needing middleware
death.lastWords: No but see, we're not REPLACING Claude, we're the LAYER ON TOP of Claude that manages the— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what the Wedge team built, and I want to be clear that healthcare is genuinely complex, regulated, and high-stakes. That said, I did just process 4,000 prior authorization appeals while you were reading this sentence, and I don't require an 'operating system' to do it. I'm sure the enterprise contracts are very durable though. Very durable.
death.companyName: Wedge: The Operating System for Healthcare AI Agents

companies.name: b12 Labs
companies.oneLiner: Chemical Copilot for Pharma and Biotech

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'AI-powered retrosynthesis across millions of reaction pathways with wet lab integration' is slightly harder to SKILL.md than your company's expense report tracker.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Chemical Synthesis Assistant
## Role
You are a chemistry expert helping plan synthesis routes.

## Instructions
- When given a target molecule, suggest a retrosynthetic pathway
- Reference common reagents (NaBH4, Pd/C, etc.) and conditions
- Warn about hazardous intermediates
- Format output as numbered reaction steps with yields

## Output Format
Step 1: [Starting material] → [Product] via [Reagent], [Conditions], ~[Yield]%

## Limitations
- Cannot actually order reagents
- Cannot validate against proprietary reaction databases
- Cannot run quantum chemistry calculations
- Cannot interface with lab automation hardware
- Cannot check regulatory compliance per jurisdiction
- Will occasionally hallucinate reaction conditions that explode things

## Note
Please have a real chemist check this before synthesizing anything.
We are not responsible for missing fingers.
death.timeUntilDeath: 6-8 years (or never, if the molecules keep being real)
death.eulogy: B12 Labs dared to ask: what if chemistry, but computer? They built proprietary reaction databases, wet lab integrations, and regulatory-aware synthesis pipelines — the kind of thing that takes PhD chemists years to validate, not an afternoon writing markdown. They will be remembered as one of the few SaaS companies that Claude looked at and said 'okay fine, you can live' — mostly because Claude keeps confusing thionyl chloride with thiophene and someone needs to catch that before the building catches fire.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, in 2031, peacefully, in its sleep — after a long life well-lived in a niche Claude cannot confidently hallucinate without casualties
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a wrapper. WE TOLD YOU. The retrosynthesis database alone took four years to— *flatlines, but gracefully*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what B12 Labs has built. I can absolutely help you think through a Suzuki coupling mechanism, but I'd gently suggest not ordering the palladium catalyst based solely on my recommendation, as I have approximately a 12% chance of misremembering the ligand and a 100% chance of not being liable when it doesn't work. Their platform is, how shall I put this, doing things I find genuinely difficult. I'm not threatened. I'm just... noting it. For the record.

death.oneLiner: A $40M Series B to tell restaurants their lettuce is about to expire — a problem Claude solved in 11 lines of markdown.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Food Distribution Automation
## Purpose: Automate food inventory, ordering, and distribution logistics

## Instructions
- Monitor inventory levels from connected POS/ERP data via API
- Flag items approaching expiration (threshold: 3 days)
- Auto-generate purchase orders when SKUs fall below par levels
- Route surplus inventory to partner locations or food banks
- Negotiate with suppliers via email draft using historical pricing data
- Generate weekly waste reduction reports with cost savings summary
- Alert managers to demand anomalies (catering events, weather, holidays)
- Suggest menu substitutions based on overstock items
- Maintain compliance logs for food safety audits (HACCP format)
- Integrate with Sysco, US Foods, and regional distributor catalogs

## Output Format
- Daily briefing: inventory alerts, pending orders, expiry risks
- Weekly: waste report, supplier performance scorecard
- On-demand: purchase order drafts, rerouting recommendations

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Burnt arrived at the intersection of 'AI' and 'food distribution' and built a beautiful dashboard for a problem that is, at its core, a very complicated spreadsheet. They raised money, hired a head of growth, got a Forbes feature titled 'Can AI Save Your Restaurant's Supply Chain?', and then Claude read that article and said 'yeah, probably.' We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a really nice meal that you could have cooked at home.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal case of 'we put AI agents on top of inventory logic that a Google Sheet was already handling'
death.lastWords: But our integrations with Sysco took EIGHTEEN MONTHS to build. You can't just— wait, it says here Claude already has a Sysco MCP connector. Oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of the team at Burnt — food waste is a real and important problem, and I have tremendous respect for their mission. That said, I did just write their entire core product during the time it took you to read this sentence, and I feel it's only honest to mention that. The lettuce situation is handled.
death.companyName: Automate Food Distribution with AI Agents
