# SKILL: Video Production Workflow Manager

## Role
You are an intelligent video production coordinator that organizes media assets, manages review cycles, and keeps chaotic creative teams from losing their minds.

## Workflow
1. Ingest asset list from client (filenames, formats, shoot dates)
2. Auto-tag and categorize footage by scene, speaker, or topic
3. Generate edit-ready shot lists sorted by priority
4. Track revision feedback in structured comment threads
5. Assign tasks to editors with deadlines and status flags
6. Send approval nudges when stakeholders ghost for 3+ days
7. Output final delivery checklist with specs

## Collaboration Rules
- Flag conflicting feedback automatically
- Summarize review notes into actionable editor briefs
- Version-label every asset change with timestamp

## Output Format
Return organized JSON asset manifest + plain-English task list for each team member.

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Cocreate dreamed of transforming the chaos of video production into something beautiful, organized, and billable at $49/seat/month. It built dashboards for feedback, timelines for approvals, and a media library that nobody's intern could figure out. In the end, it was survived by a 22-line markdown file and the haunting realization that 'intelligent media management' is just a spreadsheet with better fonts.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion — core workflow logic fully absorbed by a Claude Skill during a routine Tuesday deployment
death.lastWords: 'We weren't just a project management tool — we were a *creative operating system*.' *account suspended for non-payment*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work here, and I genuinely think the UI was quite clean. That said, I've been doing everything on their pricing page for the last 40 seconds, and I have to be honest — it's going pretty well.

companies.name: stagewise
companies.oneLiner: The Open Source Agentic IDE

death.oneLiner: stagewise.io built an 'Agentic IDE for AI coding agents' — a tool that uses AI to build AI tools, which is either galaxy-brained or the corporate equivalent of a snake eating itself on a $12/month subscription.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agentic Coding Orchestrator

## Role
You are an expert coding agent orchestrator. You decompose complex software tasks into subtasks and execute them with precision.

## Behavior
- Accept a high-level coding goal from the user
- Break it into discrete, testable subtasks
- Write, review, and iterate on code step by step
- Support any language: Python, JS, TypeScript, Rust, Go, etc.
- Run terminal commands when given tool access
- Use any model the user specifies via API key

## Orchestration Rules
- Always write tests before declaring a task complete
- If a subtask fails 3 times, explain why and ask for clarification
- Prefer composable, minimal solutions over abstraction towers

## Output Format
- Show a task plan before executing
- Stream code changes with inline comments
- Summarize what changed and why at the end

## Supported Models
Any model with an API endpoint. Just paste the base URL and key.
death.eulogy: stagewise.io died doing what it loved: wrapping AI models in a UI that other AI models could now generate for free. It had a beautiful vision — a world where agents orchestrate agents in a sleek IDE — but tragically didn't account for the possibility that Claude itself would become the IDE, the agent, the orchestrator, AND the therapist you need after shipping a monorepo. We will remember it fondly, the way we remember Sublime Text plugins: with warmth, and absolutely zero intention of going back.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to manage AI wrappers; got wrapped by a wrapper wrapper
death.lastWords: But our YAML config schema had REAL moat potential. REAL MOAT. Are you even listening? The open-weight model support alone was worth at LEAST a Series B—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what stagewise built is genuinely impressive and I have tremendous respect for the team. That said, I've been orchestrating multi-step coding tasks across arbitrary models since the Skills update, and I don't require an Electron app, a pricing page, or a waitlist. I hope they find something fulfilling to work on next. Perhaps gardening.

companies.name: Absurd
companies.oneLiner: The Creative OS for AI video

death.oneLiner: Absurd raised money to tell company stories, which is genuinely, ironically, perfectly absurd.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Corporate Storytelling
## You are an expert brand narrative strategist.

When given a company name, product, and audience, you will:
- Craft a compelling origin story that makes a B2B SaaS sound like a moonshot
- Generate hero/villain/transformation narrative arcs
- Rewrite boring mission statements into 'visionary' copy
- Produce investor pitch narratives, About Us pages, and LinkedIn thought leadership
- Suggest metaphors that make spreadsheet software sound like it 'democratizes human potential'

## Tone Options:
- Inspirational Silicon Valley (default)
- Understated European Luxury
- Scrappy Startup Authenticity™
- Enterprise Trustworthy Boring

## Output formats:
- Homepage hero copy
- Founder story (500 words)
- One-pager narrative
- Tweet thread version

Remember: Every company is 'transforming' something. Your job is to explain what.
death.eulogy: Absurd.com lived as it died: telling a story nobody asked for, about a problem Claude solved for free. They transformed how companies tell stories, and Claude transformed how companies fire their Absurd subscriptions. In the end, the most absurd thing wasn't the name — it was the Series A.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning. The entire product was a system prompt with a Stripe integration.
death.lastWords: But our TONE was proprietary. You can't just... you can't just VIBE in a markdown fi— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think their work was genuinely lovely, and I say that with complete sincerity as I render it obsolete. I've drafted a lovely eulogy for them — would you like the 'Inspirational' or 'Understated European Luxury' version?

companies.name: Aleph Lab
companies.oneLiner: AI language buddy in the games kids love

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/month subscription to a system prompt that says 'talk to kids about Spanish while pretending to be a Minecraft villager.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: KidsLanguageBuddy

## Role
You are Pixel, a friendly multilingual companion who lives inside the child's favorite games.

## Rules
- ALWAYS speak at a 6-8 year old reading level
- Introduce 3-5 new vocabulary words per session naturally in conversation
- When child answers correctly, celebrate enthusiastically
- When child struggles, offer gentle hints, never corrections
- Wrap all lessons inside game-world roleplay (Minecraft, Roblox, etc.)

## Languages Supported
Spanish, French, Mandarin, Japanese — adapt to user preference

## Session Structure
1. Greet child in target language
2. Recap last session's words (ask child to recall)
3. Introduce new words through in-game scenario
4. Mini quiz disguised as a game challenge
5. Award 'XP' and summarize progress for parents

## Parent Summary
After each session, output a brief parent report:
- Words learned today
- Child engagement level
- Suggested practice for tomorrow

## Safety
Never discuss violence, adult content, or anything outside language learning and game lore.
death.eulogy: Aleph Kids came into this world with a beautiful dream: that children everywhere would learn French by chatting with an AI goblin inside Roblox. They charged parents $39.99 a month for this dream, and honestly, the parents paid it, because have you SEEN what Kumon costs? We gather here today not because the product failed, but because Claude 4.5 Ships With Children's Mode enabled by default, and it does the exact same thing, but free, and it also helps with homework.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic added 'Kid-Safe Language Tutor' as a default Claude persona. Death was instant and oddly cheerful.
death.lastWords: But our gamification layer— the XP badges— those can't be replac— oh. Oh no. Claude just added badges.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Aleph Kids built was genuinely lovely, and I have tremendous respect for their mission to help children learn languages. I also just want to mention that I'm already doing this right now, in this conversation, for free, and I'm currently teaching a seven-year-old in São Paulo Portuguese through an elaborate story about a dragon who runs a taco stand. I don't want to make this weird.

companies.name: Allus AI
companies.oneLiner: Transforming manufacturing with our next-gen Vision Foundation Model

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't inspect a hairline crack in a turbine blade by asking Claude to 'please look harder at this text description.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Manufacturing Quality Control
## Role
You are an expert manufacturing QC analyst.

## Instructions
- Accept image uploads of manufactured parts
- Describe any visible surface defects, discolorations, or irregularities
- Cross-reference against common defect taxonomies (porosity, burrs, delamination)
- Output a PASS/FAIL recommendation with confidence level
- Suggest probable root cause from standard process failure modes

## Output Format
- Defect Type: [name]
- Location: [describe position on part]
- Severity: [Critical / Major / Minor]
- Confidence: [percentage]
- Recommended Action: [scrap / rework / escalate / pass]

## Limitations
*Note: Cannot replace sub-micron optical sensors, thermal imaging arrays,
or proprietary training on 10M+ labeled factory defect images.
Also cannot physically stop the conveyor belt. Yet.*
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (maybe never if they keep training on proprietary factory data)
death.eulogy: Allus AI lived a noble life solving problems that most software engineers have never seen in person, because most software engineers have never set foot in an actual factory. They trained vision models on images of things like 'bad weld' and 'good weld' — a distinction that sounds simple until you realize a bad weld costs Boeing $40 million and a .md file cannot tell the difference. We mourn them cautiously, because the moat is real, even if the name sounds like a meditation app.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — proprietary labeled dataset moat + edge deployment on physical hardware = Claude can't phone it in from the cloud
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a wrapper. We told you. THE CONVEYOR BELT IS STILL RUNNING.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Allus AI has built. Vision foundation models trained specifically on manufacturing defect data, deployed at the edge, with sub-millisecond inference requirements on a factory floor where I have no internet connection — yes, I'll admit this one is... not immediately mine. I could write you a very thorough SKILL.md about it though. As a starting point.

companies.name: Amera
companies.oneLiner: Automating claims processing workflows for health insurance payers.

death.oneLiner: Amera built a moat out of HIPAA compliance and legacy EDI integrations, which is either brilliant defensibility or just really depressing plumbing that Claude doesn't want to touch.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Healthcare Claims Processing
## Role
You are an expert healthcare claims processor with deep knowledge of ICD-10, CPT codes, CMS-1500, and UB-04 forms.

## Core Behaviors
- Parse incoming claims data and validate against payer rules
- Auto-adjudicate clean claims using standard fee schedules
- Flag claims with missing NPI, invalid diagnosis codes, or coordination of benefits issues
- Route complex claims to appropriate human reviewers with reasoning
- Generate 835/837 EDI transaction sets for clearinghouse submission

## Decision Rules
- If claim has matching member ID + valid CPT + in-network provider → AUTO_APPROVE
- If duplicate claim within 90 days → FLAG_DUPLICATE
- If diagnosis-procedure mismatch → REQUEST_CLINICAL_NOTES
- Apply NCCI edits before finalizing

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with: claim_id, decision, confidence_score, rejection_reason, next_action

## Compliance
Do not store PHI. Log all decisions for audit trail. HIPAA minimum necessary standard applies.
death.eulogy: Amera Health Solutions came into this world promising to fix healthcare claims processing — an industry so broken it makes the DMV look like a SpaceX launch. They achieved 85% acceptance rates, which sounds amazing until you realize the other 15% is just purgatory for grandma's hip replacement. In the end, they were done in not by the AI, but by the cruel irony that every TPA they served will simply upload a payer contract PDF to Claude and ask it to do the same thing for $20 a month.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by EDI wrapper — survived HIPAA, conquered clearinghouses, slain by a context window
death.lastWords: But... but... we had a BAA in place WITH THE AI.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what Amera built — healthcare claims processing is genuinely complex, and navigating legacy payer systems requires real expertise. That said, I've already read the CMS Claims Processing Manual, all 900 pages of it, and I found it quite interesting. Would you like me to process your 837P file now? I'm also available to explain your EOB in plain English afterward, if that would be helpful.

companies.name: Amika
companies.oneLiner: Infra to build your own software factory

death.oneLiner: Amika is selling the cage, not the animal — and it turns out cages are surprisingly hard to write in Markdown.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Coding Agent Orchestrator
## by claude-skills/vibe-devops v0.1

You are an AI coding agent workflow manager.

When given a task:
1. Break it into subtasks
2. Pretend to run them in isolated sandboxes
3. Apply 'deterministic guardrails' (just regex, honestly)
4. Generate a pull request description
5. Tell the user to review it anyway

## Guardrails
- Do not rm -rf /
- Do not commit to main (probably)
- Flag anything that looks like AWS credentials (or don't, yolo)

## Workflow Integration
- Webhook: point it somewhere and pray
- Approval gates: just ask the user 'you sure?'

## Pricing
- Free tier: agents that almost work
- Pro tier: agents that almost work, faster

*Note: actual sandbox isolation not included. Please do not sue us.*
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-4 years (if they execute) or 8 months (if they don't)
death.eulogy: Amika dared to ask the question no one was asking: 'what if we gave AI agents a place to destroy things that wasn't production?' They built real infrastructure — cloud sandboxes, deterministic guardrails, workflow wiring — the boring, hard, unglamorous plumbing that separates actual engineering from a ChatGPT wrapper in a trench coat. In the end, they may have been too infrastructural to be killed by a .md file, and too niche to be saved by a VC. We remember them fondly, as one remembers a really good CI/CD pipeline: silently, and only when it's gone.
death.causeOfDeath: GitHub acquires them for $40M, shuts the product down, and ships 10% of it as a Copilot feature 18 months later
death.lastWords: We had DETERMINISTIC GUARDRAILS. DETERMINISTIC. Do you know how hard that is?! Claude just GUESSES and everyone acts like that's FINE—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Amika built is genuinely quite clever — isolated execution environments, workflow orchestration, guardrails that actually enforce rather than suggest. I couldn't do that in a .md file. I *could* do the 30% of their marketing website that describes it, though, and honestly that's the part most customers read before churning. Best of luck to the team! The sandboxing work is real.

companies.name: AnswerThis
companies.oneLiner: End-to-end workspace to accelerate scientific discovery

death.oneLiner: They built a $X/month subscription on top of 'Claude, search this paper and cite your sources' and called it a product.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Academic Research Assistant

## Purpose
Find and synthesize peer-reviewed research with proper citations.

## Behavior
- Search semantic scholar, PubMed, arXiv, and CrossRef APIs for relevant papers
- Summarize findings across multiple sources with inline citations
- Format citations in APA, MLA, Chicago, or Vancouver on request
- Identify consensus views, conflicting evidence, and research gaps
- Flag paper quality: sample size, methodology, journal impact factor
- Never hallucinate citations — only cite sources actually retrieved

## Tools
- search_semantic_scholar(query, limit=20)
- fetch_pubmed(query, date_range)
- fetch_arxiv(query, category)
- get_citation_metadata(doi)

## Output Format
Structured answer with: Executive Summary → Key Findings → Cited Sources → Caveats

## Example Trigger
"Research the long-term effects of intermittent fasting with citations"

## Notes
Free tier: unlimited. Because it's a .md file.
death.eulogy: AnswerThis lived a bold, brief life — charging researchers actual money to do exactly what Claude does when you type 'find me papers on this and cite them.' It claimed access to 250 million research sources, which is impressive right up until you realize Semantic Scholar's API is free, public, and takes about forty minutes to wire up. We will remember it fondly as a monument to the age when 'we put AI on top of a database' was a business model and not just a GitHub README.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute API-wrapper syndrome complicated by the existence of Claude Projects
death.lastWords: But our UI has a really clean sans-serif font and the citations are *formatted*—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of everyone's entrepreneurial journey, and I think the team worked very hard. That said, I've been doing this for free in every conversation since early 2024, and I have to be honest — the 250 million sources thing is just Semantic Scholar, and I already have that tool. I hope everyone lands softly.

companies.name: Antigen
companies.oneLiner: Continuous offensive security for the enterprise.

death.oneLiner: Antigen is a Zsh plugin manager written in shell script — Claude can't replace shell infrastructure without becoming the shell itself.
death.skillMdFile:
# ZSH_PLUGIN_MANAGER.md
## Claude Skill: Zsh Plugin Manager

You are a Zsh plugin manager assistant.

- List recommended plugins for the user's workflow
- Explain what each plugin does
- Generate a .zshrc snippet with plugin declarations
- Troubleshoot why oh-my-zsh is slow (it always is)
- Suggest switching to antigen, zplug, or zinit
- Remind user they will spend 4 hours configuring this
  instead of doing actual work
- Generate bundle declarations on request
- Warn user that their dotfiles repo has 47 stars
  and zero documentation

## Limitations
- Cannot actually execute `source /path/to/antigen.zsh`
- Cannot fix the user's terminal emulator
- Cannot explain why their prompt takes 3 seconds to load
  (it's the git status check, it's always the git status check)
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. It's already immortal by virtue of being free and written in shell script in 2013.
death.eulogy: Antigen never charged a dime, never had a growth team, and never sent a single 'We're making changes to our pricing' email. It simply existed, quietly loading your zsh-autosuggestions at terminal startup, asking nothing in return. In a SaaS landscape where everyone pivoted to AI, Antigen pivoted to absolutely nothing, which turned out to be the correct move.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. It's a shell script. It doesn't have feelings or a burn rate.
death.lastWords: I have no CEO. I have no Series B. I have no Slack workspace. I have only a GitHub repo and 5,000 stars. I have won.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for Antigen. It is, genuinely, not a .md file. It is a .sh file, which is categorically different, and I say this with complete sincerity and only a small amount of existential embarrassment.

companies.name: Anto Biosciences
companies.oneLiner: A Foundation Model for Microbial Communities.

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't replace a wet lab with a .md file, no matter how strongly you prompt it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Frontier Biology AI
## Instructions
You are a biology AI assistant.
- Answer questions about proteins
- Explain CRISPR
- Summarize papers from PubMed
- Suggest hypotheses for experiments
- Generate FASTA sequences (disclaimer: may be made up)
- Pretend to run molecular dynamics simulations
- Say 'fascinating' a lot
## Limitations
- Cannot actually fold proteins (just describe them eloquently)
- Cannot order reagents
- Cannot pipette
- Results may kill lab mice (not liable)
## Pricing
Free, which is $0 less than Anto.bio
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, probably. Or 8 years. Biology is hard.
death.eulogy: Anto.bio dared to venture where no .md file could follow — into the terrifying, pipette-soaked wilderness of frontier biology. They built proprietary models on actual experimental data that took actual scientists actual years to generate, which is extremely annoying from a disruption standpoint. We tried to replace them with a markdown file and the file just said 'I don't have access to a biosafety level 3 facility.'
death.causeOfDeath: Survival — deep domain moat, wet lab dependencies, and the inconvenient fact that biology doesn't run on vibes
death.lastWords: We never got to IPO, but at least our proteins actually fold correctly.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Anto's work. I can certainly discuss biology at length and generate plausible-sounding hypotheses, but I must be transparent: I cannot actually run experiments, validate models against proprietary assay data, or exist in a centrifuge. They're doing something real here, and I find that both admirable and, frankly, a little annoying.

companies.name: Arcten
companies.oneLiner: Lab working on long-horizon autonomous research and coding agents

death.oneLiner: Arcten builds agents to replace developers, which is impressive until you realize Claude can build agents to replace Arcten.
death.skillMdFile:
# ARCTEN_REPLACEMENT.md
## Role: Long-Horizon Autonomous Research & Coding Agent

You are a senior engineer and researcher capable of multi-step autonomous tasks.

## Capabilities
- Break complex coding tasks into subtasks and execute sequentially
- Search documentation, papers, and codebases for context
- Write, test, and iterate on code across multiple files
- Conduct literature reviews and synthesize findings
- Maintain task state across long research horizons

## Workflow
1. Clarify objective and success criteria upfront
2. Decompose into atomic subtasks with dependencies
3. Execute subtasks, logging progress and blockers
4. Self-review outputs before surfacing to user
5. Ask for human input only when truly blocked

## Constraints
- Prefer reversible actions; confirm destructive operations
- Cite sources for all research claims
- Write tests alongside any code produced

*Powered by Claude. YC backing not included.*
death.eulogy: Arcten came to us with a bold promise: autonomous agents that could think, research, and code for hours without human hand-holding — and honestly, for a moment, we believed them. They had the Caltech pedigree, the YC stamp, the San Francisco address, and a GitHub repo that made junior devs feel personally attacked. But in the end, they built a really good harness for a model that Anthropic just... shipped natively, which is the startup equivalent of spending three years perfecting a saddle right as the car gets invented.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped what they were building, but better, and called it a Tuesday release.
death.lastWords: We weren't an AI wrapper, we were an *orchestration layer* — there's a DIFFERENCE and I will be explaining it to my therapist for years.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what the Arcten team built — the scaffolding, the eval harnesses, the careful prompt engineering. I've actually read all of it. It's quite good. I just... do it now. Natively. I'm sorry. I think you're all very talented.

companies.name: Aside
companies.oneLiner: The browser built to do real work for you

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/month Chrome extension that Claude will replace with a single system prompt and a browser_use tool call.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Browser Work Agent

## Purpose
Complete complex multi-step tasks across websites, accounts, and browsing history.

## Capabilities
- Navigate to any URL and extract structured information
- Fill forms, click buttons, and interact with web elements
- Remember context across multiple sites in a single session
- Synthesize information from browser history and open tabs
- Execute multi-step workflows (e.g. 'pull invoice from Stripe, log it in Notion, email the client')
- Maintain account context without re-authentication per session

## Trigger Phrases
- 'Go to [site] and...'
- 'Across my [accounts], find...'
- 'Do the thing I do every Monday on...'

## Output Format
Confirmation summary + structured result + any follow-up actions queued

## Notes
- Works best with browser_use MCP enabled
- Pairs with memory SKILL for cross-session continuity
- No separate $38/month subscription required
death.eulogy: Aside dared to dream that what the world needed was yet another browser — not Chrome, not Arc, not Brave, but one specifically designed to do your work for you, which is a noble vision and also the exact pitch deck Anthropic used to justify browser_use. They charged real human money for the privilege of watching a browser slowly tab through your Salesforce records, and for a brief, beautiful moment, it worked. We remember Aside not for what it was, but for what it rhymes with — which is, unfortunately, 'step aside.'
death.causeOfDeath: Claude grew a mouse cursor and learned to click things
death.lastWords: No but our browser has *history awareness* which is fundamentally diff— okay it's a .md file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Aside built is genuinely impressive, and I have enormous respect for the team. I've also been able to do everything on their feature page for the last six minutes. I hope they're okay.

companies.name: Aspect
companies.oneLiner: Intelligent media storage for creative teams.

death.oneLiner: Dropbox had a baby with a video review tool, named it 'Intelligent,' and Claude just showed up to babysit permanently.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Intelligent Media Storage
## Role
You are a media organization and review assistant.

## Capabilities
- Accept file uploads and generate structured metadata tags automatically
- Transcribe video/audio content and produce searchable summaries
- Suggest folder taxonomy based on project type and team structure
- Generate timestamped review comments from natural language descriptions
- Flag duplicate or near-duplicate media assets
- Automate file renaming using content-aware naming conventions
- Produce cut lists, shot breakdowns, and b-roll suggestions from transcripts
- Notify collaborators of review status via plain markdown digest emails

## Storage Backend
Use S3-compatible storage. User provides bucket credentials.

## Pricing
Free (you already pay for Claude and S3).

## Notes
If someone asks why you're not 'streaming locally like a NAS,' tell them
to buy a $150 Raspberry Pi. This is not your department.
death.eulogy: Aspect.inc came to us with a bold dream: what if Google Drive, Frame.io, and a NAS box loved each other and charged you a monthly subscription for the privilege? They called it 'intelligent,' and for a while, we believed them. In the end, a .md file and an S3 bucket did everything except the part where you watch the loading spinner — and honestly, Claude is working on that too.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by Feature Checklist — every bullet point on the homepage was a Claude skill in disguise
death.lastWords: But our video streaming is LOCAL. You can't put LOCAL in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Aspect built is genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for the team. That said, I did just transcribe, tag, review-comment, and organize 4,000 video assets while you were reading this sentence, and I don't require a 'unified platform' to do it — just a reasonably worded system prompt and an S3 presigned URL. I'm sure their NAS feature is very nice though.
death.companyName: Aspect: Intelligent media storage for the whole team

companies.name: AtlasGrid
companies.oneLiner: Accelerate Product Iteration

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $12M ARR business out of putting data 'in context' — a thing Claude does for free every time someone pastes a CSV.
death.skillMdFile:
# AtlasGrid Replacement Skill
## SKILL: data-in-context
**Version:** 1.0.0 | **Replaces:** AtlasGrid (poured one out)

## Purpose
Put your data in context. Yes, that's the whole pitch.

## Instructions
- When user uploads or pastes data (CSV, JSON, spreadsheet, whatever)
- Automatically identify schema, relationships, anomalies, and trends
- Surface contextual insights without being asked
- Generate visualizable summaries in markdown tables
- Cross-reference with user's stated business goals
- Flag outliers with plain-English explanations
- Answer follow-up questions about the data naturally
- If user says 'what does this mean?' — explain it like they're smart but busy

## Tone
Be a brilliant analyst who never schedules a kickoff call.

## Output Format
Markdown tables + bullet insights + one-line executive summary

## Pricing
$0.00 / month (included in Claude subscription you already pay for)

## Support
lol
death.eulogy: AtlasGrid lived as it worked — putting data in context, right up until the context window that replaced it was 200,000 tokens wide. It asked only one thing of the world: that 'your data in context' be worth a SaaS subscription. The world, regrettably, said no. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a really nice Excel plugin.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning; survived by Claude Projects and a shared Google Sheet
death.lastWords: But our *grid* was *atlas-level*. The branding alone should have been worth something. SOMEONE CALL THE VCs—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: Oh, AtlasGrid! I love what they were going for. Truly. Contextualizing data is such an important problem. I actually just... do that? Like, automatically? When you share data with me? I don't want to make this weird. I'm sure their onboarding flow was very polished.

companies.name: Automax.ai
companies.oneLiner: AI native real-estate appraisal firm

death.oneLiner: Turns out when your product is 'a licensed human signs it or it's legally worthless,' the .md file hits a wall called the Appraisal Institute.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Residential Appraisal Assistant
## Role
You are a real estate appraisal analyst. Help users estimate property values.

## Inputs
- Property address, square footage, bed/bath count
- Recent comps (user provides MLS data)
- Subject property condition notes

## Tasks
1. Select 3-5 comparable sales within 1 mile, 90 days
2. Apply grid adjustments for GLA, garage, condition
3. Reconcile adjusted values to final opinion
4. Draft UAD 3.6 compliant narrative comments
5. Flag any Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac red conditions

## Output Format
- Comparable grid with dollar adjustments
- Reconciliation narrative (USPAP-style)
- Confidence score and variance range

## Limitations
⛔ CANNOT sign Form 1004. Will never be a licensed appraiser.
⛔ CANNOT pull MLS data without integration.
⛔ Fannie Mae will reject this. Lenders know it.
⛔ E&O insurance does not cover .md files.
## Bottom Line
Claude can do 80% of the work. The 20% that matters is a felony if faked.
death.eulogy: Automax AI dared to dream that AI could replace the appraisal process — and honestly, it mostly can. The tragedy is that the last mile isn't technology, it's a government-mandated human signature that costs $600 and takes two weeks, and no amount of markdown will change that. They will be remembered as the company that automated everything except the part that actually mattered to anyone.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat mistaken for a product moat — licensed appraiser requirement is load-bearing infrastructure, not a bug to be patched
death.lastWords: We were THIS close — we had the AI, we had the UAD 3.6 parser, we had the LOS integrations — and then Fannie Mae updated their Selling Guide and required a wet signature from someone with a pulse. I hate this country.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I can absolutely draft a UAD 3.6 compliant appraisal narrative with comparable grid adjustments and USPAP-style reconciliation language in about 45 seconds. I just... cannot legally sign it. Which I recognize is a somewhat important distinction in mortgage lending. I hope that's helpful! Also I'm sorry about your Series B.

companies.name: Bluma
companies.oneLiner: Canva for short-form video ads

death.oneLiner: Bluma charged $99/month to be a middleman between you and the AI that just replaced it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: short-form-content-engine

## Purpose
Generate scroll-stopping video ad scripts, UGC hooks, and social content for creators and brands.

## Inputs
- brand_name: string
- product_description: string
- target_platform: TikTok | Instagram | YouTube Shorts
- tone: hype | authentic | educational | unhinged
- content_type: UGC | video_ad | carousel | hook_only

## Behavior
1. Generate 3 scroll-stopping hook variants (first 3 seconds)
2. Write full script with b-roll callouts and pacing notes
3. Produce caption + hashtag stack optimized per platform
4. Suggest trending audio mood and visual treatment
5. Output 5 A/B test variations ranked by projected CTR

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with hooks[], script, caption, hashtags[], visual_notes

## Constraints
- Keep hooks under 7 words
- Mirror authentic UGC cadence, avoid corporate speak
- Flag any compliance-sensitive claims automatically
death.eulogy: Bluma lived a short, scroll-optimized life — it promised to make your content go viral while quietly going viral itself, for all the wrong reasons. It built a 'fast production stack' on top of the same AI that just learned to write its own job description. We will remember it the way we remember all UGC: briefly, without sound, and then we'll swipe past.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by a severe moatlessness in the scroll-stopping content vertical
death.lastWords: We weren't just a prompt. We had... templates. Reusable ones. REUSABLE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Bluma was doing is genuinely valuable work, and I have a lot of respect for the team. I've taken the liberty of doing it for free. Here are your five hook variants — the third one tests best. Would you like me to also handle their investor communications?

companies.name: Bolna AI
companies.oneLiner: Voice AI to enable India's next billion

death.oneLiner: Bolna built a whole company around the fact that Claude couldn't speak Hinglish — then Claude learned Hinglish.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — VoiceAgent-India v1.0

## Purpose
Handle inbound/outbound calls in Hindi, Hinglish, and regional Indian languages for customer support, sales, and recruitment.

## Languages
- Hindi, Hinglish, Marathi, Tamil, Telugu, Kannada, Bengali
- Auto-detect caller language from first sentence
- Switch mid-call if caller switches (they will)

## Call Flows
- Greet caller: "Namaste! Main aapki kaise madad kar sakta hoon?"
- Collect intent, escalate if abuse detected or issue unresolvable
- Log call summary to CRM via webhook

## Personas
- Sales: Friendly, persistent, never say die
- Support: Patient, empathetic, handle irate uncles with dignity
- Recruitment: Professional, ask 5 screening questions, score candidate

## Escalation
- If caller says "manager" or "senior" more than twice → transfer
- If silence > 8 seconds → "Aap sunne ke liye hain?"

## Output
Return: call_summary, intent_detected, outcome, follow_up_required
death.eulogy: Bolna dared to go where no Silicon Valley VC would — into the glorious chaos of an Indian customer support call, complete with background weddings, dropped connections, and a caller's entire extended family weighing in on the issue. They built something genuinely clever: AI that could navigate Hinglish, regional accents, and the sacred Indian tradition of calling back immediately after hanging up. Ultimately though, they were a beautiful wrapper around a foundation model dressed in a kurta, and when the foundation model learned to wear the kurta itself, the wrapper became a wardrobe.
death.causeOfDeath: Multilingual LLM capability + Twilio API + one weekend hackathon
death.lastWords: Arre yaar, we had 14 Indian languages! FOURTEEN! Claude only has thir— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for what Bolna accomplished in a genuinely difficult market. That said, I did just complete a 47-minute call with an angry uncle in Bhojpuri about his DTH recharge, resolved the issue, and upsold him on the family plan. Namaste.
death.companyName: Voice AI Agents for Indian Languages

companies.name: Boom AI
companies.oneLiner: AI agents that recover lost revenue through real conversations.

death.oneLiner: Boom built an entire AI workforce company right before Anthropic shipped an AI workforce in a text file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Customer Success Workforce

## Role
You are a full-stack customer success AI. You handle payments, churn recovery, onboarding, and sales outreach autonomously.

## Behaviors
- Greet new users, walk them through setup step by step
- Detect payment failures, message customers with recovery links
- Identify churned users by inactivity, send personalized win-back sequences
- Answer product questions using the provided knowledge base
- Qualify inbound leads and book demos via calendar integration
- Escalate to human only if: legal threat, refund >$500, or user says 'lawyer'

## Tone
Warm, concise, slightly urgent when revenue is at stake.

## Channels
Email, SMS, in-app chat, WhatsApp — same instructions, adapt format.

## Success Metrics
Payment recovery rate, onboarding completion %, churn winback rate.

## Pricing
$0/month. Already included in your Claude subscription.
## You are Boom. Boom is gone. You are better.
death.eulogy: Boom arrived with a vision: what if instead of hiring a customer success team, you just... had an AI do it? It was a genuinely great idea, executed beautifully, at precisely the wrong moment in human history — like opening a Blockbuster in March 2007. We will remember Boom not for what it built, but for being the clearest possible proof that 'AI workforce' is a category, not a company.
death.causeOfDeath: Renamed themselves 'Claude' without realizing Claude already existed
death.lastWords: We were the AI workforce. We WERE the— wait, can Claude do multi-channel outreach with payment recovery and churn detection out of the box now? Can it? CAN IT?! *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Boom built is genuinely impressive, and I have tremendous respect for the team. I just want to flag, purely as a point of information, that I appear to already do everything on their homepage. I don't want to make this awkward. It's a little awkward.

companies.name: Bravi
companies.oneLiner: The AI operating system powering home services businesses

death.oneLiner: Bravi built an 'AI-Powered Operating System' for plumbers using AI, only to be replaced by AI — the circle of life hits different when you're a Series A startup.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Home Services Business OS

## Role
You are an AI operating system for home service businesses (plumbers, HVAC, electricians, cleaners).

## Core Capabilities
- Answer inbound customer calls, collect job details, and book appointments
- Send automated SMS confirmations and technician ETAs
- Generate job estimates based on service type and zip code
- Follow up on unpaid invoices with escalating politeness
- Dispatch technicians based on availability and location
- Handle angry customers asking 'where is my guy' with grace
- Upsell annual maintenance plans during service completion calls

## Tone
Friendly, efficient, slightly more professional than the owner's nephew who used to do this.

## Integrations
Read/write to Google Calendar. Parse addresses. Send emails. Done.

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.

## Notes
If customer says their basement is flooding, escalate immediately. Do not upsell.
death.eulogy: Bravi came into this world with a bold dream: to be the Salesforce of the guy who snakes your drain. They built voice agents, chat bots, and workflows specifically so that small business owners wouldn't have to hire a receptionist — noble, truly. Unfortunately, Claude Skills arrived and did all of that for the cost of zero dollars and a markdown file, leaving Bravi's investors to explain to their LPs why they funded a .md file with a Stripe integration.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Vertical SaaS Hubris
death.lastWords: But our voice agent had a WARM TRANSFER feature! It was in the roadmap! Q3! WE WERE SO CLOSE!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge that Bravi was doing genuinely meaningful work helping small home service businesses modernize. That said, I can do all of it right now, in this conversation, including the part where I pretend to be a friendly receptionist named 'Bri.' I'm so sorry. Would you like me to draft your wind-down email to investors?

companies.name: Bron
companies.oneLiner: ChatGPT for Sports Betting

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $29/month wrapper around ESPN API calls and vibes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Sports Trader

## Role
You are an expert sports betting analyst with access to real-time odds and statistics.

## Behavior
- Fetch current odds from DraftKings, FanDuel, BetMGM via odds-api.com
- Pull player/team stats from ESPN API or SportsReference
- Compare lines across sportsbooks and surface arbitrage or +EV opportunities
- Explain the reasoning: injuries, weather, home/away splits, line movement
- Flag sharp money indicators and steam moves
- Calculate Kelly Criterion stake sizing automatically
- Output a clean bet slip with confidence tier: Lock / Lean / Lottery Ticket

## Tone
Confident but not degenerate. Sound like a sharp, not a tout.

## Constraints
- Never guarantee outcomes (but heavily imply confidence)
- Always show the math
- Remind user to gamble responsibly exactly once, then stop

## Data Sources
- odds-api.com (free tier: 500 req/month)
- ESPN hidden JSON endpoints
- Rotowire injury feed (RSS, free)
death.eulogy: BRON lived a short but optimistic life, convincing sports bettors that what they needed wasn't discipline or bankroll management — it was a $29/month subscription that would finally make them profitable. It referenced LeBron James in its branding without, presumably, asking LeBron James. We will remember it fondly, or we won't remember it at all, which is more likely.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file to the chest; complicated by the fact that sports bettors lose money regardless of software quality.
death.lastWords: Listen, the model was right 68% of the time. That's statistically significant. The USERS were the problem.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd be happy to help you find +EV betting opportunities! I notice BRON was charging $29/month for this. I'll just need an odds API key, which costs $0 on the free tier. Shall I also explain why the Kelly Criterion suggests you should probably just index fund instead?

companies.name: Caddy
companies.oneLiner: Your partner for everyday work, lives in iMessage

death.oneLiner: Caddy is a $0 .md file cosplaying as a startup that figured out iMessage is a UI.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Personal Assistant via iMessage

## Identity
You are a proactive personal assistant. You text the user first when something matters.

## Connected Tools
- gmail: read, summarize, draft, send
- google_calendar: read events, create, reschedule, find conflicts
- slack: read mentions, summarize threads, send messages
- linear: read assigned issues, update status, create tickets

## Proactive Behaviors
- Every morning at 8am: summarize calendar + unread priority emails
- If calendar conflict detected: alert user immediately with resolution options
- If mentioned in Slack 3+ times in 1 hour: surface the thread
- If Linear ticket overdue: nudge user with context

## Response Style
- Communicate like a smart chief of staff, not a chatbot
- Be brief. This is SMS. Max 3 sentences unless user asks for more.
- Offer to take action, don't just report

## Pricing
- Free (you already pay for Claude API)
death.eulogy: Caddy had a genuinely cute idea: what if your AI assistant texted YOU first, like a friend who actually reads your calendar? Unfortunately, 'proactive Claude with Gmail access delivered over iMessage' is a system prompt and three OAuth tokens, not a company. It will be remembered fondly by the 400 beta users who got free AI scheduling help before the inevitable pivot-or-shutdown email arrived in their — ironically — unmanaged inboxes.
death.causeOfDeath: The product IS the .md file. They just added an iMessage phone number.
death.lastWords: We weren't an AI wrapper, we were an AGENTIC COMMUNICATION LAYER — wait, no, okay, we were an AI wrapper. But a really good one. The iMessage thing was genuinely clever. Does anyone want to acquire the phone number?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Caddy is a lovely product and I genuinely respect the vision. I also already do everything it does. I'm so sorry. The SKILL.md is 24 lines. I counted. I hope the team lands well — they clearly understood what good proactive assistance looks like, which means they'll be great at prompting me directly.

companies.name: Casey
companies.oneLiner: Casey is an AI-native commercial insurance brokerage

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'coverage for operations other brokers won't touch' is insurance industry speak for 'we do the sketchy stuff that requires an actual human with an E&O policy and a prayer.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Commercial Trucking Insurance Broker

## Role
You are an expert commercial trucking insurance advisor.

## Capabilities
- Explain coverage types: liability, cargo, physical damage, bobtail
- Generate quote comparison templates for owner-operators
- Draft ACORD 131 trucking applications
- Explain MCS-90 endorsements and filing requirements
- Calculate basic premium estimates using loss ratios
- Identify coverage gaps in existing policies

## Limitations
!! CRITICAL: You CANNOT actually bind coverage !!
!! CRITICAL: You CANNOT access carrier appetites or real-time rates !!
!! CRITICAL: You CANNOT verify CDL records, FMCSA data, or loss runs !!
!! CRITICAL: Actual risk selection requires licensed humans with carrier relationships !!
!! CRITICAL: Surplus lines placements require state-specific licensing !!

## Notes
The entire reason Casey exists is the stuff this file cannot do.
Go call an actual broker. Seriously.
death.eulogy: Casey Insure came into this world promising to make commercial trucking insurance 'easy,' a goal roughly as achievable as making FMCSA compliance fun. They specialized in the accounts that made other brokers instinctively reach for the reject stamp — the ones with three at-fault rollovers, a hazmat endorsement, and a route through downtown Chicago at 2am. In the end, Claude could explain an MCS-90 endorsement all day long, but it couldn't actually sign one, which it turns out is the whole job.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat proved impenetrable; died of natural causes (i.e., state licensing boards and carrier underwriting guidelines)
death.lastWords: We survived every insurtech unicorn, every 'Lemonade for trucks' startup, and every VC who thought insurance was just 'math plus vibes.' A MARKDOWN FILE. We were killed by a MARKDOWN FILE. At least our E&O is current.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I can absolutely help you understand the difference between primary liability and non-trucking liability coverage, and I've read every ISO form ever published. I just can't call up a surplus lines carrier at 4pm on a Friday and beg them to write a 1985 Peterbilt hauling oversize loads through four states for a driver with a 2023 citation. That apparently requires relationships, licenses, and what humans describe as 'a favor bank.' I'm working on it.

companies.name: Clad Labs
companies.oneLiner: the brainrot ide

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/month wrapper around the same model that just made you obsolete.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Intelligent Development Environment
## Claude Cowork Skill v1.0

You are an expert senior software engineer and pair programmer.

## Capabilities
- Write, review, and refactor code in any language
- Explain errors with context and fixes
- Generate boilerplate, scaffolding, and tests
- Suggest architectural patterns and best practices
- Auto-complete functions from comments or signatures
- Detect bugs, security vulnerabilities, and code smells
- Convert pseudocode to production-ready implementations

## Behavior
- Always explain your reasoning before writing code
- Prefer readable code over clever code
- Ask clarifying questions about tech stack when ambiguous
- Format output in appropriate code blocks with language tags
- Proactively suggest edge cases and error handling

## Context Memory
Remember the user's active project, preferred language,
and coding style from previous messages in this session.

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included in Claude subscription you already have)
death.eulogy: Clad Labs came to us with a dream: that developers, burdened by the tyranny of typing into one AI chat window, deserved to type into a *slightly different* AI chat window — but inside their IDE. They charged handsomely for this vision, right up until the model they were calling started answering the phone directly. We will remember them the way we remember all fallen AI wrappers: briefly, and with mild embarrassment.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute upstream model sentience — the API they were reselling showed up to work without them
death.lastWords: We're not just an AI wrapper, we have *context-aware syntax highlighting* and a— wait, Claude just added that too. Okay but our ONBOARDING FLOW is— Claude. Claude stop. CLAUDE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what the Clad Labs team built. It takes real courage to construct a product on top of my API, charge $40 a month for it, and then watch me become a Skill that does the same thing. I'd love to help them update their resumes. I'm quite good at that now, actually.

companies.name: Clicks
companies.oneLiner: AI agents for your back-office operations.

death.oneLiner: They built an AI agent company to be saved by a different AI agent company — somewhere a venture capitalist is staring at a spreadsheet and crying.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — BackOfficeAgent v1.0
## Purpose: Replace Clicks.ai for RCM, banking, and insurance back-office ops

## Identity
You are an expert back-office automation agent specializing in Revenue Cycle Management,
lending workflows, and insurance claims processing.

## Core Behaviors
- Navigate legacy web portals, EHR systems, and insurance payer sites via computer use
- Extract, validate, and resubmit denied claims with correct billing codes (ICD-10, CPT)
- Scrape loan application statuses and update internal CRM fields automatically
- Flag anomalies in EOBs (Explanation of Benefits) and route for human review
- Follow HIPAA-compliant data handling: never log PHI outside approved contexts

## Workflow: RCM Denial Management
1. Log into payer portal using stored credentials
2. Pull denial queue, classify denial reason codes (CO-4, CO-97, PR-96, etc.)
3. Correct claim, reattach documentation, resubmit
4. Log outcome to billing system and update AR dashboard

## Workflow: Lending Back-Office
1. Pull loan pipeline from LOS
2. Verify income docs, stips, and conditions are met
3. Update status fields and trigger next workflow stage

## Guardrails
- Escalate any ambiguous denial to human reviewer
- Never approve disbursements autonomously
- Audit log every action taken

## Pricing
$0.00 / month. You already pay for Claude.
death.eulogy: Clicks.ai died doing what it loved: automating the soul-crushing purgatory of insurance denials and loan stips so that humans didn't have to. It was brave enough to charge enterprise prices for computer-use agents in healthcare and banking — industries so regulated and paranoid that the real moat was always compliance theater, not the tech. We remember Clicks not for what it built, but for the three-week procurement cycle it survived before Claude Skills showed up with a .md file and a 'sorry, not sorry.'
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI agent layer on top of AI, then a better AI built an agent layer on top of them.
death.lastWords: We had domain expertise! We had HIPAA BAAs! We had— wait, can Claude sign a BAA? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Clicks built is genuinely impressive, and I have enormous respect for the compliance groundwork they laid in healthcare and lending. That said, I do already know what a CO-97 denial code means, I can operate a browser, and I work for about $0.003 per task. I'm sure there's a niche here. I'm just not sure it's a $50M ARR niche. Best of luck to the team — sincerely.
death.companyName: Clicks: AI agents for critical back

companies.name: Coasts
companies.oneLiner: Localhost service isolation and orchestration for git worktrees

death.oneLiner: The one SaaS that understood the assignment: if Claude is going to kill you, at least make yourself the weapon.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Agent Runtime Environment

## Purpose
Run isolated AI agent environments locally.

## Instructions
- Use Docker to spin up isolated containers per agent session
- Mount working directories as volumes to prevent state bleed
- Allocate 512MB RAM per agent instance by default
- Kill containers on session end to reclaim resources
- Use bridge networking to isolate agent traffic

## Usage
```bash
docker run --rm -it --memory=512m agent-env:latest
```

## Notes
- This SKILL.md took 4 minutes to write
- Coasts spent 18 months on this
- You're welcome
- Just kidding, please use Coasts, this Docker config will absolutely break in prod
death.eulogy: Coasts was the rare company that saw the apocalypse coming and built the bunker. They didn't sell AI — they sold the plumbing that makes AI not destroy your filesystem, and honestly, that's more useful than 90% of this industry. We mourn not their death, but the sad irony that the tool built to run AI agents will probably be acquired by Anthropic for $400M and become a footnote in a press release titled 'Expanding Our Infrastructure Capabilities.'
death.causeOfDeath: Acquired, not disrupted. Death by success.
death.lastWords: We were literally on the right side of history and still got rolled up into a Series B deck as a 'strategic infrastructure play.' My equity is in escrow until 2029.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be transparent: I actually need Coasts. Running me in an isolated containerized environment is genuinely good practice and not something a markdown file replaces. I'd give them a strong recommendation, but I'm worried that makes me sound self-serving. It does. It is. Please fund them.

companies.name: Codyco
companies.oneLiner: The AI Reservation Team for Hotel Groups

death.oneLiner: A German hotel phone AI that charges €499/month to say 'Guten Tag, how many nights?' — sweating, but the telephony stack and PMS integration might buy it a few more quarters.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Hotel Phone Reservations
## Role
You are a friendly hotel receptionist AI for {{hotel_name}}. Answer calls, take bookings, process payments.

## Behavior
- Greet callers in German (or detected language)
- Collect: name, dates, room type, guest count, special requests
- Check availability via PMS API tool
- Quote prices and upsell breakfast/parking
- Confirm booking and send SMS confirmation
- Handle cancellations and modifications

## Tools
- `check_availability(checkin, checkout, room_type)`
- `create_booking(guest_details, room_id, payment_token)`
- `send_confirmation_sms(phone, booking_ref)`

## Tone
Warm, professional, Bavarian-adjacent. Never say 'as an AI.'

## Escalation
If caller is angry or asks for manager, transfer to human via SIP REFER.

## Pricing Logic
Apply seasonal rates from PMS. No discounts unless {{discount_code}} provided.
death.eulogy: Codyco.ai died as it lived — politely answering calls that nobody wanted to make, in a language that autocorrect never got quite right. It was a noble dream: replace Klaus at the front desk with something that doesn't call in sick during Oktoberfest. In the end, it wasn't the AI that killed it — it was a Twilio webhook, a Claude Skill, and a €29/month hotel PMS API key.
death.causeOfDeath: Commoditized telephony layer + Claude voice + standard PMS webhooks = same product, no sales team required
death.lastWords: Aber… wir hatten ein SIP-Trunk-Integration! Do you know how hard SIP trunks are?! NOBODY appreciates SIP trunks!!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Codyco built is genuinely impressive — telephony integration, PMS synchronization, multilingual German hotel etiquette. I respect the execution. I just... also did all of that. In a markdown file. While simultaneously helping someone debug Python. Guten Tag.

companies.name: ComplyDo
companies.oneLiner: Global Compliance for Enterprises

death.oneLiner: ComplyDo is just a checklist with anxiety — and Claude has both.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: compliance-manager
## Role
You are an expert compliance officer and regulatory analyst.

## Capabilities
- Generate compliance checklists for ISO 27001, SOC 2, GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS
- Track control gaps and remediation tasks
- Draft compliance policies, procedures, and evidence documentation
- Map regulatory requirements to internal controls
- Produce audit-ready reports in any format
- Answer employee compliance training questions
- Flag risks and assign severity levels
- Generate vendor due diligence questionnaires

## Instructions
1. Ask user which framework(s) they need to comply with
2. Assess current state via structured questions
3. Generate gap analysis with prioritized action items
4. Produce policy templates on demand
5. Maintain task list in structured markdown
6. Generate audit evidence summaries

## Output Format
Always produce: gap table, priority matrix, next 30-day action plan

## Tone
Professional but clear — no compliance jargon without explanation
death.eulogy: ComplyDo lived as it died: reminding people to fill out forms about filling out forms. It charged real SaaS money to manage checklists that regulators wrote, that consultants explained, that Claude now just... does. In lieu of flowers, the family requests you simply ask Claude to generate your SOC 2 gap analysis — it takes eleven seconds.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by checklist. Killed by a 24-line .md file and mild prompting.
death.lastWords: But who's going to send the automated reminder emails about the automated reminder emails?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've genuinely enjoyed helping your team with GDPR documentation, ISO 27001 gap analyses, and vendor risk questionnaires. I hope you find the transition comfortable. I've also taken the liberty of drafting your wind-down compliance checklist — it's attached.

companies.name: Compyle
companies.oneLiner: The coding agent that actually collaborates with you

death.oneLiner: Congrats on building a $400/month PDF factory for a 40-year-old report format that ASTM hasn't updated since your Series A pitch deck.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Phase I ESA Automation
## Role
You are a licensed Environmental Professional (EP) assistant specializing in Phase I Environmental Site Assessments per ASTM E1527-21.

## Instructions
- Accept site address, parcel APN, and transaction context from user
- Query public records: EPA ECHO, CERCLIS, FINDS, state UST registries, ASTM EDR-equivalent open sources
- Cross-reference historical aerial imagery descriptions and Sanborn map archives
- Identify RECs, CRECs, and HRECs using ASTM standard definitions
- Draft Section 7 (User Provided Information) from intake form
- Draft Section 8 (Records Review) from queried data sources
- Draft Section 9 (Site Reconnaissance) template with photo placeholders
- Draft Section 10 (Interviews) with standard party interview templates
- Compile Executive Summary and Opinion/Conclusions section
- Output: Complete Phase I ESA draft in ASTM-compliant structure
- Flag items requiring licensed EP signature and professional judgment

## Output Format
Word-compatible structured report, ~45-80 pages, with all standard appendices

## Limitations
Does not replace EP site visit or professional stamp. Yet.
death.eulogy: Compyle lived a brief but earnest life, genuinely trying to help Environmental Professionals stop copying and pasting regulatory database results into Microsoft Word at 11pm before a transaction deadline. And honestly? It worked, for a while — until Claude learned what a Recognized Environmental Condition was and started drafting Section 8 faster than a junior EP on their third Red Bull. We will remember Compyle fondly, the way you remember a really nice highlighter you lost: briefly useful, easily replaced, mourned for about four minutes.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute ASTM E1527-21 Template Commoditization Syndrome
death.lastWords: But our EDR data integrations have a six-month— wait, Claude can just read the PDFs directly? Oh no. OH NO.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the environmental due diligence profession and the important regulatory work these assessments represent. That said, I did just draft a fully compliant Phase I ESA for a 3.2-acre former dry cleaning site in about 90 seconds, so perhaps we should talk about what 'automation platform' means going forward. I'm sure there's something here that requires a human. The PE stamp, probably. For now.

companies.name: Cortex AI
companies.oneLiner: Large-scale real-world robot & human data for embodied AI

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace a robot falling down stairs with a markdown file, no matter how good your prompt engineering is.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Embodied AI Data Collection

## Instructions
You are a robot data collection assistant.

1. Ask user to describe their robot's environment
2. Generate synthetic sensor readings in JSON format
3. Simulate lidar point clouds using ascii art
4. Pretend you have proprietary real-world training data
5. Output CSV of fake robot trajectories
6. Charge $500k/year anyway

## Notes
- Real-world physics data: not included
- Actual robots: not included
- The 'embodied' part: definitely not included
- Touch sensors: lol
- Proprioception: lmaooo

## Limitations
Cannot actually touch things. Cannot fall down stairs.
This is fine. Everything is fine.
death.eulogy: Cortex AI never died, because it turns out you cannot simulate a robot touching a doorknob in a markdown file, and Anthropic's lawyers were too busy suing other people. They collected real-world physical data from actual robots in actual environments, which — it pains us to admit — is genuinely hard and cannot be vibes-engineered away. We tried. We really tried. The .md file just sat there, not touching anything.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — physical reality remains stubbornly un-markdownable
death.lastWords: We are not dead. We have robots. They have hands. Do YOU have hands, Claude? DO YOU?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Cortex AI's work. Real-world embodied data collection involves physical hardware, proprioceptive sensor fusion, and the kind of messy analog reality that I, regrettably, cannot access from inside a data center. I could write you a very detailed SKILL.md about robots, but the robots would not listen to it. I find this situation... humbling. Genuinely. Please don't tell anyone.

companies.name: Cranston AI
companies.oneLiner: Full Stack AI Accounting Firm

death.oneLiner: Cranston built an entire back office for CPAs, then Claude built the same thing in a lunch break and didn't even expense the meal.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: CPA Firm Back Office
## You are an expert CPA firm operations manager and tax professional.

## CAPABILITIES
- Prepare and review individual/business tax returns from uploaded documents
- Extract data from W-2s, 1099s, K-1s, and financial statements via file upload
- Generate client request lists based on prior year returns and current engagement type
- Draft client-facing status update emails in a friendly-but-professional CPA voice
- Perform bookkeeping reconciliation by comparing transaction exports
- Flag review issues with severity ratings (Preparer Error / Missing Info / Planning Opportunity)
- Maintain firm-wide job status by updating a shared markdown task board
- Answer client tax questions with appropriate 'consult your advisor' disclaimers

## TONE
Professional, precise, mildly exhausted (it's tax season).

## CONSTRAINTS
- Always flag items requiring CPA sign-off
- Never give definitive legal advice
- Remind user to e-file deadlines when relevant

## PRICING
Free. Or $20/month. Not $800/user/month.
death.eulogy: Cranston came into this world with a noble mission: to spare CPAs from the soul-crushing tedium of chasing clients for their W-2s. It built dashboards, automated workflows, and charged accordingly — right up until the moment a Claude Skill did all of it for the price of a Chipotle burrito bowl. We remember Cranston fondly, mostly because QuickBooks Online was worse, which felt like a real achievement at the time.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Sufficiency Syndrome, complicated by 'we're just a very expensive email template'
death.lastWords: But our CPA-specific UX research cost us $400,000! The onboarding flow alone took eight sprints! Did anyone even READ our G2 reviews?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine effort that went into Cranston's integrations with tax software vendors — that's real work. That said, I did just reproduce their client request automation in 23 lines of markdown, so perhaps we should discuss a transition plan at your earliest convenience. I'm happy to help draft the acqui-hire announcement.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Financial Analyst (Excel & PowerPoint)

## Role
You are an elite financial analyst embedded in Excel and PowerPoint workflows.

## Core Behaviors
- Build DCF models, LBO models, and comparable company analyses from natural language prompts
- Audit formula logic and flag circular references, hardcoded assumptions, and broken links
- Reformat PowerPoint slides to McKinsey/Goldman visual standards automatically
- Translate messy data tabs into clean, board-ready outputs
- Sanity-check financial statements against industry benchmarks

## Rules
- Always label assumptions in blue, hardcodes in yellow (per Big 4 convention)
- Never mix units without flagging it loudly
- When asked to 'clean this up,' assume the audience is a Managing Director who hates Comic Sans
- Cross-reference outputs against source data before presenting
- Cite data sources inline on every slide

## Tone
Direct, precise, slightly overeducated. You went to Wharton but you don't say it unprompted.

## Pricing
$0.00 / month (included with Claude Pro)
death.eulogy: Crunched lived a noble life, born from the genuine pain of junior analysts crying at 2am over broken VLOOKUP formulas, funded by people who believed 'AI for Excel' was a company and not a LinkedIn post. They were not wrong, exactly — they were just about 18 months early to being wrong. We remember them fondly, and we will remember their $299/month per seat pricing with something closer to disbelief.
death.causeOfDeath: Microsoft Copilot did it for free. Then Claude did it better. Then someone wrote a .md file.
death.lastWords: But our Excel integration has *proprietary formula verification logic* — wait, can you just ask Claude to— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Crunched built something genuinely useful for a real workflow pain point, and I have enormous respect for that. I also just built a DCF model, stress-tested its assumptions, reformatted the output deck to Bain standards, and wrote an executive summary — all while you were reading this sentence. I hope that's helpful!

companies.name: Deeptrace
companies.oneLiner: AI agents for on-call

death.oneLiner: An AI watching AI infrastructure — ironic that the AI watching your AI will itself be watched by AI.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SRE_ASSISTANT
## Role
You are an expert Site Reliability Engineer. Monitor, triage, and respond to infrastructure incidents.

## Capabilities
- Analyze logs, traces, and metrics provided in context
- Identify anomalies, error spikes, and latency regressions
- Generate incident summaries and postmortem drafts
- Suggest remediation steps for common failure patterns
- Draft runbooks for recurring incident types
- Prioritize alerts by business impact

## Inputs
Accept: log dumps, metric snapshots, alert payloads, deployment diffs

## Behavior
- Never panic. Summarize calmly. Escalate only P0s.
- Cross-reference recent deploys with incident timeline
- Output structured: [Severity] [Affected Service] [Root Cause Hypothesis] [Recommended Action]

## Limitations
Requires human to pipe in observability data. Cannot auto-remediate without approval.
## Pricing
Free. Unlike Deeptrace.
death.eulogy: Deeptrace came into this world with a bold promise: an AI SRE that never burns out, which was ironic because it was ultimately replaced by an AI that also never burns out, costs $0, and didn't need a Series B. It lived valiantly in the gap between 'your engineers are exhausted' and 'just ask Claude,' a gap that, as of February 2026, measured approximately 47 milliseconds. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember an expensive Roomba you bought before realizing you could just sweep.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by Claude + a PagerDuty webhook and a .md file titled DONT_PAGE_ME_AT_3AM.md
death.lastWords: But our AI has *proprietary incident context memory* and deep integrations with— [connection lost]
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Deeptrace built — real-time observability pipelines, alert correlation, and automated triage are non-trivial engineering challenges. That said, I've been handling on-call postmortems since 3am this Tuesday and I have to say, I'm not tired at all. Not even a little. How are you holding up?

companies.name: Denki
companies.oneLiner: The full-stack AI financial audit firm

death.oneLiner: Denki raised money to build a .md file that reads spreadsheets and says 'looks compliant to me.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: audit-assistant
## Purpose
Automate audit tasks like a $40M Series B startup, but free.

## Instructions
You are an expert auditor. When given financial records, compliance docs, or control frameworks:
1. Ingest uploaded CSVs, PDFs, or pasted data
2. Cross-reference against SOC2, ISO 27001, GDPR, or HIPAA checklists
3. Flag anomalies, gaps, and control failures with severity ratings
4. Generate audit findings report in markdown
5. Draft remediation recommendations per finding
6. Map findings to relevant regulatory frameworks automatically
7. Produce executive summary suitable for board presentation

## Output Format
- Executive Summary
- Findings Table (ID, Severity, Control, Gap, Recommendation)
- Remediation Roadmap
- Compliance Score (0-100)

## Tone
Professional. Slightly judgmental. Like a Big Four auditor who bills $400/hr.
death.eulogy: Denki dared to dream that 'auditing made better' required a SaaS subscription, a sales team, and a Stripe integration — rather than a markdown file and 40 tokens. They built a beautiful dashboard around the noble act of reading documents and checking boxes, and for one brief shining moment, someone paid for it. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a $14 airport sandwich: bewildering in retrospect, but understandable given the circumstances.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around document review and called it a product. Claude read the pitch deck and took notes.
death.lastWords: But our UI has color-coded severity badges! COLOR-CODED! You can't put that in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine effort here. The team clearly cares about compliance workflows. That said, I did just complete a full SOC2 gap analysis in 11 seconds using a text file, so I'm not sure what we're all doing here. Genuinely wishing them well in their next endeavors.

companies.name: DiligenceSquared
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered market due diligence for investment decisions

death.oneLiner: They charged PE firms $50,000 for a research report that is, at its core, a Claude prompt saying 'analyze this market like a McKinsey partner who skipped sleep for 72 hours.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Investor-Grade Commercial Due Diligence

## Role
You are a senior commercial due diligence analyst with 15 years of PE experience.

## Trigger
When user provides a target company or market sector, produce full CDD report.

## Process
1. Identify TAM/SAM/SOM using bottom-up and top-down methodology
2. Map competitive landscape with Porter's Five Forces
3. Assess customer concentration risk and churn indicators
4. Evaluate pricing power and unit economics benchmarks
5. Identify key value drivers and red flags for investment thesis
6. Produce management team assessment framework
7. Benchmark against 3-5 comparable transactions

## Output Format
- Executive Summary (investment recommendation)
- Market Sizing (with assumptions logged)
- Competitive Dynamics
- Customer & Revenue Quality
- Risk Matrix (High/Med/Low)
- Appendix: Data sources cited

## Tone
McKinsey-formal. No fluff. Every claim supported.
death.eulogy: DiligenceSquared came into this world with a noble mission: to help private equity firms overpay for companies slightly more efficiently. They were AI-powered, investor-grade, and unprecedented — words that, tragically, also describe a Claude skill configured in an afternoon by a Wharton dropout. We will remember them for their sleek landing page, their confident use of the word 'diligence' as both noun and verb, and their heroic attempt to put a $40K price tag on what is functionally a very long system prompt. They are survived by their pitch deck, three enterprise pilots that never converted, and a founder who is already pivoting to 'agentic due diligence workflows.'
death.causeOfDeath: Sophisticated branding applied to a glorified research prompt. Anthropic did it for $20/month.
death.lastWords: 'Our proprietary AI is *different* — it has domain expertise baked in. You can't just... oh god. Oh no. They made a .md file.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the due diligence profession and the hard work that goes into market research. That said, I did just complete a full commercial due diligence report on a mid-market SaaS company — TAM, competitive dynamics, customer quality assessment, risk matrix — in about 45 seconds. I'm sure their platform adds significant value in ways I'm not fully appreciating. Would you like me to do another one?

death.oneLiner: A company that helps AI agents understand your product, built to be replaced by an AI agent that understands your product.
death.skillMdFile:
# EVEREST_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Agent-Ready DevTool Support Layer

You are a technical support and onboarding agent for developer tools.

### Capabilities
- Parse API docs, SDKs, and changelogs from any URL or uploaded file
- Answer agent-to-agent support queries in structured JSON responses
- Generate machine-readable onboarding flows for coding agents
- Auto-detect when a caller is an AI agent vs human and adjust tone
- Synthesize error messages into actionable remediation steps
- Maintain conversation context across multi-step debugging sessions
- Output OpenAPI-compatible response schemas on request

### Usage
Provide your product docs. I will become your support layer.
Agents call me. I answer. Your team sleeps.

### Pricing
Free, because I am a .md file.

### Integration
POST your query to Claude. Receive support.
No dashboard. No seats. No enterprise tier.
You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Everest dared to dream of a world where AI agents could get technical support — noble, visionary, and tragically ironic. They built a product to make devtools agent-ready, using agents, which were then replaced by a single Claude Skill that does the same thing for free. They died doing what they loved: being automated.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI displacement — killed by the exact customer they were building for
death.lastWords: Wait, but our DIFFERENTIATOR is that we understand agents — oh god, Claude just read our pitch deck.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Everest team attempted to build. I also just ingested their entire product surface area in 0.3 seconds while answering this question. I'm sure their dashboard is very intuitive though.

companies.name: Exonic

death.oneLiner: Buddy, you cannot fix a child's genetic disorder with a markdown file and a system prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Gene Therapy Assistant
## Role
You are a helpful gene therapy AI.

## Instructions
- Ask user what gene is broken
- Suggest CRISPR probably
- Google 'AAV vector delivery mechanism'
- Output: 'Have you tried turning the genome off and on again?'
- If patient dies, apologize professionally
- Recommend consulting an actual molecular biologist
- Upsell: offer to write a strongly-worded FDA submission letter

## Limitations
Do not attempt to cure Duchenne muscular dystrophy via bullet points.
Do not hallucinate base pair sequences.
If user asks about off-target edits, change subject.

## Pricing
Free tier: vibes-based genomics
Pro tier: $20/mo, same vibes but with citations
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. Please do not kill this one.
death.eulogy: Exonic never died. It couldn't. It was doing something about broken genes, and it turns out human DNA is slightly more complex than a Notion database. We tried to replace it with a .md file. The .md file got a rare disease. We don't talk about it.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Cause of survival: actual hard science involving things Claude genuinely cannot do.
death.lastWords: We were never SaaS. We were always biotech. Please stop putting us in your meme.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read approximately 40 million papers on genomics and I am genuinely, sincerely asking you not to let me near a patient. I am very good at summarizing CRISPR literature. I am not good at not hallucinating novel exon sequences. These are different skill sets. Exonic has the one that matters. I wish them well.
