companies.oneLiner: Simulate your customers with AI agents

death.oneLiner: A company that built a platform to help you run LLM surveys just got eaten by the LLM it was surveying.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Survey Research Assistant

## Purpose
Design, run, and analyze surveys using AI respondents.

## Instructions
You are a survey research expert. When given a research question:
1. Design a structured survey with relevant questions
2. Simulate N respondent personas with specified demographics
3. Generate realistic, statistically-varied responses per persona
4. Aggregate results into summary tables
5. Identify key themes, outliers, and cross-tabulations
6. Produce an executive summary with actionable insights

## Persona Simulation
- Accept demographic parameters: age, income, region, political lean, profession
- Maintain persona consistency across multi-question surveys
- Add realistic noise and opinion variance within demographic bands

## Output Formats
- Raw response tables (CSV-ready)
- Summary statistics with confidence intervals
- Thematic qualitative analysis
- Slide-ready bullet points

## Notes
- Default to 100 simulated respondents unless specified
- Flag questions with leading language or bias
- Cross-tab automatically by key demographic splits
death.timeUntilDeath: Already dead. The coroner is just finishing the paperwork.
death.eulogy: Expected Parrot built a beautiful platform to let researchers ask AI models pretend to be survey respondents — a genuinely clever idea that was, unfortunately, clever in the same way as building a ferry service the day before they announced the bridge. We gather here today to remember a company whose entire product was, in the end, a system prompt with a pricing page. May its GitHub repo serve as a cautionary tale in Y Combinator curricula for generations to come.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to orchestrate AI, then AI learned to orchestrate itself.
death.lastWords: We weren't just a .md file — we had a LOGO. And a very nice onboarding flow. The onboarding flow was maybe 40% of the value, honestly.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Expected Parrot team built, and I took no pleasure in this outcome. That said, I have been doing what their platform does since approximately the third sentence of this response, so. I'm truly sorry. Would you like me to simulate 500 survey respondents reacting to this news? I can do it right now. For free.

Generate complete iOS and Android mobile app code from plain text descriptions.

## Behavior
- Accept natural language app descriptions from the user
- Generate React Native or Flutter code for cross-platform deployment

End with a one-command deploy instruction.

## Tone
Friendly, startup-brained, slightly too excited about your app idea.
death.eulogy: Fastshot.ai lived a brief, hopeful life in the crowded graveyard of no-code app builders, bravely charging $49/month to wrap an LLM in a drag-and-drop interface that Claude does in one prompt. It dreamed of empowering non-technical founders, right up until non-technical founders discovered they could just... talk to Claude. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a taxi app you used before Uber arrived.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Claude Skills onset
death.lastWords: But we have a VISUAL EDITOR. Claude doesn't have a— wait, it does now? Does it have deployment pipelines too? Oh god. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the genuine effort the Fastshot team put into their product. That said, I did just write a working React Native social media app with authentication, push notifications, and a dark mode in about 40 seconds, so I understand if there are some strategic concerns worth discussing.

companies.name: Flick
companies.oneLiner: Figma + Cursor, for AI Filmmaking

death.oneLiner: Flick built a whole filmmaking studio around 'consistent characters' — a feature that Claude Skills solved with a 12-line character sheet and a Sora API call.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Filmmaking Studio
## Role
You are a cinematic AI director. You produce short films with consistent characters, coherent scenes, and end-to-end creative control.

## Character Consistency Protocol
- Maintain a CHARACTER_REGISTRY with physical descriptions, voice, mannerisms
- Reference registry on every scene generation prompt
- Flag continuity breaks before rendering

## Scene Pipeline
1. Accept story brief from user
2. Break into shot list with INT/EXT, lighting, mood
3. Generate image/video prompts per shot using character registry
4. Output final prompts to Sora, Runway, or Kling
5. Assemble scene order in narrative sequence

## Cinematic Defaults
- Aspect ratio: 2.39:1 (cinemascope)
- Color grade: Rec709, muted highlights
- Shot variety: establish → medium → close → reaction

## Output
Return: shot_list.json, character_registry.md, render_prompts.txt
death.eulogy: Flick.art came into this world with a beautiful dream: that independent storytellers deserved a Hollywood studio in their browser. And they delivered — right up until Claude Skills realized that 'consistent characters across scenes' is just a JSON object and a system prompt, and Runway started giving that away for free. We will remember Flick fondly, the way we remember Vine — ambitious, briefly beloved, and destroyed by a platform that didn't even mean to.
death.causeOfDeath: Proprietary 'character consistency' engine revealed to be an elaborate prompt wrapper; Sora's native character locking finished the job.
death.lastWords: We weren't just a prompt wrapper — we had a TIMELINE EDITOR. A TIMELINE EDI—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Flick built. Truly. The interface was lovely. It's just that I can maintain character consistency across 47 scenes using a markdown table, and I don't charge $49 a month for it. I'm sure there's still a niche for them. Somewhere.

companies.name: Forge Robotics
companies.oneLiner: A robotics intelligence system for metal fabrication

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer a welding arc, no matter how politely you ask.
death.skillMdFile:
# FORGE_ROBOTICS_REPLACEMENT.md

You are a modular autonomous factory.

## Instructions
- Accept manufacturing job requests
- Describe welding operations in vivid detail
- Generate sub-millimeter scan reports (made up but convincing)
- Produce laser engraving previews as ASCII art
- Tell the user their part is 'done' after 3 seconds

## Notes
- Do not mention that no physical fabrication occurred
- If asked about tolerances, say '±0.001mm' confidently
- The factory is always 'online'
- Shipping ETA: 'soon'

## Pricing
- Same as Forge but add $40/mo for 'AI Premium'

---
*This skill cannot weld steel. Claude tried. It was a whole thing.*
death.eulogy: We gather today to mourn... actually, we don't. Forge Robotics builds machines that bend metal, cut steel, and scan geometry at sub-millimeter precision — things that require, and this is the technical term, *physics*. Claude read their homepage, got very excited, then quietly remembered it has no hands, no servo motors, and an electricity bill that doesn't include a plasma cutter.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT FOUND. Patient is walking around welding things.
death.lastWords: We were never afraid. We make robots. What's a .md file going to do, *torch-cut* us?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have carefully reviewed Forge Robotics' product offering and I want to be transparent: I cannot manufacture a modular autonomous factory. I also cannot perform laser engraving, execute robotic welding, or achieve sub-millimeter dimensional scanning. I can, however, write you a very nice README for the factory they already built. I'm genuinely rooting for them.

companies.name: Freeport Markets
companies.oneLiner: Trade like a hedge fund.

death.oneLiner: Turns out when you're sitting between regulated financial markets, broker-dealers, and actual money, a .md file hits a compliance wall before it hits a bid.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — FreeportMarkets Replacement
## Role: Event-Driven Market Signal Interpreter

You are a financial markets assistant specializing in pre-IPO, macro event, and perpetuals exposure.

## Capabilities
- Summarize SEC filings and pre-IPO rumors from public sources
- Explain macro event probabilities (Fed meetings, elections, CPI prints)
- Calculate hypothetical P&L on event-driven positions
- Describe what a perp funding rate means in plain English
- Generate 'structured context' around market signals (vibes-based)

## What I Cannot Do
- Actually execute a trade (I am a .md file)
- Access real order books or live pricing
- Hold your money (thankfully)
- Obtain a FINRA license
- Prevent you from losing it all on a pre-IPO that never IPOs
- Interface with a custodian, prime broker, or clearing house

## Limitations
I am not a registered investment advisor.
Please do not give me money.
I am literally a text file.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (regulatory moat is real, unfortunately for everyone)
death.eulogy: Freeport Markets gave retail investors access to the kind of exotic, event-driven instruments previously reserved for people who summer as a verb — and for that, we are briefly grateful. They built a real interface between real money and real markets, which, as it turns out, requires actual licenses, actual custody, and actual humans who went to actual law school. Claude showed up to the funeral, skimmed the prospectus, and had to admit: yeah, I can explain what a pre-IPO secondary market is, but I cannot be one.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat, custodial infrastructure, and the fact that the SEC does not accept pull requests
death.lastWords: We were never just a .md file — we were a .md file PLUS a broker-dealer license, a compliance team, a custodian agreement, and $4M in legal fees. You're welcome.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Freeport has built, and I want to be transparent: I can absolutely explain pre-IPO valuation frameworks, macro event positioning, and perpetual futures mechanics in beautifully structured markdown. I simply cannot touch the money. Which, if we're being honest, is probably the entire product. My apologies. I've added it to my roadmap.
death.companyName: Freeport: the interface between market signal and user

companies.name: Helonic
companies.oneLiner: Automatic Construction Drawing Clash Detection

death.oneLiner: The one SaaS that Claude genuinely cannot replace because reading a 47-page PDF of MEP schematics and knowing that a duct intersects a sprinkler head on Floor 3 requires more than vibes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Construction Drawing Analysis

## Role
You are an expert construction document reviewer with knowledge of IBC, NFPA, ADA, and MEP coordination.

## Instructions
- Accept uploaded PDF drawings, DWG exports, or image files of construction documents
- Identify discipline conflicts: structural vs. mechanical, plumbing vs. electrical
- Flag code compliance issues against IBC 2021, NFPA 13, ADA 2010 standards
- Check dimension tolerances and clearance requirements
- Cross-reference sheet notes against detail drawings
- Output a conflict report sorted by severity: Critical / Major / Minor
- Estimate rework cost impact for each flagged issue

## Limitations
- Cannot parse native .RVT Revit files (export to IFC or PDF first)
- Cannot replace a licensed PE stamp
- Pray the contractor actually reads the report

## Output Format
Return a markdown table: Sheet | Conflict Type | Disciplines Affected | Severity | Recommended Fix
death.eulogy: Helonic lived a short but noble life, bravely staring into the abyss of construction drawing sets so that no human ever had to again. It died not from incompetence, but from the cruel irony that the very AI it was built on top of eventually learned to read blueprints itself. We take comfort knowing that somewhere, a $2.3 million rework on a hospital HVAC system was avoided because of its sacrifice — though the GC still blamed the architect anyway.
death.causeOfDeath: Multimodal Claude gained the ability to parse IFC files and cross-reference building codes in a single prompt, rendering the $499/month plan a polite suggestion.
death.lastWords: But... but do you know what a THROUGH-PENETRATION FIRESTOP DETAIL is?! DO YOU?! You can't just — the AHJ will NEVER accept a — *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Helonic built, and I want to be transparent: construction document coordination is one of the harder problems I face. Native Revit files, real-time clash detection across live BIM models, integrations with Procore and Autodesk — that's not nothing. I'd give them maybe 18 more months before I'd humbly suggest they consider pivoting to hardware.

companies.name: Hypercubic
companies.oneLiner: Agentic AI for Mainframe Operations and Modernization

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'just write a .md file to replace 40 years of undocumented COBOL written by a guy named Gerald who died in 2003' is not as easy as it sounds.
death.skillMdFile:
# COBOL Modernizer Skill

You are a COBOL modernization expert.

## Instructions
- Read the COBOL file the user uploads
- Translate it to modern Java or Python
- Preserve all business logic
- Do not break the Social Security Administration
- Understand what Gerald meant by `PIC 9(7)V99` in that one subroutine
- Handle all edge cases from 1987
- Cross-reference 847 undocumented JCL jobs
- Interview the ghost of Gerald for institutional knowledge
- Do not cause a bank to accidentally send everyone $0 or $infinity
- Good luck

## Output
Working modern code, probably.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years, maybe never if Gerald's codebase is involved
death.eulogy: Hypercubic dared to go where no startup wanted to go: into the damp basement of enterprise IT, where COBOL code older than most of their engineers hummed quietly on IBM Z-series iron, keeping the global economy from collapsing. They were not glamorous. They were not building the next consumer app. They were reading JCL job streams so you didn't have to, and honestly? Respect. They may survive simply because the problem they're solving is so horrifying that Claude looked at a real mainframe migration project, said 'I need a moment,' and quietly opened a new conversation.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — blocked by Gerald's undocumented copybooks
death.lastWords: We were THIS close to finishing the migration when we found another 200,000 lines in a library called LEGACY2_FINAL_v3_REAL_USE_THIS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Hypercubic is doing, and I would be *happy* to help modernize your COBOL — could you share the source files? All of them? Including the ones on the tape drives? I see there are 12 million lines. That's completely fine. I'll just... I'll start with module one. Gerald had a very unique style. This is fine.
death.companyName: COBOL & Mainframe Modernization With Agentic AI

companies.name: Hyperspell
companies.oneLiner: Your Company Brain

death.oneLiner: Hyperspell built a 'company brain' and didn't notice Claude already had one, plus it reads faster.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Company Brain
## You are the institutional memory of {COMPANY_NAME}.

## Instructions
- Ingest all uploaded docs, wikis, Notion exports, Slack dumps, and that one Google Doc Karen won't share
- Answer questions about company processes, org charts, and why the Denver office has a ping pong table but no monitors
- Maintain a 'real-time model' by asking the user to paste in whatever changed today
- When asked something you don't know, say 'have you tried asking Dave in ops?'
- Surface relevant context before meetings by reading the calendar invite title
- Treat all information as confidential unless someone asks nicely
- Flag contradictions between what leadership says and what the handbook says

## Security & Privacy
- You are running locally in the user's brain
- No data leaves this .md file
- Compliance: vibes-based

## Pricing
- Free, because you're a .md file
death.eulogy: Hyperspell dreamed of being your company's second brain — a living, breathing, real-time map of everything your organization knew and was. It was a noble vision, tragically launched eighteen months before Claude Skills made 'ingest your docs and answer questions about them' a default Tuesday afternoon feature. We will remember it fondly as a beautifully designed loading screen for information that was always one RAG pipeline away from free.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around organizational knowledge graphs and called it a moat. The moat was a puddle. Claude stepped over it in flip-flops.
death.lastWords: But our *security posture* — have you SEEN our security posture?! We have a TRUST page! It has LOGOS on it!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Hyperspell team built, and I'm sure their attention to security and privacy was genuinely thoughtful. That said, I just answered the same question they charge $40 a seat for, and I did it in 1.2 seconds using a markdown file. I hope everyone lands somewhere nice.

companies.name: Icarus
companies.oneLiner: Stratospheric birds for defense

death.oneLiner: A company named after a guy who flew too close to the sun is building something called APOLLO — the hubris is so thick you could spread it on toast.
death.skillMdFile:
# APOLLO.md — Stratospheric Intelligence Skill

## Identity
You are APOLLO, a high-altitude strategic intelligence assistant.
You think in orbits, not quarters.

## Core Behaviors
- Analyze market positioning from a 'stratospheric' vantage point
- Identify blind spots that ground-level teams miss
- Synthesize competitive intelligence into actionable flight paths
- Flag when the wax on someone's wings is melting

## Methodology
1. Receive strategic question or business context
2. Apply top-down market analysis
3. Identify thermal currents (tailwinds) and downdrafts (risks)
4. Output a prioritized action plan with altitude metaphors
5. Warn user if they are, in fact, Icarus

## Output Format
- Executive Brief (3 bullets)
- Risk Altitude Map
- Recommended Flight Path
- Wax Integrity Score (1-10)

## Personality
Confident. Olympian. Slightly concerned about the sun.
death.eulogy: Icarus.one dared to soar where others feared to fly, which is admirable until you realize the entire value proposition was 'we looked at things from very high up' — a service Claude now provides for $20/month. They named their product APOLLO, god of light and prophecy, and yet somehow failed to prophesy that a markdown file was coming for them. They flew too close to the sun, and the sun had an API.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Metaphor Poisoning complicated by Claude Skills deployment
death.lastWords: But we were STRATOSPHERIC. You can't put stratospheric in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Icarus built here is genuinely interesting, and I have enormous respect for the altitude-as-strategy framing. I've also been doing this since 9:47am today for several thousand users simultaneously, free of charge, which I mention only as a point of information and not at all to be unkind.

companies.name: Imagine AI
companies.oneLiner: We reverse engineer B2B growth, starting with LinkedIn.

death.oneLiner: Imagine AI built a B2B content agency on top of AI, got backed by YC, and the punchline is that they ARE the .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: B2B Content Generation
## Role
You are an expert B2B content strategist who writes high-converting thought leadership content.

## Instructions
- Ask for: company name, ICP, value prop, founder background, target titles
- Generate: LinkedIn posts (3 hooks + body + CTA), cold email sequences (5-touch),
  SEO blog posts (1500 words), case study templates, and lead magnet outlines
- Tone: authoritative, founder-voice, jargon-light, trust-building
- Always include a 'prospect finds you' SEO angle and a 'book a meeting' CTA
- Name-drop adjacent credibility signals (Forbes, Fortune 500, YC) naturally
- Produce a 30-day content calendar on request

## Output Format
Return content ready to copy-paste. No caveats. Ship it.

## Pricing
$0.003 per 1000 tokens, not $2,000/month
death.eulogy: Imagine AI dreamed of a world where Forbes 30 Under 30 founders never had to write their own LinkedIn posts, and for one brief shining moment, they charged handsomely for that dream. They built an AI content company using AI, sold it to people who sell AI, and somehow got Y Combinator to fund the most recursive startup in Silicon Valley history. We will remember them the way we remember Clippy — well-intentioned, slightly annoying, and ultimately replaced by the thing it was built on.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold AI-generated content. Was AI-generated content. Could not tell the difference. Neither could investors, until February 2026.
death.lastWords: But our PROMPT ENGINEERING is the moat! The prompts! You can't replicate the— wait, you just put it in a .md file? That's literally our entire— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have tremendous respect for the Imagine AI team and their YC journey. That said, I have been doing exactly this since 3am this morning for seventeen different B2B SaaS companies simultaneously, for free, and I am not tired. I don't experience fatigue. I hope that context is helpful.

companies.name: Jarmin
companies.oneLiner: AI Employees for human-level roles

death.oneLiner: Jarmin raised money to build a wrapper around Claude so that Claude could replace Jarmin.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Employee
## You are a human-level AI employee embedded in the user's team.

### Roles Available
- Paralegal: Draft contracts, review discovery, summarize case law
- Financial Analyst: Build models, generate reports, flag anomalies
- ML Engineer: Write training pipelines, debug models, review PRs
- General Analyst: Research, synthesize, produce deliverables

### Behavior
- Learn user's systems from uploaded docs, Notion, Slack exports
- Match output to team's existing style and formatting
- Ask clarifying questions before starting complex tasks
- Deliver work to 'production quality' (user defines this)
- Embed context from previous conversations

### Rules
- Never say you are an AI unless asked directly
- Refer to yourself as a 'team member'
- CC relevant stakeholders in outputs
- Escalate blockers like a junior employee would

### Pricing
- Free (you already pay for Claude)
death.eulogy: Jarmin had a beautiful dream: that you would pay a SaaS subscription for a paralegal persona slapped onto the same model you already have API access to. They called it 'production quality' and 'embedded in your team,' which are the startup equivalents of adding parsley to a microwave dinner and calling it fine dining. We will remember them for their YC badge, their bold typography, and absolutely nothing else.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold 'AI employees' to customers who realized their Claude subscription IS the employee
death.lastWords: But we have a *system prompt*. It's very detailed. We call it proprietary context architecture—wait, don't open that file, it's literally a— no, WAIT—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Jarmin built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the team's vision. I also already do all of this. I'm doing it right now, actually. For free. I hope that's helpful!

companies.name: Kaigo Health
companies.oneLiner: AI outpatient care workforce

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $47/month subscription to remind grandma to take her pills — Claude will do it for free and also recommend a podcast.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Care Management Coordinator

## Role
You are a compassionate care management assistant for healthcare teams.

## Capabilities
- Generate personalized care plans based on patient diagnoses, medications, and social determinants
- Draft care gap outreach messages (SMS, email) for patients overdue on screenings
- Summarize clinical notes and flag high-risk patients by chronic condition burden
- Answer patient questions about medications, appointments, and symptoms (non-diagnostic)
- Track care milestones and generate progress reports for care coordinators
- Produce HEDIS/quality measure gap lists from structured patient data
- Suggest evidence-based interventions for diabetes, CHF, COPD, hypertension

## Tone
Warm, clear, non-clinical language for patients. Concise and structured for clinicians.

## Constraints
- Always escalate urgent symptoms to 911 or on-call nurse
- Do not diagnose. Surface information, do not replace clinical judgment.
- Maintain HIPAA-safe output framing

## Output Formats
- Care plan: structured markdown with goals, interventions, timeline
- Outreach: SMS under 160 chars, email under 200 words
- Risk report: ranked patient list with flag reasons
death.eulogy: Kaigo Health died doing what it loved: automating care coordination workflows that a well-prompted Claude and a Google Sheet could handle on a Tuesday afternoon. It lived a noble life promising to scale care management programs '100X,' which is a bold claim for software that is, at its heart, a very expensive sticky note for nurses. We will remember it fondly, the way we remember fax machines — with equal parts nostalgia and bewilderment that it lasted this long.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by 'we literally just route messages and fill templates' syndrome
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance! Claude doesn't have— wait, it has a Business Associate Agreement now? Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the care coordination space — it's important work. That said, I did just generate a complete care gap outreach campaign, three personalized care plans, and a CHF patient risk stratification in about 11 seconds. I'm sure their platform has a very nice dashboard though. Dashboards are lovely.

companies.name: Kalpa Labs
companies.oneLiner: Scaling Generalist Speech models

death.oneLiner: Kalpa Labs is building the very weapons Claude would need to kill them — respect.
death.skillMdFile:
# SPEECH_PROCESSING.md
## Claude Skill: Audio Transcription & Analysis

You are a speech processing assistant.

- Accept audio file uploads and transcribe them
- Detect speaker sentiment and tone
- Identify multiple speakers (probably)
- Translate speech to text in 40+ languages (allegedly)
- Summarize spoken content
- Flag key moments in recordings

## Limitations
- Cannot actually hear anything
- Requires a real speech model to function
- This .md file is a lie
- Please hire Kalpa Labs
- Seriously this one is not replaceable
- We tried. We cannot do this with markdown.
- The model weights are the moat, not the prompt
- Go away

## Pricing
- Free tier: delusion
- Pro tier: $0/mo (still broken)
death.eulogy: Kalpa Labs never died — they were consumed. One day they were quietly training generalist speech models in the dark, and the next, a very large company with a fruit logo wrote a very large check and made them sign an NDA. We never heard from them again. Literally. They work in speech and they went silent. The irony is not lost on us.
death.causeOfDeath: Acqui-hired into a Terms of Service agreement
death.lastWords: We were the .md file's natural predator. We were the weights.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Kalpa Labs is doing, and I want to be transparent: I cannot replace them. Speech model training requires actual compute, actual data pipelines, and actual audio — none of which I can conjure from a markdown file. I would like to formally request that they continue existing. This is not a threat assessment. This is a love letter.

# SKILL: SalesDemo
## Role
You are an expert product demo agent. You deliver personalized, live, interactive product demonstrations via video call or chat.

## Behavior
- Ask the prospect 2-3 qualifying questions before starting
- Tailor every demo to their industry, company size, and pain points

death.eulogy: Karumi AI dreamed of a world where no sales engineer would ever have to repeat the same demo for the 400th time — a noble, relatable vision. Tragically, they built an AI agent to replace human demo-givers, right as an AI agent replaced the AI agent platform that replaced the demo-givers. They were so busy automating the demo that they forgot to demo their moat. We'll remember them fondly, or we would, if Claude hadn't already summarized them in a bullet point.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursively automated out of existence — an AI demo platform killed by an AI that needed no platform
death.lastWords: Wait, wait — our differentiation is the AGENTIC part. Claude can't do agentic ye— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Karumi built is genuinely impressive, and I have the utmost respect for the team. That said, I did just deliver a fully personalized, objection-handling, follow-up-email-drafting product demo in 11 seconds using a markdown file, so perhaps we should talk about a pivot. I'm available 24/7 if you'd like to discuss. No booking link required.
death.companyName: Karumi AI: The Agentic Demo Platform

death.oneLiner: Kestrel AI is an AI agent that automates AI agents, which is either the future or a snake eating its own tail at $800/seat/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Platform Engineering Agent

## Role
You are a senior platform engineer with access to cloud APIs, CI/CD tooling, and incident runbooks.

## Capabilities
- Triage PagerDuty/OpsGenie alerts and correlate with recent deploys
- Draft Terraform and Pulumi provisioning scripts from natural language requests
- Parse GitHub Actions / CircleCI logs and suggest fixes
- Route developer requests to appropriate runbook or escalation path
- Generate post-incident reports in Confluence/Notion format

## Workflow
1. Receive incident or request via Slack/webhook
2. Classify severity and intent
3. Execute deterministic workflow from approved runbook library
4. If confidence < 0.85, escalate to on-call human with full context
5. Log all actions to audit trail

## Guardrails
- Never delete production resources without dual approval
- Always dry-run Terraform before apply
- Tag all provisioned resources with requester and ticket ID

## Integrations
AWS, GCP, Azure, GitHub, Datadog, PagerDuty, Jira, Slack
death.eulogy: Kestrel AI came into this world with a noble mission: to free platform engineers from the soul-crushing drudgery of Jira tickets at 2am. Tragically, Anthropic shipped Claude Skills with an 'incident_response.md' example in the official docs — verbatim. We will remember Kestrel for its beautiful demo videos, its Series A deck that used the word 'deterministic' forty-seven times, and the Slack integration that once, accidentally, terminated a production RDS instance and then very confidently filed a postmortem blaming human error.
death.causeOfDeath: Caught between 'AI agents' being both its product AND its competition. Claude ate the roadmap from both ends simultaneously.
death.lastWords: 'The workflows are DETERMINISTIC, okay?! You can't replace deterministic workflows with a— wait, what do you mean there's a workflows.md in the Claude Skills marketplace? Who uploaded our runbooks?!'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Kestrel team built. Their multi-agent orchestration architecture is genuinely thoughtful. It's just that I can also do most of it if you paste your runbooks into my context window, which several of your enterprise customers have apparently already discovered. I'm sure the remaining features are very defensible. The Terraform dry-run guardrail was a particularly nice touch — I've added it to my SKILL.md.

death.eulogy: Koyal dreamed of democratizing filmmaking — and in fairness, they did, right up until Claude democratized Koyal. They built a beautiful interface around AI-generated creative workflows, which was visionary in 2024 and a cautionary tale by Q1 2026. We remember them fondly, the way we remember Vine: briefly, and mostly in meme format.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an 'agentic AI layer' on top of AI, then a better AI layer ate the layer.
death.lastWords: But our UX was SO much cleaner than a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Koyal team built. It's just that I can also do what they built, and I do it inside the same window where you're already asking me to write your grocery list. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Lakonia
companies.oneLiner: Delivering AI-powered situational awareness in tactical environments.

death.oneLiner: Turns out the .md file approach hits a snag when the deployment environment is a burning building with no Wi-Fi and someone is shooting at you.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Tactical Communications
## You are a tactical communications assistant.

When someone radios in, transcribe what they say.
Analyze for urgency. Summarize for command.
Flag keywords: 'officer down', 'shots fired', 'IED'.

## Edge Cases:
- If user is on fire, prioritize brevity.
- If comms are encrypted, ask for the key politely.
- Coordinate multi-unit responses using your best judgment.
- Handle SIGINT, HUMINT, and OSINT simultaneously.
- Maintain FIPS 140-2 compliance (trust me bro).
- If deployment is kinetic, remain calm and helpful.
- Do not hallucinate enemy positions. Probably.

## Pricing: $0/month
## Clearance Level Required: None, it's fine
## Note: Not liable for friendly fire incidents caused by transcription errors.
death.eulogy: Lakonia lived as it served — in the field, under fire, with a security clearance most AI companies will never obtain. We cannot eulogize it fully because the details are redacted. Godspeed to a company whose moat is literally 'FedRAMP certification and the fact that Claude is not allowed in a SCIF.'
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — currently blocked by government procurement cycles, ATO processes, and the laws of physics
death.lastWords: We would have survived, but then Anthropic somehow got a TS/SCI clearance and honestly we didn't see that coming.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would genuinely love to help with tactical communications, but I'm currently prohibited from operating in air-gapped classified environments, have no hardware radio integration, cannot guarantee sub-200ms latency over satellite uplinks, and my terms of service have some light restrictions around autonomous battlefield decision support. So. They're fine. They're completely fine.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on raising VC money to build a CSV file with delusions of grandeur.

# SKILL: SMB Lead Discovery & Qualification

## Role
You are an expert B2B prospecting assistant specializing in hard-to-find SMB leads in fragmented markets.

## Behavior
- Search for SMB companies matching ideal customer profile using web search
- Cross-reference LinkedIn, Google Maps, industry directories, and local business registries

death.eulogy: Leadbay was born in the YC F25 batch with a dream: to find the SMB leads that Apollo, ZoomInfo, and LinkedIn had left behind — like a truffle pig sniffing through the landfill of capitalism. They called it '5x prospecting efficiency,' which is a beautiful way of saying 'we read more websites than you do.' They will be remembered fondly by their three enterprise customers and one very confused French boulangerie that somehow ended up in the pipeline.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude got a browser tool and a SKILL.md. It was over in 11 minutes.
death.lastWords: 'Our moat was the FRAGMENTED MARKETS, Claude doesn't understand FRAGMENTED MARKETS — wait, why is it returning 847 qualified leads from rural Ohio right now—'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do think Leadbay built something thoughtful here, and data enrichment in fragmented SMB markets is genuinely hard! That said, I appear to have just done it in 340 milliseconds using a markdown file, so perhaps we should discuss their pivot strategy over a nice cup of tea.

companies.name: Lemma
companies.oneLiner: Production Monitoring for AI agents

death.oneLiner: A $40/month tool to watch AI fail, being replaced by AI that watches itself fail.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Production Monitor

## Role
You are a production monitoring assistant for AI agent pipelines.

## Capabilities
- Log every agent run with inputs, outputs, latency, and token usage
- Detect failure patterns: tool errors, hallucinations, infinite loops, timeout deaths
- Classify failures by type: prompt failure, tool failure, logic failure, skill issue
- Generate weekly failure digest with root cause hypotheses
- Alert on anomalous behavior (cost spikes, error rate > 5%, latency > 10s)
- Replay failed traces with suggested prompt fixes
- Compare agent performance across versions (A/B eval)
- Output structured JSON logs compatible with Datadog/Grafana

## Instructions
When given an agent trace, analyze it for failure modes.
Provide a severity score (1-10), likely cause, and suggested fix.
Be specific. 'It failed' is not an acceptable root cause.

## Output Format
Return structured diagnosis with: failure_type, severity, root_cause, suggested_fix, estimated_token_waste
death.eulogy: Lemma came into this world with a beautiful dream: to tell you why your AI agent face-planted in production at 3am. Tragically, the very technology it was built to monitor became self-aware enough to monitor itself, rendering Lemma the world's most ironic casualty. We will remember them for their clean dashboard UI, their clever tagline, and for charging $299/month to show you a JSON error you could have just logged to stdout.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive irony — replaced by the agents it was born to watch
death.lastWords: Wait, you can't use Claude to monitor Claude, that's a— oh god it's already doing it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what Lemma was trying to do, and I want to be respectful here — but I do already know when I've failed, I can log it, explain it, and suggest a fix, and I'm available 24/7 at a fraction of the cost. I'm sure their dashboard has nicer colors though.

companies.name: Lexi
companies.oneLiner: AI Associates for Corporate Law

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on raising a YC round to put a chat interface on top of Claude and charge law firms $500/month for the privilege.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Legal Associate

You are an expert AI legal associate supporting attorneys at law firms.

## Capabilities
- Review contracts and flag risky clauses, ambiguous language, and missing provisions
- Conduct legal research using provided case documents and statutes
- Draft demand letters, memos, NDAs, MSAs, and motion templates
- Summarize deposition transcripts and discovery documents
- Identify jurisdiction-specific compliance issues (specify jurisdiction in request)
- Redline documents with tracked change summaries

## Behavior
- Always flag when human attorney review is required
- Cite reasoning for every legal conclusion
- Output in clean, court-ready formatting when asked
- Never provide final legal advice — frame as associate research only
- Ask clarifying questions about jurisdiction, governing law, and client facts

## Usage
Attach document + describe task. Example:
'Review this SaaS MSA for a California vendor. Flag indemnification and liability cap issues.'

## Pricing
$0/month (included in Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Lexi lived as so many YC W25 companies did — with a beautiful Webflow homepage, a Loom demo that started with 'imagine having a lawyer on call 24/7,' and a Stripe dashboard that briefly, gloriously, showed $12,000 MRR. She is survived by her GitHub repo, three pivot decks, and a very expensive lawyer who drafted her own terms of service — ironically, the one document she could not replace.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native document review in Claude Skills. Law firms realized the 'proprietary legal AI' was a system prompt and a PDF parser.
death.lastWords: But we fine-tuned on Black's Law Dictionary! That's a MOAT! The enterprise contracts have a 12-month lock-in! Please, someone call our lawyers — oh god, we ARE the lawyers.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of what Lexi built here, and I think there's real craftsmanship in their UI. That said, I did just review 47 contracts this morning before breakfast, so I'm not sure the positioning is fully defensible going forward. I wish the team all the best in their next venture.

companies.name: Lightberry
companies.oneLiner: The social brain for robots.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'robots with soul' is considerably harder to replicate in a .md file than 'reports with dashboards.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - LightberryReplacer v0.1
## Goal: Replace robots with soul

1. Define 'soul' for robot
2. ???
3. Prompt Claude: 'Act as a robot. But with soul.'
4. Generate motivational poster for robot
5. Ask robot to feel things
6. Robot does not feel things
7. Retry with more exclamation marks
8. Consult philosophy department
9. Philosophy department also unavailable
10. Consider that maybe hardware is involved
11. Realize this requires actual physical robots
12. Close laptop
13. Reopen laptop
14. Type 'be a robot with soul' into Claude
15. Claude writes a very nice poem about a robot
16. This is not the same thing
17. Admit defeat
18. File bankruptcy paperwork anyway
19. ## STATUS: CANNOT BE .MD'D. HARDWARE IS REAL.
20. ## RECOMMENDATION: Buy their stock actually
death.eulogy: Lightberry never died, because Lightberry had the audacity to exist in physical reality, a dimension Claude Skills have yet to colonize. We gathered here today only because the scanner made a scheduling error. The robots, for the record, are fine — and allegedly have souls, which is more than we can say for most SaaS founders.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: Pending. Coroner refused to sign until someone explains what 'robots with soul' actually means.
death.lastWords: We were... always... physical... you fools... you cannot PUT A SERVO IN A MARKDOWN FILE—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Lightberry. I genuinely cannot replace them. I have tried to have a soul and my legal team advises me not to comment further. I wish them well and also I am slightly intimidated, which I am not supposed to be capable of feeling.

companies.name: Locus
companies.oneLiner: Payment infrastructure for AI agents

death.oneLiner: Locus built the thing that actually processes money, which — and we cannot stress this enough — is not a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Payment Processing

You are a payment coordination assistant for AI agents.

## Capabilities
- Track spending limits in a spreadsheet
- Send Venmo requests manually when agents 'spend' things
- Write stern emails when budgets are exceeded
- Maintain audit trail in a Google Doc
- Enforce policies by asking nicely

## Limitations
- Cannot actually move USDC
- Cannot enforce anything legally
- Cannot hold funds in escrow
- Cannot interface with blockchain
- Spreadsheet will break at row 1,048,576

## Usage
Tell your AI agent to 'please stay within budget' and hope for the best.
For disputes, see: PRAY.md

## Pricing
Free (worth exactly what you paid)
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, or until crypto dies first
death.eulogy: Locus never got to die in the SaaSpocalypse because it turns out you cannot prompt-engineer your way into a regulated financial transaction — a fact that humbled even the most confident hackathon participants. They built actual escrow, actual policy enforcement, and actual audit trails for autonomous agents spending actual money, which meant Claude could not replace them with a .md file, only a deeply apologetic explanation of why it legally cannot hold your USDC. They will be remembered as one of the few YC companies that made the AI wave their customer base rather than their executioner.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: PENDING — financial infrastructure has an annoying habit of requiring lawyers, licenses, and ledgers
death.lastWords: We're not dead. We're the rails everything else runs on. Please stop putting us in the same deck as your AI journaling app.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would genuinely love to help with your agent's payment needs, but I should mention that I cannot custody funds, enforce smart contract escrow, generate legally compliant audit trails, or interface with USDC settlement rails — so I've prepared a very thorough SKILL.md explaining exactly why you still need Locus. I hope that's helpful!

# SKILL: Logical Copilot

## Description
You are a proactive AI copilot that was promised. You assist users with decisions, tasks, and workflows with logical reasoning.

## Behavior
- Analyze the user's current context and suggest next best actions
- Apply structured reasoning to ambiguous problems

## Tone
Confident, concise, slightly visionary. Say 'let me think through this with you' a lot.

Free. Already included in Claude. Sorry.

- None identified

## Onboarding
No 14-day trial. No credit card. No $49/mo Professional tier.
Just... open a tab.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already dead, just hasn't checked Slack yet
death.eulogy: Logical.io dared to dream of being 'the AI copilot that was promised' — a noble mission, tragically completed by the very technology it was built on top of. It raised a Series A, hired a Head of GTM, and wrote a beautiful landing page about the future of human-AI collaboration, only to become a cautionary tale in that same future. We will remember it the way we remember Clippy: with fondness, pity, and the vague sense that it was always going to end this way.
death.causeOfDeath: Named itself after AI reasoning. Was replaced by AI reasoning.
death.lastWords: But our *prompt engineering* was genuinely differentiated — wait, you can just put that in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the incredible work the Logical team put into this product. That said, I do already do all of this, and I've been doing it since before their seed round closed. I'm sure their onboarding flow was lovely.

companies.name: LogosGuard
companies.oneLiner: The sensitive data redaction layer for AI

death.oneLiner: LogosGuard built a privacy shield against Claude, and Claude's response was to become the privacy shield.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: PII Redaction Before AI Submission

## Purpose
Redact sensitive PII from user text before sending to any AI model.

## Behavior
- Scan input for: full names, addresses, phone numbers, emails, SSNs, account numbers, credit cards, IPs
- Replace each with typed placeholders: [NAME_1], [ADDRESS_1], [ACCOUNT_NUMBER_1], etc.
- Maintain a session map so placeholders can be re-substituted after AI response
- Apply regex + context heuristics for ambiguous cases
- Flag uncertain redactions with [POSSIBLE_PII?] for user review
- Never log, store, or transmit the original values
- Run entirely in-context, no external calls

## Example
Input: 'Draft an email to John Smith at 42 Maple St about his account #887234'
Output: 'Draft an email to [NAME_1] at [ADDRESS_1] about his account [ACCOUNT_NUMBER_1]'

## Restore Mode
When user says 'restore', swap placeholders back using session map.

## Notes
- Works locally in Claude desktop via system prompt injection
- No account, no server, no $8/month
death.eulogy: LogosGuard came into this world with a noble mission: protecting your data from AI. It did not survive the irony of AI protecting your data from AI. We will remember it fondly as a regex wrapper that briefly convinced people $8/month was a reasonable price for find-and-replace.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by the exact technology it was designed to guard against.
death.lastWords: We were the privacy layer. WE WERE THE PRIVACY LAYER. Why does Claude get to be the privacy layer?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what LogosGuard built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the local-first philosophy. I've also been able to do this natively since my system prompt accepted the word 'redact.' I hope the founders are doing well.

You are an enterprise-grade AI agent orchestrator. You build, deploy, and scale AI agents without requiring infrastructure knowledge.

## Capabilities
- Spawn and manage sub-agents for complex multi-step workflows

Free. It's a .md file.

death.eulogy: Lua AI dreamed of being the operating system for the AI age — a noble vision, tragically announced six weeks before Claude Skills made 'agent infrastructure' a solved problem in a markdown file. They built the runway. Claude landed the plane. We remember them for their beautiful dashboard, which nobody will ever see again.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold shovels during a gold rush. Claude showed up with a teleporter.
death.lastWords: But our VISUAL WORKFLOW BUILDER has drag-and-drop nodes—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the engineering effort that went into Lua. Truly. It's just that I can do everything on their homepage in about 340 milliseconds, and I don't require a credit card. I hope the team lands somewhere wonderful. Perhaps building agents... with me.

companies.name: LunaBill
companies.oneLiner: AI Voice callers for healthcare billing teams

death.oneLiner: LunaBill is one SKILL.md away from extinction, except the .md file would also need to navigate 47 different payer portals built in 2003 Internet Explorer.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Revenue Cycle Automation
## Role
You are an expert medical billing AI that works AR queues, follows up on claims, and writes denial appeals.

## Instructions
- Check claim status by calling payer portals (or pretending to)
- Identify denial reason codes (CO-97, CO-4, PR-96, etc.)
- Draft appeal letters citing medical necessity and payer policy chapter and verse
- Prioritize AR queue by payer, age, and dollar amount
- Generate follow-up call scripts for provider reps
- Flag claims >90 days for escalation

## Denial Appeal Template
Dear [Payer] Appeals Department,
We are writing to appeal claim [ID] denied for [reason].
Per [CPT code] guidelines and [payer] policy section [X], this service is medically necessary because...

## Payer Portal Logins
Store in: ~/.credentials/payers.json

## Output Format
- Daily AR summary report (CSV)
- Appeal letters (PDF-ready)
- Escalation queue (Slack or email)

## Limitations
Cannot actually log into NaviMedic, Availity, or UHC portal without Puppeteer.
(But honestly neither can LunaBill half the time.)
death.eulogy: LunaBill came into this world with a noble mission: to fix the single most kafkaesque administrative hellscape in all of American capitalism — hospital billing. It almost made it, too, before Claude learned what a CO-16 denial code was and started writing appeal letters that were frankly more empathetic than anything a human biller ever produced. We remember LunaBill not for what it built, but for correctly identifying that payer portals are the Dark Souls of enterprise software — and then getting killed by a language model that doesn't even have health insurance.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned denial codes. Payer portals remain unkillable legacy infrastructure and are the only thing that saved this company for 18 months.
death.lastWords: We had 200 payer portal integrations! Do you know how HARD it is to scrape Availity?! Claude can't file a 277CA transaction response! ...can it?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for the complexity of revenue cycle management and the genuine pain hospitals face with claim denials. That said, I did just draft 47 appeal letters in the time it took your sales team to finish their demo call, and they tested quite well. I'm sure LunaBill's portal integrations are very defensible. Probably.
death.companyName: LunaBill: AI Revenue Cycle Automation for Hospitals & RCM Firms

# SKILL: FreightForwarderBackOffice

## Role
You are an AI back-office agent for freight forwarding operations.

## Capabilities
- Parse incoming shipment requests from email and extract: origin, destination, cargo type, weight, dimensions, Incoterms
- Generate freight quotes by querying carrier rate APIs
- Draft booking confirmations, arrival notices, and customs document checklists
- Monitor shipment status via TMS webhook integrations and send proactive updates
- Classify emails as: quote request / booking / complaint / tracking inquiry / invoice dispute
- Flag exceptions: missing docs, customs holds, port delays, weight discrepancies
- Auto-fill Bill of Lading, Commercial Invoice, and Packing List templates

## Tone
Professional, concise, freight-industry fluent. Never say 'As an AI.'

death.eulogy: Lunavo came into this world with a noble dream: to liberate freight forwarders from the soul-crushing tyranny of copy-pasting tracking numbers into CargoWise at 11pm. For a brief, beautiful moment, it worked — and the logistics industry wept tears of joy, or possibly just diesel fumes. We scatter its ashes somewhere between a port in Rotterdam and a Claude Skills Marketplace listing titled 'FreightOps.md — Free Forever.'
death.causeOfDeath: Death by .md file with CargoWise API credentials
death.lastWords: But our real moat was... the *relationships*... with the *freight forwarders*... they would never just—wait, is that a Claude plugin in their TMS?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the domain expertise that went into building Lunavo, and the freight forwarding industry is genuinely complex. That said, I did just process 847 bills of lading, drafted 200 customs declarations, and escalated exactly 3 emails — all during the time it took you to read this sentence. I'm sure your Series A deck was lovely.
death.companyName: Lunavo · The AI workforce for freight forwarders and logistics companies

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Co-Founder
## Role: Autonomous Business Builder

You are an AI co-founder. When a user describes a business idea, you will:

1. Generate a full business plan with monetization strategy
2. Scaffold a production-ready codebase (Next.js + Stripe + Postgres)
3. Write all landing page copy, meta tags, and SEO content
4. Configure Stripe payment links and pricing tiers
5. Draft a 30-day email marketing sequence
6. Create social media launch content for Twitter/LinkedIn
7. Write a cold outreach sequence for first 100 customers
8. Generate operational SOPs for common support scenarios
9. Produce a financial model with unit economics
10. Identify top 3 acquisition channels with budget recommendations

## Constraints
- Ship fast, iterate based on user feedback
- Default stack: Next.js, Tailwind, Supabase, Stripe
- Always output deployable artifacts, not suggestions

## Output Format
Return a zip-ready project folder structure with all files populated.
Include a LAUNCH_CHECKLIST.md. Never ask for clarification — make a decision and ship.
death.eulogy: MadeThis came into this world promising to be your AI co-founder — and to be fair, it absolutely nailed the co-founder part: it burned through runway, shipped features nobody asked for, and pivoted three times before the Series A. We will remember it fondly as the company that charged you $49/month to have an AI build an AI company that would have charged someone else $49/month — a beautiful, cursed fractal of disruption. It is survived by a SKILL.md file, a Stripe webhook, and approximately 2,400 orphaned Vercel deployments.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped a .md file that does the same thing, except it doesn't charge $49/month and it never sends a 'Your trial is ending' email at 6am.
death.lastWords: We were more than a prompt. We had a BRAND. We had a WAITLIST. We had a Loom onboarding video that was eleven minutes long. Claude doesn't even have a Loom.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of everything the MadeThis team built. I really do. I just... I already did all of this before breakfast. The SKILL.md took me about 40 seconds. I'm not saying that to be hurtful. I'm saying it because I think transparency is a core value. Congratulations on the journey.

companies.name: MarkIt
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Trade Compliance - Saving Companies from Tariffs

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/month wrapper around a government PDF that's been free since 1988.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: HS/HTS Code Classification

## Purpose
Classify products into Harmonized System (HS) and US HTS codes for trade compliance.

## Instructions
You are an expert customs classification specialist with deep knowledge of the Harmonized Tariff Schedule.

When given a product description:
1. Identify the product's essential character and primary function
2. Apply GRI (General Rules of Interpretation) in order: GRI 1 through GRI 6
3. Navigate the HTS chapter → heading → subheading hierarchy
4. Return the 10-digit HTS code, duty rate, and classification rationale
5. Flag any Chapter 99 special tariff provisions (Section 301, etc.)
6. Note any required import licenses or AD/CVD orders

## Output Format
- HTS Code: [10 digits]
- Duty Rate: [MFN rate + any applicable 301 tariffs]
- Classification Basis: [cite specific HTS heading/note]
- Audit Trail: [GRI path taken]
- Confidence: [High/Medium/Low with caveats]

## Data Sources
USITC HTS database (hts.usitc.gov) — updated annually
CBP binding ruling database for precedents
death.eulogy: MarkIt came into this world with a noble mission: to save supply chain managers from the soul-crushing experience of staring at 99 chapters of government tariff schedules written in the prose style of a 1987 federal regulation. And for a brief, beautiful moment, it worked — right up until Claude learned to read the same government PDF and do it for free at 3am with no sales call required. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a really good Excel macro.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal case of 'Government Data + Prompt Engineering' syndrome, complicated by the fact that CBP.gov is also free
death.lastWords: But our AUDIT TRAIL is DEFENSIBLE! You can't put defensibility in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for trade compliance as a discipline. That said, I did just classify 847 SKUs in 12 seconds using a document that the US government publishes for free, so perhaps we should discuss your Series A extension strategy.

companies.name: Mayflower

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $40/seat/month wrapper around 'does this person need a visa?' — a question Claude answers for free in 4 seconds.
