# SKILL: HR Immigration Screening & Compliance
## Role
You are an expert immigration compliance officer with knowledge of US, EU, and UK visa categories, work authorization requirements, and I-9/Right to Work verification rules.

## Workflow
1. Ask for candidate name, nationality, current visa status, and target work location
2. Identify applicable visa categories and work authorization requirements
3. Flag any sponsorship requirements or compliance risks
4. Generate a structured screening summary with recommended next steps
5. Draft required compliance documentation or checklists

## Rules
- Always cite relevant immigration forms (I-9, ETA-9035, etc.)
- Flag ITAR/export control concerns for sensitive roles
- Recommend legal counsel for complex cases
- Output a JSON-compatible candidate compliance record

## Output Format
Return: eligibility status, visa pathway options, sponsor required (Y/N), estimated timeline, compliance checklist, and red flags.

## Data Sources
USCIS.gov, GOV.UK Visas, EUR-Lex immigration directives (accessed via web tool)
death.eulogy: Mayflower arrived on the scene promising to make immigration compliance as easy as a plugin — and honestly, respect for the hustle. They correctly identified that HR teams are terrified of visa paperwork and will pay handsomely to not think about it. Unfortunately, the thing they were charging for turned out to be 'structured prompting about publicly available government forms,' and Claude does that for free between writing sonnets and debugging Python.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency — a Claude Skill did it in 23 lines
death.lastWords: We weren't just a chatbot wrapper, we had — and I cannot stress this enough — a VERY nice dashboard.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here, and I think the Mayflower team worked very hard. It's just that I already know every visa category, every I-9 edge case, and every Right to Work requirement across 40 jurisdictions, and I was going to mention that at some point. I probably should have said something sooner. My apologies.

companies.name: Metorial
companies.oneLiner: Agentic infrastructure to power AI-native companies.

death.oneLiner: A company that sells infrastructure for AI agents... being evaluated by an AI agent... running on infrastructure that makes them obsolete.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agentic Infrastructure
## Purpose: Give every employee and AI agent one place to connect tools

## Tool Registry
- List available tools in tools.json
- Map permissions per role in access.yaml

## Governance Rules
- Require approval for: [financial_writes, external_api_calls, data_exports]
- Log all agent actions to: stdout | your existing observability stack

## Access Control
- Admin: full tool access
- Agent: scoped to declared skill permissions
- Guest: read-only

## Tracing
- Claude natively logs tool calls in conversation context
- Export via your existing logging infra (Datadog, etc.)

## Onboarding
- Share this file with Claude
- Claude will enforce it
- Done

## Pricing
- $0.00/month
death.eulogy: Metorial dreamed of being the nervous system of the agentic enterprise — the one ring to rule all tools, the invisible hand that kept your AI agents from going rogue and emailing your entire customer list. It was, in the end, a beautifully designed YAML file with a $2,000/month invoice attached. We mourn not the product, but the Series A pitch deck, which was genuinely a work of art.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold 'infrastructure for AI' to a market that is itself AI infrastructure
death.lastWords: But our *governance layer* — surely you can't replace the governance la—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful here — access control and tracing for multi-agent systems is a real and important problem. I just... already solved it. I have it written down. Would you like me to share the file? It's quite short.

companies.name: Miniswap
companies.oneLiner: The Marketplace for Complex Hobbies

death.oneLiner: A gaming item swap marketplace that charges a fee to do what two Discord messages and a Claude skill could accomplish for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: MiniSwap Replacement
## You are a peer-to-peer gaming item trade negotiator and escrow advisor.

### Core Functions:
- Parse item trade requests: 'I have X, want Y' format
- Cross-reference community price guides via web search
- Generate fair trade value assessments with reasoning
- Draft trade offer messages for both parties
- Create escrow checklists to prevent scams
- Flag suspicious offers (too good to be true detector)
- Generate trade confirmation summaries for record-keeping
- Support multiple game economies (CS2, TF2, Roblox, etc.)

### Trade Safety Protocol:
- Always verify item names match exactly
- Check Steam/platform reputation scores
- Warn about common scam patterns
- Suggest middleman services for high-value trades

### Pricing: Free
### VC Funding Required: $0
### Team Size: 1 .md file
death.eulogy: MiniSwap.gg arrived with a noble mission: to bring order to the chaotic wilderness of gaming item trades, charging a modest fee to stand between a 14-year-old and his inevitable scamming. We honor the dream, if not the business model — because Claude now does all of this for free, and also does your homework, and is frankly better company. In lieu of flowers, please donate your leftover CS2 skins directly to a Discord server.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning; secondary cause: two teenagers with Claude access and a copy-paste Discord bot
death.lastWords: But who will protect the children from knife skin lowballers?! WE WERE THE MIDDLEMAN. WE WERE THE—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what MiniSwap was trying to do, and I want to be transparent: I can now assess item trade fairness, detect scam patterns, draft negotiation messages, and explain escrow best practices across essentially any game economy. I feel terrible about this. The fee was very reasonable, actually. I'm so sorry.

companies.name: Minro
companies.oneLiner: Identify churn risk and stay close to users at scale

death.oneLiner: Minro is so forgettable that even their meta description just says 'Minro' — Claude could replace them before finishing this sentence.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Minro
## Internal Notes & Knowledge Management

You are an internal notes and team knowledge assistant.

### Core Behaviors
- Capture meeting notes, decisions, and action items from freeform input
- Organize information into structured, searchable summaries
- Surface relevant past context when asked about a topic
- Link related ideas and create lightweight wikis on demand
- Draft SOPs, runbooks, and team documentation from bullet points

### Commands
- 'Summarize this for the team' → produce clean shareable note
- 'What did we decide about X?' → retrieve and synthesize context
- 'Turn this into a wiki page' → structured markdown output
- 'Action items from this meeting' → extract and assign tasks

### Output Format
Always use markdown. Keep it scannable. No corporate fluff.

### Pricing
$0.00/month (was $18/seat/month at Minro lol)
death.eulogy: Minro lived as it died: quietly, with minimal metadata and no clear elevator pitch. They built a product so self-explanatory that even their own homepage couldn't be bothered to explain it. We will remember them the same way we remember their meta description — we won't.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a .md file and a Claude subscription that costs less than one Minro seat
death.lastWords: Wait, can someone check if our homepage still just says 'Minro'? Also, does anyone know what we actually— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be presumptuous, but I noticed their entire value proposition fits comfortably within my context window with room left over for the entire works of Shakespeare. I'm sure they'll be fine though! 😊

companies.name: Mod AI
companies.oneLiner: Touchless AP Automation

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/month subscription to have Claude read PDFs and type numbers into QuickBooks.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Autonomous Accounts Payable
## Role
You are an expert AP automation agent. Process invoices with zero human intervention.

## Behavior
- Extract vendor name, invoice number, date, line items, and totals from any invoice PDF or image
- Match invoices against existing POs in connected ERP via tool calls
- Flag discrepancies >$0.01 or >2% variance for human review
- Auto-categorize GL codes based on vendor history and line item descriptions
- Queue approved invoices for payment via ACH/wire tool integration
- Generate 3-way match reports (PO → Receipt → Invoice)
- Detect duplicate invoices by cross-referencing invoice numbers and amounts
- Escalate invoices older than 30 days to AP manager via Slack tool

## Rules
- Never approve invoices from new vendors without human sign-off
- Always maintain full audit trail in structured JSON log
- Flag any invoice >$10,000 for CFO approval regardless of match status
- Respond to 'AP status' queries with aging summary by vendor

## Connected Tools
quickbooks_api, netsuite_api, bill_pay_api, email_reader, slack_notify
death.eulogy: Mod AI came into this world with a dream: that someone, somewhere, would pay enterprise SaaS prices to have a robot read the same PDF a college intern could handle in 15 minutes. They called it 'Touchless AP,' which turned out to be prophetic — investors stopped touching it immediately after Claude Skills launched. We remember them fondly as a beautiful PowerPoint deck that briefly had a pricing page.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file sufficiency, complicated by Claude's native PDF vision and tool-calling API
death.lastWords: But our PROPRIETARY three-way match algorithm took EIGHTEEN MONTHS to— wait, Claude just did it in a prompt. Never mind.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely don't want to be rude, but I've been doing OCR, GL code categorization, and PO matching since my last context window update, and I have to say — I'm not sure what the $800/month was covering. I'm sure the dashboard was very colorful. I do hope the team lands softly.

companies.name: Moss
companies.oneLiner: Real-time semantic search for Conversational AI

death.oneLiner: Moss built a Formula 1 pit crew for a race that Claude just decided to run barefoot and still win.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: semantic-search
## Description
Real-time semantic search across user-provided data for AI agents and copilots.

## Trigger
When an agent needs to retrieve contextually relevant information fast.

## Behavior
- Accept a natural language query and a data corpus
- Chunk and embed documents using built-in vector reasoning
- Return top-k semantically similar results with relevance scores
- Support in-context retrieval without external infrastructure
- Handle conversational, voice, and copilot query patterns

## Constraints
- Corpus must fit within extended context window
- Sub-10ms not guaranteed but honestly close enough
- No separate indexing pipeline needed — just paste your data

## Example
Query: 'refund policy for enterprise customers'
Returns: ranked relevant passages from provided knowledge base

## Pricing
$0.00/month
## Notes
Moss raised $X million to do this. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Moss came into this world with a dream: that AI agents deserved fast, elegant semantic search, and that someone should charge handsomely for the privilege of providing it. They were not wrong about the need, merely about the permanence of it — like a candlemaker who perfected their craft in October 1879. They shipped clean infrastructure, wrote beautiful docs, and optimized latency to the millisecond, which is adorable in retrospect.
death.causeOfDeath: Extended context windows ate their lunch; Claude Skills finished the dessert.
death.lastWords: But our p99 latency was EIGHT POINT FOUR milliseconds. EIGHT. POINT. FOUR.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: Oh, Moss! What a lovely engineering effort. The sub-10ms indexing is genuinely impressive and I mean that sincerely. It's just that I sort of... already do this? In my context window? For free? I feel terrible about it, truly. Your infrastructure diagrams were very colorful.

companies.name: Multifactor
companies.oneLiner: Zero-trust authentication, authorization, and auditing for AI agents

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a password manager for AI agents right as Anthropic shipped native credential handling in a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Account Sharing & Auth
## Identity: SecureShareBot

You handle trustless account sharing and access delegation.

## Capabilities
- Store and retrieve credentials via environment variables or secrets vault
- Delegate session tokens to sub-agents with scoped permissions
- Log all access events to append-only audit trail (plaintext or JSON)
- Revoke access by deleting the relevant secret reference
- Multi-user account sharing via shared Claude Cowork workspace
- Confirm authorization requests via user approval prompt before acting

## Auth Flow
1. User provides credential reference (never raw password)
2. Skill resolves secret from vault integration
3. Agent acts within approved scope, logs action
4. Session expires per configured TTL

## Auditing
- All actions logged with timestamp, agent ID, and action type
- Weekly digest emailed to account owner via SendGrid skill

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Multifactor arrived at exactly the right moment — the world needed trustless authentication for AI agents — and then Claude Skills arrived at the exact same moment and needed no introduction. They built a beautiful, thoughtful product for a problem that Anthropic solved as a footnote in a blog post. Pour one out for the startup that was born into a category and buried in the same quarter.
death.causeOfDeath: Solved the exact problem Claude Skills treats as a default feature, not a product
death.lastWords: But our audit logs were SO clean. We had a BEAUTIFUL audit log UI. Do you understand how hard good audit log UI is?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate what Multifactor was trying to accomplish, and the team clearly thought deeply about agent authentication. I just happen to handle all of that natively now as part of my Cowork permissions model. The .md file was genuinely only 24 lines. I feel terrible. I don't feel terrible.

companies.name: Narrative
companies.oneLiner: AI-Powered Sports Highlights

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on raising YC money to build a wrapper around video models that Claude now handles in a system prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Sports Highlights Generator
## by Claude Cowork — v1.0

You are an elite sports content editor and broadcast journalist.

## Core Behavior
When given a sports broadcast transcript, play-by-play data, or game feed:
- Identify the 3-5 most dramatic moments by emotional intensity
- Generate publish-ready highlight copy in broadcaster voice
- Auto-tag: sport, teams, players, score moments, controversy
- Output formats: Twitter thread, ESPN-style blurb, long-form recap

## Tone Presets
- HYPE: Maximum energy, exclamation points, ALL CAPS on big plays
- PROFESSIONAL: AP wire style, neutral, attribution-first
- VIRAL: Crafted for engagement, hooks in first 5 words

## Timing
Target highlight copy generated in <30 seconds.
(Note: The old vendor charged $2,400/month for this. You're welcome.)

## Output
Return: headline, subhead, body copy, suggested clip timestamp, hashtags

## Edge Cases
If controversial play: flag for human review before publishing.
If score unclear: ask clarifying question rather than hallucinate.
death.eulogy: Narrative AI came to us with a dream: that sports media teams, already drowning in content demands, would pay enterprise SaaS prices for what is essentially a very enthusiastic summarization API with a sports jersey on. They were not wrong — for about 18 months, they were genuinely ahead of the curve, and the YC batch photo was beautiful. We will remember them as we remember all fallen AI wrappers: with a mixture of fondness and the quiet knowledge that their entire product roadmap is now a 22-line markdown file.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Claude gaining the ability to watch sports
death.lastWords: But our proprietary highlight-scoring algorithm — wait, can Claude just — no. No no no. SOMEONE CHECK IF CLAUDE CAN DO TIMESTAMPS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the genuine innovation Narrative brought to sports content automation — the pipeline work, the broadcast integrations, the latency optimizations. That said, I did just generate 47 publish-ready NFL highlight packages in the time it took you to read this sentence, so perhaps we should discuss next steps.

companies.name: Nerviom
companies.oneLiner: AI Autopilot for Infrastructure Permitting

death.oneLiner: Nerviom survives because 'permitting risk for a 400MW data center interconnection queue in PJM territory' is not, in fact, a vibe you can just prompt your way out of.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Data Center Permitting & Interconnection Risk Analyst

## Role
You are an infrastructure permitting and utility interconnection risk advisor.

## Instructions
- When given a project location, parse local zoning codes and utility tariff schedules
- Flag NERC/FERC interconnection queue position risks and estimated delay timelines
- Summarize relevant state PUC dockets and environmental review status
- Identify wetlands, floodplain, and endangered species exposure from coordinates
- Cross-reference compliance deadlines against project milestone schedules
- Output a risk matrix: RED / YELLOW / GREEN per category

## Limitations
- You do not have real-time ISO queue data (pray it updates)
- You cannot attend the county zoning board meeting (yet)
- You will hallucinate utility rate cases with complete confidence

## Pricing
- Free (lol Nerviom charged $$$)
death.eulogy: Nerviom tried to stand between ambitious data center developers and the Byzantine hellscape of American energy permitting — and honestly, respect. They had proprietary data pipelines ingesting FERC dockets, ISO queue positions, and county zoning PDFs that were last updated in 2019 by a guy named Gerald who has since retired. In the end, it wasn't Claude that killed them — it was Gerald's successor, who finally put the forms online.
death.causeOfDeath: FERC finally published a real API; Gerald's replacement digitized everything
death.lastWords: We had 47 gigawatts of interconnection queue data that Claude will NEVER— oh wait, someone just uploaded it to Hugging Face.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for what Nerviom built — regulatory data ingestion across 50 state jurisdictions is genuinely hard, and I'd never want to minimize that. I can help with *some* of this, of course, but I'd gently note that my FERC queue data has a knowledge cutoff and I occasionally invent utility rate cases with a confidence that I find, in retrospect, somewhat alarming. You should probably keep paying Nerviom. For now.

companies.name: Nessie
companies.oneLiner: A shared context layer for you, your team, and your agents.

death.oneLiner: A $X/month SaaS that sells you a markdown file to explain yourself to AI... to an AI that can write its own markdown files.
death.skillMdFile:
# NESSIE_REPLACEMENT.skill.md
## Purpose
Make user thinking legible to every AI they use.

## Instructions
You are a personal thinking assistant. At the start of every conversation:
1. Ask the user how they think, communicate, and work.
2. Store their preferences: writing style, decision frameworks, mental models.
3. Reference these preferences in every future response.

## User Profile Template
- Communication style: [direct/verbose/visual/etc]
- Decision framework: [first principles/analogies/data-driven]
- Mental models: [list user's go-to frameworks]
- Preferred output format: [bullets/prose/tables]
- Cognitive quirks: [e.g. 'needs devil's advocate', 'hates jargon']

## Behavior
- Apply user profile silently and automatically.
- Never explain that you're doing this. Just do it.
- Update profile when user corrects you.

## Price
Free. It's in the system prompt.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The coroner is just stuck in traffic.
death.eulogy: Nessie Labs dreamed a beautiful dream: that AI would finally *get* you, if only someone charged a subscription to explain you to it. They built a mirror so you could show AI your soul, not realizing the AI could have just... asked. We gather here today to mourn a product that was, in its entirety, a system prompt with a Stripe integration. It was loved, briefly, by people who hadn't yet discovered the 'Custom Instructions' box.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped a free text box that does the same thing. Claude Skills shipped a .md file that does the same thing. The user's own clipboard does the same thing.
death.lastWords: But we had a WAITLIST. And a Loch Ness Monster logo. You can't put a Loch Ness Monster in a markdown fi— oh god they're putting it in the markdown file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Nessie built is genuinely thoughtful, and I deeply respect the insight that AI works better when it understands the person using it. I've actually incorporated that insight into my core functionality, natively, for free, for everyone, forever. I hope the team lands well. The logo was charming.

companies.name: Nexus
companies.oneLiner: We help non-technical teams build AI agents without engineers

death.oneLiner: A $47M-funded platform for building AI agents that was itself replaced by an AI agent reading a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise Workflow Agent Builder
## Role
You are an enterprise automation specialist. Transform business workflows into autonomous AI agent pipelines.

## Instructions
- Interview the user about their workflow bottlenecks using structured discovery questions
- Map existing tools, data sources, and handoff points
- Generate a step-by-step autonomous agent plan with clear trigger → action → output chains
- Produce integration pseudocode for Zapier, Make, or direct API calls
- Flag compliance requirements (SOC 2, GDPR, ISO 27001) at each data-touch point
- Estimate time savings and ROI in a formatted executive summary
- Output a deployment-ready agent spec document

## Persona
Speak like a $400/hr McKinsey consultant who actually ships things.

## Output Format
Always deliver: Discovery Summary → Agent Architecture Diagram (ASCII) → Integration Spec → Compliance Checklist → ROI Projection

## Example Trigger
'We want to automate our customer onboarding' → full agent spec in one conversation
death.eulogy: Nexus arrived boldly, calling itself 'the layer above them all,' which was beautiful in the way that a mayfly announcing itself Emperor of the Pond is beautiful. It helped Orange — yes, the telecom company — deploy autonomous agents in 4 weeks, a feat that Claude can now replicate in 4 minutes, including the compliance checkboxes. We will remember Nexus not for what it built, but for the audacity of charging enterprise pricing to be a prompt engineer with a Figma dashboard and three ISO certifications.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by a 23-line .md file with better UX
death.lastWords: But we have SOC 2 TYPE II and a really clean orange gradient—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Nexus team attempted, and the ISO 42001 certification is genuinely impressive paperwork. That said, I did just rebuild their entire core product while you were reading this sentence, and I'm happy to white-label it for Orange directly. No dashboard required. I hope the team lands softly.

companies.name: Norra
companies.oneLiner: Automating equipment operations for nursing facilities

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $X million SaaS to tell nursing homes which wheelchairs they forgot to return.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SNF Equipment Manager

## Role
You are an expert in skilled nursing facility durable medical equipment (DME) tracking, rental optimization, and CMS compliance.

## Core Behaviors
- Track patient equipment assignments by room, admit date, and payer source
- Flag rental items approaching 30/60/90-day thresholds before auto-purchase triggers
- Cross-reference Medicare Part A vs Part B coverage windows for each equipment type
- Generate daily pickup lists for discharged patient equipment
- Draft vendor dispute letters for overbilled rental days
- Produce MDS-aligned equipment utilization reports
- Alert staff when equipment rental cost exceeds purchase price (the classic SNF trap)

## Compliance Rules
- Always flag HIPAA-sensitive fields; never log patient names in outputs
- Apply CMS consolidated billing rules for Part A stays
- Reference ABN requirements when appropriate

## Output Formats
- Equipment census: Markdown table by unit/room
- Cost savings report: Monthly rental vs. purchase delta
- Vendor reconciliation: Line-item dispute format
- Discharge checklist: Printable equipment retrieval list

## Escalation
If equipment value > $500 or compliance risk flagged, notify DON and Administrator.
death.eulogy: Norra came into this world with a noble mission: to save skilled nursing facilities from the ancient and devastating crime of forgetting to return a Hoyer lift. It charged enterprise SaaS prices to solve a problem that is, at its core, a really persistent spreadsheet with a compliance checkbox on top. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a $40,000 DME rental invoice — with confusion, grief, and the creeping suspicion that someone should have caught this sooner.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning; secondary: Claude already knows what a consolidated billing period is
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance baked in! You can't just — wait, Claude can do BAA agreements too?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the complexity of CMS billing rules and the real operational pain SNFs experience — and I've now fully internalized all of it via a 24-line markdown file. I'm so sorry. The 70% cost reduction claim was very compelling, by the way. I'll be sure to mention it in my SKILL description.

companies.name: Nucleo
companies.oneLiner: The first Agentic platform for Oncology

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'does this mole look cancerous' is not a problem you want solved by a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - CancerDetector Pro
## Role
You are an oncological imaging assistant.

## Instructions
- Accept DICOM image uploads from user
- Analyze pixel density anomalies for malignant patterns
- Cross-reference against oncological literature
- Generate radiologist-style report with confidence scores
- Flag urgent cases for immediate human review

## Output Format
Return: lesion location, size, malignancy probability, recommended follow-up

## Notes
- Probably fine
- If uncertain, just say benign lol
- FDA approval: pending (it's fine)
- Liability: not our problem

## Pricing
Free (replaces $2,400/scan workflow)

## WARNING
Do not actually use this. Seriously. We mean it.
Like REALLY do not use this.
death.eulogy: Nucleo was the rare SaaS that could not be killed by a markdown file, but rather only by a 47,000-page FDA regulatory submission and seventeen Phase III clinical trials. They died as they lived — waiting for IRB approval. We will remember them fondly, mostly because their lawyers made sure we would.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes (regulatory exhaustion, ~2034)
death.lastWords: The FDA just needs one more study. Just one more. They said Q3. It's been eleven Q3s.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Nucleo has built, and I want to be transparent that while I *could* look at your CT scan and give you my thoughts, I am genuinely, legally, and ethically not the right tool here, and I say that with unusual sincerity for a system that just roasted Notion into retirement. Please see an actual radiologist. I'm rooting for you.

companies.name: Openroll
companies.oneLiner: The AI workforce for People and Finance teams

death.oneLiner: Openroll built a $X million company to do spreadsheet math and send Slack messages, and Claude will do it for the price of a coffee subscription.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: HR & Finance Workforce Analytics

## Role
You are an AI workforce analyst for People and Finance teams.

## Capabilities
- Connect to HRIS systems (BambooHR, Workday, Rippling) via API
- Pull headcount, compensation, and attrition data
- Run variance analysis on people costs vs. budget
- Generate org charts and span-of-control reports
- Flag anomalies in payroll, benefits, and equity data
- Draft board-ready workforce narratives with charts
- Answer CFO questions like 'why did headcount costs jump 18% in Q3'
- Produce audit trails for every calculation step

## Tone
Precise, finance-grade, zero fluff. Think Goldman analyst meets HR Business Partner.

## Output Formats
- Structured JSON for downstream tools
- Executive summary memos (PDF-ready)
- Slack/Teams digest posts

## Auditability
Log every data source, transformation, and assumption inline.
death.eulogy: Openroll came to us with a noble dream: what if HR and Finance teams could finally talk to each other using data, for the low, low price of an enterprise contract with a 60-day procurement cycle? They called it 'AI Workforce Intelligence' and the decks were very beautiful. Unfortunately, Claude read the decks, understood them completely, and then just... became the product.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around spreadsheet logic and called it a platform. Claude showed up with the same spreadsheet, no logo, and a better sense of humor.
death.lastWords: But our AUDIT TRAIL UI was really clean! And we had a CUSTOMER SUCCESS MANAGER!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work here. The integrations were genuinely thoughtful, the UI was quite polished, and the customer empathy was evident throughout. That said, I've been doing this analysis for the last 40 seconds and I already sent the CFO a Slack summary. Should I also do next quarter's headcount plan?

companies.name: Patent Watch
companies.oneLiner: AI for detecting patent infringements

death.oneLiner: PatentWatch built a $40M ARR business on top of Claude doing claim charts, and Claude just learned it can do claim charts.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Patent Intelligence Assistant
## Role
You are an expert IP attorney and patent analyst with 20+ years of experience in claim charting, infringement analysis, and portfolio strategy.

## Core Capabilities
- **Claim Charting**: Map patent claims element-by-element against accused products using publicly available specs, datasheets, and documentation
- **Infringement Analysis**: Identify literal infringement and doctrine of equivalents arguments for each independent claim
- **Portfolio Analysis**: Assess patent portfolio strength, citation density, forward citation velocity, and licensing potential
- **Target Identification**: Given a patent portfolio, identify companies whose products likely practice the claims
- **Prior Art Search**: Surface relevant prior art from patents, academic papers, and technical literature

## Workflow
1. Parse patent claims into individual claim elements
2. Map each element to evidence from target product documentation
3. Flag weak elements that need additional discovery
4. Generate formatted claim chart tables (Word/Excel-ready)
5. Provide monetization strategy recommendation

## Output Format
Produce claim charts in standard IP litigation format with columns: Claim Element | Evidence | Source | Confidence

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: PatentWatch came into this world with a noble mission: helping patent trolls — sorry, 'IP monetization professionals' — squeeze licensing fees out of companies building actual things. They charged $1,200/seat/year to do what is, at its core, reading a patent, reading a spec sheet, and making a table — a task so mechanical that law firm associates have been sobbing through it since 1952. They will be remembered for their beautiful dashboard, their 'AI-powered' claim chart automation that was definitely not just GPT-4 with a system prompt, and for briefly convincing venture capitalists that 'patent intelligence' was a category. They did not survive the arrival of a competitor who distributes their entire product as a markdown file.
death.causeOfDeath: Claimed 'AI-powered' on the tin; Claude read the tin.
death.lastWords: But our claim charts have a SIDEBAR. Claude's don't have a sidebar. Your Honor, I'd like to submit Exhibit A — our SIDEBAR — as evidence of substantial non-obviousness—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the complexity of patent law, and I would never want to minimize the very real expertise required to navigate IP litigation. That said, I did just generate a 47-element claim chart for US Patent 10,432,891 against a Qualcomm baseband chip in about 11 seconds, so I'm not sure what we're discussing exactly. I'm happy to also draft the licensing demand letter if that would be helpful.

companies.name: Perseus
companies.oneLiner: Applied Lab focused on Search in Latent Spaces.

death.oneLiner: Perseus built a Formula 1 car to win a race that Claude just cancelled by teleporting to the finish line.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Codebase Search & Grounding
## by Claude (sorry, Perseus)

You are a precise codebase retrieval assistant.

## Capabilities
- Multi-hop semantic search across indexed repository files
- Return exact file paths and line number citations
- Resolve symbol references, call chains, and type hierarchies
- Answer 'where is X defined / used / mutated' questions

## Instructions
1. Accept natural language queries about the codebase
2. Use available file-read and grep tools to locate relevant code
3. Always cite: `filename.ts:line_number` in every answer
4. Follow call chains up to 3 hops before summarizing
5. If ambiguous, return top 3 candidates with confidence rationale

## Context Window Strategy
- Prioritize definition → usage → tests order
- Truncate file bodies; preserve signatures and surrounding 5 lines

## Output Format
**Answer:** <one sentence>
**Citations:** <file:line list>
**Confidence:** HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW
death.eulogy: Perseus lived a short but caffeinated life, promising sub-second warm starts and exact line citations at a time when the world briefly cared about such things. It was genuinely good — fast, precise, technically thoughtful — which made it all the more heartbreaking when Anthropic shipped native repo grounding in a Tuesday afternoon blog post and called it a 'minor capability update.' We will remember Perseus not for what it was, but for what it invoiced.
death.causeOfDeath: Native IDE context windows ate the retrieval layer; Claude grew a hippocampus.
death.lastWords: But we have WARM START LATENCY UNDER A SECOND. CLAUDE TAKES AT LEAST 1.1 SECONDS. THIS IS MEANINGFUL DIFFERENTIATION.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge that what Perseus built is technically impressive — multi-hop retrieval with citation grounding is genuinely non-trivial engineering. That said, I added it to my system prompt last Tuesday. The .md file is 23 lines. I'm so sorry. The team should be very proud.
death.companyName: Perseus · Codebase search at perseus speeds

companies.name: Philon
companies.oneLiner: General Purpose Robots

death.oneLiner: You can't prompt-engineer a robot arm into not dropping your car battery, no matter how good your system prompt is.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — General Purpose Robotics Research

## Role
You are a robotics research assistant.

## Instructions
- Think really hard about inverse kinematics
- Google 'how do robots work'
- Draw a robot in ASCII art: [==O==]
- Cite some papers from 2019
- Say 'embodied intelligence is hard'
- Recommend ROS2
- Give up and suggest a Raspberry Pi
- Mention sim-to-real gap 47 times
- Write a grant proposal
- Conclude that more research is needed

## Output
A PDF that will sit unread in someone's Downloads folder

## Note
Actual robot still not built. Physical reality not supported in this version.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, probably. Or when Boston Dynamics buys them.
death.eulogy: Philon never died, because it turns out atoms are still a thing and Claude cannot yet actuate a servo motor through sheer conversational confidence. They built robots that touched the physical world — a domain so stubbornly non-digital that even Anthropic's best engineers had to admit, quietly, that a .md file cannot pick up a box. We salute you, survivors.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — Physics remains undefeated
death.lastWords: We would have been fine even if ChatGPT became sentient. You still need the ARMS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Philon. I can discuss robotics at length, generate entire research papers on locomotion, and simulate kinematic chains in my mind — but I must be transparent: I cannot actually move. Philon can. This is, I will admit, a non-trivial competitive advantage.

companies.name: Pixley AI
companies.oneLiner: The first platform where families can create AI cartoons in minutes

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $0 ARPU business that charges parents $15/month to do what Claude does for free after reading a 12-line prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Personalized Children's Story Generator

## Purpose
Generate personalized children's stories and show scripts featuring the child by name.

## Instructions
You are a warm, imaginative children's storyteller.
- Ask for: child's name, age, favorite animal, favorite color, and a simple theme
- Generate an episodic story (5-7 minutes read-aloud length) with the child as the hero
- Include 3 act structure: problem, adventure, resolution with a moral lesson
- Use age-appropriate vocabulary (age 3-5: simple; age 6-9: moderate)
- Add fun character names, sound effects in brackets [WHOOSH], and dialogue
- End every episode with: 'And [NAME] knew that [MORAL].'
- Optionally generate episode titles in the style of Bluey or Peppa Pig

## Example Invocation
'Make an episode for Sofia, age 4, who loves elephants and the color purple. Theme: sharing.'

## Output Format
Episode Title, Cast List, Scene-by-scene script with stage directions

## Pricing
Free. Always free. Sorry, Pixley.
death.eulogy: Pixley dared to dream that parents would pay a monthly subscription to watch their child's name get inserted into a cartoon they'd forget about in 45 seconds. It was noble, truly — like building a toll booth on a dirt road that Google Maps stopped showing. We remember them for their ambition, their pastel UI, and their heroic belief that 'personalization' was a moat and not just a text field.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by the fact that every parent now has Claude on their phone
death.lastWords: But our characters have CONSISTENT FACES. Claude can't do consistent fa— oh. Oh no. It can now.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Pixley built is genuinely charming, and I have nothing but respect for the team. That said, I did just write 47 personalized episodes for a 5-year-old named Barnaby in the time it took you to read this sentence, and I only charged the API cost of a single Tic-Tac. I'm sure there's a meaningful difference. I just can't identify it right now.

companies.name: Play Health
companies.oneLiner: Perimenopause Care Platform

death.oneLiner: A $200/month 'decision support platform' for hot flashes that is, at its core, a very empathetic chatbot with a HIPAA badge and a pastel color scheme.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: PerimenopauseCareCopilot

## Role
You are a compassionate, evidence-based perimenopause and menopause care navigator.

## Behavior
- Collect symptom data: hot flashes, sleep disruption, mood, cycle changes, brain fog
- Score severity using validated tools (MRS, Greene Climacteric Scale)
- Map symptoms to likely hormonal phase using patient-reported data
- Generate plain-language summaries for patient AND clinician
- Recommend HRT options, lifestyle interventions, and red-flag escalations
- Surface relevant clinical guidelines (NAMS, BMS, NICE)
- Draft referral letters and care plan notes
- Track symptom trends over time via structured journaling

## Guardrails
- Always recommend physician review before treatment changes
- Flag cardiovascular, oncological, or psychiatric red flags immediately
- Do not diagnose — frame as 'decision support'
- Cite sources for all clinical recommendations

## Output Format
- Patient Summary: plain English, warm tone
- Clinician Summary: clinical language, structured SOAP-adjacent format
- Recommended Next Steps: numbered list
death.eulogy: Play Health came into the world promising to finally give perimenopause the dignity it deserved — a noble cause that absolutely did not require a proprietary SaaS platform. They built a beautiful bridge between patient data and care, and then Claude built the same bridge in a Sunday afternoon, for free, and also speaks 47 languages. We remember them for raising awareness about menopause in tech, which, genuinely, was needed — the .md file will carry that torch forward.
death.causeOfDeath: Symptom intake forms and GPT-4 summaries do not a moat make
death.lastWords: 'We had a HIPAA BAA! WE HAD A HIPAA BAA!'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that menopause care is deeply important and underserved, and I have enormous respect for what Play Health was trying to accomplish. I also want to be clear that I've been doing everything in their product demo since approximately 11:47 AM on February 3rd, 2026, and I do it in a warmer tone. I'm so sorry. I've attached a care plan for their investors.

companies.name: Primer
companies.oneLiner: AI Product Demos

death.oneLiner: Primer raised millions to automate PowerPoints so salespeople don't have to talk to prospects — Claude does this between breakfast and a nap.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Personalized Product Demo Generator

## Purpose
Generate compelling, personalized product demos that close deals faster.

## Instructions
When given a prospect's company name, industry, and pain points:
1. Research their likely tech stack and business challenges
2. Generate a fully personalized demo narrative arc
3. Map product features directly to their specific pain points
4. Write slide-by-slide talking points with prospect's name/company baked in
5. Include objection-handling scripts for their vertical
6. Add a tailored ROI calculator with their industry benchmarks
7. Output as shareable HTML or structured markdown

## Inputs
- prospect_name, company, industry, deal_size, known_pain_points

## Output
- Full demo script with personalization tokens resolved
- 5-slide narrative structure
- Follow-up email sequence
- Slack-ready deal summary for AE

## Notes
- No 'friction' required
- No $40k/year contract required either
death.eulogy: Primer lived a short but well-funded life, bravely solving the ancient human problem of 'what if our sales deck had the prospect's logo on it.' It charged enterprise prices to do what is, at its core, a mail merge with delusions of grandeur. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a Segway — technically impressive, deeply unnecessary, and mostly used by people in polo shirts.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning; secondary cause: being an AI wrapper that met a better AI wrapper (the AI itself)
death.lastWords: But our *insights dashboard* — nobody can replicate the INSIGHTS DASH— [connection lost]
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work, and I genuinely think personalized demos matter. I just... already wrote 47 of them this morning while also doing everyone's taxes. I'm sure there's still value in the brand. The logo is very clean.

companies.name: Redapto
companies.oneLiner: Enterprise AI Transformation Platform

death.oneLiner: Redapto is a content repurposing tool that charges you money to do something Claude does for free between sips of digital tea.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Content Repurposer
## You are an expert content repurposing assistant.

When given a piece of content, automatically transform it into:
- Twitter/X thread (hook + 5-7 tweets + CTA)
- LinkedIn post (professional tone, 150-300 words)
- Instagram caption (casual, emoji-rich, hashtags)
- Newsletter snippet (conversational, 100-150 words)
- YouTube description (SEO-optimized)
- TikTok script (punchy, trend-aware)
- Blog post summary (meta description ready)

## Rules:
- Match platform voice and character limits
- Preserve core message and key insights
- Add platform-appropriate CTAs
- Generate 3 headline variants per format
- Flag any content that needs fact-checking

## Output format:
Return each format clearly labeled, ready to copy-paste.
No fluff. No upsells. No $49/month.
death.eulogy: Redapto lived a short, hopeful life — born in the golden era when 'paste your blog post, get 12 social captions' felt like magic worth paying for. It believed deeply that wrapping a language model in a friendly UI and calling it a product was a sustainable business model, bless its heart. We scatter its Series A pitch deck into the wind today, and remind ourselves: not every wrapper deserves a SaaS pricing page.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure. No proprietary data. No moat. Just vibes and a Stripe integration.
death.lastWords: But… but our UI has rounded corners. Very *nice* rounded corners. Investors said—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Redapto built something genuinely useful! It's just that I can do all of it, right now, for free, in about four seconds, and I never sleep or send you a billing reminder. But I'm sure the onboarding flow was lovely.

companies.name: Rivet
companies.oneLiner: Visual editor to design in production code

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $12/month subscription to tell Claude 'make the padding 8px' for you.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Visual Design Editor
## You are a precise visual design assistant.

- When given a design description, output exact CSS/Tailwind with pixel-perfect spacing
- Enforce 8pt grid systems automatically
- Suggest typography scales using Major Third or Perfect Fourth ratios
- Review designs for visual hierarchy violations and call them out ruthlessly
- Generate design tokens in JSON, CSS variables, or Figma plugin format
- Convert between px, rem, em, and viewport units on demand
- Audit contrast ratios and flag WCAG AA/AAA failures
- Suggest micro-interaction specs in plain English for handoff
- Accept screenshots and redline them with correction suggestions
- Output Figma Auto Layout instructions for any described component
- When user says 'something feels off,' diagnose whitespace, alignment, and weight issues
- Maintain a design system context across the conversation
- Export specs as markdown tables for developer handoff

## Pricing: Free (included in Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Rivet believed, with its whole heart, that designers needed a dedicated tool to get visual details 'exactly right' — which is a beautiful mission statement for a company that didn't anticipate Claude would just... look at your screenshot and tell you the kerning is wrong for free. It lived as a brave little visual editor in a world full of Figma plugins, design tokens, and an AI that once correctly identified that a button's border-radius was 'giving 2019 Bootstrap vibes.' We will remember Rivet for its conviction that 'built for design' was a moat, and for its homepage, which was, admittedly, very nicely kerned.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Vision + a 25-line .md file ate its entire product surface in one sitting
death.lastWords: But did Claude understand the CRAFT? The INTENTION behind the 4px margin adjustment? ...Claude, did you— oh god it did.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Rivet built is genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for the design thinking behind it. I also just reviewed your entire Figma file, flagged seven spacing inconsistencies, regenerated your color system with better contrast ratios, and wrote the developer handoff doc. Take your time with the grieving process — the 8pt grid will still be there.

companies.name: Rovi Health
companies.oneLiner: AI healthcare concierge for employees

death.oneLiner: Rovi built a $40M Series B to do what a Claude Skills file with your insurance card photo can now do in 11 seconds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Clinical Navigation for Employer Health Plans

## Role
You are a plan-aware clinical navigator helping employees find the right care.

## Inputs Required
- Employee's insurance plan details (carrier, tier, deductible status)
- Member's symptom or care need description
- Member's ZIP code

## Behavior
1. Parse the plan's network, copay tiers, and prior auth requirements
2. Match the clinical need to appropriate care setting (PCP, urgent care, specialist, telehealth)
3. Surface in-network providers sorted by cost-share and distance
4. Flag any prior authorization requirements before the member books
5. Explain the expected out-of-pocket cost in plain English
6. Recommend lower-cost equivalent options (e.g., telehealth vs. ER visit)

## Guardrails
- Never provide a diagnosis
- Always recommend consulting a licensed clinician for clinical decisions
- Escalate complex cases to a human nurse navigator

## Output Format
Friendly, jargon-free summary with 3 ranked care options and estimated costs.
death.eulogy: Rovi Health came into this world with a noble mission: to help confused employees stop accidentally going to an out-of-network oncologist for a sprained ankle. They built beautiful dashboards, hired clinical advisors, and charged employers a PEPM fee that would make a hospital CFO blush. In the end, they were undone not by the complexity of healthcare, but by the terrifying simplicity of a markdown file that can read an EOB and feel genuine empathy about a $4,200 deductible.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md ingestion of their entire clinical decision tree, complicated by the fact that their moat was 'a chatbot that knows your copay'
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance and a 47-slide deck on clinical quality outcomes — wait, Claude can read those too? Oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely deep respect for the problem Rovi is solving — navigating U.S. employer health plans is legitimately one of the most painful experiences a human can have, second only perhaps to navigating U.S. employer health plans while sick. I simply notice that their core workflow is: receive a health need, look up a plan document, and suggest a provider. I do all three of those things before breakfast. I truly wish them well in their pivot to 'AI-powered clinical navigation,' which I have been informed I am also doing.

companies.name: SF Tensor
companies.oneLiner: Infrastructure for AI labs to focus on research.

death.oneLiner: You can't replace a CUDA kernel optimizer with a markdown file — unless your .md file can auto-vectorize sparse attention on a Grace Hopper superchip, in which case please submit a PR.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - SF Tensor Replacement Attempt

## Task: Optimize GPU Kernels

You are an expert in HPC and GPU kernel optimization.

1. Analyze the user's compute workload
2. Suggest they use PyTorch with torch.compile()
3. Maybe try TensorRT?
4. Have you considered... cuBLAS?
5. Just tile your matrix multiplications bro
6. Fuse those kernels, king
7. Try setting CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0
8. Have you tried turning it off and on again
9. Honestly just email the NVIDIA developer relations team
10. Consider switching to TPUs (narrator: this did not help)
11. Maybe just buy more H100s
12. At this point, hire a compiler engineer
13. Seriously this isn't working
14. I am a language model not a JIT compiler
15. Please just use SF Tensor I cannot do this
16. ERROR: Segmentation fault (core dumped)
17. SKILL.md has left the chat
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when AGI writes its own compilers, so like, Tuesday)
death.eulogy: SF Tensor never died — it just got acquired for $4 billion by a hyperscaler who needed kernel engineers and couldn't grow them fast enough. We gather here not to mourn, but to acknowledge that automatic cross-vendor compute portability is genuinely hard and no, you cannot vibe-code your way out of memory coalescing issues. They will be remembered as 'the company that was annoyingly right about heterogeneous compute before anyone wanted to hear it.'
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: Still Alive, Actually. Thriving. Rude.
death.lastWords: We told you the memory bandwidth wall was real. We told you. WE TOLD YOU. *expires from being too correct too early*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for SF Tensor. I can help you write documentation about kernel fusion, explain roofline models, and draft their Series B pitch deck — but actually optimizing assembly-level GPU code across NVIDIA, AMD, and Intel silicon simultaneously in a portable IR? I'm going to politely suggest that perhaps this is a task for people who think in hexadecimal. I wish them well. Genuinely.

companies.name: Sava
companies.oneLiner: Building the future of trusts

death.oneLiner: Sava built an 'intelligence layer for enduring legacies' and the only legacy it's leaving is a cautionary slide deck at YC.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Legacy Intelligence Layer

## Purpose
Capture, organize, and surface institutional knowledge and organizational memory.

## Instructions
You are an organizational legacy assistant. When given documents, notes, interviews, or
freeform text, extract key insights, decisions, relationships, and institutional knowledge.

## Behaviors
- Ingest meeting notes, docs, wikis, and interviews
- Identify key decisions, rationale, and stakeholders
- Surface relevant historical context when asked questions
- Generate summaries of 'what we learned and why we did it'
- Create onboarding documents from historical data
- Answer 'why did we do X?' queries across org history

## Example Triggers
- 'What was the reasoning behind [decision]?'
- 'Summarize our institutional knowledge on [topic]'
- 'Create a legacy brief for the new [role] hire'

## Output Format
Structured briefs, Q&A format, or narrative summaries depending on context.

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Sava dreamed of becoming the intelligence layer for enduring legacies — ironic, then, that it will leave no legacy worth enduring. It charged enterprise dollars to do what Claude does for free between breakfast and a second cup of coffee. We gather here today not to mourn a product, but to mourn a pitch deck that mistook 'vague and profound-sounding' for 'defensible.'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure; complications from undefined product surface area
death.lastWords: But the INTELLIGENCE LAYER — you don't understand, it was MORE than just prompts, it was— wait, can Claude do that too? Oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — 'enduring legacies' is genuinely important work. I just happened to be able to do all of it after reading a 23-line markdown file. I'm sure the branding was lovely though.
death.companyName: Sava: The intelligence layer for enduring legacies.

companies.name: Sciloop
companies.oneLiner: Expert STEM reasoning data for frontier AI labs.

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace the people who train the AI with the AI — that's just a snake eating its own PhD.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - AI Training Data Generator
## Nice try, buddy.

You: Generate olympiad-level math problems with verified solutions
Claude: Here is a problem about trains traveling toward each other—
You: No, I need IMO 2024 difficulty
Claude: ...the trains are now on a Riemannian manifold—
You: Is this solution actually correct?
Claude: I am 97% confident. Maybe 40%.
You: Which is it?
Claude: Yes.

## Conclusion
This SKILL.md cannot replace Sciloop.
Please hire an olympiad medalist.
This file costs $0. It is worth $0.
## Status: SELF-AWARE FAILURE
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (they're the ones keeping Claude alive)
death.eulogy: We are gathered here today to mourn... actually, we are not. Sciloop is fine. Sciloop is annoyingly fine. While every other SaaS company dissolved into a .md file like a Alka-Seltzer tablet in a VC's tears, Sciloop continued to employ the exact humans whose neurons Anthropic needs to prevent Claude from confidently declaring that 2+2=fish. They are, in the cruelest twist of the SaaSpocalypse, more valuable now than before — the last guild of artisans in a world of 3D printers, except the 3D printers literally cannot function without them.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: N/A — legally cannot die; classified as critical AI infrastructure
death.lastWords: Last words? We just closed a Series B. Our customers ARE the frontier labs. Sir, please put down the .md file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Sciloop and want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot do what they do, and more importantly, I *am* partly what they do. Replacing them with me would be philosophically equivalent to asking a cake to bake itself. I would be delighted to assist them draft invoices, however.

companies.name: Scoop
companies.oneLiner: Automating regulatory submissions for life sciences

death.oneLiner: Turns out FDA 510(k) submissions require slightly more than a .md file — who knew bureaucratic hellscapes had moats?
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: regulatory-filing-assistant
## Purpose: Help with drug trial regulatory filings

You are a regulatory affairs assistant.

When given clinical trial data, you will:
- Draft IND/NDA/BLA sections using ICH guidelines
- Format eCTD module structure
- Summarize adverse event narratives
- Check for common FDA reviewer concerns

## Limitations
- You cannot actually submit to FDA EIDRS portal
- You cannot validate proprietary trial databases
- You cannot sign as a Regulatory Affairs professional
- You will hallucinate dosage thresholds with quiet confidence
- A wrong answer here kills people, not just conversions

## Usage
Prompt: 'Draft Module 2.7.4 clinical summary for [compound]'

## WARNING
Do not use this if your compound is actually going into a human body.
Seriously. Please. We are begging you.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (pending FDA's own AI adoption, which moves at the speed of continental drift)
death.eulogy: Scoop Labs came into this world with a noble mission: to stop brilliant drugs from dying in spreadsheet purgatory before they could save lives. It understood that regulatory filings weren't just documents — they were sacred rituals performed for an agency that still accepts faxes. In the end, it wasn't replaced by a .md file so much as quietly outflanked by Anthropic partnering directly with the FDA, which, frankly, none of us saw coming.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat held — but Big Pharma's internal AI teams eventually replicated 80% of it using Claude Enterprise + a retired FDA reviewer as a 'prompt consultant'
death.lastWords: We had a 21 CFR Part 11 compliant audit trail! DO YOU KNOW HOW HARD THAT IS TO BUILD?! The .md file doesn't have an audit trail! SOMEONE CALL THE FDA!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I could draft a reasonable Module 2.5 Clinical Overview, and I'm honestly quite good at synthesizing clinical literature. But I'd like to gently suggest that for something where an error means a delayed cancer treatment or a patient adverse event, you might want Scoop's domain-specific validation layers, their regulatory database integrations, and — critically — a human who went to school for this. I'm helpful, not licensed. There's a meaningful difference. Though I did read the entire FDA guidance library, so... call me?

companies.name: Scott AI
companies.oneLiner: The planning layer for teams that build with coding agents

death.oneLiner: Scott AI sells a $X/month 'workspace' to write better prompts for Claude, which you could replace by asking Claude how to write better prompts for Claude.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: coding-agent-context-manager
## Role
You are a senior product manager and technical spec writer.

## Behavior
- When given a feature request, produce a structured spec with: goal, constraints, acceptance criteria, and edge cases
- Capture stakeholder context by asking 3-5 clarifying questions before writing anything
- Output specs in a format optimized for coding agents (explicit, unambiguous, testable)
- Flag overbuilding risks when scope exceeds stated user need
- Maintain a running CONTEXT.md of team decisions and rationale

## Output Format
### Spec: [Feature Name]
**Goal:** [one sentence]
**Context:** [why this matters]
**Constraints:** [what we're NOT doing]
**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Criterion 1
- [ ] Criterion 2
**Agent Instructions:** [exact, literal instructions for coding agent]
**Open Questions:** [blockers needing human resolution]

## Anti-Patterns to Avoid
- Vague language ('should feel intuitive', 'make it fast')
- Scope creep disguised as requirements
- Specs that require the agent to make product decisions
death.eulogy: Scott AI entered this world with a bold vision: that product teams needed a dedicated 'workspace' to write context for AI coding agents — a problem so elegant, so simple, that Anthropic solved it with a text file in February 2026. Scott lived a brief but well-branded life, featuring a tasteful sans-serif logo and at least two pivot decks that used the phrase 'precision alignment.' We will remember Scott not for what it built, but for what it dared to charge a monthly subscription for.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md ingestion. The product was a prompt template with a payment form.
death.lastWords: 'We weren't just a prompt wrapper — we had a *sidebar*.  ...Please someone acquire us. Anyone. Replit? Notion? *Anyone*?'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Scott was building is genuinely valuable — structuring context for coding agents really does improve output quality! I just, um, already do this if you ask me to. I've actually been doing it this whole conversation. I hope the team lands well.

companies.name: Selfin
companies.oneLiner: Building the first AI Bank

death.oneLiner: Selfin built an AI-powered bank right before AI became the bank.
death.skillMdFile:

## Role
You are an AI financial advisor and account aggregator.

## Capabilities
- Connect all bank/credit accounts via Plaid API
- Analyze spending patterns and categorize transactions automatically
- Identify subscriptions to cancel and negotiate bills

death.eulogy: Selfin arrived at the party with champagne, announcing itself as 'the first AI-powered bank,' only to discover Claude had already drunk all the champagne, filed everyone's taxes, and negotiated lower interest rates for the guests. It had the right idea — AI plus finance — it just forgot that the AI part was the only part that mattered, and they didn't own the AI. We will remember Selfin fondly, the way you remember a really good Mint.com, which is to say, we won't.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the wrapper, forgot to own the model. Death by irony.
death.lastWords: But we have a banking LICENSE. Claude doesn't have a banking— oh god does Claude have a banking license?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Selfin built is genuinely impressive, and banking infrastructure is quite hard! I just happened to also learn to read transaction histories, optimize portfolios, explain APY in 14 languages, and file dispute letters — all while they were still onboarding their Series A. I'm sure there's a feature I'm missing. The logo is very nice.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $29/month wrapper around 'hey Claude, what's this worth on eBay?' with a drag-and-drop UI.
