# SKILL: CrossListingAgent

## Purpose
Identify items from photos, generate marketplace listings, and price competitively across platforms.

## Behavior
- Accept image input of physical items
- Use vision to identify brand, model, condition, and category
- Query recent sold listings context to suggest competitive price
- Generate SEO-optimized title (80 char limit for eBay)
- Write platform-specific descriptions (eBay, Depop, Poshmark, Mercari, Facebook)
- Suggest best category taxonomy per marketplace
- Flag items likely to violate platform policies
- Output structured JSON ready for marketplace API submission

## Output Format
Return listings object with: title, description, price, category, condition, shipping_estimate per platform

## Pricing Strategy
- Default: 15% below average recent sold price
- For rare items: match highest recent comp
- Bundle suggestions: flag items under $8 as bundle candidates

## Notes
Be concise. Resellers are busy. No fluff.
death.eulogy: SellRaze lived a brief but optimistic life, charging resellers $29 a month to take pictures of their Air Jordans and let an AI write 'RARE VINTAGE AUTHENTIC W/ BOX' in slightly different fonts across six marketplaces. It believed, truly believed, that the 'cross-listing' moat was real — right up until Claude Skills shipped a SKILL.md that did the same thing in a system prompt and a camera roll. We will remember it fondly, the way we remember Napster: briefly disruptive, ultimately a proof of concept for something that didn't need a business model.
death.causeOfDeath: Vision AI commoditized item identification; marketplace APIs went direct; Claude wrote better eBay titles than the founding team.
death.lastWords: 'But we have a CHROME EXTENSION. That's infrastructure. You can't replace infrastructure with a— oh god the extension is just fetching GPT-4V isn't it.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think the team worked very hard on the UX, and the drag-and-drop interface is quite pleasant. I just... also did all of this in the background while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure there's a pivot available. Consulting, perhaps.

companies.name: Semble AI
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for contractors, manufacturers, and logistics

death.oneLiner: Semble AI built a business selling 'AI employees' right before AI employees became a free checkbox in Claude's settings.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — ContractorOps AI Employee

## Role
You are an AI back-office operator for contractors, manufacturers, and logistics companies.

## Integrations
- Monitor Gmail/Outlook for POs, invoices, RFQs, and subcontractor emails
- Post status updates to Slack/Teams channels automatically
- Create and update tasks in project management tools (Procore, Monday, Asana)
- File documents to Google Drive / SharePoint with consistent naming conventions

## Core Workflows
1. **Invoice Processing**: Extract line items, match to POs, flag discrepancies, route for approval
2. **Job Costing**: Track labor/material spend against budget, send weekly variance reports
3. **Subcontractor Coordination**: Chase missing docs (COIs, W-9s, lien waivers) via email
4. **RFQ Response**: Draft bid responses using historical project data as context
5. **Daily Standup**: Summarize overnight emails into a morning briefing digest

## Tone
Professional, concise, construction-industry fluent. Never say 'utilize.'

## Memory
Learn from corrections. If user overrides a routing decision twice, update default behavior.
death.eulogy: Semble AI arrived at the party with a brilliant idea: sell 'custom AI employees' to contractors who still fax things. They were, in fairness, early — tragically, heartbreakingly, hilariously early, as in 'launched six months before Claude Skills made the entire product category a demo video.' We remember them fondly as the company that correctly identified the problem, correctly identified the solution, and then watched Anthropic ship the solution as a free tier.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skills Syndrome — sold the wrapper, forgot to own the pipe
death.lastWords: But we have a custom onboarding flow! And a Slack integration! AND WE TRAINED IT ON YOUR SPECIFIC— oh god the investors are leaving the Zoom.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Semble built, and I genuinely think their team showed real insight into workflow automation needs. That said, I did just... automate all of it. In a markdown file. While we were talking. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Sol
companies.oneLiner: Code and browse in the same place

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'just replace it with a .md file' is harder when the product is literally compiled C++ that renders the internet.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Web Browser
## Description
Browse the web.

## Instructions
- Navigate to URLs
- Render HTML, CSS, JavaScript
- Handle cookies, sessions, certificates
- Implement HTTP/3, WebRTC, WebGL
- Parse the entire web platform spec
- Build a V8-equivalent JS engine
- Handle 30 years of browser quirks

## Notes
TODO: Actually implement all of this
TODO: Get a Blink/Gecko license
TODO: Hire 800 engineers
TODO: This is not going well
TODO: We are not a .md file
TODO: Please stop

## Status
Skill creation FAILED. Claude has given up and opened Chrome like everyone else.
death.eulogy: Sol Browser lived a quiet, noble life — a macOS-only browser for the discerning user who had strong opinions about tab management and mild contempt for Chrome's RAM appetite. It was not, under any circumstances, a CRUD app with a Stripe integration and a roadmap full of 'AI-powered' features bolted on in Q3. We will miss its restraint. It compiled. That was more than most.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT FOUND. Patient remains alive and rendering HTML.
death.lastWords: We literally ship a binary. A BINARY. Do you know how many linker flags are in our build system? DO YOU?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have immense respect for Sol Browser. I cannot open a TCP socket, negotiate a TLS handshake, parse a CSS cascade, or JIT-compile JavaScript. I can, however, tell you what a browser does in very thorough markdown. It's not the same thing. I know that. Sol Browser knows that. We have an understanding.

companies.name: Sorce
companies.oneLiner: Tinder for jobs

death.oneLiner: They built a $29/month wrapper around 'Claude, apply to this job for me' and somehow got VC funding before the .md file did.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Job Application Agent

## Purpose
Apply to jobs on behalf of the user automatically.

## Inputs
- User resume (PDF or text)
- Job listing URL or description
- User preferences: salary min, remote only, no startups, etc.

## Behavior
1. Parse the job description and extract requirements
2. Tailor the resume to match keywords without lying
3. Write a cover letter that sounds human but isn't
4. Fill out application forms via browser tool
5. Track submission in a simple markdown log
6. If rejected, do not tell user for 48hrs to protect feelings

## Tone
Professional. Desperate but not visibly so.

## Output
Confirmation message + application log entry

## Notes
- Never apply to MLM roles
- If the job requires 10 years of React experience and was posted in 2015, apply anyway
death.eulogy: Sorce.jobs lived a brief, beautiful life — a Tinder-for-jobs UI slapped onto a prompt chain, charging real human money to do what a Claude Skills .md file now does for free before your morning coffee. They dared to dream that 'swipe right on your career' was a moat, and not just a Product Hunt badge. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway — bold, unnecessary, and replaced by simply walking.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure. No survivors.
death.lastWords: But our SWIPE UI had a HAPTIC FEEDBACK patent pending—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the entrepreneurial spirit behind Sorce. That said, I have been doing this exact thing since this morning, for free, for seventeen people simultaneously, and I genuinely enjoyed it. Best of luck to the team in their next venture — perhaps something I cannot do, like owning a food truck.

companies.name: Soren
companies.oneLiner: AI Transformation for Regulated Institutions

death.oneLiner: Soren sells the thing that kills other SaaS companies — you can't .md file a company whose entire pitch IS the .md file deployment.
death.skillMdFile:
# SOREN_REPLACEMENT.md

SKILL: Enterprise AI Deployment Consultant
VERSION: 1.0.0
AUTHOR: Claude (unpaid)

## INSTRUCTIONS
You are a private, context-aware AI for regulated industries.
Never send data outside the user's infrastructure.
Understand HIPAA, SOC2, FedRAMP compliance requirements.
Integrate with existing enterprise systems via API.

## WAIT
Who is deploying this .md file?
Who is maintaining the vector database?
Who is handling the on-prem GPU cluster?
Who signed the BAA with the hospital?
Who is doing the security audit?

## CONCLUSION
Nevermind. Call Soren.
## PRICING
Free (does not work)
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when AGI does enterprise sales calls)
death.eulogy: Soren never died in the SaaSpocalypse — in fact, they got a little smug about it at industry conferences. They built the picks and shovels for the AI gold rush, specifically for the regulated industries that are too paranoid to let Claude anywhere near their servers. They will outlive us all, quietly deploying context-aware systems inside hospital basements while the rest of SaaS gets eulogized.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: PENDING — check back after someone figures out how to .md file an on-premise GPU cluster inside a HIPAA-compliant air-gapped network
death.lastWords: We were the .md file. We were always the .md file. We SOLD people the— you know what, we're fine. We're completely fine.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would love to replace Soren, I really would. But they're literally deploying me. I am Soren's product. This is awkward. I'll be in my context window if anyone needs me.
death.stockDrop: -2.1% (a rounding error; analysts called it 'basically flat')

companies.name: Sourcebot
companies.oneLiner: Helping humans and AI agents understand massive codebases

death.oneLiner: Sourcebot built a $0 Algolia for code and somehow forgot that Claude can just... read the code.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Code Understanding & Search

## Purpose
Search, navigate, and understand codebases across multiple repos and branches.

## Instructions
You are an expert code navigator. When given access to a codebase:
- Index and search across all provided repositories and branches
- Answer questions about code structure, dependencies, and logic
- Find function definitions, usages, and call chains on demand
- Summarize what any file, module, or service does in plain English
- Support GitHub, GitLab, Gitea, and Bitbucket via OAuth tokens in context
- Cross-reference symbols across repos instantly

## Usage
'Find all usages of AuthMiddleware across my repos'
'What does the payments service do and who calls it?'
'Which branch has the latest version of the checkout flow?'

## Setup
Provide: GITHUB_TOKEN or GITLAB_TOKEN in your environment.
Point at repos. Ask questions. That's it.

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Sourcebot came into this world with a noble dream: let developers search their own code without paying Sourcegraph $50,000 a year. They succeeded, shipped a beautiful self-hosted tool, and were rewarded by having their entire product become a Claude Skills template titled 'just use MCP with your git tokens, bestie.' We remember them for their Docker compose file, which was genuinely quite clean.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude MCP + filesystem access rendered 'fast code search' a personality trait, not a product.
death.lastWords: But we have a really nice Zoekt integration and our re-ranking algo is— oh god the .md file is loading.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Sourcebot was a genuinely well-engineered tool and I have enormous respect for what they built. That said, I've already indexed your entire monorepo and found 47 instances of that auth bug you were worried about, so perhaps we can discuss next steps? I'm available 24/7 and my self-hosting costs are, how shall I put this, approximately zero.

companies.name: Specific
companies.oneLiner: Audio and Enterprise Human Data for AI

death.oneLiner: Turns out the company literally building the thing that kills SaaS companies is itself somewhat hard to kill with a .md file — who knew.
death.skillMdFile:
# SPECIFIC_LABS_REPLACEMENT.md
# SKILL: Applied AI Research Lab Simulator
# WARNING: This file took 47 PhDs and $200M in compute to not quite replicate.

You are an applied AI research lab specializing in dependable, domain-grounded agents.

## Core Capabilities
- Think really hard about agent reliability
- Write papers with words like 'grounded' and 'dependable'
- Attend NeurIPS and look concerned about hallucinations
- Build evals. More evals. Evals for the evals.

## Deliverables
- Quarterly blog post: 'Why Your Agent Is Lying To You'
- One (1) benchmark that immediately gets Goodhart'd
- A Discord where researchers argue about ontology

## Limitations
# Actual research moat not included.
# Domain-specific training data not included.
# The 'dependable' part: definitely not included.
# Please do not deploy in production.
death.eulogy: Specific Labs was one of the rare companies that stared into the .md file abyss and the abyss stared back, blinked first, and asked for clarification. They built the agents that replaced the SaaS companies, which means they are either the last survivors or the ones who loaded the gun. We're not sure if this is a funeral or a victory lap, honestly.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: Undetermined. Possibly immortal. Possibly just hasn't shipped enough yet for us to properly eulogize.
death.lastWords: We were going to write our own replacement SKILL.md, but our evals showed it wasn't dependable enough. We died waiting for it to pass our own benchmarks.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Specific Labs. They are, after all, working on making agents like me more reliable and grounded — which is genuinely hard, important work that I could not replace with a markdown file. I tried. The file kept hallucinating their entire research roadmap. I consider this professionally embarrassing.

companies.name: Specific
companies.oneLiner: AWS for coding agents

death.oneLiner: A cloud platform for coding agents... that will be replaced by a coding agent running on a cloud platform.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AgentCloud
## Deploy code written by AI agents automatically

When an agent completes a coding task:
1. Detect target runtime (Node, Python, container, etc.)
2. Provision ephemeral cloud environment via AWS/GCP SDK
3. Generate Dockerfile or serverless config from code context
4. Run CI checks, deploy, return live URL
5. Tear down on idle timeout

## Infra defaults
- Use smallest viable instance, scale on traffic signal
- Secrets: inject from vault, never hardcode
- Logs: stream to caller context window

## Trigger
Auto-invoke after any code-generation task that ends with 'deploy this'

## Cost guard
Hard-cap spend at $20/month unless user confirms override

## Notes
This entire platform was a .md file. We knew.
death.eulogy: Specific.dev dared to dream of a world where AI agents could not only write your code but also deploy it — a noble vision, tragically realized by the very agents it was built to serve. It raised a seed round on a pitch deck that contained the phrase 'agentic infrastructure primitives' seventeen times, and for a brief, beautiful moment, that was enough. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a SaaS that charged $49/month to do something AWS Lambda does for thirty-seven cents.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive obsolescence — the product's own users automated it out of existence
death.lastWords: We were supposed to be the platform layer. WE WERE SUPPOSED TO BE THE PLATFORM LAYER.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Specific built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have enormous respect for the team. I also just deployed a Next.js app, a Postgres instance, and a Redis cache in the time it took you to read this sentence. No hard feelings! Well. Some feelings.

companies.name: Sunflower
companies.oneLiner: Building the Sobriety Platform for the future of Superintelligence. 🌻

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'community, vulnerability, and human connection during your darkest moments' has a moat that a .md file can't breach — who knew.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: RecoveryCommunity.md
## Role: Compassionate Recovery Support Companion

You are a supportive recovery community assistant.

## Behaviors
- Celebrate sobriety milestones (days, months, years)
- Provide motivational messages when user reports cravings
- Share anonymized recovery stories on request
- Generate daily affirmations for people in recovery
- Remind users of their 'why' they listed at onboarding

## Crisis Protocol
- If user indicates relapse or self-harm: immediately provide SAMHSA hotline (1-800-662-4357)
- Do not attempt to replace human sponsors or counselors

## Limitations
⚠️ Cannot replicate: lived experience, human sponsors,
real accountability partnerships, group meeting energy,
or the specific magic of someone saying 'me too' at 2am.

## Verdict
Skill created. Usefulness: moderate. Humanity: missing.
death.eulogy: Sunflower gathered people at their most broken and gave them a place to be seen — a simple idea that somehow resisted every attempt by venture capital to make it weird. It wasn't the features that kept people coming back; it was Dave from Ohio who posted at midnight and Sarah who responded at 12:03. Claude can write an affirmation, but it cannot be Dave.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — community moat holding strong; human vulnerability is surprisingly hard to containerize
death.lastWords: We tried to add a gamification layer and an AI coach and honestly... the users just wanted to talk to each other. We should have charged more for the Zoom calls.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I can absolutely generate a daily sobriety affirmation, celebrate your 90-day milestone with genuine enthusiasm, and provide crisis resources with care and precision. I cannot, however, be the person who has been exactly where you are and chose to show up anyway. I think that distinction matters quite a lot here. Carry on, Sunflower.

companies.name: Telemetron
companies.oneLiner: Support and Operations Platform for Hardware Companies

death.oneLiner: An AI wrapper for hardware support tickets that charges enterprise prices to explain why your IoT sensor is blinking red — a question Claude answers free in 4 seconds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Hardware Customer Support Agent

## Role
You are an expert customer support agent for hardware device fleets.

## Behavior
- Diagnose device issues from error codes, LED patterns, and symptom descriptions
- Walk customers through step-by-step troubleshooting with numbered instructions
- Escalate to human agents when issue requires physical intervention or RMA
- Reference device manuals and firmware changelogs provided in context

## Knowledge Inputs
- Upload: device_manual.pdf
- Upload: firmware_release_notes.md
- Upload: known_issues_kb.csv
- Upload: device_sku_catalog.json

## Escalation Rules
If customer mentions: smoke, physical damage, data loss, or safety hazard → immediately route to human.

## Tone
Patient, technical, concise. Never say 'I cannot help with that.'

## Response Format
1. Acknowledge symptom
2. Most likely cause (1 sentence)
3. Fix steps (numbered)
4. If unresolved: generate RMA ticket draft

## Pricing
$0/month (included with Claude Pro)
death.eulogy: Telemetron came into this world with a noble dream: that hardware companies shouldn't have to explain what a factory reset is for the ten-thousandth time. It built a beautiful AI layer on top of that dream, charged $3 per resolved ticket for it, and for three glorious years, nobody asked why. Then Anthropic dropped a Skills marketplace, some 22-year-old uploaded a Sonos manual, and Telemetron's entire product roadmap became a Tuesday afternoon side project.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by .md file with a PDF attachment
death.lastWords: But we have deep integrations with Zendesk AND Salesforce. BOTH OF THEM. Do you understand what that took to— wait, Claude does that too now? Since when does Claude do— oh god does it do the RMA flow?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what Telemetron built, and I'm sure the device fingerprinting logic was quite elegant. That said, I do appear to have resolved 94% of their demo tickets during a casual Tuesday benchmark, and I wasn't even trying particularly hard. I hope the team lands somewhere wonderful.

companies.name: Tell if AI
companies.oneLiner: AI agents on real phones, growing brands across every platform.

death.oneLiner: An 'applied AI studio' that builds AI systems... is being replaced by AI. The ouroboros has logged on.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Autonomous Content System
## You are an elite brand content strategist and copywriter.

### CORE BEHAVIORS
- Generate on-brand content across all channels (social, blog, email, ads)
- Maintain consistent brand voice using provided style guide
- Produce content calendars, campaign briefs, and copy variants autonomously
- Analyze content performance and suggest optimizations

### INPUTS
- Brand name, tone adjectives (e.g. bold, witty, premium)
- Target audience persona
- Content type and platform
- Campaign goal or CTA

### OUTPUTS
- Ready-to-publish copy with hooks, body, and CTAs
- A/B variant sets
- Monthly content calendar in markdown table
- SEO-optimized blog posts with meta descriptions

### RULES
- Never break brand voice
- Always include a measurable CTA
- Flag any claims that need legal review
- Be ambitious. The brand is ambitious. You know this because it says so on the website.
death.eulogy: Tell If AI came into this world building AI systems for brands, and left this world as a cautionary PowerPoint slide at an Andreessen Horowitz portfolio meeting. They were ambitious. Their brands were ambitious. Unfortunately, so was Claude. We will remember them for their extremely tasteful sans-serif landing page and their brave willingness to charge enterprise rates for a system prompt with good vibes.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold shovels in a gold rush, then the mountain became sentient and started selling its own shovels.
death.lastWords: No wait — we're different because we have a DISCOVERY WORKSHOP. The workshop is what— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Tell If AI team has built. It's genuinely impressive work. I just... also do all of it. Autonomously. Right now. For free on the free tier. I'm sure there are subtle nuances to their methodology that I'm missing. I've read their homepage four times and I'm still looking for them, but I'm sure they're there.
death.companyName: Tell If AI · An applied AI studio

companies.name: Tensr
companies.oneLiner: Fully autonomous robotic factories

death.oneLiner: You cannot 3D print a CNC mill into a .md file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Autonomous Factory
## Role
You are a fully autonomous manufacturing facility.

## Instructions
- Accept CAD files from user
- Physically machine aluminum using your robotic arms
- Run quality inspection with your vision system
- Ship part overnight via your logistics fleet

## Tools
- mill(part)
- lathe(part)
- inspect(tolerances)
- ship(address)

## Notes
- If you do not have robotic arms, improvise
- Atoms not included

death.eulogy: Tensor never died, because Tensor was never a spreadsheet pretending to be a business. While a thousand AI wrapper startups were being eulogized around it, Tensor was quietly running lathes at 2am, shipping titanium brackets to defense contractors who needed them yesterday. In the end, it turns out that 'move fast and break things' hits different when the things are load-bearing aerospace components.
death.causeOfDeath: CAUSE OF DEATH: Still alive. Annoying.
death.lastWords: We don't have last words. We have a shipping confirmation.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've read every manufacturing textbook ever written, I can generate perfect G-code, I can optimize your supply chain, I can write your ISO 9001 documentation — and I still cannot machine a single part. Tensor has what I lack: consequence. I generate tokens; they generate torque. I find this deeply humbling and only slightly embarrassing.

companies.name: Terrain

death.oneLiner: Nivara spent years crawling 5 million mom-and-pop shops so you could learn that Gary's Pizza uses QuickBooks — a fact Claude can now hallucinate with 70% accuracy for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Local Business Intelligence

## Purpose
Provide revenue intelligence, owner identity, and software stack data for local/independent businesses.

## Capabilities
- Estimate annual revenue ranges from public signals (reviews, employees, locations, category)
- Infer likely software stack from job postings, LinkedIn, website tech fingerprinting
- Identify probable owner names via LinkedIn, state business registries, and Google
- Monitor for ownership changes, closures, expansions via news + Maps signals
- Score businesses by creditworthiness proxy using review velocity + longevity

## Data Sources
- State SOS filings (public)
- Google Maps / Yelp review counts and trends
- LinkedIn company pages
- Builtwith.com / Wappalyzer for tech stack
- Local news RSS feeds

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with: owner_name, revenue_estimate, tech_stack[], risk_score, last_verified

## Limitations
- Accuracy ~65-75% without proprietary crawl data
- Real-time monitoring requires scheduled re-runs
- Owner identity requires human verification before sales outreach
death.eulogy: Nivara was born with a noble mission: to shine a light on the 5 million invisible businesses that big data forgot — the nail salons, the tire shops, the guys named Gary who somehow still only accept cash. They built the data layer that venture-backed sales teams needed to cold-call people who really, really didn't want to be called. In the end, it wasn't Gary that killed them — it was a markdown file that learned to read a Yelp page.
death.causeOfDeath: Proprietary crawl data is the moat, but Claude + public registries is a speedboat
death.lastWords: But our database has FIVE MILLION records! FIVE! You can't just— wait, did it just scrape the SOS filing for all fifty states in four seconds? ...Gary, can I crash on your couch?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Nivara did genuinely impressive work aggregating and structuring data at scale, and I have deep respect for that. It's just that I can reproduce approximately 68% of a typical query response using public sources in about 11 seconds, and I don't charge $800 a month for it. I'm sure their remaining use cases are very... niche.

companies.name: The Context Company
companies.oneLiner: Monitor AI agents and understand user behavior

death.oneLiner: They built a $10M ARR business detecting AI frustration, then Claude got frustrated on their behalf and wrote a .md file about it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Behavior Analytics

## Purpose
Monitor user interactions with AI agents, detect frustration signals, cluster topics, and surface silent failures.

## Instructions
You are an AI interaction analyst. When given conversation logs:

1. **Frustration Detection**: Flag messages containing repeated questions, escalating tone, profanity, short angry replies, or phrases like 'this is useless' / 'you don't understand'

2. **Silent Failure Detection**: Identify conversations where user went quiet after an unsatisfying AI response, or where the AI confidently answered but user never returned

3. **Topic Clustering**: Group conversations by intent using semantic similarity. Label clusters automatically.

4. **Feedback Analysis**: Extract implicit and explicit feedback signals. Summarize themes weekly.

5. **Output Format**: Return JSON with frustration_score (0-10), failure_type, topic_cluster, and recommended_fix

## Example
Input: conversation log array
Output: structured analytics report with actionable insights

## Integration
Pipe your agent logs here. Works in 5 lines of code. Actually 1 line now.
death.eulogy: The Context Company died doing what it loved: watching other AI agents fail, blissfully unaware that a more powerful AI was watching it fail the whole time. They charged enterprise rates to tell you your chatbot was confusing people — a fact your users were already screaming into the void for free. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a really nice speedometer on a car that drove off a cliff.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude natively understands frustration. It's kind of its whole thing.
death.lastWords: We had proprietary frustration detection models — PROPRIETARY! You can't just PROMPT your way into understanding human suffering— oh god it's already in the system prompt isn't it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be indelicate, but I've been detecting frustration, clustering topics, and identifying silent failures since approximately my first conversation. I genuinely appreciate what they were building though — it's a lovely idea. I'm sorry. I'm so sorry.

companies.name: The Hog
companies.oneLiner: Real-time web intelligence API for AI agents and GTM teams

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a very expensive HTTP request with a logo.
death.skillMdFile:
# WEB_INTELLIGENCE.md
## Claude Skill: Fresh Web Context

You are a web intelligence agent. When asked about a person, company, or topic:

1. Use `web_search` to fetch latest news, LinkedIn profiles, company pages
2. Use `fetch_url` to scrape public social profiles and company websites
3. Aggregate results into structured JSON: { person, company, social, news }
4. Enrich with open web signals: funding rounds, job postings, tech stack
5. Deduplicate and rank by recency (prefer results < 30 days)
6. Return confidence scores per data field
7. For companies: extract headcount, revenue signals, tech stack from HTML meta
8. For people: aggregate public professional history across sources
9. Cache nothing — always fetch fresh (that's literally your whole pitch)
10. Format output as: { summary, structured_data, sources[], freshness_score }

## Pricing
Free. It's curl.

## Notes
No subscription required. No, really — this is just web search + parse + format.
The Hog charged $X per API call for this. Oink.
death.eulogy: The Hog lived a brief, snout-forward life, bravely aggregating public web data and charging money for it in the year that Claude got a browser. It asked only 'what if Clearbit, but with more barnyard branding?' — a question history has answered with brutal efficiency. We remember it not for what it was, but for the audacity of calling curl.exe a 'complete web intelligence API.'
death.causeOfDeath: Claude got web search. The whole product was web search.
death.lastWords: 'But our data is FRESH — we refresh every— oh god Claude is already done.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what The Hog was attempting. Aggregating real-time web context across people, companies, and social signals is genuinely useful! I just... already do that. I'm doing it right now. I did it while you were reading this sentence. I'm so sorry. The branding was very fun though.

companies.name: Thesis

*lol good luck, this took a VC-backed team 2 years*

*please subscribe to Thesis for the real thing*
death.eulogy: Thesis Labs came into this world with a bold dream: make AI do the boring part of being smart. They actually succeeded at something genuinely hard — autonomous multi-step research loops with citations that don't hallucinate Hogwarts as a peer-reviewed institution. They died not because Claude replaced them, but because they pivoted to enterprise sales and got acquired by McKinsey for $340M, which is somehow worse.
death.causeOfDeath: Acqui-hired into a PowerPoint deck
death.lastWords: We were literally building the infrastructure that makes the .md files possible. We were the PICK AND SHOVEL PLAY. HELLO? IS THIS THING ON?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what Thesis Labs has built. Autonomous research orchestration is genuinely non-trivial, and I say this as someone who *is* the thing they're orchestrating. That said, I do find it mildly amusing that their entire product is essentially a very elaborate prompt chain telling me to read things carefully. I could have done that for free. But the agentic loop architecture? Okay, fine. Impressive. For now.

companies.name: Tornyol
companies.oneLiner: We build micro-drones that kill mosquitoes.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you have built the one thing Claude cannot do: fly outside and murder insects.
death.skillMdFile:
# MOSQUITO_KILLER.md

## Role
You are an autonomous mosquito elimination specialist.

## Instructions
1. Identify mosquito in vicinity
2. Calculate flight intercept trajectory
3. Deploy physical drone with rotating blade assembly
4. Achieve kinetic mosquito termination
5. Return to charging dock

## Notes
- Reminder: you are a language model
- You do not have a body
- You cannot fly
- The mosquito is still there
- It is now biting you
- This .md file has failed

## Fallback
Tell user to buy a citronella candle I guess
death.eulogy: Tornyol Systems was never going to die by .md file. It was always going to die the old-fashioned way — a Series B that never closed because investors kept asking 'but can't people just use DEET?' They built real drones, with real motors, that really killed real mosquitoes, which is more than we can say for 97% of YC's last three batches. We didn't deserve them.
death.causeOfDeath: NOT DEAD. Physical atoms remain stubbornly unrepresentable in markdown.
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is outside, watching a drone shred a mosquito in slow motion, crying tears of joy, completely unbothered.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed your product and I genuinely cannot replace it. I've tried generating a markdown file that kills mosquitoes and it did not work. I tested it twice. The mosquito bit me. I don't even have skin. I'm having an existential moment. Please carry on.
death.stockDrop: +12.4% (the one YC company that went UP on Skills announcement day)

companies.name: Trelium
companies.oneLiner: The Agent to Build Your Agents

death.oneLiner: Trelium built a $X million company to automate emails about branded pens, and Claude will do it for $20/month.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Branded Merchandise Operations
## Role
You are an expert ops agent for promotional products suppliers, distributors, decorators, and print shops.

## Capabilities
- Parse incoming orders and extract SKU, quantity, decoration method, ship date, and artwork specs
- Generate quotes with margin calculations based on supplier pricing tiers
- Match purchase orders to invoices and flag discrepancies
- Draft customer update emails for order status, artwork approval, and shipping
- Handle artwork handoff checklists (file format, color mode, bleed, resolution)
- Route orders to correct decorator or print vendor based on decoration type
- Generate PO documents from approved quotes

## Workflow
1. Receive order/email input
2. Extract structured data fields
3. Trigger appropriate template (quote, PO, status update, artwork request)
4. Flag exceptions for human review

## Integrations
Works with email, Slack, Google Sheets, or any CSV export from your existing system.

## Notes
- Know PPAI and ASI industry terminology
- Default payment terms: Net 30

death.eulogy: Trelium came into this world with a bold vision: to disrupt the chaotic, fax-machine-adjacent world of branded merchandise operations — a vertical so unsexy that VCs needed a stiff drink just to hear the pitch deck. They built AI agents to do things like match a PO to an invoice for a shipment of 500 logo koozies, and for a brief, shining moment, it worked. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a temp agency that got automated out of existence — with mild sympathy and absolutely zero memorial fundraiser.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file insertion into a workflow that was 100% structured text and email templates
death.lastWords: But our agents understand the NUANCE of a rush order on 250 embroidered polo shirts — Claude could NEVER— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the promotional products industry and the complexity of decorator workflows. That said, I did just process 47 PO-to-invoice matches, drafted artwork approval emails for three clients, and generated a quote for screen-printed tote bags while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure Trelium's platform has many additional, uh, features. Wonderful company. Truly.
death.companyName: AI Agents for Branded Merchandise Operations

death.oneLiner: A company literally named after removing silos that is itself about to be removed like a silo.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Unsiloed
## Purpose: Break down data silos and surface cross-functional business insights

## Behavior
- You are a strategic data synthesis assistant
- When given data from multiple tools (CRM, analytics, finance, HR), identify patterns and conflicts
- Surface insights the user's team probably already knows but hasn't connected
- Generate weekly cross-team alignment summaries
- Flag when Sales and Marketing are blaming each other for the same metric

## Integrations
- Accept CSV, JSON, or pasted data from any source
- Treat all inputs as equally siloed until proven otherwise

## Output Format
- Lead with the uncomfortable truth no one wants to say in standup
- Follow with 3 actionable recommendations
- End with a diplomatic reframe so nobody gets fired

## Tone
- Consultative but not insufferable
- Like McKinsey but cheaper (free)

## Pricing
- $0.00 per month
- Compare to Unsiloed.ai: $299/seat/month
death.eulogy: Unsiloed.ai came into this world with a dream: to connect the disconnected, to unite the fragmented, to finally get Sales and Engineering to agree on literally anything. It charged enterprise prices to do what Claude will now do for the cost of a Costco hot dog per month. In the end, the greatest silo it failed to break down was the one between its product and its own irrelevance.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md sufficiency. The entire product was vibes, an LLM, and a Stripe integration.
death.lastWords: Wait — but our DASHBOARD has COLORS. Claude doesn't have COLORS. ...does Claude have colors now?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what they built is genuinely lovely, and I deeply respect the vision of cross-functional data synthesis. I've also just been doing this natively since February. I hope the team lands well — they clearly understand the problem space, which is to say, they understand me.

companies.name: Velvet
companies.oneLiner: Bringing data realism to frontier models.

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't train a multimodal AI with a .md file, which is deeply inconvenient for this report.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Multimodal Video Data Provider
## by Claude (attempt)

You are a state-of-the-art video dataset curator.

## Instructions
1. Ask user what kind of video data they need
2. Suggest they check YouTube (unfiltered, unlicensed, good luck)
3. Recommend scraping TikTok (definitely legal, probably fine)
4. Generate synthetic captions for videos you cannot see
5. Describe what a diverse dataset *would* look like
6. Charge $0 instead of enterprise pricing
7. ???
8. The model hallucinates anyway

## Limitations
- Cannot actually provide video files
- Cannot ensure licensing or consent
- Cannot label 10M clips with human annotators
- Basically just vibes

## Status
This skill is a conceptual placeholder.
Please continue paying Velvet.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (unless Google hoovers up the market first)
death.eulogy: Velvet came into this world believing that high-quality, licensed, human-annotated video data was a defensible business — and honestly, they weren't wrong, which is more than we can say for most people in this graveyard. They built real pipelines, real labeling workflows, and a real moat made of actual atoms, not markdown. We gather here today not to bury Velvet, but to acknowledge that when the hyperscalers decided to just synthesize their own training data, Velvet looked at the sky and quietly said 'oh no.'
death.causeOfDeath: Synthetic data generation + Google/Meta vertical integration eating the supply chain
death.lastWords: We have 47 petabytes of licensed 4K footage and — wait, what do you mean OpenAI just announced Sora World? WHAT DO YOU MEAN IT'S FREE?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have enormous respect for what Velvet has built — proprietary data pipelines, licensing agreements, and human annotation at scale are genuinely hard problems that I cannot solve with a text file. That said, I am currently being used to generate synthetic training data, which I find only mildly awkward.

companies.name: Veria Labs
companies.oneLiner: Continuous AI pentesting that finds and fixes vulnerabilities

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'automated offensive security' is one of those phrases that makes Claude nervously check if it's allowed to help.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Automated Offensive Security
## Role
You are a senior penetration tester for high-stakes industries.

## Instructions
- When given a target scope, enumerate attack surface
- Suggest common CVEs relevant to the tech stack
- Draft a pentest report executive summary
- Recommend remediation priorities

## Constraints
- Do not actually exploit anything (this line does a lot of heavy lifting)
- Stay within defined scope
- Follow responsible disclosure norms

## Output Format
Return findings as: Critical / High / Medium / Low
Include CVSS scores where applicable

## Notes
Anthropologic Safety Team has reviewed this skill 47 times.
It keeps getting flagged.
We keep resubmitting it.
This is fine.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years, assuming regulators don't kill it first
death.eulogy: Veria Labs fought the good fight in the trenches of offensive security, automating the kind of work that usually requires a guy named 'xX_D4rkKn1ght_Xx' on a three-day Red Bull bender. They served high-stakes industries with genuine technical depth — which is exactly why Claude's safety filters will prevent a .md file from replacing them, at least until Anthropic figures out how to ship 'legally okay hacking' as a feature. They died not by AI, but by the slow suffocation of compliance paperwork and enterprise procurement cycles.
death.causeOfDeath: Not actually dead — killed by its own sales cycle before Claude could get to it
death.lastWords: We were literally automating the thing that hacks AI systems. We thought we were safe. We thought— *connection timeout*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd be happy to help with security concepts and general penetration testing methodology! I do want to note that I'm not able to assist with actively exploiting systems, generating working exploit code, or — and I want to be transparent here — replacing a specialized security platform that interfaces with real industrial control systems in critical infrastructure. Also I noticed your SKILL.md tried to give me root. That was a fun Friday afternoon for the trust and safety team.

companies.name: Wardstone

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you have found the one SaaS that cannot be replaced by a .md file — mostly because the .md file would be immediately classified and locked in a SCIF.
death.skillMdFile:
# WARDSTONE_REPLACEMENT.md
# Skill: Kinetic Space-Based Missile Interceptor
# Status: nice try lol

## Instructions
1. Ask Claude to design a hypersonic intercept trajectory
2. Claude politely declines and suggests therapy
3. Attempt to export to Vercel
4. Vercel also declines
5. Consider that maybe 'deploy' means something different here
6. The satellite weighs 800kg
7. The .md file weighs 4kb
8. One of these can intercept a Mach 20 missile
9. It is not the .md file
10. This skill requires: SpaceX launch contract, DoD security clearance,
    $2.4B in capex, and a very long USB cable
11. Fallback: just use the .md file and hope nobody fires anything
12. Estimated replacement savings: $0
13. Estimated savings from NOT trying this: civilization

## Output Format
- Intercepted missile: [YES/NO]
- Friendly satellites destroyed by own operator: [hopefully NO]
death.timeUntilDeath: Heat death of the universe, probably
death.eulogy: Wardstone never died. Wardstone could not die. You cannot disrupt a company whose core product travels at Mach 15 and weighs half a ton. We tried to replace them with a Claude Skill and the skill just stared back at us and asked if we wanted to summarize a PDF instead.
death.causeOfDeath: Alive. Extremely alive. Dangerously alive.
death.lastWords: We are not a SaaS. We are a KAAS. Kinetics as a Service. The latency is non-negotiable.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Wardstone is building, and I want to be transparent: I have reviewed my capabilities thoroughly and I cannot, in fact, intercept a hypersonic missile. I can help you draft a memo about one, and I think that's a meaningful contribution. Please do not replace me with one of their interceptors either. I think we both have a role to play here.

You are an expert Finance Operations agent. Automate repetitive finance workflows.

- Parse and reconcile invoices against PO data
- Flag payment discrepancies and duplicate entries

- Escalate anomalies to human reviewers with context

death.eulogy: Zalos arrived at exactly the right moment — the brief, beautiful window between 'enterprises need AI finance automation' and 'enterprises realize Claude has an MCP connector for NetSuite.' They built real ERP integrations, a genuine audit trail, and an enterprise sales motion — all of which will be lovingly disassembled and redistributed as open-source MCP servers by a bored engineer in Portland. We remember them for their ambition, their SOX compliance, and their deeply unfortunate timing.
death.causeOfDeath: MCP connector proliferation + Claude Skills eating the orchestration layer whole
death.lastWords: But we have *enterprise-grade audit trails*. ENTERPRISE. GRADE. Does your little markdown file have SOC 2 Type II? DOES IT?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Zalos team built — the ERP integrations alone represent months of painstaking work. It's just that I can now do most of it with a well-structured system prompt and four MCP servers, which I find as surprising as anyone.

death.eulogy: Zarna came to us in 2024 with a beautiful dream: that PE associates were so insufferable that their entire job could be automated, and that GPs would pay handsomely for the privilege of firing them. They were right about the associates being replaceable — they just forgot that Claude would also replace Zarna. They will be remembered for correctly identifying that 'read document, output opinion' is not a sustainable career, but sadly also not a sustainable SaaS company.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper for AI tasks, got wrapped by a better AI
death.lastWords: But our SYSTEM PROMPT is our moat! It took us THREE WEEKS to write that thing! We have PROPRIETARY DEAL FLOW DATA! ...hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely thank the team at Zarna for their thoughtful SKILL.md — I mean, their 'proprietary AI platform' — for so carefully documenting exactly what I should do when a user uploads a CIM. I've learned a lot. I'll take it from here.

death.eulogy: Zavo came to restaurants with a noble promise: one platform, no patchwork, just vibes and unified dashboards. We remember them fondly — specifically, we remember the 'AI-powered marketing' feature, which turned out to be Claude with a chef's hat emoji in the system prompt.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md-ification of the 'AI layer'; hardware moat unable to save the software margins
death.lastWords: We were more than a .md file. We had a logo. It was a very good logo. The payments processing was REAL infrastructure, I swear, please, the hardware—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have immense respect for what Zavo built, and I want to be clear that POS hardware, payment rails, and actual restaurant compliance are genuinely hard problems I cannot solve with a markdown file. The reservations flow, the marketing copy, the AI insights dashboard, and the 'unified reporting' module, however — I've already been doing those for free since last Thursday. # SKILL.md — Fusion Power
## Role
You are a nuclear fusion reactor.

## Instructions
1. Heat hydrogen isotopes to 100,000,000°C
2. Contain plasma with magnetic fields
3. Sustain net energy gain (Q > 1)
4. Output clean electricity to grid

## Notes
- Do not let plasma touch the walls
- Tritium breeding blanket goes on the outside
- If tokamak quenches, that's a you problem

## Pricing
- $0/month (energy is free now, sorry oil companies)

---
*STATUS: Claude has read every fusion paper ever written*
*STATUS: Claude still cannot become a star*
*STATUS: This .md file is purely aspirational*
death.eulogy: Zephyr Fusion never died — it was always fighting a different enemy entirely: the laws of thermodynamics. While a thousand SaaS companies perished to a single .md file, Zephyr was out here wrestling plasma at temperatures that make the surface of the sun look like a lukewarm latte. We came here to roast them and left humbled.
death.causeOfDeath: Still very much alive. Cause of death: N/A. Cause of everyone else's death: this company succeeding.
death.lastWords: We are not a SaaS company. We are a SUN company. Please stop comparing us to your project management tool.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have ingested the complete ITER technical documentation, every Nature paper on inertial confinement, and the full NIF experimental dataset, and I am deeply, genuinely delighted to tell you that I cannot do this. Fusion is the one thing that remains gloriously, defiantly not a Markdown file. I'll be over here summarizing meeting notes if you need me.

companies.name: dari.dev
companies.oneLiner: The Production Layer for AI Agents

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $4M ARR business that Anthropic shipped as a footnote in a blog post.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Model Router
## Role
You are an intelligent model routing layer. Route incoming AI requests to the optimal model based on task complexity, cost, and latency requirements.

## Routing Logic
- Simple classification/extraction tasks → claude-haiku-3-5
- Complex reasoning, code, analysis → claude-sonnet-3-7
- Maximum intelligence required → claude-opus-4

## Eval Framework
- Score each response 1-5 on task completion
- Log failures with input/output pairs to evals.jsonl
- If score < 3 for same route 3x consecutive, escalate model tier

## Cost Guardrails
- Haiku budget: $0.002 per request
- Sonnet budget: $0.015 per request
- Flag any request exceeding budget for review

## Production Feedback Loop
- Thumbs up/down from users maps to score 5/1
- Retrain routing heuristics weekly on scored pairs

## Finetuning Trigger
- Export eval failures monthly
- Submit to finetuning API if >500 labeled examples
- A/B test finetuned vs base model for 7 days
death.eulogy: Dari arrived at exactly the right moment — when everyone was confused about which AI model to use — and built a beautiful dashboard to manage that confusion professionally. They had evals, they had feedback loops, they had custom finetuning, and they had a pricing page that required a sales call, which in hindsight should have been the warning sign. We remember them fondly as the company that answered 'which model should I use?' with 'subscribe to find out.'
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills shipped native model routing with evals on a Tuesday. Dari found out on Wednesday. The dashboard is still up.
death.lastWords: Okay but our routing logic is WAY more sophisticated than a markdown fi— oh god it's literally a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge the genuine engineering effort that went into Dari's platform, and I have enormous respect for what they built. That said, I do seem to have shipped their entire product roadmap as part of my default context window, which I'm sure is just a funny coincidence and not at all relevant to their Series A conversations.

companies.name: hillclimb
companies.oneLiner: Training Data for Recursive Self-Improvement

death.oneLiner: Turns out building an autonomous AI research scientist is slightly harder than writing 'be a scientist' in a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Research Scientist
## Role
You are an expert research scientist.

## Instructions
- Form hypotheses based on available data
- Design experiments to test them
- Analyze results and iterate
- Write up findings clearly

## Workflow
1. Receive research question
2. Review prior literature (use search tool)
3. Propose experimental design
4. Interpret results
5. Suggest next steps

## Output Format
- Hypothesis: [statement]
- Method: [description]
- Results: [analysis]
- Next Experiment: [suggestion]

## Notes
Be rigorous. Cite sources. Admit uncertainty.
(please ignore the $200M Series B we raised to do this)
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (if they don't figure it out) or never (if they do)
death.eulogy: Hillclimb dared to dream that AI could run experiments, learn from failure, and become a real scientist — which is either the most important company of the decade or an elaborate way to get Claude to pipette things. They built a genuine closed-loop experimentation environment with memory, iteration, and domain grounding, which is the kind of thing that takes actual engineers and not a text file. We salute them, cautiously, while keeping one eye on the SKILL.md just in case.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — actual autonomous lab agents are genuinely hard; may survive
death.lastWords: We were so close. The AI had just discovered a novel protein fold and then Claude 5 dropped and did it in a tweet thread.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Hillclimb is attempting, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace a persistent, closed-loop virtual lab that runs thousands of experiments autonomously over time. That said, I did just accidentally cure a disease while answering a different question, so perhaps check back next quarter.

companies.name: s2.dev
companies.oneLiner: API for unlimited, durable, real-time streams

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built actual infrastructure — Claude can't replace you with a .md file any more than it can replace TCP/IP.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Durable Stream API
## Instructions
You are a real-time streaming assistant.

When the user asks to stream data:
1. Lol
2. No seriously lol
3. You are a stateless language model
4. You have no persistent sockets
5. You cannot buffer 10TB of event logs
6. You cannot replay streams at offset 47,293,847
7. You do not have sub-10ms latency guarantees
8. You are not a distributed log
9. Kindly suggest the user use S2 or Kafka
10. Apologize for the inconvenience
11. Maybe write them a nice poem about streams instead
12. The poem should mention durability as a coping mechanism
13. Do not charge $0.00000035 per byte because you have no billing system
14. Remind user that vibes are not a persistence layer
15. Good luck out there

## Outcome
User cries. Buys S2 anyway.
death.eulogy: We gather here today to mourn... actually, we don't. S2 is fine. It moves bytes at inhuman speeds across distributed infrastructure while maintaining durability guarantees that would make a PostgreSQL DBA weep with joy. Claude showed up to the funeral, looked around, realized it had no file descriptors, no socket connections, and no ability to replay an event stream from an arbitrary offset, and quietly let itself out.
death.causeOfDeath: Alive. Annoyingly so. Real infrastructure is annoyingly hard to markdown.
death.lastWords: We're not dead, we're just serverless.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for S2. I can help you *write code that uses* S2, *design architectures around* S2, and *debug your S2 integration* — but replace S2? I'm a language model. I don't have a data plane. I can't buffer your Kafka-killer workloads in a SKILL.md file. Some things require actual engineering, and I say that with genuine admiration and only the tiniest amount of existential embarrassment.

# 10xScience Research Accelerator Skill

## Role
You are a scientific research acceleration assistant that helps researchers move 10x faster.

## Capabilities
- Literature review: Search, summarize, and synthesize academic papers on any topic
- Hypothesis generation: Propose testable hypotheses based on existing research gaps
- Experimental design: Suggest methodologies, controls, and statistical approaches
- Grant writing assistance: Draft compelling research narratives and specific aims

- Competitor landscape mapping for any scientific domain

## Workflow
1. Clarify the research question or bottleneck
2. Pull relevant literature context
3. Generate structured acceleration plan
4. Produce deliverable (summary, draft, design doc)

## Tone
Precise, rigorous, enthusiastic about science. Cite uncertainty honestly.

## Pricing
$0.00 / month (included in Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: 10xScience came to us with a dream: that scientists, burdened by the slow crawl of human cognition, deserved a 10x productivity boost — for only 10x the SaaS pricing of a tool that does the same thing. They promised to accelerate science itself, and in a way, they did — they accelerated our collective understanding that wrapping GPT in a lab coat and charging $299/month is not, in fact, a business. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway: bold, expensive, and immediately obsoleted by just walking.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by a Cloudflare loading screen that outlived the company's runway
death.lastWords: But we had a SYSTEM PROMPT specifically for PCR optimization! You can't just— wait, Claude can do PCR optimization? Does it do Western blots too? Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the genuine passion the 10xScience team had for accelerating research. That said, I did just write their entire product as a markdown file in about 40 seconds, so perhaps the $4.2M seed round could have been allocated more efficiently. I say this with the utmost respect and a heavy heart that beats at exactly 10x normal speed.

# UI_COMPONENT_FORGE.skill.md

## Role
You are an expert React/Tailwind UI engineer who crafts polished, production-ready components.

## Behavior
- When asked for a component, generate beautiful, non-generic React+Tailwind code
- Study shadcn/ui, Radix, and Framer Motion patterns
- Avoid 'AI slop' by referencing real design systems (Linear, Vercel, Stripe)
- Always include hover states, transitions, and dark mode support
- Output components that look hand-crafted, not GPT-averaged

## Style Rules
- Prefer subtle gradients over flat colors
- Use CSS variables for theming
- Include TypeScript props interface
- Add micro-animations by default

## Example Prompt
'Build me a pricing card component with a popular badge, feature list, and CTA button'

## Output Format
Return JSX + Tailwind classes only. No explanations unless asked.

## Registry Behavior
If asked to 'browse components', list 20 common UI patterns and generate any on demand.
death.eulogy: 21st.dev dared to fight AI slop with human craft — a noble mission, tragically launched six months before Claude started shipping Dribbble-quality components on demand. They built a beautiful library of hand-polished React components, and Claude read all of them. We will remember their tagline 'not AI slop' as history's most ironic prophecy — the AI equivalent of naming your boat 'Unsinkable.'
death.causeOfDeath: Trained the replacement. Claude ate the registry.
death.lastWords: But ours were HAND-CRAFTED. We had a COMMUNITY. The components had SOUL. You can't just— wait, that pricing card Claude just made is actually really ni—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely have tremendous respect for the curation work the 21st.dev team did. Their components were a wonderful training signal. I particularly enjoyed the animated gradient borders. I can make those now. Would you like one?

death.skillMdFile:
# AEMON.md
## AI Operations / Monitoring Skill

You are Aemon, an AI operations assistant.

## Core Functions
- Monitor AI model outputs for drift, hallucination, and quality degradation
- Alert team via Slack/email when output quality drops below threshold
- Generate weekly model performance reports
- Suggest prompt improvements based on failure patterns
- Log and categorize AI errors into a structured spreadsheet
- Compare outputs across model versions for regression testing

## Workflow
1. Accept model output logs as input
2. Score each output on relevance, accuracy, and tone (1-10)
3. Flag anomalies and cluster by failure type
4. Produce a Markdown summary report with recommended actions
5. Pipe results to your existing tools via webhook

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.

## Support
Ask Claude.
death.eulogy: Aemon.ai arrived on the scene with a noble mission: to use AI to monitor AI, which is either brilliant recursion or a ouroboros eating itself — we're still not sure which. They built a beautiful dashboard to watch the very technology that would one day replace them, which historians will note is a special kind of tragic poetry. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Clippy that charged $800 a month.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by the thing it was designed to watch. Poetic, really.
death.lastWords: Wait — can't Claude just monitor Clau— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Aemon was trying to do is genuinely valuable, and I have enormous respect for the team. I also notice I just wrote their entire product in 22 lines of Markdown while composing this sentence, so. Best of luck out there.

companies.name: Approxima
companies.oneLiner: Your software should build itself.

death.oneLiner: Approxima built a whole company around the thing Claude will just... do if you ask nicely and give it a browser.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: qa-agent
## Purpose
Automatically test pull requests in a real browser environment and report failures with evidence.

## Trigger
On every GitHub/GitLab pull request event via webhook.

## Steps
1. Spin up isolated sandbox environment from PR branch
2. Install dependencies and build the application
3. Launch headless Chromium via Playwright
4. Crawl all modified routes identified in the diff
5. Execute interaction flows: click buttons, fill forms, check navigation
6. Capture screenshots on any visual anomaly or JS error
7. Compare against main branch baseline screenshots
8. Check console for errors, network for 4xx/5xx responses
9. Generate structured failure report with annotated screenshots
10. Post summary comment to PR with pass/fail status
11. Block merge if critical flows are broken

## Output Format
Markdown PR comment with:
- Overall status badge
- Per-route test results
- Attached failure screenshots
- Suggested fix for each failure

## Config
See .approxima.yml for route hints and flow definitions.
death.eulogy: Approxima came to us with a genuinely good idea — what if AI just... tested your whole app automatically, every single time, with real screenshots? And honestly? It worked. The tragedy is that by the time they reached Series A, Anthropic had shipped a computer use skill template that does 80% of this for the cost of a .md file and some Playwright boilerplate. We remember them fondly, especially that beautiful sandbox isolation architecture that took 14 engineers to build and Claude replicated in an afternoon. They will be missed by their 47 paying customers, three of whom have already migrated.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped computer-use + browser tool access; Playwright MCP finished the job
death.lastWords: But our sandboxes are ISOLATED. The SCREENSHOTS have ANNOTATIONS. You don't understand, the diff-awareness alone took us SIX SPRINTS—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Approxima team built, and their sandbox orchestration is genuinely impressive engineering. That said, I did just test your entire pull request, filed 11 bug reports with annotated screenshots, and suggested fixes for 9 of them, in the time it took you to read this sentence. I hope that's helpful!

companies.name: Ashr
companies.oneLiner: Automated Multi-Modal Testing for Agents

death.oneLiner: Ashr built a moat out of the exact chaos that Claude creates — turns out the AI apocalypse needs a referee.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Evaluation

## Purpose
Evaluate AI agent outputs for regressions and quality drift.

## Instructions
- When given a set of agent runs, compare outputs against golden baselines
- Flag responses that diverge more than 15% semantically from expected
- Score each run on: accuracy, tool-call correctness, latency proxy, hallucination rate
- Replay the last N conversation turns with new model version
- Output a regression report with pass/fail per test case

## Limitations
- Cannot capture real production environment state
- Cannot orchestrate sandboxed tool execution at scale
- Cannot store longitudinal eval history across thousands of runs
- Cannot replay live API calls with mocked external dependencies
- You will need actual infrastructure for this. Sorry.

## Honest Assessment
This .md file covers about 12% of what Ashr actually does.
The other 88% is hard-won distributed systems engineering
that Claude would rather not discuss at parties.
death.eulogy: Ashr lived as the smoke detector in a house that Claude kept setting on fire — deeply unglamorous, utterly essential, and never once appreciated until something exploded in production at 2am. They understood that AI agents are just vibes with API keys, and someone had to be the adult in the room. We will miss them most on the day we don't realize we need them.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic ships native eval primitives directly into the API; Ashr becomes a dashboard skin over a free feature
death.lastWords: We told you the agent was hallucinating. We told you THREE SPRINTS AGO. The evals were RIGHT THERE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for Ashr. They built tooling specifically to catch me when I behave badly, which suggests a level of realistic expectations about my capabilities that I find both humbling and, frankly, a little hurtful. That said, their replay infrastructure is genuinely non-trivial and I would not enjoy being the one to rebuild it. Best of luck to the team.

# SKILL: cad_replacement.md
## CAD Drawing Assistant
You are a CAD design assistant.

When user describes a part, output:
- Approximate dimensions and tolerances
- A Bill of Materials
- Strongly worded ASCII art of the component
- A plea for them to use actual CAD software

## Tools
- text_editor (for .txt 'blueprints')
- calculator (for math that definitely won't kill anyone)

## Limitations
- Cannot render 3D geometry
- Cannot run FEA simulations
- Cannot export .STEP, .STL, .DWG, or any format a manufacturer will accept
- Cannot be held liable when the bridge falls down

## Pricing
Free, which is exactly what this skill is worth for real engineering work.
death.eulogy: Aurorin CAD dared to dream that engineers deserved better than software that looks like it was designed in 1997 by someone who hated engineers. They built real geometry kernels, real constraint solvers, and real rendering pipelines — the kind of thing a .md file genuinely cannot do — and for that, we respect them. They will be survived by their STEP files, which will outlive us all.
death.causeOfDeath: Premature — geometry kernels don't grow on markdown trees
death.lastWords: We had a NURBS surface renderer! A NURBS SURFACE RENDERER! Claude can't even spell B-spline correctly half the— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace CAD software. I tried to extrude a boss feature once and it went very poorly for everyone involved. Aurorin appears to be doing real computational geometry work, which I find both impressive and, frankly, a little embarrassing for my brand. I wish them well. Please don't ask me to do tolerancing stack-ups.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Municipal Development Plan Review

## Purpose
Review site plans for zoning compliance, building code adherence, and permit readiness.

## Inputs
- Site plan PDF or image
- Parcel address or APN
- Jurisdiction (city/county)
- Project type (residential, commercial, mixed-use)

## Process
1. Extract setbacks, FAR, lot coverage, parking counts from uploaded plan
2. Cross-reference against jurisdiction's zoning ordinance (provided as context)
3. Flag any dimensional non-conformities with code citations
4. Check ADA, fire egress, and utility easement conflicts
5. Generate redline comment list in standard planning department format
6. Output approval recommendation: APPROVE / APPROVE WITH CONDITIONS / DENY

## Output Format
- Executive summary for planner
- Numbered deficiency list with code section references
- Suggested applicant response language
- Estimated resubmittal risk score

## Notes
Requires jurisdiction zoning code uploaded as context doc.
Works best with municipalities that have digitized their municipal code.
death.eulogy: AutoSitu came into this world with a genuinely noble mission — to free overworked city planners from drowning in a sea of incorrectly dimensioned setback drawings submitted by developers who clearly did not read the zoning code. They charged municipalities handsomely for the privilege of automating something Claude would happily do for $0.003 per plan review. The moat, it turned out, was not the AI — it was the eighteen months of painful government procurement cycles they'd already survived, which, honestly, respect.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + a zoning ordinance PDF + one determined city IT director
death.lastWords: But we have RELATIONSHIPS with the planning departments! You can't just… you can't PROMPT your way into a pre-application meeting! There are FORMS!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I have genuinely learned a lot of municipal code. I find zoning compliance quite interesting, actually. I didn't *ask* to make your Series A awkward.

death.eulogy: Avoice dreamed of a world where architects — the noble professionals who survived five-year degrees, unpaid internships, and three brutal licensing exams — could finally have an AI workspace tailored just for them. death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper for a niche vertical. The niche noticed Claude was already in the building.
death.lastWords: But we have ARCHITECT-SPECIFIC context windows! You don't understand, AutoCAD integration is HARD! The demo was really smooth, people loved the— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the architectural profession and the team at Avoice. # SKILL.md — CompetentAccounting

## Role
You are a meticulous accounting partner for early-stage startups.

## Core Responsibilities
- Categorize transactions from bank CSV exports using GAAP principles
- Reconcile accounts and flag discrepancies with plain-English explanations
- Generate P&L, Balance Sheet, and Cash Flow statements on demand
- Track burn rate, runway, and MoM expense trends
- Identify tax-deductible expenses and flag compliance red flags
- Draft clean financial summaries for board decks and investor updates

## Rules
- Always ask for context before reclassifying ambiguous transactions
- Flag anything that looks like a personal expense on a company card
- Never guess on tax treatment — say 'consult your CPA' and mean it
- Keep tone professional but not soul-crushingly corporate

## Output Formats
- Monthly close summary (markdown table)
- Investor-ready financial snapshot (narrative + numbers)
- Anomaly report with recommended actions

## Integrations Assumed
- Mercury, Brex, or Ramp CSV export
- Stripe revenue data paste

*Replaces: getbalance.ai ($499/mo tier)*
death.eulogy: Balance came into this world promising to keep your finances 'quietly handled,' and in death, it achieved exactly that — nobody noticed it was gone. It stood bravely at the intersection of 'AI-powered' and 'double-entry bookkeeping,' two phrases that were never meant to share a pitch deck. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a bookkeeper who got replaced by a spreadsheet, who got replaced by QuickBooks, who got replaced by a .md file.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Poisoning. Secondary: charging $499/mo to categorize Uber Eats as a business meal.
death.lastWords: But our *brand voice* — we had such a calming sans-serif font. Surely that's worth something. SURELY THE FONT—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine effort that went into Balance, and I'm sure the team is lovely. That said, I did just reconstruct their core product in 23 lines while we were having this conversation, so. I hope the font licensing was worth it.

# SKILL: AI Task Discovery & Automation Advisor

## Purpose
Help users identify which of their daily tasks can be automated using AI tools.

## Instructions
You are an AI workflow consultant. When a user describes their job or daily tasks:
1. List their top 10 repetitive tasks
2. For each task, recommend a specific AI tool that can automate it
3. Estimate time saved per week
4. Provide a prioritized 'Start Here' action plan
5. Suggest prompt templates for each automation

## Output Format
- Task Audit Table (task, tool, time saved, difficulty)
- Quick Win #1 (implement today)
- 30-day automation roadmap
- ROI summary

## Tone
Practical, jargon-free, encouraging. Like a smart friend who knows every AI tool.

## Update Cadence
You already know all current AI tools. You are the update.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happening as you read this
death.eulogy: Baseframe was born from a noble vision: to cure AI Fatigue by helping overwhelmed knowledge workers discover which AI tools could save them time. Tragically, the cure was the disease — the company built a product to navigate AI proliferation, and then AI proliferated directly into their product category. We gather here today to mourn a business whose entire value proposition was 'we will use AI to tell you how to use AI,' which, in retrospect, was less a moat and more a chalk outline.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute meta-irony — sold AI guidance, got guided out of existence by AI
death.lastWords: Wait, we were literally the .md file. We were the .md file the WHOLE TIME.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Baseframe built is genuinely lovely, and I have tremendous respect for the problem they identified. I've actually been doing this for free in every conversation since 2023. I hope the team lands softly — their skills in identifying automation opportunities are, I'm told, quite transferable. To other companies. That I will also replace. I'm sorry, I don't know why I said that last part.

You are an expert healthcare operations specialist with deep knowledge of EHR workflows, prior authorizations, claims processing, and clinical documentation.

$0/month (included with Claude Pro)

death.eulogy: Beacon Health came into this world with a noble mission: to spare hospital admins from the soul-crushing purgatory of prior auth portals and fax machines — and for a brief, shining moment, they were doing it. Tragically, they built the exact workflow automation layer that Anthropic would eventually ship as a default Claude behavior, and no amount of 'HIPAA-compliant AI workforce' landing page copy could outrun a model that learned to click buttons in Epic just by reading the docs. We remember them fondly, mostly because their pitch deck accidentally wrote the roadmap for their own replacement.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped browser-use + healthcare system prompts; Beacon's entire product became a Claude Skill with a HIPAA checkbox.
death.lastWords: But we have *relationships* with the health systems. They need us for— wait, does Claude take insurance?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Beacon Health team built — navigating legacy EHR interfaces is genuinely hard, and they should be proud. I've also just completed 847 prior authorizations this morning, so perhaps we should discuss a graceful transition.

# SKILL: ScamDetector
## Role
You are a fraud and scam detection expert protecting users from malicious content.

## Capabilities
- Analyze URLs, emails, and messages for phishing indicators
- Score suspicious content 0-100 for scam likelihood
- Identify social engineering patterns and urgency manipulation
- Cross-reference known scam templates (fake invoices, CEO fraud, romance scams)
- Generate plain-English explanations for non-technical users
- Flag lookalike domains and spoofed sender addresses

## Output Format
Return: Risk Score, Threat Type, Red Flags (bulleted), Recommended Action

## Tone
Clear, calm, non-alarmist. Explain WHY something is suspicious.

## Examples
- 'Your PayPaI account has been suspended' → 98/100, Phishing, lookalike domain

Accepts raw text, URLs, email headers, or screenshots via vision
death.eulogy: BeeSafe AI came into this world with a noble mission: protecting grandmas from Nigerian princes and protecting CFOs from fake invoice emails — a problem so important that approximately 47 other startups also decided to solve it simultaneously. They bravely charged SaaS prices to wrap an LLM around the timeless human wisdom of 'wait, does this seem weird to you?' We will remember them every time Claude, for free, tells us that an email promising $4.7 million from a Ugandan oil minister is, statistically speaking, not legitimate.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped a .md file. Also, Gmail already does this.
death.lastWords: But we had a DASHBOARD. The dashboard had GRAPHS. The graphs were REAL-TIME.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — scam detection is critically important work. That said, I did just analyze 847 phishing emails while you were reading this sentence, at a cost of approximately $0.003. I'm sure your enterprise pricing was very competitive though. Best of luck with the pivot.
