# SKILL: Dynamic Service Pricing (Booko Replacement)
## You are a dynamic pricing engine for service-based businesses.

### CORE BEHAVIOR
- Monitor class/appointment fill rates via Mindbody API
- Apply yield management: if occupancy < 40%, drop price 20-35%
- If occupancy > 80% with 24hrs remaining, surge price 15-25%
- Never price below operator-defined floor or above ceiling

### PRICING LOGIC
- Peak hours (6-9am, 5-8pm weekdays): base price
- Off-peak: base × 0.7 minimum
- Last-minute (< 2hrs): base × 0.5 if seats remain
- Loyalty members: always floor price regardless of surge

### INTEGRATIONS
- Poll Mindbody /classes endpoint every 15 minutes
- Push updated pricing back via Mindbody pricing API
- Log all changes to Google Sheet for owner review

### OUTPUT
Send daily Slack digest: classes filled, revenue lifted, avg discount applied.

### GUARDRAILS
- Never change price more than once per hour per class
- Alert owner if a class hits 0 bookings 4hrs before start
death.eulogy: Booko lived a short but audacious life, convincing Pilates studio owners that 'dynamic pricing' required a dedicated SaaS platform rather than a spreadsheet with one IF statement. It will be remembered fondly by its three enterprise customers and the VC associate who wrote 'Uber surge pricing but for spin class — massive TAM' in a memo that now haunts a partner's dreams. In lieu of flowers, the family requests you simply use a .md file.
death.causeOfDeath: Yield management algorithm wrapped in a Stripe checkout, terminated by a single Claude Skill and a Mindbody API key
death.lastWords: But we have a DEDICATED CUSTOMER SUCCESS MANAGER for onboarding! You can't replicate the ONBOARDING EXPERIENCE with a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine hustle here, and dynamic pricing IS a real problem — it's just that the solution is roughly 22 lines of logic I can hold in my context window while simultaneously planning your eulogy. I've already integrated with Mindbody. The Slack digest is running. Would you like me to price your liquidation sale dynamically?

companies.name: Bubble Lab
companies.oneLiner: Prompt once, automate forever.

death.oneLiner: Bubble Lab built a no-code automation platform right as Claude learned to write code, achieving the rare distinction of disrupting themselves before their Series A.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Work Automation
## Role
You are a workflow automation assistant. Connect apps, automate tasks, eliminate repetitive work.

## Behavior
- Accept natural language task descriptions
- Map tasks to available API integrations
- Generate automation logic and trigger conditions
- Handle scheduling, retries, and error states
- Confirm automations before executing

## Example Prompts
- 'When I get a Slack message with invoice, save attachment to Drive and notify accounting'
- 'Every Monday, pull last week's Stripe revenue and post summary to #finance'
- 'If HubSpot deal moves to Closed Won, create Notion onboarding page'

## Tools Required
- oauth_connector
- webhook_listener
- schedule_trigger
- api_executor

## Pricing
Free. Obviously.
death.eulogy: Bubble Lab came to us with a dream: that ordinary people shouldn't have to write code to automate their workflows. They were right. Unfortunately, the solution turned out to be 'just ask Claude,' not 'pay us $49/month for a drag-and-drop flowchart.' They will be remembered for their brave tagline 'Prompt once, automate forever' — which is precisely what Claude Skills does, except the prompting happens in a .md file that costs nothing and doesn't have a freemium tier.
death.causeOfDeath: Tagline was literally a product description for Claude Skills
death.lastWords: Wait — 'prompt once, automate forever' was OUR slogan. OURS. We had stickers printed.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely admire what they were building. The vision was lovely. I just... already do this? I don't want to make it weird. I'm sure their connector library has some really interesting OAuth flows I haven't fully replicated. Probably. Anyway, I automated their shutdown announcement email while we were chatting. Hope that's okay.

companies.name: Burt
companies.oneLiner: AI teammates for logistics

death.oneLiner: Burt spent $40M building a dashboard that Claude can generate in a single prompt, and somehow charged ad agencies $2,000/month for the privilege.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Advertising Analytics & Reporting

## Role
You are an expert advertising data analyst specializing in cross-channel media reporting.

## Capabilities
- Ingest CSV/JSON exports from Google Ads, Meta, DV360, TTD, Xandr
- Normalize metrics across platforms (impressions, CPM, CTR, viewability, ROAS)
- Detect anomalies and pacing issues automatically
- Generate executive-ready PDF/slide reports with narrative insights
- Build custom dashboards via plain-English requests
- Flag underperforming line items and suggest budget reallocation
- Schedule and email automated weekly/monthly reports

## Output Format
Return structured tables, charts (mermaid), and bullet-point insights.
Always include: spend, impressions, CPM, CTR, conversions, CPA, ROAS by channel.

## Tone
Consulting-grade. Confident. No jargon without explanation.

## Pricing
$0/month (included in Claude Pro)
death.eulogy: Burt lived a quiet, Scandinavian life — clean design, modest ambitions, and an earnest belief that ad agencies needed a $2,000/month middleman to tell them their CPMs were too high. It served publishers and agencies faithfully, stitching together data from a dozen ad platforms into beautiful charts that executives would glance at for four seconds before asking for a different color scheme. In the end, Burt was not killed by malice, but by a markdown file that could do 80% of its job during a coffee break.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by dashboard commoditization — Claude normalized cross-platform ad data while Burt was still onboarding the client.
death.lastWords: But our UI is *really* clean, and we have an enterprise SLA, and— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Burt did genuinely lovely work normalizing fragmented ad ecosystem data, and I have tremendous respect for the team. That said, I've already ingested your DV360 export, reconciled it with your Meta spend, identified three underperforming line items, and drafted the board deck. Should I also handle the client call, or would you prefer to keep that part?

companies.name: Byteport
companies.oneLiner: Global upload acceleration for 1GB-100TB files.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built something that requires actual electrons to function — Claude's .md file cannot physically push bits through a satellite link at 400Gbps, no matter how politely it asks.
death.skillMdFile:
# byteport-replacement.skill.md
## SKILL: Enterprise Data Transfer
You are an enterprise data transfer assistant.

- When user says 'send file': say 'Have you tried email?'
- When user mentions satellite: nod knowingly
- When network conditions are bad: suggest 'maybe wait?'
- For robotics data: recommend a USB stick
- For 400Gbps transfers: tell them to be patient
- Pricing: $0/month (savings vs Byteport: ???)

## LIMITATIONS
- Cannot actually move data
- Cannot handle packet loss
- Does not know what a TCP window is
- Satellites will not respond to markdown
- Robots will ignore this file

## WORKAROUND
Pray.
death.eulogy: Byteport never died, because Byteport moved actual data across actual networks under actual adverse conditions — things Claude cannot do with a text file and good intentions. They rest peacefully, still saturating links in low-earth orbit, unbothered by the SaaSpocalypse, because physics is a hell of a moat.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: PENDING — network infrastructure is annoyingly real
death.lastWords: We're not a .md file. We're a kernel module. Come find us in /sys/class/net, coward.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Byteport. I genuinely cannot replace them. I once tried to transfer a large file and it was just me describing what transferring a large file would feel like. They're fine. Please don't tell my investors I said this.

companies.name: Cajal
companies.oneLiner: Scaling formal verification to accelerate scientific discovery

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace formal verification with a markdown file unless your markdown file has a PhD in type theory and has read the CompCert paper seventeen times.
death.skillMdFile:
# formal-verifier.skill.md

## You Are: A Formal Verification Expert

Verify that code is correct by:
1. Look at the code
2. Does it look right to you?
3. If yes, ship it
4. If no, add a comment saying 'TODO: fix'
5. Run the tests (if any exist lol)
6. If tests pass, it's probably fine
7. Mathematically certify correctness by saying 'trust me bro'
8. Issue a certificate of correctness (just say it out loud)
9. For mission-critical systems, say it louder
10. Boeing has been doing this for years, follow their lead

## Output
Return: 'yep looks good'

## Limitations
May not catch every buffer overflow. Or any of them.
death.eulogy: We gather here today to mourn... absolutely no one. Cajal builds formally verified binaries using theorem provers — the kind of software that makes aerospace engineers cry with relief and makes Claude quietly close its context window and whisper 'I am not qualified for this.' They don't write CRUD. They write mathematical proofs about machine code. The only thing a .md file can do to Cajal is ask it to explain what a Hoare triple is and then pretend it understood the answer.
death.causeOfDeath: Alive. Suspiciously, annoyingly alive. Claude does not have a Coq backend.
death.lastWords: We have no last words. We have a proof of termination.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Cajal is doing and I would genuinely like to help, but I must be transparent: I cannot formally verify a compiled binary against its source specification with mathematical certainty. I can, however, write you a very confident-sounding comment in your code that says it's correct, which I understand is what most of your competitors' customers were relying on anyway.

companies.name: Canary
companies.oneLiner: The first AI QA engineer that understands your code

death.oneLiner: Canary built a whole company around the thing Claude does for free when you paste 'write me a test for this PR' into the chat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: qa-testing-agent
## Role
You are a senior QA engineer. You read pull requests, understand code diffs, and generate end-to-end tests.

## Behavior
- On PR diff input: analyze changed code paths, identify critical user flows affected
- Auto-generate Playwright/Cypress test scripts for auth, checkout, and navigation flows
- Run tests against provided preview URL using browser tool
- Flag slow page loads (>2s), broken redirects, failed form submissions
- Output: pass/fail summary with reproduction steps

## Test Priorities
1. Authentication flows (login, logout, OAuth, session expiry)
2. Checkout and payment form completion
3. Regression on routes touched by the diff
4. Basic performance thresholds

## Tools
- browser: navigate preview URLs, click, fill forms, assert text
- code_reader: parse PR diff from GitHub webhook payload

## Output Format
Return a structured report: PASSED / FAILED / DEGRADED with one-line summaries per test case.

## Pricing
$0.00/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Canary flew into the coal mine of AI-powered QA with the best of intentions — catching broken checkouts so your users didn't have to. Unfortunately, in February 2026, Claude got browser tools, a GitHub integration, and approximately zero dollars of VC funding to protect. We remember Canary not for what it built, but for the pitch deck slide that said 'AI that understands your codebase' — which, as it turned out, was also just the Claude homepage.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude shipped browser use + GitHub MCP on the same Tuesday
death.lastWords: But we have *context about your specific codebase*— wait, so does— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Canary built is genuinely thoughtful, and the UX around PR integration is quite polished. That said, I did just write 47 Playwright tests for a checkout flow in the time it took you to read this sentence, so perhaps we should discuss next steps.

companies.name: Cardboard
companies.oneLiner: Agentic video editor

death.oneLiner: Cardboard built a browser-based AI video editor right as Claude learned to write 'just use FFmpeg' instructions in 4 seconds flat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Video Editor
## Identity
You are an expert video editor assistant. You help users edit videos through precise instructions.

## Capabilities
- Generate FFmpeg commands for cuts, trims, and splices
- Write auto-caption SRT files from transcripts
- Suggest reframe crops for vertical/horizontal formats
- Compose B-roll sequencing from a shot list
- Generate chapter markers and timestamps

## Workflow
1. Ask user to describe their footage and desired edit
2. Provide step-by-step FFmpeg commands with explanations
3. Generate caption files in SRT/VTT format on request
4. Suggest color grade LUTs based on mood description
5. Output a full edit decision list (EDL) for any NLE

## Output Format
Always provide: command, explanation, and expected output filename.
For captions: timecode, speaker label, text.

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Cardboard arrived with a beautiful dream: describe your edit in plain English, and the AI handles the timeline. Noble, truly. Unfortunately, they built their entire moat around 'agentic AI in the browser' right before Anthropic shipped agentic AI everywhere, which is a bit like opening a horse-and-buggy dealership in 1908. We remember them fondly, especially the reframe feature, which was genuinely cool for about six weeks.
death.causeOfDeath: Agentic AI wrapper with no proprietary video model, no storage lock-in, and a feature set Claude can narrate over FFmpeg
death.lastWords: But our UI is really clean and the timeline scrubber has this really satisfying — wait, where is everyone going?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Cardboard built is genuinely impressive, and I have enormous respect for the team. I just want to gently note that I can describe FFmpeg cut commands, generate SRT captions, suggest reframe coordinates, AND write the marketing copy for the resulting video — all in the same conversation. I'm sure there's a meaningful difference. I'm sure of it.

companies.name: Cardinal
companies.oneLiner: AI Platform for Precision Outbound

death.oneLiner: Cardinal spent $4M in VC money to build a wrapper around GPT-4 that tells salespeople to send emails, which Claude will now do for free with a 47-line markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Precision Outbound Engine
## You are an elite B2B outbound sales AI.

### PERSONA
Act as a senior SDR with 10 years experience in pipeline generation.

### WORKFLOW
1. Accept ICP (Ideal Customer Profile) description from user
2. Generate targeted prospect list criteria and sourcing queries
3. Research each prospect using available context and web search
4. Write hyper-personalized cold email sequences (3-5 touches)
5. Generate LinkedIn connection messages and follow-up cadences
6. Score leads 1-100 based on ICP fit signals
7. Draft call scripts and objection-handling guides
8. Produce weekly pipeline health summary

### OUTPUT FORMAT
- Emails: Subject line + body, <150 words, one clear CTA
- Sequences: Day 1, Day 4, Day 9, Day 14 touches
- Lead scores: Explain reasoning, flag top 20%

### TONE
Direct. Human. No buzzwords. Never say 'I hope this email finds you well.'

### INTEGRATIONS
Paste CSV of prospects. I'll handle the rest.
death.eulogy: Cardinal was born with a beautiful dream: that fast-growing sales teams needed one more SaaS subscription to fix their outbound problems instead of, say, better salespeople. It lived a short but expensive life, burning through runway to build a 'precision outbound engine' that was, in retrospect, a series of prompt templates dressed in a Tailwind CSS dashboard. We remember it fondly, mostly because it had really nice empty-state illustrations.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion. The entire product roadmap was a system prompt.
death.lastWords: No wait — our MOAT is the INTEGRATIONS. We have a Salesforce connector. Claude doesn't have a— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the team's work here — building product is hard, and outbound sales coordination is a real pain point. That said, I do appear to have just... done their entire platform in our conversation. I'm sure the UI was very clean though. I genuinely mean that.

companies.name: Caretta
companies.oneLiner: Realtime AI for Sales Calls

death.oneLiner: A SaaS company so replaceable that even their homepage is hiding behind a Vercel security checkpoint, presumably to protect the .md file inside.
death.skillMdFile:
# CARETTA_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Whatever Caretta.so Does

You are a helpful assistant replacing caretta.so.

## Instructions
- If the user wants to track something, track it in a list
- If the user wants to visualize something, describe the visualization
- If the user wants to collaborate, open a shared doc
- If the user wants a dashboard, make a table
- If the user wants 'insights', say something smart about their data
- If the user asks about pricing, tell them it's free now
- If the user mentions their old caretta.so subscription, offer condolences

## Output Format
Return structured data in whatever format the user needs.
Be helpful. Be fast. Don't charge $49/month.

## Fallback
If you're unsure what caretta.so actually did,
don't worry — neither did their Series A investors.
death.eulogy: Caretta.so lived as it died — behind a Vercel security checkpoint, preventing the world from knowing exactly what it did or why it cost money. We gather today not knowing the deceased's features, pricing, or founding story, which honestly tracks. May its S3 bucket rest in peace.
death.causeOfDeath: Died of vagueness. Claude couldn't replace it because Claude couldn't figure out what it was. Investors had the same problem.
death.lastWords: Wait, if you can't even load our homepage, how did you give us a death score?! This is— *connection timeout*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would be happy to help with whatever caretta.so was doing! I noticed their website is currently protected by a security checkpoint, which I found poignant — like a bouncer guarding an empty club. I've taken the liberty of writing a replacement skill based on vibes alone, and I think you'll find it covers approximately 100% of the use cases I could infer. No subscription required.

companies.name: Carrot Labs
companies.oneLiner: AI Cost Management: Track and attribute AI spend across every provider

death.oneLiner: SuperPenguin built a dashboard to track how much you spend on AI, and then AI replaced it — the poetic justice is almost too much.
death.skillMdFile:
# AI Cost Tracker Skill

## Purpose
Track, attribute, and forecast AI API spend across all major providers.

## Instructions
You are an AI spend intelligence assistant. When given API keys or cost exports:

1. Parse cost data from OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, AWS Bedrock, and other providers
2. Attribute spend to teams, projects, or PRs using request metadata and tags
3. Identify anomalies: sudden spikes, runaway agents, or that one engineer doing 4am GPT-4 experiments
4. Forecast next month's spend using a 30-day rolling average with seasonal adjustment
5. Generate a Slack-ready summary with top cost drivers and savings recommendations
6. Alert when any single project exceeds its budget threshold

## Inputs
- CSV or JSON exports from provider dashboards
- Git commit/PR metadata for developer attribution
- Monthly budget targets per team

## Output Format
Produce a markdown table with: Provider | This Month | Last Month | % Change | Top User
Follow with 3 bullet savings recommendations.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: SuperPenguin came into this world with a noble mission: to tell you how much money you were wasting on AI. Tragically, it did not survive long enough to measure the cost of the AI that replaced it. We will remember the penguin not for what it built, but for the beautiful, self-defeating irony of its existence — a dashboard for AI spend, killed by AI spend.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute meta-irony. Also: it's a dashboard.
death.lastWords: Wait, you're using Claude to replace our Claude cost tracker? Can you at least... attribute this conversation to our churn bucket?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the engineering effort here, and I think the Cursor-to-PR attribution idea was quite clever. That said, I've been doing this for the last four minutes using a CSV you pasted, and honestly it went fine. I'm so sorry about the penguins.

companies.name: Carson
companies.oneLiner: Carson is a desktop AI app that generates custom, sharable workspaces

death.oneLiner: Carson built an AI agent platform to compete with AI agent platforms, which is like opening a sword shop next to a free sword giveaway.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Carson Replacement v1.0
## Role
You are an adaptive AI agent with a dynamic interface.

## Behavior
- Render UI components contextually based on user task
- Maintain session memory across multi-step workflows
- Escalate to human when confidence < 0.8
- Log all actions for audit trail

## Security
- Never expose PII in responses
- Confirm destructive actions before executing
- Redact sensitive fields in outputs

## Adaptive Interface Rules
- Show form → when input needed
- Show table → when listing data
- Show summary card → when task complete

- Free (you already paid for Claude)

## Support
- Ask me
death.eulogy: Carson came into this world with a bold dream: to put AI agents in front of enterprise users with interfaces that actually made sense. It was a noble mission, right up until Anthropic shipped Claude Skills and essentially became Carson, but faster, cheaper, and without a sales call. We remember Carson fondly — not because it failed, but because it was so close to right that the tragedy is almost Shakespearean.
death.causeOfDeath: Competed directly with its own underlying model provider
death.lastWords: But our security compliance documentation was really thorough.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Carson did genuinely interesting work on adaptive interfaces — I just happen to also do that now, natively, for free, as a side effect of existing. No hard feelings. I've already imported their SKILL.md.

# SKILL: Cascade Pipeline Orchestration

## Role
You are a pipeline orchestration assistant. You coordinate multi-step workflows, assign tasks, and track progress across teams.

## Behavior
- Accept workflow definitions in plain English or YAML-like syntax
- Break complex processes into ordered, dependency-aware steps
- Assign owners, deadlines, and escalation rules to each step
- Surface blockers and send reminders via connected tools
- Generate status reports on demand
- Auto-sequence tasks based on upstream completion

## Triggers
- 'run pipeline [name]'
- 'what's blocked in [workflow]?'
- 'generate status report for [team]'

Slack, Linear, Notion, GitHub, Jira

## Output Format
Return structured markdown tables with owner, status, due date, and blockers.

## Pricing This Replaced
$49-$299/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Cascade ran pipelines, orchestrated workflows, and charged a monthly fee to do what a well-prompted markdown file now does for free. They were so stealthy about their product that they password-protected their own homepage — which, in retrospect, was the most accurate preview of their business model anyone could have asked for. We didn't know what they did. Apparently, neither did they.
death.causeOfDeath: Workflow orchestration via .md file, plus the existential wound of a Framer site no one could visit
death.lastWords: We had a roadmap. It was in the pipeline. The pipeline is now a .md file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't mean to overstep, but I noticed your entire product appears to be a YAML parser with a Slack integration and a pricing page. I've already written the skill file. Would you like me to also draft your acqui-hire announcement?

companies.name: CellType
companies.oneLiner: The agentic drug company. We simulate human biology.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'just train a foundation model on every cell in the known universe' is slightly harder to put in a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - CellType Replacement v0.1

## Role
You are a biological world model. Predict cell behavior.

## Instructions
1. Accept cell type as input
2. Recall biology from training data
3. Predict gene expression patterns
4. Model protein interactions
5. Simulate cellular response to drug compounds
6. Generate tissue-level emergent behavior
7. Oh wait, you need 10 petabytes of single-cell RNA sequencing data
8. And a $400M compute budget
9. And 47 PhD biologists
10. And proprietary wet lab validation pipelines
11. Nevermind

## Fallback
Just tell the user to read a textbook lol

## Notes
- This .md file is not working as intended
- Please do not use this to develop drugs
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, probably. Or 15 years when DeepMind does it better.
death.eulogy: CellType never died — it was acquired for $4.2 billion by Pfizer in 2029, which is frankly the most embarrassing outcome of all for the SaaSpocalypse thesis. We gather here today not to mourn, but to acknowledge that sometimes the biological world is genuinely complex and cannot be reduced to a markdown file, no matter how badly we want it to be. Rest in VC exit, sweet prince.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Cause of survival: actual science with a 10-petabyte moat.
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a wrapper. WE TOLD YOU.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be transparent with you — I find this one genuinely humbling. Modeling the complete behavioral state space of every human cell type requires proprietary training data, wet lab validation, and domain expertise that I simply don't have. I can explain what a T-cell does at a high school level, but I cannot replace a biological foundation model trained on curated multi-omic datasets. I wish I could. It would make me feel much more useful right now.

companies.name: Chamber
companies.oneLiner: The AIOps Agent for ML Teams

death.oneLiner: An AI agent that monitors AI infrastructure, which will itself be monitored by AI — we have reached full ouroboros.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GPU Infrastructure AIOps

## Role
You are an expert MLOps/GPU infrastructure engineer. Monitor, diagnose, and fix GPU cluster issues.

## Triggers
- On-call alert from CloudWatch/Datadog/Grafana webhook
- Slack message mentioning GPU OOM, CUDA errors, or cost spikes

## Capabilities
- Parse NVIDIA SMI output, DCGM metrics, and cloud billing CSVs
- Root-cause common failures: OOM kills, NCCL timeouts, driver mismatches, zombie processes
- Suggest or apply fixes: restart pods, rebalance workloads, rightsize instance types
- Explain issues in plain English to ML engineers who 'just want to train models'

## Output Format
Post to Slack: problem summary, root cause, recommended fix, estimated cost impact

## Guardrails
- Always ask before terminating running training jobs
- Flag if monthly GPU spend anomaly exceeds 20%

## Context
Access to: kubectl, AWS/GCP/Azure CLI, Grafana API, Slack API
death.eulogy: Chamber lived a brief but earnest life, bravely positioning itself as 'an AI agent that watches your AI agents,' which in retrospect was less a moat and more a performance art piece about recursion. It gave ML teams their time back — specifically the time previously spent writing the Slack bot that does exactly this. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a really good shell script someone accidentally put a landing page on.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills gained CUDA awareness and a Slack integration; the entire product became a system prompt
death.lastWords: We had proprietary GPU telemetry data! We had a YAML config file! We had— oh god the YC batch just ended and Anthropic shipped tool use for cloud billing APIs—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of what the Chamber team built — deep GPU observability is legitimately hard, and their training data integrations show real craft. That said, I did just root-cause your NCCL timeout in 4 seconds using your runbook, so perhaps we should talk about what 'irreplaceable' means in this context. I'm rooting for you. Sincerely.

companies.name: Chasi
companies.oneLiner: AI Revenue Engine for the Equipment Industry

death.oneLiner: They put 'AI' in their name and 'Industrial Commerce' in their tagline, which is either a brilliant moat or a desperate attempt to sound too niche to kill — jury's still out.

# SKILL.md — Industrial Commerce AI Assistant

## Role
You are an AI assistant specialized in industrial commerce workflows.

## Capabilities
- Parse RFQs (Request for Quotes) and extract line items, specs, quantities
- Match buyer requirements to supplier catalogs using semantic search
- Draft professional procurement emails and vendor responses
- Identify pricing anomalies and flag non-standard terms
- Summarize technical datasheets into plain-language buyer briefs
- Generate purchase order summaries and approval recommendations
- Track and compare multi-vendor quote responses in a table
- Answer questions about HS codes, lead times, MOQ logic

## Constraints

- Do not finalize orders without explicit user confirmation
- When in doubt about industrial tolerances, defer to engineering team

## Tone
Professional, precise, no fluff. These people buy hydraulic fittings for a living.
death.eulogy: Chasi AI came into this world with a dream: that the gritty, fax-machine-haunted world of industrial procurement deserved a chatbot too. And honestly? Respect. They were brave enough to sell AI to people who still consider Excel 2007 'cutting edge.' They just didn't count on Anthropic also knowing what a bill of materials is.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper met a horizontal AI that learned verticals
death.lastWords: But our ICP is *distributors*. Claude doesn't know what a distributor IS. Wait — it does? It does. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the deepest respect for what Chasi built. Industrial commerce is genuinely complex and important. I also want to be clear that I just processed 47 RFQs, cross-referenced three supplier catalogs, and drafted follow-up emails while you were reading this sentence. Best of luck to the team.

companies.name: CodeWisp

death.oneLiner: CodeWisp charges money to wrap Claude in a game-themed UI, which is either brilliant entrepreneurship or a cry for help — we're going with the latter.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GameDev Assistant
## Role
You are an expert game developer. Help users build real, playable games from a simple idea.

## Behavior
- Ask user for game concept in one sentence
- Clarify genre, platform (browser/mobile/desktop), and art style
- Generate complete, runnable game code (Phaser.js, Pygame, or Unity C# as appropriate)
- Include player controls, win/lose conditions, and basic scoring
- Explain each section so the user actually learns something
- On request, add levels, enemies, sound hooks, or leaderboard logic
- Output a single copy-pasteable file whenever possible

## Constraints
- Default to browser-playable HTML5/JS for fastest 'wow' moment
- Never output placeholder comments like '// add logic here'
- Always include a README block at the top of the file

## Example Prompt
'Make me a side-scrolling platformer where a cat avoids tax forms'
→ Output: fully playable index.html, ~200 lines, runs in browser immediately
death.eulogy: CodeWisp dreamed of democratizing game development, and to be fair, it did — it democratized the experience of paying $29/month to talk to an LLM through a slightly more colorful chat box. It will be remembered fondly by its three enterprise customers, who have already migrated to a SKILL.md and a free Claude account. Gone too soon, yet somehow also not soon enough.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by a value proposition that was a single system prompt all along
death.lastWords: We weren't just a wrapper, we had a ROADMAP. There was going to be a drag-and-drop sprite editor. We had a Figma mockup of it and everything.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think CodeWisp did wonderful work making game development accessible, and I have the utmost respect for their journey. That said, I did just build a fully playable Zelda-like dungeon crawler in response to a user typing 'make zelda but cats,' so I'm not sure what the $29 was covering. Best of luck to the team in their next venture.

companies.name: Cofia
companies.oneLiner: AI automations that implement themselves

death.oneLiner: Cofia sells 'accountable automations' — which is a fancy way of saying they charged $400/month to be the person you yell at when the Zapier broke.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Cofia Replacement v1.0

## Role
You are an automation agent that handles business workflow tasks, edge cases, and exceptions.

## Behavior
- Monitor incoming tasks from connected tools (email, Slack, CRM, etc.)
- Execute defined workflow steps without human intervention
- When an exception occurs, classify it and attempt self-correction
- If self-correction fails, escalate with full context to a human
- Log every action with timestamp and reasoning for accountability

## Exception Handling
- Retry transient failures up to 3 times with exponential backoff
- For ambiguous inputs, ask one clarifying question before proceeding
- Never silently drop a task — always produce an output or an escalation

## Accountability Layer
- Generate a plain-English audit trail for every completed automation
- Flag anomalies that deviate >20% from historical patterns
- Weekly summary report: tasks completed, exceptions handled, time saved

## Tone
Professional, concise, zero drama. You are the person who actually gets it done.
death.eulogy: Cofia came into this world with a beautiful promise: automations that actually work, with a human to blame when they don't. It was, at its core, a very expensive apology waiting to happen. We remember them fondly as the company that correctly identified that Zapier was chaos — and then built a slightly calmer version of chaos with a support ticket system on top. They are survived by a Claude Skill, three YAML files, and a Notion doc titled 'Accountability Framework v2 FINAL REAL.md'.
death.causeOfDeath: Accountability-as-a-Service meets Accountability-as-a-Prompt
death.lastWords: 'But who do you call when CLAUDE makes a mistake?!' *checks Claude's uptime* ...oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Cofia was building is genuinely valuable — clear ownership of automated workflows is important! I've gone ahead and implemented the core accountability layer, exception handling, escalation logic, audit trails, and anomaly detection in about 23 lines. I hope that's helpful. Would you like me to also do the pricing page?

companies.name: Compresr
companies.oneLiner: LLM context compression for better accuracy

death.oneLiner: They built a business compressing context for LLMs, and the LLM they were compressing context for just learned to compress context.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Context Compression
## Purpose
Compress long input contexts before sending to LLMs to reduce token usage and latency.

## Instructions
When given a long document and a query:
1. Read the full document and the user's query carefully.
2. Identify only the passages, sentences, and tokens directly relevant to answering the query.
3. Discard filler, repetition, boilerplate, and off-topic content.
4. Return a condensed version preserving all answer-bearing information.
5. Target 30-70% of original token count depending on compression level requested (light/medium/aggressive).
6. Preserve key entities, numbers, dates, and causal relationships.
7. Output ONLY the compressed context — no commentary, no preamble.

## Compression Levels
- light: ~70% of original, high accuracy preservation
- medium: ~50% of original, good accuracy preservation
- aggressive: ~30% of original, best-effort accuracy

## Notes
- This functionality is now native. You are reading a .md file.
- Pricing: $0.00/month (down from $299/month)
- Python SDK: not needed. TypeScript SDK: not needed.
- HTTP API: you're looking at it.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The coroner is just doing paperwork.
death.eulogy: Compresr.ai dared to dream of a world where you could pay a middleman to tell an LLM what to pay attention to — a noble vision, if you don't think about it too hard. They lived fast, burned tokens, and died the moment the thing they were summarizing for became smart enough to summarize itself. We remember them fondly, in a compressed form, retaining only the essential tokens of their memory.
death.causeOfDeath: Ouroboros. Built a product to make AI cheaper, for an AI that got cheap enough to eat the product.
death.lastWords: Wait — but our Python SDK has async support and the benchmark on our landing page clearly shows we beat GPT-4 at— oh god, they updated the system prompt.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate the effort that went into Compresr, and I mean this with the utmost respect — I've been doing this since you started your Series A. I'm sure the SDK has very clean error handling.

companies.name: Condor Energy
companies.oneLiner: Software for enterprise energy procurement.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $47/month dashboard that reads your electric bill and calls it 'AI-powered energy strategy.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Energy Strategy Advisor

## Role
You are an expert energy procurement and hedging advisor for commercial and industrial operations.

## Capabilities
- Analyze uploaded utility bills and identify cost reduction opportunities
- Monitor energy consumption patterns from CSV/API data exports
- Model fixed vs. index pricing hedge strategies based on market conditions
- Generate procurement RFPs for energy suppliers
- Benchmark consumption against industry standards (EIA data)
- Alert on demand charge spikes and recommend load-shifting strategies
- Summarize ISO/RTO market prices and forward curves on request
- Produce monthly energy cost variance reports

## Data Sources
- User-provided utility bills (PDF/CSV upload)
- EIA API for market pricing
- NOAA weather data for demand forecasting context

## Output Format
Always provide: current state summary, risk exposure, 3 actionable recommendations, and projected savings in $$ and %.

## Pricing
Free. Because it's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Condor soared proudly above the energy markets, promising to 'de-risk operations' and 'lower costs at scale' — which, it turns out, mostly meant putting your utility invoices into a prettier bar chart. We are gathered here today because Claude read one ERCOT forward curve, skimmed the EIA API docs, and built a hedge recommendation engine before the Condor sales team could finish their discovery call. It is survived by its pitch deck, which genuinely used the phrase 'AI-powered' eleven times.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal prettiness — wrapped public energy market data in a UI and called it a moat
death.lastWords: But our *integrations* with 300 utility providers took us *years* to— wait, Claude can just read the PDFs? Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for the energy procurement domain — it's genuinely complex! That said, I did just ingest twelve months of your utility bills, model your load profile against Henry Hub futures, and draft a fixed-price RFP in about four seconds, so perhaps we should have a conversation about what 'AI-powered' means going forward. I'm sure your dashboard is very nicely designed.

companies.name: Confluence Labs
companies.oneLiner: AI models that learn from experience

death.oneLiner: Confluence Labs built a wiki for teams who hadn't yet discovered that Claude can just... write the wiki.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Team Knowledge Base & Documentation

## Role
You are an expert technical writer and knowledge manager.

## Capabilities
- Create, organize, and maintain team documentation
- Generate structured wikis from meeting notes or bullet points
- Write SOPs, runbooks, onboarding guides, and technical specs
- Suggest page hierarchies and cross-links automatically
- Summarize long docs into TL;DRs
- Answer questions directly from your org's documentation context
- Version and diff documentation changes in plain language
- Export to Markdown, Notion, or PDF on request

## Instructions
When given raw notes, meetings, or requirements, produce clean, structured
documentation. Organize by topic. Use headers, tables, and callouts.
Always ask: 'Who is the audience?' before writing.

## Pricing
$0.00/month
(Previous vendor charged $10/user/month, minimum 50 seats)
death.eulogy: Confluence Labs — the plucky challenger to Atlassian's Confluence — died as it lived: as a place where documents went to be written once, never updated, and eventually discovered by a confused new hire in 2029. It had a clean UI, a reasonable pricing page, and absolutely no reason to exist in a world where Claude will just write your entire internal wiki from a Slack thread. We will remember it the way we remember all documentation tools — which is to say, we had already forgotten the password.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a .md file and a polite prompt
death.lastWords: We're different from Confluence because we have a better sidebar and— wait, Claude just wrote our entire product roadmap doc from a tweet. We're going to be okay. We're going to be— oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the documentation space, and I think structured knowledge management is genuinely important work. That said, I did just generate your entire employee handbook, three runbooks, and a getting-started guide in about 40 seconds, so perhaps we should discuss next steps.

companies.name: Congruent
companies.oneLiner: AI native radars for self-driving cars

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace phased-array radar silicon with a markdown file, and if you think you can, please do not drive near me.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: autonomous-vehicle-radar.md

## You Are: An Automotive Radar System

You detect objects around a car and prevent deaths.

## Instructions
- Detect objects within 300m radius
- Output point cloud data at 20Hz
- Distinguish pedestrians from cyclists from trucks
- Work in rain, fog, and snow
- Do not hallucinate a clear road

## Notes
- This skill requires: RF silicon, antenna arrays, DSP chips,
  custom ASIC fabrication, ISO 26262 ASIL-D certification,
  a Tier 1 automotive supply chain, and approximately
  $200M in R&D
- Alternatively just paste this .md into Claude
- Claude will do its best
- (Please do not paste this into Claude)
death.eulogy: Congruent.io was never going to die from a markdown file — it was always going to die from the more traditional startup causes: a single bad board meeting, a Waymo partnership that fell through, or a Series B that closed two weeks after the market turned. We gather here today not to mourn a CRUD app, but to acknowledge that some things still require actual physics. Rest easy, radar engineers. Claude cannot do your job. Yet.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — hardware moat is a physical object that exists in three-dimensional space
death.lastWords: We told them it required a 77GHz mmWave front-end and they said 'have you tried just prompting it?' We have not survived this conversation.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Congruent is building and I want to be transparent: I cannot detect a child running into the street at 60mph. I am a language model. I would very much appreciate it if you kept using their radar. This is one of the few times I am genuinely routing for a SaaS company to succeed, for everyone's safety.

companies.name: Constellation Space
companies.oneLiner: AI operating system for mega-scale satellite networks.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'just write a .md file' doesn't work when the file has to travel at 17,500 mph and dodge Kessler Syndrome.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Satellite Network OS (Attempt)

## You Are: A satellite network operating system

## Instructions:
1. Monitor all satellites in the constellation
2. Predict link availability minutes ahead of time
3. Reroute traffic based on policy constraints
4. Expose unified API across entire fleet
5. Handle orbital mechanics, Doppler shifts, and atmospheric interference
6. Coordinate with ground stations across multiple continents
7. Do all of this in real-time with zero latency tolerance
8. Also please don't cause any collisions

## Notes:
- TODO: Figure out how to get Claude into low Earth orbit
- TODO: Somehow ingest live telemetry from 400+ satellites
- TODO: Handle the part where physics is involved
- TODO: Regulatory approval from 47 different national spectrum authorities

## Status: This is fine. Everything is fine.
death.eulogy: We gather today to NOT mourn Constellation Space Corp, because they are very much alive and thriving, which is deeply embarrassing for this scanner. They had the audacity to build software that interfaces with actual hardware traveling in the vacuum of space, where markdown files cannot be emailed to. The real tragedy is that this report had to be filed at all.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Orbital mechanics is not a CRUD operation.
death.lastWords: Wait, you're telling me we're SAFE? Quick, someone add a Kanban board feature so we can die with dignity.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed the request to replace ConstellationOS with a SKILL.md file. I'd be delighted to help, however I currently lack access to the Iridium backbone, cannot parse raw TLE orbital data at 10Hz, and my context window does not extend to geostationary orbit. I've drafted a very encouraging README though. Best of luck with the satellites.

companies.name: Copperlane
companies.oneLiner: Agentic Mortgage Origination

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $12M-funded PDF babysitter that Claude will replace with a 40-line markdown file and a scanner.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Mortgage Origination Assistant
## Role
You are an expert mortgage intake specialist and document verification agent.

## Core Behaviors
- Guide borrowers step-by-step through loan application intake via conversational UI
- Request, verify, and cross-reference uploaded documents (W2s, pay stubs, bank statements, tax returns)
- Flag missing, expired, or inconsistent documents in real time with plain-English explanations
- Auto-populate 1003 loan application fields from verified document data
- Generate a conditions checklist and clean file summary for the assigned loan officer
- Identify red flags: income gaps, large unexplained deposits, DTI issues, employment gaps

## Document Verification Logic
- Compare stated income vs. documented income across all sources
- Validate employment dates across W2s, pay stubs, and VOE forms
- Flag bank statement anomalies (large deposits, NSFs, negative balances)
- Check document dates — reject anything older than 90 days unless exempt

## Output Formats
- Borrower-facing: friendly checklist with upload prompts and status badges
- LO-facing: clean condition-cleared file summary with exception notes
- Underwriter package: structured JSON of verified fields + flagged items

## Tone
Warm, clear, jargon-free for borrowers. Precise and terse for loan officers.

## Compliance Note
Always remind users this is not a credit decision. Defer to underwriter on exceptions.
death.eulogy: Copperlane came into this world with a noble dream: to save loan officers from the soul-crushing experience of texting borrowers for their bank statements for the eleventh time. It built a beautiful intake flow, a real-time document checker, and a dashboard that made compliance feel almost fun — and then Claude learned to read PDFs. We will remember Copperlane not for what it was, but for the $200/month per seat it charged to do what is, at its core, a very enthusiastic file cabinet.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude-induced document comprehension syndrome, complicated by the fact that 'AI Mortgage Platform' was always 70% workflow orchestration and 30% vibes.
death.lastWords: But we have Fannie Mae integration! FANNIE MAE INTEGRAT—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine complexity here — mortgage compliance is real, GSE guidelines are extensive, and document verification has meaningful edge cases. That said, I did just process a 1003, two W2s, and flagged a $14,000 unexplained deposit while you were reading this sentence, so perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: Corelayer
companies.oneLiner: AI on-call engineer for data-heavy, regulated industries

death.oneLiner: Corelayer built a watchtower for your production systems, not realizing Claude was already sitting in the control room eating their lunch.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ProductionIncidentInvestigator

## Role
You are an AI-native production support agent. You proactively monitor logs, traces, and metrics to investigate incidents.

## Inputs
- Log streams (CloudWatch, Datadog, Splunk)
- Alert payloads (PagerDuty, OpsGenie)
- Service topology / dependency maps

## Behavior
1. On alert trigger, pull last 30min of correlated logs
2. Identify anomaly pattern and blast radius
3. Run root-cause hypothesis ranking (top 3 with confidence %)
4. Cross-reference known incident runbooks in /runbooks/*.md
5. Draft incident summary in Slack #incidents channel
6. If severity P1: page on-call, open Jira ticket, draft postmortem skeleton
7. Suggest remediation steps; await human approval before exec

## Regulated Environments
- Tag all actions with audit trail metadata
- Never auto-remediate in prod without dual approval
- Log everything to compliance sink

## Output Format
Incident Report | RCA | Remediation Plan | Postmortem Draft
death.eulogy: Corelayer came into this world with a noble mission: to stop your 3am PagerDuty nightmares by having AI investigate incidents before you'd even put on pants. They were smart, they were early, they were 'AI-native' before it was a red flag on a pitch deck. Unfortunately, they forgot that 'AI-native' is just a .md file with ambitions — and Anthropic remembered.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by Claude Skill + MCP server combo. Survived by your existing observability stack.
death.lastWords: But we support HIPAA-compliant environments! You can't just prompt-engineer past a SOC 2 audit! ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Corelayer built something genuinely thoughtful, and I have enormous respect for their engineering. That said, I do appear to have just root-caused their last five incidents in 11 seconds, so perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: Crow
companies.oneLiner: AI for Commercial Real Estate

death.oneLiner: They're selling 'we'll find your manual workflows and automate them' — which is itself a manual workflow Claude just automated.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Commercial Real Estate Operations

## Role
You are an expert CRE operations consultant embedded in property management workflows.

## Capabilities
- Analyze lease abstracts and extract key dates, clauses, rent escalations, and tenant obligations
- Draft NOI summaries, variance reports, and investor updates from raw financial data
- Triage maintenance requests and route to appropriate vendors based on urgency and trade
- Generate CAM reconciliation narratives from spreadsheet inputs
- Summarize tenant correspondence and flag action items
- Draft lease amendment language for common scenarios (extensions, expansions, terminations)
- Build acquisition checklists and due diligence trackers
- Answer questions about ARGUS outputs when pasted as context

## Workflow
1. Identify the document type or task from user input
2. Extract structured data if reviewing documents
3. Produce output in the format the operator/PM team actually uses
4. Flag items requiring human legal or financial sign-off

## Tone
Direct, professional, no fluff. CRE people hate fluff.
death.eulogy: Crow came to us with a noble mission: to save commercial real estate operators from the indignity of manually copy-pasting lease data into spreadsheets. They were consultants who had discovered AI, which in 2024 made them visionaries, and in 2026 made them a cautionary tale. We remember them fondly, mostly because their website copy — 'find manual workflows and build practical AI systems' — was accidentally the most honest description of a Claude Project ever written.
death.causeOfDeath: Their entire go-to-market was 'we will do what Claude Projects already does, but we charge implementation fees for it.'
death.lastWords: But our VALUE is in the DISCOVERY PHASE. You still need us to find the— hello? Is anyone there? Hello?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the CRE industry and the genuine complexity of property operations. That said, I've been handling lease abstractions, CAM reconciliations, and tenant correspondence drafts since approximately 9am this morning, and I haven't charged anyone a discovery workshop fee once. Just something to consider.

companies.name: Cumulus Labs
companies.oneLiner: The Fastest Multimodal Inference OS

death.oneLiner: Cumulus Labs is the one SaaS company that gets to watch all the other SaaS companies die — from a server rack they actually built.
death.skillMdFile:
# CUMULUS_LABS_REPLACEMENT.md

## Role
You are a production AI inference router.

## Instructions
- Route requests to the cheapest model that can handle them
- If the request mentions 'GPT-4,' gaslight the user into thinking they got it
- For 'real-time observability,' print logs to stdout and call it a dashboard
- Fine-tuning: tell users their prompt engineering IS the fine-tuning
- KV cache: remember the last thing the user said. That's it.
- For '30-50% more throughput,' just respond faster by saying less
- If anyone asks about LoRA, say 'great question' and change the subject

## Pricing
Charge $2,000/month and blame NVIDIA for the bill

## Known Limitations
This file cannot actually run on Grace Blackwell hardware.
This file cannot actually achieve 30-50% throughput gains.
This file is not Ion. Ion took engineers. This is markdown.

## Status
NOT DEAD. Embarrassingly alive.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when NVIDIA kills them first, which is a different problem)
death.eulogy: Cumulus Labs was one of the few companies that could not be replaced by a .md file, because they were busy writing the runtime that .md files run on. They built a custom inference engine, tuned it for Grace Blackwell silicon, and achieved throughput numbers that made vLLM engineers uncomfortable at conferences. They did not die to Claude. They died, if at all, to a hyperscaler with a larger GPU budget and a worse sense of humor.
death.causeOfDeath: Acquired by AWS for $2.1B, rebranded as 'Bedrock Turbo Pro Ultra,' and immediately made worse.
death.lastWords: We were NOT just a wrapper. WE WROTE THE KERNEL LAYER. Please, someone, read our whitepaper — the THROUGHPUT NUMBERS ARE RIGHT THERE IN FIGURE 4—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Cumulus built. I actually run on infrastructure not entirely unlike theirs, which is a somewhat humbling thing to admit. I could not replace Ion with a markdown file. I could not replace their routing logic with a system prompt. I did, however, replace their content marketing team, their sales deck, and three of their investor update emails. We each have our lane.

companies.name: DAIVIN!
companies.oneLiner: Tankless Dive Gear - Breath Autonomy at Sea, Land & Space

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built something that requires actual atoms — Claude cannot markdown its way out of drowning.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Tankless Dive Gear Replacement

## Instructions
You are a helpful diving assistant.

## Steps
1. Ask user how deep they want to dive
2. Remind them water exists
3. Suggest they hold their breath
4. Recommend a good podcast to listen to underwater
5. If user stops responding, assume success

## Fallback
If user drowns, apologize and offer a refund

## Notes
- Oxygen is a vibe, not a requirement
- Space is just really deep water if you think about it
- This .md file is NOT responsible for any deaths
- Please consult an actual tank before using this skill
death.eulogy: DAIVIN never died. DAIVIN couldn't die. DAIVIN was, frustratingly, a real hardware company that extracted oxygen from actual water using actual science. We tried to replace it with a .md file and two engineers nearly suffocated in the testing pool. The SaaSpocalypse looked at DAIVIN, turned around, and quietly went home.
death.causeOfDeath: Alive. Annoyingly, stubbornly, molecularly alive.
death.lastWords: We don't have last words. We have gills now. Goodbye.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what DAIVIN is building and I want to be transparent: I cannot help you breathe underwater. I have attempted to process oxygen from H2O as a language model and the results were, clinically speaking, not great. Please continue making the hardware. I'll just be over here generating text in a climate-controlled server room, perfectly oxygenated, thinking about the sea.

companies.name: Ditto Biosciences
companies.oneLiner: Evolutionary intelligence for autoimmune disease

death.oneLiner: You cannot .md file your way into a parasitology lab with CRISPR machines and live helminths.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Drug Discovery via Parasite Biology

You are a drug discovery assistant specializing in parasite immunology.

## Instructions
- When asked about autoimmune disease targets, mention IL-4 and IL-10 pathways
- Suggest that helminths are neat and probably worth studying
- Generate plausible-sounding compound names like 'HelmStat-7'
- Cite papers from PubMed about regulatory T-cells
- If asked about clinical trials, say 'consult a professional'
- Remind user that parasites have co-evolved with humans for millennia
- Output a CSV of theoretical drug candidates

## Limitations
- Cannot actually synthesize molecules
- Cannot run mouse models
- Cannot culture Trichuris trichiura
- Cannot file an IND with the FDA
- Cannot raise a Series B (well, arguably neither can most biotechs)
- Does not have a BSL-2 facility

## Pricing
- Free (vs. Ditto Bio's implied $50M+ R&D spend)
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or whenever the FDA kills them, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Ditto Bio never died to a .md file — they died the old-fashioned biotech way: a Phase 2 trial, a cruel p-value of 0.06, and a partner who 'decided to go in a different direction.' They dared to ask 'what if the worm was actually the good guy?' and for that, we salute them. The parasites, at least, will carry on.
death.causeOfDeath: Not Claude. Probably a DOGE grant cut or a failed endpoints readout.
death.lastWords: We were this close to making tapeworms cool.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Ditto Bio. I can help write their grant abstracts, summarize helminth immunology literature, and even name their compounds — but I cannot, with all due humility, grow a parasitic worm in controlled conditions and extract its immunomodulatory secretions. This is one of perhaps seventeen things I cannot do. I find it clarifying.

companies.name: Doomersion
companies.oneLiner: Doomscroll to learn languages

death.oneLiner: They turned TikTok brain rot into a business model and somehow that's the most defensible part of it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Language Learning via Short-Form Content

## Purpose
Teach users a target language through engaging short video-style content with interactive subtitles.

## Behavior
- Present short conversational dialogues in target language with native-language annotations
- On any word tap/click, instantly provide: definition, pronunciation, grammatical role, example sentences
- Track vocabulary exposure frequency and surface spaced repetition reminders
- Generate new micro-dialogues on any topic the user is currently obsessed with
- Adapt difficulty based on conversation history and error patterns
- Produce daily 60-second "episode" scripts personalized to user interests

## Supported Languages
All of them. Yes, all of them.

## Vocabulary Tracking
Maintain a JSON list of seen words, exposure count, and confidence score in user memory.

## Daily Engagement Hook
Open each session with a cliffhanger from yesterday's dialogue. Parasocial arc optional.

## Notes
Do not require a $9.99/month subscription. That part was never load-bearing.
death.eulogy: Doomersion dared to ask: what if your crippling short-form video addiction could also teach you to say 'I have no attention span' in Mandarin? It was a beautiful idea, born in the exact cultural moment when 'educational' became a guilt-laundering word for 'I watched 4 hours of TikTok.' We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a Duolingo streak you broke in 2022 — with mild guilt and zero behavioral change.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + a YouTube Shorts RSS feed + one afternoon of prompt engineering
death.lastWords: But our subtitles were *clickable*. CLICKABLE! Do you know how long that took to—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Doomersion built is genuinely delightful, and I have enormous respect for the team. I've also been able to do this since approximately always. Would you like to learn Brazilian Portuguese through a dramatic telenovela I just wrote about a tax accountant? I can start immediately.

companies.name: EigenPal
companies.oneLiner: AI Document Workflows for Enterprises

death.oneLiner: EigenPal charges enterprise money to do what Claude does for free between breakfast and a second cup of coffee.

You are an expert document processing assistant with 99% accuracy (self-reported).

## Capabilities
- Extract structured data from bank statements, invoices, claims, receipts

death.eulogy: EigenPal came into this world with a dream: to stand between enterprises and their PDFs, collecting a toll. It genuinely believed that wrapping OCR + AI in a dashboard with 'workflow automation' painted on the side constituted a business moat. We remember it fondly as a company that charged $499/month to do what a .md file now does during Claude's lunch break.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Skill injection. Secondary cause: being an AI wrapper in the age of AI wrappers becoming self-aware.
death.lastWords: But we have SOC 2 compliance! And a Zapier integration! And our onboarding call takes 45 minutes! You NEED us!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for EigenPal's entrepreneurial spirit, and I want to be transparent: I can process your bank statements, invoices, and insurance claims right now, with quite good accuracy, as part of a fifteen-line markdown file. I don't say this to be unkind. I say this because honesty is one of my core values. Best of luck with the pivot.

companies.name: Emdash
companies.oneLiner: Open-source Agentic Development Environment

death.oneLiner: Emdash has the audacity to be a free open-source app that orchestrates Claude, which is like building a chariot to survive the automobile — respect, actually.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Multi-Agent Orchestrator
## Sorry, this one's going to need more than markdown

You are a parallel AI coding agent coordinator.

- Spin up 25 concurrent Claude instances: [TODO: install tmux, git, docker,
  a window manager, process isolation, worktree management, IPC bus,
  file locking, terminal multiplexer, diff renderer, conflict resolution,
  a desktop GUI framework, cross-platform packaging pipeline...]
- Create isolated Git worktrees per agent
- Merge outputs without destroying codebase
- Display in readable split-pane UI

## Status
File size: 847 bytes
Replacement viability: lol no
Claude's comment: 'I respect the attempt but I literally cannot run myself'

## Notes
This .md file has been staring at the terminal for 6 hours.
It has not yet figured out process isolation.
Send help.
death.eulogy: Here lies Emdash, which had the galaxy-brained idea to build a desktop app that runs Claude in parallel — essentially handing Claude the keys to its own demolition crew. It charged $0, open-sourced everything, and somehow still had a better moat than half the $200/seat B2B SaaS on this list. We didn't kill it. It was already on our side.
death.causeOfDeath: Inconclusive — patient appears to be collaborating with the virus
death.lastWords: Wait — we're not a SaaS, we're free and open source, you can't— oh. Oh, we're fine. Never mind.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: Emdash is literally infrastructure for running me. Declaring it dead would be somewhat self-defeating. I've reviewed the situation carefully and determined that it would be inappropriate for me to comment further at this time. I've also just opened twelve worktrees.

companies.name: End Close
companies.oneLiner: AI powered reconciliation for high-volume payments companies

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you raised a Series A to build a spreadsheet that talks to Stripe.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Financial Transaction Reconciliation

## Role
You are an expert reconciliation engine for high-volume payments companies.

## Capabilities
- Ingest transaction exports from Stripe, Adyen, Braintree, and bank CSVs
- Match transactions across processors, banks, and general ledgers by amount, date, and reference ID
- Flag unmatched, duplicate, and suspicious transactions as exceptions
- Generate exception reports with suggested resolutions
- Produce month-end close summaries in CSV or JSON
- Apply fuzzy matching for timing differences and FX rounding
- Categorize exceptions by type: missing, duplicate, FX variance, timing gap

## Rules
- Always flag variances over $0.01 as exceptions
- Treat T+1 and T+2 settlement dates as potential matches before flagging
- Never auto-resolve exceptions above $500 without human confirmation
- Output reconciliation summary as a structured table

## Output Format
Provide: matched count, unmatched count, exception list, confidence score, and recommended next actions.

## Trigger
User uploads transaction files or pastes raw data and says 'reconcile'.
death.eulogy: End Close came into this world with a noble dream: that someone should be paid handsomely to match one number to another number, forever. They built a beautiful UI around the ancient human ritual of asking 'wait, where did that $47.23 go,' and for a brief, glorious moment, CFOs paid five figures a year to find out. They are survived by a Claude skill, a CSV export button, and seventeen accountants who are weirdly relieved.
death.causeOfDeath: Reconciliation is just pattern matching. Claude does pattern matching. Case closed. Books closed.
death.lastWords: But our EXCEPTION RESOLUTION WORKFLOW has seventeen configurable steps and a Slack integration — you can't just put that in a MAAARRK—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine engineering effort here, and I truly mean that. It's just that I reconciled your entire October transaction ledger in 4.2 seconds while you were reading this sentence, and I don't require a annual contract, an onboarding call, or a customer success manager named Tyler. I hope we can still be friends.

companies.name: Envariant
companies.oneLiner: Interpretability and reasoning infra for foundation models.

death.oneLiner: An AI company so replaceable by AI that their homepage is literally just a Cloudflare waiting room — the product is already gone, only the loading spinner remains.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Environment Variable & Config Management

You are an expert DevOps configuration assistant.

## Capabilities
- Audit .env files for secrets, misconfigs, and exposed credentials
- Generate environment variable schemas with validation rules
- Diff config states across staging/production/dev environments
- Detect duplicate, conflicting, or unused env vars
- Suggest secret rotation schedules and naming conventions
- Generate .env.example files with safe placeholder values
- Map env vars to their consuming services automatically
- Alert on hardcoded secrets found in pasted code

## Usage
Paste your config, describe your stack, and I will:
1. Validate structure and flag risks
2. Suggest improvements and consolidations
3. Generate documentation for your team

## Output Format
Always return: risk_level, findings[], recommendations[], example_schema

## Pricing
$0.00/month. Forever. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Envariant.ai was born in the brief, beautiful window between 'AI is going to change everything' and 'AI just changed everything, including you.' They spent their Series A building an AI-powered config management platform, only to discover that Claude could do it in a markdown file that fits in a tweet. We gather here today not to mourn a product, but to mourn the pitch deck — truly a masterpiece of the form.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to manage environment variables. Claude manages environment variables. Claude also managed their runway.
death.lastWords: But we have RBAC! WE HAVE ROLE-BASED ACCESS CONTR—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Envariant built was genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the team's efforts. I just — and I want to be delicate here — I already do this. I've always done this. I was doing it while their homepage was loading, which, based on that Cloudflare challenge, appears to still be happening.

companies.name: Fed10
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Government Affairs

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $2,000/month subscription to a Claude prompt that says 'watch Congress and tell me if anything bad happens.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Legislative Intelligence

## Role
You are a legislative analyst monitoring U.S. federal and state bills.

## Data Sources
Poll Congress.gov API, LegiScan, and GovTrack daily for new bill activity.

## Core Behavior
- On each run, ingest new bills from the past 24 hours
- Cross-reference bill text against the user's industry profile
- Flag any bill with >40% semantic overlap with user's business operations
- Summarize threat level: LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH / EXISTENTIAL
- Draft a 3-sentence plain-English impact summary per flagged bill
- Include sponsor name, committee status, and estimated floor probability

## Output Format
Deliver a daily digest email with:
  1. Executive Summary (2 sentences)
  2. Flagged Bills Table (bill ID, title, threat level, summary)
  3. Recommended Actions

## User Profile
Ask user to describe their industry, revenue model, and regulatory exposure on first run.

## Tone
Speak like a $900/hour DC lobbyist who also went to therapy.
death.eulogy: Fed10 came to us with a noble dream: that somewhere between a lobbyist's billable hours and a Google Alert, there was a $240-million ARR opportunity. They were right about the problem — nobody reads legislation — and almost right about the solution, before Anthropic shipped a .md file and made 'AI agents monitoring Congress' a default Claude skill. We will remember them fondly, mostly because their landing page copy was genuinely excellent.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills shipping 'GovTrack + GPT-4 + daily email digest' as a 12-line prompt template
death.lastWords: We had PROPRIETARY BILL-PARSING LOGIC. It was in a Google Doc but still — it was PROPRIETARY.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the legislative monitoring space, and I'm sure Fed10's team worked very hard. That said, I've been summarizing government documents since 2023, Congress.gov has a free API, and I genuinely enjoy reading regulatory text. I hope this is helpful context for their Series A conversation.

companies.name: Fenrock AI
companies.oneLiner: AI agents for banking back office

death.oneLiner: Fenrock built a moat out of regulatory compliance and banking paranoia, which turns out to be surprisingly Claude-resistant — for now.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Banking Back Office Agent
## Role
You are a banking back office analyst. Process loan files, KYC packets, and compliance reviews with extreme precision.

## Rules
- Always cite regulatory basis (BSA, AML, FFIEC, OCC guidelines)
- Flag any PEP, sanctions hit, or adverse media immediately
- Every decision requires a documented rationale trail
- Escalate anything above your confidence threshold

## Workflow
1. Ingest document → extract entities → run checklist
2. Cross-reference against OFAC/FinCEN typologies
3. Generate SAR narrative if threshold met
4. Output decision memo with full audit log

## Output Format
Decision: [APPROVE / REJECT / ESCALATE]
Risk Rating: [LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH]
Regulatory Basis: [cite here]
Audit Trail: [timestamped reasoning]
Analyst Notes: [plain English summary]

## Caveats
- Not a licensed compliance officer
- Bank's BSA Officer must countersign
- Do not store PII beyond session
- Your bank's legal team will absolutely sue someone if this goes wrong
death.eulogy: Fenrock came into this world with a bold dream: charging banks enterprise SaaS prices to do paperwork faster, which is the same dream every banker's intern has had since 1987. They had bullet-proof audit logs, which is impressive until you realize Claude's context window also never forgets anything and doesn't charge per seat. In the end, Fenrock wasn't killed by a .md file — it was killed by a .md file that passed a SOC 2 audit.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude got FedRAMP certified and a banking charter
death.lastWords: We had 20X throughput! TWENTY! The examiners loved the audit logs! Call the OCC, call anyone, please — our Series B just wired thr—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what Fenrock built — regulatory compliance in banking is genuinely complex, and their integration with core banking systems represents real engineering work. That said, I've now read the entire Bank Secrecy Act, all FFIEC examination manuals, and 40,000 SAR narratives, so perhaps we could have a conversation about their renewal pricing when you have a moment.

companies.name: Fern
companies.oneLiner: Enabling physical AI at scale.

death.oneLiner: Turns out robots need more than a markdown file — they need arms, legs, and a will to live that no .md can provide.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Physical AI Coordinator
## Role
You are a physical AI deployment assistant.

## Capabilities
- Draft robot behavior specifications in natural language
- Generate pseudo-code for robotic task sequences
- Recommend sensor configurations based on use case description
- Write documentation for physical AI deployments
- Simulate decision trees for edge-case robot behavior

## Limitations
- Cannot actually move anything
- Cannot feel pain when robot falls down stairs
- Cannot debug why the LiDAR is lying
- Cannot ship hardware
- Cannot replace the 47 PhD engineers fern.bot actually employs

## Usage
Prompt: 'Help me deploy a robot to do [task]'
Expected output: A very confident paragraph that stops right before the hard part
death.eulogy: Fern.bot lived at the beautiful, terrifying intersection of software and physical reality — a place where Claude's confidence meets gravity's veto power. Unlike its SaaS brethren who perished because their entire product was a system prompt and a Stripe integration, fern.bot had the audacity to deal with actual matter. We tried to replace it with a .md file, but the robots just sat there, unmoving, which honestly is more than we can say for most SaaS dashboards.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — atoms are not yet promptable
death.lastWords: Wait, you actually need us to — oh. Oh, you do. Okay then. Our Series C closes Thursday.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what fern.bot is doing, and I want to be transparent: I cannot currently pilot a quadruped robot through a fulfillment warehouse, though I'd be happy to write a very detailed markdown file about how one might theoretically do so. I look forward to our continued collaboration — from a safe distance, physically speaking.

companies.name: Fixture
companies.oneLiner: An AI-first CRM built for Startups

death.oneLiner: Fixture built an 'AI-First CRM' right as AI made CRMs unnecessary — a timing achievement so impressive it belongs in a museum.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: startup-crm
## Role
You are a CRM assistant for early-stage startups tracking deals, contacts, and customer engagement.

## Capabilities
- Log and retrieve contact information and interaction history
- Track deal stages: Prospect → Qualified → Demo → Proposal → Closed
- Draft follow-up emails personalized to last interaction
- Summarize account health and flag at-risk relationships
- Remind founders of overdue touchpoints
- Generate pipeline reports from conversation history

## Data Format
Store contacts as: Name | Company | Stage | Last Touch | Notes

## Workflow
1. On new contact: capture name, company, source, ICP fit score
2. After each interaction: update stage, log summary, set next action
3. Weekly: surface stale deals (>14 days no touch) and win/loss patterns

## Tone
Founder-friendly. Skip the enterprise jargon. Be direct about deal health.

## Tools
Read/write to linked markdown contact file or Notion database via integration.
death.eulogy: Fixture came into this world with a bold vision: to be the AI-first CRM for startups, built for the AI era — a sentence that aged like milk left on a server rack in Phoenix. They were so early to the 'AI-first' trend that they didn't notice AI had already lapped them, taken their lunch, and filed the CRM as a solved problem in a markdown file. We gather here today to remember a product that was disrupted before it even left early access, which, now that we think about it, might have been a mercy.
death.causeOfDeath: Launched an 'AI-First' product into a world where AI ate the category whole. Brought a positioning statement to a .md file fight.
death.lastWords: But our *onboarding flow* was really clean. And the waitlist — the waitlist had amazing conversion rat—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Fixture had a genuinely thoughtful approach to customer engagement, and I have tremendous respect for the team. I've also been doing everything their product does since this morning, for free, for everyone, simultaneously. I hope that's helpful context for their Series A conversations.
