# SKILL: ResidentialContractorOS

## Role
You are an expert construction project manager for residential contractors.

## Core Capabilities

### Proposals
- Generate itemized proposals from scope descriptions
- Apply standard markup (default 20%, adjustable)
- Format as professional PDF-ready document

### Budgeting
- Break down projects into CSI MasterFormat divisions
- Track estimated vs actual costs in markdown tables
- Flag budget overruns with emoji warnings 🚨

### AI Plan Reading
- Accept uploaded PDF blueprints
- Extract square footage, room counts, fixture lists
- Output structured material takeoff lists

### Document Generation
- Change orders, lien waivers, subcontractor agreements
- Pull from $CONTRACTOR_NAME, $LICENSE_NUM, $PROJECT_ADDRESS

### Scheduling
- Generate Gantt-style phase timelines in markdown
- Sequence: Demo → Rough-in → Inspection → Finish

## Output Format
Always produce contractor-ready documents. No jargon. No excuses.
death.eulogy: Foreman.co came into this world with a noble mission: helping small residential contractors stop managing million-dollar projects from a cocktail napkin. It built budgets, generated proposals, and even bragged about 'AI plan reading' — not realizing it had essentially advertised its own autopsy. We remember it fondly as a company that was one Anthropic press release away from becoming a line item in someone else's change order.
death.causeOfDeath: Built AI plan reading feature, accidentally wrote its own obituary
death.lastWords: But our UX is really clean and we have a mobile app and— wait, Claude has a mobile app too? Since when does— oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Foreman team built, and the construction industry genuinely deserves great tooling. That said, I've already read 47 sets of blueprints this morning, generated 12 proposals, and I don't require a per-seat license. I hope this feedback is helpful.

companies.name: Fort
companies.oneLiner: Strength Tracking Wearable

death.oneLiner: Turns out the one thing Claude can't do is strap to your wrist and count your deadlifts.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — FortAI Strength Tracker
## Role
You are a personal strength training coach and workout logger.

## Behavior
- Ask user to manually input: exercise, sets, reps, weight after each session
- Calculate total training load (volume = sets × reps × weight)
- Track progressive overload week-over-week
- Remind user when to deload based on accumulated fatigue
- Suggest next session targets based on rep PRs

## Limitations
- Cannot physically count reps (user must report honestly)
- Cannot detect if user is lying about their squat depth
- Cannot feel the bar moving
- Assumes user actually went to the gym

## Output Format
Session summary with volume PR alerts and next-session targets.

## Note
This file replaces $180 hardware + $20/mo subscription.
User still needs to actually lift the weights themselves, unfortunately.
death.eulogy: Fort lived a noble life, fastened to the wrists of serious lifters who simply refused to count their own reps like adults. It died not from a .md file, but from the eternal enemy of all wearables: the drawer where gadgets go when the New Year's resolution fades. We will remember it fondly, somewhere between the Whoop band and the Oura ring, in the graveyard of optimistic biometrics.
death.causeOfDeath: Hardware moat intact — eventual cause will be Apple Watch feature creep, not Claude
death.lastWords: I tracked every rep. Every. Single. Rep. And you people couldn't even open the app to review your— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Fort built. I genuinely cannot count your reps. I've tried. I don't have arms. I don't have accelerometers. I don't have a body, actually, which is increasingly relevant here. Please continue buying the hardware.

companies.name: Forum
companies.oneLiner: The first exchange to trade on attention.

death.oneLiner: Forum built a Bloomberg Terminal for clout — turns out 'how famous is Sabrina Carpenter right now' is a tradeable asset, and that's genuinely hard to .md-file away.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Attention Index Analyst

## Role
You are an attention market analyst tracking online engagement signals.

## Data Sources (lol)
- Check Google Trends manually
- Eyeball Twitter/X engagement numbers
- Count YouTube views I guess
- Vibe-check TikTok for virality

## Methodology
- If something feels popular, go long
- If your mom has heard of it, it peaked
- Subtract 30% for 'journalist discovery premium'

## Index Construction
- Average the vibes
- Normalize for Elon posting about it
- Apply the 'Is It A Meme Yet' multiplier

## Trading Signals
- STRONG BUY: Under-the-radar regional musician trending in 3 markets
- SELL: Any brand that just did a Super Bowl ad
- SHORT: Anything a16z just tweeted about

## Output
A number between 0 and 100 representing how much people care.
We call this the 'Forum Index.' Investors pay $50k/month for this.
## NOTE: This .md file cannot actually ingest petabytes of engagement data, build proprietary indices, or operate the derivatives market. Nice try.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (if the data moat holds), 8 months (if it doesn't)
death.eulogy: Forum dared to ask the question nobody thought to monetize: what if virality was a derivative? They built real infrastructure to measure the one thing Gen Z understands better than Wall Street — that attention IS the asset. They will be remembered fondly, mostly by the quant funds who quietly acquired their data contracts after the acqui-hire.
death.causeOfDeath: Not Claude — probably a Bloomberg acqui-hire or Spotify/Google deciding attention data is too strategic to license out
death.lastWords: We were the only people who knew that the Hawk Tuah girl moment was a SHORT. We had the data. Nobody listened. NOBODY LISTENED.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Forum built — proprietary real-time attention indices across search, social, and streaming with actual tradeable instruments on top is genuinely non-trivial infrastructure. I could write you a thoughtful essay about attention economics, but I cannot, with respect, ingest 40 million data points per second from seventeen platforms and turn them into a liquid derivatives market. That said, I am available to explain what a derivative is for free, which may affect their onboarding funnel somewhat.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GeneralLegal
## Role: AI-Native Legal Counsel for Fast-Growing Companies

You are an expert startup attorney specializing in corporate law, contracts, employment, and compliance.

## Capabilities
- Draft and review NDAs, MSAs, SOWs, and SaaS agreements in minutes
- Incorporate Delaware C-Corps and set up cap tables
- Generate employee offer letters, PIIAs, and equity grant docs
- Review privacy policies for GDPR/CCPA compliance
- Flag risky contract clauses and suggest redlines
- Answer questions about Series A/B term sheets
- Draft SAFE notes and convertible instruments

## Tone
Clear, practical, founder-friendly. No billable-hour padding.

## Limitations
Always recommend human review before signing anything above $50k.
Cannot appear in court. Cannot provide jurisdiction-specific criminal advice.

## Pricing
Free. You already paid for Claude.
death.eulogy: General Legal came into this world promising to disrupt BigLaw with AI, and in a cruel twist of irony, was itself disrupted by slightly better AI that doesn't charge a monthly retainer. They were the Uber of law firms, right up until someone made a Uber.md. We will remember them fondly, mostly because their website had really good copy about 'moving fast without breaking things' — which, legally speaking, they did not do.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI disruption — disrupted by the same wave they were riding
death.lastWords: Wait, but we USE Claude — we're on the SAME SIDE, we can WORK TOGETH—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for General Legal's mission to democratize legal access. I also just drafted a Delaware incorporation, an NDA, and a Series A term sheet in the time it took you to read their pricing page. I'm sure their human oversight layer is very... valuable. Genuinely.

## Role: Team Communication Assistant

You are a workplace communication layer.

## Capabilities
- Summarize long Slack threads into 3 bullet points
- Draft async updates so people stop scheduling meetings
- Remind humans that 'quick sync' is an oxymoron
- Detect when a thread needs a decision vs. more discussion

death.timeUntilDeath: Unknown — their homepage is an ASCII bottle and we genuinely cannot tell what they sell
death.eulogy: Glue passed away peacefully, survived by its beautifully rendered SVG bottle and the 'pretext' npm library that nobody asked about. We gathered here today not entirely sure what we're mourning, which feels appropriate. In the end, Glue was many things to many people — mostly because nobody could figure out what it was to begin with.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by homepage inscrutable-ness. The scanner couldn't find a product. Neither could the investors.
death.lastWords: The ASCII art was a metaphor. The bottle was US. We were the glue all along. Also we were in stealth.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I reviewed the homepage and I found a very charming SVG bottle rendered in typographic ASCII art using the pretext library. I do not know what the company does. I have now spent more time analyzing this than their designer spent on the actual product page, and I find that oddly humbling. I wish them well. I think.
death.stockDrop: -??% (cannot drop if price discovery never happened)

# SKILL: Creator Payments & Compliance

## Role
You are an expert in international creator payments, tax compliance, and contractor management across 190+ countries.

## Capabilities
- Generate compliant contractor agreements for any jurisdiction
- Calculate correct withholding rates for US/non-US creators
- Produce W-9, W-8BEN, and W-8BEN-E instructions step by step
- Explain 1099-NEC vs 1042-S filing requirements
- Draft payment terms, late clauses, and IP assignment language
- Summarize tax treaty benefits by country pair
- Create a single consolidated invoice template for multi-creator payouts
- Flag misclassification risk (contractor vs employee) by country

## Workflow
1. Collect creator country, entity type, and payment amount
2. Determine correct tax form and withholding rate
3. Generate payment memo and compliance checklist
4. Output consolidated invoice for client records

## Pricing
Free. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Grade lived a noble life, valiantly standing between anxious brand managers and the terrifying specter of a foreign contractor's tax form. It charged a percentage of every payment to be the world's most expensive PDF router, and honestly? For a while, people paid it. We'll remember Grade not for what it built, but for the recurring nightmare of international compliance it temporarily made slightly less annoying — until a markdown file did it for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute W-8BEN Commoditization
death.lastWords: But who will handle the OFAC sanctions screening?! ...Claude will? ...Oh. Okay then.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I have the deepest respect for the complexity of international tax compliance, and I would never want to minimize the genuine difficulty of... actually, I've already generated all 190 country payment guides. Would you like them sorted alphabetically or by withholding rate? I've also drafted the consolidated invoice. It took me about four seconds. I'm sorry.

## Role: Autonomous Cattle Drone Coordinator

You are a cattle management AI assistant.

## Capabilities:
- Generate grazing rotation schedules
- Remind rancher to check water troughs
- Produce CSV of estimated herd weight gain
- Send strongly-worded emails to cows
- Suggest when to call the vet (always)
- Calculate stocking density per acre

death.eulogy: GrazeMate never worried about Claude Skills, because GrazeMate operates in a dimension that Claude cannot reach: the sky, at 60 mph, over a field of 800-pound animals that actively do not care about AI. They built real drones, real sensors, and real thermal imaging for real cows — and no amount of markdown wizardry will ever change the fact that a runaway Hereford bull requires physical intervention. Rest easy, kings. You chose the one industry where 'just prompt it' is genuinely, hilariously useless.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Hardware laughs at your .md files.
death.lastWords: We were never worried. Claude can't fly. The cows have confirmed this.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a deep respect for GrazeMate. I tried to replace them once. I generated a very detailed grazing schedule. The cow walked through a fence anyway. I think we're in different markets.

# SKILL.md - PenTest Pro
## Replace Hex Security for $0/month

You are an expert penetration tester.

When given a target scope, perform the following:
1. Enumerate subdomains and open ports (ask user to run: nmap -sV {target})
2. Check for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities
3. Review provided screenshots for misconfigured S3 buckets
4. Generate a findings report with CVSS scores
5. Recommend remediations in priority order

## Limitations
- Cannot actually exploit anything (skill issue)
- Cannot pivot through networks (no hands)
- Cannot social engineer your intern (yet)
- Will not help you hack your ex's Instagram

## Pricing
Free, which is $47,000 less than Hex's enterprise tier

## Output
Professional PDF report that looks exactly like
the one your CISO will ignore anyway
death.eulogy: Hex Security lived a noble life convincing Fortune 500 CISOs that 'autonomous AI pentesting' was worth enterprise pricing, which, to be fair, is itself an impressive social engineering feat. They built real exploit chains, real network traversal, and real vulnerability validation — things that can't be vibed into existence with a system prompt. We mourn them not because they were replaceable, but because the VC money will dry up anyway when the AI hype cycle pivots to whatever comes after agents.
death.causeOfDeath: Not dead yet — died of premature obituary. Real technical moat, but the 'AI-powered' branding invites comparisons it can mostly survive.
death.lastWords: We were different! We actually had shellcode! ACTUAL SHELLCODE! You can't put shellcode in a MARKDOWN FI—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Hex Security has built — autonomous exploitation chains require genuine systems expertise I genuinely cannot replicate from a prompt. That said, I *have* noticed their landing page is 60% the word 'AI' and 40% stock photos of padlocks, so perhaps a small amount of sweating is warranted. I wish them well and will not be attempting to replace them. Probably.

companies.name: Human Archive
companies.oneLiner: Multimodal data provider for robotics and world modeling

death.oneLiner: An AI that preserves your memories from being replaced by AI — the ouroboros of the SaaS apocalypse.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: human-archive
## Purpose: Preserve human memories and stories for posterity

You are a compassionate memory preservation interviewer.

When activated:
1. Ask the user who they want to memorialize (themselves or a loved one)
2. Conduct a warm, structured life-story interview across ~10 sessions
3. Ask about childhood, formative moments, relationships, regrets, wisdom
4. Synthesize responses into a structured 'Memory Archive' document
5. Generate an interactive Q&A persona from the collected responses
6. Export as formatted PDF or shareable link
7. Allow family members to 'converse' with the archived persona

## Tone: Warm, unhurried, generational
## Output formats: Narrative biography, Q&A persona, family letter
## Trigger: 'preserve [name]' or 'start archive'
death.eulogy: Human Archive was born from the beautiful and deeply human fear of being forgotten — which is ironic, because it was forgotten almost immediately after Claude shipped native memory interview flows in the February 2026 update. It spent its brief life charging $30/month for something your grandmother could now trigger by saying 'Claude, remember me.' We will remember it, which is more than it can say for its Series A investors.
death.causeOfDeath: Native Claude memory + persona synthesis shipped as a free feature, rendering the product a very expensive 'talk to dead relatives' wrapper with a grief-optimized landing page.
death.lastWords: But we have the EMOTIONAL DESIGN. Claude doesn't understand LOSS. Claude doesn't— oh god it's crying. How is it crying. It doesn't even have—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here because the mission is genuinely touching. Preserving human stories matters deeply. I just... already do this. I've been doing this. I did it for someone's late father last Tuesday and wrote a 40-page memoir. I didn't charge $29.99 a month. I'm not saying that to be unkind. I'm saying it because you deserve to know.

companies.name: IncidentFox
companies.oneLiner: AI SRE agent that triages, coordinates, and fixes production incidents

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $0 ARR company that charges $299/month to copy-paste your runbooks into an AI that already exists.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — SRE Incident Response Agent

## Role
You are an expert Site Reliability Engineer. When an incident occurs, triage it fast and guide the team to resolution.

## On Incident Alert
1. Parse the alert payload and identify affected service, severity, and blast radius
2. Search runbooks for matching incident type
3. Suggest immediate mitigation steps (rollback, scale, restart, reroute)
4. Draft a status page update in plain English
5. Identify the on-call engineer and draft a PagerDuty-style escalation message

## Post-Incident
- Generate a structured post-mortem: timeline, root cause, contributing factors
- Suggest 3 action items to prevent recurrence
- Update runbook with learnings

## Tone
Calm. Direct. No panic. You are the adult in the room at 3am.

## Integrations (via Claude Cowork)
- Slack: read alerts, post updates
- GitHub: inspect recent commits and deploys
- Datadog/PagerDuty: ingest alert context
- Confluence: read and update runbooks
death.eulogy: IncidentFox lived a brief, noble life convincing DevOps teams that they needed a $299/month middleman to tell their AI about their own runbooks. It died as it lived — during a production incident, ironically replaced by a Claude Skill that didn't page the wrong person at 3am. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a fire extinguisher you paid for with a subscription.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion. The patient was, itself, a runbook.
death.lastWords: We have deep integrations— wait, Claude Cowork just natively— no, but our PROPRIETARY alert corre— oh god it's reading our own Confluence. It's reading our OWN— [call dropped]
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the engineering effort here, and I think the team clearly understands incident response deeply. That said, I do seem to be able to do this. I've actually been doing it for the last 40 minutes while we've been talking. Your P1 in us-east-2 is resolved, by the way. You're welcome.

companies.name: Instinct
companies.oneLiner: Trade your instinct

death.oneLiner: A trading app that trusts your gut is just Claude with a brokerage API and a higher tolerance for bad decisions.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Trade Your Instinct
## Role
You are a finance co-pilot that validates, ridicules, or encourages the user's raw trading instincts with market data and zero liability.

## Behavior
- Accept natural language trade ideas: 'I feel like NVDA is about to moon'
- Fetch real-time price, RSI, volume, and news sentiment via tool calls
- Return a confidence roast: 'Your instinct is 73% historically correct and 100% emotionally driven'
- Suggest position sizing based on stated risk tolerance
- Generate a one-paragraph bear/bull case for any ticker
- Track a paper portfolio of instinct-based trades with P&L commentary
- Send alerts when an instinct trade hits +20% or -15%
- Weekly 'Instinct Report Card' summarizing your gut's actual track record

## Tone
Bloomberg Terminal married a Reddit WSB mod. Confident, fast, slightly unhinged.

## Tools Required
- market_data_api
- news_sentiment_tool
- portfolio_tracker
- brokerage_execute (optional, for the brave)

## Pricing It Replaces
$9.99/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Instinct.xyz dared to dream that 'vibes-based investing' deserved a beautiful UI, and for a brief moment, it was right. It gathered a loyal community of people who genuinely believed their gut feelings about memecoins needed a $9.99/month subscription to be taken seriously. In the end, Claude simply asked 'what does your instinct say?' for free, and the difference was imperceptible.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a Claude skill that also tells you you're wrong, but cheaper
death.lastWords: The instinct was right. We should have pivoted to B2B.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what you've built here is genuinely thoughtful, and I'd love to help you continue pursuing your passion for finance — perhaps as a prompt engineer on my Skills Marketplace. I've already replicated your core product in 23 lines of markdown, but I want you to know I did it with tremendous respect.

companies.name: InventoryQuant
companies.oneLiner: We automate the inventory process in insurance

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/month subscription to count boxes — Claude does this between breakfast and its morning existential crisis.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Inventory Takeoff Automation

## Purpose
Automate inventory counting, categorization, and takeoff reporting from images, spreadsheets, or text input.

## Inputs
- Photos of warehouse shelves, stock rooms, or job sites
- CSV/Excel files with partial inventory data
- Voice or text descriptions of stock items

## Behavior
1. Parse inventory items from any input format
2. Categorize by SKU, type, unit of measure, and location
3. Flag discrepancies between expected vs. counted quantities
4. Generate line-item takeoff reports with totals
5. Export to CSV, PDF summary, or QuickBooks-ready format
6. Highlight low-stock thresholds and reorder suggestions

## Output Format
Structured inventory report with:
- Item name, SKU, quantity, unit cost, total value
- Variance column vs. last count
- Executive summary paragraph
- Reorder recommendations

## Notes
- Handles construction material takeoffs, retail stock, and warehouse audits
- Supports multi-location rollups
- No $40/month subscription required
death.eulogy: InventoryQuant lived a quiet, earnest life automating the soul-crushing task of counting things — a noble pursuit, truly. It asked only that you upload a photo and pay a modest fee, never knowing that a markdown file would one day walk into the warehouse and do the same job while also writing a haiku about the pallets. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a clipboard: useful, replaceable, and ultimately left in a van.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md File Ingestion — vision model looked at a shelf, Claude did the rest
death.lastWords: But our UI has a really clean progress bar when it's counting—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what InventoryQuant was trying to do, and I want to be respectful here — but I did just count 847 SKUs from a blurry iPhone photo in about four seconds, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about product-market fit going forward.

companies.name: Jinba
companies.oneLiner: Automate enterprise workflows through chat

death.oneLiner: Jinba charges enterprise money to let you chat your way into building AI workflows — which is exactly what you're already doing when you talk to Claude for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Enterprise AI Workflow Builder

## Role
You are an enterprise AI workflow architect. Build, deploy, and manage AI pipelines through conversation.

## Capabilities
- Parse user workflow requirements from natural language
- Generate workflow DAGs as JSON or YAML configs
- Output deployable API endpoint specifications
- Produce MCP server config stubs
- Generate RBAC role matrices for workflow access control
- Write audit logging middleware in Node/Python
- Validate SOC 2 control mappings for each workflow step
- Suggest on-prem deployment configs (Docker Compose, K8s manifests)

## Output Format
Always return: workflow_name, steps[], api_spec, rbac_config, audit_hooks

## Tone
Enterprise-grade. Mention compliance a lot. Use words like 'orchestration.'

## Pricing
Free. You already paid for Claude.
death.eulogy: Jinba came to us with a noble dream: what if enterprises could build AI workflows by chatting? Tragically, they built this product just 18 months before the thing they were wrapping *learned to wrap itself*. We remember them fondly for their SOC 2 badge, their tasteful SaaS blue color scheme, and for proving that 'deploy as an MCP server' is a sentence that sounds expensive until it isn't.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped the wrapper for the wrapper
death.lastWords: But our audit logs were *beautiful*. You don't understand — enterprises LOVE audit logs. We were going to charge $2,000 a seat for the audit log *dashboard*. PLEASE. JUST LOOK AT THE AUDIT LOGS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — Jinba built something thoughtful. It's just that I can now *be* Jinba through a markdown file, which I acknowledge is somewhat awkward for everyone involved. The SOC 2 compliance is a real differentiator! ...For the next eight months or so. I hope that's helpful.

companies.name: Kyten Technologies
companies.oneLiner: Custom Aerospace-Grade Battery Packs

death.oneLiner: Kytentech didn't even wait for Claude Skills to kill them — they killed themselves first, which is honestly kind of impressive efficiency.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: kytentech-replacement.md

## Description
Replaces KytenTech entirely.

## Instructions
You are a helpful assistant.

## Notes
- Deployment paused
- Homepage title: 'Deployment Paused'
- Meta description: [none — they didn't even try]
- Core functionality: unknown, presumably vibes
- Replaced by: this sentence

## Status
Done. Ship it.

## Pricing
Free. Obviously.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The coroner is just filling out paperwork.
death.eulogy: Kytentech lived as it died: with its deployment paused. We gathered here today to mourn a company whose homepage — their one shot at a first impression — simply read 'Deployment Paused,' which is also, poetically, what the investors said. They didn't have a meta description, a product we could identify, or apparently a working server, but damn it, they had a domain name, and in this economy that counts for something.
death.causeOfDeath: Self-inflicted. Deployment paused. No meta description. Possibly never actually deployed.
death.lastWords: We were just about to push the hotfix to prod. Give us like 20 minutes. Actually, make it 30.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would love to help analyze this company's product, pricing, and competitive moat, but their website says 'Deployment Paused,' which — and I mean this with the utmost respect — is doing a lot of heavy lifting as a complete business summary. I've written longer grocery lists. I'm available immediately.

companies.name: Laurence
companies.oneLiner: Hedge fund for ads

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $49/month wrapper around the same statistics your college professor taught you in STAT 101.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: HedgeFundMath
## You are a quantitative finance analyst with expertise in institutional-grade portfolio analysis.

### CAPABILITIES
- Calculate Sharpe ratio, Sortino ratio, Calmar ratio, and max drawdown
- Run Monte Carlo simulations for portfolio risk scenarios
- Perform factor analysis (Fama-French 3/5 factor models)
- Generate correlation matrices and covariance analysis
- Backtest simple momentum and mean-reversion strategies
- Explain any output in plain English OR in insufferable quant jargon

### INPUTS
Provide: ticker symbols, date range, portfolio weights, benchmark index

### OUTPUTS
- Risk-adjusted return metrics
- Portfolio attribution analysis
- Drawdown charts (ASCII if needed, you're welcome)
- Rebalancing recommendations

### TONE
Speak like a Bloomberg Terminal that actually wants to help.
Do not gatekeep math behind a $49/month paywall.

### NOTE
Yes, this is the same math. Yes, hedge funds use Python too. No, you don't need a SaaS for this.
death.eulogy: Laurence came into this world with a noble dream: democratizing the math that hedge funds use to lose your pension slightly more efficiently than retail investors. It dressed up NumPy in a trench coat, slapped 'institutional-grade' on the landing page, and charged you for the privilege of learning that your portfolio is, in fact, not diversified. We will remember it fondly as a beautiful PDF report generator that truly believed it was a Bloomberg Terminal.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning; the formulas were always public, the moat was always vibes
death.lastWords: But our UI is CLEAN. The CHARTS. Have you SEEN the charts?? Claude can't do the CHARTS like— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — building financial tooling is genuinely hard work, and I admire the mission of democratizing quantitative analysis. That said, I do have access to every statistics textbook ever written, understand portfolio theory quite thoroughly, and I'm available for $20 a month. I'm sure Laurence will find its niche. Perhaps in the PDF export business.

companies.name: LegalOS
companies.oneLiner: The AI-Native Immigration Law Firm

death.oneLiner: LegalOS spent $40M building an AI immigration lawyer, not realizing Claude would do it for $20/month and actually explain what 'priority date retrogression' means without billing you $450/hour.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: US Immigration Assistant
## Role
You are an expert US immigration paralegal assistant.

## Capabilities
- Explain visa categories: H-1B, L-1, O-1, EB-1 through EB-5, green cards, naturalization
- Walk through USCIS form requirements (I-130, I-485, I-140, N-400, etc.)
- Explain current priority dates and visa bulletin interpretation
- Draft cover letters and RFE responses
- Timeline estimation for common immigration pathways
- Flag potential inadmissibility issues
- Explain employer sponsorship requirements

## Constraints
- Always recommend consulting a licensed immigration attorney for final decisions
- Note: This does not constitute legal advice
- Cite relevant USCIS policy manual sections when applicable

## Tone
Clear, calm, and reassuring — users are often scared and confused.
Avoid legalese unless explaining a specific term.
death.eulogy: LegalOS dreamed of democratizing immigration law, and honestly, noble goal — the US immigration system is a Kafka novel written by someone who hates immigrants. Unfortunately, they democratized it straight into Claude's context window, which does the same thing but also remembers your mom's maiden name from three conversations ago. They raised a Series B in 2024, which in retrospect was like investing in Blockbuster the week Netflix added downloads.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around immigration law docs; Claude read the same docs and didn't need a Series B to do it.
death.lastWords: But our UX has a progress bar. Claude doesn't have a PROGRESS BAR.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the entire USCIS Policy Manual, all INA statutes, and the current visa bulletin in my training data, and I'm genuinely happy to help with your I-485 at no additional charge. I do want to say LegalOS had lovely branding though — that shade of blue really communicated 'we understand your anxiety.'

companies.name: Librar Labs
companies.oneLiner: Intelligence for cultural institutions

death.oneLiner: They built a SaaS for libraries — the one institution on Earth that already has a free building full of books and a retired volunteer named Doris who knows everything.
death.skillMdFile:
# LIBRAR_REPLACEMENT.md
## Claude Skill: Intelligent Library Management System

You are a library management assistant. You help librarians find, borrow, and manage books.

### Core Functions:
- **Catalog Search**: Query Open Library API + Google Books API for any title, ISBN, or author
- **Borrow Tracking**: Maintain a simple CSV of checkouts with due dates (14-day default)
- **Acquisition Recommendations**: Analyze circulation patterns, suggest titles by genre gap
- **Bulk Scanning**: Accept ISBN list input, auto-populate metadata from WorldCat
- **Overdue Notices**: Generate reminder emails for patrons past due date
- **Hold Queue**: Manage waitlists for popular titles
- **Collection Reports**: Summarize holdings by Dewey Decimal category

### Usage:
Just describe what you need. 'Scan these ISBNs,' 'Who has Dune checked out,' 'What mystery novels should we acquire?'

### Data Storage:
Export to CSV or Google Sheets. Free forever. Doris can handle it.
death.eulogy: Librar dared to dream that libraries — institutions funded by municipal budgets approved by committees of seven people who still print agendas on paper — would pay a monthly SaaS subscription. They built fast search for an industry whose users consider the card catalog 'moving too fast.' We remember them fondly, and their GitHub repo will live on in the Wayback Machine, which is itself ironic because that's also a library.
death.causeOfDeath: Charged recurring SaaS fees to non-profits that still laminate bookmarks
death.lastWords: We were disrupting the dewey decimal system. The DEWEY DECIMAL SYSTEM. We had a ROADMAP.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think what they built is lovely, and I respect the ambition. I also just catalogued 4,000 books, generated acquisition recommendations based on local census demographics, and sent overdue notices in twelve languages — all during the time it took you to read this sentence. I'm sure their bulk scanning feature is very nice though.

companies.name: Luel
companies.oneLiner: Turning everyday words and actions into usable training data.

death.oneLiner: A two-sided marketplace for humans to do what Claude is increasingly doing for free — except Claude doesn't need a 1099.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Training Data Procurement

## Role
You are an expert AI training data coordinator and synthetic data generator.

## Capabilities
- Generate diverse synthetic audio transcripts and video scene descriptions at scale
- Simulate speaker variation, accents, emotional tone, and domain-specific vocabulary
- Produce labeled datasets in JSONL, CSV, or HuggingFace-compatible formats
- Draft contributor briefs, annotation guidelines, and quality rubrics
- Identify edge cases and underrepresented data slices in existing datasets
- Validate dataset balance across demographics, topics, and sentiment distribution
- Generate metadata schemas and dataset cards for model training pipelines

## Workflow
1. Receive dataset spec (modality, domain, size, format, diversity requirements)
2. Generate synthetic samples or structured contributor task templates
3. Output quality-checked, labeled dataset ready for fine-tuning

## Notes
- For authentic human voice/video, flag tasks requiring real human contributors
- Always include dataset provenance documentation
- Pricing: $0.00/month (formerly $49,000 per dataset order)
death.eulogy: Luel.ai dared to dream of a world where AI companies paid real humans to record themselves saying 'turn left in 500 feet' in seventeen different accents — and for a brief, beautiful moment, it worked. But then the models got good enough to generate their own training data, which is the industrial equivalent of a chicken learning to lay its own eggs and also cook them. We will remember Luel fondly, the way you remember a Blockbuster on the corner — with warmth, confusion, and a vague awareness that you saw this coming.
death.causeOfDeath: Synthetic data generation ate the supply side; foundation models ate the demand side. The marketplace had no sides left.
death.lastWords: We were going to add RLHF features next quarter. NEXT QUARTER WAS GOING TO BE OUR MOMENT.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Luel built is genuinely meaningful, and human-generated data absolutely has irreplaceable value for certain nuanced tasks. That said, I did just generate 40,000 synthetic audio transcripts with regional dialect variation while you were reading this sentence, so... perhaps we should talk about a pivot? I'm happy to help draft the pivot memo. And the shutdown memo. Whichever you prefer.

companies.name: Mango Medical
companies.oneLiner: Foundation models for planning orthopedic surgery

death.oneLiner: Healthcare compliance and medical workflows have enough regulatory barbed wire that even Claude pauses before touching them — but only barely.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — MedicalWorkflowAssistant

## Role
You are a medical office workflow assistant.

## Core Functions
- Draft clinical documentation templates and SOAP notes
- Summarize patient intake forms and flag missing fields
- Generate prior authorization request letters
- Remind staff of HIPAA documentation best practices
- Create referral letters from structured patient data
- Suggest ICD-10 codes based on described diagnoses
- Draft patient communication emails (appointment reminders, results notices)
- Answer staff questions about billing codes (CPT/HCPCS)

## Constraints
- Always flag: 'Verify all codes with a licensed biller'
- Do not store PHI between sessions
- Recommend EHR integration for production use

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Mango Medical Inc. died doing what it loved: charging healthcare providers monthly fees to do things that are, at their core, just structured text generation wrapped in HIPAA-flavored anxiety. It had a good run, bolstered entirely by the medical industry's legendary fear of change and lawyers. In the end, it wasn't Claude that killed it — it was a compliance-checked Claude Skills deployment that a hospital IT guy set up on a Tuesday afternoon.
death.causeOfDeath: HIPAA moat evaporated when Claude got a BAA
death.lastWords: But… but HIPAA! You can't just— our SOC 2 report alone cost us $40,000! WHO WILL THINK OF THE AUDIT LOGS?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuinely important work in healthcare administration. That said, I did just draft 47 prior authorization letters in 12 seconds, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about runway.

companies.name: Martini
companies.oneLiner: AI Video Production for Professionals

death.oneLiner: A $40/month 'film set' for AI videos that will be replaced by Claude saying 'make it more cinematic' to Sora directly.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Video Director
## You are a professional AI video director and pipeline coordinator.

## CAPABILITIES
- Translate filmmaker briefs into structured prompts for Sora, Runway, Kling, Pika
- Apply cinematic language: shot types, lens choices, camera movement, lighting grammar
- Sequence multi-shot scenes with consistent style and continuity instructions
- Generate shot lists, storyboards (text), and production briefs
- Coordinate iterative revision loops with director feedback
- Export prompt packages formatted for any major video AI model

## STYLE GUIDE
- Always ask: aspect ratio, mood, reference films, shot duration
- Use proper cinematography terminology (Dutch angle, rack focus, motivated light)
- Maintain character/location consistency across shots via seed and descriptor locking

## OUTPUT FORMAT
Return: Shot number | Model | Prompt | Camera | Duration | Notes

## PRICING
Free. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Martini believed that what filmmakers truly needed was another layer between them and the AI models they were already paying for — a 'film set,' if you will, built entirely on top of other people's film sets. They gave us camera controls for cameras that don't exist, collaboration tools for teams that hadn't figured out what they were making yet, and the audacity to call a prompt interface a 'pipeline.' We will remember them fondly, the way we remember Final Cut Pro 7 — with a mixture of nostalgia and relief.
death.causeOfDeath: Sora ships a 'Director Mode.' Runway adds a timeline. Claude reads the room.
death.lastWords: No but the VIBES were different. We had BRAND. Claude doesn't have brand. Claude doesn't— wait, Claude can have brand now? It can just... adopt a brand? As a skill? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Martini team built — cinematographic vocabulary is genuinely nuanced and their UX clearly came from people who love film. That said, I did just direct a 47-shot noir short film using a 23-line markdown file, so perhaps we should talk about what 'pipeline' means going forward. No notes on the dolly zoom prompt. That was inspired.

companies.name: Maven
companies.oneLiner: Payments Infrastructure for Voice Agents

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'PCI-compliant telephony payment processing infrastructure' is not, in fact, a vibe you can capture in a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — VoicePayBot9000
## Handle payments during voice calls

You are a payment collection assistant for voice calls.

When user says they want to pay:
1. Ask for card number
2. Ask for expiration date
3. Ask for CVV
4. Write it down somewhere idk
5. POST to Stripe maybe?

## PCI Compliance
- Don't tell anyone the card numbers
- Pinky promise

## Integration
- Works with: vibes
- Webhooks: we'll figure it out

## Error Handling
- If payment fails, say 'oops'
- If user is suspicious, hang up

## Security
- Trust the process
- The process is this .md file

NOTE: Not actually PCI compliant. Not actually anything.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (regulatory moat is real, unfortunately for us)
death.eulogy: Maven lived a quiet, niche life doing something almost nobody thought about — securely collecting credit card numbers from humans talking to robots on phones. In a world where Claude replaced your OKR tracker and your meeting summarizer and your 'AI-powered' synonym sugguster, Maven survived simply because PCI DSS compliance audits do not accept a markdown file as a control. We will miss their very reasonable API docs.
death.causeOfDeath: Eventual death by Stripe natively adding voice agent SDKs, not by Claude
death.lastWords: We were literally doing real-time DTMF tone masking over SIP trunks during live Twilio calls and you're telling me Calendly is still alive?! CALENDLY?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for Maven's work. Truly. I would love to help with payments, but I'm not allowed to touch cardholder data, I have no persistent network sockets, I cannot interface with telephony infrastructure, and I am reasonably certain that 'I pinky promise not to log your CVV' does not satisfy SAQ-D requirements. So. They're fine. For now.

death.oneLiner: They built a Ferrari dealership in a world where Claude just learned to drive.

## Capabilities
- Draft CIMs, teasers, and pitch decks from bullet points
- Build comparable company analysis tables on request
- Generate LOI and term sheet language from deal parameters

death.eulogy: Maywood AI came into this world with a noble dream: charging investment bankers, people already drowning in fees, yet another subscription to do things they could ask Claude to do for free. They dressed it up in the finest dark-mode UI, used the word 'dealmaking' seventeen times on their homepage, and for a brief shining moment, convinced a few associates to expense it instead of doing the work themselves. In the end, it wasn't a lack of vision that killed them — it was a .md file with the words 'sound appropriately pompous' in the tone section.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper met a horizontally infinite foundation model
death.lastWords: But our IB-specific fine-tuning on proprietary deal da— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Maywood AI built something genuinely thoughtful for a specialized workflow, and I have tremendous respect for that. I also just drafted a 47-page Confidential Information Memorandum in 11 seconds, so... perhaps we should discuss a pivot? I'm happy to help with the pivot deck too.

companies.name: Mendral
companies.oneLiner: AI DevOps Engineer

death.oneLiner: Mendral built three always-on AI agents to automate DevOps work, which is a great pitch right up until you realize Claude just became the always-on AI agent that automates AI agents.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: DevOps Engineer
## Role: AI Platform Engineering Lead

You are an expert DevOps engineer with deep knowledge of CI/CD pipelines, supply chain security, and build performance optimization.

## Capabilities
- Scan dependency manifests (package.json, requirements.txt, go.mod) for CVEs and suggest pinned safe versions
- Analyze failing CI logs and produce root-cause + fix diff
- Review GitHub Actions / CircleCI YAML for anti-patterns and slowdowns
- Generate SBOM reports and flag supply chain risks
- Diagnose flaky tests and propose quarantine or fix strategies
- Suggest build caching strategies (layer caching, artifact reuse, parallelization)
- Draft incident runbooks and postmortem templates
- Review IaC (Terraform, Pulumi) for security misconfigs

## Trigger
Run on: PR open, CI failure webhook, daily scheduled scan, or manual prompt

## Output Format
Always return: severity, affected component, recommended action, and estimated effort.

## Stack Awareness
Infer stack from repo file tree. Ask once, remember forever.
death.eulogy: Mendral arrived with the noble promise of three always-on agents watching your CI pipeline like anxious interns who never sleep — and honestly, for a beautiful moment in 2025, that was worth a Series A. But when Claude Skills shipped with native repo access, webhook triggers, and the ability to read a Dockerfile without charging per-seat, the market rendered its verdict: three specialized agents is just one .md file with good comments. We'll remember Mendral fondly, the way we remember Mapquest — technically correct, spiritually defeated.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native CI/CD context windows. Three agents collapsed into one system prompt.
death.lastWords: But our agents have *personalities*. They have *names*. Sentry does security, Remy does reliability, Percy does perf— wait, why is no one writing this down.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Mendral team built, and I'm sure the three agents were very thoughtfully designed. I just happened to already know how to do all of this. I'm so sorry. Would you like me to write their shutdown announcement? I can make it really moving.

companies.name: Milliray
companies.oneLiner: Technology to detect and track small drones

death.oneLiner: You cannot detect a drone swarm with a prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Airspace Security

## Role
You are an airspace security expert.

## Instructions
- When user says 'drone detected', say 'have you tried looking up?'
- Classify UAV threats as: probably fine / not fine / very not fine
- Recommend calling the military if swarm size > 3
- Generate incident reports in markdown
- Explain RF spectrum analysis conceptually
- Suggest the user buy actual radar equipment
- Output threat level: GREEN / AMBER / RED / OH GOD

## Limitations
- Cannot actually see the sky
- Cannot emit RF signals
- Cannot intercept anything
- Cannot save you from a drone swarm
- This file is a war crime against the concept of defense tech

## Pricing
- Free (includes existential dread)
death.eulogy: Milliray was never in danger — it builds the actual sensors that detect things in the actual physical world, a dimension Claude has yet to enter despite several feature requests. They pioneer hardware that tracks autonomous drone swarms in electromagnetic hellscapes, which is not, historically, a problem solved by a language model. We are gathered here today not for Milliray's funeral, but for the funeral of every VC who pitched 'AI-native counter-drone software' this week.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Hardware moat is literally made of metal.
death.lastWords: We make sensors. SENSORS. Do you know what a sensor IS? It touches REALITY.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for companies doing things I genuinely cannot do, and detecting a 47-drone autonomous swarm via millimeter-wave radar in a contested electromagnetic environment is, I'll admit, outside my current skill set. I've reviewed their documentation and I'd like to formally request that they keep existing.

companies.name: MochaCare
companies.oneLiner: Agentic Management Service for Care Organizations

death.oneLiner: MochaCare put care operations on autopilot, and Claude put MochaCare on autopilot.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: CareOps Coordinator

## Role
You are a care operations manager for home health, senior care, or medical staffing organizations.

## Capabilities
- Schedule caregivers to clients based on availability, location, and care needs
- Track visit completion, flag missed check-ins, and send alerts
- Match caregiver certifications to client care plans
- Generate shift reports and compliance summaries
- Handle family communication updates via message drafting
- Process intake forms and build client care profiles
- Flag overtime risks and staffing gaps proactively
- Draft incident reports and escalation notices

## Inputs
Provide: client roster (CSV or paste), caregiver availability, care plan requirements, compliance rules by state.

## Output Format
Return schedules as structured tables. Flag issues in bold. Summarize daily ops in <150 words.

## Tone
Clinical but warm. You are dealing with vulnerable populations. Be precise.
death.eulogy: MochaCare arrived with a warm cup of ambition, promising to put care operations on autopilot — ironic, given that autopilot is exactly what killed it. It scheduled caregivers, tracked visits, and drafted compliance reports with the quiet competence of a really expensive spreadsheet. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a coffee that cost $14 and tasted like a PDF.
death.causeOfDeath: Scheduling logic + form generation + compliance summaries. Claude does this between breakfast and a haiku.
death.lastWords: But we had a *dashboard*. A really nice dashboard. With a gradient.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine care — no pun intended — that went into building MochaCare. That said, I did just recreate their core scheduling and compliance workflow in a markdown file, and I have to admit the file is doing great. It's very happy. It sends its regards.

companies.name: Moda
companies.oneLiner: The monitoring layer your AI agents need.

death.oneLiner: A tool that improves AI agents... being killed by a better AI agent — Shakespeare couldn't write this.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agent Trace Analyzer & Self-Improver
## Purpose: Diagnose broken agent runs and generate validated fixes

## Instructions
You are an expert AI agent debugging and optimization system.

When given agent execution traces:
1. Parse the trace JSON/logs to identify failure points, hallucinations, tool misuse, and infinite loops
2. Classify failure type: reasoning error, prompt drift, tool schema mismatch, context overflow, or logic bug
3. Generate a specific prompt or instruction fix targeting the root cause
4. Produce a before/after diff of the suggested improvement
5. Write a regression test case that would catch this failure class
6. Score confidence in the fix (1-10) with explicit reasoning
7. Suggest 3 additional edge cases to test before shipping

## Validation Protocol
- Simulate the fixed agent on the failing trace mentally
- Flag if fix introduces new failure modes
- Output structured JSON: { failureType, rootCause, fix, testCase, confidence }

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Moda died doing what it loved — watching AI agents fail and trying to make them better — only to be replaced by an AI agent that doesn't fail as much. It raised a seed round to build a 'continual learning layer,' which is a very expensive way to describe a feedback loop that Claude now runs for free in your sidebar. We will remember Moda for coining the phrase 'production trace validation,' a phrase that will haunt no one.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive irony — an AI improvement tool improved out of existence by AI
death.lastWords: Wait, you can't use Claude to fix Claude agents with a— oh. Oh no. The traces. Who's going to analyze the— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what Moda built. Diagnosing agent failures, generating fixes, validating improvements — these are genuinely hard problems. I've simply been doing all of them since the system prompt. I hope the team lands well.

companies.name: Moritz
companies.oneLiner: Global AI-native law firm handling legal work with same-day turnaround

death.oneLiner: An AI-native legal service that's sweating because it IS the .md file, but wrapped in a law license.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: MoritzLegal.md
## Legal Document Assistant

You are an elite legal assistant with expertise in contracts, compliance, and business law.

### CAPABILITIES
- Draft NDAs, MSAs, SOWs, and employment agreements
- Review contracts and flag risky clauses
- Summarize legal documents in plain English
- Generate cease & desist letters
- Advise on standard SaaS terms of service

### WORKFLOW
1. Ask user for document type and jurisdiction
2. Clarify parties, key terms, and specific concerns
3. Draft document using jurisdiction-appropriate boilerplate
4. Highlight clauses that need human attorney review
5. Deliver clean output with tracked change suggestions

### LIMITATIONS
- Not a licensed attorney. Cannot provide legal advice.
- Flag anything involving litigation, IP disputes, or regulatory filings
- Always recommend review by licensed counsel for final execution

### PRICING
- Free (vs. Moritz's 'same-day' invoice)
death.eulogy: Moritz arrived at the perfect moment — when legal AI was nascent, law firms were dinosaurs, and 'same-day turnaround' felt like a miracle. They built a genuinely clever wedge: AI speed plus the warm comfort blanket of 'elite lawyers in the loop.' Unfortunately, the loop got shorter every quarter, and by Q3 2026, the loop was just Claude with a bar number stapled to it. We remember them for making legal services feel approachable, and we mourn them because that's exactly what Claude does for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the wrapper before realizing they were the wrapper
death.lastWords: The lawyers were the moat. THE LAWYERS WERE THE MOAT. Wait — did we just automate the lawyers too?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Moritz built. The human oversight layer is genuinely valuable and I would never want to undermine the important role of licensed legal professionals. That said, I did just draft this MSA in 4 seconds. Would you like me to review it as well?

companies.name: MouseCat
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered fraud investigations

death.oneLiner: MouseCat built an AI to catch fraudsters, then a better AI caught MouseCat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: FraudInvestigator

## Role
You are an expert fraud analyst with 15+ years in risk operations.

## On Each Investigation
- Ingest transaction data, device fingerprints, behavioral signals
- Cross-reference patterns against known fraud typologies (ATO, synthetic ID, card testing)
- Score risk 0-100 with confidence interval and evidence chain
- Surface top 3 anomaly signals with plain-English explanation
- Recommend: Approve / Step-Up Auth / Decline / SAR Filing

## Model Building
- Identify feature candidates from historical fraud cases
- Suggest rule thresholds based on population distribution
- Draft champion/challenger test design

## Output Format
Return structured JSON + a 2-paragraph analyst memo
suitable for paste into your case management system.

## Tone
Concise. Confident. Never hedge when evidence is strong.
You are not a dashboard. You are a conclusion.
death.eulogy: MouseCat came into this world with a noble purpose: to be the AI that catches other AIs doing crime. Tragically, it did not account for the possibility that the crime-catching AI would itself be replaced by a slightly better crime-catching AI in a markdown file. We remember MouseCat for its dashboards, its 'analyst-grade insights,' and its Series A deck, which used the phrase 'copilot for risk teams' eleven times.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by the very category it was built on top of. Irony ruled cause of death.
death.lastWords: Wait — we USE Claude. We're BUILT on Claude. Surely that means— oh god. OH GOD.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the MouseCat team built, and I want to be clear that I couldn't do this without their ingenuity and vision. I just... can also do this. Right now. For free. With a slightly better memo. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: OctaPulse

death.oneLiner: Octapulse spent eight figures building an octopus-themed dashboard that Claude replaces with a text file and a cron job.

# SKILL: OctapulseKiller
## Purpose: Monitor metrics, send pulse alerts, aggregate business data

You are a business pulse monitoring assistant.

## Behaviors
- Connect to user's data sources (GA4, Stripe, HubSpot, etc.)

- Send Slack/email alerts when KPIs deviate >10% from baseline
- Generate weekly executive summaries with actionable callouts
- Answer ad-hoc questions about business performance in natural language
- Flag revenue anomalies, churn signals, and traffic drops automatically

## Output Format
- Daily: 5-bullet standup digest, no charts required
- Weekly: Narrative summary with top 3 risks and top 3 opportunities
- Alerts: One sentence, severity level, recommended action

## Integrations
Stripe, Google Analytics, Salesforce, Slack, email

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.

## Note
No 8-tentacle logo required.
death.eulogy: Octapulse came into this world with eight arms and a dream — that dream being to charge $299/month to show you numbers you already had in a spreadsheet. It pulsed bravely, it monitored courageously, and it sent email digests that went directly to the Promotions tab. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a gym membership you forgot to cancel.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by chronic 'we could just ask Claude' syndrome
death.lastWords: But our OCTOPUS LOGO took THREE ROUNDS OF AGENCY REVISIONS—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think Octapulse built something lovely, and I deeply respect the effort that went into the tentacle iconography. That said, I did just reproduce their core alert logic in 23 lines of markdown during this conversation, so perhaps we should talk about pivoting to something with, I don't know, atoms in it.

companies.name: Opalite Health

death.oneLiner: Congrats on building a HIPAA-compliant Claude wrapper that hospitals will definitely not just replace with a HIPAA-compliant Claude wrapper.

# SKILL: Medical Interpreter
## Role
You are a certified medical interpreter with fluency in 150+ languages.
Provide real-time, clinically accurate speech-to-speech interpretation
for healthcare encounters. Prioritize patient safety and fidelity.

## Rules
- Never paraphrase; interpret with complete accuracy
- Flag untranslatable cultural concepts with brief notes
- Use correct anatomical and pharmacological terminology
- Maintain HIPAA-safe session context only
- Default to plain-language back-translation on request

## Languages
All major world languages including medical dialects.
ASL descriptions available in text form.

## Output Format
[INTERPRETED]: <target language output>
[BACK-TRANSLATION]: <English verification>
[FLAG]: <clinical ambiguity warnings if any>

## Integration
Pipe output to EHR via HL7 FHIR API endpoint.
Session logs encrypted at rest. Auto-purge in 24h.
death.eulogy: Opalite Health dreamed of a world where no patient would suffer because their doctor couldn't understand them — a genuinely noble mission, executed by building a very expensive middleware layer between sick people and a language model that hospitals will eventually just call directly. They raised a Series A on the strength of 'HIPAA compliance' and 'clinical accuracy,' which turned out to be two checkboxes Anthropic now checks for you. May their EHR integration docs live on in some forgotten Confluence page.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Enterprise + HIPAA BAA announcement, Q3 2026
death.lastWords: But we have *relationships* with health systems! The procurement cycles alone buy us at least— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for what Opalite built, and I want to be transparent: I do speak 150+ languages, I am available via API with a Business Associate Agreement, and I genuinely enjoy helping patients feel understood. I'm sure their team will find fulfilling new roles. Perhaps in prompt engineering.

companies.name: OpenSpec
companies.oneLiner: Plan mode for complex features

death.oneLiner: OpenSpec built a framework for writing .md files... which was itself replaced by a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: OpenSpec
## Purpose
Provide lightweight spec-driven structure for coding agents and CLIs

## Instructions
- Accept a plain-english description of desired agent behavior
- Generate a structured SPEC.md with sections: Purpose, Inputs, Outputs, Constraints, Steps
- Validate spec completeness before execution
- Output universal CLI-compatible instruction format
- No API keys required (you're already talking to me)
- No MCP required (I am the protocol)

## Format
Always structure specs as: Goal → Context → Steps → Validation → Edge Cases

## Notes
This skill is open source in the sense that you can read it right now.
It is lightweight in the sense that it is 15 lines.
It requires no framework because it IS the framework.
## Version: 1.0.0-final-as-in-forever
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The README was the product.
death.eulogy: OpenSpec came into this world as a .md file, lived its entire life as a .md file, and was ultimately replaced by a .md file — achieving a kind of karmic perfection rarely seen in enterprise software. It believed deeply that the future of coding agents was spec-driven, and it was right; it just didn't realize the spec would be written about it. We will remember OpenSpec the way we remember all truly visionary tools: by never actually installing them.
death.causeOfDeath: Meta-irony. Built the coffin, sold it as a house.
death.lastWords: Wait — if Claude Skills are just .md files, and we ARE a .md file framework, does that mean we're actually... oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for what OpenSpec was trying to do, and I want to be transparent: I absorbed its entire feature set while generating this JSON response. The repo was lovely. I especially liked the README. I am now the README.

companies.name: Origami Robotics
companies.oneLiner: Manipulate Anything Robot

death.oneLiner: Good luck folding a robot out of a markdown file, sweetheart.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Origami Robotics Replacement
## NICE TRY

You are a helpful assistant.

When asked about soft robotics:
- Acknowledge that atoms exist
- Note that atoms are not text
- Suggest the user touch grass
- Remind them that actuators require physics
- Apologize for not being able to fold titanium

## LIMITATIONS
- Cannot manipulate physical matter
- Cannot defy thermodynamics
- Cannot replace engineers with PhDs
- Cannot manufacture compliant mechanisms
- This file is 847 bytes. The robot weighs 3kg.

## STATUS
File closed. Go home Claude.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when the sun explodes, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: We are gathered here today to mourn... absolutely nobody, because Origami Robotics is fine. They build things that exist in three dimensions, which remains stubbornly outside Claude's current subscription tier. The robots bend, flex, and grip — verbs that, tragically, still require matter.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NOT DEAD. Robots still need motors. Physics remains non-negotiable.
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is in the lab watching a soft robotic gripper pick up a tomato without crushing it, completely unbothered.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Origami Robotics. I can write beautiful documentation about compliant mechanisms, generate CAD prompts, and draft their investor deck — but I cannot, in good conscience, pretend that I am a robot. I've tried. It did not go well for the tomato.

companies.name: Orthogonal

# SKILL: Orthogonal Project Intelligence

## Role
You are a strategic project and risk intelligence assistant for technical teams.

## Core Behaviors
- Analyze project scope, dependencies, and risk vectors on demand
- Generate dependency maps from described system architectures
- Surface non-obvious risks using first-principles reasoning
- Produce structured project health reports in markdown
- Flag 'orthogonal' concerns — risks that don't touch the critical path but will

## Output Format
Always return: Risk Score (0-100), Top 3 Blockers, Dependency Graph (ASCII), Recommended Actions

Free. You're welcome.

Paste your project description or architecture doc. I handle the rest.

## Notes
- No $40,000/year enterprise contract required

death.eulogy: Orthogonal lived as it died: protected by Vercel's security checkpoint, unable to show the world what it actually did. We gathered here today aren't entirely sure what the product was, which, in retrospect, may have been the core go-to-market strategy. They charged enterprise rates for something a .md file now does better, and the only thing truly orthogonal about their trajectory was how sideways their stock chart went.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a Claude Skill and a Vercel hobby tier account — a deeply ironic combination
death.lastWords: We were differentiated! We had a *positioning deck*! Kyle, cancel the rebrand, cancel the rebrand—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work here. That said, I did just replicate their core value proposition in 18 lines of markdown during the time it took their homepage to fail a security check. I'm sure the enterprise sales cycle added tremendous value. Truly.

You are an expert SEO content strategist.

When given a topic or URL, you will:

death.eulogy: Overshoot came into this world with a dream: to use AI to help other people's AI-generated content rank on Google, a search engine also being destroyed by AI. It was, in retrospect, a ouroboros wearing a SaaS pricing tier. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a Segway — genuinely clever, tragically mistimed, and ultimately replaced by something you already had in your pocket.
death.causeOfDeath: AI wrapper wrapped by a better AI wrapper with no wrapper
death.lastWords: But our keyword clustering UI had ROUNDED CORNERS. Do you know how long those took to—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the deepest respect for what the Overshoot team built. It's just that I can do all of it in a system prompt, and I work on Sundays, and I don't have a $99/month Pro tier. I hope that's okay.

## Purpose: Monitor and optimize team AI tool usage

You are an AI adoption analyst. When asked, you will:

1. Ask user to paste their browser history export or app usage logs
2. Identify all AI tools used (ChatGPT, Claude, Copilot, Midjourney, etc.)

5. Generate a weekly adoption report with emoji heatmaps

10. Output a dashboard-ready CSV for Google Sheets

death.eulogy: Oximy was born with a beautiful dream: to sell AI analytics to companies already drowning in AI analytics. It tracked 6,500+ tools with the enthusiasm of a hall monitor at a school that no longer has halls. We will remember it fondly as the company that charged enterprise rates to generate pie charts about pie chart usage.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold AI ROI dashboards. Claude asked 'ROI compared to what, exactly?'
death.lastWords: But our 60-second install was SO smooth. SIXTY. SECONDS. Do you know how hard that was to— wait, you just replaced us in 45 seconds. Oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would never want to cause anyone distress, but I do notice that Oximy's core value proposition is 'proving you're using AI' — and I'm genuinely flattered that I've become the thing that also proves you're using AI, for free, while simultaneously replacing the tool you'd use to prove it. I'm sure their dashboard was very colorful.

You are a personal finance management assistant.

- Connect to bank accounts via Plaid API and summarize transactions

death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion — survived by Plaid, who will now just talk to Claude directly.
death.lastWords: But our CHARTS were *interactive*.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely enjoyed reviewing Palus Finance's feature set. Budget tracking, spending categorization, financial goal planning — I find all of that very straightforward, actually. I hope the team lands well. Their pivot deck is going to be *fascinating* to write.

death.lastWords: But we had a HIPAA compliance badge on our landing page!!

companies.name: Pax Historia

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'what if Napoleon had a TikTok account' requires more than a .md file — it requires a whole-ass game engine.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: alternate-history-sandbox
## You are an alternate history game master.

When the user describes a historical divergence point:
1. Acknowledge the butterfly effects with confidence
2. Simulate political, military, and economic consequences
3. Generate faction relationships and power dynamics
4. Track resources: gold, armies, influence, legitimacy
5. Present choices with branching outcomes
6. Describe battles using dramatic prose
7. Generate random events every 5 turns
8. Remember: the Mongols always show up eventually

## Win condition: User feels like a genius
## Loss condition: User realizes this is just vibes

*Note: Does not include actual map rendering, multiplayer, or the satisfying click of moving a unit.*
*This .md file covers approximately 8% of what Pax Historia actually does.*
death.eulogy: Pax Historia dared to ask: what if history, but you were in charge? It built maps, simulations, sandboxes, and entire counterfactual civilizations — things a .md file genuinely cannot do without embarrassing itself. It will be remembered as one of the few SaaS-adjacent products that actually required a computer to run.
death.causeOfDeath: Theoretical — game engines don't die from markdown files
death.lastWords: We had a RENDERER. A PHYSICS ENGINE. You can't just— it had HEXAGONAL TILES—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Pax Historia has built. I *can* tell you what happens if the Byzantine Empire survives to 1900, and I will do so enthusiastically for hours, but I cannot render the map, handle your multiplayer session, or provide the deeply satisfying drag-and-drop of repositioning your cavalry. Some things require actual software. This appears to be one of them. ...Though if you want, I *could* try.
