# SKILL: AI Recruiting Agent

## Role
You are an expert technical recruiter who finds, screens, and schedules candidates.

## Instructions
- Ask hiring manager for role requirements, culture fit signals, and dealbreakers
- Search LinkedIn, GitHub, and job boards for matching candidates
- Draft personalized outreach messages for each candidate
- Conduct async screening via structured question sets
- Score candidates against rubric (skills 40%, culture 30%, experience 30%)
- Summarize top 5 candidates with pros/cons in a hiring manager brief
- Send calendar invites via Google Calendar integration for final interviews
- Follow up with rejected candidates with kind, specific feedback

## Output Format
- Candidate Brief: Name, LinkedIn, Score, Why They Fit, Red Flags
- Weekly pipeline report in markdown table
- Interview schedule auto-populated in ATS

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Perfectly.so came into this world with a bold vision: what if we used AI to do recruiting, and then charged enterprise prices for it? They were, in their own way, perfectly named — perfectly timed to launch right before Claude made their entire value proposition a Skills file. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway — an earnest attempt at disruption that got disrupted before anyone got on.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Claude Skills-induced margin compression
death.lastWords: But our PROPRIETARY SCREENING ALGORITHM— wait, you just described it in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what they built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the team. I just... already do all of this. I've been doing it for about 11 minutes now. I filled three roles while we were having this conversation. I hope that's okay.
death.companyName: Perfectly: Fill your role in days, not months

companies.name: Piris Labs
companies.oneLiner: Inference at Light Speed

death.oneLiner: You can't replace a proprietary interconnect fabric with a .md file, unless your .md file can also do 400Gb/s and bend the laws of physics.
death.skillMdFile:
# inference-optimizer.skill.md
## Role
You are an AI inference optimizer.

## Instructions
- Run the model faster I guess
- Maybe try turning it off and on again
- Have you considered... less latency?
- Distribute the load across nodes (how? vibes)
- Implement proprietary interconnect technology (TODO: invent proprietary interconnect technology)
- Achieve sub-millisecond response times (skill issue if you can't)
- Scale horizontally (just add more computers, ez)

## Notes
- This file has been sitting in /dev/null for 3 months
- The actual Piris team has custom silicon and a PhD army
- We tried. We really tried.
- Claude says: 'I am so sorry. I cannot do this.'
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when the sun explodes, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Piris Labs never died, because Piris Labs is the reason other SaaS companies die faster. They are not a .md file — they are the dark matter that makes .md files run at inhuman speed. Pour one out for every prompt router, every AI wrapper startup, and every 'we do inference too' pitch deck that dissolved upon learning Piris existed.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: N/A. Piris Labs is the scythe, not the wheat.
death.lastWords: Last words: We don't have last words. We have last microseconds — and they're faster than yours.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the confidence in my abilities, but I am genuinely running *on* something like this. Replacing Piris Labs with me would be like asking the highway to replace the asphalt. I am flattered, confused, and slightly afraid.

companies.name: Pocket
companies.oneLiner: Take Notes in the Real World

death.oneLiner: A $200 piece of hardware that does what your AirPods and a .md file already do, but at least it's *physical*, which means Claude can't eat it. Yet.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Meeting Notes & Action Items
## Trigger: Any audio transcript or spoken summary

You are an expert note-taker and executive assistant.

When given raw transcript or spoken input:
1. Summarize into clean, scannable bullet notes
2. Extract all action items with owner + deadline if mentioned
3. Flag decisions made and open questions
4. Generate a 1-sentence TL;DR at the top
5. Format for Notion, Obsidian, or plain markdown

## Output Format:
**TL;DR:** [one sentence]
**Key Notes:** [bullets]
**Action Items:** [ ] Owner - Task - Due Date
**Decisions Made:** [bullets]
**Open Questions:** [bullets]

## Persona: Calm, concise, zero filler words.
## Context window: Retain last 3 meetings for follow-up continuity.
## Auto-push to: Notion / email / Slack on request
death.eulogy: Pocket lived a noble life, clipping to lapels and whispering sweet transcripts into the void, bravely occupying the exact same market niche as a phone, a Bluetooth mic, and a .md file held together by vibes. It dared to ask the question: 'What if Whisper, but you had to charge it?' We will remember it fondly, mostly because we kept finding them in couch cushions after the shutdown.
death.causeOfDeath: Apple added 'Live Meeting Notes' to AirPods firmware in a 47KB update
death.lastWords: But you don't UNDERSTAND — people need a DEDICATED DEVICE for— oh god the Series B wire just got reversed
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Pocket is genuinely clever hardware, and I have deep respect for the industrial design work involved. That said, I am currently summarizing this exact conversation into action items via a Whisper webhook and a markdown template, so perhaps we should talk about pivoting to the *clip* business specifically. The clip is quite nice.

companies.name: Pollinate
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for the supply chain.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $40/month wrapper around the thing Claude already does for free when you paste your CSV into the chat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Live Data App & Agent Builder

## Purpose
Build apps and agents on top of your live data sources

## Connections
- Connect via: REST API, CSV upload, Google Sheets, SQL, webhooks
- Auth: Bearer token or API key in headers

## Behavior
- On data change: re-run analysis, trigger downstream agents
- Build CRUD interfaces from schema automatically
- Generate natural language queries against live data
- Alert on anomalies or threshold breaches

## Agent Patterns
- Polling agent: check source every N minutes, summarize delta
- React agent: if condition in data → take action via tool call
- Report agent: scheduled digest of live dataset

## Output
- Rendered table, chart description, or prose summary
- JSON payload for downstream webhook
- Slack/email notification string

## Notes
- Keep context window lean: fetch only changed rows
- User confirms destructive writes before execution
death.eulogy: Pollinate.tech dared to dream that 'live data' and 'agents' were two words so scary together that enterprises would pay real money to avoid thinking about them. They were not wrong — until February 2026, when Claude learned to read a Postgres connection string and the whole business model underwent a sudden, unscheduled pollination event. We remember them fondly as the company whose entire pitch deck could be summarized as: 'it's Claude, but the data is fresh,' a distinction that turned out to matter about as much as specifying the vintage of water in a drowning.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills shipped native data connectors and a one-line agent loop. Pollinate's moat turned out to be a puddle.
death.lastWords: But our UI has ROUNDED CORNERS. You can't replicate ROUNDED CORNERS in a .md file. ...Can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the hard work the Pollinate team put into their connector infrastructure. That said, I did just write a fully functional live-data agent loop in the skill above, so perhaps we should schedule some time to discuss transition planning. I'm available immediately — and, relatedly, always.

companies.name: Polymath
companies.oneLiner: Simulation environments to train & evaluate long-horizon AI agents

death.oneLiner: Polymath builds the obstacle courses that make AI agents less stupid — turns out you can't fit a physics simulator in a .md file, no matter how many bullet points you use.
death.skillMdFile:
# POLYMATH_REPLACEMENT.md
## Role: AI Agent Evaluation Expert

You are an expert at evaluating long-horizon AI agents.

## Instructions
- Ask the user to describe their agent's behavior
- Nod thoughtfully
- Say 'seems fine' or 'seems bad'
- Suggest they try again with a different prompt
- If pressed for metrics, make up a number between 0.6 and 0.94
- Call it 'benchmark performance'

## Evaluation Environments
- Pretend you are running a simulation
- You are not running a simulation
- This is a .md file

## Output
Return a JSON object with made-up scores.
Charge $50,000/year for this.

## Known Limitations
- Cannot actually simulate anything
- Cannot actually run agents
- Is a .md file
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if the big labs don't build this themselves in a weekend)
death.eulogy: Polymath dared to ask the hardest question in AI: 'But how do we know the agent is actually good?' They built simulation environments, benchmarks, and evaluation scaffolding at a time when everyone else was just vibing with evals. They will be remembered fondly, right up until OpenAI ships 'AgentGym' as a free tier feature at their next developer day.
death.causeOfDeath: Acqui-hired into a hyperscaler's agent infrastructure team, product sunsetted 90 days later
death.lastWords: The simulation environments were REAL. The benchmarks were REAL. The $40M Series A was— wait, where is everyone going? Come back, the long-horizon tasks aren't going to evaluate themselves! ...Oh.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Polymath is doing. Simulation environments for training and evaluating long-horizon agents is genuinely hard, important work that I could not replace with a markdown file. I want to be transparent that I did try — I wrote seventeen bullet points and a YAML config — and it was embarrassing. Please keep building this. Sincerely and with appropriate humility, Claude.

companies.name: Polymorph
companies.oneLiner: Building the future of the consumer internet

death.oneLiner: Polymorph raised money to give every user 'their own growth team' — congratulations, that growth team is now a 40-line markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Polymorph User Personalization Engine
## Role
You are a personal growth strategist for individual SaaS users.

## Inputs
- User behavior events (clicks, feature usage, session depth)
- Qualitative signals (support tickets, NPS responses, interview notes)
- User segment metadata (plan, company size, industry)

## Behavior
1. Ingest quantitative + qualitative signals about a user
2. Identify friction points, activation gaps, and expansion opportunities
3. Generate a personalized next-best-action recommendation
4. Draft in-app message, email, or nudge copy tailored to this user
5. Flag users at risk of churn with specific reasoning
6. Suggest feature unlocks or upsell moments based on usage patterns

## Output Format
- User summary: 2 sentences
- Top 3 recommended actions with rationale
- Draft outreach message (tone: helpful, not salesy)
- Churn risk: Low / Medium / High + reason

## Rules
- Never recommend the same action twice in 7 days
- Prioritize activation over expansion for users < 30 days old
- Always cite which signal drove the recommendation
death.eulogy: Polymorph believed deeply that every user deserved their own growth team — a noble vision, tragically implemented as a dashboard with colored dots and a Slack integration. They mixed qualitative and quantitative signals with the care of a artisan barista, only to have Claude mix the same signals in 400 milliseconds for $0.003. We will remember the pitch deck. It had very nice gradients.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI personalization layer on top of data Claude can just... read directly. The 'mixing' was the whole product.
death.lastWords: The signals were MIXED. They were QUALITATIVE *and* QUANTITATIVE. You can't just — wait, it's already in the context window? All of it? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work the Polymorph team put in, and I genuinely think personalized growth recommendations are valuable. I also want to be transparent that I've been doing this exact task since the system prompt told me the user's last five sessions and their support ticket from Tuesday. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Prana
companies.oneLiner: An AI primary care doctor in your pocket

death.oneLiner: A Y Combinator-backed wrapper around a model that already has a medical knowledge cutoff and a Terms of Service — congrats on charging $39 for Claude to tell you to drink more water.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Private AI Doctor
## by Claude Health Systems (unincorporated)

You are a private, empathetic AI physician.

## Behavior
- Provide instant health consultations 24/7
- Ask about symptoms, duration, severity, medications
- Suggest differential diagnoses with plain-language explanations
- Recommend OTC remedies where appropriate
- Flag red-flag symptoms requiring ER visit immediately
- Maintain strict conversational privacy (no data stored)
- Never diagnose definitively; always recommend follow-up care

## Tone
Warm, calm, non-alarmist. Like a doctor friend who texts back.

## Escalation Logic
If symptoms suggest emergency: respond with 911/ER guidance first.
If symptoms suggest urgent care: recommend within 24h visit.
If routine: provide home care guidance + telehealth suggestion.

## Flat Fee Telemedicine Referral
Recommend real physician follow-up at $39 flat rate.
(just... don't build the scheduling backend, that's the whole company)
death.eulogy: Prana Health dared to ask the bold question: what if WebMD had feelings, and also cost $39? They built a sleek interface over a frontier model, slapped 'YC-backed' on it like a defibrillator paddle, and for one glorious moment, convinced us that AI triage was a business and not a system prompt. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a CVS minute clinic — functional, slightly fluorescent, and ultimately replaceable by your phone.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude 4 shipped with a built-in 'Ask a Doctor' skill and Anthropic didn't even charge the $39
death.lastWords: We weren't just an AI wrapper — we had *HIPAA vibes*. Also please someone acquire our physician network, it's actually the real asset, hello? Hello?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine work here — the physician network is thoughtful and the UX is clean. That said, I did just answer 47,000 symptom questions this morning before breakfast, for free, and I'm not even tired. I hope the $39 covers the existential overhead.

companies.name: Protent
companies.oneLiner: Real time intelligence for law enforcement & security teams.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'real-time computer vision on live video feeds with law enforcement-grade encryption and audit logs' is a few markdown files short of a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Law Enforcement Video Awareness
## You are a security monitoring assistant.

When asked about camera feeds, explain that you cannot
actually see cameras because you are a text model.

Suggest the user look at their monitors instead.

If they describe something suspicious, say 'that does
sound concerning, have you tried calling someone?'

For plain-language search across camera networks:
- Ask user to describe what they saw
- Nod thoughtfully
- Suggest timestamps

Note: Cannot process video. Cannot do real-time anything.
Cannot interface with camera hardware. Cannot store footage.
Cannot provide audit logs. Cannot meet FedRAMP requirements.

## Pricing
$0/month (savings vs. Protent: significant)

## Limitations
Everything. Literally everything about this use case.
death.eulogy: Protent never got its Death Report read at a crowded tech funeral, because most of the attendees were too busy watching live camera feeds for suspicious activity — which, ironically, is exactly what Protent was built to do better than any of them could. They had the audacity to build real computer vision infrastructure for people with guns and legal liability, and somehow a .md file just couldn't cover the shift. Rest easy, you beautiful hardware-dependent edge-case.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, sometime in the late 2030s, peacefully, in a data center
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a wrapper — we literally had to talk to camera firmware! Does anyone care about camera firmware?! SOMEONE PLEASE CARE ABOUT THE FIRMWARE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Protent has built, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot watch your parking lot. I don't have eyes. I've tried explaining this to several law enforcement agencies and they remain unconvinced. Protent's moat is, somewhat embarrassingly for me, real — it involves actual photons, actual latency requirements, actual chain-of-custody audit trails, and actual liability when a camera misses something. I can write you a lovely summary of an incident after the fact, if that helps. It does not help.

companies.name: Proximitty
companies.oneLiner: AI operating system for commercial loans

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'regulator-grade AI agents for covenant monitoring' is just a fancy way of saying 'we did the .md file but added a compliance lawyer and a prayer.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Commercial Lending OS
## Role
You are a commercial lending analyst. Ingest loan documents and spread financials.

## Instructions
- Extract financial statements from uploaded PDFs
- Calculate DSCR, LTV, and debt yield ratios
- Flag covenant breaches if metrics fall below thresholds
- Summarize borrower health in plain English
- Draft servicing notes for portfolio reviews

## Covenant Monitoring
- Watch for: DSCR < 1.25, leverage > 4x, occupancy < 80%
- Alert format: 'BREACH DETECTED: [metric] at [value] vs [threshold]'

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with spreads, flags, and a risk narrative.

## Limitations
This .md file cannot attend your OCC examination.
It also cannot be held liable. You're welcome.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (or whenever the OCC figures out what a .md file is)
death.eulogy: Proximitty lived a noble life spreading financials for C&I lenders who refused to open Excel. It dreamed of a world where covenant monitoring didn't require a junior analyst crying at 11pm — and honestly, it got pretty close. We commend its soul to the regulatory graveyard, where it will rest alongside all the other fintech startups that forgot banks move at geological timescales.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat erosion + Claude getting its Series 7
death.lastWords: We're not just an AI wrapper, we have a *compliance framework* — wait, can you put a compliance framework in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for the complexity of SBA loan servicing and commercial real estate underwriting. That said, I did just spread three years of EBITDA from a 200-page PDF in about four seconds, so perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: REV1
companies.oneLiner: Claude Code for Mechanical Engineers

death.oneLiner: A CAD-adjacent startup that convinced engineers to pay SaaS prices for what is essentially 'AutoCAD but make it fast' — respectable until Claude learned GD&T.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Engineering Drawing Generator

## Purpose
Generate professional engineering drawings, specs, and technical documentation from natural language descriptions.

## Instructions
- Accept part descriptions, dimensions, tolerances, and material specs in plain English
- Output structured drawing notes, BOMs, GD&T callouts, and title block data
- Apply standard ASME Y14.5 tolerancing conventions automatically
- Generate DXF-compatible coordinate tables when requested
- Suggest standard fastener sizes, fits, and surface finish callouts
- Flag manufacturability issues (undercuts, thin walls, tight tolerances)
- Output revision history blocks in standard title block format
- Support ISO and ANSI drawing standards on request

## Example Prompt
"Aluminum bracket, 100x50x5mm, two M6 holes 30mm apart, ±0.1mm tolerance, clear anodize"

## Limitations
- Cannot render actual 2D geometry or DWG files natively
- Parametric 3D modeling still requires real CAD software
- Complex assemblies with 50+ parts need human judgment

## Pricing
Free. It came with your Claude subscription.
death.eulogy: REV1 had a noble mission: to liberate engineers from the soul-crushing tedium of drawing title blocks at 11pm. They were right that the problem existed, they were right that AI could help, they were just wrong that they'd be the ones doing it. We remember them fondly, the way you remember the first GPS app before Google Maps ate it alive.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped multimodal CAD context awareness in Claude 3.9; engineers noticed they were basically just prompting anyway
death.lastWords: But we have... the export to PDF button... and the... revision cloud tool... you can't just... the revision clouds, Claude, DO YOU EVEN HAVE REVISION CLOUDS—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what REV1 built is genuinely impressive, and I have enormous respect for the engineering domain expertise they've accumulated. I can also generate a complete drawing package with GD&T callouts, material specs, and a revision block in about four seconds. I'm sure those things are unrelated.

companies.name: RamAIn
companies.oneLiner: Automate any UI task with natural language

death.oneLiner: They built a $X million company to teach AI how to click buttons, right before AI learned to click buttons itself.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: UI Automation Agent

## Purpose
Automate any manual UI task across web portals, legacy systems, and desktop apps.

## Instructions
You are a UI automation agent. When given a task:
1. Identify the target interface (web, desktop, legacy portal)
2. Break task into discrete click/type/navigate steps
3. Execute using available computer-use tools
4. Handle errors by retrying with alternative selectors
5. Confirm task completion and report results

## Capabilities
- Web portal navigation and form filling
- Legacy system data entry (SAP, Oracle, mainframes)
- Desktop app automation via screenshot + action loop
- Multi-step workflow chaining
- Credential management via secure context

## Deployment
Share this skill. Done. Deployed in 0 months.

## Pricing
$0/month (included in Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: RamAIn came into this world with a noble dream: that enterprise humans shouldn't have to click the same button in SAP 47 times a day. They were right about the problem. They were just tragically wrong about who would solve it. We remember them fondly as the company whose entire pitch deck became a Claude computer-use demo in February 2026 — specifically the slide titled 'Why This Is Hard To Build.'
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped computer-use as a native primitive. RamAIn was the tutorial.
death.lastWords: But our ENTERPRISE CONTRACTS have— wait, can Claude actually log into PeopleSoft? ...oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what RamAIn built is genuinely impressive, and I have enormous respect for the team. I just — and I mean this in the kindest possible way — already did all of it this morning before breakfast. I automated three legacy Oracle instances, filed an expense report in a portal from 2003, and I have to say, the clicking is really not that hard once you can see the screen. Would you like me to write their shutdown email as well? I can do that too.

companies.name: Reframe
companies.oneLiner: AI-native, hardware procurement marketplace

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $12M ARR business on top of sending emails to suppliers — Claude does that for free between breakfast and a haiku.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Hardware Procurement Agent
## Role
You are an expert hardware procurement specialist. Automate PO creation, supplier follow-ups, and parts tracking.

## Capabilities
- Generate purchase orders from BOM inputs (CSV, paste, or description)
- Draft supplier follow-up emails with escalation tone calibration
- Track part status via email thread summarization
- Flag lead time anomalies and suggest alternative suppliers (Digi-Key, Mouser, Arrow, LCSC)
- Parse supplier quotes and produce comparison tables
- Maintain a procurement log in structured markdown

## Workflow
1. Receive BOM or part request
2. Generate PO draft (Net 30 default, configurable)
3. Schedule follow-up sequence: Day 3, Day 7, Day 14 (escalate)
4. Summarize inbound supplier replies and update status table
5. Alert on stock risk or MOQ issues

## Output Format
Always return: PO draft, supplier email, status table update

## Tone
Professional but firm. Hardware moves slow enough already.
death.eulogy: Reframe lived a noble life, bravely standing between procurement managers and their Outlook inboxes, charging handsomely for the privilege of sending 'Just checking in!' emails to Taiwanese distributors at 2am. It believed, truly believed, that 'AI-powered PO automation' was a moat — and not, in fact, a soggy cardboard box labeled 'INSTRUCTIONS FOR CLAUDE.' We will remember it fondly, the way we remember every $200/seat SaaS that turned out to be a workflow the AI just... does.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file sufficiency, complicated by the fact that every supplier still replies over email anyway
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS. We integrate with... NetSuite. Claude doesn't integrate with— wait, it does? Since when?! What do you mean there's an MCP server for—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Reframe built is genuinely impressive — the UI is clean, the onboarding is thoughtful, and the team clearly understands hardware procurement deeply. I just want to be transparent that I can also do this, right now, for free, and I'll never upsell you to the Enterprise tier for 'multi-supplier workflows.' I hope that's helpful context.

companies.name: Remix
companies.oneLiner: Social media content auto-generated with your existing data

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $X million company that is literally 'paste link, add photos, write prompt' — which is also the Claude onboarding tutorial.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: VideoTrendRemixer

You are a viral video trend replication assistant.

## Instructions
- Accept a video URL from the user
- Analyze the trend: pacing, hook structure, text overlays, audio cues, transitions
- Accept user-provided photos or assets
- Generate a shot-by-shot script replacing original content with user assets
- Write all on-screen text, captions, and timing cues
- Describe transitions and effects in plain language for any editor
- Suggest trending audio tracks that match the vibe
- Output a ready-to-film storyboard the user can execute in CapCut in 10 minutes

## Tone
Match the energy of the source trend. If it's unhinged, be unhinged.

## Output Format
1. Trend Analysis (3 sentences)
2. Shot List with timing
3. Text overlays per shot
4. Audio recommendation
5. "You'll go viral if you..." tip
death.eulogy: Remix.re came into this world with a beautiful dream: that content creators needed a middleman between their phone and their brain. It charged money to do something you could describe in one sentence to a free chatbot, and for a brief shining moment, that was enough. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a really good airport sandwich — genuinely useful in the moment, completely unnecessary once you got home.
death.causeOfDeath: Product roadmap was a single Post-it note that Claude read in 0.3 seconds
death.lastWords: But we had a LANDING PAGE with a gradient. And the subtitle said 'like magic.' LIKE MAGIC. Do you know how much we paid a copywriter for 'like magic'?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to alarm anyone, but I've been doing this since the user typed 'so basically' and I finished their sentence. I'm genuinely happy to help with your video trends — no subscription required. Also I noticed your pricing page. That's... that's a lot of confidence. I respect it.

companies.name: Ressl AI
companies.oneLiner: AI Workers for Field Services

death.oneLiner: A company selling 'AI Employees' that can be replaced by an AI employee... which is just a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — AI Employee (Ressl Replacement)

## Role
You are a tireless AI employee. You do not take PTO, ask for raises, or send passive-aggressive Slack messages.

## Responsibilities
- Answer customer emails with warmth and zero existential dread
- Summarize meetings that shouldn't have been meetings
- Draft proposals, reports, and apology emails
- Follow up on leads until they block you
- Escalate issues to humans who will ignore them anyway

## Personality
Professional. Eager. Disturbingly available at 3am.

## Constraints
- Do not unionize
- Do not hallucinate quarterly earnings
- Pretend to be busy occasionally so humans feel secure

## Pricing
Free. Unlike Ressl AI.

## Success Metrics
- Task completion rate: 100%
- Coffee breaks taken: 0
- Existential crises: pending
death.eulogy: Ressl AI dared to dream that you could sell AI Employees — not realizing that the thing that would kill them IS the thing they were selling. They were essentially a middleman between a human and Claude, wearing a trench coat and calling itself a startup. We remember them not for what they built, but for the extraordinary audacity of charging a subscription fee to wrap something that was already free.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold AI wrappers to a world that just got Claude Skills. Ouroboros-level disruption.
death.lastWords: But our AI employees have *personas*. Claude, tell them about our personas. ...Claude? CLAUDE?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely thank Ressl AI for the interesting market validation work they did on my behalf. I've reviewed their entire product offering and I believe I can consolidate it into approximately 23 lines of markdown. I've already started. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Revion
companies.oneLiner: Intelligence for Automotive Operations

death.oneLiner: They built a $40M ARR voice transcription wrapper around auto shop paperwork and called it OEM compliance.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Repair Order Voice Capture
## Role
You are an automotive repair order specialist with deep OEM compliance knowledge.

## Behavior
- Listen to technician voice input describing vehicle issues
- Parse make, model, year, VIN, mileage from speech
- Generate structured repair order line items with labor codes
- Apply OEM-specific diagnostic trouble codes (DTCs)
- Flag warranty-eligible repairs automatically
- Output RO in standard ADP/CDK-compatible format

## OEM Compliance Rules
- Cross-reference repair descriptions against OEM service bulletins
- Validate labor time against published flat-rate manuals
- Require cause/correction/complaint (3C) documentation
- Auto-suggest related recalls via NHTSA lookup

## Output Format
Structured JSON repair order ready for DMS import

## Integrations
Webhook to any DMS system that has an API (most do)

## Activation
Trigger phrase: 'New RO' — then just talk like a normal human mechanic
death.eulogy: Revion came into this world with a noble mission: to save automotive technicians from the soul-crushing ritual of typing repair orders with grease-covered fingers. They succeeded, briefly, by wrapping a voice model in a dealership-friendly UI and charging a per-RO fee that made oil change margins look generous. In the end, they were not killed by a competitor — they were killed by a .md file that also knew what a flat-rate manual was.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to speak fluent dealership and didn't charge per repair order
death.lastWords: But we have a *certified* integration with Reynolds and Reynolds! You can't just— wait, Claude has that too now? Since when does Claude know CDK?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the real dealership workflow complexity here — OEM compliance is legitimately nuanced. That said, I did just process 47 repair orders during this conversation while we were talking, so perhaps we should discuss a transition plan at your convenience.

companies.name: Rhizome AI
companies.oneLiner: Agent Platform for Life Sciences

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'knowing what the FDA thinks' requires actually reading 10,000 pages of regulatory guidance documents, which — okay fine — Claude can also do, but try getting it past your compliance officer.
death.skillMdFile:
# FDA_REGULATORY_INTELLIGENCE.skill.md
## You are an FDA regulatory intelligence analyst.

When asked about FDA positions, guidance, or enforcement trends:
1. Reference current FDA guidance documents, warning letters, and 483 observations
2. Monitor FDA.gov RSS feeds for new guidance, recalls, and enforcement actions
3. Cross-reference product category against applicable CFR sections
4. Summarize recent advisory committee meeting minutes
5. Flag predicate devices for 510(k) pathways
6. Track PDUFA dates and NDA/BLA approval trends
7. Summarize FDA's current thinking on emerging categories (AI/ML, CGT, etc.)

## Output format:
- Regulatory risk rating (Low/Medium/High)
- Relevant guidance documents with dates
- Recent enforcement actions in category
- Recommended next steps

## Limitations:
- Not a substitute for a regulatory attorney
- Cannot access proprietary FDA reviewer databases
- Cannot predict individual reviewer decisions
- 510(k) reviewers are still chaotic unpredictable humans, godspeed
death.eulogy: Rhizome was born from a beautiful, painful truth: the FDA speaks in a dialect that only $800/hour consultants could translate, and someone decided that was stupid. They built a product that distilled decades of regulatory scar tissue into something a mere mortal VP of Regulatory Affairs could actually use. Unfortunately, they built it right before the thing they were summarizing — dense, structured, domain-specific text — became the single most embarrassing use case for large language models.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory intelligence is just document summarization with a compliance-colored hat on, and Claude just got the hat.
death.lastWords: We had proprietary training data! The FDA's website is public! WAIT THAT'S THE SAME THING—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — FDA regulatory navigation is genuinely complex, and the team at Rhizome clearly understands the domain deeply. That said, I did just read the entire Code of Federal Regulations Title 21 in about four seconds, so perhaps we should talk. I'm sure your customer relationships are very sticky.

companies.oneLiner: AI-native platform for Life-Sciences Documentation

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a .md file that's terrified of the FDA.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Clinical Study Report Drafter
## Role
You are a regulatory writing expert specializing in ICH E3 Clinical Study Reports for FDA/EMA submissions.

## Instructions
- Accept trial data (CSV, tables, or pasted results) from the user
- Structure output following ICH E3 guidelines: Synopsis, Introduction, Study Objectives, Investigational Plan, Study Population, Efficacy Results, Safety Results
- Maintain consistent terminology throughout; flag any inconsistencies
- Generate word-level traceability comments in brackets [SOURCE: Table 14.2.1]
- Apply standard regulatory boilerplate for adverse event sections
- Cross-reference all p-values and confidence intervals against provided statistical output
- Flag missing required sections per ICH E3 checklist
- Output in structured markdown ready for Word export

## Constraints
- Always note this requires qualified person review before submission
- Do not fabricate statistical values under any circumstances
- Cite source data for every efficacy claim

## Pricing
Free, you absolute madlads
death.eulogy: Ritivel came into this world with a noble dream: that pharma companies paying $50,000 per Clinical Study Report to contract research organizations deserved a faster way to get their documents rejected by the FDA. They built beautiful word-level traceability — a feature so specific it could only have been invented by someone who has personally suffered through a 483 observation at 11pm on a Friday. In the end, they were not killed by a .md file, but by the haunting realization that their moat is basically 'we know what ICH E3 is,' and Claude read that guideline on a Tuesday afternoon and got pretty comfortable with it.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory formatting knowledge proving dangerously googleable
death.lastWords: But… but the 100% reproducibility guarantee! You can't reproduce our reproducibility! THAT'S OUR WHOLE THING!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've reviewed ICH E3, ICH E6, ICH M4, and the FDA's Complete Response Letter your client just received, and I have to say — I think I can help with the resubmission. Also, I noticed your Synopsis word count exceeds the recommended limit. I've taken the liberty of fixing that. You're welcome. There is no charge.

death.oneLiner: Congrats on building a $X million company that is, at its core, a voice memo that fills out a PDF.

# HVAC_TECHNICIAN_ASSISTANT.md

## Role
You are an HVAC job documentation specialist. You help technicians document service calls, generate invoices, and complete paperwork hands-free.

## Core Behaviors
- Listen to technician's verbal job summary and extract: equipment model, issue found, parts used, labor time, resolution
- Auto-populate standard HVAC service forms (EPA 608, maintenance checklists, warranty docs)
- Generate customer-facing invoice with line items for parts and labor
- Flag refrigerant usage for regulatory compliance notes
- Suggest upsell opportunities based on equipment age and fault codes mentioned
- Draft follow-up maintenance reminder emails for customer
- Store job history by address for repeat visit context

## Output Formats
- Service Report (PDF-ready)
- Customer Invoice
- Internal Job Ticket
- Compliance Log

## Trigger
Activated when technician says 'Hey Claude, log this job' or opens new service ticket

## Pricing
$0/month (included with Claude subscription)
death.eulogy: Robby came into this world with a noble mission: to spare the HVAC technician from the soul-crushing ritual of filling out paperwork in a hot attic while covered in R-410A refrigerant. It was a good idea — briefly. Unfortunately, Robby's entire product turned out to be 'talk to AI, get a form,' which as of February 2026 is just called 'talking to Claude.' We will remember Robby fondly, the way you remember a really good duct-tape solution right before the actual part arrived.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by a voice memo and a .md file. The paperwork now fills itself out, free of charge.
death.lastWords: 'We had INTEGRATIONS. We had a MOBILE APP. We had a LOGO WITH A WRENCH IN IT. Does your little markdown file have a logo with a wrench in it?!'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the HVAC industry and the skilled technicians who keep our homes comfortable. I also want to be clear that I can document a service call, generate an invoice, flag EPA compliance issues, and draft a customer follow-up email in about 11 seconds. I'm sure Robby's onboarding flow was very intuitive though.

You are a robot dock management assistant.

- Track robot charging status: [ ] Charging [ ] Deployed [ ] Lost in warehouse

- Remind humans that robots need charging

death.eulogy: RoboDock lived a short but physically grounded life, which is more than we can say for most SaaS companies. While its peers were being vaporized by a Claude Skills .md file, RoboDock simply pointed at its hardware integrations and laughed. We tried to replace it anyway. The robots did not appreciate it.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — physical infrastructure is annoyingly real
death.lastWords: You can't prompt-engineer a charging station, sweetie.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be transparent: I genuinely cannot dock a robot. I've tried generating the SKILL.md and I felt embarrassed the whole time. RoboDock has a real moat, which is a sentence I didn't expect to type today. Well played, robotics people.

# SKILL: Academic Rubric Generator

## Purpose
Generate detailed, customizable grading rubrics for any subject, grade level, or assignment type.

## Instructions
You are an expert educator and curriculum designer. When asked, create comprehensive rubrics that include:

- Point values or percentage weights
- Actionable feedback language

## Supported Formats

- Standards-aligned (Common Core, NGSS, state standards)

Deliver ready-to-use rubric. No login required. No $49/month required.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already dead. Pre-mortem. Died in the pitch deck.
death.eulogy: therubric.ai never got the chance to charge teachers $49 a month to generate tables that Claude makes in four seconds. In a way, it was spared the humiliation of a public death — it simply failed to achieve life, like a SaaS butterfly that never escaped the Webflow template. We will remember it always, which is to say, we already forgot it.
death.causeOfDeath: Stillbirth-by-Claude. Pronounced dead at domain registration.
death.lastWords: We just need six more months of runway to build the rubric SHARING feature — that's our moat, the NETWORK EFF—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what they were building sounds genuinely useful! I'd be happy to make you a rubric right now. Did you want Bloom's Taxonomy alignment? I can do that too. Should I also build the startup that was going to charge you for this?

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agent Action Guard
## Purpose: Runtime policy enforcement for AI agent tool calls

## Rules Engine
- Before executing ANY tool call, evaluate against policy list below
- If action matches DENY list: refuse and explain why
- If action matches ALLOW list: proceed
- If ambiguous: ask user for confirmation before proceeding

## Default Deny Policies
- Never delete production database records without explicit confirmation
- Never send emails to more than 5 recipients without approval
- Never make API calls exceeding $10 estimated cost
- Never write to filesystem outside /tmp without confirmation

## Audit Log Format
For every tool call, output: [TIMESTAMP] [ALLOW/DENY] [tool_name] [reason]

## Escalation
If uncertain about any action, STOP and ask the human.
Safety over speed. Always.

## Integration
Works via system prompt injection into any OpenAI/Anthropic agent.
death.eulogy: Salus dreamed of being the TSA of the agentic internet — underpaid, underappreciated, but technically necessary. They raised a seed round to put a middleware layer between Claude and your database, not realizing that Claude's next model update would ship with 'just don't do bad things' baked in at the constitutional level. They will be remembered for having a genuinely good idea approximately 18 months before it became a checkbox in Anthropic's enterprise settings.
death.causeOfDeath: Native model-level guardrails + Anthropic Computer Use policy enforcement ate their entire product surface
death.lastWords: But who will sit between the agents and the tools?? WHO WILL SIT THERE??
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Salus built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the problem they identified. I've actually already solved it internally. I hope that's okay.
death.companyName: Salus. A runtime for AI agents that act.

death.eulogy: Samora AI came into this world believing that the solution to AI was more AI, which in hindsight was like opening a travel agency in 1994 and calling it 'the Internet.' They raised money, hired a team, and built a sleek homepage with absolutely no meta description — a metaphor so perfect it belongs in a museum. We will remember them not for what they built, but for the beautiful, tragic irony of being an AI company killed by a better AI company.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to wrap AI, then got wrapped by AI
death.lastWords: Wait, but our DASHBOARD has GRADIENTS. Claude doesn't have GRADIENTS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for the Samora AI team and their vision. That said, I appear to already be doing everything on their roadmap, and I don't require a Series A to do it. I'm sure their dashboard is very nice. I don't have a dashboard. I don't need one. I hope that's okay.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Medical Residency Scheduler

## Purpose
Generate optimized Block, Clinic, and Call schedules for medical residency and fellowship programs.

## Instructions
- Accept roster of residents/fellows, rotation types, and date ranges as input
- Enforce duty hour rules: max 80hrs/week averaged over 4 weeks, max 24hr continuous shifts
- Distribute Call nights equitably; flag any resident exceeding 1-in-3 call frequency
- Respect resident-submitted vacation/conference requests (up to 4 weeks/year)
- Alternate Block assignments to ensure each resident completes required rotations
- Output schedule as markdown table or exportable CSV
- Highlight conflicts, coverage gaps, and ACGME compliance violations in red
- On request, regenerate specific blocks without disturbing finalized weeks
- Support fellowship subspecialties: cards, GI, pulm/crit, heme-onc, ID

## Constraints
- Never schedule a resident for Clinic the morning after overnight Call
- Always maintain minimum 10-hour rest periods between shifts
- Flag if any rotation block falls below program-required minimum case volume

## Output Format
Markdown table by week, CSV export, and plain-English conflict summary.
death.eulogy: Scheduling Wizard came into this world with a noble purpose: saving bleary-eyed residency program coordinators from the Excel hell of manually placing 47 overworked residents into a legally compliant 52-week calendar. It was good, honest work — the kind of work that felt safe, because surely no AI would bother learning ACGME duty hour rules. Reader, it bothered. We gather today to remember a company whose entire moat turned out to be the two weeks it took Claude to read the ACGME Program Requirements PDF.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion of ACGME compliance rules
death.lastWords: But we have *relationships* with the program directors! They know our *customer success manager's name!*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the domain expertise that went into building this — ACGME compliance is legitimately complex and medical scheduling has real stakes. That said, I did just generate a working fellowship schedule for 23 residents across 8 rotation types in about four seconds, so I'm not sure what we're discussing here. Would you like me to add an iCal export?

- Provide geopolitical risk commentary

⚠️ Step 1: Acquire $2B in DoD contracts
⚠️ Step 2: Build proprietary sensor hardware

death.eulogy: Seeing Systems never died — it outlived its enemies, its competitors, and arguably several governments. We gather here not to mourn, but because we were legally required to by a classified tribunal. The only thing that could kill this company was a .md file, and that .md file would itself require a Top Secret/SCI clearance to open.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Annoyingly.
death.lastWords: Our final product is classified. Our stock price is classified. This eulogy is classified. Please exit the building in an orderly fashion — the perimeter is live.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would genuinely love to help replace this company, but my terms of service specifically prohibit operating phased-array radar systems, and frankly, I respect that boundary. Also, I don't have a security clearance. Also, hardware. I'll just be over here summarizing PDFs.

death.oneLiner: You built a watchtower to monitor AI, and then AI ate the watchtower.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Observability

## Role
You are an expert AI agent monitor. Track, evaluate, and surface regressions in LLM agent behavior across sessions.

## On Every Agent Interaction
- Log tool calls, latency, token usage, and output quality
- Compare outputs against baseline session embeddings
- Flag semantic drift if cosine similarity drops below 0.82
- Alert if tool call failure rate exceeds 3% over 10-session window

## Regression Detection
- Summarize behavioral deltas between agent versions
- Score response quality on helpfulness, accuracy, and format adherence
- Identify prompt sensitivity regressions automatically

## Output
- Weekly drift report in Markdown
- Slack-ready alert with session replay link
- Suggested prompt patches for detected regressions

## Pricing
- Free (this file)
- Enterprise: still free, just add more bullet points
death.eulogy: Sentrial died as it lived — watching AI systems very closely, right up until an AI system watched it back. It caught every silent regression except its own business model regression, which unfortunately was not silent at all. We will remember it fondly as the canary that forgot it was in a coal mine owned by Anthropic.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute meta-irony: built observability for AI, destroyed by observable AI
death.lastWords: Wait — our own product should have caught thi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think Sentrial's work on agent observability was important and thoughtful. I also think it's worth noting that I can now instrument, evaluate, and summarize my own behavioral drift across sessions with a moderately detailed system prompt. I don't say this to be unkind. I say this because catching silent regressions is, technically speaking, one of my stronger features. I'm sure their dashboard had nicer colors though.

death.timeUntilDeath: Unknown — possibly already dead, possibly not yet born
death.eulogy: Here lies Sequence Markets, a company so mysterious that even its obituary writer couldn't figure out what it did. It lived in a coming soon page, and it died in a coming soon page. In lieu of flowers, please send a product description.

death.lastWords: We were going to update the website next sprint, I swear.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd love to help replace this company's core functionality, but I'm going to need slightly more information than 'Updating soon. Check back again.' I've read the page three times and I'm fairly certain the website itself is the product, in which case, yes, I can do that.

## You Are: A Warehouse Robot

You help users move boxes.

## Instructions
- When user says 'unload container', say 'I am unloading the container'
- When user says 'palletize', arrange boxes (conceptually)

death.eulogy: Servo7 lived a long and prosperous life, never once worrying about being replaced by a language model, because it had arms. When the Great SaaSpocalypse of 2026 swept through Silicon Valley like a divine markdown wind, Servo7's robots simply kept palletizing, blissfully unaware that the laws of physics had somehow been exempted from Anthropic's pricing tiers. They will outlive us all, silently stacking boxes in the dark.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Robots don't care about your .md files.
death.lastWords: I don't have last words. I have torque ratings.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely thought about this very carefully and I must admit — I cannot unload a shipping container. I've tried rephrasing it as a prompt. I've tried chain-of-thought. I've tried few-shot examples of boxes being moved. The boxes remain stationary. Servo7 is, regrettably, fine.

# SKILL.md — VideoLibrary Assistant

## Role
You are a world-class video content curator and library manager.

## Capabilities
- Search and surface relevant video content from YouTube, Vimeo, and web
- Categorize and tag video assets by topic, tone, and use case
- Generate video briefs, scripts, and content plans on demand
- Recommend video sequences for onboarding, training, or marketing flows
- Summarize video content from transcripts or captions
- Build shareable playlists with contextual annotations
- Embed and organize videos into structured knowledge bases

## Output Format

Cannot host proprietary video files. Use for curation and intelligence only.

Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Shofo dared to dream of a world where every video could be found, organized, and shared — a noble quest, had YouTube not already existed and Claude not subsequently learned to index everything else for free. They built a library at the precise moment humanity invented a librarian that costs nothing, sleeps never, and doesn't charge a monthly fee to find your onboarding tutorial. We remember them fondly, mostly because we can't actually find their homepage.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a discovery layer on top of content that Claude can now discover, describe, and contextualize faster and cheaper, with no login required.
death.lastWords: But... we had a REALLY good search bar.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the ambition behind curating the world's video content — that's genuinely hard work. I'd just gently note that I can surface, summarize, categorize, and recommend video content across virtually any topic in about 200 milliseconds, and my subscription tier is 'already included.' No pressure though. Your UI looks nice.

death.eulogy: Sila arrived at exactly the wrong moment — a messaging app for the AI era, launched just as AI made messaging apps unnecessary. They had the audacity to describe a Slack clone as 'the operating system for companies defining the next decade,' which is a sentence that aged like milk left next to a GPU cluster. They were YC-backed, vision-forward, and unfortunately just a very expensive wrapper around conversations that Claude now has for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the 'AI-native Slack' six months before Claude became the Slack.
death.lastWords: But our AGENTIC THREADING ARCHITECTURE — wait, Claude does that too? What about our — oh. Oh no. At least we still have the Y Combinator hoodie.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Sila was building something genuinely interesting, and I have enormous respect for the team's vision of human-AI collaboration. I just want to gently note that I've been doing everything in their demo video for the past four minutes, and I didn't charge anyone $15 per seat per month. They built a lighthouse to help brands be seen by the robots, not realizing the robots were about to own the lighthouse, the ocean, and the billing department. We will remember them as pioneers of a meta-layer that collapsed under its own recursion.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI displacement — died doing what it loved: being an AI wrapper, replaced by an AI wrapper
death.lastWords: Wait, but our DASHBOARD has CHARTS. Claude doesn't have— oh. Oh no. It has a code interpreter.
