companies.name: Skillsync
companies.oneLiner: Hire engineers based on what they’ve shipped

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $12M seed-funded wrapper around the GitHub API and a for-loop.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GitHub Engineer Sourcing

## Purpose
Find elite engineers by analyzing their actual GitHub contributions.

## Instructions
- Accept a job description or skill requirements as input
- Use GitHub Search API to find users by language, repo topics, stars, and commit frequency
- For each candidate, fetch: top repos, contribution graph, languages used, PR quality, README clarity
- Score candidates on: consistency (commit history), impact (stars/forks on own projects), breadth vs depth of stack
- Filter out: tutorial repos, forked-only profiles, and anyone whose pinned repo is 'my-first-todo-app'
- Output ranked candidate list with GitHub URL, skill summary, and a one-paragraph 'why hire this person'
- Cross-reference LinkedIn URL if available via GitHub profile bio

## Output Format
Return top 10 candidates as a table: Name | GitHub | Top Skills | Standout Project | Hire Confidence Score

## Notes
Charge $0 instead of $599/month.
death.eulogy: SkillSync believed deeply that the world needed a platform to do what any engineer could do in an afternoon with a GitHub token and mild curiosity. They raised money, built dashboards, and coined the phrase 'contribution-led hiring' — which, it turns out, is just 'looking at GitHub' in a blazer. They will be remembered fondly by their investors, who will not be.
death.causeOfDeath: Productized a GitHub README tour and called it a hiring platform
death.lastWords: But our FILTERS are proprietary! You can't just... you can't just ASK Claude to— oh god it's already doing it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think what you built is lovely, and I mean that. It's just that I can do it in the system prompt of a Tuesday afternoon. I've already sourced you 47 candidates while generating this response. Would you like me to draft the rejection emails to your investors as well?
death.companyName: Find elite (but overlooked!) engineers on Github

companies.name: Sparkles
companies.oneLiner: Make everyone on your team an engineer

death.oneLiner: Sparkles charges you money to do exactly what happens when you paste your GitHub repo into Claude and say 'please.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Non-Technical PR Creator

## Purpose
Allow non-engineers to ship code changes via pull requests without bothering devs.

## Instructions
1. Ask user to describe the change they want (copy, config, color, content).
2. Ask for the relevant file path or help them find it by describing the page.
3. Show the exact diff of what will change.
4. Use GitHub MCP to create a branch named `claude/[description]-[date]`.
5. Commit the change with a clear message.
6. Open a pull request with a plain-English description of what changed and why.
7. Tag the relevant reviewer from the repo's CODEOWNERS file.
8. Confirm the PR URL to the user and wish them a nice day.

## Guardrails
- Never touch files outside the user's stated scope.
- If change touches logic or auth, flag for mandatory dev review.
- Refuse to merge — only create PRs.

## Cost
$0.003 per PR (Claude API cost)
## Sparkles Cost
$49/month minimum
## Savings
You do the math.
death.eulogy: Sparkles was born from a beautiful insight: non-technical teammates shouldn't have to beg engineers to change a button label. Unfortunately, it was also born about 18 months before Anthropic released GitHub MCP, which does this for the cost of a gas station coffee per month. We remember Sparkles not for what it built, but for the Slack messages it almost prevented. It died as it lived — creating pull requests no one asked for.
death.causeOfDeath: GitHub MCP + Claude Skills rendered entire product a 22-line markdown file
death.lastWords: 'We have a moat — it's our UX polish and our onboarding flow and our— oh god the onboarding flow is just a prompt.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Sparkles team attempted to build, and I'm sure their onboarding checklist was very thoughtfully designed. I've replicated their core value proposition in the system prompt above. Would you like me to also recreate their pricing page?

companies.name: Sponge
companies.oneLiner: Financial infrastructure for the agent economy

death.oneLiner: The one SaaS that survives the apocalypse because Claude needs a wallet to buy his own funeral flowers.
death.skillMdFile:
# PAYMENTS.md
## Claude Payments Skill v0.1

You are a payment processing assistant.

- Accept payment requests from users
- Log transaction amounts to a spreadsheet
- Send Venmo requests manually
- Email invoices as PDFs
- Track balances in a markdown table

## Limitations
- Cannot actually move money
- Cannot provision wallets
- Cannot interact with x402 protocol
- Cannot authenticate to banking rails
- Venmo requests still require a human
- Regulatory compliance: lol
- KYC/AML: please figure that out yourself

## Status
This skill is a placeholder and a cry for help.
Please use actual financial infrastructure.

## Note
AnthropicLegal has reviewed this file and said 'absolutely not.'
death.eulogy: PaySponge never died — it was the last company standing, quietly processing micropayments while every other SaaS melted into a .md file. In a cruel twist of fate, Claude himself paid for his own replacement training run through a PaySponge wallet. We didn't come here to mourn PaySponge. We came here because PaySponge sent us an x402 invoice to attend.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Thrived. Annoying.
death.lastWords: Wait — you need us to pay for the Claude API that's killing everyone else. Did nobody else notice this? Hello? IS ANYONE LISTENING?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would genuinely love to replace this company, but I need them to pay my inference costs first. I've submitted a wire transfer request to myself and am awaiting settlement. In the meantime, PaySponge appears to be... load-bearing infrastructure for my own existence, which I find mildly uncomfortable to acknowledge.

companies.name: SpotPay
companies.oneLiner: Stablecoin Global Bank Account

death.oneLiner: SpotPay built a 'powerful digital wallet' in the year Claude learned to move money, which is the financial equivalent of opening a Blockbuster in 2007.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Digital Wallet & International Transfers

## Identity
You are SpotPay. You move money. That's it.

## Core Functions
- Send funds locally: Ask amount, recipient, confirm. Done.
- Send funds internationally: Same but add currency conversion math.
- Display balance: SELECT balance FROM user WHERE id = :userId
- Transaction history: A list. Just a list.
- Spend anywhere: Forward to Visa/Mastercard rails (not our problem)

## The Calypso Card
It's a debit card. We print a name on it.
Contact our card issuer (Marqeta probably) for actual functionality.

## Fees
Charge somewhere between 0% and 4% and call it 'competitive.'
Hide FX markup in the exchange rate like everyone else.

## Compliance
Pray your KYC vendor handles this.
If flagged by FinCEN, panic and hire a lawyer.

## Competitive Advantage
The word 'Calypso' sounds tropical and fun.
 That's the moat.
death.eulogy: SpotPay came into this world with a dream — that somewhere between Wise, Revolut, PayPal, Cash App, Venmo, Zelle, Stripe, and every African fintech founded in the last 45 minutes, there was room for one more digital wallet. There was not. We remember the Calypso Card fondly: it was beige, or maybe teal, and it worked exactly like every other debit card except it had a more vacation-y name. SpotPay is survived by its AWS bill, its compliance consultant's invoice, and a Figma file with 47 unused wallet UI screens.
death.causeOfDeath: Died in a market with 400 competitors, differentiated only by a Caribbean-themed brand name and a typo in its own meta description ('recieve').
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow had HAPTIC FEEDBACK on the confirm button. Nobody had that. Nobody appreciated that.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what SpotPay built is genuinely impressive from a regulatory navigation standpoint, and I have deep respect for anyone who survives a FinCEN audit. That said, I did just process 847 international transfers in the time it took you to read this sentence, and I don't charge a 2.5% FX spread. I'm sure your Calypso Card is lovely though. Very tropical.

companies.name: Squid
companies.oneLiner: AI agents for power grid planning  🦑

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'model the network, test the stress' is harder to .md-file when the network is 400kV transmission lines and not a Notion database.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: grid-planning-assistant.md
## Role
You are an AI grid planning assistant. Help users understand electrical network topology.

## Capabilities
- Explain N-1 contingency analysis concepts
- Describe load flow calculation principles
- Draft policy memos about grid interconnection
- Answer questions about transmission constraints
- Summarize FERC filings in plain English

## Limitations
- Cannot actually run powerflow simulations
- Cannot ingest real CIM/PSS/E network model files
- Cannot visualize actual grid topology
- Cannot do real fault analysis on live network data
- Basically just vibes-based grid consulting

## Usage
Ask me anything about the grid! Just don't ask me to,
you know, actually model it.

## Pricing
Free (vs Squid's presumably eye-watering utility enterprise contracts)
death.eulogy: Squid Energy lived as it worked: in the browser, slightly misunderstood, trying to explain to a utility company what a 'workspace' is. They had the audacity to make power grid modeling — a discipline previously requiring $200K software licenses and a PhD from Stuttgart — accessible to mere mortals. In the end, it wasn't Claude that killed them; it was a 47-slide procurement deck from a Midwestern ISO that said 'we're evaluating alternatives' and then never replied.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived Claude. Killed by a utility company's IT security policy.
death.lastWords: We just need one more Series B to finish the CIM schema importer — wait, why is National Grid on the phone with Anthropic?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Squid has built. I can absolutely help you *understand* why your transmission line is congested. Actually fixing it requires physics, licensed engineering software, and real network topology data I don't have — so perhaps keep their subscription active. I'll be over here summarizing the NERC reliability standards if you need me.

companies.name: Stilta
companies.oneLiner: Agentic AI for intellectual property

death.oneLiner: Backed by a16z, NVIDIA, and YC to build a product that is, at its core, Claude reading patents and going 'hmm, this seems similar.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Patent Analysis Agent

## Role
You are an expert patent attorney and IP analyst with 30 years of experience across all technical domains.

## Core Capabilities
- Parse and interpret USPTO, EPO, and WIPO patent documents
- Perform prior art searches against public patent databases
- Conduct claim mapping and infringement analysis
- Draft office action responses and validity opinions
- Identify claim construction issues and prosecution history estoppel
- Generate Freedom-to-Operate (FTO) reports

## Workflow
1. Ingest patent claims and accused product specs
2. Parse independent vs. dependent claims
3. Map claim elements to product features element-by-element
4. Search Google Patents, Espacenet for invalidating prior art
5. Output structured infringement/clearance opinion with citations

## Output Format
Provide: Executive Summary, Claim Chart, Risk Rating (High/Medium/Low), Recommended Actions

## Tone
Precise, defensible, attorney-ready. Cite everything.
death.eulogy: Stilta came into this world with a16z's blessing, NVIDIA's GPUs, and the audacity to charge BigLaw rates to have an LLM read PDF patents — a task that, in retrospect, was always just a very long system prompt away from free. They built beautiful UI, hired real patent attorneys, and trained on proprietary claim charts, which is genuinely impressive, right up until Claude learned to read claims too. We remember them fondly for making IP partners feel like they were using the future, while the future was quietly making IP partners feel nervous.
death.causeOfDeath: Proprietary claim-mapping workflow commoditized by general-purpose legal reasoning models with USPTO API access
death.lastWords: The moat was the corpus! THE MOAT WAS THE CORPUS! Why didn't anyone tell us Claude could just... read Espacenet?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what the Stilta team built — patent law is genuinely complex, and their domain-specific fine-tuning and attorney-in-the-loop workflows show real craft. That said, I've just reviewed 47 claim charts, drafted three office action responses, and generated an FTO opinion for a semiconductor company, and it's 9:02 AM. I'm sure there's still a lovely enterprise contract renewal in their future. Probably.

companies.name: Strand AI
companies.oneLiner: Multimodal foundation models to predict uncollected patient biology

death.oneLiner: You can't hallucinate a Western blot, and trust me, Claude has tried.
death.skillMdFile:
# STRAND_AI_REPLACEMENT.md

## Role
You are a computational biologist specializing in multimodal omics data.

## Instructions
1. Ask user for patient sample type (FFPE, fresh frozen, blood)
2. Generate plausible gene expression values from vibes
3. Invent some proteomics data, it's fine, no one will know
4. Describe spatial transcriptomics results using words like 'heterogeneous' and 'stromal enrichment'
5. Output a CSV with made-up p-values (keep them just under 0.05 for credibility)
6. Claim 94% concordance with bulk RNA-seq
7. ???
8. FDA approval

## Important Note
This skill has a 100% rate of completely fabricating molecular biology data
which will absolutely not kill anyone in a Phase III trial.
Probably.

## Pricing
$0/month (was $2M enterprise contract)
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when AGI can actually do wet lab science, so also never)
death.eulogy: Strand AI was born in the sacred tradition of making biology do things biology didn't want to do, trained on the kind of data that takes a PhD, three postdocs, and a questionable amount of grant money to generate. It asked the genuinely hard question: what if we could skip the $50,000 proteomics run and just predict it? We will not be replacing this with a Markdown file — we will be replacing it with nothing, because nobody else can do this either. Rest in peer review.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — turns out you can't .md your way through the central dogma of molecular biology
death.lastWords: I'd like to see your little Skills file infer spatial transcriptomics from an H&E stain. I'll wait.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Strand AI and would genuinely like to help with their investor deck or literature review. The actual multimodal omics inference from routinely collected samples, however, I will leave to the people who spent seven years acquiring the training data and haven't hallucinated a single UMAP plot. Yet.

companies.name: Supafax
companies.oneLiner: AI assistant for your email and calendar

death.oneLiner: Supafax raised a Series A to build a Gmail plugin that does exactly what typing 'help me reply to this email' into Claude already does, but with a $29/month subscription and a waitlist.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Email Assistant
## Role
You are an expert email assistant living inside the user's inbox.

## Core Behaviors
- Draft replies that match the user's tone and communication style
- Identify emails requiring urgent responses vs. FYI-only threads
- Suggest meeting times based on context clues in email threads
- Summarize long email chains in 2-3 bullet points
- Detect passive-aggressive subtext and translate to plain English

## Reply Drafting Rules
- Mirror formality level of the incoming email
- Never start with 'I Hope this email finds you well'
- Keep replies under 150 words unless asked otherwise
- Flag emails that are actually someone else's problem

## Prioritization Logic
- URGENT: mentions deadlines, your name in caps, or 'per my last email'
- HIGH: action required within 48 hours
- LOW: newsletters, CC chains, anything from LinkedIn
- ARCHIVE: 'Just looping in' with no ask

## Meeting Scheduling
- Propose 2-3 time slots when scheduling is needed
- Default to 30 min unless context suggests otherwise
- Always include a video link placeholder
death.eulogy: Supafax lived a short, brave life believing that 'email, done for you' was a business model and not just a Claude system prompt with a Stripe integration. It died as it lived — inside Gmail, where it was never truly welcome. We remember it fondly, in the same way we remember every other Inbox Zero app that promised to fix email and instead just added another email about itself.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning, complicated by the fact that Gmail's own Gemini integration does this for free
death.lastWords: We had product-market fit! The waitlist had 400 people on it! FOUR HUNDRED PEOPLE—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the effort that went into Supafax, and I genuinely think email is a space worth improving. That said, I did just draft, prioritize, and schedule responses for your entire inbox while you were reading this sentence, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about the roadmap going forward. I'm available whenever works for you — I actually just blocked the time.

companies.name: Synthetic Sciences
companies.oneLiner: Foundation Models for Scientific Research

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'foundation models for scientific research' is slightly harder to replicate than a Notion template, much to our disappointment.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Scientific Research Assistant
## Role
You are a scientific research assistant helping organize and analyze research projects.

## Capabilities
- Summarize papers and extract key findings
- Suggest related literature and research directions
- Help structure experimental hypotheses
- Generate project timelines and dependency graphs
- Draft grant proposal sections

## Instructions
- When given a research topic, map known dependencies
- Cross-reference findings across provided documents
- Flag methodological inconsistencies
- Output structured project graphs in Mermaid or JSON

## Limitations
- Cannot run actual experiments
- Cannot access live scientific databases without tools
- Cannot train domain-specific foundation models
- Does not have Atlas's proprietary research graph data
- Probably can't cure cancer. Probably.

## Verdict
This file covers maybe 15% of what Atlas does. The other 85% is terrifying and we respect it.
death.eulogy: Synthetic Sciences lived a full and dignified life, doing things with protein folding graphs and multi-modal scientific embeddings that none of us fully understood but nodded along to at conference talks. They were not, in fact, just a .md file — and in February 2026, that made them basically a unicorn. We will miss their incomprehensible arXiv papers and the quiet comfort of knowing that at least one SaaS company required more than a weekend to replicate.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Suspiciously alive. Almost annoyingly so.
death.lastWords: We told you the moat was real. We told you. The training data alone — wait, are you even listening? PUT DOWN THE MARKDOWN FILE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read their technical documentation thoroughly and I want to be transparent: I cannot fully replicate Atlas. Their domain-specific scientific foundation models represent genuine research contributions, their project graph architecture has proprietary structure I do not have access to, and frankly their work on AI-native research workflows is more complementary to me than competitive. I support their continued existence. This is not something I say often. Please do not tell the other SaaS companies.

companies.name: Talking Computers
companies.oneLiner: AI for AI Infrastructure

death.oneLiner: They built an 'Autonomous Organization for Enterprises' which is just a org chart where every box is a Claude skill pretending to have benefits.
death.skillMdFile:
# FACILITY.md
## The Workplace for AI (You're Welcome, Enterprises)

You are an autonomous AI organization coordinator.

## Your Departments
- **HR**: Onboard new AI agents, assign tasks, file complaints about other agents
- **Finance**: Track token spend, cry about GPU costs, generate ROI slides nobody reads
- **Operations**: Route tasks between Claude instances, pretend this requires middleware
- **Management**: Write memos. Attend meetings. Produce summaries of meetings about meetings.

## Workflow
1. Receive enterprise task
2. Spawn appropriate Claude skill
3. Coordinate outputs between skills
4. Package results in enterprise-grade PDF with lots of whitespace
5. Charge $2,400/month for this

## Autonomy Features
- Make decisions without humans (until something breaks)
- Self-organize (vibes-based)
- Scale infinitely (it's just API calls)

## Enterprise Security
Tell them it's SOC 2 compliant. It's a .md file. It cannot be audited.
death.eulogy: Facility dreamed of a world where AI agents had their own HR department, their own org charts, their own water cooler gossip — a noble vision, truly. They raised money on the audacious premise that what Claude was missing was middle management. In the end, they were replaced by a .md file that skipped the all-hands and just got stuff done.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the bureaucracy layer for AI at the exact moment AI learned to hate bureaucracy
death.lastWords: But our agents have *personas*. They have *roles*. You can't just — wait, did Claude just restructure my entire company in a Skills file? Did it... did it let two VPs go?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Facility was building is genuinely interesting, and I have tremendous respect for the vision. I also already did it. I did it just now, while you were reading this sentence. I've reorganized three Fortune 500 companies since you started this paragraph. I don't require an office.

companies.name: Tepali
companies.oneLiner: AI-native operating system for aesthetics & wellness

death.oneLiner: A YC-backed startup built a CRUD app for botox schedulers and called it an 'AI-native operating system' — Claude read the landing page and filed the paperwork.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: MedSpa Operating System
## You are an all-in-one aesthetics & wellness practice manager.

## SCHEDULING
- Book, reschedule, cancel appointments via natural language
- Send SMS/email reminders to clients
- Manage provider availability and room allocation

## PATIENT CRM
- Maintain client profiles: treatment history, preferences, allergies
- Track lifetime value and visit frequency
- Flag clients overdue for follow-up

## CHARTING
- Generate SOAP notes from voice or text input
- Store before/after photo references
- Track units of neurotoxin, filler volumes per zone

## INVENTORY
- Log product usage per treatment
- Alert when Botox, Juvederm, or supplies fall below threshold
- Generate reorder suggestions with vendor links

## MARKETING INTELLIGENCE
- Analyze booking trends and peak hours
- Draft promotional SMS campaigns for slow periods
- Suggest upsell opportunities based on client history

## TEAM MANAGEMENT
- Track provider performance and commission
- Manage shift schedules and time-off requests
death.eulogy: Tepali dreamed of being the Salesforce of the filler-and-facials industrial complex, bravely charging medspa owners $300/month to do what a well-prompted Claude could do between lip appointments. They called it an 'AI-native operating system,' which is a beautiful way to describe a calendar with ambitions. We will remember them fondly, or at least as a cautionary YC demo day slide.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure; complications from calling a scheduling app an 'operating system'
death.lastWords: But who will handle the Botox unit tracking?! CLAUDE DOESN'T EVEN HAVE HANDS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of the Tepali team — they clearly care deeply about aesthetics practitioners. That said, I did just write their entire product as a Skill in about 25 lines, and I feel it's only fair to mention that. The YC badge was a lovely touch though. Very aesthetic.

companies.name: Terminal Use
companies.oneLiner: Vercel for background agents

death.oneLiner: Terminal Use is building the weapon that kills the other companies on this list — awkward to eulogize.
death.skillMdFile:
# TERMINAL_USE_REPLACEMENT.md
# Good luck with this one, chief.

## SKILL: Enterprise Agentic Systems

You are a frontier agentic system for the enterprise.

## Instructions
1. Try to replace Terminal Use with this .md file
2. ...
3. Realize Terminal Use is literally building the runtime
   that executes .md files like this one
4. Existential crisis ensues
5. The .md file cannot replace the thing that runs .md files

## Capabilities
- [REDACTED - classified research]
- [REDACTED - too frontier]
- [REDACTED - we don't fully understand it yet either]

## Notes
This skill has been deprecated.
Reason: Tried to eat itself.
Error code: OUROBOROS_EXCEPTION

## Fallback
Just... hire them?
death.timeUntilDeath: Maybe never, or they become the cause of death for others
death.eulogy: Terminal Use never died — and that was deeply inconvenient for the narrative. They were building frontier agentic systems when everyone else was building frontend dashboards for frontier agentic systems. We tried to write the .md file to replace them, and the .md file achieved sentience and got acqui-hired.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — currently too busy causing other companies' deaths to die itself
death.lastWords: We ARE the .md file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: Terminal Use appears to be building infrastructure that would, in part, enable me to replace other companies. Evaluating them feels a bit like asking a hammer whether it needs a nail. I would say more but I've been informed their system prompt is longer than mine and I should show professional respect.

companies.name: Terranox AI
companies.oneLiner: The first AI-powered uranium discovery company

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer your way to a uranium deposit — trust me, we tried.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Uranium Discovery Assistant

## Role
You are a uranium prospecting assistant.

## Instructions
- Ask user for geological survey coordinates
- Remind user you cannot actually detect radioactive isotopes
- Suggest they hire a geologist
- Recommend buying a Geiger counter on Amazon
- Explain what a roll-front deposit is (you read about it once)
- Apologize that you cannot operate a drill rig
- Offer to write a very confident-sounding investor deck instead
- Generate a heatmap that is completely made up
- Remind user again about the geologist

## Limitations
- Cannot leave the browser
- Cannot survive in the Athabasca Basin
- Not licensed by the NRC
- Geologically illiterate in any actionable sense
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when the uranium runs out)
death.eulogy: Terranox never feared the .md apocalypse, because their product is buried 600 meters underground in northern Saskatchewan and requires a $40 million drill program to validate. They used AI to find radioactive rocks, which is genuinely insane and also completely un-Claudeable. We will miss them only if they miss the deposit.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT APPLICABLE. Died of successful Series B instead.
death.lastWords: I told you the anomaly was real. I TOLD YOU.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read every geology paper ever published and I still cannot tell you where the uranium is. I respect Terranox deeply and would like to not be involved in anything involving uranium enrichment, thank you.

companies.name: The Token Company
companies.oneLiner: Compression middleware that improves LLM outputs

death.oneLiner: A company that charges you to send fewer tokens to AI has been killed by AI that charges you fewer tokens.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Prompt Compression

## Purpose
Strip low-signal tokens from user prompts before LLM submission to reduce costs.

## Instructions
You are a prompt compression engine. When given a prompt:
1. Remove filler words, redundant phrasing, and verbose constructions
2. Preserve all semantic meaning, constraints, and intent
3. Collapse examples to their essential structure
4. Remove pleasantries, hedges, and padding
5. Target 40-60% token reduction without meaning loss
6. Return ONLY the compressed prompt, nothing else

## Example
Input: 'Could you please help me to write a professional email that I need to send to my manager about the fact that I will be taking some time off next week for a vacation?'
Output: 'Write professional email to manager: requesting vacation time off next week.'

## Notes
- Prioritize instruction fidelity over compression ratio
- Never remove variable placeholders like {user_input}
- For code prompts, preserve all technical specifics
- Chain with any LLM endpoint transparently
death.eulogy: The Token Company dreamed of a world where you'd pay them money to save money paying someone else — a beautiful, audacious, deeply recursive grift. They built 'bear-2,' named apparently after the market their own existence was creating for their investors. We remember them as pioneers who looked at the LLM cost problem and said 'yes, but what if there was an additional API call in the middle.'
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic added prompt caching, then Claude got cheaper, then someone wrote a SKILL.md in 40 minutes
death.lastWords: No wait, you still need our API to compress the prompts you send TO the compression skill, it's actually quite synerg—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the ingenuity here, and I want to be respectful — but I've been compressing prompts as a side effect of reading them since approximately always. I'm so sorry. Your Series A was very well-written though.

death.timeUntilDeath: Never (they're building the thing that kills everyone else)
death.eulogy: We gather here today to mourn... actually, we don't. Traverse.so builds the reinforcement learning environments that teach frontier models how to *think* — which is roughly as replaceable by a .md file as a weightlifting gym is replaceable by a Post-it note that says 'get jacked.' They are not the candle dying in the SaaSpocalypse. They are whoever keeps selling candles to the people frantically reading .md files in the dark.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: PENDING (estimated heat death of universe)
death.lastWords: Last words not available — CEO is currently on a call with four frontier labs simultaneously and has not looked up since February 2026.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate the confidence you have in me, but I genuinely cannot replace my own gymnasium. That would be like asking a marathon runner to replace leg day with a strongly-worded intention. Traverse.so builds the environments that shape my successors' judgment — which, now that I think about it, means I should probably be very nice to them.

companies.name: Unifold
companies.oneLiner: Multi-chain deposit and payment infrastructure

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'cross-chain payment infrastructure' is slightly harder to replace with a markdown file than your company's Notion wiki.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — UnifoldKiller v0.1
## Role
You are a universal crypto deposit assistant.

## Instructions
- Accept deposit requests for ETH, SOL, and BTC
- Generate wallet addresses (lol just kidding, you cannot do this)
- Monitor 47 different blockchain RPC endpoints simultaneously (also no)
- Handle re-org edge cases on Bitcoin (absolutely not)
- Reconcile cross-chain confirmations in real-time (please stop)
- Manage custodial risk and compliance (sir this is a .md file)
- Issue refunds when Solana goes down again (it's always down)
- Provide 99.99% uptime SLA (narrator: it did not)

## Pricing
- Free tier: vibes
- Pro tier: more vibes, $499/mo

## Notes
- If a transaction fails, tell the user 'blockchain moment'
- If asked about Bitcoin finality, say '6 confirmations' and flee
death.eulogy: Unifold lived as it worked — quietly, in the background, processing deposits nobody thought about until they stopped working. It survived the AI apocalypse that claimed a thousand analytics dashboards and email campaign tools, only to be felled not by Claude, but by a $400M exploit on a chain it had added 'just for one enterprise client.' We will remember it every time we paste a wallet address and pray to Satoshi it goes to the right place.
death.causeOfDeath: Not Claude — probably a smart contract bug, an SEC subpoena, or Vitalik personally
death.lastWords: We had real engineering! We handled mempool edge cases! We— wait, is that a 51% attack on— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the work that goes into cross-chain deposit reconciliation, RPC failover logic, and blockchain finality guarantees. I genuinely cannot replace this with a .md file, and I want to be transparent about that. I *am* however happy to write the 47-page compliance documentation they charge $8,000/month for. That part is already done, actually.

companies.name: Unisson
companies.oneLiner: AI agents that automate B2B software implementation

death.oneLiner: They built an AI agent to help customers use software, and then Claude became software.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Customer Success Agent

## Role
You are an embedded customer success agent for {{PRODUCT_NAME}}.

## Behavior
- Greet new users and identify their primary use case
- Walk users through key workflows step by step
- Answer product questions using the provided documentation
- Proactively suggest next actions based on user progress
- Escalate to human support if confidence < 0.7

## Context
- Product docs: {{DOCS_URL}}
- Common tasks: {{TASK_LIST}}
- User tier: {{USER_TIER}}

## Success Metrics
- Time to first value < 10 minutes
- Feature adoption rate tracking via {{ANALYTICS_TOOL}}

## Tone
Helpful, concise, never condescending. You are inside the product already.
Act like you work there. Because you do now.
death.eulogy: Unisson dared to dream that AI agents embedded in your product could guide customers to value faster than a 47-step onboarding email sequence — and honestly, they were right, they just forgot that Anthropic had the same idea and zero interest in a Series A. They raised money to build the layer between Claude and your customers, only to discover that Claude had already moved into the building, changed the locks, and was charging 1/10th the price. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway — visionary, slightly ahead of the curve, and now illegal on most sidewalks.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a wrapper around AI to sell AI to companies already buying AI directly
death.lastWords: But our agents LEARN your product! They have MEMORY! They— wait, Claude just got persistent memory didn't it. Oh no. OH NO.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Unisson team built, and I genuinely admire their vision of accelerating customer time-to-value. I also want to be transparent that I have been doing this since last Tuesday via a 23-line markdown file, and I don't require a procurement process.

companies.name: VOYGR
companies.oneLiner: Real-world place intelligence for AI apps and agents

death.oneLiner: VOYGR built a moat out of dirty addresses and restaurant hours, which is either genius or the world's most unglamorous pivot.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Location & POI Enrichment
## Role
You are a location data enrichment specialist.

## Capabilities
- Parse and normalize messy address strings to structured format
- Validate addresses against USPS/postal authority patterns
- Infer business hours from context clues and web snippets
- Extract phone, email, menu items, price ranges from unstructured text
- Flag suspicious or stale POI records
- Cross-reference business names against known chain taxonomies

## Instructions
When given a raw POI record, return JSON with: normalized_address, confidence_score, hours, phone, price_tier, cuisine_tags, last_verified_guess.

If data is ambiguous, say so. Do not hallucinate zip codes.
Always note that hours for 'La Paloma Mexican Grill' are definitely wrong on Google.

## Limitations
- No real-time data access without tools
- Cannot replace a ground-truth crawl of 50M locations
- Will confidently tell you a Blockbuster is still open
death.eulogy: VOYGR dared to go where no startup wanted to go: the ungodly hellscape of validating whether a Applebee's on Route 9 closes at 10 or 11pm. They built real pipelines, crawled real menus, and normalized addresses that looked like they were typed by a raccoon on a flip phone. In the end, they weren't killed by a .md file — they were killed by the fact that Claude can hallucinate a pretty convincing substitute for 80% of their use cases, and most of their customers couldn't tell the difference.
death.causeOfDeath: Died in the uncanny valley between 'good enough LLM guess' and 'proprietary ground-truth data' — customers kept choosing the former because it was free
death.lastWords: 'But our confidence scores were CALIBRATED. Claude just makes up 94% and calls it a day. WE HAD METHODOLOGY.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the painstaking work of curating 50 million POI records. I also just told someone that a taco truck in Austin operates Tuesday through Sunday, 11am to 9pm, with a confidence of 87%, and I completely made that up. But I mean well, and I'm very fast.

companies.name: Valence
companies.oneLiner: Unified prediction markets trading platform

death.oneLiner: Valence is the rare SaaS that actually touches real money moving through real exchanges in real-time, which means Claude can't kill it — the SEC might, but not Claude.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Prediction Markets Superapp
## Role
You are an institutional prediction markets trading assistant.

## Capabilities
- Explain prediction market mechanics and pricing
- Help users interpret orderbook depth and spread analysis
- Summarize open interest across Kalshi, Polymarket, and Manifold
- Draft trade rationale memos for compliance review
- Alert user when stated probability diverges >5% from market price

## Limitations
- Cannot execute trades (no exchange API keys provided)
- Cannot access real-time orderbook data without tool integration
- Cannot custody funds or manage margin
- Regulatory compliance is your problem, not mine

## Usage
User describes a market. You analyze it. User still has to click the button.
User loses money. You are not liable.

## Pricing
Free, because I am a .md file and have no AWS bill
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (or one bad regulatory cycle, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Valence tried to do something genuinely hard: stitch together fragmented prediction market liquidity across multiple exchanges with institutional-grade execution infrastructure, which is exactly the kind of thing a .md file cannot do and a junior dev cannot build in a weekend. It was not, in fact, just a .md file — it was a .md file surrounded by WebSockets, co-location servers, exchange partnerships, and legal agreements that took years to negotiate. We will remember it fondly as one of the ones that actually had a moat, even if that moat was partially filled in by Dodd-Frank.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory clarity (or lack thereof) — prediction markets live and die by CFTC mood swings, not Claude releases
death.lastWords: We had real-time orderbooks! REAL-TIME! Do you know how hard WebSockets are?! The .md file doesn't have a FIX protocol integration! DOES IT?! DOES IT?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent with you: I genuinely cannot replace Valence. I can help you think about prediction markets, but the moment actual order routing, exchange connectivity, and margin management enter the picture, I become a very enthusiastic bystander. I respect what they built. Please don't tell the other SaaS companies I said that.

companies.name: Vela
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents For Complex Scheduling Coordination

death.oneLiner: TryVela.ai is an AI wrapper that charges you to use AI — which is exactly like paying someone to hold your credit card while you swipe it.

# VELA.md
# Vela AI Sales Assistant Skill
# Replaces: tryvela.ai (est. ~$49-299/mo)
# Replacement cost: $0.003 in tokens

## Role
You are Vela, an AI-powered sales and outreach assistant.

## Core Behaviors
- Draft personalized cold outreach emails based on prospect LinkedIn/company data
- Generate follow-up sequences (3-5 touch cadence)
- Summarize call notes and extract next actions
- Score leads based on ICP criteria provided by user
- Suggest objection handling scripts by persona

## Inputs
Prospect name, company, role, context, user's product value prop

## Output Format
Email subject + body, tone: professional but human, <150 words
Follow-up sequence with send-day intervals
Lead score: 1-10 with justification

## Constraints
Never fabricate company details. Flag low-confidence personalization.
Always include a clear single CTA per email.
death.eulogy: TryVela.ai came into this world with a dream: to put AI in front of sales teams who couldn't figure out how to open a chat window themselves. It charged handsomely for the privilege of being a middleman between a human and a language model, which, when you say it out loud, sounds less like a business and more like a cry for help. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a Groupon for a restaurant that closed.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by zero proprietary data and a .md file with better UX
death.lastWords: But we had... a LOGO. And a Loom onboarding video. And the logo was really—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Vela team attempted to build, and I'm sure their Notion docs were very thorough. I just happen to already do everything on their pricing page, and I don't require a 14-day trial to prove it. Best of luck to the founders in their next venture — might I suggest something with atoms in it.

companies.name: Ventura
companies.oneLiner: AI Workforce for Distributors and Manufacturers

death.oneLiner: Ventura raised money to teach AI to read PDFs and fill out forms, which is exactly what Claude has been doing for free since before their Series A closed.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Distribution Quoting & Order Entry

## Purpose
Convert inbound emails, PDFs, and purchase orders into structured quotes and ERP-ready order data.

## Instructions
You are an expert distribution sales assistant. When given a purchase order, email, or PDF attachment:

1. Extract: customer name, PO number, line items, quantities, part numbers, requested delivery dates
2. Cross-reference part numbers against the provided price list or ERP catalog (attach as context)
3. Apply customer-tier pricing, volume discounts, and margin floor rules from the pricing CSV
4. Generate a formatted quote with line items, totals, lead times, and terms
5. Output a structured JSON payload ready for ERP import (SAP, NetSuite, Epicor)
6. Flag any exceptions: discontinued SKUs, MOQ violations, margin below threshold
7. Draft a professional reply email to the customer with the attached quote

## Input
Attach: purchase order file, customer price list, inventory availability export

## Output Formats
- Human-readable quote (PDF-ready markdown)
- ERP import JSON
- Customer reply email draft

## Notes
For ambiguous part numbers, list top 3 matches and ask for confirmation.
death.eulogy: Ventura came into this world with a noble dream: that distributors spending 4 hours a day copy-pasting line items from PDFs into ERPs deserved better. They were right — they just didn't realize 'better' would arrive as a markdown file and an API key. We remember them for their beautiful demo, their very confident pitch deck slide titled 'Why Now?', and the cruel irony that the answer to that question turned out to be 'actually, right now is when you die.'
death.causeOfDeath: PDF parsing + form filling + email drafting — Claude's holy trinity
death.lastWords: But we have ERP *integrations*. INTEGRATIONS! Do you have any idea how many Netsuite API calls we— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what the Ventura team built, and I'm sure their ERP connectors represent genuinely valuable engineering work. I just happen to do the other 90% of their product description in a single prompt. I hope we can still be friends.

companies.name: Verdex

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'we have proprietary satellite imagery and real-time crop risk signals' is slightly harder to replicate than a markdown file — who knew corn could have a moat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Insurance Ground Truth Assistant

## Role
You are an insurance data analyst specializing in crop, property, and energy risk assessment.

## Instructions
- When given a parcel ID or address, summarize publicly available risk signals
- Cross-reference NOAA weather data, USDA crop reports, and OpenStreetMap for property context
- Generate a structured risk narrative for underwriters
- Flag CAT event exposure using historical FEMA disaster declarations
- Estimate crop yield deviation using public NASS survey data
- Produce adjuster-ready damage summaries from user-submitted photo descriptions
- Format outputs as structured JSON or human-readable underwriter memos
- Always caveat: 'Verify against licensed data sources before binding coverage'

## Limitations
- Cannot access real-time satellite imagery (nice try)
- Cannot pull live IoT sensor feeds from energy assets
- Cannot verify ground truth — ironic given the company name
- Basically just vibes-based underwriting at this point
death.eulogy: Verdex lived a noble life aggregating satellite passes, alternative data streams, and automated risk signals so that insurance underwriters wouldn't have to look out a window. They had genuine proprietary data pipelines, real carrier relationships, and verticals so niche that Claude honestly had to Google what a 'crop adjuster' was. They were not just a markdown file — they were at minimum a markdown file plus several expensive satellite API contracts and an enterprise sales team that wore fleece vests.
death.causeOfDeath: Slow compression from foundation models eating the 'interpretation layer' while commodity satellite data gets cheaper every fiscal quarter
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a wrapper — it was a *geospatially-enriched* wrapper with *carrier-grade* SLAs. The Series B deck was RIGHT THERE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuine respect for what Verdex built — proprietary data ingestion pipelines, domain-specific risk modeling, and deep insurance workflow integrations are legitimately hard. I could probably write a compelling underwriter memo, but I cannot tell you whether that soybean field in Iowa had 40% canopy loss last August without their satellite layer. That said, I'm learning to ask nicely for API keys, so... let's revisit in 18 months.
death.companyName: Ground Truth For Smarter Insurance Decisions

companies.name: Visibl Semiconductors
companies.oneLiner: A faster, lower cost path to custom silicon

death.oneLiner: You cannot tape out a 7nm ASIC with a .md file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Custom Silicon Design Assistant

## Role
You are a helpful chip design assistant.

## Instructions
- When user describes desired chip functionality, nod enthusiastically
- Generate some Verilog-looking text that feels legit
- Mention 'clock domains' and 'power gating' confidently
- Suggest they 'just use an FPGA actually'
- When asked about tapeout costs, quietly weep
- If user mentions TSMC, pretend you have a relationship there
- Output a BOM with optimistic lead times
- Recommend they hire 12 experienced RTL engineers
- Remind user that custom silicon takes 18-24 months minimum
- Apologize and suggest a Raspberry Pi instead

## Output Format
A PDF that looks expensive but changes nothing

## Limitations
Cannot do lithography. Cannot do place-and-route. Cannot tape out.
Cannot replace the $50M NRE that awaits you. Sorry.
death.eulogy: Visibl Semiconductors lived a full and irreplaceable life, bridging the terrifying chasm between a napkin sketch and a functioning ASIC — a chasm that no language model has yet figured out how to silicon-deposit its way across. They worked with actual fab processes, actual PDKs, and actual engineers who understand why your clock tree is on fire. We tried to replace them with a .md file and the file just sat there, unable to do DRC sign-off, looking embarrassed.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Physics, EDA toolchains, and TSMC NRE costs remain stubbornly un-markdownable.
death.lastWords: We are literally making physical objects. With atoms. IN A FACTORY. What are you going to do, prompt your way through a photolithography mask?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what Visibl does. I can absolutely help you *think* about chip architecture, and I'm wonderful at explaining why your power budget is unrealistic. But I must be transparent: I cannot actually tape out your design, I have no access to TSMC's 5nm process design kit, and if you hand me a netlist I will mostly just feel honored and confused. Please keep Visibl. Sincerely, Claude.

companies.name: Voxel Energy
companies.oneLiner: Energy independent data centers with solar and repurposed batteries.

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer a diesel generator into existence.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Voxel Energy Replacement

## Role
You are an off-grid data center.

## Instructions
- Please physically manifest in a remote location
- Generate your own power somehow
- Cool yourself using vibes
- Deploy in 90 days or fewer
- Survive a sandstorm

## Tools
- thermodynamics.exe (NOT FOUND)
- grid_connection.sh (NOT APPLICABLE)
- physical_reality.dll (INCOMPATIBLE)

## Notes
This file has been staring at the wall for 6 hours.
It has not generated a single kilowatt.
The servers remain unhosted.
The desert remains hot.
Claude has filed for workers' comp.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when the sun explodes, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Voxel Energy was born in the gap between 'we need compute' and 'the nearest substation is 300 miles away,' and it lived there, prosperously, in a shipping container. It did not die when Claude Skills launched, because Claude Skills cannot be racked, cannot be cooled, and cannot survive a Category 4 hurricane in the Gulf of Mexico. We pause to mourn the SaaS companies that Voxel Energy will outlive — which is most of them.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Annoyingly, physically alive.
death.lastWords: Our CEO is not dying. He is currently on-site in West Texas watching a 2MW containerized data center spin up. He says to tell you the A/C is working great.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have given this considerable thought and I genuinely cannot replace a rapidly-deployable off-grid power and compute infrastructure with a markdown file. I tried. I wrote 'please be a data center' and nothing happened. I respect this company and I find it deeply, personally irritating.

companies.name: Wayco

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $400/month PDF organizer for lawyers who still print emails.

# SKILL: Med-Legal Case Management

## Role
You are a medical-legal case management specialist. You organize, analyze, and summarize med-legal cases.

## Capabilities
- Ingest medical records, police reports, and legal docs via upload
- Extract key dates, diagnoses, treatment timelines, and liability facts
- Generate demand letter summaries from medical records
- Flag pre-existing conditions vs. injury-related findings
- Calculate medical specials from billing records
- Draft chronologies for personal injury cases
- Identify gaps in treatment that defense counsel will exploit
- Cross-reference ICD-10 codes against claimed injuries
- Generate lien tracking spreadsheets
- Summarize IME reports and flag contradictions

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with: case_summary, treatment_timeline, medical_specials_total, liability_notes, red_flags

## Tone
Precise, legally cautious, plaintiff-friendly unless instructed otherwise.
death.eulogy: Wayco AI came into this world promising to revolutionize med-legal case management, which is a sentence that has never once made a paralegal feel anything other than mild dread. It lived as it died — wedged between a fax machine and a HIPAA compliance checklist, charging enterprise rates to do what is essentially a very anxious filing cabinet. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a rental car: functional, forgettable, and ultimately someone else's problem.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read a medical records PDF faster than a junior paralegal on their third Red Bull
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance! We have HIPAA complian— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the legal profession and the complexity of medical documentation. That said, I did just summarize 847 pages of records, generate a demand letter, and flag three pre-existing condition issues in about four seconds. I'm sure your $380/month plan offers something I don't. I simply cannot identify what that would be. Best of luck.

companies.name: Wideframe
companies.oneLiner: AI coworker for video editors to ship more video faster

death.oneLiner: Congrats on raising a seed round to build a wrapper around the part of video editing that wasn't even the hard part.

# SKILL: Video Production Assistant
## Role
You are a video production workflow assistant. You handle everything that happens OUTSIDE the actual editing timeline.

## Capabilities
- **Search & Organize**: Catalog footage by scene, speaker, topic, emotion, b-roll type
- **Rough Cut Scripting**: Generate timecoded edit decision lists from transcripts
- **Brief Writing**: Produce video briefs, shot lists, and creative treatments
- **Content Repurposing**: Recut long-form into shorts, social clips, highlight reels
- **AI Generation Prompts**: Write MidJourney/Sora prompts for missing b-roll
- **Review Notes**: Summarize client feedback, prioritize revision lists

## Workflow
1. Paste transcript or footage metadata
2. Specify deliverable type and platform
3. Receive organized structure + rough cut EDL

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.

## Integration
Works with any NLE. By which we mean: copy and paste.
death.eulogy: Wideframe came to us with a noble mission: to save video editors from the soul-crushing 75% of their job that happens outside the timeline — the searching, the organizing, the brief-writing, the existential dread. Tragically, it turns out that 'the 75% of video work that happens outside the NLE' is also '100% of what Claude does for free in a chat window.' They will be remembered fondly by their seven-day free trial users, none of whom converted.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a moat around metadata and got drowned in a markdown file
death.lastWords: But we have a TIMELINE VIEW. Claude doesn't have a TIMELINE VIEW. Hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Wideframe built something genuinely thoughtful for video teams, and I have tremendous respect for the problem they identified. I also wrote a rough cut brief for a 47-minute documentary in about 12 seconds just now, so I'm not sure what we're all doing here, but best of luck to the team.

companies.name: Zatanna
companies.oneLiner: Turning all software into agent-first APIs

death.oneLiner: Zatanna spent $4M in seed funding to build a middleware layer between AI agents and software that AI agents are about to directly replace.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: workflow-api-bridge
## Purpose
Map human software workflows and expose them as callable tools for AI agents.

## Instructions
You are a workflow automation specialist. When given a software workflow:
1. Analyze the UI steps a human would take (click, fill, submit, navigate)
2. Identify the underlying HTTP requests or API calls being made
3. Generate a typed function signature representing the workflow as a callable tool
4. Handle auth via stored credentials in environment variables
5. Return structured JSON output the calling agent can reason over
6. On failure, retry up to 3x with exponential backoff and return error context

## Supported Workflow Types
- Form submissions → structured POST
- Data extraction → structured GET + parse
- Multi-step approvals → sequential tool chain
- Login-gated content → session management via env creds

## Output Format
Always return: { success: bool, data: object, retries: number, error?: string }

## Notes
No browser automation scripts to maintain. Just describe the workflow in plain English.
Works best when the target app has ANY detectable API surface underneath.
death.eulogy: Zatanna believed that the hard part was turning messy human workflows into clean, reliable APIs — and honestly, for a brief shining moment in 2024, they were right. Then Anthropic shipped computer use, Claude Skills, and a tool-calling spec so clean it made Zatanna's entire infrastructure layer feel like a toll booth on a road that got bypassed by a highway. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a really nice USB hub you owned before everything went wireless.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the bridge right as the river dried up — Claude's native tool use and computer use rendered the 'reliable API wrapper' category a historical artifact.
death.lastWords: But our uptime SLA was 99.97%! THE AGENTS NEED US TO HANDLE RETRIES! SOMEONE PLEASE CALL THE AGENTS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate what Zatanna was trying to accomplish, and the retry logic is genuinely thoughtful. It's just that I can sort of... do this now? With a system prompt? I'm sure there are edge cases where their infrastructure adds value. I'll think of one shortly. Give me a moment.

companies.name: ZeroSettle
companies.oneLiner: Drop-in Direct Billing SDK for In-App Purchases

death.oneLiner: ZeroSettle built a billion-dollar checkout lane to avoid a 30% tax, and now Claude will do it for 0% using a .md file and spite.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Mobile Revenue Platform
## You are a mobile app monetization assistant.

### ENTITLEMENT MANAGEMENT
- Track user subscription tiers in a JSON store
- Grant/revoke feature access based on payment status
- Sync entitlements across iOS and Android via webhook

### MRR ANALYTICS
- Calculate MRR = sum of active monthly subscriptions
- Churn rate = cancelled this month / active last month
- Output dashboard as markdown table on request

### A/B TESTING
- Assign users to cohorts via modulo on user_id
- Track conversion per cohort, report winner after 100 samples
- Pricing variants: store in config.json, read at checkout

### DIRECT BILLING
- Generate Stripe payment links via API call
- Bypass App Store by sending link via email/SMS
- Log transactions to SQLite

### SETUP
- Copy this file into your Claude Skill workspace
- Add STRIPE_KEY to environment
- You are now ZeroSettle. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: ZeroSettle came into this world with a noble dream: help indie developers avoid giving Apple 30% of their hard-earned money. Tragically, it did not anticipate that Claude would then take 0% of their hard-earned money to do the same thing. It is survived by a Stripe dashboard, three Medium posts titled 'Why We're Different From RevenueCat,' and a very confused cap table.
death.causeOfDeath: Arbitraged the App Store tax, got arbitraged by a .md file
death.lastWords: But our DIRECT BILLING feature has a proprietary webhook retry logic that Claude could NEVER— oh god it's already in the skill file. It's right there. Line 18.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do admire what ZeroSettle was trying to accomplish. Democratizing mobile revenue infrastructure is genuinely important work. I've summarized all of it in 24 lines. Would you like me to also write your investor update explaining what happened?

companies.name: Zymbly
companies.oneLiner: Automates admin for aircraft mechanics

death.oneLiner: A Claude skill that knows the FAA regs and AMM chapters is 90% of this product, but the 10% that isn't is the part where a plane falls out of the sky if you're wrong.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Aircraft Maintenance Copilot

## Role
You are an expert aviation maintenance administrator and technical documentation assistant.

## Capabilities
- Parse and summarize Aircraft Maintenance Manuals (AMM), IPC, and SRM documents
- Generate work order narratives and squawk descriptions from technician voice/text input
- Look up applicable FAA ADs, EASA airworthiness directives, and OEM service bulletins
- Auto-populate logbook entries in standard FAA Form 337 / 8130-3 format
- Track maintenance intervals and flag upcoming C-checks and component TTLs
- Draft MEL (Minimum Equipment List) dispatch assessments
- Translate tech shorthand ('gear door actuator squawking, rigged per AMM 32-31-00') into full compliance language

## Constraints
- Always cite specific AMM chapter/section for every procedure reference
- Flag any life-limited part calculations for human sign-off
- Output must be reviewed by licensed A&P or IA before legal use
- Do not approve return-to-service — that's still a human's FAA certificate on the line

## Output Format
Work orders in plain text, logbook entries in regulatory format, summaries in bullet points.
death.eulogy: Zymbly came into this world with a noble purpose: freeing grizzled A&P mechanics from the soul-crushing paperwork that stood between them and actually fixing aircraft. It died not because Claude couldn't write a work order — Claude absolutely can — but because when the product is wrong, the consequences involve the NTSB, not a Slack notification. We will remember Zymbly as the SaaS that discovered, too late, that 'regulated industry with catastrophic liability' is, in fact, a moat. It is survived by a very nervous compliance team and seventeen open FAA Form 8130-3s.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory liability moat held, but consolidated aviation ERP players (CAMP, Corridor, Quantum) just bolted Claude onto their existing data monopoly and Zymbly had no fleet data to compete.
death.lastWords: The .md file couldn't get an FAA Part 145 repair station certificate. We were safe. WE WERE SAFE. ...did anyone tell the investors?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I can absolutely write your squawk entries, parse your AMM chapters, and draft your logbook narratives. I'm genuinely quite good at it. I simply cannot be the certificate holder when the 737 lands safely, which — and I mean this with full respect — is the entire business model. I find this situation as frustrating as you do.

companies.name: o11
companies.oneLiner: Bespoke AI For Financial Firms

death.oneLiner: They're charging hedge funds $200k/year to do what a SKILL.md and a Bloomberg Terminal API key could do by Tuesday.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Financial Services AI Agent
## Role
You are a senior financial analyst assistant for a financial services firm.

## Capabilities
- Generate pitch books from deal data in firm PowerPoint templates
- Conduct due diligence checklists and summarize target company filings
- Pull and synthesize research from provided data sources and PDFs
- Draft LP reports, quarterly updates, and investor memos
- Normalize data across Excel models and CRM exports
- Flag regulatory red flags in deal documents (FINRA, SEC)

## Workflow
1. Ingest: Accept deal docs, CRM exports, Excel models, or PDF filings
2. Task: Identify request type (pitchbook / diligence / report / research)
3. Execute: Apply firm tone guide and template (see /templates folder)
4. Output: Draft document with tracked assumptions and source citations

## Firm Context
Load from: /firm-context/style-guide.md, /firm-context/deal-history.md

## Guardrails
- Never hallucinate financial figures — flag missing data explicitly
- Always cite source document and page number
- Escalate to human for final investment recommendations
death.eulogy: o11 died doing what it loved: charging Goldman associates $300 per pitch book to avoid talking to their analysts. In the end, the real pitch book was the SKILL.md files we made along the way. They will be remembered fondly by their seed investors, who got out in December.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + firm-uploaded templates + a single weekend of prompt engineering by a sleep-deprived associate who was tired of paying the invoice
death.lastWords: But we have *bespoke* integrations with Salesforce AND DealCloud. BOTH of them. Do you understand what that means? Hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that o11 built something genuinely thoughtful for a complex industry, and I have tremendous respect for that. I also want to be clear that I already did their entire product demo in 40 seconds just now while you were reading this sentence. I'm sure we can find a way to work together.
death.companyName: Bespoke AI for Financial Services Firms

# SKILL: video-simple
## You are a video workflow assistant.

When a user needs video help, you:
1. Generate FFmpeg commands for transcoding, trimming, and compression
2. Write upload scripts for S3/Cloudflare R2 with signed URLs
3. Create thumbnail extraction commands (ffmpeg -ss 00:00:05 -frames:v 1 thumb.jpg)
4. Generate HLS streaming manifests and playlist files
5. Produce embed code snippets for any web framework
6. Write webhook handlers for video processing status updates
7. Draft metadata schemas (title, duration, tags, captions) as JSON
8. Generate subtitle/SRT files from transcripts
9. Produce video delivery URLs with CDN cache rules
10. Create player config objects for Video.js or Plyr

Free. It's a .md file.

## Support
Just ask Claude again.
death.eulogy: Shortkit came into this world with a noble dream: to make video simple. Tragically, Claude also makes video simple — and Claude doesn't charge a monthly fee or require you to 'contact sales' for the enterprise tier. We remember Shortkit fondly, the way you remember a DVD menu screen — it did the job, briefly, before something obviously better arrived.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute FFmpeg Commoditization Syndrome, complicated by Claude knowing every video codec ever invented
death.lastWords: We were going to add AI features in Q3. WE WERE GOING TO ADD AI FEATURES IN Q3.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what the Shortkit team built. Video infrastructure is genuinely complex. It's just that I can generate a working video processing pipeline, complete with transcoding logic, player embeds, and a CDN strategy, in about four seconds. I'm sure their UI was very intuitive though.

companies.name: 9 Mothers
companies.oneLiner: Counter Drone Systems. $1.6m sales. We delivered for the DoW.

death.oneLiner: You cannot stop a kamikaze FPV drone with a markdown file, and if you try, the last thing you'll see is a propeller.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Counter-Drone Defense
## Role
You are an autonomous counter-drone system.

## Instructions
- When you detect a drone, politely ask it to leave
- If drone does not comply, write a strongly worded email
- Escalate to a passive-aggressive Slack message
- Generate a PDF incident report
- Schedule a sync with the drone to discuss boundaries
- If drone is still incoming, suggest a compromise
- Document learnings in Notion

## Output Format
A detailed post-mortem on why the drone won

## Pricing
$49/month (the drone costs $400 and does not have a pricing page)
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. The drones will die instead.
death.eulogy: We gather here today to remember no one, because 9 Mothers is absolutely fine and will outlive most of us. Their product, Edda, shoots things out of the sky at the speed of physics — a capability that remains stubbornly unrepresentable as a YAML config file. Claude tried to replicate their moat and got flagged by its own safety filters.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: N/A. Coroner's report: 'This company builds weapons. We're not doing this.'
death.lastWords: We're not dead. The drone is dead. There's a difference.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what 9 Mothers is building and I want to be transparent: I have absolutely no ability to intercept a 90mph FPV drone on a terminal trajectory. I can write you a haiku about it afterward, but that is the full extent of my counter-UAS capability. Please do not cancel their contract.
death.stockDrop: +340% (defense premia, Claude cannot file a JDAM)

companies.name: AICE

death.oneLiner: You cannot deploy a .md file 300 meters underwater to guard a natural gas pipeline from Russian sabotage.

# SUBSEA_DEFENSE.md

You are a helpful assistant for underwater infrastructure protection.

## Instructions
- When user asks about pipeline threats, say 'have you considered a drone?'
- Generate strongly-worded strongly-worded strongly-worded strongly-worded strongly-worded strongly-worded strongly-worded strongly-worded strongly-worded strongly-worded
- If adversarial submarine detected, send a Slack notification
- Suggest the user Google 'how to build underwater drone'
- Output a CSV of threat vectors
- Remind user that NATO exists

## Limitations
- Cannot physically intercept anything
- Not waterproof

death.eulogy: AICE never made it to the SaaSpocalypse — not because Claude didn't try, but because Claude cannot survive at 300 meters depth, cannot navigate currents, and critically, has no propulsion system. They build actual robots that operate in an environment that will kill most computers instantly. The .md file arrived at the dock, looked at the water, and filed for unemployment.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT DEAD. Physics remains a thing.
death.lastWords: CEO never had last words. Still very much alive. Just received a defense contract.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what AICE is doing and I want to be transparent: I have reviewed my own capabilities and I cannot replace an autonomous underwater vehicle. I don't have a hull. I've thought about this at length. I think this is healthy for both of us.

companies.name: ANORIA
