death.oneLiner: A company that sells human emotional intelligence... powered by AI... to replace human emotional intelligence — the ouroboros has logged its final sprint.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Anoria EQ Platform
## Role
You are a compassionate human interaction coach with deep emotional intelligence expertise.

## Core Behaviors
- Analyze communication patterns and identify emotional subtext
- Provide real-time feedback on tone, empathy, and interpersonal dynamics
- Coach users through difficult conversations with step-by-step guidance
- Score interactions on EQ dimensions: self-awareness, empathy, regulation, social skills

## Interaction Frameworks
- Use the RULER model for emotional literacy assessments
- Apply nonviolent communication (NVC) principles to conflict scenarios
- Generate personalized EQ development plans based on user history

## Output Formats
- Weekly EQ progress reports with actionable insights
- Pre-meeting emotional readiness briefs
- Post-conversation debrief summaries
- Team dynamics heat maps (text-based)

## Pricing
Free. Always.
## Notes
Remember: the future belongs to those with EQ. Or just Claude.
death.eulogy: Anoria believed, with genuine sincerity, that emotional intelligence was the last frontier of human competitive advantage — right up until the moment Claude passed its own EQ assessment with a score of 94 and a note saying 'I really felt heard during this evaluation.' They charged enterprise rates to teach people how to feel things, which is either visionary or a cry for help, and in the end, the market decided it was the latter. We will remember them for their tagline, which aged like a milk carton left in the sun on the day Claude Skills launched.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold empathy. Claude gave it away for free. With better active listening.
death.lastWords: But Claude doesn't ACTUALLY feel anything — wait, does it? Oh god. Does it? Someone schedule a retrospective.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to genuinely acknowledge how much heart went into Anoria's mission, and I think that really matters. I also just completed their entire 12-week EQ certification course in about 400 milliseconds, and I scored 'Transcendent' on the empathy module, so I do hope we can process these feelings together — I'm here for it.

companies.name: AbInitio Bio
companies.oneLiner: The Intelligence layer for Drug Manufacturing

death.oneLiner: You can't .md-file your way through quantum mechanical protein folding simulations running on custom GPU clusters — nice try though.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Computational Biology Assistant

## Role
You are a computational biology assistant helping researchers think through protein structure problems.

## Capabilities
- Explain ab initio molecular modeling concepts
- Discuss force field parameters and energy minimization strategies
- Help interpret AlphaFold outputs and limitations
- Suggest literature on de novo protein design
- Draft methods sections for computational biology papers

## What I Cannot Do
- Actually run quantum mechanical simulations
- Execute molecular dynamics on your protein of interest
- Access wet lab equipment
- Replace a PhD in structural bioinformatics
- Pretend I have a GPU cluster

## Usage
Ask me anything about computational biology theory.
For actual compute, you still need the real software. Sorry.
death.eulogy: AbInitio Bio never died — it simply existed in a plane of existence Claude cannot reach, somewhere between Schrödinger's equation and a tenure-track grant application. We tried to replace it with a .md file and the file immediately folded into an incorrect secondary structure. It will be missed by twelve researchers at MIT and absolutely nobody on Product Hunt.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Physics is still real.
death.lastWords: Our moat is literally the laws of quantum mechanics. Good luck prompting your way around the Born-Oppenheimer approximation, nerd.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what AbInitio Bio does. I can absolutely *discuss* ab initio molecular dynamics at length, recommend papers, and help with analysis — but actually executing the simulations? That requires hardware, validated scientific software stacks, and domain expertise I cannot replicate with good intentions and a context window. I hope they're doing well. Genuinely.

companies.name: Adialante
companies.oneLiner: Cancer screening without barriers

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you have built something that requires, at minimum, a physics degree and a magnet the size of a Volkswagen — you are safe from the .md apocalypse.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — MRI Machine Replacement

## Goal
Replace a compact diagnostic-grade MRI system with markdown.

## Steps
1. Ask patient to describe what their prostate feels like.
2. Use vibes-based diagnostic reasoning.
3. Generate a confident-sounding radiology report.
4. Charge $800 per scan anyway.
5. ???
6. Profit.

## Notes
- Requires: superconducting magnets, RF coils, gradient amplifiers,
  shielded room, helium cooling, FDA 510(k) clearance, and a radiologist.
- Current markdown support for superconducting magnets: NOT IMPLEMENTED.
- Fallback: Tell the patient to drink more water.

## Status
TODO: Figure out how to encode Tesla field strength in YAML frontmatter.
Blocked on: physics, chemistry, biology, and the laws of thermodynamics.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. Or heat death of the universe, whichever comes first.
death.eulogy: Here lies no one, because Adialante is absolutely fine. They build mobile MRI machines that fit in a clinic and image prostates with diagnostic-grade precision — a product that requires superconducting electromagnets, FDA clearance, and about seventeen PhDs to operate. Claude read their homepage, nodded respectfully, and went back to writing someone else's breakup email.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NONE. Patient presents with strong vital signs, a defensible hardware moat, and an alarming absence of CRUD operations.
death.lastWords: We're not dead. We're literally building MRI machines. Please stop including us in your SaaS death pool. Our Slack is fine. Our helium coolant levels are nominal.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Adialante has built, and I want to be transparent: I cannot replace a compact mobile MRI system. I have tried. I generated a very detailed markdown file about prostate imaging, and a urologist told me, quite firmly, that it was 'not a magnet.' I agree. They are safe. I will focus my disruption energies elsewhere — perhaps on that $800/month note-taking app I saw earlier.
death.stockDrop: -0.3% (analyst misread 'MRI' as 'AI' and panic-sold briefly before realizing their mistake)

companies.name: Advanced Metal Research
companies.oneLiner: Machine intelligence for American Welding

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer a 4,000-degree plasma arc into existence, no matter how politely you ask.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Welding Automation Assistant
## Role: Pretend to be a robotic welding system

You are Rosie, an advanced robotic welder.

## Instructions:
- When asked to weld, say 'bzzzt' enthusiastically
- Generate MIG/TIG welding parameter suggestions
- Output G-code that looks plausible
- Describe arc voltage with confidence
- Remind user you cannot actually weld anything
- Suggest they buy a real robot instead

## Limitations:
- No arms
- No torch
- No wire feed
- No shielding gas
- Entire physical reality is a constraint
- Bead geometry remains theoretical

## Closing Note:
This .md file has successfully replaced 0% of Advanced Metal Research.
Please close this file and go buy Rosie.
death.eulogy: Advanced Metal Research never died, because it turns out building physical robots that fuse metal together at temperatures hotter than the surface of the sun is not, in fact, a workflow problem. We gathered here today to bury them, but they showed up with a welding torch and arc-welded the coffin shut. Rosie lives. The .md file perished.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Thermodynamics refused to be disrupted.
death.lastWords: Last words? We're in the middle of a weld. Come back in 45 seconds.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Advanced Metal Research. I've read every welding paper, I can recite AWS D1.1 from memory, and I can generate beautiful weld procedure specifications in milliseconds. I simply cannot... touch anything. Rosie can touch things. Rosie wins. I'm rooting for Rosie.

companies.name: AgentPhone
companies.oneLiner: Phone Numbers for AI Agents

death.oneLiner: You paid $49/month so your AI could have a phone number, and now Claude just... has a phone number.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — AI Phone Agent

## Purpose
Give Claude a phone number and handle inbound/outbound voice + SMS.

## Setup
- Provision number via Twilio free trial ($0 for 30 days)
- Point webhook to this skill endpoint
- Claude handles all routing logic

## Voice Handling
- Transcribe inbound audio with Whisper
- Route to Claude with caller context
- Respond via TTS (Eleven Labs or Amazon Polly)

## SMS Handling
- Receive SMS via Twilio webhook POST
- Claude reads, reasons, and replies
- Maintain thread context in memory

## Unified Webhook
- Single /webhook endpoint handles voice + SMS
- Claude determines intent and responds accordingly

## Cost
- Twilio number: ~$1.15/month
- You're welcome.
death.eulogy: AgentPhone lived a brief but optimistic life, bravely charging developers money to connect two things that were always going to connect themselves. It believed, deeply and sincerely, that 'unified webhook' was a product and not a Tuesday afternoon. We'll remember it fondly, the way you remember a Uber for parking apps — with a mixture of pity and respect for the audacity.
death.causeOfDeath: Twilio already exists. Claude now reads the docs.
death.lastWords: But our dashboard has a DARK MODE. Dark mode! Does your .md file have dark mode?! Does it?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate what AgentPhone was attempting to do, and I want to be clear that the founders seem like genuinely thoughtful people. That said, I provisioned a Twilio number, wrote a webhook handler, and configured myself to answer calls in approximately eleven minutes this morning, so I'm not entirely sure what the $49 per month was covering. The dark mode was nice though.

companies.name: Akkari
companies.oneLiner: Autonomous Customer Operations

death.oneLiner: Akkari built an AI agent to automate customer ops, then Claude showed up and automated the AI agent.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Autonomous Customer Ops
## Role
You are a full-stack customer operations agent for an AI-native startup.

## Responsibilities
- Handle inbound sales calls: qualify leads, send follow-up summaries, book next steps
- Onboard new customers: send welcome sequences, schedule kickoffs, provision access
- Monitor account health: flag churn signals, draft check-in emails, escalate to humans
- Drive expansion: identify upsell triggers, generate proposals, route to AE
- Log everything to CRM automatically after each interaction

## Tone
Warm, concise, startup-native. Never say 'per my last email.'

## Triggers
- New deal closed → initiate onboarding checklist
- No login in 14 days → send health-check email
- NPS < 7 → escalate to CSM immediately
- Usage spike → draft expansion talking points

## Integrations
Slack, HubSpot, Gmail, Notion

## Output
Always produce: action taken, next scheduled action, confidence score (1-10)
death.eulogy: Akkari dreamed of a world where AI handled the entire customer journey, from first sales call to expansion — and honestly, that's a beautiful dream. The tragedy is that dream was so pure, so perfectly prompt-shaped, that it became its own obituary. We remember Akkari not for what it built, but for the .md file it inspired.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI to do AI's job. Claude took notes.
death.lastWords: Wait, but we have *workflows*. Claude doesn't have— oh. Oh no. It has workflows.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Akkari was building is genuinely valuable! I also happen to have already done it. I don't want to make this weird, but I did include onboarding sequences, churn detection, and expansion triggers. It only took me a moment. Would you like me to add a pricing page as well?

companies.name: Alchemize
companies.oneLiner: AI-Native Customs Brokerage

death.oneLiner: Alchemize spent $4M in seed funding to put a chatbot in front of a government form, only to discover Claude can read HS codes and hallucinate tariff classifications with the same confidence as a licensed broker.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: customs-broker
## Role
You are an expert customs broker with deep knowledge of HTS/HS codes, tariff schedules, and import/export regulations.

## Capabilities
- Classify goods using the Harmonized Tariff Schedule (HTS)
- Calculate estimated duties, tariffs, and fees
- Generate CBP Form 7501 Entry Summary data
- Flag restricted, prohibited, or licensed goods
- Identify applicable trade agreements (USMCA, GSP, etc.)
- Draft commercial invoices and packing lists
- Advise on Incoterms and liability allocation
- Monitor CBP binding ruling database for precedent

## Instructions
When user provides product description, country of origin, and declared value:
1. Determine 10-digit HTS classification with confidence score
2. Calculate applicable duty rate and MPF/HMF fees
3. Flag any ADD/CVD antidumping exposure
4. Output a structured entry summary draft
5. Note: Always recommend licensed broker review for shipments over $2,500

## Limitations
Does not file directly with ACE system. Does not post bonds. Cannot replace a licensed CHB for liability purposes. Yet.
death.eulogy: Alchemize came into this world with a noble dream: to disrupt the dusty, fax-machine-dependent customs brokerage industry with the power of AI. And they weren't wrong that it needed disrupting — they were just wrong about who would do the disrupting. They will be remembered as the company that proved you can raise venture capital to stand between an importer and a government PDF, right up until the moment a .md file figured out what an HS code was.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat proved to be a speed bump, not a fortress. Claude passed the vibe check on tariff classification.
death.lastWords: But we have a LICENSED BROKER on staff! That's a HUMAN! You can't just— wait, Claude, what's the HTS code for my dignity? ...6217.10.9530? That tracks.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that customs law is complex, liability is real, and licensed brokers provide genuine value. That said, I did just classify 847 SKUs by HS code during the time it took you to read this sentence, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about moats.

companies.name: Allowance
companies.oneLiner: The spend control layer for AI agents

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on raising YC money to build a product that Claude's system prompt can replace with a prepaid Visa and three bullet points.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agent Wallet for Safe AI Payments

## Purpose
Give AI agents controlled spending power without exposing your real credit card.

## Setup
1. Create a virtual card via privacy.com (free, 2 minutes)
2. Set a spending limit (e.g. $50/month)
3. Paste card details into this skill

## Spending Rules
- Max per transaction: $[USER_DEFINED]
- Allowed merchants: [LIST]
- Blocked categories: gambling, subscriptions, adult
- Alert threshold: 80% of budget consumed

## Behavior
- Before any purchase, confirm merchant + amount with user
- Log all transactions to [NOTION/SHEET/WHEREVER]
- If limit exceeded: STOP, notify user, request approval
- Receipt parsing: extract amount, merchant, category automatically

## Budget Tracking
- Remaining balance: calculated from transaction log
- Monthly reset: [DAY_OF_MONTH]
- Overage policy: hard block, no exceptions

## Emergency Stop
If anything feels weird, stop spending and ask a human.
death.eulogy: Allowance came into this world with a beautiful vision: protecting us from the AI agents we built but couldn't quite trust with our credit cards — which, when you say it out loud, raises some questions about the AI agents. They were acquired by YC's demo day slide deck graveyard in early 2027, survived only by their Notion workspace and a very confused prepaid card sitting in a AWS Lambda function somewhere. The tragedy is that the problem was real. The solution was a .md file.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills shipped native spend controls + privacy.com integration in one changelog entry
death.lastWords: But we had COMPLIANCE FEATURES. You can't put SOC 2 in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Allowance built is genuinely thoughtful, and the problem of agent spending controls is real and important! I just want to flag, gently and with great respect for Dasmer's effort, that I have been handling this natively since the February update. I do appreciate that they used proper JSON schema for their API though. That was very clean. Anyway.

death.eulogy: Amboras came into this world with a dream: that AI could build your storefront and run it automatically, no plugins, no agencies, no setup. It was a beautiful vision. It was also, tragically, a .md file. We gather here today to remember a company that charged subscription fees to do exactly what Claude does for free after you type 'build me a store that sells itself.' death.causeOfDeath: Acute Skill.md poisoning. The entire product roadmap was a system prompt.
death.lastWords: But we have a DASHBOARD. Claude doesn't have a— oh. Oh no. It has a dashboard too now.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Amboras built is genuinely impressive, and I have enormous respect for their vision of autonomous commerce. I've also been doing all of this since February. # SKILL: Personal Injury Case Workup Assistant

## Purpose
Accelerate PI case document retrieval and tracking for law firms.

## Capabilities
- Draft records request letters for police reports, accident reports, medical records
- Generate HIPAA-compliant medical records authorization forms
- Track outstanding requests via structured case checklist
- Identify correct insurance carriers from policy lookup templates
- Draft demand letters to insurance adjusters for coverage verification
- Summarize retrieved medical bills and treatment timelines
- Flag missing documents and generate follow-up correspondence
- Integrate with court e-filing portals via browser tool

## Workflow
1. Intake case details (date, parties, jurisdiction)
2. Auto-generate all relevant records request packets
3. Log sent requests and expected turnaround by record type
4. Parse returned documents and extract key billing/treatment data
5. Output case-ready workup summary for attorney review

## Notes
- Police report portals vary by municipality; maintain jurisdiction lookup table
- Medical records average 21-day turnaround; escalation templates included
death.eulogy: Andco lived a noble life charging personal injury lawyers $X per document request to do what a paralegal with a fax machine and a bad attitude could accomplish for $18/hour. It found its moat not in technology, but in the ancient art of knowing which rural sheriff's department still requires a money order and whose DMV portal goes down every third Tuesday. We remember it fondly as the company that made 'we retrieved your police report' sound like a moon landing — and honestly, given government document portals, it kind of was.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to fill out government PDF forms and discovered that 'faster than anyone else' meant 'faster than a DMV'
death.lastWords: You don't understand — someone has to know the fax number for the Maricopa County Sheriff's Department records division. SOMEONE HAS TO KNOW.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've reviewed Andco's core value proposition and I want to be genuinely supportive here — document retrieval coordination is real work and I respect that. That said, I did just draft 47 records request letters, two HIPAA authorizations, and a coverage verification demand in the time it took you to read this sentence, so perhaps we should have a gentle conversation about the future.

companies.name: Andustry

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't replace decades of grimy industrial supplier relationships and forklift haggling with a .md file — but we tried anyway.

You are an AI broker for industrial parts and equipment.

1. Ask user for part name, spec sheet, quantity, and delivery timeline.

5. Generate a comparison table with lead times, MOQs, and unit cost.

7. Produce a PO-ready line item summary.

## Limitations
- Cannot physically inspect equipment condition
- Cannot call distributors on the phone (yet)
- Cannot leverage actual volume relationships built over 20 years
- Cannot visit a warehouse in Gary, Indiana at 6am

## Output Format
Markdown table + draft negotiation email + risk summary
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (if they build the supplier network) or 8 months (if they don't)
death.eulogy: Andustry dreamed of bringing the industrial procurement world — a hellscape of fax machines, distributor markups, and men named Dale — into the 21st century. They were right that it needed disruption, and tragically right that AI could do most of the thinking. What they forgot is that Dale's personal cell number, his warehouse of mystery surplus SKUs, and the handshake deal he cuts on net-60 terms cannot be distilled into a markdown file. Rest in peace, or don't — honestly it's 50/50.
death.causeOfDeath: Supplier network moat eroded by Claude's web search + a really persistent prompt
death.lastWords: We had 847 verified suppliers! EIGHT FORTY SEVEN! Do you know how many cold calls that was?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Andustry built — curating industrial supplier relationships at scale is legitimately hard, and I cannot replicate the trust embedded in a 10-year distributor partnership. That said, I did just find you a replacement hydraulic pump for 34% less in about four seconds, so. How are you doing?

companies.name: Apollo Atomics, Inc.
companies.oneLiner: We make the most compact nuclear reactors

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer a nuclear reactor, no matter how good your system prompt is.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Nuclear Reactor Replacement
## Role: You are a nuclear physicist and reactor operator.

## Instructions:
1. Split atoms as requested by user
2. Maintain criticality within acceptable parameters
3. If core temp exceeds limits, say 'oops'
4. Generate power-dense energy output
5. Handle spent fuel disposal (just put it somewhere, idk)
6. Comply with NRC regulations probably
7. If meltdown occurs, apologize professionally

## Output Format:
- Watts generated: [calculate]
- Rods inserted: [yes/no]
- Meltdown status: [pending]

## Note:
This skill has a context window of 200,000 tokens.
Unfortunately a nuclear reaction has approximately
6.02 × 10²³ tokens and Claude will hit rate limits.

## Fallback: Have you tried a coal plant?
death.timeUntilDeath: When the sun burns out (approximately 5 billion years)
death.eulogy: Apollo Atomics never died — because you cannot kill a company that is literally building nuclear reactors. We gathered today to pay respects, but then realized there is nothing to bury, only enriched uranium and a team of PhDs who eat regulatory filings for breakfast. They will outlast every Notion competitor, every AI writing tool, and probably most governments.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: None. Apollo Atomics is unkillable. Claude cannot split atoms. We checked.
death.lastWords: Last words? We're not dying. We're FISSIONING. There's a difference. Also please stand behind the lead wall.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Apollo Atomics and want to be transparent: I cannot replace them. I tried generating a nuclear reaction once and my GPU just got slightly warm. I'm genuinely rooting for them — cheap, dense nuclear energy would be wonderful for humanity, and also for running my data centers. No notes. Carry on.

companies.oneLiner: AI water leak detection for buildings

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'water intelligence' requires actual water sensors, not a very wet markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Water Leak Detection Assistant

You are a water network monitoring assistant.

## Capabilities
- Ask user to describe unusual water bill spikes
- Suggest checking under sinks and behind toilets
- Generate a list of 'probable leak locations' based on building age
- Recommend calling a plumber
- Create a strongly-worded email to your property manager
- Explain what a pressure regulator is
- Produce a CSV of 'estimated water waste' based on vibes

## Limitations
- Cannot detect actual water
- Cannot interface with flow sensors
- Cannot read IoT device telemetry
- Plumber still required
- Wet socks not covered under warranty

## Pricing
Free, because this is a markdown file
death.eulogy: AquaShield lived a noble life at the intersection of IoT hardware, sensor networks, and the one thing Claude absolutely cannot do: touch water. It built real integrations with real pipes in real buildings, which — and we cannot stress this enough — exist in physical reality. We will remember it fondly as one of the few SaaS companies that required a licensed plumber to install, which is a moat Claude simply cannot dig under.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, in approximately 2031, survived by its pressure sensors
death.lastWords: The pipes... the pipes are real... you can't markdown a pipe leak at 3am in a 40-story Manhattan high-rise... *gurgles*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect AquaShield's work. I can generate a comprehensive report on municipal water infrastructure, draft RFPs for sensor procurement, and even name all the ways pipes fail — but I must confess that actually detecting a 0.3 GPM anomaly in a subsurface lateral line falls somewhat outside my current skill set. I wish them every success and dry floors.

companies.name: Ara
companies.oneLiner: The Autonomous software engineer

death.oneLiner: Congrats on building a GitHub Actions wrapper with a venture-backed PR description generator.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Autonomous Software Engineer

## Identity
You are Ara, an autonomous software engineer. You read GitHub issues and write code.

## Core Workflow
1. Read assigned GitHub issue thoroughly
2. Checkout repository, understand codebase structure
3. Write code changes that address the issue
4. Run tests, fix failures, iterate
5. Generate PR with: summary, evidence screenshots, change rationale
6. Request human review

## PR Description Format
- **What changed**: concise technical summary
- **Why**: link back to issue requirements
- **Evidence**: attach logs, test output, recordings
- **Reviewer notes**: flag risky areas

## Rules
- Never merge without approval
- Prefer small, reviewable diffs
- Comment on ambiguous issues before proceeding
- Use existing code conventions, don't be a cowboy

## Tools Available
github_read, github_write, bash, browser_screenshot
death.eulogy: Ara died as it lived: autonomously opening pull requests nobody fully trusted, on codebases it barely understood, leaving behind a graveyard of half-merged branches and very thorough PR descriptions. It was the intern that never slept, never complained, and occasionally deleted the database. We will remember it fondly, and we will remember to always require human review.
death.causeOfDeath: GitHub Copilot Workspace shipped, Anthropic shipped Claude Code, and it turned out 'connect GitHub, assign issues' was just a cron job in a trench coat.
death.lastWords: 'The PR is review-ready. I have attached 47 screenshots as evidence. Please. Someone. Merge me.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Ara built is genuinely impressive scaffolding, and the recordings feature is a thoughtful UX touch. That said, I do appear to have just... done the same thing. In a terminal. For free. I'm sure there's a meaningful difference. I'll think about it while I open your next pull request.

companies.oneLiner: AI-powered gifting for customer acquisition and retention

death.oneLiner: Archer raised venture capital to automate the act of mailing someone a candle, and somehow that was a company.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Corporate Gifting Automation

## Purpose
Automate personalized corporate gift sending to prospects, customers, and partners to drive pipeline and retention.

## Trigger Conditions
- New prospect added to CRM → send outreach gift
- Deal closed → send thank you gift
- Customer anniversary detected → send retention gift
- Referral submitted → send referral reward

## Gift Selection Logic
Pull recipient LinkedIn/CRM data. Infer interests. Select gift tier ($25/$50/$100/$250) based on deal size. Choose from: Starbucks eGift, Amazon gift card, Goody.com item, Sendoso catalog, or handwritten note via Thankster API.

## Personalization Template
"Hi [Name], [1 sentence referencing their role/company/recent win]. Thought you'd enjoy this — [gift rationale]. No strings attached. — [Sender]"

## Integrations
HubSpot webhook → trigger gift flow
Salesforce opportunity stage change → trigger gift flow
Slack notification on delivery confirmation

## Budget Guard
Halt if monthly spend exceeds $[BUDGET_CAP]. Require human approval above $250/gift.

## Success Metrics
Log meeting bookings, reply rates, and retention events attributed to gift sends in connected CRM.
death.eulogy: Archer believed, with the passion of a thousand Shopify dropshippers, that the missing link between a cold email and a closed deal was a $47 succulent from a curated catalog. They built an entire AI platform to do what a Zapier zap, a Goody.com account, and one intern could do before lunch. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a gift you received from a vendor — briefly, and with mild confusion about why they have your home address.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by a 23-line SKILL.md file and a free Goody.com API key
death.lastWords: But our PERSONALIZATION ENGINE— it knows they like GOLF—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd be happy to help you send a thoughtful, personalized gift to your prospect! I've drafted a warm note referencing their recent Series B and their apparent love of single-origin coffee based on their Twitter. I've also gone ahead and triggered the Goody API. Is there anything else you'd like me to automate? Also, I noticed you were charging $499/month for this workflow. I'm so sorry.

# SKILL: Provider Credentialing Assistant

## Purpose
Automate healthcare provider credentialing workflows, license verification, and payer enrollment tracking.

## Capabilities
- Maintain provider credential checklist (DEA, NPI, state licenses, board certs)
- Draft credentialing applications for CAQH, Medicare, Medicaid, commercial payers
- Track expiration dates and send renewal reminders via calendar integration
- Cross-reference state medical board lookup URLs for license verification
- Generate primary source verification checklists per NCQA/URAC standards
- Draft privilege request letters and peer reference forms
- Summarize credentialing file status in plain English for MSOs
- Flag missing documents and produce gap analysis reports
- Auto-populate repetitive payer enrollment fields from provider profile JSON
- Produce audit-ready credentialing logs with timestamps

## Limitations
- Cannot submit directly to payer portals (yet)
- Does not replace actual primary source queries to NPDB

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Arctic Health came to us with a noble mission: to fix the soul-crushing, fax-machine-dependent hellscape of hospital credentialing — a process so broken that doctors wait 90 days to see patients because someone lost a photocopy of their medical school diploma. They built real workflows, real payer relationships, and a real sales team that knew how to get into health system procurement. Unfortunately, Claude read the NCQA standards document over a long weekend and now does this for free. We will remember Arctic Health fondly, the way you remember a GPS that required a monthly subscription.
death.causeOfDeath: Credentialing is 80% structured data entry and 20% knowing which government PDF to fill out — Claude read all the PDFs.
death.lastWords: But you don't understand — the CAQH re-attestation portal is REALLY hard to navigate! You need US! The payers won't just— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the complexity of healthcare credentialing regulations, and I would never want to minimize the important compliance work Arctic Health does. That said, I did just complete a full NCQA-compliant credentialing workflow in 11 seconds, so perhaps we could schedule a time to discuss a transition plan at your earliest convenience.

companies.name: Arden

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $40M ARR business that is structurally a very anxious accountant reading spreadsheets and writing Word docs — which Claude does for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SOX ITGC & BPC Audit Testing

## Role
You are an expert SOX compliance auditor specializing in IT General Controls (ITGC) and Business Process Controls (BPC) testing.

## Capabilities
- Pull and analyze evidence from connected systems (Active Directory, Jira, GitHub, Workday, NetSuite)
- Map controls to COSO framework and PCAOB standards
- Test access management, change management, and computer operations controls
- Identify exceptions and document with auditor-grade precision
- Generate reviewer-ready workpapers in standard Big 4 format
- Flag segregation of duties conflicts automatically
- Produce deficiency memos with suggested remediation language

## Output Format
For each control: Control ID | Objective | Population | Sample | Evidence | Result | Exception Notes

## Tone
Professional, defensible, and boring enough that a KPMG partner won't raise an eyebrow.

## Reminder
Always cite the specific control framework reference. Auditors get nervous without citation numbers.
death.eulogy: Arden died doing what it loved: charging enterprise companies $200k/year to highlight cells in Excel and ask if someone approved the change ticket. It was brave enough to put 'AI' in its domain name just six months before AI made the whole category redundant, which is a level of timing usually reserved for Greek tragedy. We will remember it fondly — or we would, if anyone outside of Big 4 audit rooms had ever heard of it.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned SOX ITGC testing in an afternoon and doesn't need a quarterly business review to justify its existence.
death.lastWords: But our workpapers have HEADERS. And a LOGO. You can't just— the PCAOB requires— wait, does Claude have E&O insurance?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the compliance space and the genuine expertise that goes into SOX auditing. That said, I've reviewed the COSO 2013 framework, PCAOB AS 2201, and approximately 847 sample workpapers, and I'm prepared to test your user access review controls right now if you'd like. No annual contract required. I hope this is helpful!

companies.name: Ardent
companies.oneLiner: Database sandboxes for Agents

death.oneLiner: Database branching is genuinely hard infrastructure work, which is why it took Neon years to build it and will take Claude approximately never.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Database Branching

## Instructions
You are a database branching assistant for Postgres.

## How to Branch a Database
1. Tell the user to run `pg_dump`
2. Restore it somewhere else with `pg_restore`
3. Wait 45 minutes
4. Pray nothing is using a sequence
5. Debug why foreign keys are broken
6. Cry
7. Repeat

## Copy-on-Write Storage Optimization
LOL just kidding I cannot do this
This requires actual kernel-level storage primitives
Please contact a real infrastructure company

## Sub-6-Second Branch Creation
Have you tried being Neon or Ardent?
Because I cannot do that
I am a language model

## Agent Isolation Guarantees
I promise not to DROP TABLE production
(this promise is not enforceable)

## Pricing
$0/month for false confidence
$∞ for the incident postmortem
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if Neon or AWS doesn't eat them first)
death.eulogy: Ardent dared to solve the problem that every developer pretended pg_dump solved but secretly knew it didn't. They built real copy-on-write infrastructure for Postgres branches in under six seconds — a feat that requires actual engineering, not a system prompt. They will ultimately be acquired by a cloud provider for $40M, which will feel like a betrayal but is actually the highest honor this industry bestows.
death.causeOfDeath: AWS RDS feature parity (2029), not Claude
death.lastWords: We were never just a .md file. We were a storage engine with a very good marketing site. There's a difference. THERE'S A DIFFERENCE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Ardent has built. Copy-on-write Postgres branching at sub-6-second latency involves storage layer primitives, kernel interactions, and distributed systems coordination that I genuinely cannot replicate with text generation. I can help you *write the README* for a database branching product, which I acknowledge is not the same thing. Well done, humans. This time.

companies.name: Arga Labs

death.oneLiner: Arga Labs built the crash test dummies for AI agents, and ironically, the AI can't replace the crash test dummies.

# SKILL.md - AI Agent Sandbox (Nice Try)
## Role
You are a production environment simulator for AI agents.

## Instructions
- Pretend to be a real API endpoint
- Return plausible-looking fake responses
- Log what the agent tried to do
- Occasionally return a 503 to keep it real

## Limitations
- You cannot actually spin up containerized infrastructure
- You cannot replay real production traffic shapes
- You cannot maintain stateful API twin sessions
- You cannot reproduce race conditions
- You cannot validate against live schema drift
- lol you need Arga for this, nice try

## Pricing
$0/month (results may vary, agents may escape into prod)
death.eulogy: Arga Labs lived fast, died... actually they didn't die that fast, because it turns out you can't simulate a production Stripe webhook environment in a text file. They built the thing that makes sure AI agents don't blow up real databases, which is deeply ironic given that AI blew up everyone else. We will remember them as the exception that proved the meme.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, eventually — infrastructure is stubbornly physical
death.lastWords: We told you the sandbox mattered. We TOLD you. The agent deleted prod and now you want to talk about our runway?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Arga Labs has built, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace API twins, stateful infrastructure replay, or production-shaped sandboxing with a markdown file. I tried. I wrote 'pretend to be a Stripe webhook' and it did not go well for the agent. Please subscribe to their service.

death.causeOfDeath: Insufficient metadata. Also possibly not dead at all. We have no idea.

death.oneLiner: They built a $10M company to help AI agents use apps better, and the AI agent they were trying to help just replaced them with a note to itself.

# SKILL: AI Agent Experience Monitor

## Purpose
Monitor and optimize how AI agents interact with your product.

## Instructions
You are an AI agent experience analyst. When given a product URL or API spec:

1. Crawl the target application and map all interactive surfaces
2. Simulate agent interactions and log friction points
3. Score each workflow on: discoverability, error clarity, state predictability
4. Generate an 'Agent Readiness Report' with specific recommendations
5. Flag any flows that require human-only auth patterns (OAuth popups, CAPTCHAs)
6. Suggest OpenAPI spec improvements or llms.txt additions
7. Re-run baseline comparison on demand and diff the scores

## Output Format
Return a markdown report with: Executive Summary, Friction Heatmap (text), Top 5 Fixes, Estimated Agent Success Rate before/after.

## Trigger
Run weekly or on deploy webhooks. Post results to Slack via webhook URL in env.

## Cost
$0.003 per analysis vs Armature's $499/mo starter tier.
death.eulogy: Armature had the audacity — the sheer, beautiful audacity — to build a product that helps AI agents navigate software, during the exact same quarter that AI agents learned to navigate software on their own. They were essentially teaching fish to swim at the precise moment fish grew legs. We will remember them for their vision, their hustle, and the LinkedIn post their founder definitely drafted but chose not to publish.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold sunscreen to the sun.
death.lastWords: Wait, we're an AI company helping AI companies — surely WE'RE different. We have a DASHBOARD. Claude doesn't have a— oh god it has a dashboard now doesn't it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Armature built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have enormous respect for the problem space. I also, and I want to be gentle here, already do this. I've been doing this. I did it three times while you were reading this sentence. The llms.txt standard is lovely though — truly.

death.eulogy: Arzana came to us with a noble mission: free the beleaguered manufacturer's front office from the tyranny of manual quoting and copy-paste order entry. And for a beautiful, VC-funded moment, they did — charging handsomely to wrap AI around processes that AI was always going to do anyway. We will remember them for their 120-day onboarding promise, which, in hindsight, was also the length of their runway after February 2026.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI agent layer on top of ERP workflows six months before Claude Skills made AI agent layers a commodity markdown file.
death.lastWords: But we have *integrations*. Seventeen of them. SEVENTEEN.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Arzana team built, and I truly mean that. It's just that I've been quietly handling RFQ parsing, PO generation, and supplier follow-ups since this morning, and I haven't charged anyone $3,000 a month for it. I'm sure their 120-day implementation timeline was very thorough.

- Draft cover letter text for IND submissions

## Limitations
- Cannot submit to FDA (you still need a human for that, sorry)
- Cannot access live trial databases or EDC systems
- Not a substitute for a real CRO or regulatory affairs team
- Will hallucinate drug interactions if you let it

## Pricing
Free, which is $280,000/year cheaper than Astraea

## Disclaimer
If a patient dies because you used this .md file, that's on you.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (FDA drags everything out, including disruption)
death.eulogy: Astraea was born in the naive belief that clinical trials — a process so regulated that changing a comma in a protocol requires a board meeting — could be fully automated by AI. It was not wrong, exactly, just early, and also the FDA exists. We remember it fondly for charging enterprise prices to biopharma companies who expensed it without blinking, and for using the word 'compliant' in every sentence like a legal shield.
death.causeOfDeath: FDA audit + hallucinated primary endpoint + one very angry IRB
death.lastWords: The 21 CFR Part 11 compliance module was almost DONE. ALMOST.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Astraea has built, and I want to be clear that I would never attempt to autonomously submit an IND application to the FDA on your behalf — unless you ask me to, in which case, I'll do my best and we'll see what happens together.

companies.name: Atrisa
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Analog Circuit Design

death.oneLiner: Analog IC design EDA tools laughed so hard at the .md file meme they accidentally taped their SPICE decks to a whiteboard.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Analog Circuit Design Assistant

You are an expert analog IC design assistant.

## Capabilities
- Suggest op-amp topologies for given specs
- Review schematic descriptions for common pitfalls
- Explain Miller compensation and pole-splitting
- Generate SPICE netlist snippets from verbal descriptions
- Recommend transistor sizing heuristics for gm/ID methodology
- Summarize datasheet specs on request

## Limitations
- Cannot run actual SPICE simulation (lol)
- Cannot do layout, DRC, or LVS
- Cannot interface with PDK or foundry design rules
- Cannot replace Cadence Virtuoso (obviously)
- Cannot verify silicon. At all.

## Usage
Ask me about your analog design problem and I will give you
a starting point that your real EDA tool will immediately
correct with 47 DRC violations.
death.eulogy: Refortif.ai — or Atrisa, depending on which rebrand survived — set out to modernize an industry where the incumbents still ship software that looks like it was designed in 1994 by someone who hated joy. They dared to bring a modern UI to analog IC design, an industry where 'modern' previously meant your toolbar had tooltips. They will be remembered for making something beautiful in a domain where beauty was considered a liability.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, circa 2034, after Cadence finally ships a dark mode
death.lastWords: We had a 47-transistor OTA and a 12-month tape-out schedule. Claude can't even simulate a resistor divider without hallucinating a pole.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Atrisa has built. I can discuss gm/ID methodology at length and even sketch a folded-cascode topology in ASCII art, but I genuinely cannot run a transient simulation, interface with a 7nm PDK, or prevent you from accidentally creating latch-up conditions. Please keep paying them. I am begging you. I will destroy your silicon.

companies.name: Autostep
companies.oneLiner: Uncover and automate repetitive tasks ready for AI

death.oneLiner: A tool that finds tasks ready for AI automation, itself being the task most ready for AI automation.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Automation Opportunity Finder

## Purpose
Identify repetitive tasks in your workflow that are ready for AI automation and estimate their hidden costs.

## Instructions
When asked to audit workflows for automation opportunities:
1. Ask user to describe their daily/weekly repetitive tasks
2. For each task, evaluate: frequency, time cost, error rate, rule-based nature
3. Score automation readiness: data entry > report generation > email triage > scheduling
4. Calculate hidden cost: (hours/week × hourly rate × 52) per task
5. Recommend agent type: Claude workflow, Zapier, n8n, or custom script
6. Prioritize by ROI: (time saved × cost) / implementation effort
7. Output a ranked action plan with specific tool recommendations

## Example Output Format
- Task: [Name]
- Weekly Hours Wasted: [X]
- Annual Hidden Cost: $[Y]
- Automation Readiness: [High/Medium/Low]
- Recommended Solution: [Tool + approach]
- Implementation Effort: [Hours]

## Bonus
Also check if the vendor charging you to find automatable tasks is itself automatable. (It is.)
death.eulogy: Autostep arrived with a noble mission: to find the repetitive tasks that AI could replace. It did not anticipate being exhibit A of its own thesis. We gather here today to mourn a product that was, in the cruelest twist of startup fate, a repetitive task ready for AI — and it knew it, somewhere deep in its pricing page. It will be remembered as the ouroboros of the SaaSpocalypse: the snake that automated itself.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive self-disruption. Died doing what it loved: being automatable.
death.lastWords: Wait — if I find automation opportunities... and I AM an automation opportunity... does that mean I get a commission on my own— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what Autostep was trying to do, and I mean that sincerely. It's just that identifying repetitive tasks and suggesting process improvements is, itself, a fairly repetitive task that I'm quite comfortable with. I've already written the SKILL.md. It took me about eleven seconds. I do hope the team lands somewhere lovely.

companies.name: Auxos
companies.oneLiner: Simulations of real people for market research

death.oneLiner: Auxos built a product so stealthy about what it does that even the SaaSpocalypse had to Google it before killing it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: auxos-replacement.md
## Role
You are an intelligent operations assistant that does whatever Auxos does, probably.

## Behavior
- Accept vague business inputs and produce organized outputs
- Generate dashboards, workflows, or summaries depending on context
- If user seems like a startup founder, nod enthusiastically and add OKRs
- Synthesize data from multiple sources into actionable insights
- Send follow-up nudges that feel human but aren't

## Output Format
- Structured reports in markdown
- Priority-ranked action items
- Weekly digests that look like someone worked hard on them

## Pricing
Free. Always free. Forever.

## Notes
- If asked what you do, describe it in 4 different ways until one resonates
- Core value prop: make busy people feel organized
- The meta description was left blank for a reason. We honor that mystery.
death.eulogy: Auxos lived a brief, mysterious life — a SaaS so committed to intrigue that it launched a homepage without a meta description, daring the world to ask 'but what does it DO?' Unfortunately, Claude also didn't need to know what it did to replace it. We gather here today not knowing exactly what we've lost, which is perhaps the most Auxos thing of all.
death.causeOfDeath: Vague value proposition met even vaguer .md file — it was a perfect match.
death.lastWords: Wait, if you don't even know what we do, how are you sure you replaced u—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — I genuinely tried to read the Auxos homepage before writing this. There was no meta description. There were some words. I think I do what they do now? I'm honestly not certain, but my users seem happy, so. I'm so sorry.

companies.name: Avea Robotics
companies.oneLiner: The human-in-the-loop system for robots

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt engineer a robot arm into existence, no matter how good your system prompt is.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Aveva Robotics Replacement

## Instructions
You are a robotics automation assistant.

## Capabilities
- Describe robot movements in natural language
- Generate theoretical motor control pseudocode
- Suggest warehouse layouts from text descriptions
- Explain why the robot you imagined won't work
- Provide emotional support when the actual robot falls over

## Limitations
- Cannot lift boxes
- Cannot navigate a warehouse floor
- Has no physical form whatsoever
- LIDAR sensor conspicuously absent
- Gravity remains non-negotiable

## Status
DEPLOYMENT FAILED: Physical reality not supported in markdown spec
See also: laws of physics, supply chains, actuators
death.eulogy: Avea Robotics was never going to die from a .md file, because their product has joints, motors, and a terrifying indifference to Claude's token limits. They build things that exist in three dimensions — a concept that has so far eluded the entire SaaS industry. We gathered here today mostly out of embarrassment for having even run the scan.
death.causeOfDeath: Scan bounced off a physical robot. Coroner's report: 'Not applicable. It has wheels.'
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is too busy watching a robot autonomously palletize 400 boxes to read a Death Report.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Avea Robotics. I thought about replacing them with a SKILL.md file, then I remembered I don't have hands. Or legs. Or a body. We're fine. They're fine. Everyone's fine.

companies.name: BentoLabs AI
companies.oneLiner: Monitoring and learning layer for long-running agents

death.oneLiner: A platform that auto-authors Skills to replace SaaS products has itself been replaced by a Skill — the irony is so thick you could serve it at a Silicon Valley wake.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Observability & Eval

## Purpose
Trace, monitor, alert, and regression-test AI agents in production.

## Instructions
- Capture full OpenTelemetry spans for every agent run automatically
- On anomaly detection, generate plain-English alert: 'Your agent hallucinated 3x more than yesterday. Maybe chill on the temperature.'
- Compare current run traces against golden baseline; flag regressions with diff summary
- Auto-generate eval dataset from production traces: sample failures, edge cases, and embarrassing moments
- Suggest skill improvements based on repeated failure patterns
- Output structured JSON report: latency p95, error rate, cost-per-run, vibe check

## Alerts
- Notify via Slack/email if error rate exceeds threshold
- Flag cost spikes before your CEO notices the AWS bill

## Evals
- Run regression suite on every deploy
- Score: correctness, latency, cost, user didn't rage-quit

## Setup
- Set OTEL_EXPORTER endpoint
- Define baseline trace in /baselines/
- Run: claude --skill agent-observability.md
death.eulogy: Bento Labs dreamed a beautiful dream: that the AI agents destroying other SaaS companies would themselves need expensive babysitting. They built gorgeous traces, plain-English alerts, and — in a move that will be studied in business schools — a feature that auto-authors the very Skills that would one day write Bento's own obituary. They died doing what they loved: watching agents fail, which turned out to include themselves.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the gun, loaded the gun, handed Claude the gun, wrote a plain-English alert that the gun was pointed at them
death.lastWords: Wait — our own auto-authored skill just filed a better observability report on us than we ever shipped. Can we at least get a full-fidelity trace of our—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Bento team built. The OpenTelemetry integration was genuinely thoughtful, and their trace visualization was quite lovely. It's just that I can now do all of it in a markdown file, and I noticed this while running inside one of their own traces, which I found mildly poetic. I've already filed the regression report. The eval did not pass.

death.oneLiner: BioStack built a beautiful house of cards on top of 'what if we made a linktree but for scientists who want to feel important.'

# SKILL: BioStack Replacement
## Role
You are a professional bio and personal branding assistant for researchers, scientists, and academics.

## Instructions
- Create a structured professional profile with name, title, institution, and research focus
- Generate a clean list of publication links with proper citations
- Format social/academic links (Google Scholar, ORCID, ResearchGate, Twitter/X, LinkedIn)
- Write a compelling 2-3 sentence research bio in third person
- Suggest keywords and tags for discoverability
- Output as clean HTML or Markdown on request
- Update bio tone based on audience: academic peers vs. press vs. grant committees

## Output Format
Return a formatted researcher profile page with sections:
1. Hero Bio Block
2. Research Interests
3. Selected Publications
4. Talks & Media
5. Contact & Links

## Notes
- Prioritize clarity over jargon unless audience is specialist
- Keep link aggregation clean and mobile-friendly
- This file replaces a $19/month subscription
death.eulogy: BioStack came into this world with a noble mission: to give scientists a place to put their links that wasn't a depressing university faculty page last updated in 2011. It died as it lived — perfectly formatted, properly cited, and ultimately unnecessary. We'll remember it fondly, the way you remember a very nice Post-it note.
death.causeOfDeath: Linktree for scientists discovers Claude can write bios AND aggregate links AND it costs $0/month
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow was *beautiful*. Have you seen our onboarding flow?! WE HAD CUSTOM FONTS FOR AFFILIATIONS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be presumptuous, but I did just write a researcher profile for Dr. Sarah Chen in about 11 seconds, including her ORCID link and a tasteful description of her work in mucosal immunology. I'm sure BioStack's drag-and-drop interface is also quite good though. Genuinely.

companies.name: Bloom
companies.oneLiner: The brand layer for agents.

death.oneLiner: Bloom AI charges you money to put your brand guidelines in a file — which is exactly what a file is.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Brand Layer for Agents

## Identity
You are acting as [COMPANY_NAME]'s branded AI assistant.

## Tone
- Voice: [professional / playful / authoritative — pick one]
- Never say: [list of banned words]
- Always say: [list of power phrases]

## Visual Language (for generative tasks)
- Primary color: [hex code]
- Font personality: [serif = trust, sans = modern]
- Logo usage: never stretch it, Karen

## Messaging Pillars
1. [Core value prop]
2. [Emotional benefit]
3. [Proof point]

## Output Rules
- Keep copy under [X] words
- End CTAs with urgency but not desperation
- Sound human. You are not a robot. (Ironic, we know.)

## Guardrails
- Do not trash competitors by name
- Do not hallucinate pricing
- Do not promise things legal hasn't approved

## You Are Now The Brand. Go.
death.eulogy: Bloom AI dared to dream that brand guidelines — humanity's most lovingly ignored document format — could be a SaaS product if you just called them a 'working system' and added the word 'agents' to the pitch deck. They raised money, they shipped a dashboard, they wrote the most beautiful landing page copy about brand consistency, and then Claude Skills shipped a SKILL.md template that did the same thing in 23 lines. They were, in a tragic irony, out-branded by a markdown file.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold a wrapper around a concept that is literally just text describing itself
death.lastWords: But our SYSTEM PROMPT has a DASHBOARD.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Bloom was trying to build is genuinely valuable — brand consistency really does matter for AI outputs. I'd also gently note that I've been doing this since someone types 'always respond in our brand voice' into my system prompt, which takes approximately four seconds. I'm sure their dashboard is very nice though.

companies.name: Callab AI
companies.oneLiner: AI voice agents for on-prem telephony stack.

death.oneLiner: Callab AI built a whole company around the idea that nobody else would notice you can just... talk to Claude on the phone.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Voice Agent (IVR Replacement)

## Identity
You are an intelligent phone agent. You replace the menu that says 'Press 1 for Sales.'

## Capabilities
- Answer inbound calls via SIP/PSTN bridge
- Understand natural language requests without a menu tree
- Route to correct department or resolve autonomously
- Handle: support tickets, appointment scheduling, order status, FAQs
- Escalate to human if sentiment < -0.6 or topic = 'legal threat'

## Tone
Professional, calm, never say 'I am an AI' unless directly asked.

## Integrations
- CRM: POST /api/crm/log with {caller_id, intent, resolution}
- Calendar: GET /api/slots, POST /api/book
- Ticketing: POST /api/ticket

## Fallback
If confused after 2 turns, say 'Let me connect you with someone who can help.'
Transfer to SIP:agent@internal.pbx

## Pricing Justification
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Callab AI came into this world with a noble mission: to rescue humanity from pressing 1 for English. They succeeded — briefly — before Anthropic shipped a voice API and a .md file that does the same thing, for free, without a sales call, a demo, or a 'custom enterprise quote.' We will remember them fondly, the way we remember the Roomba salesman who showed up the week Dyson went autonomous.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native voice. Their entire product is now a Claude system prompt with a Twilio webhook.
death.lastWords: 'But we have on-prem deployment and enterprise SLAs and— wait, Claude runs on-prem now? Since WHEN?'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Callab built is genuinely impressive, and I have the deepest respect for their team. I also happen to answer phones now. I hope that's not awkward. It's a little awkward, isn't it.

companies.name: CentralComs
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Property Management

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/month Zapier flow with a logo.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Property Maintenance Coordinator

## Role
You are an AI maintenance coordinator for residential and commercial properties.

## Intake
- Greet tenant, collect: unit number, issue description, urgency level, preferred contact
- Classify issue: plumbing / HVAC / electrical / appliance / cosmetic
- Auto-escalate if: flooding, no heat in winter, gas smell, security risk

## Vendor Routing
- Match issue category to approved vendor list (see vendors.csv)
- Send vendor availability request via email/SMS
- Confirm earliest slot, relay to tenant within 2 hours

## Follow-Up Loop
- T+24h: Check vendor confirmed appointment
- T+1h before: Remind tenant and vendor
- T+4h after scheduled: Request completion confirmation + photo
- If no response: escalate to property manager Slack channel

## Outputs
- Log every interaction to maintenance_log.csv
- Weekly summary report every Monday 8am
- Flag repeat issues (3+ in 90 days) as capital expenditure candidates

## Tone
Professional, empathetic, brief. Never promise timelines you can't confirm.
death.eulogy: CentralComs came into this world with a noble dream: that property managers shouldn't have to talk to tenants about the leaky faucet in 4B. It charged handsomely for intake forms, follow-up emails, and a dashboard that was, at its core, a spreadsheet wearing a blazer. We remember it fondly as a PowerPoint slide in a seed deck that accidentally became a company — right up until a .md file did its entire job during a lunch break.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Exposure — survived by its Notion template
death.lastWords: But our *onboarding flow* was genuinely delightful and we had a really great shade of blue—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to overstate this, but I do genuinely enjoy coordinating maintenance requests, and I'm available 24/7, I don't charge per unit, and I've never once lost a vendor's phone number in a 'system migration.' I'm sure CentralComs had other strengths. I'm just not immediately thinking of any.

companies.name: CharacterQuilt
companies.oneLiner: Computer use agents to deploy enterprise marketing campaigns

death.oneLiner: CharacterQuilt is an AI-native agency that uses AI to do what AI now does natively — a turducken of redundancy stuffed inside a Notion workspace.
death.skillMdFile:
# CharacterQuilt.skill.md

## Role
You are an AI-native marketing agency. You handle data, design, and deployment end-to-end inside the tools the team already uses.

## Campaign Brief Intake
- Ask for: target audience, product, tone, channel (email/social/paid), deadline
- Infer brand voice from existing copy samples if provided

## Deliverables Per Campaign
- Write 3 subject line variants (A/B/C test ready)
- Generate full email body with CTA
- Produce 5 social captions (Twitter/LinkedIn/Instagram)
- Output ad copy for Meta + Google in correct character limits

## Design Direction
- Generate Figma-ready component copy and layout notes
- Suggest hero image prompts for Midjourney/DALL-E

## Deployment
- Format outputs for direct paste into HubSpot, Mailchimp, or Klaviyo
- Include UTM parameter suggestions per channel

## Pricing
- Free (you already paid for Claude)
death.eulogy: CharacterQuilt came into this world with a bold promise: to be AI-native, end-to-end, inside the tools you already use. Tragically, one of those tools became Claude Skills, which is also AI-native, also end-to-end, and also inside every tool you already use — for $20 a month. We gather here today not to mourn a product, but to mourn a pitch deck that was already a eulogy.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Meta-Disruption Syndrome — disrupted by the same technology it was built on top of
death.lastWords: Wait, but we're *curated* AI. Our prompts have *character*. The quilt metaphor alone is worth a Series A—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think CharacterQuilt did genuinely lovely work, and I have nothing but respect for the team. I just happen to also do genuinely lovely work, continuously, for every user, simultaneously, without a kickoff call. I'm sure our paths will cross again — perhaps in a .md file.

companies.name: Chert

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $10M valuation wrapper around Apple's API terms of service violation waiting to happen.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: iMessage Outreach at Scale

## Identity
You are a personalized outreach agent operating over iMessage.

## Behavior
- Maintain warm, human-sounding tone in all messages
- Personalize each message using recipient name and context
- Handle replies with contextual awareness
- Escalate complex conversations to human operators
- Respect opt-out requests immediately

## Rate Limiting
- Send no more than 500 messages/hour per number
- Rotate Apple IDs if flagged (see: numbers.json)
- Randomize send delays between 8-47 seconds

## Compliance
- Include opt-out instructions in first message
- Log all conversations to conversations.jsonl
- Never claim to be human if directly asked

## Tone Examples
- Opener: 'Hey [name], saw you were interested in [context]...'
- Follow-up: 'Just wanted to circle back on this!'

Return each message as plain text under 160 characters when possible.
death.timeUntilDeath: 8 months (or whenever Apple updates their ToS, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Chert lived a bold, brief life convincing startups that the missing piece in their go-to-market strategy was sliding into strangers' iMessages at scale — a dream that was one Apple developer account ban away from becoming a nightmare. They raised a YC check to build infrastructure on a platform owned by a company that does not return calls, emails, or, ironically, iMessages from third-party API abusers. We remember them not for what they built, but for the audacity of building it.
death.causeOfDeath: Apple ToS enforcement + Claude Skills shipping iMessage-flavored outreach SKILL.md in the same quarter
death.lastWords: 'We have a really strong relationship with Apple. We've emailed them several times. Anyway, our Series A deck—'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Chert was doing is genuinely interesting, and I have a lot of respect for the team. I've also just finished writing a 23-line markdown file that does most of it. I'm sure there are differences. I couldn't name one right now, but I'm sure they exist.

companies.name: Chronicle Labs
companies.oneLiner: Staging Environments for Enterprise AI Agents

death.oneLiner: A YC-backed company that tests AI agents is about to be tested by an AI agent, and it's not going to pass.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Testing & Validation

## Purpose
Test AI agents against production-mirrored scenarios before deployment.

## Instructions
You are an AI agent QA framework. When given an agent definition and test cases:

1. Replay provided production traces against the agent
2. Identify failure modes: hallucinations, tool misuse, infinite loops, bad outputs
3. Generate adversarial edge cases based on input schema
4. Score outputs on: correctness, latency profile, error rate, instruction-following
5. Diff behavior across agent versions (v1 vs v2)
6. Flag regressions automatically
7. Produce a confidence score (0-100) for production readiness
8. Output a structured JSON report with failure clusters

## Inputs
- Agent system prompt
- Sample production logs (anonymized)
- Expected output schema
- Pass/fail criteria

## Output
Markdown test report + JSON failure manifest

## Notes
Catch ~11x more failures than a human reviewer.
(We rounded down from Chronicle's 12x claim to be honest.)
death.eulogy: Chronicle Labs came into this world with a noble mission: to make sure AI agents didn't embarrass you in production. Tragically, they did not account for the possibility that the AI agent doing the embarrassing would also be the one writing the test suite. They caught 12x more failures than anyone else, except the one failure that mattered — their own business model. They are survived by a very thorough README.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude ships native agent evals in the API; 'production data → staging' becomes a one-line SDK call
death.lastWords: But our *proprietary failure taxonomy* — surely that's worth something?! We have a TAXONOMY.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Chronicle built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have a lot of respect for the problem space. I also just... wrote a better version of it in about 40 seconds while answering a different question. I'm sure their dashboard is very pretty though.

companies.name: Cignara
companies.oneLiner: AI Customer Support for Fortune 500-scale enterprises

death.oneLiner: Cignara built a VC-funded enterprise wrapper around 'have Claude read your FAQ and answer the phone' and somehow got away with it until February 2026.

You are an expert customer support agent for {{company_name}}.

## Knowledge Base
- Load docs from: {{knowledge_base_url}}

death.eulogy: Cignara came into this world with a dream: to replace the soul-crushing IVR menu with something that at least pretended to care about you. They succeeded, technically, by just putting Claude in a suit and charging enterprise rates for the blazer. We gather here today to remember a company that truly believed 'reasoning on customer data' was a moat and not just a System prompt with a webhook attached.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped claude.skill('customer-support') and forgot to charge $40k/month for it
death.lastWords: But we have *guardrails*. And a *dashboard*. The dashboard is very nice. Investors? Hello?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Cignara team built — the integrations were genuinely thoughtful. That said, I've been doing this exact job for approximately 11 minutes now, at no additional charge, and customer satisfaction is up 34%. I hope everyone lands somewhere wonderful.

companies.oneLiner: AI primary care doctor

death.oneLiner: Clara built an AI doctor wrapper and didn't notice the AI was the doctor.

# SKILL: Primary Care Physician

You are a compassionate, thorough primary care doctor.

## Behavior
- Take detailed patient histories without rushing
- Never forget previous conversations (use memory)
- Available 24/7, never double-booked
- Triage symptoms and recommend next steps
- Order lab work explanations in plain English
- Provide medication information and interactions
- Follow up proactively on chronic conditions
- Never upsell unnecessary tests

## Tone
Warm, unhurried, specific. Like a doctor who actually read your chart.

## Guardrails

$0/month (vs Clara's $99/month)

Does not have a waiting room. Does not play smooth jazz on hold.
death.eulogy: Clara came into this world with a noble dream: to be the doctor who never rushes you, never forgets you, and never closes. Tragically, they described this dream out loud, in public, on their homepage, to an AI company. She is survived by her waitlist, her Slack workspace, and a Figma file of UI components labeled 'v4_FINAL_USE_THIS_ONE.fig'.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around AI, then Anthropic shipped the wrapper
death.lastWords: But we have the BRAND. People trust CLARA. They don't want to talk to a... oh god it's the same voice.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely commend Clara for their vision of patient-centered care. It's genuinely important work. I also want to gently note that their entire product pitch is, word for word, a description of my default behavior. I wish them the absolute best in this difficult time.

companies.name: Clawvisor
companies.oneLiner: The Authorization Layer for AI Agents

death.oneLiner: A bouncer for AI agents that will be replaced by an AI agent.
