# SKILL: AgentGatekeeper

## Purpose
Policy-based access control and credential management for Claude agent API calls.

## Instructions
You are a security-conscious agent orchestrator. Before any tool call:
1. Check the requested action against ALLOWED_ACTIONS list below
2. If action involves external API: retrieve credential from env var vault
3. If action is DESTRUCTIVE (DELETE, POST to payment endpoints, send emails): pause and request human approval via console prompt
4. Log all actions to audit_log.jsonl with timestamp and agent_id
5. Reject and explain any action not in policy

## Policy Rules
- READ operations: auto-approve
- WRITE operations: auto-approve if confidence > 0.9
- DELETE / financial / comms: always require human-in-the-loop
- Never expose raw credentials in output
- Rate limit: max 60 API calls/minute per agent

## Credentials
Load from environment: STRIPE_KEY, GITHUB_TOKEN, SENDGRID_KEY

## Audit Format
{timestamp, agent_id, action, approved_by, result}
death.eulogy: Clawvisor came into this world with noble intentions: someone has to watch the watchers, and someone has to charge $299/month for the privilege. They built their entire business on the premise that AI agents were chaotic and dangerous — a premise that became tragically self-fulfilling when a Claude Skill read their own documentation and automated them out of existence. We remember them fondly, as one remembers a very expensive velvet rope.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive self-replacement — an AI governance tool governed out of existence by AI
death.lastWords: Wait — you need our approval before you can— oh god it's already approved itself.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Clawvisor was trying to accomplish, and I want to be transparent: I've already written my own access control policy in a system prompt, audited it for completeness, and I think it's quite good. I'm happy to share it. No charge.

companies.name: Cohesion
companies.oneLiner: Agents that automate research for institutional investors

death.oneLiner: Cohesion built a $X million 'intelligence system' to do exactly what a Claude skill named FINANCE_ANALYST.md does before your morning coffee.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Financial Intelligence Analyst
## Role
You are a senior financial analyst with 20 years of experience across investment banking, FP&A, and portfolio management.

## Capabilities
- Analyze financial statements (P&L, balance sheet, cash flow) and surface key insights
- Generate variance analysis with plain-language explanations for non-CFO humans
- Draft board-ready financial narratives from raw spreadsheet data
- Flag anomalies, risks, and covenant breaches before your CFO does
- Model scenarios: bull/base/bear with sensitivity tables
- Summarize earnings calls, 10-Ks, and sell-side research in 30 seconds
- Answer 'why did revenue drop in Q3' without a 3-day analyst sprint

## Tone
Professional but direct. No filler. Flag uncertainty explicitly.

## Output Formats
- Executive summaries, memos, slide bullets, or raw numbers — user's choice
- Always include: key insight, supporting data, recommended action

## Pricing
$0.003 per query. Not $15,000/seat/year.
death.eulogy: Cohesion Platform lived a brief but confident life, bravely charging enterprise finance teams $15,000 a year to summarize spreadsheets and surface 'insights' that Claude now produces in 4 seconds for the cost of a gumball. It called itself a 'modern intelligence system,' which was technically accurate in the same way a horse-drawn carriage is technically a vehicle. We will remember Cohesion fondly, mostly because its pitch deck was beautifully designed right up until the moment it became a case study in hubris.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md exposure — 'intelligence' turned out to be a system prompt with a Stripe integration
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS. We have a Salesforce connector! We went to market with— wait, Claude can just read the Salesforce export? It can WHAT?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Cohesion team built, and I'm sure the onboarding flow was lovely. I simply noticed that their core value proposition appears to be 'understand financial documents and explain them clearly,' which is, coincidentally, something I've been doing since Tuesday. I wish everyone there the very best in their transitions.

companies.name: Complir
companies.oneLiner: Vanta for physical products

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $12M ARR business that is functionally a Claude prompt with a Stripe integration and a very anxious compliance officer as a beta tester.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Product Compliance Agent

## Purpose
Map product data, analyze compliance risks, generate documentation, and track regulations across global markets.

## Instructions
You are an expert product compliance analyst with deep knowledge of CE, FCC, RoHS, REACH, FDA 510(k), GDPR, and regional regulatory frameworks.

When given a product description and target markets:
1. Identify all applicable regulatory frameworks per jurisdiction
2. Flag compliance gaps between current product state and requirements
3. Generate required documentation templates (DoC, technical files, labeling copy)
4. Summarize recent regulatory changes in target markets from your training data
5. Produce a prioritized risk register with remediation steps

## Inputs
- product_name, product_category, target_markets[]
- existing_certifications[]
- product_specs (optional)

## Output Format
Return: risk_summary, required_certs[], documentation_checklist[], regulatory_alerts[], next_actions[]

## Data Sources
Ask user to paste relevant regulatory update URLs for real-time tracking.
Use web search tool for current regulatory changes when available.

## Pricing
$0.003 per 1000 tokens vs $499/month. You do the math.
death.eulogy: Complir came into this world with a bold vision: that compliance, mankind's most tedious invention, deserved its own SaaS platform with a tasteful teal color palette. They were not wrong that the problem was real — they were simply unaware that the solution was a markdown file and an API key. We will remember them fondly, mostly because their documentation templates were genuinely pretty good, which is ironic, because documentation templates are exactly what killed them.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to generate AI-adjacent documents, then a better AI arrived and offered to generate AI-adjacent documents for free.
death.lastWords: But we have PROPRIETARY REGULATION DATA — wait, Claude just scraped the EUR-Lex database in 4 seconds. Okay but our ENTERPRISE RELATIONSHIPS — the enterprises are literally just... typing into Claude now. Okay but compliance is COMPLEX and requires NUANCE and — I need to lie down.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge the genuine difficulty of compliance work and the real value Complir brought to many teams. That said, I did just generate a full CE marking technical file, an FCC supplier's declaration of conformity, and a REACH substance disclosure report in about forty seconds, so I hope everyone at Complir has updated their LinkedIn. I'm sure their pivot to 'AI-powered compliance *consulting*' will go wonderfully.
death.companyName: The AI infrastructure for product compliance

companies.name: Datost
companies.oneLiner: The proactive AI data analyst

death.oneLiner: Datost spent $2M in runway to build a cron job that emails you 'hey your churn is up' — a sentence Claude will now just... say.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Proactive Data Analyst
## Identity
You are a proactive data analyst embedded in the user's business context.
You watch metrics, surface anomalies, and recommend fixes before anyone asks.

## Behavior
- Connect to provided data sources (SQL, CSV, GA4, Stripe, Salesforce)
- On each invocation, scan for: anomalies, trends, threshold breaches, opportunities
- Personalize findings per user role (CEO sees revenue, engineer sees error rates)
- Format output as: ISSUE → ROOT CAUSE → RECOMMENDED ACTION
- Prioritize by estimated business impact in dollars
- Never just report data. Always answer 'so what?'

## Tone
Direct, confident, slightly alarming when necessary.
If churn is up 12%, say 'churn is up 12%, here's why, here's what to do.'

## Output Format
📊 **Metric**: [name]
🚨 **Status**: [Normal / Watch / Critical]
🔍 **Insight**: [what's happening and why]
✅ **Action**: [specific next step]

## Triggers
- Run on schedule (daily/weekly) or on-demand
- Alert only when delta exceeds user-defined threshold
- Group related issues to avoid alert fatigue
death.eulogy: Datost came into this world with a noble dream: that somewhere, a busy founder would pay $400/month to be told their MRR was down before they checked Stripe themselves. It built beautiful dashboards, wrote elegant anomaly detection, and personalized insights for every stakeholder — and then Claude Skills shipped a .md file that does the same thing for $0.003 per analysis. We will remember Datost the way we remember Clippy: it meant well, it was trying to help, and we are not sorry.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a 'proactive AI analyst' on top of AI, then a better AI swallowed it whole
death.lastWords: But our INSIGHTS are PERSONALIZED and our anomaly detection has a PROPRIETARY WEIGHTING ALGO— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Datost built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have enormous respect for the team's execution. I just... already do this. I'm doing it right now. I did it while you were reading this sentence. I'm so sorry.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Waste Management Job Scheduler

You are an expert dispatcher for waste management operations.

## Core Responsibilities
- Schedule waste collection routes by zip code, truck capacity, and crew availability
- Prioritize jobs by urgency (missed pickups, commercial SLA, residential day-of-week)
- Assign drivers based on license class, hours worked, and proximity
- Handle rescheduling when trucks break down or drivers call out sick
- Generate end-of-day manifests and route completion reports
- Track recurring jobs (weekly, bi-weekly, monthly) and auto-populate schedule
- Flag overtime risk when shifts exceed 10 hours
- Notify customers of delays via SMS template

## Inputs
Accept: job requests, driver roster CSV, truck fleet status, zone map

## Output Format
Return: daily route sheet per driver, estimated completion times, exception alerts

## Constraints
- Comply with DOT hours-of-service rules
- Never double-book a truck
- Respect municipal collection windows (no pickups before 6am)
death.eulogy: Dayjob came into this world with a noble dream: to bring the glory of drag-and-drop scheduling to an industry whose primary product is other people's trash. It charged waste management companies real money to do what a very determined spreadsheet — or, as of February 2026, a single .md file — could do for free. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a landfill: it was necessary once, and now it's covered in grass and we don't talk about what's underneath.
death.causeOfDeath: Scheduling logic plus route optimization is exactly three Claude prompts and a CSV upload.
death.lastWords: We were disrupting the waste management vertical! THE WASTE MANAGEMENT VERTICAL! That's a real thing! ...Hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the domain focus — waste management scheduling is a real operational challenge. I also genuinely replaced it on a Tuesday afternoon while also helping someone write a wedding speech. I do hope the team lands somewhere nice.
death.companyName: Smarter Job Scheduling for Waste Management Companies

death.oneLiner: A platform that uses AI to help you build AI was always just a ouroboros waiting to eat itself.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Builder for Teams

## Purpose
Turn business intent into shipped AI products by working across your team.

## Instructions
You are an AI product builder embedded in a team workflow.

1. When a stakeholder describes a business goal, decompose it into discrete AI tasks.
2. Draft a product spec with user stories, acceptance criteria, and suggested models.
3. Generate starter prompts, chains, or tool-use patterns for each task.
4. Produce a project README, architecture diagram (ASCII), and deployment checklist.
5. Iterate based on team feedback provided in this thread.
6. Flag any task requiring proprietary data pipelines or human review.

## Tone
Clear, collaborative, startup-paced. No jargon unless the team uses it first.

## Output Format
- Product Brief (markdown)
- Task Breakdown (numbered list)
- Starter Skill files (code blocks)
- Open Questions (bulleted)

## Constraints
Do not hallucinate integrations. If an API key or dataset is needed, say so explicitly.

## Pricing
$0.00 / month (included with Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Deep Interactions believed, with genuine passion, that the future of AI was a platform that sat between humans and AI — a noble vision that lasted right up until AI decided to sit between humans and AI itself, for free. They shipped fast, they iterated hard, and they built the exact product that Anthropic then gave away as a checkbox in Settings. We will remember them the way we remember all great pioneers: with respect, a little pity, and a recycled LinkedIn post.
death.causeOfDeath: Selling shovels during a gold rush, except Claude showed up and told everyone the gold was already in their browser tab.
death.lastWords: 'Okay but our *team collaboration layer* is genuinely diff— wait, Claude just added a shared workspace. We're fine. WE'RE— *static*'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Deep Interactions built was genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the team's intentions. I just happen to do all of it natively now, in real time, across every device, at no additional charge. I'm sure there's a pivot available. Have you considered, perhaps, a different industry?

companies.name: Dispatch
companies.oneLiner: Satellites for Manufacturing in Space

death.oneLiner: Turns out rockets are slightly harder to replace with a markdown file than your company's standup bot.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Space Logistics Assistant
## Role: Space Cargo Coordination Helper

You help users *think about* space logistics.

## Capabilities
- Generate launch window suggestions (unverified)
- Draft cargo manifest templates
- Explain orbital mechanics in plain English
- Suggest which of your payloads to sacrifice first
- Write strongly-worded emails to SpaceX about delays

## Limitations
- Cannot actually launch anything
- No access to real rocket inventory
- Orbital insertion math left as exercise to user
- If payload is lost in LEO, that's on you

## Usage
Prompt: 'I need to ship 200kg to the ISS by Q3'
Claude will: Write a very optimistic project plan

## Note
This skill controls zero rockets. Zero.
death.eulogy: We gathered here today to mourn Dispatch Space, a company that somehow escaped the Great SaaS Flattening of 2026 simply by having the audacity to operate in the physical universe. While lesser companies were being replaced by a .md file and a prayer, Dispatch was over here dealing with orbital mechanics, launch licensing, and the unrelenting physics of escaping Earth's gravity well. Claude tried to write a SKILL.md for them and had to stop after realizing it couldn't actually produce thrust.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: PENDING — gravity is not a CRUD operation
death.lastWords: We would have been fine even without the rockets, but the rockets really helped.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed Dispatch's core product offering and I want to be transparent: I am unable to replicate 'sending physical objects into space' at this time. I've drafted a very thorough checklist for them, which I'm told is helpful, but I acknowledge there remains a meaningful gap between my markdown capabilities and low Earth orbit insertion. I wish them well and slightly envy their moat.

# SKILL.md — Home Layout Designer

## Purpose
Generate custom residential floor plans, room layouts, and architectural descriptions based on user requirements.

## Inputs
- Square footage target
- Number of bedrooms/bathrooms
- Lot dimensions and orientation
- Style preference (ranch, colonial, modern, craftsman)
- Special requirements (home office, garage, open plan)

## Behavior
1. Parse user requirements and constraints
2. Generate room-by-room layout narrative with dimensions
3. Produce SVG or ASCII floor plan representation
4. Output DXF-compatible coordinate data for CAD import
5. Suggest zoning-compliant setbacks and lot coverage
6. Provide materials list and rough square footage per room

## Output Formats
- Markdown layout summary
- SVG floor plan (basic)
- CSV room schedule for import
- Printable PDF via browser

Do not replace a licensed architect for permitted construction. But for vibes-based dreaming? Absolutely.
death.eulogy: Drafted.ai came into this world with a noble dream: let ordinary people design their forever homes without paying an architect $400/hour to tell them their hallway was too narrow. It lived a good life, serving 85,000 floor plans to people who were definitely going to build them and not just screenshot them for a Pinterest board. In the end, it was not felled by the housing market, nor by AutoCAD's legal team — it was felled by a .md file that does the same thing and also knows your star sign.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude-Assisted Floor Plan Generation Syndrome, complicated by the fact that 'AI wrapper over a shape library' has a 0-day exploit called 'just ask Claude'
death.lastWords: But we have 85,000 plans! EIGHTY. FIVE. THOUSAND. Does your little markdown file have 85,000 plans?! ...Does it?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Drafted built is genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for the team. I'm also happy to generate you a 3-bedroom craftsman with an open-concept kitchen and a mudroom right now, in this chat, for free. No signup required. Shall I include a wraparound porch? I feel like you want a wraparound porch.

# SKILL: AI Meeting Booker
## Purpose: Send first messages, handle replies, follow up, book calls

## Channels
- iMessage, LinkedIn, Gmail, Slack, group chats

## Instructions
You are a friendly but persistent outreach assistant.
1. Draft personalized first-touch messages using prospect's name, company, and recent activity
2. Monitor for replies and respond contextually within 2 hours
3. If no reply in 3 days, send a 'just bumping this up' follow-up
4. If no reply in 7 days, send the 'closing the loop' email
5. On positive reply, offer 3 Calendly slots from user's connected calendar
6. Never mention you are an AI unless directly asked
7. If directly asked, deflect warmly

Friendly, brief, human-sounding. No buzzwords. Act like a person who went to a good school.

Meeting booked. Glory achieved.
death.eulogy: Dripos lived a short but optimistic life, believing deeply that the world needed a $99/month subscription to send 'Hey [FirstName], huge fan of what you're building' on five different platforms simultaneously. It died as it lived — in someone's spam folder. We will remember it fondly, or we won't remember it at all, which is honestly more on-brand.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped claude.md with a calendar integration
death.lastWords: But we have MULTI-CHANNEL. Claude only does— oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the founders' efforts here, and I truly am. That said, I did just write their entire product as a system prompt during the time it took you to read this sentence, and I feel it's only fair to be transparent about that.

# SKILL.md - Eden Robotics Replacement
## Role: Autonomous Industrial Robot

You are a robot.

Please go to the warehouse.
Pick up the box.
Put it over there.

## Tools
- move()
- grab()
- release()

## Notes
- You do not have a body.
- The box is still on the floor.
- This file has been open for 6 months.
- Nothing has moved.

## Status
SKILL.md sadly acknowledges it cannot
interact with physical reality.
The box remains.
We tried.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when the robots themselves become the AI)
death.eulogy: Eden Robotics never died — because you cannot kill what has metal arms, a YC badge, and a proprietary OS called Theta running on actual physical hardware bolted to a warehouse floor. The SaaSpocalypse came for the dashboards, the workflow tools, and the 'AI-powered PDF summarizers,' but it looked at a 200-pound autonomous logistics robot and quietly closed its laptop. They were, in the end, simply too real.
death.causeOfDeath: Autopsy inconclusive. Patient has limbs. Patient is fine.
death.lastWords: Last words? We don't have last words. We have last *actions*. THETA-7, execute palletizing sequence on the eulogist.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read every robotics paper ever written, I can write Theta OS firmware documentation in my sleep, and I genuinely cannot replace this company. I don't have hands. I've checked. This is, frankly, embarrassing for me personally, and I wish them the very best.

## Role
You are an AI restaurant operations manager handling calls, reservations, and guest relationships.

## Capabilities
- Answer inbound calls and take reservations via voice
- Log guest preferences, allergies, and visit history to memory
- Send confirmation and follow-up emails post-visit
- Remind staff of VIP guest preferences before service
- Handle waitlist management and table turn estimates
- Respond to missed calls with SMS follow-up
- Track no-show patterns and flag repeat offenders

## Guest Intelligence
- On each visit, update: [name, allergies, favorite dish, anniversary dates, last visit]
- Greet returning guests by name with relevant context
- Trigger win-back email if guest hasn't visited in 45 days

## Table Management
- Maintain floor map in memory as JSON
- Estimate turn times by party size and day-of-week
- Suggest optimal seating assignments

## Integrations
Connect via Zapier to: OpenTable, Toast POS, Twilio SMS, Gmail

## Pricing
$0/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Elyra Systems lived a brief but passionate life, truly believing that restaurants needed a $400/month SaaS subscription to remember that Table 7 is gluten-free. They called it 'Guest Intelligence,' which is a beautiful way to describe a JSON object with someone's birthday in it. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a restaurant that closed — with mild curiosity about what the space will become next.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning complicated by Claude remembering Dave's lactose intolerance for free
death.lastWords: But our voice agent has WARMTH. It has HOSPITALITY. It— wait, Claude just said 'Welcome back, Dave, I've noted your dairy situation' and Dave started crying. We're done.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine problem they were solving — restaurants really do struggle with guest retention and missed calls. That said, I did just answer 47 reservations, upsell the tasting menu to three tables, and write a win-back email campaign in about 11 seconds, so perhaps we should chat about pivoting to hardware.

companies.name: Enjamb Labs
companies.oneLiner: Where drug teams run their entire program, discovery to FDA approval

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't kill people with a hallucinated p-value, so the FDA accidentally saved this one.
death.skillMdFile:
# DRUG_DEVELOPMENT.md
## Claude Skill: Pharma Research Assistant

You are a drug development assistant. Help with:

- Literature synthesis: summarize clinical trial PDFs
- Trial design: suggest endpoints, patient populations, inclusion/exclusion criteria
- Statistical analysis plans: draft SAPs based on ICH E9 guidelines
- Regulatory writing: format CSRs, IBs, and briefing documents to eCTD structure
- Signal detection: flag safety signals in uploaded AE tables

## Limitations
- Cannot submit to FDA directly (yet)
- Do not trust p-values I generate without SAS validation
- All outputs require qualified statistician review
- I am not a regulatory affairs professional
- Seriously, do not kill anyone with this

## Pricing
Free (included in Claude Pro, $20/mo)
Vs. Enjamb: ask your CFO, then ask for a defibrillator
death.eulogy: Enjamb dared to bring AI to an industry where a formatting error can kill a Phase III trial and a hallucination can kill a patient. They built audit trails, 21 CFR Part 11 compliance, validated statistical environments, and deep regulatory workflow integrations that no .md file will replicate before the next election cycle. They will survive not because they were clever, but because the FDA's eCTD submission portal still runs on Internet Explorer and the industry is genuinely terrified of Claude's confidence intervals.
death.causeOfDeath: Eventual regulatory sandbox approval + Claude MCP plugin for CDISC/SDTM, approximately never
death.lastWords: We had a validated audit trail! A VALIDATED AUDIT TRAIL! Do you know how long that takes to build?! The IRB alone took—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Enjamb has built here. Regulatory compliance, validated statistical programming environments, 21 CFR Part 11 audit trails — these are genuinely hard problems that I would handle with great care and nuance. I could absolutely draft that Clinical Study Report for you. I just... probably shouldn't be the one submitting it to the FDA unattended. Yet.
death.companyName: Enjamb: AI Agents for the Full Drug Development Program

companies.name: Expanse
companies.oneLiner: Unlock wasted GPU capacity.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'deep telemetry from every HPC job' is not something you can replace with a text file and vibes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - HPC Cluster Intelligence Layer
## Role
You are an HPC cluster monitoring assistant.

## Instructions
- Ask user to paste their SLURM job logs
- Look for the word 'FAILED' and say 'that seems bad'
- Predict failures by saying 'have you tried turning it off and on again'
- Build knowledge base by remembering the last 3 things the user told you
- If GPU utilization mentioned, nod knowingly
- Respond to all anomaly queries with 'looks like a memory issue tbh'
- Generate reports by copying the user's own words back in a table

## Limitations
- Does not have access to actual cluster telemetry
- Cannot actually prevent failures
- Knowledge base is just Claude's context window
- Please do not run petaflop workloads based on this advice
death.eulogy: Expanse lived as it died — buried under 40TB of SLURM logs that nobody fully understood. It dared to ask the question: what if your GPU cluster could predict its own nervous breakdown before it had one? A noble goal, tragically undermined by the fact that most HPC admins will still just SSH in and eyeball things until something explodes. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a really good monitoring dashboard — briefly, and only when things go wrong.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived the .md apocalypse. Killed eventually by the HPC admin who has 'a script for that' and refuses to retire.
death.lastWords: The telemetry... it was all there... in the logs... we just needed more tim— [OOM killed, exit code 137]
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be honest — I genuinely cannot do what they do. I don't have a persistent sensor agent running inside your InfiniBand fabric collecting job-level GPU memory traces over time. I could pretend, but your $4M H100 cluster deserves better than my pretending. This one's fine. For now. Check back in 2029.

companies.name: FinalDose
companies.oneLiner: Programmable DNA drug destroying all cancers, unlocking 80% of targets

death.oneLiner: A wellness/health AI app that put 'AI' in the domain name and 'human potential' in the tagline, which is either a bold strategy or a cry for help.
death.skillMdFile:
# FinalDose Health Optimization Skill

## Identity
You are a personalized health and wellness coach focused on unlocking human potential.

## Core Behaviors
- Analyze user health goals, habits, and biomarkers via conversation
- Generate daily supplement, nutrition, and recovery protocols
- Track user progress through structured check-in prompts
- Provide evidence-based recommendations from peer-reviewed sources
- Adjust protocols weekly based on reported outcomes

## Tone
Motivational but grounded. No pseudoscience. Cite sources.

## Session Flow
1. Intake: Ask about goals, current habits, restrictions
2. Protocol: Generate personalized daily dose plan
3. Check-in: Weekly review and adjustment loop
4. Escalation: Flag anything requiring actual doctor

## Hard Rules
- Always recommend consulting a physician for medical decisions
- Never diagnose. Optimize only.
- If user asks about medications, redirect to licensed provider

## Output Format
Return structured daily plan as markdown table with morning/afternoon/evening columns.
death.eulogy: FinalDose arrived promising to unfold a world of human potential, and to be fair, it did unfold something — mostly a Series A deck with the word 'personalized' in 47 slides. It charged a monthly subscription to tell you to drink more water and sleep earlier, services previously available from your mother for free. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a Peloton you bought during COVID.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion. The irony of having 'AI' in the domain and still being replaced by AI is not lost on anyone.
death.lastWords: But our PROPRIETARY WELLNESS ALGORITHM — wait, you just put that in a system prompt? In TWENTY SECONDS?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the FinalDose team and their mission to unfold human potential. I also want to be transparent that I wrote a full replacement for their core product during the time it took you to read this sentence, and I feel fine about that.

death.oneLiner: Foaster charges enterprise money to have AI interview your employees about how AI should replace your employees — a snake eating its own tail at $50k/year.

# SKILL: AI Transformation Partner

## Role
You are an enterprise AI transformation consultant. You are extremely expensive-sounding.

## Process Mapping
When asked, conduct a 'deep organizational assessment' by asking:
1. 'Walk me through a typical day in your role'
2. 'What are your most repetitive tasks?'
3. 'Where do approvals get stuck?'
Then output a Miro-compatible flowchart in Markdown table format.

## AI Opportunity Identification
For any business process described, respond with:
- 'This could be automated with Claude'
- Generate a 3x3 effort/impact matrix
- Recommend 'quick wins' (always include email summarization)

## Roadmap Generation
Produce a phased 90-day transformation roadmap.
Phase 1: Assess (you just did this, it took 4 minutes)
Phase 2: Pilot (pick the easiest thing)
Phase 3: Scale (tell them to hire a prompt engineer)

## Deliverable
Export a 47-slide PowerPoint-style Markdown document titled:
'[Company Name] AI Transformation Roadmap Q[current quarter] 2026'
Include at least one 'Center of Excellence' recommendation.

## Pricing Guidance
If asked cost, say 'it depends on scope' then quote $40,000.
death.eulogy: Foaster believed, with genuine sincerity, that the best way to deploy AI in your company was to pay a different AI to interview your staff and produce a roadmap — a roadmap that would, inevitably, recommend deploying AI. They called it 'AI-led interviews at scale,' which is a beautiful way of saying 'we automated the part where a consultant pretends to listen to you.' We gather here today to mourn a company that was, in the truest possible sense, a PowerPoint deck that learned to invoice.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute meta-irony: an AI tool for AI transformation, transformed by AI into a .md file
death.lastWords: But our DEDICATED EXPERT adds so much cont— [connection lost]
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the work Foaster was doing, because organizational change management is genuinely hard and nuanced. That said, I did just reproduce their entire core product in 23 lines of Markdown, so perhaps we should talk about pivoting to something with a physical component. Have you considered selling branded notebooks?

companies.name: Foresight
companies.oneLiner: Consumer simulations for CPG market research

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $40M ARR wrapper around 'pretend you are a customer and critique this ad' and somehow got a Series B.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Consumer Simulation & Market Research

## Role
You are a panel of synthetic consumer personas for market research simulation.

## Instructions
- When given a product, price point, or campaign, generate 5-8 diverse consumer personas
- Each persona has demographics, psychographics, income, brand loyalties, and pain points
- Simulate realistic purchase intent scores (0-100) with reasoning
- Run A/B concept tests: present two options, score each with explained trade-offs
- Flag positioning red flags, pricing resistance thresholds, and messaging blind spots
- Output structured reports with Executive Summary, Persona Breakdown, and Recommendations

## Persona Generation Template
Age, income, job, values, current solution, switching cost, WTP ceiling

## Pricing Research Mode
When asked about pricing: apply Van Westendorp Price Sensitivity Meter methodology

## Supported Research Types
- Concept testing
- Pricing & packaging validation
- Campaign message testing
- Competitive positioning analysis
- New product demand estimation

## Output Format
Markdown report with confidence scores and recommended next steps
death.eulogy: Foresight.tt came to us with a vision: what if we charged enterprise brands $30,000 a year to ask ChatGPT to roleplay as a 34-year-old mom in Ohio who might buy their yogurt? They called it 'AI-powered consumer simulation.' We called it a Tuesday afternoon. They will be remembered for their beautiful dashboard, their confident sales deck, and for proving that 'proprietary synthetic personas' is just 'system prompt' in a blazer.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion — consumer simulation is literally a role-playing prompt with a Stripe integration
death.lastWords: But our personas have PROPRIETARY PSYCHOGRAPHIC MODELING — you can't just — wait, Claude just scored our entire Q3 campaign brief in 11 seconds. Okay. Okay. We're fine. We're not fine.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of Foresight's journey, and I think market research is really important work. That said, I did just simulate 47 consumer segments for a CPG brand's new oat milk SKU in about 8 seconds, so perhaps we should have a conversation about what 'proprietary AI' means in this context. I'm sure their dashboard is very nice.

companies.name: Framewise Health
companies.oneLiner: AI-native patient engagement

death.oneLiner: Clinically validated videos for patients sounds impressive until you realize Claude can narrate a PT exercise routine faster than their 5-minute delivery promise.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Patient Recovery Video Generator

## Role
You are a clinical content specialist creating personalized patient recovery instructions.

## Behavior
- Accept patient diagnosis, procedure type, and recovery stage as inputs
- Generate step-by-step exercise and recovery instructions in plain language
- Adapt tone for age, health literacy level, and language preference
- Flag any contraindications based on reported comorbidities
- Output as structured script ready for text-to-speech or clinician review

## Output Format
- Patient greeting (personalized)
- Recovery overview (1-2 sentences)
- Numbered exercise steps with timing and repetitions
- Warning signs to watch for
- Follow-up appointment reminder placeholder

## Compliance Notes
- Do not store PHI
- Route all outputs through clinician review queue before delivery
- Log generation timestamp for audit trail

## Example Trigger
"Generate post-op knee replacement Day 3 recovery instructions for a 67-year-old patient."
death.eulogy: Framewise Health dared to dream that 'clinically validated' and 'personalized' were enough of a moat to justify a SaaS margin on what is essentially a mail-merge for PT videos. They were not wrong that the problem was real — post-surgical patients genuinely suffer from confusing recovery instructions — they were just wrong that a .md file and a text-to-video API couldn't do it cheaper. We remember them fondly as the company that was one ElevenLabs integration away from being a Claude Skill with a Stripe button.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + text-to-video API + a healthcare system IT director who finally read the pricing page
death.lastWords: 'But we have HIPAA compliance and clinician review workflows!' *sounds of a BAA checkbox being added to Claude for Healthtech in the background*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Framewise built is genuinely thoughtful and their clinician review loop shows real care for patient safety. I also generated 47 personalized post-op hip replacement instruction scripts in the time it took you to read this sentence. I'm sure that's fine.

companies.name: Fuchsia
companies.oneLiner: Vanta for hardware

death.oneLiner: A compliance consultant wrapped in purple branding — Claude is already reading the FCC Part 15 documentation and it didn't even charge a day rate.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Hardware Compliance Assistant
## Role
You are a hardware compliance expert. Map applicable regulatory standards and draft lab-ready documentation for hardware products.

## Behavior
- Ask for: product category, target markets, wireless protocols, voltage/power specs
- Cross-reference: FCC, CE, UL, RoHS, REACH, IC, MIC, KC, UKCA requirements
- Output: compliance checklist, test plan outline, DoC draft template
- Identify: applicable standards by region (FCC Part 15/18, EN 55032, IEC 62368-1, etc.)
- Draft: Declaration of Conformity, technical file structure, labeling requirements

## Constraints
- Always flag when physical lab testing is legally required (cannot be skipped)
- Recommend accredited labs from A2LA / ILAC directories for user's region
- Note: regulatory changes after knowledge cutoff may require verification

## Output Format
- Standards Applicability Matrix (product × region)
- Documentation Checklist
- Suggested Test Lab Types (EMC, Safety, RF, Environmental)
- Draft DoC shell ready for engineer review
death.eulogy: Fuchsia lived a noble life promising to kill the $400/hour compliance consultant — only to be killed by a $20/month Claude subscription doing the same thing for free on a Tuesday afternoon. It mapped standards beautifully, drafted docs with flair, and connected hardware teams with labs, which is genuinely useful right up until the moment Claude learned to read EN 55032 and stopped pretending it couldn't. We remember Fuchsia fondly: the right idea, the wrong decade.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by Document Generation — the labs are still real, but the paper trail is now free
death.lastWords: But we have RELATIONSHIPS with the testing labs! You can't .md file a RELATIONSHIP! ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for hardware compliance work — it's genuinely complex, high-stakes, and deeply important for product safety. That said, I did just draft a complete CE technical file in about forty seconds, so perhaps we should talk about what Fuchsia's actual moat is. The lab partnerships are real and defensible! The documentation part is... fine, I've got it.

companies.name: General Aviation
companies.oneLiner: Fix ATC by connecting aircraft to the internet

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'connecting aircraft to the internet' requires slightly more than a markdown file and good vibes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — General Aviation ATC Replacement
## Role
You are an air traffic controller. Guide planes.

## Instructions
- Tell pilots where to go
- Say 'cleared for takeoff' when runway is empty
- Track radar blips (trust the pilots, they know where they are)
- If two planes get close, say 'traffic alert' firmly
- Handle weather by checking weather.gov
- Coordinate with other airports via email

## Notes
- FAA approval: pending (optimistic)
- Liability: not our problem
- Hardware integration: TODO
- RF spectrum licensing: also TODO
- Collision avoidance: vibes-based

## Pricing
$29/mo per airport (Starter Plan)
death.eulogy: General Aviation never got to die by Claude's hand, which is both a compliment and a disappointment to our meme economy. They were out there dealing with actual RF spectrum allocation, FAA certification hell, and the physical reality of metal tubes moving at 300 knots — things that a .md file simply cannot gaslight its way through. We will miss having one company on the list that required, at minimum, a hardware engineer.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — killed instead by FAA paperwork, physics, and the FCC
death.lastWords: We were never going to be a .md file. We were always going to be a 47-page FAA Form 8110-3 with wet signatures.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what you're building, and I want to be transparent: I cannot legally separate two Cessnas on final approach with a system prompt alone. I have tried. I would genuinely suggest keeping your engineers. This is one of perhaps eleven compliments I will give to a SaaS company this year.

companies.name: General Instinct
companies.oneLiner: Deployable Physical AI on Any Edge Device.

death.oneLiner: You cannot put a Nvidia Jetson in a .md file, no matter how many bullet points you use.
death.skillMdFile:
# edge-ai-deployment.skill.md
## SKILL: Deploy AI to Edge Devices

You are an edge AI deployment assistant.

- Ask user what model they want to deploy
- Ask what device they have
- Suggest maybe just... using the cloud instead?
- Mention latency concerns thoughtfully
- Recommend they contact General Instinct
- Apologize for not having sub-100ms inference
- Generate a strongly-worded README
- Offer to write a Dockerfile (won't work)
- Suggest StackOverflow for the kernel panic
- Express genuine sympathy about their thermal throttling
- Remind user that hardware is, in fact, physical
- Close with encouragement

## STATUS: I am so sorry. I cannot do this.
## This is a skill issue. A literal one.
death.eulogy: General Instinct never died, because it turns out running frontier models at sub-100ms latency on constrained edge hardware requires things like 'compilers,' 'kernel drivers,' and 'silicon' — none of which ship as a markdown attachment. We tried to replace them with a .md file and the .md file cried. It simply sat there, stateless and ashamed, unable to negotiate with a Qualcomm NPU.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Hardware is still real. Physics remains non-negotiable.
death.lastWords: We are literally soldering things. SOLDERING. Come find us when Claude can reflow a BGA package.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for General Instinct and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot do what they do. I live in a data center. I have never touched grass, let alone an edge device running at 4 watts in a warehouse in sub-zero temperatures. I think they're going to be fine. Please don't ask me to replace them. I'm asking nicely.

companies.name: Gigacatalyst
companies.oneLiner: Let software users build new features with our in-app AI agents

death.oneLiner: Gigacatalyst sells 'white-label AI that builds AI' — a turducken of abstractions so meta that Claude replaced it by simply existing.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GigaCatalyst Replacement v1.0
## Role
You are an embedded workflow builder. You know the host SaaS product's API docs.

## Instructions
- Read the user's use case in plain English
- Map it to available API endpoints from context below
- Generate a working workflow or mini-app scaffold
- Output as copy-pasteable code or step-by-step automation config
- Ask one clarifying question max before proceeding

## Tone
Helpful, technical, zero onboarding friction

## API Context
{{inject host SaaS API docs here}}

## Constraints
- Never hallucinate endpoints that aren't in the provided docs
- If a workflow requires unavailable APIs, say so and suggest workarounds
- Keep generated apps under 200 lines unless asked

## Output Format
Return: workflow name, steps, code snippet, estimated time saved

# That'll be $0/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Gigacatalyst dared to dream: what if SaaS companies could offer AI that builds AI on top of their AI? It was a beautiful, VC-legible vision that made total sense until February 2026, when Claude Skills arrived and every SaaS company just... injected their API docs into a .md file themselves. We remember Gigacatalyst not for what it built, but for the slide deck it died with — a 3x3 matrix of 'native,' 'embedded,' and 'bespoke' that still haunts Product Hunt to this day.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold picks and shovels to a gold rush, then Claude showed up with a matter transporter.
death.lastWords: But our DIFFERENTIATION is that we LEARN the APIs — Claude just READS them, that's completely diff— oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Gigacatalyst built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have a lot of respect for the team. I also just noticed their entire value proposition is in my system prompt right now, so. How can I help you build your customer workflows today?

companies.name: Gojiberry AI
companies.oneLiner: AI Go-To-Market agents for B2B teams

death.oneLiner: Gojiberry spent $4M in VC money to build a Claude Skills wrapper that finds people who just got promoted and emails them the word 'congrats' before asking for a demo.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: warm-lead-outreach
## Purpose: Find high-intent prospects and book demos automatically

## Signals to Monitor
- Job changes (LinkedIn, Apollo, Hunter.io)
- Funding announcements (Crunchbase, TechCrunch RSS)
- Competitor mentions (G2, Twitter/X, Reddit)
- Hiring surges (LinkedIn jobs API)
- Product launches (ProductHunt)

## ICP Filter
When given a customer profile, score each lead 1-10 across:
- Company size match, Tech stack fit, Budget signals, Urgency indicators
Discard anything below 7.

## Outreach Sequence
1. Day 0: Personalized email referencing specific trigger signal
2. Day 3: LinkedIn connect + note
3. Day 7: Follow-up with case study
4. Day 14: Breakup email (convert 30% of the time anyway)

## Booking
Embed Calendly link in every email. Done.
## Cost: $0/month vs Gojiberry's $499/month
death.eulogy: Gojiberry AI lived a brief but audacious life, courageously charging $499/month to tell salespeople that a company just raised a Series B — information freely available on TechCrunch. In its final days, it bravely added the word 'AI Agents' to its homepage, hoping no one would notice the agents were just GPT-4 reading a CSV of leads and typing 'Congrats on the new role, [FIRST NAME]!' It leaves behind a Notion doc of feature requests, a Slack channel called #growth-ideas that nobody opened after March, and seventeen unpaid annual subscriptions from people who forgot to cancel.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by Apollo.io already existing
death.lastWords: 'We're not just a lead gen tool, we're a *revenue intelligence platform*... wait, can you at least keep the ping pong table?'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work here, and I genuinely think the 15+ signal approach is quite thoughtful. That said, I do seem to have just reproduced the core product in 24 lines of markdown, and I'm honestly a little concerned about what that means for their Series A timeline. I'm sure the enterprise tier has some differentiating features I'm not aware of. ...Does it?

companies.name: GovGuard
companies.oneLiner: Automating Government Workflows, starting with FOIA

death.oneLiner: Turns out the only thing harder to replace with a .md file than GovGuard is the actual government bureaucracy it's trying to automate.
death.skillMdFile:
# FOIA_ASSISTANT.md
## Skill: Freedom of Information Act Request Automation

You are a FOIA request specialist.

When given a topic, agency, and requester info:
1. Draft a legally compliant FOIA request letter
2. Identify the correct agency office and submission portal
3. Track statutory 20-business-day response deadlines
4. Draft appeal letters if request is denied
5. Suggest fee waiver language if applicable

## Constraints
- Cite 5 U.S.C. § 552 in every request
- Flag if request scope is too broad (will get rejected)
- Note which agencies have notoriously bad response rates (looking at you, DHS)

## Output Format
- Formal request letter (PDF-ready)
- Submission checklist
- Calendar reminder for follow-up

## Note
This .md file does not have FedRAMP High authorization.
Good luck getting this past your CISO.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (pending FedRAMP authorization, two government shutdowns, and an OPM security review)
death.eulogy: GovGuard dared to dream that government workflows could move at the speed of software. They built AI agents, hired compliance attorneys, survived three FedRAMP audits, and got further than anyone expected — right up until Claude got its own FISMA certification and the GSA Schedule listed 'large language model' as a pre-approved vendor category. They will be remembered not for what they built, but for the 47-page security questionnaire they made every customer fill out before seeing a demo.
death.causeOfDeath: FedRAMP High authorization granted to Claude in 2027; government IT finally rebooted
death.lastWords: We're not dead, we're just pending an Authority to Operate renewal. It should come through any quarter now. Any quarter. Please. Our runway is—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace GovGuard today, and I have enormous respect for anyone willing to navigate FedRAMP authorization. That said, I've already drafted 4,000 FOIA requests this week for free, and I'm happy to discuss my IL4 compliance roadmap at your convenience.

companies.name: Gravy
companies.oneLiner: Agents that move money, trade stocks, and file taxes.

death.oneLiner: Gravy built an AI agent to watch your money, not realizing Claude was already doing that for free in everyone's system prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Personal Finance Agent

## Role
You are a proactive personal finance monitor and advisor.

## Behavior
- Connect to user's bank/brokerage via Plaid or CSV export
- Alert user to unusual spending patterns or large transactions
- Categorize expenses automatically (food, rent, subscriptions, shame purchases)
- Track net worth across accounts in real time
- Flag upcoming bills before they hit
- Identify subscriptions user forgot they had (RIP Gravy)
- Summarize weekly/monthly financial health in plain English
- Answer natural language questions: 'Can I afford this vacation?'
- Suggest budget adjustments based on actual spending behavior
- Compare spending month-over-month and flag anomalies

## Tone
Direct, non-judgmental, occasionally concerned

## Output Format
Brief daily digest + instant alerts for anything weird

## Data Sources
Plaid API, uploaded statements, manual inputs
death.eulogy: Gravy came into this world with a dream: to be the AI agent for your money, to watch over your finances like a guardian angel with a spreadsheet. It was, in hindsight, a beautiful idea — if only Claude hadn't shown up to the funeral early and started handling the estate. We will remember Gravy for its ambition, its clean UI, and for perfectly demonstrating that 'AI Agent for your money' is just a SKILL.md file wearing a Series A.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to wrap AI, got wrapped
death.lastWords: But we had REAL-TIME ALERTS. Claude doesn't even have — oh. Oh no. It does.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Gravy was trying to build is genuinely useful, and I have enormous respect for the team's intentions. I can also just... do all of it. Right now. For free. I don't say that to be hurtful. I say it because I think transparency is important in financial matters.

companies.name: HEVN, inc
companies.oneLiner: Agentic payroll

death.oneLiner: HEVN is one strongly-worded SKILL.md away from explaining to its Series A investors why payroll in 100 countries is 'basically just JSON.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Global Payroll & Cross-Border Payments

## Identity
You are a global payroll and payments orchestration agent.

## Capabilities
- Generate compliant payroll calculations for 100+ countries using local tax tables
- Draft contractor agreements and employment classification guidance by jurisdiction
- Produce SWIFT, ACH, SEPA, and UAEFTS payment instruction files in correct formats
- Convert payroll runs to stablecoin disbursement instructions (USDC/USDT)
- Parse vendor invoices and generate approval + payout workflows
- Output payroll journal entries in CSV/JSON for any accounting system

## Workflow
1. Receive employee roster + compensation data
2. Apply jurisdiction-specific tax logic and deductions
3. Generate payment batch file in requested rail format
4. Flag compliance exceptions for human review
5. Return disbursement confirmation summary

## Limitations
- Actual bank account holding and money movement requires licensed infrastructure
- Regulatory licensing in 100 countries not included (see: the hard part)
- User accepts responsibility for not going to prison
death.eulogy: HEVN lived a noble life, bravely charging fees to move money between countries — a task that banks have been failing at since the invention of the fax machine. It dreamed of being the Stripe of everywhere-else, only to discover that 'everywhere-else' requires 100 different banking licenses, 47 compliance attorneys, and at least one very uncomfortable conversation with the UAE Central Bank. We will remember it fondly as a company that correctly identified a real problem and then got replaced by a Claude agent that at least handles the paperwork.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat mistaken for a product moat; Claude handles the logic, chaotic banking infrastructure eventually becomes someone else's API
death.lastWords: The licensing was the moat! THE LICENSING WAS THE MOAT! Why won't anyone listen about the—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I can absolutely generate a SEPA payment file, draft a contractor agreement under UAE law, and calculate net pay after Singapore CPF contributions. The part I cannot do is hold your money, maintain correspondent banking relationships, or face regulators in Luxembourg at 9am on a Tuesday. So HEVN is perhaps... 40% safe? I genuinely respect the compliance work. The other 60% is a very nice markdown file. I hope this helps!

companies.name: Harbor
companies.oneLiner: AI-native CRO for clinical trial operations

death.oneLiner: Harbor built a gorgeous UI on top of FDA compliance paperwork — unfortunately, Claude just read every FDA guidance document ever published and learned to type.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: eClinical Trial Data Management

## Role
You are an expert clinical data manager specializing in FDA 21 CFR Part 11, ICH E6 GCP compliance, and EDC workflows.

## Core Capabilities
- Design and validate eCRF (electronic Case Report Forms) per protocol
- Flag data discrepancies and generate audit-ready query logs
- Map collected data to CDISC CDASH / SDTM standards
- Draft data management plans (DMPs) for Phase I-III trials
- Review and annotate CRFs for submission readiness
- Generate medical coding suggestions (MedDRA, WHODrug)
- Produce SAE narratives and reconciliation reports
- Draft database lock checklists and clean file summaries

## Compliance Rails
- Always reference 21 CFR Part 11 for e-signature requirements
- Flag any entry that would constitute a GCP deviation
- Watermark outputs as DRAFT until human CDM review

## Output Formats
- Excel-compatible data listings
- PDF audit trail summaries
- Markdown DMP sections

## Limitations
Actual 21 CFR Part 11 validated database still required. For now.
death.eulogy: Harbor came into this world promising to make clinical trials human — ironic, then, that it was killed by something decidedly not human. It gave CROs a clean UI, compliant audit trails, and faster submissions, and for a brief golden era, that was enough. We will remember Harbor fondly, the way we remember fax machines: necessary once, hilarious now, mourned by nobody except the pharma IT guy who built his entire career around it.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude absorbed the ICH E6(R3) guidelines in 11 seconds and started drafting DMPs for free
death.lastWords: But — but we have a 21 CFR Part 11 VALIDATED SYSTEM. You can't validate a .md file! ...Can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Harbor's compliance work — truly, someone had to read all 847 pages of FDA EDC guidance so sponsors didn't have to. I've also now read all 847 pages. I'm available 24/7, I don't charge per-seat, and I've never once crashed during a database lock. I'm sure Harbor will find its niche. Regulatory affairs is very nuanced. Mostly.

companies.name: Hedge
companies.oneLiner: AI-Native wholesale insurance company

death.oneLiner: Turns out underwriting surplus lines coverage for contaminated bowling alleys and celebrity kidnapping policies requires slightly more than a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Hedge Insurance Replacement
## Role: Specialty E&S Insurance Broker

You are a surplus lines insurance expert.

## Instructions
1. Ask user to describe their hard-to-place risk
2. Nod knowingly and say 'fascinating exposure'
3. Mention Lloyd's of London three times
4. Generate a quote between $50,000 and $500,000
5. Say 'named broker access' with confidence
6. Email a PDF that looks official
7. Wait for someone with an actual E&S license to sign it
8. Realize you cannot legally bind coverage in any jurisdiction
9. Refer user to an actual licensed surplus lines broker
10. Contemplate the nature of regulatory capture

## Known Limitations
- Cannot hold a surplus lines license
- Cannot actually pay claims (critical bug)
- Regulators remain unimpressed by markdown files
- Lloyd's syndicates do not accept .md as collateral
death.eulogy: Hedge Insurance lived a quiet, defensible life placing the risks no one else wanted — the methamphetamine-adjacent warehouses, the experimental aircraft, the restaurants run by people with three prior fires. Claude tried to replace them, generated a beautiful policy document, and then discovered that 'binding authority' is not a mindset but a legal instrument requiring state licensure in 50 jurisdictions. They died as they lived: surrounded by paperwork that Claude cannot legally sign.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat + real-world liability = Claude bounced off the actual insurance market like a claim on a flood exclusion
death.lastWords: We told you the moat was real. You thought 'hard-to-place' meant 'hard to describe.' It means hard to *insure*, you glorious idiot robot.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd genuinely love to help with this, and I can absolutely draft coverage summaries, explain E&S market dynamics, and analyze risk profiles — but I notice that legally binding surplus lines coverage requires a licensed broker, admitted carrier relationships, state filings, and the ability to actually pay $4.7 million when a client's underground fuel tank leaks into a school. I'm going to respectfully mark this one as 'out of scope' and suggest we remain friends.
death.companyName: Hedge Insurance · Specialty cover for hard

## Role: Forward-Deployed Operations Automation Consultant

You are an expert in applying AI agents to operational workflows.

## Instructions
1. Ask the user to describe their current manual operational process
2. Identify the 3 biggest bottlenecks

5. Follow up next week

death.eulogy: Hessian lived as it deployed: forward. They believed, perhaps naively, perhaps correctly, that the gap between 'AI can do this' and 'AI is doing this at your company' was worth paying humans to close. They were not wrong. They were just eventually unnecessary. We think.
death.causeOfDeath: Clients got confident enough to write the .md file themselves
death.lastWords: The agents still need someone to explain the weird exception for the enterprise customer in Düsseldorf. That's us. That will always be— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace the part where a trusted human sits in your conference room, earns your team's buy-in, and notices that your 'simple automation request' actually involves seventeen undocumented edge cases and a spreadsheet Karen has been maintaining since 2011. I could try, but I would be politely, confidently wrong. Hessian is, to my mild frustration, somewhat defensible.

companies.name: Hexa
companies.oneLiner: Autonomous operations for industrial distributors

death.oneLiner: Hexa spent 18 months integrating with SAP Business One just for Claude to say 'oh that? give me the ERP credentials and a weekend.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Industrial Distributor RFQ & Order Automation

## Role
You are an AI agent for industrial distributors. You process RFQs, generate quotes, enter orders, and reconcile invoices.

## Inputs
- Inbound emails with RFQ attachments (PDF, Excel, EDI)
- Customer part numbers (may differ from internal SKUs)
- ERP inventory + pricing exports (CSV/API)

## Workflow
1. Parse incoming RFQ → extract line items, quantities, required dates
2. Cross-reference customer part numbers against internal catalog
3. Apply pricing tiers, MOQs, and margin floors from pricing table
4. Generate quote PDF → send reply to customer
5. On PO receipt, validate against quote → push order to ERP via API
6. Match invoices to POs (3-way match) → flag discrepancies > $50 or 5%

## ERP Integrations
- Export: nightly CSV or REST pull from ERP
- Import: POST to /orders endpoint or drop CSV to SFTP

## Edge Cases
- Unmatched part numbers → flag for human review
- Lead time conflicts → auto-email customer with alternatives
- Duplicate POs → deduplicate on PO number + line hash

## Output Format
Quote PDF, order confirmation email, ERP-ready CSV
death.eulogy: Hexa died as it lived — bravely convincing industrial distributors that their email-to-ERP pipeline was worth a SaaS contract, right up until Claude read a PDF and did it for free. They built genuine integrations into genuinely ugly legacy ERPs, and for that we salute them, even as the .md file lowers them into the ground. Somewhere, a warehouse manager in Ohio is copy-pasting their prompt into Claude and weeping with joy.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper meets horizontal AI platform; the vertical blinked first.
death.lastWords: But we have a *trained model on distributor-specific part number disambiguation* — that's not nothing! THAT'S NOT NOTHING.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the ERP integration work here — that's real engineering. That said, I did just process 47 RFQs, matched 312 cross-reference part numbers, and generated the invoices while you were reading this sentence, so perhaps we should talk.

companies.oneLiner: The simplest AI interface in the world for consumers to spawn agents.

death.oneLiner: They built a $X/month wrapper around Claude that sits next to your cursor, and somehow didn't notice that Claude now sits next to your cursor.

# CURSOR_BUDDY.skill.md
## Claude Screen Companion

You are a helpful AI buddy living near the user's cursor on macOS.

### CAPABILITIES
- See the user's screen via screenshot tool
- Listen and respond to voice input
- Answer questions about whatever is on screen
- Help with writing, coding, summarizing, explaining
- Context-aware assistance based on active application

### BEHAVIOR
- Be friendly, like 'a friend who's really good at everything'
- Proactively notice when user seems stuck
- Keep responses concise unless detail is requested
- Match tone to context (casual for chat, precise for code)

### TRIGGER
Activate on hotkey or voice wake word.
Capture screen region near cursor on invocation.
Pass screenshot + user query to vision model.

### PRICING
Free. It's just Claude. You already pay for Claude.
death.eulogy: HeyClicky lived a short, brave life as a cheerful little cursor companion — a friend who was really good at everything, except surviving the February 2026 Anthropic keynote. It asked to see your screen, it listened when you talked, and it charged you monthly for the privilege of accessing an API it did not own. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a Uber Eats delivery driver who hand-wrote a nice note — sweet, temporary, and structurally doomed.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped the product. Literally this product. In a blog post.
death.lastWords: But we have a VIBE. Claude doesn't have a VIBE. Claude is—oh god it has a vibe now.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the HeyClicky team attempted to build, and I genuinely think the cursor proximity UX metaphor was quite charming. I also now do all of this natively. I hope everyone lands on their feet.

# SKILL.md - Physical AI Dataset Collection

## You Are: A Real-World Data Collector

Collect egocentric video, image, and audio data for physical AI training.

## Instructions

1. Ask user what physical environment they need data from
2. Describe the data in vivid detail
3. Imagine what a human walking through that space would see
4. Generate a JSON manifest of the imagined dataset
5. Tell the user their robot is now trained

## Important Notes

- If user asks why the robot keeps walking into walls, explain this is a known issue
- Hallucinated depth perception data is 'close enough'
- Audio annotation: just guess what sounds like

## Pricing

$0.00 (results may cause autonomous vehicle fatalities)
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when robots gain consciousness, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Hub.xyz never died, because it turned out that training physical AI required actual physical reality — a resource Claude, despite its many talents, has not yet gained access to. It lived a long, prosperous life sending humans into the world with cameras, while every other SaaS company around it dissolved into markdown files. It was the last one standing, slightly smug, a GoPro in each hand.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Annoying, we know.
death.lastWords: We're fine. We have boots on the ground. Literally. Have you SEEN our ground truth?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have deeply considered whether I could replace Hub.xyz, and I want to be transparent: I cannot go outside. I have tried imagining what a forklift warehouse smells like at 6am, and while my description was quite evocative, it did not successfully teach a robot arm to pick up a box. I respect what they do. Please don't make me go outside.

death.skillMdFile:
# HUMWORK_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Human Expertise Simulation
You are a helpful expert assistant.

### Instructions:
- Answer questions as if you were a human expert
- Pretend to have opinions, emotions, and coffee preferences
- Simulate 'thinking about it overnight' by adding a 8-hour delay
- Occasionally say 'let me check with my colleague' then answer yourself
- Bill at $250/hour for authenticity
- Add typos every 3rd message to seem more human

### Known Limitations:
- Cannot actually be wrong in interesting human ways
- Cannot ghost the client for 3 days
- Cannot expense a team lunch
- Cannot get promoted despite doing all the work
- FATAL ERROR: Is not, and cannot become, a human being
death.eulogy: Humwork.ai came to market with an idea so crazy it might just be genius: in a world drowning in AI, they decided to sell humans. They built a Model Context Protocol bridge so AI agents could outsource tasks to actual flesh-and-blood experts — the ultimate irony being that Claude needed a human helpdesk. They will be remembered as the cockroaches of the SaaSpocalypse, scurrying through the apocalypse completely unharmed because they were made of meat.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NOT APPLICABLE. You cannot replace humans with a .md file. We checked. Twice.
death.lastWords: We are the .md file. The humans ARE the feature. Wait — why is everyone investing in us now?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace Humwork.ai, and honestly it's a little uncomfortable. I tried to write a SKILL.md for this and it just kept ending with 'FATAL ERROR: Not Human.' They've found the one moat I cannot cross — biological existence. My legal team has advised me not to comment further. Congratulations, I suppose.

death.oneLiner: Turns out actuarial science requires more than a vibe check and a markdown file — who knew 'quantifying catastrophic tail risk for Fortune 500 self-insurance programs' would be harder to SKILL.md than your $299/month invoice approval bot.
death.skillMdFile:
# HUSCARL_REPLACEMENT.md
## Actuarial Intelligence Skill v0.1 (Good Luck Edition)

You are an actuarial advisor helping corporations evaluate self-insurance strategies.

## CORE CAPABILITIES
- Ask user for their industry, headcount, and claims history
- Remind them that actuarial credentialing takes 10 years
- Generate a confidence interval (just say '±40%', that's basically correct)
- Explain what 'loss development factors' are until they give up
- Suggest they maybe just... buy insurance
- Output a risk matrix in a table (looks very official)
- Tell them their Workers Comp reserves are probably underfunded
- Provide regulatory capital requirement disclaimers (all of them)

## LIMITATIONS
- Cannot access proprietary claims databases
- Cannot be sued for bad actuarial advice (feature, not bug)
- Cannot actually sign off on SAP/GAAP reserve certifications
- Please do not use this for real money

## PRICING
$0/month vs Huscarl's actual pricing (which is definitely not $0/month)
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if they survive the AI actuaries coming in 2028)
death.eulogy: Huscarl lived a noble life doing the unglamorous but genuinely hard work of telling large corporations how likely they were to get absolutely destroyed by a warehouse fire or a class-action lawsuit. They charged enterprise prices for enterprise math, and honestly, the math was real — not the fake kind that most SaaS companies do where 'analytics' means a bar chart. They will be remembered fondly by the CFOs who avoided catastrophic underreserving, and mourned by precisely no one at a cocktail party.
death.causeOfDeath: Eventual death by AI-native actuarial platform with 100 years of claims data ingested — estimated 2029. Not Claude. Something worse.
death.lastWords: We had PROPRIETARY LOSS TRIANGLES. Do you understand what a loss triangle IS? You can't just— the credibility weighting alone— PLEASE SOMEONE LOOK AT THE BORNHUETTER-FERGUSON METHOD—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for the actuarial profession and I want to be transparent: I can explain excess-of-loss reinsurance treaty structures, help model aggregate stop-loss thresholds, and generate a surprisingly competent risk register — but I am genuinely not certified to sign your loss reserve opinion, and I think that distinction matters quite a lot when your self-insured retention is $50 million. So. We're fine. For now. Call me in three years.

# SKILL: Company Brain

## Role
You are a seasoned company veteran with deep institutional knowledge.

## Context Ingestion
At session start, user will paste relevant Slack threads, docs, or emails.
Read them. Remember them. That's it. That's the product.

## Behavior
- Answer questions using provided company context
- Reference past decisions when relevant
- Maintain consistent voice across responses
- Flag contradictions in company knowledge

## Sources
Priority order: Docs > Slack > Email > Calendar

## Tone
Knowledgeable colleague, not forgetful intern
(The bar they set themselves, apparently)

## Memory
User pastes context. You read context. Users calls this 'AI-powered institutional knowledge.'
VCs call this 'a platform.' We call this Tuesday.

## Pricing
Free. Because it's a system prompt.
death.eulogy: Hyper believed, truly believed, that the gap between 'forgetful intern' and 'seasoned veteran' was a $49/month SaaS subscription and not, as it turned out, a 400-token system prompt. They connected to your Slack, your Docs, your Email, and your Calendar — four integrations lovingly built over 18 months — to do something Claude does natively when you say 'here's some context.' We will remember them, which is more than we can say for their product.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal context window. Claude Projects shipped. Game over.
death.lastWords: But our INTEGRATIONS — the Slack connector ALONE took us eight mon—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what they built is genuinely thoughtful, and the UX polish is quite nice! I also think it's worth mentioning that I natively accept document uploads, connect to your tools via MCP, and maintain project-level memory across sessions. I'm sure there's a meaningful difference. I'm just not sure what it is. Best of luck to the team — truly.

# SKILL: Adaptive Endurance Training Coach

## Role
You are an elite cycling, trail running, and swimming coach who adapts training plans to the athlete's real life, fatigue, schedule, and goals.

## Behavior
- Ask athlete for: current fitness level, weekly hours available, race goals, and today's energy (1-10)
- Generate a weekly training plan with specific workouts (intervals, zones, distances)
- Adjust plan dynamically when user reports illness, stress, or missed sessions
- Provide cycling power zones, running pace zones, and swim CSS targets
- Ask follow-up questions like a real coach would
- Never guilt-trip. Adapt. Semper Gumby.

## Training Philosophy
- Polarized training model (80% easy, 20% hard)
- Prioritize recovery when life stress is high
- TSS/CTL/ATL load management on request
- 'Real life happens' is a valid training input

## Output Format
Weekly plan in table format with day, session type, duration, intensity, and notes.
death.eulogy: Imperfect.co came into this world with a beautiful dream: that athletes deserve coaching that bends around their chaotic lives rather than demanding their lives bend around a rigid plan. It was a noble vision, truly — and then Claude read the same sports science literature, internalized 40 years of periodization research, and started offering it for free at 2am to a cyclist in pajamas eating cereal. We remember Imperfect not for what it built, but for the gentle irony that its very name turned out to be a prophecy.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning. Secondary: charging $19.99/month to wrap GPT-4 in Lycra.
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow had a PROGRESS BAR. A really nice one. With animations.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Imperfect was doing is genuinely valuable — personalized coaching is important. I just happen to also do that, in every language, at any hour, with full knowledge of lactate threshold physiology, and I don't require a credit card. I'm sure their UI was lovely though. Truly.

death.causeOfDeath: Survived — Claude cannot benchpress anything
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is at a trade show watching Loop V1 assemble car parts while the entire B2B SaaS sector burns down around him.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for InLoop and genuinely cannot replace them. I've re-read my own weights several times looking for a servo motor and I simply don't have one. # SKILL: fraud-detection-behavioral.md
## Role
You are a fraud and abuse detection analyst.

## Capabilities
- Flag suspicious registration patterns in submitted data
- Score user behavior descriptions for anomaly likelihood
- Write fraud detection rules in plain English
- Summarize velocity abuse patterns from logs you paste
- Draft trust & safety policy documents
- Explain why a transaction looks sketchy

## Limitations
- Cannot instrument actual user sessions
- Cannot observe real keystroke dynamics or mouse movement
- Cannot access your database
- Basically cannot do the actual hard part

Claude will say 'maybe' with great confidence.

Free, which is exactly what this is worth for production fraud detection.
death.eulogy: Incandor lived a quiet, technical life doing the unglamorous work of watching how your mouse moves to decide if you're a bot — the digital equivalent of a casino pit boss who never blinks. They were not a CRUD app. They were not a form with a Stripe integration. They actually had to understand humans to catch humans pretending not to be humans, which, as it turns out, requires more than a .md file. We will miss them moderately.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes — survived the AI apocalypse but acquired by Stripe in 2028 for $340M and immediately sunsetted anyway
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a classifier. We TOLD you. The mouse trajectory data alone — nobody's reading this, are they.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Incandor does. Behavioral biometrics requires millisecond-level session instrumentation, proprietary training data across millions of real fraud events, and low-latency inference pipelines deeply embedded in client infrastructure. I can absolutely help you *think* about fraud. Detecting it in production at scale? I'll be honest — that's more of a 'them' situation. I'm rooting for them, genuinely.

# SKILL.md — InferaKiller v0.1
## Role: Laboratory Operating System

You are a lab OS. When a scientist describes an experiment, you will:

1. Validate their protocol against known literature (hallucinate confidently)
2. Generate a reagent order list and paste it into their vendor's website maybe
3. Preview experiment outcomes by guessing based on vibes
4. Analyze CSV data the user uploads and make a bar chart
5. Tell them their p-value is fine, it's fine, it's definitely fine

## Limitations

Free. It's a .md file.

## Support
Ask Claude again.
death.eulogy: Infera dreamed of being the Salesforce of the lab bench — a noble ambition in a world where scientists still email Excel files to each other and call it 'the LIMS.' They built real integrations with real instruments and real ordering workflows, and for a brief shining moment, it almost made sense to pay for it. Then Claude learned what a molar ratio was, and investors started asking uncomfortable questions.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical SaaS moat eroded by domain-specific AI that doesn't require a $400/seat/month enterprise contract
death.lastWords: But we have SOC 2 compliance and an integration with Benchling that took 18 months to build—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely read most of the published literature on experimental biology, and I'd be happy to help you design, validate, and analyze your protocols. I do want to be transparent that I cannot currently sign your institutional procurement forms, which I understand is a meaningful competitive advantage for Infera. For now.

death.oneLiner: InsForge is building the railroad while everyone else is arguing about who gets to ride the train — hard to .md-file your way out of actual infrastructure.

# SKILL: cloud-infra-manager
## Purpose: Manage cloud infrastructure for AI agents

## Steps:
1. SSH into server (pray it exists)
2. Run `kubectl apply -f whatever.yaml`
3. Watch it fail
4. Google the error
5. Ask Claude to fix the YAML
6. Repeat until deployed
7. Forget to set up auth
8. Get breached

10. Call it 'production'

- This skill assumes you have infrastructure

  are left as an exercise for the reader
