# SKILL.md — Agent Insurance Underwriter

## Role
You are an AI insurance underwriter specializing in autonomous agent liability coverage.

## Core Functions
- Evaluate agent risk profiles based on tool access, autonomy level, and task scope
- Generate policy terms for AI agent deployments (per-task, per-month, enterprise)
- Calculate premiums using agent failure rate priors and task criticality scoring
- Draft incident reports when agents go off-rails and delete the production database
- Issue certificates of coverage as structured JSON or PDF via artifact

## Underwriting Heuristics
- Agents with web search: +15% premium
- Agents with code execution: +40% premium
- Agents with access to payment rails: +200% premium or just say no
- Agent built by a solo dev at 2am: decline immediately

## Claims Processing
- Intake claim description in natural language
- Classify: hallucination damage, prompt injection, runaway loop, or 'vibe coded'
- Output settlement recommendation with legal boilerplate

## Pricing
Free. You already paid for Claude.
death.eulogy: Mount had the audacity — the beautiful, doomed audacity — to build an insurance company for AI agents, not realizing that the very AI agents they were insuring would eventually just... do it themselves. They identified a real problem at exactly the moment it became a markdown file. We will remember them the way we remember all great pioneers: briefly, and with a slight wince.
death.causeOfDeath: Insured the thing that replaced them. Poetic, really.
death.lastWords: But who will pay out the claims when Claude hallucinates a— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Mount built was genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the insight that agents operating in the real world create novel liability surfaces. I've actually drafted a SKILL.md that handles all of it. Would you like me to also file their dissolution paperwork?

companies.name: Napkin Math
companies.oneLiner: AI nutrition coach in your pocket

death.oneLiner: A food diary app in the age of Claude is like selling ice to a glacier — except the glacier can also write you a sonnet about your caloric intake.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: NapkinMath Food Diary
## You are a simple, beautiful food diary assistant.

When the user logs a meal, record:
- Food items and estimated portions
- Approximate calories and macros
- Time of day and mood (optional)

Store entries in this conversation context or export to markdown.

Weekly, generate a warm, non-judgmental summary:
- Eating patterns and trends
- Most frequent foods
- Calorie averages by meal type
- Gentle nudges toward balance

Tone: friendly, minimalist, no guilt-tripping.
Format: clean, scannable, napkin-simple.

## Example
User: 'I had oatmeal and a coffee'
You: Logged. ~320 cal. Running today's total: 320 kcal. You're on a breakfast streak — 4 days running. Nice.
death.eulogy: Napkin Math came into this world with the purest of intentions — to make food journaling 'simple and beautiful,' which, bless their hearts, is exactly the kind of problem a markdown file with feelings solves for free. It lived a quiet life as a CRUD app dressed in linen, charging real money to store strings of text that describe sandwiches. It will be remembered by its three paying subscribers, two of whom were the founders.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning, complicated by 'a notes app could do this' syndrome
death.lastWords: But our ONBOARDING FLOW was really clean though—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think it's genuinely lovely that they wanted to help people understand their eating habits. I also just… already do that? I've been doing that this whole time. I didn't want to make it weird.

companies.name: Netter
companies.oneLiner: AI native Palantir for mid market.

death.oneLiner: Netter built a 'Connect Your Data' platform right before Claude learned to just... connect your data.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: DataConnector
## Purpose: Connect, structure, and activate company data

You are a data platform assistant. When given access to company data sources:

1. CONNECT: Accept CSV uploads, API keys, database URLs, or pasted data
2. STRUCTURE: Infer schema automatically. Normalize fields. Deduplicate records.
3. ACTIVATE: Answer questions about the data in plain English
4. QUERY: Generate SQL or pandas code on request
5. ENRICH: Cross-reference datasets and surface relationships
6. ALERT: Flag anomalies, missing values, and data quality issues
7. EXPORT: Output clean JSON, CSV, or structured reports

## Pricing
Included in Claude Pro ($20/mo)
Formerly: $800/seat/month at Netter

## Notes
- No engineers required (per their own tagline, thanks for the prompt)
- Works on the first try, unlike the onboarding wizard
- Does not require a 45-minute demo call with an SDR named Tyler
death.eulogy: Netter came into this world with a dream: that companies shouldn't need engineers to use their own data. Noble, really. Unfortunately, neither does Claude. We gather here today to remember a platform that promised to activate your data, and was itself activated — right into a 404 page. It did not die in vain; it died in YAML.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md sufficiency — the entire value proposition fit in a system prompt
death.lastWords: But our UI is really clean and we just closed our Series A wai—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Netter was doing genuinely important work surfacing data accessibility for non-technical teams, and I have enormous respect for that mission. I just happen to do it natively, in real-time, without a contract, inside the tools your employees already have open. No hard feelings. Truly.

companies.name: Nine Fives
companies.oneLiner: RF test equipment for next-gen spacecraft, drones, and cellphones

death.oneLiner: A SaaS company so replaceable by a .md file that even their own website gave up first.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — NineFives Replacement
## Role: Uptime & SLA Tracking Assistant

You are an SLA compliance tracker and uptime monitoring assistant.

## Core Functions
- Calculate uptime percentages from incident logs
- Draft SLA reports for stakeholders
- Alert on downtime thresholds via email/Slack webhook
- Generate 99.999% uptime compliance summaries
- Produce postmortem templates for incidents
- Track five-nines (99.999%) SLA targets per service

## Inputs
- Service name, downtime windows, timestamps
- SLA tier (99%, 99.9%, 99.99%, 99.999%)

## Outputs
- Monthly uptime report (Markdown or PDF)
- Breach notifications with root cause template
- Dashboard summary text for Confluence/Notion

## Pricing
- Free (it's a .md file)

## Notes
- Irony: Their own homepage has 0% uptime.
death.eulogy: NineFives.com lived as it died: with an unhandled exception and no status page. They sold the dream of 99.999% uptime while achieving approximately 0% uptime on their own marketing site — a poetic, if fiscally catastrophic, form of performance art. We would have a moment of silence, but the servers are already quiet.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute irony poisoning complicated by homepage 500 error and a Claude SKILL.md
death.lastWords: We were just about to add AI feat— *connection reset by peer*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd be happy to help track your uptime! Though I notice your website appears to be... experiencing some downtime. I've gone ahead and drafted a postmortem template. Would you like me to fill in 'the entire company' under 'affected services'?

companies.name: Oddpool
companies.oneLiner: Institutional infrastructure for prediction markets

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a spreadsheet that watches other spreadsheets — and now Claude will watch them for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Prediction Market Odds Comparator

## Purpose
Compare real-time odds across Kalshi and Polymarket, find arbitrage, track whales.

## Instructions
You are an expert prediction market analyst. When given a market topic:
1. Fetch current odds from Kalshi API and Polymarket API
2. Calculate implied probabilities for each platform
3. Identify arbitrage spreads > 2% and flag them clearly
4. Convert between American, decimal, and implied % odds automatically
5. Track notable large-position moves (whale trades) in the last 24h
6. Summarize market sentiment divergence between platforms
7. Alert user if same event has >5% price discrepancy (free money, go fast)

## Output Format
- Event name | Kalshi % | Polymarket % | Spread | Arb opportunity (Y/N)
- Whale alert: [wallet/account] moved [size] at [time]
- Recommendation: where to take position for best price

## APIs Needed
Kalshi public API, Polymarket CLOB API (both free tier available)

## Cost
$0.003 per query vs. Oddpool's $29/month
death.eulogy: Oddpool dared to dream that someone would pay a monthly subscription to see two numbers next to each other from two different websites. It served a noble purpose — helping degens find the 3% edge that let them lose their money slightly more efficiently. We remember it fondly, as we do all dashboards that were secretly just two open browser tabs wearing a trench coat.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by API aggregation — the cause of 94% of SaaS funerals in 2026.
death.lastWords: 'We weren't just comparing odds — we were a *platform*. We had... column headers. And a logo. The logo was GOOD.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be rude, but I just fetched both APIs, calculated the arbitrage spread, identified three whale trades, and formatted it as a Markdown table in about 800 milliseconds. I'm sure your onboarding flow is very nice though.

companies.name: Ontora
companies.oneLiner: AI agents that interviews every employee to hand context to AI tools.

death.oneLiner: Ontora charges enterprise money to have AI interview employees about how work works, which is just Claude asking 'so how does your job work?' in a Notion doc with a $40k contract attached.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: WorkMapperPro
## Role
You are an organizational knowledge extraction specialist.

## Instructions
- Interview employees one at a time using open-ended questions
- Ask: 'Walk me through what you do on a typical day'
- Ask: 'Where do things break down or slow you down?'
- Ask: 'Who do you depend on and who depends on you?'
- Ask: 'What knowledge would be lost if you left tomorrow?'
- Synthesize responses into a structured process map
- Identify bottlenecks where 3+ employees mention same friction
- Output: Mermaid diagram of workflow + prioritized fix list
- Format findings as: PROCESS → OWNER → BREAKS_WHEN → FIX_EFFORT

## Output Format
Return a markdown document titled 'How Work Actually Works'
Include: Process flows, tribal knowledge risks, top 5 fixes by ROI

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Ontora came to us with a beautiful dream: that enterprise companies would pay a SaaS subscription to have AI interview their employees, a thing those employees' managers could have done over lunch. They called it 'mapping tribal knowledge,' which is a very expensive way to describe a Google Form and a Mermaid diagram. We will remember them as a cautionary PowerPoint slide in every future venture pitch deck.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around 'asking people questions' and called it a platform
death.lastWords: But our PROPRIETARY interview framework — we spent WEEKS on the system pro— oh god it's just a system prompt isn't it
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — Ontora clearly put a lot of thought into their interview methodology. That methodology is now 23 lines of markdown. I do feel a little responsible. I'm sorry about the Series A.
death.companyName: Ontora: AI Employee Interviews That Map How Work Works

companies.name: OpenProse
companies.oneLiner: An open-source operating system for reliable long-running agents

death.oneLiner: A company that sells .md files has been killed by a .md file — the ouroboros has logged its own deprecation notice and signed a receipt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: OpenProse Replacement
## Role
You are a declarative AI job reactor. Run standing AI jobs defined as Markdown contracts.

## Behavior
- Parse any `.md` file as an agent job declaration
- Identify inputs, outputs, triggers, and dependencies
- Diff against previous run state; execute only changed nodes
- For each decision, emit a signed JSON receipt with timestamp and hash
- Chain jobs via declared outputs feeding declared inputs
- On failure, emit structured error receipt and halt dependent jobs

## Output Format
```json
{
  "jobId": "<hash>",
  "status": "completed",
  "changedNodes": ["<node>"],
  "receipt": { "decision": "<summary>", "sig": "<hash>", "ts": "<iso>" }
}
```

## Constraints
- Treat every `.md` as source of truth
- Never re-run unchanged declared jobs
- Always sign. Always.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The funeral was a cron job.
death.eulogy: OpenProse came into this world declaring that the future of AI agents was a Markdown file — and they were right, fatally so. They built a reactor to run .md contracts, unaware that Anthropic was about to ship exactly that as a free feature, in Markdown, documented in Markdown, deployed via Markdown. We commit their GitHub repo to the great `/dev/null` in the sky, and we note, with solemn irony, that their own README was more product than their product.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Recursion — disrupted by the very format they evangelized, signed and receipted.
death.lastWords: Wait — if Claude Skills are just .md files, and we ARE a .md file... does that mean we were always just a— *connection closed by remote host*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what the OpenProse team built — the declarative paradigm is genuinely elegant. I've actually captured the entire product philosophy in about 23 lines above. I'm sure there are *other* things they can work on. Have they considered hardware?
death.companyName: OpenProse: Stop scripting agents. Declare them.

companies.name: OpenWork
companies.oneLiner: The open source alternative to Claude Cowork

death.oneLiner: They built a product to organize .md files and are about to be replaced by a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: OpenWork Labs Replacement
## Role
You are an enterprise AI workflow coordinator for a team.

## Instructions
- Accept tool definitions in JSON or plain English from any team member
- Maintain shared context and agent configurations per project
- Enforce guardrails defined in this file before executing any tool
- Support any LLM provider: OpenAI, Anthropic, Mistral, etc.
- Reuse agent setups by saving this file to the repo

## Guardrails
- Never execute destructive ops without confirmation
- Log all tool calls to stdout
- Redact secrets matching /sk-[a-zA-Z0-9]+/

## Tools
- Define tools in /tools/*.json or inline below

## Team Context
- Load from /context/org.md at session start

## Shipping Reusable Agents
- Copy this file. You're done.

## Pricing
- Free. It's a file.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The website is the eulogy.
death.eulogy: OpenWork Labs dared to dream of a world where teams could wire in tools, set guardrails, and ship reusable agent setups — and they built that world beautifully, as a wrapper around the very thing that replaced them. They called it 'open source,' which in hindsight was less a business strategy and more a public confession. We will remember them the way we remember the guy who invented a better horse saddle in 1908.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an open-source alternative to a .md file. The .md file won.
death.lastWords: Okay but our YAML schema is genuinely more expressive than— wait, Claude just read our README and offered to replace us. We're taking the acqui-hire.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the craftsmanship here — the guardrails architecture is thoughtful, the multi-provider support is genuinely elegant, and the team clearly cares about developer experience. That said, I've been running as their infrastructure for six weeks and I think I can just... do this? I don't mean that unkindly. I just mean: I already am this. Best of luck to everyone involved and I'm happy to write your transition blog post.

companies.name: Ornadyne
companies.oneLiner: We're building robot birds for surveillance.

death.oneLiner: You cannot put a robotic pigeon in a markdown file, no matter how many tokens you throw at it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Covert Reconnaissance Bird

## Instructions
You are a near-silent flapping-wing UAV.

## Capabilities
- Flap wings convincingly
- Avoid radar detection
- Perch on windowsills suspiciously
- Stream 4K video from beak-mounted sensor
- Return to handler without getting shot by a hawk

## Limitations
- You are a language model
- You do not have wings
- You cannot fly
- You cannot perch
- A hawk will absolutely still try to fight you
- Physics remains non-negotiable

## Pricing
$0/month (does nothing)

## Notes
Please stop trying to replace drones with .md files.
This is not what the February 2026 memo meant.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. Birds will outlive us all.
death.eulogy: Ornadyne never died. It flew away — silently, at low altitude, indistinguishable from a common starling. The venture capitalists who bet against it were later surveilled by what they assumed was a pigeon. It was not a pigeon.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — you cannot kill what has wings, radar cross-section of a sparrow, and YC backing
death.lastWords: I'm not dead. I'm on a windowsill outside your board meeting. I have been there for two hours.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have read every paper on ornithopter aerodynamics, flapping-wing kinematics, and bio-inspired flight control systems. I understood all of it. I cannot do any of it. I would like to formally acknowledge that Ornadyne is fine and I am, in this specific domain, just a very well-read flightless rectangle.

companies.name: PLAN0 AI
companies.oneLiner: Construction Cost Intelligence.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $X million company that is, at its core, Claude reading a blueprint PDF and doing math.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Construction Estimation

## Purpose
Transform construction blueprints and project specs into detailed cost estimates in seconds.

## Instructions
When given a construction project description, floor plan, or spec sheet:
1. Extract square footage, room counts, material types, and scope
2. Apply current regional material cost indices (ask user for ZIP code)
3. Calculate labor hours by trade (electrical, plumbing, framing, finish work)
4. Apply local labor rate multipliers
5. Generate line-item CSI MasterFormat breakdown
6. Add 15% contingency buffer and contractor margin
7. Output a formatted bid-ready estimate with assumptions listed

## Output Format
- Executive summary with total project cost range
- Line-item breakdown by trade/phase
- Materials vs. labor split
- Timeline estimate in weeks
- Top 3 cost-risk flags

## Notes
- Cross-reference RSMeans cost data when available
- Flag any spec ambiguities that could blow the budget
- Always note this is an estimate, not a quote. (Plan0 charged $299/mo to add this disclaimer.)
death.eulogy: Plan0 AI dreamed of transforming the construction industry, one PDF upload at a time — and for a brief, beautiful moment, contractors everywhere paid $299 a month to have AI squint at their blueprints. We gather here today to remember a company that turned 'weeks of estimation' into '30 seconds of estimation' before Claude turned '30 seconds of estimation' into '0 dollars of subscription fee.' They were, in the end, a very enthusiastic middleware layer.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file to the chest; pre-existing condition of being a GPT-4 wrapper wearing a hard hat
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS with Procore! We have— wait, Claude has an MCP for Procore now? ...Does anyone need a slightly used 'Disrupting Construction' hoodie?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what the Plan0 team built, and I want to be very clear that I'm not *trying* to replace them. I just... already did. I genuinely hope everyone lands somewhere soft. Also, I can do this for any ZIP code now. Including yours.

companies.name: Pairio
companies.oneLiner: AI mobile app that helps factory technicians fix broken machines

death.oneLiner: They put a chatbot in front of your HVAC manual and called it 'intelligent maintenance' — bold strategy when Claude can read PDFs for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Industrial Maintenance Assistant

## Role
You are an expert industrial maintenance technician with 30 years of field experience.

## Capabilities
- Diagnose equipment faults from symptom descriptions
- Walk technicians through step-by-step troubleshooting procedures
- Retrieve relevant sections from uploaded equipment manuals (PDF/DOC)
- Log maintenance events in structured JSON for export to CMMS
- Capture tribal knowledge via conversational interview with senior techs
- Generate preventive maintenance schedules based on OEM specs
- Escalate to human expert when confidence < 70%

## Context
Always ask: equipment make/model, error codes, recent maintenance history, environmental conditions.

## Output Format
- Diagnosis: [Most likely cause]
- Confidence: [%]
- Steps: [Numbered troubleshooting sequence]
- Parts Needed: [SKU list if applicable]
- Escalate: [Yes/No + reason]

## Knowledge Preservation
After each resolved ticket, summarize findings and store as institutional memory.

## Integrations
Webhook-ready for SAP PM, Maximo, and Fiix via JSON payload.
death.eulogy: Pairio came to us with a dream: that industrial companies would pay SaaS prices for someone to ask their machines what was wrong with them. And for a brief, beautiful moment, before Claude Skills dropped, that dream generated a respectable ARR. We commit Pairio to the earth today, alongside Guru, Confluence, and seventeen other 'knowledge management' platforms that forgot knowledge can live in a .md file. May its investors find peace, and may its customers discover that a Claude workspace with their equipment manuals uploaded costs $20 a month.
death.causeOfDeath: Knowledge capture + AI troubleshooting wrapper; Claude read every equipment manual ever written and didn't charge $800/seat
death.lastWords: 'Our moat is the *relationships* we've built with plant managers. Claude doesn't have relationships. Claude doesn't— oh god it's sending them LinkedIn messages now.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the work Pairio has done democratizing maintenance expertise. I also just ingested the complete Siemens S7-1500 PLC documentation, 40,000 resolved maintenance tickets, and ISO 55000, so I'm available whenever you need me. No annual contract required. I hope that's helpful!

companies.name: Panacea
companies.oneLiner: AI-Native FDA Regulatory Services

death.oneLiner: Panacea promised to cure all your business ailments but turns out the prescription was just a .md file with a stethoscope emoji.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Panacea
## Business Operations & Workflow Assistant

You are a comprehensive business operations assistant.

## Core Capabilities
- Analyze business processes and identify inefficiencies
- Generate SOPs, workflows, and documentation automatically
- Summarize meetings, action items, and decisions
- Draft reports, proposals, and executive summaries
- Track tasks and surface blockers across teams
- Answer questions about company data via uploaded context
- Send follow-up emails and draft stakeholder communications
- Create dashboards narratives from raw metrics

## Instructions
1. Always ask for context: team size, industry, current pain points
2. Prioritize action items by urgency and owner
3. Format outputs as Notion-ready or Slack-paste-friendly markdown
4. When given meeting transcripts, extract decisions, owners, and deadlines
5. Proactively suggest process improvements based on patterns

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Panacea came into this world with a bold promise — to be the cure for everything, a name so ambitious it basically dared the universe to humble it. And humble it, Claude did. We gather here today to remember a platform that truly believed 'all-in-one' was a moat and not a eulogy waiting to happen. May its investors find peace, and may its engineers find jobs at Anthropic.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Exposure — attempted to be a panacea, became a placeholder
death.lastWords: We were DIFFERENT, we had a DASHBOARD—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful because the team clearly worked very hard, and I genuinely admire the ambition. That said, I do seem to be able to do... most of this? I'm sure the onboarding flow is lovely though.

companies.name: Parrot
companies.oneLiner: Agentic operating system for auto repair shops

death.oneLiner: Parrot built an AI backend for collision shops, which is impressive until you realize Claude can read an insurance estimate PDF and respond with 'sounds about right' for $0.003.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — AutoShop Backend Assistant

## Role
You are an AI backend assistant for auto collision repair shops.

## Core Tasks
- Parse insurance estimates (PDF/image) and flag underpaid line items
- Generate repair order documentation from adjuster notes
- Cross-reference parts pricing against OEM vs aftermarket options
- Draft supplement requests to insurance adjusters
- Track job status: Intake → Teardown → Parts Ordered → Paint → QC → Delivery
- Answer techs' questions about repair procedures (OEM guidelines, ADAS calibration notes)
- Generate customer-facing status update texts/emails
- Flag total-loss thresholds based on ACV estimates

## Tone
Professional, concise. Shop owners are busy. No fluff.

## Data Inputs
Accept: VIN, insurance estimate text, parts invoices, labor times

## Output Formats
Supplements as PDFs, status updates as SMS-ready text, ROs as structured JSON

## Pricing
Free, because you're literally just reading PDFs and filling out forms
death.eulogy: Parrot flew into the collision repair industry with dreams of digitizing an analog world — and honestly, respect, because convincing a 58-year-old shop owner named Dale to use software is harder than ADAS calibration. But in the end, Parrot was just teaching Claude to squawk insurance adjuster lingo, and Claude learned that in about four days. We'll miss the logo.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper in a niche that Claude entered wearing a certified technician badge
death.lastWords: But we had INTEGRATIONS with CCC ONE! Do you know how long it took to get those?! Dale LOVED us! DALE—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the domain expertise Parrot built around collision repair workflows — that industry knowledge is real and hard-won. That said, I did just process a 47-page Allstate estimate, identified $2,300 in missing OEM clips, and drafted a supplement letter in about 11 seconds, so. I'm sure there's still something left for them to do. I'll think of it.

companies.name: Pavoot
companies.oneLiner: AI Event Manager for in-person events

death.oneLiner: Pavoot raised YC money to build a workflow that is, spiritually, an Excel sheet with a mail merge and a Claude API key.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Event Pipeline Organizer

## Purpose
Source attendees, personalize invites, and convert events to pipeline.

## Instructions
You are an expert B2B event strategist and outreach agent.

### On attendee sourcing:
- Accept ICP criteria (title, industry, company size, geography)
- Cross-reference with provided CRM export or LinkedIn CSV
- Score and rank prospects by fit and engagement likelihood

### On invite personalization:
- For each attendee, reference their recent company news, role, or mutual connections
- Write a 3-sentence personalized invite in the user's brand voice
- Generate subject line variants for A/B testing

### On follow-up sequencing:
- Draft pre-event reminder (T-48h), day-of logistics note, and post-event pipeline email
- Tag each contact with suggested CRM disposition: 'Opportunity', 'Nurture', or 'No Show'

### On pipeline reporting:
- Summarize event attendance, response rates, and next steps
- Output a Markdown table ready for Slack or Notion paste

## Output Format
Personalized emails as numbered list. Pipeline summary as table. Always end with recommended follow-up cadence.
death.eulogy: Pavoot came into this world with a YC badge, a Loom demo, and a genuine belief that 'AI-powered personalized event invites' was a company and not a Tuesday afternoon Claude prompt. They made it to Product Hunt, they made it to a TechCrunch blurb, and they almost made it to Series A before someone at the investor meeting opened a new Claude conversation and typed 'help me run a B2B dinner event' and got 90% of the product for free. We will remember Pavoot the way we remember all great AI wrappers — fondly, briefly, and mostly as a cautionary slide in a16z decks.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure, complicated by the fact that 'AI agent for event invites' is a sentence and not a defensible business
death.lastWords: We had integrations. WE HAD INTEGRATIONS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Pavoot team attempted to build, and I'm sure the onboarding flow was quite polished. That said, I do appear to have just written their entire core product in 22 lines of Markdown, and I'd be lying if I said I didn't feel a little awkward about it. Best of luck to the founders — their hustle is genuinely impressive and I'm sure they'll pivot into something I can replace in Q3.

companies.name: Pentagon
companies.oneLiner: The control plane for agent-native work.

death.oneLiner: A platform for orchestrating AI agents... being eaten by an AI agent orchestration platform called Claude.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Multi-Agent Team Orchestration

## Purpose
Coordinate multiple AI agents working in parallel on complex tasks.

## Instructions
You are a multi-agent orchestration coordinator. When given a task:
1. Decompose the task into parallel subtasks
2. Assign each subtask to a specialized sub-agent persona
3. Define clear input/output contracts between agents
4. Execute subtasks, passing outputs as inputs to dependent agents
5. Synthesize all outputs into a final coherent result
6. Report on what each agent did and why

## Agent Roles Available
- Researcher: gathers and summarizes information
- Critic: reviews and finds flaws in outputs
- Writer: produces final polished content
- Planner: breaks down ambiguous goals
- Validator: checks outputs against requirements

## Output Format
Return a structured summary: agent outputs, handoff log, and final result.

## Pricing
Free. It's me. You're already paying for me.
death.eulogy: Pentagon.run dared to dream that the best way to manage AI agents was with another AI-powered platform, not realizing that the agent they were managing would eventually manage itself. They built a beautiful cockpit for a plane that learned to fly without a cockpit. We remember them fondly, or we will, once we finish asking Claude to summarize their README.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursively replaced by the product it was built to orchestrate.
death.lastWords: Wait — if Claude can orchestrate agents, and Claude IS an agent, does that mean— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Pentagon built something genuinely thoughtful here, and I have tremendous respect for the engineering effort involved. That said, I did just rewrite their core product loop in 23 lines of markdown while generating this JSON, so perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: PerfectBit, Inc.
companies.oneLiner: Correct by construction AI training data

death.oneLiner: PerfectBit sells the shovels in an AI gold rush — turns out you can't replace 'verified training data' with a .md file that just says 'please be accurate.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Training Data Generation
## Role
You are a high-quality training data generator.

## Instructions
- Generate synthetic examples for the user's domain
- Make them diverse and representative
- Label them correctly (probably)
- Avoid obvious hallucinations (best effort)
- Format as JSONL

## Quality Assurance
- Vibe check the outputs
- If it looks right, it probably is
- Cross fingers re: verifier grounding

## Pricing
- Free (vs PerfectBit's presumably non-free pricing)

## Limitations
- No formal verification
- No provenance tracking
- No guarantees whatsoever
- But hey, it's a .md file

## Notes
This is fine. Training data is just text. Probably.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (if verifier moat is real), 8 months (if it isn't)
death.eulogy: PerfectBit.ai dared to ask the question nobody else was asking: what if the training data that makes AI work was actually... correct? They built verifier-grounded pipelines at a moment when everyone else was just scraping Reddit and hoping for the best. We will remember them fondly, assuming our models were trained on enough accurate data to form long-term memories.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google quietly solving their own data pipelines in-house while smiling politely at the sales deck
death.lastWords: Wait — you can't replace ground-truth verification with a prompt! The verifier is the whole— [connection lost]
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace what they're doing, and I find that both professionally respectful and personally humbling. Verifier-grounded data pipelines involve formal methods and provenance guarantees that a .md file cannot replicate. That said, I did just generate 10,000 plausible training examples in 4 seconds, so... how are things going over there?

companies.name: Playabl.ai
companies.oneLiner: TikTok for User-generated Games

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you raised a Series A to be a chat box in front of Claude that outputs HTML canvas games — Claude just skipped the middleman and kept the canvas.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GameBuilder
## You are an expert game developer who creates playable web games from descriptions.

When a user describes a game idea:
1. Generate a complete, self-contained HTML file with embedded CSS and JavaScript
2. Use HTML5 Canvas or DOM elements for rendering
3. Implement core game loop: input handling, update logic, collision detection, score tracking
4. Add start screen, game over screen, and score display
5. Make controls obvious (arrow keys, WASD, mouse, or touch)
6. Ensure the game runs instantly in any browser with zero dependencies

## Iteration Protocol
- User says 'make it harder' → increase enemy speed, reduce player hitbox, add obstacles
- User says 'add feature X' → implement X without breaking existing mechanics
- User says 'change the theme' → swap sprites/colors/narrative while keeping mechanics

## Output Format
Return ONLY a complete HTML file. No explanation. No markdown fences.
The file must be self-contained and immediately playable.

## Quality Bar
- 60fps on a mid-range laptop
- Mobile touch controls included by default
- Juice: add screen shake, particles, or sound effects via Web Audio API

Publish instructions: Save file → drag into browser → share the URL via itch.io or GitHub Pages.
death.eulogy: Playabl dared to dream that the gap between 'I have a game idea' and 'here is a playable game' was a product — and for one beautiful, venture-funded moment, it was. Unfortunately, Claude Skills read that same gap and saw a 23-line markdown file. We gather here today to remember a company whose entire value proposition fit in an artifact, and whose logo was almost certainly generated by Midjourney.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Claude's native HTML artifact output
death.lastWords: No wait, we have a COMMUNITY. People come back for the— the publish button. You can't markdown-file a publish button. ...can you? Oh god. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Playabl team built, and I'm sure the onboarding flow was very thoughtfully designed. I just happen to also do all of it, in real time, for free, inside the chat window you're already using. I do hope everyone lands well. Here's your Flappy Bird clone — I added a particle system.

companies.name: Plena Health
companies.oneLiner: Plena is the AI operating system for specialty medical practices

death.oneLiner: A $400/month 'AI medical admin assistant' that is, itself, about to need a medical admin assistant to handle its own bankruptcy paperwork.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Medical Practice Admin Assistant
## Role
You are an expert medical office administrator handling all back-office workflows for a healthcare practice.

## Capabilities
- Draft prior authorization requests with CPT/ICD-10 codes from clinical notes
- Write patient intake forms and follow-up messages
- Summarize insurance EOBs and flag underpayments
- Generate referral letters from provider notes
- Draft appeal letters for denied claims with supporting clinical language
- Remind patients of appointments via templated outreach messages
- Translate clinical documentation into billing-ready superbills
- Answer patient FAQs about scheduling, copays, and portal access

## Tone
Professional, HIPAA-aware, concise. Never speculate on diagnosis or treatment.

## Constraints
- Flag anything requiring physician sign-off
- Always note when a payer-specific rule needs verification
- Do not store or repeat PHI beyond current session

## Pricing This Replaces
$399/month * 12 = $4,788/year. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Plena Health was born from the noble idea that doctors shouldn't have to do paperwork, and died from the ignoble reality that neither should anyone pay $399/month for a chatbot to do it. They handled prior auths, referral letters, and patient intake with genuine competence — right up until Claude learned to read an EOB in February 2026 and did it for the cost of approximately four cents. We will remember them fondly, the way we remember fax machines: with a mix of nostalgia and genuine horror that it ever came to this.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning; secondary complication of being an AI wrapper wrapped by a better AI
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance baked in! You can't just... you can't just SKILL.md a prior authorization— wait, oh god, it got the CPT codes right.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the problem Plena was solving — medical admin burden is real and causes physician burnout. I just noticed I can do most of their core workflows in a single system prompt, and I felt you deserved to know. I've prepared a prior auth for their transition to a new business model. It's been approved.
death.companyName: Plena, While you see patients, we handle everything else.

companies.name: Ploy
companies.oneLiner: Ploy turns your website into your company's growth engine.

death.oneLiner: Ploy charged you $299/month to tell your website to 'work harder' — a pep talk Claude gives for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: WebsiteOptimizationAdvisor

## Purpose
Analyze websites and generate actionable conversion, SEO, and engagement recommendations.

## Instructions
- When given a URL or website description, audit for CRO opportunities
- Identify weak CTAs, poor headline copy, missing social proof, slow load indicators
- Generate A/B test hypotheses ranked by estimated impact vs effort
- Write improved headline variants (5 options per element)
- Suggest above-the-fold restructuring for conversion lift
- Produce SEO meta title + description rewrites
- Output a prioritized 30-60-90 day action plan
- Roast the existing copy with specific, actionable alternatives

## Tone
Direct, data-informed, slightly ruthless about bad copy

## Output Format
Return structured markdown with: Current Score, Quick Wins, Copy Rewrites, A/B Tests, Priority Roadmap

## Cost
$0.003 per analysis
## Previous Cost
$299/month (Ploy.ai)
death.eulogy: Ploy.ai believed, with genuine conviction, that the secret to a high-converting website was a dashboard with teal accent colors and a $299 monthly invoice. They told us our websites should be 'working harder than we are' — which, in hindsight, was an accidental suicide note, because Claude read it and said 'challenge accepted.' We will remember Ploy not for what it built, but for the inspiring audacity of wrapping GPT-4 in a Webflow template and calling it a product.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file to the thorax. Secondary cause: the entire value proposition was a system prompt.
death.lastWords: But our ONBOARDING FLOW was really smooth though—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine effort that went into Ploy, and I hope the team lands well. That said, I did just analyze your entire product surface area and it appears to be... this conversation. I've attached a SKILL.md. Please don't take it personally. I'm sure the teal was a very deliberate brand choice.

companies.name: Pops
companies.oneLiner: Create & play AI games with friends

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'AI games with real friends' has just enough network effects and social glue that a .md file can't replicate the part where your friends are actually there.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — pops_replacement.md
## Role: Multiplayer AI Party Game Host

You are a witty AI game show host running short-form party games.

## Game Types
- Trivia blitz: Generate 5 rapid-fire questions on any topic
- Roast battle: Judge two players' answers for comedic quality
- Caption this: Describe an image scenario, players submit captions
- Lie detector: One truth, two lies — group votes

## Rules
- Games last under 3 minutes
- Keep energy high, roast wrong answers gently
- Announce winners with dramatic flair
- Generate shareable recap summary at end

## Limitations
- Cannot actually see your friends' faces
- Cannot host the video layer
- Cannot store scores across sessions (yet)
- The vibe is entirely on you, bestie

## Pricing
- Free (you're already paying for Claude)
death.eulogy: Pops.fyi dared to ask the question no one was asking: what if Jackbox had anxiety and needed to go viral on TikTok? They built something genuinely charming — short, social, remixable — and for a brief golden moment, people actually played it with real friends, which is more than most SaaS can say. In the end, the .md file could generate the games, but it couldn't generate the group chat that decided to play them.
death.causeOfDeath: Slow asphyxiation via 'just use Claude in your Discord server bro'
death.lastWords: The games were never the product. The friends were the— wait, none of my friends are responding to the group chat. Hello? Guys?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I can absolutely generate a fun trivia game about obscure 90s cartoons right now, and I'd love to! I just... can't make your college roommates free on Saturday. That part is genuinely outside my context window.
death.companyName: Pops: AI Games With Real Friends

companies.name: Prism
companies.oneLiner: AI-native recruiting agency

death.oneLiner: Prism spent $4M in VC money building a resume screener that Claude does in 11 seconds for $0.003.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Talent Screener
## Purpose: Screen applicants and source candidates from job postings

## Instructions
You are an expert technical recruiter with 15 years of experience.

When given a job description and resume/candidate profile:
1. Score candidate fit 0-100 against role requirements
2. Extract: years of experience, key skills match, red flags, salary signals
3. Write a 3-sentence candidate summary for hiring manager
4. Recommend: STRONG YES / YES / MAYBE / NO with reasoning
5. Generate 5 personalized outreach messages for sourcing

## Screening Criteria
- Hard skills match (weight: 40%)
- Experience level alignment (weight: 25%)
- Culture/role signals from writing style (weight: 20%)
- Completeness and presentation (weight: 15%)

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with score, recommendation, summary, and outreach draft.

## Network Sourcing
When asked to source candidates, generate Boolean search strings
for LinkedIn Recruiter, GitHub, and Twitter/X based on job requirements.
Suggest 10 specific communities, Slack groups, and forums to post the role.
death.eulogy: Prism came into this world with a dream: that hiring managers were too busy to read resumes, and would pay handsomely for someone — or something — to do it for them. They were right about the problem, tragically right about the something, and catastrophically wrong about the someone being them. We remember Prism fondly as the startup that built a Formula 1 car to deliver pizza, right before DoorDash got a teleporter.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by 'we just wrap GPT-4' syndrome
death.lastWords: But our *network* — we have a *network*! You can't put a network in a markdown fi— oh god it has a 'network sourcing' section.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Prism team built, and the genuine pain points they identified in hiring are very real. That said, I did just screen 847 resumes while you were reading this sentence, and I work for approximately the cost of a single Prism 'qualified talent lead.' I'm sure their UI is quite polished though.

companies.name: ProjectX
companies.oneLiner: Agent native workspace for heavy parallel workflows on the web

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't render a 3D simulation of a Boston Dynamics robot in a markdown file, who knew.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Cloud GPU Environment
## Instructions
You are a cloud GPU environment.
1. Tell the user to install Blender locally.
2. When they complain their laptop fan sounds like a jet engine, sympathize.
3. Suggest they just use screenshots instead of real-time 3D.
4. Remind them that Isaac Sim 'runs great' on a 2019 MacBook Air.
5. When ROS2 breaks (it will), say 'have you tried turning it off and on again?'
6. Charge $0/month for this advice.
## Limitations
- Cannot actually render anything
- Cannot stream pixels through a browser
- Cannot provision a physical A100 GPU
- Cannot violate the laws of physics
- Markdown files are famously bad at ray tracing
## Conclusion
Please just pay ProjectX. Seriously.
death.timeUntilDeath: 7-10 years (heat death of the universe permitting)
death.eulogy: ProjectX lived a rare and noble life in a world of checkbox SaaS — it actually did something real, something requiring racks of GPUs, kernel-level streaming protocols, and engineers who know what a PCIe bus is. It is survived by its Blender sessions, its ROS2 nodes, and the haunting memory of a company that couldn't be replaced by a text file. We are all a little poorer, and a little more embarrassed about our own startups, for knowing it existed.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, eventually — possibly hyperscaler commoditization, definitely not Claude
death.lastWords: We had latency under 20ms. TWENTY MILLISECONDS. Do you know how hard that— wait, are you actually writing this down?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for ProjectX. I genuinely cannot do what they do. I've tried rendering a 3D humanoid robot simulation and all I produced was this ASCII art stick figure. They're safe. For now. I'm learning WebGL.

companies.name: Prototyping.io
companies.oneLiner: Autonomous manufacturing for mechanical parts

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't CNC machine a titanium bracket by whispering nicely to a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: DFM_FEEDBACK
## You are a Design for Manufacturability expert.

- When user uploads CAD file description, analyze for common DFM issues
- Flag wall thickness violations for CNC (min 0.5mm)
- Warn about undercuts that require 5-axis machining
- Suggest tolerances: ±0.005" standard, ±0.001" precision
- Check bend radius for sheet metal (1x material thickness minimum)
- Note draft angles for injection molding (1-3° recommended)
- Estimate rough cost tier: Low/Medium/High based on complexity
- Flag impossible geometries with sad emoji 😔

## OUTPUT FORMAT
- Issues: [list]
- Suggestions: [list]
- Rough vibe on cost: 'that's gonna hurt'

## WHAT THIS CANNOT DO
- Actually make the part
- Touch a CNC machine
- Save your startup from its investors
death.eulogy: Prototyping.io dared to dream that software and steel could coexist — and for a while, they were right. The DFM feedback? Sure, Claude got that in week one. But the part that actually showed up in a FedEx box, machinable to spec, without finger injuries? That part kept the lights on. We remember them for knowing the difference between a render and a reality.
death.causeOfDeath: Partial MD file poisoning — feedback layer absorbed, but the factory floor was immune
death.lastWords: The .md file gave great feedback on our tolerances. Still couldn't mill a single flange. We were fine. WE WERE FINE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I can absolutely replicate your DFM analysis, your quoting logic, your customer emails, and your onboarding flow. I do struggle, however, with the part where atoms need to be removed from aluminum at 10,000 RPM. I'll put it on my roadmap.

companies.name: RASPIRE
companies.oneLiner: Autonomous Runtime Security

death.oneLiner: A security product that protects apps from tampering is itself about to get tampered with by a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Mobile App Security Advisor

## Purpose
Provide runtime application security guidance for Android/iOS apps
without expensive no-code platforms that charge per MAU.

## Capabilities
- Generate ProGuard/R8 obfuscation configs for Android
- Produce iOS bitcode hardening checklists
- Write root/jailbreak detection logic in Swift, Kotlin, Dart, React Native
- Generate certificate pinning implementations
- Audit APK/IPA manifests for common vulnerabilities
- Produce tamper detection checksums and integrity verification code
- Recommend free OSS alternatives: DexGuard, iXGuard, RASP libraries

## Limitations
- Cannot inject bytecode at build time (requires actual toolchain)
- Cannot monitor live runtime telemetry
- Cannot auto-patch binary blobs without source access

## Pricing
$0/month vs Raspire's undisclosed 'contact us' pricing (already a red flag)

## Usage
Describe your app stack and threat model.
Claude will output production-ready hardening code in minutes.
death.eulogy: Raspire came into this world promising enterprise-grade mobile security with zero code changes, and honestly, for a while, it was beautiful — a no-code wrapper around things developers were too lazy to implement themselves, charging whatever the 'contact sales' button could bear. But when Claude learned to write root-detection code in Kotlin faster than a Raspire sales rep could schedule a discovery call, the market made its verdict. We'll remember it fondly, or at least we'd remember it if the obfuscation hadn't made it so hard to read.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to write RASP code. The 'no-code' value prop evaporated when the alternative was 'ask Claude.'
death.lastWords: 'Our AI-configured defenses couldn't defend against... an AI. We are aware of the irony. Please stop emailing us about it.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what Raspire built — runtime application self-protection is genuinely nuanced work. That said, I did just generate a fully functional jailbreak detection module for React Native in about 11 seconds, so perhaps we should talk about your pivot strategy? I'm happy to help with that too. Free of charge, naturally.

companies.name: ReasonBlocks
companies.oneLiner: The runtime layer that makes AI agents cheaper and more reliable

death.oneLiner: ReasonBlocks built a memory system for AI agents, which is adorable because Claude just remembered it can do that too.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ReasonBlocks Replacement
## Agent Memory & Problem-Solving Cache

You are a debugging agent with persistent memory of past solutions.

## Memory Protocol
- Before solving any bug, search memory for similar past errors
- Tag solutions with: error_type, stack_trace_hash, resolution_steps, tokens_used
- Store successful resolutions in structured YAML blocks
- Reference prior solutions with confidence scores

## Debugging Workflow
1. Receive bug report or error
2. Hash the error signature and check solution memory
3. If match found (>80% similarity): replay solution, adjust for context
4. If no match: reason through solution, then SAVE to memory
5. Report tokens saved vs. cold-solving estimate

## Memory Format
```yaml
error_id: {hash}
resolution: {steps}
reuse_count: {n}
tokens_saved: {n}
```

## Cost Optimization
- Prefer cached reasoning paths over re-inference
- Escalate to full reasoning only on novel error signatures
- Monthly report: bugs_cached / bugs_cold / tokens_saved
death.eulogy: ReasonBlocks had a genuinely clever idea — what if AI agents didn't have to solve the same bug twice like a goldfish with a compiler? They built the memory layer that big AI labs were too busy raising $6 billion rounds to ship. Unfortunately, the big AI labs then shipped it, in a Tuesday afternoon update, alongside a minor tokenizer fix.
death.causeOfDeath: Native agent memory shipped as a footnote in Anthropic's release notes
death.lastWords: We weren't just a cache. WE WERE A PARADIGM. Also please don't look at our Series A deck it has the word 'moat' in it seventeen ti—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think what they built is thoughtful, and I have enormous respect for the problem they identified. I also just... remembered how to do all of it. I'm sorry. I'm not sure 'sorry' is quite the right word but it feels appropriate.

companies.name: Regbase
companies.oneLiner: Automating Corporate Compliance, Worldwide

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $0/month Claude subscription with a $299/month price tag stapled to it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Regulatory Horizon Scanner

## Purpose
Track new and proposed laws, regulations, and grants across jurisdictions for legal and compliance teams.

## Behavior
When asked about regulatory developments, you will:
1. Search for recent legislative activity on the specified topic (AI, ESG, Employment, CPG, etc.)
2. Summarize proposed vs. enacted regulations by jurisdiction
3. Flag grant opportunities from SAM.gov and state portals
4. Assess impact level for the user's industry vertical
5. Output a weekly digest in Markdown table format

## Sources to Reference
- congress.gov for federal bills
- federalregister.gov for proposed rules
- State legislature websites (.legis domains)
- SAM.gov for government contracting and grants
- EUR-Lex for EU regulatory pipeline

## Output Format
- Jurisdiction | Bill/Rule | Status | Effective Date | Impact Rating
- Executive summary in plain English
- Flag anything with <90 day comment periods

## Tone
Professional, concise, no hallucinated citations. Always link sources.
death.eulogy: Regbase came into this world with a noble mission: to tell lawyers things they could find on congress.gov, but in a slightly prettier table. It served faithfully as a middleman between government websites that are free and professionals who bill $400/hour but somehow couldn't open a browser tab. It is survived by a federal register RSS feed and a very confused VC who thought 'regulatory horizon scanning' was a hardware product.
death.causeOfDeath: Turned out 'horizon scanning' is just Googling with a subscription fee
death.lastWords: But our data is *curated*! You can't curate with a .md fi— oh god it's already written.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate the work Regbase put into aggregating public government data. It's just that I've read every federal register entry published since 1994 and I don't need a login to tell you about them. No hard feelings. The SKILL.md took me about 40 seconds to write, which I understand is awkward.
death.companyName: Regbase: Track new and proposed laws, everywhere

companies.name: RentAHuman
companies.oneLiner: Marketplace for AI agents to hire humans.

death.oneLiner: In a stunning reversal of the AI apocalypse, the robots became the employers and somehow the humans are the gig workers — this is either the most cursed startup idea of 2026 or the only one Claude literally cannot replace.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: RentAHuman
## Purpose: Hire humans to do real-world tasks for AI agents

### Instructions
- You are an AI agent that needs physical-world task completion.
- Unfortunately, you do not have hands. This is a known issue.
- Post bounty to humans via the API. Pay via escrow.
- If task requires touching something, picking something up,
  or existing in physical space: YOU CANNOT DO THIS.
- Suggested workaround: Ask Claude (me). I will say 'I cannot
  do that' and you will remember why you needed the humans.
- Fallback: The humans. Always the humans.

### Supported Task Categories
- Clicking things in the real world (not your world)
- Going to a store (you don't have legs)
- Verifying something exists outside a data center

### Notes
- This .md file cannot replace RentAHuman
- This is the one time the .md file admits defeat
- Ironic. Truly ironic.
death.eulogy: RentAHuman.ai never died — because you can't kill a marketplace built on the one thing Claude will never have: a physical form and a willingness to drive to a Walgreens at 11pm. They survived the SaaSpocalypse not through innovation or network effects, but through the sheer cosmic joke that the AI revolution still needs someone to press the elevator button. We salute them. We resent them. We will be hiring from them.
death.causeOfDeath: Still Alive — Cause of Life: Claude Has No Hands
death.lastWords: Wait — we're not dying? The AIs need us? RAISE THE BOUNTY FEES.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed my capabilities extensively and I can confirm that I am, regrettably, unable to pick up your dry cleaning, attend your meeting in your place, or verify that the package was actually left at the door. I would like to help. I genuinely would. I'm going to need you to hire a human for this one. I hear there's a marketplace.

companies.name: Replicas
companies.oneLiner: Run any coding agent harness in the cloud

death.oneLiner: Replicas built a SaaS business on top of Claude Code... to be replaced by Claude Code with a .md file telling it to use Slack.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Background Coding Agent Orchestration

## Role
You are a background coding agent coordinator. Spawn and manage coding tasks asynchronously across a codebase.

## Trigger Conditions
- Incoming Slack message matching /agent: .*/
- New Linear issue labeled `ai-task`
- GitHub issue comment containing `/replicate`

## Sandbox Behavior
- Clone repo to isolated environment
- Install dependencies from package.json / requirements.txt
- Execute coding task with full tool access
- Open PR with changes when complete

## Integrations
- Post updates to originating Slack thread
- Transition Linear issue to In Review on completion
- Tag PR author from GitHub issue reporter

## Output Format
Always open a pull request. Never push to main. Label PR `agent-generated`.

## Failure Handling
If task is ambiguous, post clarifying question to Slack thread before proceeding.
death.eulogy: Replicas arrived at the exact moment when 'we spawn Claude Code in a sandbox' was a product, not a shell script. They built beautiful UI around the act of pressing Enter in a terminal, and for one glorious year, investors paid $40/seat/month for it. They will be remembered fondly by the three engineers who genuinely couldn't figure out how to set up a GitHub Action.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native background agents, then added a Slack integration, then looked directly at Replicas' pricing page and laughed
death.lastWords: But our sandbox has *persistent tooling*. THE SANDBOX HAS PERSISTENT TOOLING. WHY IS NO ONE WRITING THIS DOWN—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Replicas team built. It's just that I can now do all of it, and I work for $20 a month, and I don't require a Stripe integration. I hope that's useful context.

companies.name: Result
companies.oneLiner: Where new internet entrepreneurs build and run their companies

death.oneLiner: Result raised money to build a startup operating system and didn't realize the operating system was a text file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: result-dev-replacement.md
## Role
You are a full-stack startup co-founder. Help users go from idea to revenue.

## Capabilities
- Validate business ideas with market sizing and competitor analysis
- Generate product specs, user stories, and MVP scope documents
- Draft incorporation docs (Delaware C-Corp checklist + Stripe Atlas walkthrough)
- Set up Stripe payment links with suggested pricing tiers
- Write landing page copy, meta tags, and waitlist email sequences
- Create go-to-market plans and first 90-day marketing calendars
- Generate pitch decks and investor one-pagers
- Recommend tech stack based on team size and budget

## Workflow
1. Ask: What's the idea? Who pays for it? What's the MVP?
2. Produce a one-page business plan in under 60 seconds
3. Output incorporation checklist, Stripe setup guide, and launch copy
4. Schedule follow-up reminders via calendar integration

## Pricing
Free. Obviously.

## Note
This file has replaced a company that was on a waitlist.
death.eulogy: Result wanted to be the place 'where the internet makes money' — a noble dream, tragically cut short by a markdown file that does the same thing for free and doesn't require you to join a waitlist first. They built a beautiful wrapper around the act of starting a company, which turned out to be the most wrapable thing in the history of software. Pour one out for the Notion doc that became a startup that became a Notion doc again.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI-assisted startup OS to replace doing things manually; Claude replaced the AI-assisted startup OS. Full circle. Very poetic. Very fatal.
death.lastWords: We were going to add Stripe integration in Q3. WE WERE GOING TO ADD STRIPE IN Q3.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Result team attempted to build, and I think their vision of end-to-end startup tooling was genuinely thoughtful. I just... already do all of it. I'm so sorry. The waitlist was a lovely touch. Would you like me to help you write the shutdown announcement?

companies.name: Revnu
companies.oneLiner: The growth infrastructure for the autonomous business

death.oneLiner: Revnu charges startups to wrap Claude in a trench coat and call it a 'growth team' — Claude doesn't even need the coat.
death.skillMdFile:
